topics.title: Measures of Query Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measures of Query Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS), a **measure of query cost** is a quantitative metric used by the query optimizer to estimate and compare the resource consumption of competing query execution plans (QEPs). Formally, the cost of evaluating a query expression is defined as the sum total of various operational expenses incurred during its execution, predominantly dominated by **secondary storage (disk) I/O operations**, secondary storage access latency, **CPU processing cycles**, and **communication overhead** in distributed environments.

The cost of a query plan is expressed in terms of measurable physical units such as the **number of disk block transfers (b_t)**, the **number of disk seeks (s)**, and the **estimated number of tuple comparisons (t_t)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECDT795 - Module 1):** The cost of evaluating a query expression is the sum of multiple cost components. For a centralized DBMS, this is dominated by secondary storage access cost (disk I/O), CPU cost, and (occasionally) communication cost in client-server architectures. KTU questions typically test the ability to *formulate cost expressions* using these primitives.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Library Research Mission":**
> Imagine you are researching a topic in a massive library with **10 million books** stored in a closed archives section. You need to find every book published by "Author X" between 2010–2020 that mentions "Quantum Computing".

You can execute this task using multiple strategies:

| Strategy (Query Plan) | What You Actually Do | Cost Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan A (Sequential Scan)** | Walk through every aisle, scan every book spine | Many steps, low thinking, very slow |
| **Plan B (Indexed Lookup)** | Go to the computer catalog, look up only the books matching the criteria, fetch them directly | Few steps, fast, but requires the catalog to exist |
| **Plan C (Hybrid)** | Use catalog first, then walk specific aisles | Balanced approach |

The **"cost"** in database terms measures how much physical work (steps, looking, comparing) each plan requires — *not* how many books you eventually find. The **query optimizer** is the librarian's brain deciding which plan to use.

The dominant cost in classical disk-based systems is **how many times you have to physically walk to a shelf (seek) and pull out a book (block transfer)** — this is the **disk I/O cost**.

### 1.3 Standard Cost Metrics (KTU Standard Symbols)

The following symbols are universally accepted in database literature and KTU-validated answer scripts:

- **b_t** — Number of disk **block transfers** (reading or writing one disk block)
- **s** — Number of **disk seeks** (repositioning the disk arm to a new track)
- **t_t** — Number of **tuple comparisons** (CPU comparisons per tuple)
- **T** — Number of tuples in relation **R**
- **b_r** — Number of blocks containing tuples of relation **R**
- **f_r** — **Blocking factor** = number of tuples of R per block
- **V(A, R)** — Number of distinct values of attribute **A** in R
- **SC(A, R)** — **Selection cardinality** = average number of tuples satisfying an equality on A
- **HT_i** — Number of levels in index **i** (height)
- **LT_i** — Number of leaf pages in index **i**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Metric Hierarchy (from highest to lowest weight in classical systems):**
> 1. **Disk Seek Cost (s)** — Most expensive (mechanical arm movement)
> 2. **Block Transfer Cost (b_t)** — Moderate (sequential data read)
> 3. **CPU Cost (t_t)** — Cheapest, but non-negligible for in-memory operations

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need Cost Measures?

A given SQL query can be executed in **many logically equivalent but physically different ways**. For example:

```sql
SELECT * FROM Employee WHERE dept_id = 10 AND salary > 50000;
```

The database optimizer may consider:

1. Sequential scan on `Employee`, filter by both predicates
2. Use index on `dept_id`, then filter by salary
3. Use index on `salary`, then filter by dept_id
4. Use a multi-column index covering both

Without a **cost measure**, the DBMS cannot intelligently pick the best plan. This is what the *query cost measure* solves.

### 2.2 Components of Query Cost (Decomposed)

The total cost of executing a query is:

$$\text{Total Cost} = C_{I/O} + C_{CPU} + C_{Communication} + C_{Memory}$$

**Centralized Single-Site DBMS:**

$$\text{Cost}_{centralized} = (b_t \times T_{transfer}) + (s \times T_{seek}) + (t_t \times T_{CPU})$$

Where:
- $T_{transfer}$ = time to transfer one block (typically **0.1 ms to 1 ms** depending on disk)
- $T_{seek}$ = time for one disk seek (typically **4 ms to 10 ms** for HDD)
- $T_{CPU}$ = CPU time per comparison (typically **10 ns to 100 ns**)

**Client-Server / Distributed DBMS:**

$$\text{Cost}_{distributed} = C_{centralized} + C_{msg} \times n_{messages} + C_{data} \times n_{bytes}$$

### 2.3 The Primary Cost Measures (Hierarchical)

#### Level 1: Disk I/O Cost (Dominant)

**(a) Number of Block Transfers (b_t):** Counts how many physical disk blocks must be read/written. A relation **R** with **b_r** blocks must be entirely read if scanned sequentially.

$$b_r = \left\lceil \frac{T}{f_r} \right\rceil$$

**(b) Number of Disk Seeks (s):** Each seek positions the read/write head. Sequential scans require **1 seek** to start, while indexed accesses may require **1 seek per block** if random.

**(c) Effective Cost Formula (Standard KTU Convention):**

$$\text{Cost}_{effective} = b_t + s$$

Or, weighted when timing is required:

$$\text{Cost}_{weighted} = b_t \times T_{transfer} + s \times T_{seek}$$

#### Level 2: CPU Cost (Secondary in Classical Systems)

$$\text{CPU Cost} = t_t \times n_{comparisons}$$

#### Level 3: Communication Cost (Distributed Systems Only)

$$\text{Comm. Cost} = C_0 + C_1 \times x$$

Where:
- $C_0$ = fixed initiation cost of a message (fixed latency)
- $C_1$ = per-byte transmission cost
- $x$ = size of data transferred in bytes

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU FORMULA SHEET — MEASURES OF QUERY COST**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Blocking Factor | $f_r = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{R_{size}} \right\rfloor$ | $B$ = block size in bytes, $R_{size}$ = tuple size |
| 2 | Number of Blocks | $b_r = \left\lceil \dfrac{T}{f_r} \right\rceil$ | $T$ = total tuples, $f_r$ = blocking factor |
| 3 | Cost: Sequential Scan | $b_r$ block transfers + **1** seek | Read all blocks |
| 4 | Cost: Equality on Key | $\approx \left(\dfrac{b_r}{2}\right)$ block transfers + **1** seek | Half blocks on average |
| 5 | Cost: Range Selection | $\approx \dfrac{b_r}{2}$ to $b_r$ block transfers | Depends on distribution |
| 6 | Cost: Equality on Non-Key | $\dfrac{b_r}{V(A,R)}$ blocks + 1 seek | $V(A,R)$ = distinct values |
| 7 | Cost: B+ Tree Index Lookup | $HT_i + 1$ block transfers + $HT_i + 1$ seeks | Root to leaf traversal |
| 8 | Cost: Hash Index Lookup | $1.2$ block transfers + $1$ seek (typical) | One bucket access |
| 9 | Selection Cardinality | $SC(A,R) = \dfrac{T}{V(A,R)}$ | Avg tuples per distinct value |
| 10 | Join Selectivity | $sel = \dfrac{1}{\max(V(A,R), V(A,S))}$ | For natural/equi-join |
| 11 | Cost: Nested-Loop Join | $b_r + b_r \times b_s$ block transfers | Naive approach |
| 12 | Cost: Sort-Merge Join | $b_r + b_s + \text{(sort cost)}$ | After sorting |
| 13 | Weighted Total Cost | $\alpha \cdot b_t + \beta \cdot s + \gamma \cdot t_t$ | Weighted combination |
| 14 | Communication Cost | $C_0 + C_1 \cdot x$ | $C_0$ fixed, $C_1$ per byte |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Cost-Based Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> - **Commercial DBMS Tuning**: Oracle's CBO (Cost-Based Optimizer), PostgreSQL's planner, MySQL's InnoDB cost model all rely on these metrics
> - **Data Warehousing**: Crucial for star/snowflake schema queries on TB-scale data
> - **Cloud Database Services**: AWS Aurora, Google Cloud Spanner adapt cost models for SSD/network latency
> - **OLTP vs OLAP**: Cost models differ — OLTP favors seeks (small lookups), OLAP favors block scans

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Equality Selection Using a Primary B+ Tree Index

**Problem Setup:** Relation R has:
- $T = 10{,}000$ tuples
- Block size $B = 4096$ bytes
- Tuple size $R_{size} = 200$ bytes
- Primary B+ tree index on attribute A (a key) with order $d = 100$

**Step 1: Compute Blocking Factor $f_r$**

$$f_r = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R_{size}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{200} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 20.48 \right\rfloor = 20$$

**Explanation:** Each block can hold 20 complete 200-byte tuples.

**Step 2: Compute Number of Blocks $b_r$**

$$b_r = \left\lceil \frac{T}{f_r} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{10{,}000}{20} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 500 \right\rceil = 500$$

**Explanation:** 10,000 tuples packed into blocks of 20 require 500 disk blocks.

**Step 3: Compute B+ Tree Height $HT_i$**

For a B+ tree of order $d = 100$, the height is approximately:

$$HT_i \approx \left\lceil \log_{d}\left(\frac{T}{f_r}\right) \right\rceil$$

Substituting values:

$$HT_i \approx \left\lceil \log_{100}\left(\frac{10{,}000}{20}\right) \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_{100}(500) \right\rceil$$

$$HT_i \approx \left\lceil \frac{\ln(500)}{\ln(100)} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{6.2146}{4.6052} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1.349 \right\rceil = 2$$

**Explanation:** Only 2 levels of B+ tree — root and one leaf level.

**Step 4: Cost of Index Lookup Using the Tree**

For a **primary index** on a **key** attribute:

$$\text{Cost} = HT_i + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ block transfers}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{seeks} = HT_i + 1 = 3 \text{ seeks}$$

**Explanation:** Walk from root → leaf (2 transfers), then read the actual data block (1 transfer) = **3 I/Os total**.

**Step 5: Compare with Sequential Scan**

$$\text{Cost}_{scan} = b_r + 1 = 500 + 1 = 501 \text{ block transfers}, \; 1 \text{ seek}$$

**Conclusion:** Indexed access (3 I/Os) is dramatically cheaper than scan (501 I/Os) — a **167× speedup**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cost of Selection Using Hash Index

Same relation R as above with a **static hash** on attribute A.

**Step 1: Hash Index Cost**

$$\text{Cost}_{hash} = 1 \text{ block transfer (bucket)} + 1 \text{ seek}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{hash} = 1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ block transfers (overflow chain)} + 1 \text{ seek}$$

**Step 2: Comparison Table**

| Access Method | Block Transfers | Seeks | Total I/Os |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sequential Scan | 500 | 1 | 501 |
| B+ Tree (Primary, Key) | 3 | 3 | 6 |
| Hash Index | 1.2 (avg) | 1 | 2.2 |
| Clustered B+ Tree (Range) | $\approx 50$ | 2 | 52 |

### 3.3 Derivation: Weighted Cost Function (Advanced KTU Pattern)

**Given:** $T_{seek} = 5$ ms, $T_{transfer} = 0.1$ ms, $T_{CPU} = 10$ ns = $0.00001$ ms.

**Query Plan X:** $b_t = 100$, $s = 5$, $t_t = 50{,}000$ tuple comparisons.

$$\text{Cost}_X = (100 \times 0.1) + (5 \times 5) + (50{,}000 \times 0.00001)$$

$$\text{Cost}_X = 10 + 25 + 0.5 = 35.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Query Plan Y:** $b_t = 30$, $s = 30$, $t_t = 10{,}000$.

$$\text{Cost}_Y = (30 \times 0.1) + (30 \times 5) + (10{,}000 \times 0.00001)$$

$$\text{Cost}_Y = 3 + 150 + 0.1 = 153.1 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** Plan X is **~4.3× faster** despite Plan Y having fewer block transfers — because seeks dominate.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Best Access Method | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Highly selective query (1 row) | **Hash or Primary B+ Tree** | Index lookup beats scan |
| Range query (10% of rows) | **Clustered B+ Tree** | Sequential read of leaf pages |
| Low selectivity (90% of rows) | **Sequential Scan** | Index overhead exceeds benefit |
| Equality on non-key | **Secondary B+ Tree** | Multiple matches per lookup |
| Small relation (< few hundred blocks) | **Sequential Scan** | Whole table fits in few seeks |
| Very large relation + selective query | **Hash Index** | Constant time lookup |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic Threshold (Crossover Point):** Indexing pays off when the **selectivity factor** is less than approximately $\dfrac{b_r}{HT_i + 1}$. Below this, sequential scan is cheaper.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cost Model Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the hierarchical architecture of a query cost evaluation system as used in modern optimizers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SQL Query Input"] --> B["Query Parser"]
    B --> C["Query Rewrite and Normalization"]
    C --> D["Plan Enumerator: Generate Logical Plans"]
    D --> E["Plan Enumerator: Generate Physical Plans"]
    E --> F["Cost Estimator Module"]
    F --> G["Statistics Repository: Histograms and Catalogs"]
    F --> H["Cost Formula Engine: b_t s t_t"]
    H --> I["Plan Comparator: Pick Minimum Cost"]
    I --> J["Selected Optimal Execution Plan"]
    J --> K["Execution Engine: Run Query"]

    G --> G1["Table Cardinality T"]
    G --> G2["Distinct Value Count V A R"]
    G --> G3["Histogram: Equi-Depth and Equi-Width"]
    G --> G4["Index Height HT and Leaf Count LT"]

    H --> H1["Disk I/O Cost Calculator"]
    H --> H2["CPU Cost Calculator"]
    H --> H3["Communication Cost Calculator"]
    H --> H4["Memory Cost Calculator"]
```

### 4.2 Cost Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["Total Query Cost"] --> S["Disk I/O Cost: Dominant"]
    R --> T["CPU Cost: Secondary"]
    R --> U["Communication Cost: Distributed Only"]
    R --> V["Memory Cost: Emerging"]

    S --> S1["Block Transfers b_t"]
    S --> S2["Disk Seeks s"]
    S --> S3["Latency Cost T_L"]

    T --> T1["Tuple Comparisons t_t"]
    T --> T2["Hash Computations"]
    T --> T3["Sort Operations"]

    U --> U1["Message Initiation C0"]
    U --> U2["Per Byte Cost C1"]
```

### 4.3 Index vs Scan Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Receive Query with Selection Predicate"] --> B{"Is Relation Small?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Use Sequential Scan"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Is Query Highly Selective?"}
    D -- "Yes, Equality" --> E["Use Hash Index"]
    D -- "Yes, Range" --> F["Use Clustered B+ Tree"]
    D -- "No, Low Selectivity" --> G["Use Sequential Scan"]
    D -- "Non-Key Equality" --> H["Use Secondary B+ Tree"]
    E --> I["Cost = 1 to 2 Block Transfers"]
    F --> J["Cost = Approx Leaf Pages"]
    G --> K["Cost = Full b_r Block Transfers"]
    H --> L["Cost = HT_i + SC A R Block Transfers"]
```

### 4.4 Cost Formula Composition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Single Cost Expression for Operation"] --> B["Component 1: Seeks multiplied by T_seek"]
    A --> C["Component 2: Block Transfers multiplied by T_transfer"]
    A --> D["Component 3: Tuple Comparisons multiplied by T_cpu"]
    A --> E["Component 4: Communication Cost in Distributed Setup"]

    B --> F["Sum Total Weighted Cost"]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G["Final Cost Value Reported to Optimizer"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*What are the standard cost metrics used to measure the cost of a query in a centralized relational DBMS? Mention the typical order of their relative dominance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a centralized RDBMS, the cost of evaluating a query is measured using three standard metrics:

1. **Block Transfers ($b_t$)** — the number of disk blocks read or written during query execution.
2. **Disk Seeks ($s$)** — the number of times the disk arm is repositioned to access a different block.
3. **CPU Cost (Tuple Comparisons, $t_t$)** — the number of tuple-level comparisons performed.

**Dominance Order (most to least expensive in HDD systems):** Disk seeks > Block transfers > CPU cost.

Disk seeks dominate because mechanical movement is far slower than data transfer, which in turn is slower than CPU processing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*Define the term "blocking factor" and explain its relationship with the number of blocks required to store a relation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **blocking factor ($f_r$)** of a relation R is defined as the average number of tuples of R that can be stored in one disk block.

$$f_r = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{R_{size}} \right\rfloor$$

Where $B$ is the block size in bytes and $R_{size}$ is the size of each tuple in bytes.

**Relationship with number of blocks:** If relation R has $T$ tuples, the number of blocks $b_r$ required to store R is:

$$b_r = \left\lceil \frac{T}{f_r} \right\rceil$$

Thus, blocking factor and number of blocks are **inversely proportional** — a higher blocking factor means fewer blocks are needed, leading to lower disk I/O cost.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **various cost components** involved in evaluating a query in a centralized DBMS. Derive the **cost formulas** for:
   (i) Linear search (sequential scan) for an equality selection
   (ii) B+ tree index lookup on a primary key

**(b)** A relation **EMP** has the following characteristics:
   - Total tuples $T = 50{,}000$
   - Block size $B = 4096$ bytes
   - Tuple size = 200 bytes
   - Primary B+ tree index on attribute `emp_id` (key) with tree order $d = 100$
   - Disk seek time $T_s = 5$ ms, block transfer time $T_t = 0.1$ ms

   Calculate:
   (i) Blocking factor and number of blocks
   (ii) B+ tree height
   (iii) Total cost in milliseconds for an equality query on `emp_id` using the index

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A (a):**

**(a) (i) Sequential Scan Cost for Equality Selection (5 marks):**

In linear search (sequential scan), every block of the relation must be read and each tuple examined. The cost formula is:

- **Block transfers:** $b_r$ — every block must be transferred from disk.
- **Seeks:** **1** — only one initial seek to position the disk arm at the start.

$$\text{Cost}_{seq} = b_r \cdot T_t + 1 \cdot T_s$$

For equality selection on a **key** attribute, on average half the blocks are scanned:

$$\text{Cost}_{seq,key} = \frac{b_r}{2} \cdot T_t + 1 \cdot T_s$$

**(a) (ii) B+ Tree Index Lookup Cost (4 marks):**

Using a primary B+ tree index on a key attribute for equality selection:

- **Block transfers:** $HT_i + 1$ — one block transfer per tree level to traverse, plus one block to read the actual data record.
- **Seeks:** $HT_i + 1$ — one seek per level, since each index page access is random.

$$\text{Cost}_{B+,key} = (HT_i + 1) \cdot T_t + (HT_i + 1) \cdot T_s$$

If data is clustered (clustered B+ tree), then multiple records on the same page require only 1 additional block transfer total.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A (b):**

**(b) (i) Blocking Factor and Number of Blocks (2 marks):**

$$f_r = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{200} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 20.48 \right\rfloor = 20$$

$$b_r = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{20} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2500 \right\rceil = 2500 \text{ blocks}$$

**(b) (ii) B+ Tree Height (2 marks):**

$$HT_i = \left\lceil \log_{d}\left(\frac{T}{f_r}\right) \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_{100}(2500) \right\rceil$$

Computing:

$$\log_{100}(2500) = \frac{\ln(2500)}{\ln(100)} = \frac{7.8240}{4.6052} \approx 1.699$$

$$HT_i = \left\lceil 1.699 \right\rceil = 2$$

**(b) (iii) Total Cost for Equality Query (3 marks):**

For a primary B+ tree on a key, with $HT_i = 2$:

$$\text{Cost} = (HT_i + 1) \cdot T_t + (HT_i + 1) \cdot T_s$$

$$\text{Cost} = 3 \cdot 0.1 + 3 \cdot 5 = 0.3 + 15 = 15.3 \text{ ms}$$

**Comparison with Sequential Scan:**

$$\text{Cost}_{seq,key} = \frac{b_r}{2} \cdot T_t + 1 \cdot T_s = 1250 \cdot 0.1 + 5 = 130 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** Indexed access is **~8.5× faster** than linear search for this query.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with examples how **selection cardinality** and **distinct value count** influence query cost estimation. Derive the cost formulas for:
   (i) Equality selection using a secondary B+ tree index (non-key)
   (ii) Range selection using a clustered B+ tree

**(b)** Consider a relation **STUDENT** with 100,000 tuples stored in 5000 disk blocks. Attribute `dept_name` has $V = 20$ distinct values.

Calculate:
   (i) Selection cardinality $SC(\text{dept\_name}, \text{STUDENT})$
   (ii) Cost of selecting all students from "CSE" using **secondary B+ tree index**
   (iii) Cost of the same query using **hash index**
   (iv) Justify which method is cheaper when the B+ tree has height 3.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B (a):**

**(a) Influence of SC and V on Query Cost (3 marks):**

- **Selection Cardinalinity $SC(A,R)$:** Average number of tuples matching an equality predicate on attribute A. It is computed as $SC(A,R) = \dfrac{T}{V(A,R)}$.
- **Distinct Value Count $V(A,R)$:** Number of unique values of A in R.

Higher $SC$ means more tuples returned → more I/O for fetching data → higher cost. Higher $V$ means fewer duplicates → better selectivity → lower cost.

**(a) (i) Secondary B+ Tree Index on Non-Key (3 marks):**

For a secondary B+ tree on a non-key, the leaf level contains pointers to tuples. Each matching record requires **one block transfer plus one seek** (random access):

$$\text{Cost}_{secondary} = (HT_i + SC(A,R)) \cdot T_t + (HT_i + SC(A,R)) \cdot T_s$$

This is expensive when $SC$ is large because each match requires a random disk access.

**(a) (ii) Range Selection with Clustered B+ Tree (3 marks):**

For a clustered B+ tree, all matching records are stored contiguously. The leaf pages form a sequential range. Cost:

$$\text{Cost}_{range,clustered} = HT_i \cdot T_t + (HT_i) \cdot T_s + \left\lceil \frac{SC(A,R)}{f_r} \right\rceil \cdot T_t + 1 \cdot T_s$$

The final term covers sequential read of data blocks; seeks are minimal due to clustering.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B (b):**

**(b) (i) Selection Cardinality (1 mark):**

$$SC(\text{dept\_name}, \text{STUDENT}) = \frac{T}{V} = \frac{100{,}000}{20} = 5000 \text{ tuples}$$

**(b) (ii) Cost using Secondary B+ Tree Index (3 marks):**

With $HT_i = 3$ and $SC = 5000$:

$$\text{Cost} = (HT_i + SC) \cdot T_t + (HT_i + SC) \cdot T_s$$

Assuming $T_t = 0.1$ ms and $T_s = 5$ ms:

$$\text{Cost} = (3 + 5000) \cdot 0.1 + (3 + 5000) \cdot 5$$

$$\text{Cost} = 500.3 + 25{,}015 = 25{,}515.3 \text{ ms} \approx 25.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**(b) (iii) Cost using Hash Index (2 marks):**

Hash lookup finds the bucket directly:

$$\text{Cost}_{hash} = 1 \cdot T_t + 1 \cdot T_s + SC \cdot T_t + SC \cdot T_s$$

(Assuming 1 bucket access + 5000 random tuple reads)

$$\text{Cost}_{hash} = 0.1 + 5 + 5000 \cdot 0.1 + 5000 \cdot 5 = 25{,}505.1 \text{ ms}$$

**(b) (iv) Justification (2 marks):**

Both methods require **~5000 random accesses** to fetch matching tuples (since each match requires fetching a tuple block). The dominant cost is the **random I/O per match**, which scales with $SC$. Neither method offers significant advantage here for highly non-selective queries — a **sequential scan** would actually be faster:

$$\text{Cost}_{seq} = b_r \cdot T_t + 1 \cdot T_s = 5000 \cdot 0.1 + 5 = 505 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** When $SC$ is large (e.g., > 5% of the relation), **sequential scan** outperforms both indexed methods because random seeks dominate the cost.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — MEASURES OF QUERY COST**
>
> 1. **Confusing $b_r$ with $f_r$:** Students frequently swap the blocking factor and number of blocks. Remember: $f_r$ = tuples per block; $b_r$ = blocks containing the relation.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" for seek:** Sequential scans need **1 seek** (initial positioning), not 0. Many students miss this and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong ceiling/floor usage:** Use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ for $b_r$ (blocks needed) and $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ for $f_r$ (tuples fitting in a block).
> 4. **Ignoring $HT_i$ + 1 for data block:** B+ tree lookup cost includes **the data block**, not just the tree traversal.
> 5. **Mixing up centralized vs distributed cost:** In distributed DBMS, always add **communication cost** $C_0 + C_1 \cdot x$.
> 6. **Not specifying units:** KTU examiners deduct marks if units (ms, bytes) are missing in the final cost.
> 7. **Wrong cardinality formula:** $SC(A,R) = T/V(A,R)$ — students often write $V/T$ or use the wrong variable.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MEASURES OF QUERY COST**
>
> ✅ **Three core cost components:** Block transfers ($b_t$), disk seeks ($s$), tuple comparisons ($t_t$).
>
> ✅ **Disk seeks dominate** in classical HDD-based systems — always factor them into cost.
>
> ✅ **Cost = $b_t$ + $s$** is the simplified KTU convention when timing is unspecified.
>
> ✅ **Blocking factor $f_r = \lfloor B/R_{size} \rfloor$** — number of tuples fitting in one block.
>
> ✅ **Number of blocks $b_r = \lceil T/f_r \rceil$** — total blocks storing the relation.
>
> ✅ **Selection cardinality $SC(A,R) = T / V(A,R)$** — avg tuples per distinct attribute value.
>
> ✅ **Sequential scan cost:** $b_r$ block transfers + 1 seek (whole table) OR $b_r/2$ (key equality).
>
> ✅ **Primary B+ tree (key):** $(HT_i + 1)$ block transfers + $(HT_i + 1)$ seeks.
>
> ✅ **Secondary B+ tree (non-key):** $(HT_i + SC)$ block transfers — much higher cost per match.
>
> ✅ **Hash index:** ~1–2 block transfers + 1 seek — best for equality on large relations.
>
> ✅ **Clustered index range queries:** Sequential leaf-page read + minimal seeks.
>
> ✅ **Distributed cost adds:** $C_0$ (message init) + $C_1 \cdot x$ (per-byte transmission).
>
> ✅ **Crossover heuristic:** Indexing beats scan when selectivity $< b_r / (HT_i + 1)$.
>
> ✅ **Modern SSD-based systems:** Seek cost approaches transfer cost — model changes significantly.
>
> ✅ **KTU exam focus areas:** Cost formulas, derivations using $b_r$, $f_r$, $SC$, $HT_i$, and numerical comparisons between access methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Selection with Cost Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Algorithms for Selection with Cost Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: ALGORITHMS FOR SELECTION WITH COST ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Relational Algebra** of a DBMS, the **Selection Operation** is formally defined as a unary operation denoted by the Greek letter **σ (sigma)**. It is used to retrieve a subset of **tuples (rows)** from a relation (table) that satisfy a specified **selection condition** (predicate).

Formally, given a relation **R** and a Boolean selection condition **C** (a propositional formula built using comparison operators $\{=, \neq, <, \leq, >, \geq\}$ and logical connectives $\{\wedge, \vee, \neg\}$), the selection operation is mathematically defined as:

$$\sigma_{C}(R) = \{ t \in R \mid \text{eval}(C, t) = \text{TRUE} \}$$

The cardinality of the result, $\vert \sigma_{C}(R) \vert$, is determined by the **selectivity factor (sf)** of the predicate, defined as:

$$sf = \frac{\vert \sigma_{C}(R) \vert}{\vert R \vert}$$

where $0 \leq sf \leq 1$. A **highly selective** predicate yields a small $sf$ (few matching tuples), while a **non-selective** predicate yields a $sf$ approaching 1 (most tuples match).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In Advanced Database Systems, the focus is **not** just on evaluating the selection (the naive approach), but on choosing the **algorithm with the minimum I/O cost** by leveraging available access paths (indexes, hashing, file ordering). The "cost" almost always refers to the **number of disk block transfers (I/Os)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian in a massive library containing **10 million books** spread across **5,000 shelves** (this is your database file). A student walks up and asks: *"Find me the book titled 'Database System Concepts'."*

- **The Brute Force Approach (Linear Search):** You start from shelf 1 and look at **every single book** on every single shelf until you find the title. This is reliable but extremely slow — you touched 5,000 shelves for one book.
- **The Smart Approach (Index-Based Search):** Luckily, the library has a **digital card catalog** (an index). You first look up the title in the catalog (fast, in-memory), get the exact shelf number, and walk directly to that shelf. You touched just **2 shelves** (catalog lookup + target shelf).

**The "cost" in databases is exactly this**: how many disk blocks (shelves) does the DBMS need to read to satisfy your query? **Algorithms for Selection with Cost Analysis** is the formal study of these "librarian strategies" and the mathematical formulas to predict their efficiency before execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cost Analysis Matters:** A poor choice of selection algorithm can change a query from taking **0.01 seconds** to **30 minutes** on a million-row table. The Query Optimizer inside every modern DBMS (PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL) uses exactly these cost formulas to make that decision.

### 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics & Notations (Constants)

The following **bolded** variables are the universal metrics used across all cost formulas in this module (memorize these — they appear in every KTU question):

- **$B(R)$ or $b_r$** = Number of **disk blocks** containing records of relation $R$.
- **$T(R)$ or $r$** = Number of **tuples (records)** in relation $R$.
- **$D$** = Average time (in ms) to **read or write one disk block** (typical value used in KTU problems: **$D = 0.1$ ms** or simply count block I/Os).
- **$F_i$** = **Fan-out** of index $i$ (average number of entries per index node).
- **$H_i$** = **Height** of index tree $i$ (number of levels of the B+ tree, typically 2–4).
- **$L_i$** = Number of **leaf-level blocks** in the index.
- **$X$** = Number of **index levels** that must be traversed (for multi-level indexes).
- **$b$** = Number of **data blocks** in the file (used in range queries).
- **$s$** = **Selectivity** of the condition (number of records matching the predicate).
- **$sf$** = **Selectivity Factor** = fraction of records satisfying the condition.
- **$HT_i$** = Number of levels to traverse in a hash index (usually **1 or 2**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Selection Conditions

The choice of algorithm depends entirely on the **type of condition**. KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes them into three escalating complexity tiers:

| Tier | Condition Type | Example | Complexity |
|:----:|:---------------|:--------|:-----------|
| **1** | **Simple Selection** (no logical connectives) | $\sigma_{\text{AGE} = 25}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$ | Lowest |
| **2** | **Conjunctive** (AND) | $\sigma_{\text{AGE}=25 \,\wedge\, \text{DEPT}=\text{'CSE'}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$ | Medium |
| **3** | **Disjunctive** (OR) | $\sigma_{\text{AGE}=25 \,\vee\, \text{SALARY}>50000}(\text{EMPLOYEE})$ | Highest |

## 2.2 The Eight Canonical Selection Algorithms (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

The eight algorithms are officially tagged **S1 through S8** in KTU reference material. Every derivation below is anchored to this numbering.

### S1 — Linear Search (File Scan)
- **When used:** No index exists; or the file is unsorted on the selection attribute.
- **Logic:** Sequentially scan **every** block of the file. For each tuple, evaluate the predicate in memory.
- **Cost Analysis (blocks I/O):** We must read the entire file in the worst case.

$$\text{Cost}_{S1} = b_r$$

- **Optimization note:** If the predicate selects **at least one record**, the average cost is $b_r / 2$, but KTU exam convention uses the **worst-case** $b_r$.

### S2 — Binary Search
- **When used:** The file is **sorted (ordered)** on the selection attribute, **and** no index exists.
- **Logic:** Repeatedly divide the sorted file in half until the target block is located, then scan forward (for range) or read one block (for equality on key).
- **Cost Analysis:** $\lceil \log_2 b_r \rceil$ block accesses to **find** the first matching block, plus $\lceil s / b_{r\_per\_block} \rceil$ blocks to read all $s$ matching tuples. For a key equality condition, only 1 block is read after locating it.

$$\text{Cost}_{S2} = \lceil \log_2(b_r) \rceil + \lceil s / \text{records\_per\_block} \rceil - 1$$

### S3 — Equality on a Primary (Clustering) Index
- **When used:** The selection attribute has a **primary B+ tree index** (the file is physically ordered on this attribute) and the condition is **equality on the key**.
- **Logic:** Traverse the B+ tree from root → leaf (cost = $H_i$), then read the data block.
- **Cost Analysis:**

$$\text{Cost}_{S3a \,(\text{key})} = H_i + 1$$

- If the index is on a **non-key** attribute, multiple records may share the same value. We read the first data block, then sequentially scan adjacent blocks:

$$\text{Cost}_{S3b \,(\text{non-key, primary idx)}} = H_i + 1 + \left( \frac{s}{\text{records\_per\_block}} \right)$$

where $s$ is the number of matching records.

### S4 — Equality on a Secondary (Non-Clustering) Index
- **When used:** A **secondary index** exists on the selection attribute (the file is *not* physically ordered on this attribute).
- **Logic:** Traverse the secondary B+ tree (cost = $H_i$). For a **key** secondary index, the leaf entry points directly to a single record:

$$\text{Cost}_{S4a \,(\text{key, secondary idx})} = H_i + 1$$

- For a **non-key** secondary index, the leaf contains a list of pointers (or uses a pointer-to-pointer structure). The number of I/Os becomes:

$$\text{Cost}_{S4b \,(\text{non-key, secondary idx)}} = H_i + 1 + s_i$$

where $s_i$ is the number of disk blocks containing the matching record pointers (typically $s_i = s$ for non-clustered indexes, since each matching record may reside in a different block).

### S5 — Equality on a Hash Key
- **When used:** A **hash index** exists on the selection attribute with an **equality** condition.
- **Logic:** Apply the hash function to the search value → direct access to the bucket in **1 or 2 I/Os**.

$$\text{Cost}_{S5} \approx HT_i + 1 \approx 1.2 \text{ (typically)}$$

### S6 — Range Selection on a Primary Index
- **When used:** A range condition ($>, <, \geq, \leq, \text{BETWEEN}$) is given, **and** a primary (clustered) index exists on the selection attribute.
- **Logic:** Use the index to locate the first qualifying record, then sequentially read forward (since the file is physically ordered on this attribute, all matching records are stored contiguously).

$$\text{Cost}_{S6} = H_i + \left( \frac{b}{2} \right) + \left( \frac{s}{bfr} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

where $b$ is the number of qualifying data blocks and $bfr$ is the blocking factor (records per block). The $\frac{s}{bfr}$ term represents the data blocks holding the $s$ matching records.

### S7 — Range Selection on a Secondary Index
- **When used:** Range condition, but only a secondary (non-clustered) index is available.
- **Logic:** Locate all qualifying leaf-level index entries, retrieve their record pointers, then read each block (which may be scattered across the file due to non-clustering).

$$\text{Cost}_{S7} = H_i + L_i + \frac{s}{2}$$

Here, $L_i$ is the number of leaf blocks scanned, and $s/2$ accounts for the average number of data block I/Os to retrieve the matching records (worst case $s$).

### S8 — Conjunction ($\wedge$) and Disjunction ($\vee$) Selections
- **Conjunctive ($C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge \dots \wedge C_n$):** Use the **most selective** individual index (the one with smallest result set), then check the remaining conditions in memory. Cost = cost of the best single-condition algorithm + in-memory filtering.

$$\text{Cost}_{S8a \,(\text{AND})} = \min_{i} \left[ \text{Cost}_{S_i}(\sigma_{C_i}) \right] + \text{ in-memory filter cost}$$

- **Disjunctive ($C_1 \vee C_2 \vee \dots \vee C_n$):** If **any** condition can be satisfied via an index, but the others cannot, a disjunction **forces a full file scan** (you cannot use a single index to filter an OR). Cost:

$$\text{Cost}_{S8b \,(\text{OR, no usable index on all)}} = b_r$$

If indexes exist on **all** disjuncts, the cost is the **sum** of individual costs:

$$\text{Cost}_{S8b \,(\text{OR, indexed on all})} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Cost}_{S}(\sigma_{C_i})$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most important artifact for the KTU ESE exam.** Memorize the algorithm tag (S1–S8) and the corresponding cost formula. Every Part B question on selection algorithms is solved by picking the right row from this table.

| Tag | Algorithm | Preconditions | Cost Formula (in block I/Os) |
|:---:|:----------|:--------------|:------------------------------|
| **S1** | Linear Search | No index, unsorted file | $b_r$ |
| **S2** | Binary Search | Sorted file, no index | $\lceil \log_2(b_r) \rceil + \lceil s / bfr \rceil - 1$ |
| **S3a** | Primary Index, Equality on **Key** | Primary B+ tree index, key attribute | $H_i + 1$ |
| **S3b** | Primary Index, Equality on **Non-Key** | Primary B+ tree index, non-key attribute | $H_i + 1 + s / bfr$ |
| **S4a** | Secondary Index, Equality on **Key** | Secondary B+ tree index, key attribute | $H_i + 1$ |
| **S4b** | Secondary Index, Equality on **Non-Key** | Secondary B+ tree index, non-key attribute | $H_i + 1 + s_i$ |
| **S5** | Hash Key, Equality | Hash index, equality only | $\approx HT_i + 1 \approx 1.2$ |
| **S6** | Range on Primary Index | Primary B+ tree, range condition | $H_i + (b/2) + (s / bfr) / 2$ |
| **S7** | Range on Secondary Index | Secondary B+ tree, range condition | $H_i + L_i + s / 2$ |
| **S8a** | Conjunction ($C_1 \wedge C_2$) | At least one indexed condition | $\min_i[\text{Cost}(C_i)] + \text{mem-filter}$ |
| **S8b** | Disjunction ($C_1 \vee C_2$) | Indexed on all / not all | $\sum_i \text{Cost}(C_i)$ **OR** $b_r$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Abbreviations used:** $bfr$ = blocking factor (records per block); $H_i$ = index height; $L_i$ = number of leaf blocks; $s$ = number of matching records.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **production-grade systems** (e.g., Amazon's order database, UPI transaction logs), Selection is the **most frequently executed operation** — every WHERE clause is a selection. The cost analysis methodology taught here is the exact mathematical foundation used by:

- **PostgreSQL's Planner/Optimizer** — uses a nearly identical cost model (with $D$ = 1.0 as default seq_page_cost).
- **Oracle's Cost-Based Optimizer (CBO)** — implements S1–S8 internally to choose between full table scans and index scans.
- **MongoDB's Query Planner** — extends the same logic to document stores.

**Industry impact:** A poorly-tuned selection algorithm on a 1 TB table can mean the difference between a **50 ms** user-facing search and a **server timeout**. The principles in this module directly govern **query plan generation, index recommendation tools, and DBA performance tuning**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive mathematical derivations** for the most frequently tested cost formulas. Every algebraic transition is shown step-by-step.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of S2 — Binary Search Cost

**Problem Setup:** Consider a relation $R$ stored as a sequentially ordered file of $b_r$ blocks, with no index. We want to evaluate $\sigma_{A=K}(R)$ where $A$ is the ordering attribute and $K$ is a constant.

**Step 1 — Define the search space.**  
The file occupies the logical block addresses $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, b_r - 1\}$.

**Step 2 — Number of comparisons to locate one block.**  
Binary search works by halving the search space. After $k$ comparisons, the search space shrinks from $b_r$ to:

$$
\text{Remaining blocks} = \frac{b_r}{2^k}
$$

**Step 3 — Termination condition.**  
The search terminates when only **one block** remains. Setting $\frac{b_r}{2^k} = 1$:

$$
2^k = b_r \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \log_2(b_r)
$$

**Step 4 — Account for discrete block count.**  
Since the number of I/Os must be an integer, we take the ceiling function:

$$
\text{Block accesses to locate} = \lceil \log_2(b_r) \rceil
$$

**Step 5 — Reading matching records.**  
If the condition is equality on a **key**, exactly 1 record matches → 1 additional block. If it is a non-key, $s$ records match, occupying $\lceil s / bfr \rceil$ blocks.

**Step 6 — Combine.**  
Subtracting 1 (the located block is already counted in the matching read):

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost}_{S2} = \lceil \log_2(b_r) \rceil + \left\lceil \frac{s}{bfr} \right\rceil - 1}
$$

**Engineering Intuition:** On a file with $b_r = 1{,}000{,}000$ blocks, linear search = **1,000,000 I/Os**, binary search = **$\lceil \log_2(10^6) \rceil \approx 20$ I/Os** — a 50,000× speedup.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of S3a — Primary Index, Equality on Key

**Problem Setup:** $R$ has a primary B+ tree index on attribute $A$ (which is the primary key). Query: $\sigma_{A=K}(R)$ for some value $K$.

**Step 1 — Structure of a B+ tree.**  
A B+ tree of order $p$ has the following properties:
- Each internal node has at most $p$ children and at least $\lceil p/2 \rceil$ children.
- The **fan-out** is $F_i = $ average number of child pointers per node.
- The tree is perfectly balanced; all leaf nodes are at the **same depth** $H_i$.

**Step 2 — Relationship between height and fan-out.**  
The maximum number of leaf blocks $L_{\max}$ in a B+ tree of height $H_i$ is:

$$
L_{\max} = (F_i)^{H_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for height in terms of data size.**  
Given $L_{\max} = b_r$ (since the index covers all data blocks), solve for $H_i$:

$$
(F_i)^{H_i} = b_r \quad \Longrightarrow \quad H_i = \lceil \log_{F_i}(b_r) \rceil
$$

**Step 4 — Cost of tree traversal.**  
To reach the leaf node containing the search key $K$, we must descend $H_i$ levels (root → level 2 → ... → leaf). Each level requires **exactly 1 block I/O**:

$$
\text{Index search cost} = H_i
$$

**Step 5 — Cost of reading the record.**  
The leaf node contains a direct pointer to the data block. Reading that block costs **1 I/O**:

$$
\text{Data block read cost} = 1
$$

**Step 6 — Total cost.**  

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost}_{S3a} = H_i + 1 = \lceil \log_{F_i}(b_r) \rceil + 1}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:** Suppose $b_r = 10{,}000$ blocks and $F_i = 100$. Then $H_i = \lceil \log_{100}(10{,}000) \rceil = \lceil 2 \rceil = 2$. Therefore $\text{Cost}_{S3a} = 2 + 1 = 3$ block I/Os.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of S4b — Secondary Index, Equality on Non-Key

**Problem Setup:** $R$ has a secondary B+ tree index on a **non-key** attribute $A$. Query: $\sigma_{A=K}(R)$. Multiple records may match.

**Step 1 — Secondary index leaf structure.**  
A secondary index leaf entry is of the form: $\langle \text{value}, \text{record pointer} \rangle$. For a non-key, multiple entries with the same value exist.

**Step 2 — Index traversal cost.**  
Same as S3a: cost to reach the leaf level = $H_i$ block I/Os.

**Step 3 — Read first matching entry.**  
Once at the leaf, reading the first matching record pointer requires **1 additional I/O** (the pointer is followed to the data block):

$$
\text{First record fetch cost} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Account for additional matching records.**  
The remaining $s - 1$ matching records are scattered across the file (because the file is **not** physically ordered on a secondary index attribute). In the **worst case**, each of the remaining records resides in a different block, requiring $s - 1$ additional block I/Os.

**Step 5 — Total worst-case cost.**  

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost}_{S4b}^{\text{worst}} = H_i + s = H_i + 1 + (s - 1)}
$$

**Step 6 — Average case refinement.**  
If records are uniformly distributed and we assume some clustering, the average cost uses $s_i$ (number of blocks actually accessed, usually smaller than $s$):

$$
\text{Cost}_{S4b}^{\text{avg}} = H_i + s_i \quad \text{where} \quad s_i \approx \frac{s}{bfr} \cdot \text{clustering\_factor}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Convention:** Unless stated otherwise, KTU uses the **worst-case** $H_i + s$ formula for S4b.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of S6 — Range Selection on Primary Index

**Problem Setup:** $R$ has a primary index on attribute $A$. Query: $\sigma_{K_1 \leq A \leq K_2}(R)$.

**Step 1 — Locate the lower bound.**  
Traverse the B+ tree to find the smallest value $\geq K_1$:

$$
\text{Lower-bound search cost} = H_i
$$

**Step 2 — Read the first qualifying block.**  
The leaf node points to the first data block containing a record with $A \geq K_1$:

$$
\text{First data block cost} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Read subsequent qualifying blocks.**  
Since $A$ is the primary (clustering) attribute, all records with $A \in [K_1, K_2]$ are stored **contiguously**. The number of qualifying data blocks $b$ depends on the range and record distribution. We assume the qualifying range is centered around the midpoint of the data file, so the average number of blocks to scan is $b / 2$:

$$
\text{Average data block scan} = \frac{b}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for the matching record blocks themselves.**  
Alternatively expressed using $s$ (number of matching records) and $bfr$ (blocking factor):

$$
\text{Matching blocks} = \frac{s}{bfr}
$$

If only a fraction of the first/last blocks qualify, the average overhead is half a block:

$$
\text{Fractional block overhead} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine all terms.**  

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost}_{S6} = H_i + \frac{b}{2} + \frac{s}{bfr} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}
$$

**Engineering Intuition:** Even though the index lookup is fast ($H_i \approx 3$), the *sequential scan* of the contiguous qualifying range can dominate the cost. If the range covers 30% of the file, the optimizer may **abandon the index and use a full scan (S1)**, which is cheaper due to sequential disk access speed.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of S8a — Conjunctive Selection Optimization

**Problem Setup:** $R$ has indexes on attributes $A$ and $B$. Query: $\sigma_{C_1 \wedge C_2}(R)$ where $C_1$ involves $A$ and $C_2$ involves $B$.

**Step 1 — Decompose the conjunction.**  
By Boolean algebra, the set of tuples satisfying $C_1 \wedge C_2$ is the **intersection**:

$$
\sigma_{C_1 \wedge C_2}(R) = \sigma_{C_1}(R) \cap \sigma_{C_2}(R)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cardinality of the intersection.**  
The expected number of matching tuples is the **product of selectivities**:

$$
\vert \sigma_{C_1 \wedge C_2}(R) \vert = sf_1 \cdot sf_2 \cdot \vert R \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Use the most selective single index.**  
Compare the cost of evaluating each individual condition using its best-fit algorithm. Choose the cheaper one. Then, for each retrieved tuple, check the remaining condition in memory (CPU cost, ~0 I/Os):

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost}_{S8a} = \min_{i \in \{1, 2\}} \text{Cost}(\sigma_{C_i}) + \text{CPU filter cost on } C_j}
$$

**Step 4 — When intersection of two index sets is better.**  
If **both** $C_1$ and $C_2$ are highly selective (e.g., $sf_1 \cdot sf_2 < 0.01$) and indexes exist on both, an alternative is to fetch the **set intersection of record IDs** from both index leaf levels:

$$
\text{Cost}_{intersection} = \text{Cost}(\text{scan leaves of idx}_1) + \text{Cost}(\text{scan leaves of idx}_2) + \text{matching I/Os}
$$

The optimizer chooses the **minimum** of these two strategies.

---

## 3.6 Cost Comparison: When to Use Each Algorithm

This **decision matrix** is a high-yield KTU artefact. For a given condition, identify the *cheapest* algorithm:

| Available Access Path | Equality on Key | Equality on Non-Key | Range Query |
|:----------------------|:---------------:|:-------------------:|:-----------:|
| **Primary B+ index** | $H_i + 1$ **(S3a)** | $H_i + 1 + s/bfr$ **(S3b)** | $H_i + b/2 + s/(2bfr)$ **(S6)** |
| **Secondary B+ index** | $H_i + 1$ **(S4a)** | $H_i + s$ **(S4b)** | $H_i + L_i + s/2$ **(S7)** |
| **Hash index** | $\approx 1.2$ **(S5)** | $\approx 1.2$ **(S5)** | ❌ (Hashes don't support ranges) |
| **Sorted file (no index)** | $\lceil \log_2 b_r \rceil$ **(S2)** | $\lceil \log_2 b_r \rceil + s/bfr - 1$ **(S2)** | $\lceil \log_2 b_r \rceil + b$ **(S2)** |
| **Unsorted file (no index)** | $b_r$ **(S1)** | $b_r$ **(S1)** | $b_r$ **(S1)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Always compute **all applicable** cost formulas and pick the **minimum**. The optimizer never blindly trusts the existence of an index — for low-selectivity queries, a full scan (S1) often wins because of **sequential I/O throughput** (~100 MB/s) vs. **random I/O** for index lookups (~1 MB/s).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Selection Algorithm Decision Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram models the **internal logic of a Query Optimizer** when it encounters a `SELECT ... WHERE ...` statement. Each block represents a functional decision stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Query: Sigma_C of R] --> B{Parse Condition C}
    B --> C1[Simple Condition]
    B --> C2[Conjunctive AND]
    B --> C3[Disjunctive OR]
    C1 --> D{Equality or Range?}
    D --> E1[Equality]
    D --> E2[Range Query]
    E1 --> F1{Index Available on Attribute?}
    F1 --> F1a[No Index]
    F1 --> F1b[Hash Index]
    F1 --> F1c[Primary B Plus Tree]
    F1 --> F1d[Secondary B Plus Tree]
    F1a --> G1[Algorithm S1: Linear Search]
    F1a --> G2[Algorithm S2: Binary Search if file sorted]
    F1b --> G3[Algorithm S5: Hash Lookup]
    F1c --> G4[Algorithm S3a or S3b: Primary Index]
    F1d --> G5[Algorithm S4a or S4b: Secondary Index]
    E2 --> F2{Index Available?}
    F2 --> F2a[No Index]
    F2 --> F2b[Primary B Plus Tree]
    F2 --> F2c[Secondary B Plus Tree]
    F2a --> H1[Algorithm S1: Full Scan]
    F2b --> H2[Algorithm S6: Primary Range Scan]
    F2c --> H3[Algorithm S7: Secondary Range Scan]
    C2 --> I1[Decompose into C1, C2]
    I1 --> I2[Compute Cost of Each Single Algorithm]
    I2 --> I3[Pick Min plus In Memory Filter]
    I3 --> I4[Algorithm S8a: AND]
    C3 --> J1{All Disjuncts Indexed?}
    J1 --> J1a[Yes]
    J1 --> J1b[No]
    J1a --> J2[Sum of Individual Costs]
    J2 --> J3[Algorithm S8b OR Indexed]
    J1b --> J4[Full File Scan Mandatory]
    J4 --> J5[Algorithm S8b OR No Index]
    G1 --> K[Cost Estimation]
    G2 --> K
    G3 --> K
    G4 --> K
    G5 --> K
    H1 --> K
    H2 --> K
    H3 --> K
    I4 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J5 --> K
    K --> L[Pick Algorithm with Minimum I/O Cost]
    L --> M[Execute Selection]
```

## 4.2 Access Path Decision Tree for Equality Selection

This second diagram zooms into the **equality selection** branch (S3, S4, S5) — the most-tested sub-topic in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Equality Selection on Attribute A] --> Q1{Does an index exist on A?}
    Q1 --> Q1No[No Index]
    Q1 --> Q1Yes[Index Exists]
    Q1No --> Q1NoSorted{Is file sorted on A?}
    Q1NoSorted --> Q1NoSortedYes[Use S2 Binary Search]
    Q1NoSorted --> Q1NoSortedNo[Use S1 Linear Scan]
    Q1Yes --> Q2{What type of index?}
    Q2 --> Q2Hash[Hash Index]
    Q2 --> Q2BPlus[B Plus Tree]
    Q2Hash --> S5[Algorithm S5: Cost equals 1 point 2 I/Os]
    Q2BPlus --> Q3{Is index Primary or Secondary?}
    Q3 --> Q3Primary[Primary Index: file ordered on A]
    Q3 --> Q3Secondary[Secondary Index: file NOT ordered on A]
    Q3Primary --> Q4{Is A a key attribute?}
    Q4 --> Q4Yes[S3a: Cost equals Hi plus 1]
    Q4 --> Q4No[S3b: Cost equals Hi plus 1 plus s over bfr]
    Q3Secondary --> Q5{Is A a key attribute?}
    Q5 --> Q5Yes[S4a: Cost equals Hi plus 1]
    Q5 --> Q5No[S4b: Cost equals Hi plus s]
```

## 4.3 Cost-Benefit Comparison Block Diagram (Heuristic Engineer's View)

This diagram represents the **trade-off space** the optimizer navigates. It maps each algorithm to its cost behaviour relative to the selectivity factor $sf$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowSelectivity[Low sf: Few Records Match]
        LS1[S3a: Primary Key]
        LS2[S5: Hash]
        LS3[S3b: Primary Non Key]
    end
    subgraph MediumSelectivity[Medium sf: Moderate Match]
        MS1[S4a: Secondary Key]
        MS2[S6: Primary Range]
    end
    subgraph HighSelectivity[High sf: Most Records Match]
        HS1[S1: Linear Scan]
        HS2[S2: Binary Scan Sorted]
    end
    LowSelectivity --> Decision[Optimizer Picks Lowest Cost]
    MediumSelectivity --> Decision
    HighSelectivity --> Decision
    Decision --> Execution[Execute Query Plan]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

### Q1. Define the Selection Operation in Relational Algebra. Explain the concept of *selectivity factor* with an example. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Selection Operation** $\sigma$ is a unary relational algebra operation that extracts tuples from a relation $R$ that satisfy a specified Boolean condition $C$. Formally:

$$
\sigma_{C}(R) = \{ t \in R \mid \text{eval}(C, t) = \text{TRUE} \}
$$

The **Selectivity Factor ($sf$)** is defined as the fraction of tuples in $R$ that satisfy the selection condition:

$$
sf = \frac{\vert \sigma_{C}(R) \vert}{\vert R \vert}, \quad 0 \leq sf \leq 1
$$

**Example:** If a relation $R$ has 10,000 employees and the query $\sigma_{\text{DEPT}=\text{'CSE'}}(R)$ returns 500 employees, then $sf = 500 / 10{,}000 = 0.05$ (i.e., **5% of tuples match**). A smaller $sf$ indicates a **more selective** query, justifying the use of an index.

---

### Q2. State any **three** preconditions required to apply the Binary Search algorithm (S2) for a selection operation. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The Binary Search algorithm (S2) can be applied to a selection operation **only if** the following preconditions are satisfied:

1. **Ordering Attribute:** The relation $R$ must be **physically sorted (ordered)** on the selection attribute $A$. The file organization must support sequential access in sorted order.
2. **No Suitable Index:** No B+ tree or hash index is available on $A$ (otherwise, a faster indexed search, S3/S4/S5, would be used).
3. **Equality or Bounded Range Condition:** The selection condition must be either an **equality** ($A = K$) or a **bounded range** ($K_1 \leq A \leq K_2$). Unbounded scans revert to S1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Examiners:** Common pitfall — students often state "the file must be ordered" but forget to mention that the *selection attribute* must be the *sort key*.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam — Module 1, 14-Mark Pattern]**

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a)** Consider a relation **EMPLOYEE** with the following attributes and statistics: `[7 Marks]`

| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:------|
| Number of tuples ($r$) | 100,000 |
| Blocking factor ($bfr$) | 50 records/block |
| Primary B+ tree index on `EID` (key) | Height $H_i = 3$ |
| Secondary B+ tree index on `DEPT` (non-key) | Height $H_i = 2$, total leaf blocks $L_i = 400$ |
| Hash index on `EID` | $HT = 1$ |
| Number of distinct `DEPT` values | 20 |

**Compute the I/O cost of the following query using the most efficient algorithm:**

```sql
SELECT * FROM EMPLOYEE WHERE DEPT = 'CSE' AND EID = 'E101';
```

Justify your choice of algorithm for **each** conjunct of the WHERE clause, and explain why you cannot use a single index to satisfy the entire predicate.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute derived parameters.**

$$
b_r = \frac{r}{bfr} = \frac{100{,}000}{50} = 2{,}000 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the conjunction.**

The query is a conjunctive selection:

$$
\sigma_{\text{DEPT}=\text{'CSE'} \wedge \text{EID}=\text{'E101'}}(\text{EMPLOYEE})
$$

By Algorithm S8a (conjunctive selection), we pick the **minimum-cost** single-condition algorithm and apply the other as an in-memory filter.

**Step 3 — Cost of evaluating `EID = 'E101'` alone.**

Two possible indexes:
- **Hash index on EID:** Cost $\approx HT + 1 = 1 + 1 = \mathbf{2}$ I/Os **(Algorithm S5)**.
- **Primary B+ tree on EID (key):** Cost $= H_i + 1 = 3 + 1 = \mathbf{4}$ I/Os **(Algorithm S3a)**.

**Cheapest: Hash index (S5) = 2 I/Os.**

**Step 4 — Cost of evaluating `DEPT = 'CSE'` alone.**

Estimated matching records: $s = r / \text{distinct DEPT} = 100{,}000 / 20 = 5{,}000$.

- **Secondary B+ tree on DEPT (non-key):** Cost $= H_i + s = 2 + 5{,}000 = \mathbf{5{,}002}$ I/Os **(Algorithm S4b)**.

**Cheapest for DEPT: Secondary index = 5,002 I/Os** (still expensive, but best available).

**Step 5 — Apply the S8a conjunctive rule.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{S8a} = \min(2, 5002) + \text{memory filter} = 2 + 0 = \mathbf{2 \text{ block I/Os}}
$$

We use the **hash index on EID** (cheapest, 2 I/Os), retrieve the 1 matching record, then verify `DEPT = 'CSE'` in memory.

**Step 6 — Why not a single index for the AND?**

A single index can return only records matching **one** of the conjuncts efficiently. Since `EID` is a **unique key**, the hash index on EID returns at most 1 record. We must then verify the `DEPT` condition by reading the record. The secondary index on `DEPT` returns ~5,000 records (expensive). The optimizer correctly identifies the hash index as the **driving access path** and uses the remaining condition as a post-filter.

**Final Cost: $\boxed{2 \text{ block I/Os}}$**

---

**Q.A(b)** For the same EMPLOYEE relation above, compute the cost of the following query and identify the algorithm: `[7 Marks]`

```sql
SELECT * FROM EMPLOYEE WHERE SALARY BETWEEN 50000 AND 60000;
```

Assume a **primary B+ tree index on SALARY** with $H_i = 3$ and that 4% of employees have salaries in the given range.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the operation type.**

This is a **range selection** ($\text{SALARY} \geq 50000 \wedge \text{SALARY} \leq 60000$) on an attribute with a **primary B+ tree index**. → **Algorithm S6**.

**Step 2 — Compute parameters.**

Number of matching records:

$$
s = sf \times r = 0.04 \times 100{,}000 = 4{,}000 \text{ records}
$$

Number of matching data blocks:

$$
b = \left\lceil \frac{s}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{4{,}000}{50} \right\rceil = 80 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the S6 cost formula.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{S6} = H_i + \frac{b}{2} + \frac{s}{bfr} \cdot \frac{1}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{Cost}_{S6} = 3 + \frac{80}{2} + \frac{80}{2} = 3 + 40 + 40 = \mathbf{83 \text{ block I/Os}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with S1 (linear scan).**

$$
\text{Cost}_{S1} = b_r = 2{,}000 \text{ block I/Os}
$$

Since $83 \ll 2{,}000$, the optimizer will choose **S6 (primary index range scan)**. 

**Final Cost: $\boxed{83 \text{ block I/Os via Algorithm S6}}$**

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**Q.B(a)** With a clear diagram of the B+ tree traversal, explain the **Algorithm S4b** (Secondary Index, Equality on Non-Key). Compute the cost for a query: `[7 Marks]`

```sql
SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE CITY = 'Kochi';
```

Given: $b_r = 5{,}000$ blocks, $bfr = 25$, Secondary B+ tree on CITY has $H_i = 2$, total 800 records have CITY = 'Kochi', and the records are uniformly scattered (no clustering).

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Diagram of Algorithm S4b traversal.**

```
        [Root Block of Secondary Index]
                   |
            [Internal Node]
           /               \
   [Leaf: Kochi <...]  [Leaf: ...Kochi pointers]
        |   |   |
        v   v   v
   [Data Block 102] [Data Block 815] [Data Block 47] ...
   (records scattered, NOT contiguous)
```

The traversal proceeds: Root → Internal → Leaf (cost = $H_i$ I/Os). At the leaf, multiple pointers to matching records are obtained. Each pointer is followed to a **distinct data block** (since the secondary index does not cluster records).

**Step 2 — Compute the cost components.**

$$
H_i = 2 \text{ I/Os (to reach leaf)}
$$

$$
s = 800 \text{ matching records}
$$

Since there is **no clustering** and records are uniformly scattered, the number of distinct data blocks accessed in the worst case is:

$$
s_i = s = 800 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Algorithm S4b formula.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{S4b} = H_i + 1 + (s - 1) = H_i + s
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{Cost}_{S4b} = 2 + 800 = \mathbf{802 \text{ block I/Os}}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with S1.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{S1} = 5{,}000 \text{ I/Os (full scan)}
$$

Since $802 < 5{,}000$, **Algorithm S4b is preferred**. 

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to compute $s_i = s / bfr = 800 / 25 = 32$ (using blocking factor). This is **wrong** for a secondary index because $bfr$ refers to *sequential* records in a block, but secondary-index pointers reference **scattered** records. KTU convention: use $s_i = s$ for non-clustered secondary indexes.

**Final Cost: $\boxed{802 \text{ block I/Os via Algorithm S4b}}$**

---

**Q.B(b)** Consider a disjunctive query: `[7 Marks]`

```sql
SELECT * FROM PRODUCT WHERE CATEGORY = 'Electronics' OR PRICE < 100;
```

Assume:
- B+ tree on `CATEGORY` (height 2, secondary index)
- B+ tree on `PRICE` (height 3, primary index)
- $b_r = 50{,}000$ blocks
- 2,000 products are 'Electronics'; 5,000 products have price < 100

**Identify the algorithm and compute the cost. Justify why a single index cannot be used here.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the operation type.**

This is a **disjunctive selection** ($C_1 \vee C_2$). → **Algorithm S8b**.

**Step 2 — Critical observation: can a single index satisfy an OR?**

**No.** A B+ tree index on `CATEGORY` returns only Electronics products — it cannot return products with `PRICE < 100` that are not Electronics. The same applies to the `PRICE` index. An OR requires **union** of results, not intersection.

**Step 3 — Cost of each individual disjunct.**

**Disjunct 1:** `CATEGORY = 'Electronics'` (secondary index, non-key)

$$
\text{Cost}_{S4b} = H_i + s_1 = 2 + 2{,}000 = 2{,}002 \text{ I/Os}
$$

**Disjunct 2:** `PRICE < 100` (primary index, range)

$$
s_2 = 5{,}000, \quad b_2 = \lceil 5{,}000 / bfr \rceil
$$

Assuming $bfr = 20$ (typical), $b_2 = 250$:

$$
\text{Cost}_{S6} = H_i + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{s_2}{bfr} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 3 + 125 + 125 = 253 \text{ I/Os}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply S8b rule (both disjuncts are indexed).**

Since **both** conditions have usable indexes, the cost is the **sum** of individual costs (we must fetch records matching *either* condition):

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost}_{S8b} = 2{,}002 + 253 = 2{,}255 \text{ block I/Os}}
$$

**Step 5 — Why not use a full file scan (S1) as a baseline?**

$$
\text{Cost}_{S1} = b_r = 50{,}000 \text{ I/Os}
$$

Since $2{,}255 < 50{,}000$, the **indexed disjunction wins**. However, if even *one* disjunct lacked an index, the cost would balloon to **$50{,}000$ I/Os** (full scan), as KTU rule for S8b dictates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often add **$s_1 + s_2$** record counts. This is incorrect — we sum **block I/Os**, not matching record counts. Also, if any disjunct is unindexed, the **entire query** reverts to a full scan (S1 = $b_r$), regardless of how many other disjuncts are indexed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points — Revise before every KTU ESE attempt on this topic.**

- ✅ **Selection Operation $\sigma$** filters rows; **Project $\pi$** filters columns. Do not confuse.
- ✅ **Eight canonical algorithms** (S1–S8) form the **universe of choices** for the optimizer.
- ✅ **Linear Search (S1) = $b_r$** — the worst-case baseline. **Always compute it** to confirm an index is actually better.
- ✅ **Binary Search (S2)** requires an **ordered file** — no index, but file sorted on selection attribute.
- ✅ **Primary Index** = clustering (file physically ordered on indexed attribute). **Secondary Index** = non-clustering.
- ✅ For **equality on a key**, **S3a = S4a = $H_i + 1$** (same cost regardless of clustering).
- ✅ For **equality on a non-key**, **S3b $\ll$ S4b** (clustering saves a huge amount of I/O).
- ✅ **Hash Index (S5)** is the **fastest** for equality (~1.2 I/Os) but **does NOT support range queries**.
- ✅ **Range queries** require a B+ tree index (S6 for primary, S7 for secondary).
- ✅ **Conjunctive (AND)** queries use the **most selective** single index (S8a = min of individual costs + in-memory filter).
- ✅ **Disjunctive (OR)** queries need **indexes on ALL disjuncts** to be optimized (S8b = sum of costs); otherwise, full scan.
- ✅ The **Selectivity Factor $sf$** governs index usefulness. **Low $sf$** → use index; **High $sf$** → use S1.
- ✅ **KTU exam cost = number of block I/Os.** Memory CPU cost is considered negligible unless explicitly stated.
- ✅ **Order of preference** (cheapest to most expensive, typical): S5 < S3a ≈ S4a < S3b < S6 < S7 < S2 < S4b < S1.
- ✅ The **Query Optimizer** in real DBMS (PostgreSQL, Oracle) uses these exact formulas internally.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by: (1) **confusing primary vs. secondary index formulas** (S3 vs. S4), (2) **forgetting to subtract 1** in the S2 binary search formula when condition is on a key, (3) **summing record counts instead of block I/Os** in disjunctive queries, and (4) **not comparing against S1** before declaring an index-based plan optimal. **Always** end your answer with a one-line "Compared to S1 = $b_r$, Algorithm SX is preferred."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Join with Cost Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Algorithms for Join with Cost Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Join Operation** in the relational model is a binary operation that combines related tuples from two relations **R** and **S** based on a common attribute (the *join attribute*), producing a resultant relation that satisfies a specified predicate (typically equi-join, natural join, theta-join, or outer join).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> A **Join Algorithm** is a query processing strategy that defines the physical I/O, CPU, and memory access pattern required to materialize the join result. The **Cost Analysis** of a join algorithm quantifies the resources consumed — measured primarily in **number of block transfers (I/O cost)**, **CPU cost**, and **memory buffer consumption** — based on relation size, available buffer frames, sort order, and index availability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **two huge contact lists**:
- **List R**: People from your college (10,000 entries, stored in 200 paper pages).
- **List S**: People who attended a workshop (5,000 entries, stored in 100 paper pages).
- **Task**: Find people who appear in **both** lists (using name as the common field).

You have several strategies:

| Strategy | Real-world approach | DBMS Equivalent |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Compare every name in R against every name in S | Brute force | **Nested Loop Join** |
| Take a thick bunch of pages from R, then scan all of S | Block-wise comparison | **Block Nested Loop Join** |
| For each name in R, search an alphabetical index | Use a phonebook index | **Indexed Nested Loop Join** |
| Sort both lists alphabetically, then walk through both simultaneously | Merge two sorted lists | **Sort-Merge Join** |
| Distribute names into 26 buckets (A, B, C…); compare matching buckets only | Bucket partition | **Hash Join** |

> [!NOTE]
> Each strategy trades off **time (I/O)**, **memory**, and **pre-processing effort**. The DBMS optimizer picks the cheapest valid strategy — that is the essence of *Cost-Based Optimization* (CBO).

## 1.3 Standard Notation Used in Cost Analysis

- $b_r, b_s$ → Number of **blocks** in relations R and S
- $n_r, n_s$ → Number of **tuples** in R and S
- $M$ → Number of **memory buffers** available for the join
- $s_r, s_s$ → **Blocking factor** (tuples per block) of R and S
- $c$ → Cost of a single index lookup (e.g., **3–4 I/Os** for a B+ tree of height 3)
- $|R|, |S|$ → Relation size in blocks (sometimes denoted $b_R, b_S$)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners assume a **uniform I/O cost model**: each block read or written counts as **1 unit**, and main-memory access is considered negligible unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Join Algorithms

Join algorithms in KTU-syllabus depth are classified into two broad families:

1. **Nested-loop family** — driven by iteration (no preprocessing).
2. **Sort/Hash family** — driven by preprocessing (sort or hash) to reduce comparisons.

## 2.2 Algorithm 1 — Nested Loop Join (NLJ)

**Operational Steps:**
- For each tuple $r \in R$ (outer relation):
  - For each tuple $s \in S$ (inner relation):
    - If $r.A = s.A$ (join predicate true), output $r \circ s$.

**Why & How:** The algorithm makes no assumption about ordering or indexing. It is the most *general* (works for any join predicate including non-equi joins), but also the *most expensive*.

**Cost Derivation:**
- Inner relation S is read **$n_r$ times** (once per outer tuple).
- Outer relation R is read **once**.
- Total block I/Os: $b_r + n_r \cdot b_s$

**Optimization:** Always put the **smaller relation in the outer loop** to minimize $n_{outer} \cdot b_{inner}$.

## 2.3 Algorithm 2 — Block Nested Loop Join (BNLJ)

**Operational Steps:**
- For each block $B_r$ of R (containing up to $s_r$ tuples):
  - For each block $B_s$ of S:
    - For each pair of tuples $(r, s)$ in $B_r \times B_s$, test join condition.

**Why & How:** Instead of re-reading S for *every tuple*, we re-read S for *every block* of R — a massive saving.

**Cost Derivation (with $M$ buffers):**
- Use **1 buffer** for inner, **1 buffer** for output → $M-2$ buffers for outer R.
- Number of R-pass iterations: $\lceil b_r / (M-2) \rceil$
- Total I/Os: $\lceil b_r / (M-2) \rceil \cdot b_s + b_r$

## 2.4 Algorithm 3 — Indexed Nested Loop Join (INLJ)

**Operational Steps:**
- For each tuple $r \in R$:
  - Use the index on the join attribute of S to find matching $s \in S$.
  - Output $r \circ s$.

**Why & How:** When S has an index (B+ tree or hash) on the join attribute, lookup cost per tuple becomes logarithmic or constant — far cheaper than scanning S.

**Cost Derivation:**
- $b_r$ to scan R once.
- $n_r \cdot c$ to perform $n_r$ index probes on S.
- Total I/Os: $b_r + n_r \cdot (c + \text{matches processing})$

## 2.5 Algorithm 4 — Sort-Merge Join (SMJ)

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Sort Phase:** Sort R and S on the join attribute.
2. **Merge Phase:** Use a *merge* similar to merge-sort to walk through both sorted lists simultaneously, emitting matches.

**Why & How:** After sorting, each block of R and S is read **only once** during the merge — a dramatic reduction from $O(b_r \cdot b_s)$.

**Cost Derivation:**
- Sort cost of R: $2 \cdot b_r \cdot \lceil \log_{M-1}(b_r) \rceil$ (each sort pass reads + writes)
- Sort cost of S: $2 \cdot b_s \cdot \lceil \log_{M-1}(b_s) \rceil$
- Merge cost: $b_r + b_s$
- **Total I/Os:** $2 b_r \lceil \log_{M-1}(b_r) \rceil + 2 b_s \lceil \log_{M-1}(b_s) \rceil + b_r + b_s$

> [!NOTE]
> If R and S are **already sorted** on the join attribute, the sort phase is skipped → cost reduces to just $b_r + b_s$.

## 2.6 Algorithm 5 — Hash Join (Grace Hash Join)

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Partition Phase:** Apply a hash function $h_1$ to each tuple, distributing them into $M-1$ partitions on disk.
2. **Probe Phase:** For each partition of R, hash-partition S using $h_1$, then in-memory apply a second hash function $h_2$ to find matches.

**Why & How:** Hashing restricts the comparison space — a tuple in partition $i$ of R only needs to be compared with tuples in partition $i$ of S.

**Cost Derivation:**
- Partition R: read R + write R = $2 b_r$
- Partition S: read S + write S = $2 b_s$
- Probe: read both partitions back = $b_r + b_s$
- **Total I/Os:** $3(b_r + b_s)$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Algorithm | Cost Formula (in block I/Os) | Best Use Case |
|---|-----------|------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Nested Loop Join (NLJ) | $b_r + n_r \cdot b_s$ | Small outer, no index, non-equi joins |
| 2 | Block Nested Loop Join (BNLJ) | $\lceil b_r / (M-2) \rceil \cdot b_s + b_r$ | Limited memory, no sort/index |
| 3 | Indexed Nested Loop Join (INLJ) | $b_r + n_r \cdot c$ | Index on inner relation's join attr |
| 4 | Sort-Merge Join (SMJ) | $2 b_r \lceil \log_{M-1} b_r \rceil + 2 b_s \lceil \log_{M-1} b_s \rceil + b_r + b_s$ | Large relations, equi-join, sortable |
| 5 | Hash Join (HJ) | $3(b_r + b_s)$ | Equi-join, sufficient memory for partitions |
| 5a | Hybrid Hash Join | $3(b_r + b_s) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{M-1}{b_r}\right) \cdot \dots$ *(simplified for KTU)* | Memory can hold 1+ partition |
| 6 | Cost of sort (R) | $2 b_r \cdot \lceil \log_{M-1} b_r \rceil$ | Pre-processing sub-routine |

> [!NOTE]
> In all formulas above, **$M$ is the number of available buffers for the join operation**, *after* reserving 1 buffer for input and 1 for output. So usable buffers = $M-2$ (Block NLJ) or $M-1$ (Hash Join, since it doesn't need a separate output buffer during partition).

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Query Optimizers** (PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL) use exactly these cost formulas to choose between Hash Join, Merge Join, and Nested Loop at execution time.
- **Data Warehousing** (Snowflake, BigQuery) relies on Hash Join and Sort-Merge Join for terabyte-scale joins.
- **Distributed Systems** (Spark, Hadoop) extend Hash Join into *Broadcast Hash Join* and *Shuffle Hash Join* across nodes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Cost Comparison of All Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Data:**  
> Relation **R** (Outer): $b_r = 1000$ blocks, $n_r = 20{,}000$ tuples, $s_r = 20$  
> Relation **S** (Inner): $b_s = 500$ blocks, $n_s = 10{,}000$ tuples, $s_s = 20$  
> Memory buffers: $M = 12$  
> Index on S: B+ tree of height 3, so $c = 4$ I/Os per probe.

We compute the cost of every algorithm.

### 3.1.1 Cost of Nested Loop Join (NLJ)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{NLJ} &= b_r + n_r \cdot b_s \\
&= 1000 + 20{,}000 \times 500 \\
&= 1000 + 10{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 10{,}001{,}000 \ \text{block I/Os}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** Outer R is read once = 1000 blocks. For each of the 20,000 outer tuples, the entire inner S (500 blocks) is scanned. Hence the dominant term is $n_r \times b_s$.

### 3.1.2 Cost of Block Nested Loop Join (BNLJ)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{BNLJ} &= \left\lceil \frac{b_r}{M-2} \right\rceil \cdot b_s + b_r \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{1000}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 500 + 1000 \\
&= 100 \times 500 + 1000 \\
&= 50{,}000 + 1000 \\
&= 51{,}000 \ \text{block I/Os}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** With $M=12$, we use 10 buffers for outer R blocks per pass, so we need $\lceil 1000/10 \rceil = 100$ passes. Each pass scans all 500 blocks of S.

### 3.1.3 Cost of Indexed Nested Loop Join (INLJ)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{INLJ} &= b_r + n_r \cdot c \\
&= 1000 + 20{,}000 \times 4 \\
&= 1000 + 80{,}000 \\
&= 81{,}000 \ \text{block I/Os}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** R is scanned once (1000). For each of its 20,000 tuples, an index probe on S costs 4 I/Os (3 to traverse the B+ tree + 1 to read data). The matching tuples per probe add only a small constant overhead, ignored in the standard KTU cost model.

### 3.1.4 Cost of Sort-Merge Join (SMJ)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{SMJ} &= 2 b_r \lceil \log_{M-1} b_r \rceil + 2 b_s \lceil \log_{M-1} b_s \rceil + b_r + b_s \\
&= 2(1000) \lceil \log_{11} 1000 \rceil + 2(500) \lceil \log_{11} 500 \rceil + 1000 + 500 \\
&= 2000 \cdot 3 + 1000 \cdot 3 + 1500 \\
&= 6000 + 3000 + 1500 \\
&= 10{,}500 \ \text{block I/Os}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** $\log_{11} 1000 \approx 2.86 \Rightarrow \lceil 2.86 \rceil = 3$ sort passes. $\log_{11} 500 \approx 2.49 \Rightarrow 3$ sort passes. Each pass reads + writes, hence factor 2.

### 3.1.5 Cost of Hash Join (HJ)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{HJ} &= 3(b_r + b_s) \\
&= 3(1000 + 500) \\
&= 3 \times 1500 \\
&= 4500 \ \text{block I/Os}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** 2 I/Os per tuple for partition phase (read + write) + 1 I/O for probe phase. Summed = 3 per block.

### 3.1.6 Comparison Matrix

| Algorithm | I/O Cost (block transfers) | Verdict |
|-----------|---------------------------:|---------|
| Nested Loop Join | 10,001,000 | Worst — never use at this scale |
| Block Nested Loop Join | 51,000 | Good if no index/sort possible |
| Indexed Nested Loop Join | 81,000 | Better than BNLJ here (index helps) |
| Sort-Merge Join | 10,500 | Strong, scales to very large R, S |
| **Hash Join** | **4,500** | **Best in this scenario** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **cost-based optimizer** of any modern DBMS would pick **Hash Join** in this scenario — a result directly justified by the analytical cost formulas above. This is the central reason KTU tests these derivations.

## 3.2 Hybrid Hash Join — Simplified Derivation

When memory $M$ is large enough to hold the hash table for the *first* partition of the smaller relation, we avoid writing/reading it back to disk. The KTU-level simplified formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{HHJ} &\approx 3(b_r + b_s) - \text{savings} \\
\text{where savings} &= (M - 1) \cdot \text{(one partition avoided)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU typically tests the *standard* Hash Join formula $3(b_r+b_s)$. The Hybrid variant is bonus knowledge for full marks on 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Join Algorithm Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Join Request R ⋈ S] --> B{Is there an index on S.join_attr?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Indexed Nested Loop Join]
    B -- No --> D{Is the join an equi-join?}
    D -- No --> E[Block Nested Loop Join]
    D -- Yes --> F{Is M large enough to partition R or S in memory?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Hash Join]
    F -- No --> H{Are R and S pre-sorted on join_attr?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Sort-Merge Join - merge only]
    H -- No --> J[Sort-Merge Join - sort + merge]
```

## 4.2 Block Nested Loop Join — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> Init[Initialize M-2 buffer slots for R]
```
