```mermaid
    Init --> LoadR[Load next chunk of M-2 blocks of R into memory]
    LoadR --> LoadS[Load first block of S into buffer]
    LoadS --> Compare[Compare each R-tuple chunk with current S-block]
    Compare --> Match{Matches?}
    Match -- Yes --> Out[Append to output buffer]
    Match -- No --> NextS[Move to next S-block]
    NextS --> MoreS{More S blocks?}
    MoreS -- Yes --> LoadS
    MoreS -- No --> ResetS[Reset pointer to first S-block]
    ResetS --> MoreR{More R chunks?}
    MoreR -- Yes --> LoadR
    MoreR -- No --> Done([Output complete])
    Out --> Compare
```

## 4.3 Sort-Merge Join — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input R and S] --> B[Sort R on join attribute]
    A --> C[Sort S on join attribute]
    B --> D[Sorted R]
    C --> E[Sorted S]
    D --> F[Merge Phase]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Current R value = Current S value?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Emit join tuple; advance S pointer]
    G -- No --> I{R value less than S value?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Advance R pointer]
    I -- No --> K[Advance S pointer]
    H --> G
    J --> G
    K --> G
    G -- All consumed --> L([Output complete])
```

## 4.4 Hash Join (Grace) — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input R] --> B[Apply h1 to join attribute]
    A2[Input S] --> C[Apply h1 to join attribute]
    B --> D[Partition R into M-1 buckets on disk]
    C --> E[Partition S into M-1 buckets on disk]
    D --> F[Read bucket Ri into memory]
    E --> G[Read bucket Si into memory]
    F --> H[Build in-memory hash table on h2 for Ri]
    G --> I[Probe with Si using h2]
    I --> J{Match found?}
    H --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[Output join tuple]
    J -- No --> L[Discard]
    K --> M{All tuples in Si processed?}
    L --> M
    M -- No --> I
    M -- Yes --> N{All buckets processed?}
    N -- No --> F
    N -- Yes --> O([Output complete])
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block-I/O Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    NLJ[NLJ: b_r + n_r x b_s] -->|Brute force| Worst((WORST))
    BNLJ[BNLJ: ceil-br_r_div_M-2_times b_s + b_r] -->|Blockwise| Mid((MID))
    INLJ[INLJ: b_r + n_r x c] -->|Indexed| Mid
    SMJ[SMJ: 2 b_r log b_r + 2 b_s log b_s + b_r + b_s] -->|Sorted| Better((BETTER))
    HJ[HJ: 3 b_r + b_s] -->|Hashed| Best((BEST))
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the Block Nested Loop Join algorithm. Derive its cost formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Block Nested Loop Join (BNLJ) improves upon the basic NLJ by reading the **outer relation in blocks** rather than tuple-by-tuple. Using $M$ buffers, we allocate $M-2$ buffers for outer R and 1 for inner S. For each chunk of $M-2$ blocks of R, we scan the entire inner S, comparing tuples block-by-block. The cost formula is:
>
> $$\text{Cost}_{BNLJ} = \left\lceil \frac{b_r}{M-2} \right\rceil \cdot b_s + b_r$$
>
> This reduces the dominant $n_r \cdot b_s$ term of NLJ to $\lceil b_r/(M-2) \rceil \cdot b_s$, yielding massive savings when $M$ is small but $M-2 > 1$.

*(1 mark for idea, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for the saving explanation)*

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Compare Sort-Merge Join and Hash Join. State one scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | Sort-Merge Join | Hash Join |
> |--------|----------------|-----------|
> | Cost | $2b_r\lceil\log_{M-1}b_r\rceil + 2b_s\lceil\log_{M-1}b_s\rceil + b_r + b_s$ | $3(b_r + b_s)$ |
> | Memory | Less sensitive | Needs $M-1 \geq \sqrt{\min(b_r, b_s)}$ for one-pass |
> | Equi-join only? | **No** — works for range joins | **Yes** — only equi-joins |
> | Pre-sorted input | Skips sort phase | Cannot skip |
> | Preferred when | (a) R, S already sorted, (b) non-equi join, (c) limited memory | (a) Large equi-join, (b) abundant memory, (c) no pre-existing order |

*(1 mark per row of comparison + 1 mark for scenarios)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Sort-Merge Join** algorithm in detail with a suitable diagram. Derive the cost formula.  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two relations **R** and **S** have $b_r = 2000$ blocks, $b_s = 1500$ blocks, and the system has $M = 11$ buffers. Compute the cost of:
- (i) Block Nested Loop Join
- (ii) Sort-Merge Join

State which algorithm the optimizer should choose.  
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Algorithm & Cost Derivation (7 marks)**

> **Algorithm Steps:**
> 1. **Sort Phase:** Sort R on the join attribute using external sort (cost: $2 b_r \lceil \log_{M-1} b_r \rceil$). Similarly sort S (cost: $2 b_s \lceil \log_{M-1} b_s \rceil$).
> 2. **Merge Phase:** Use two pointers — one in sorted R, one in sorted S. Walk through both lists:
>    - If $r.A = s.A$, emit the join tuple; advance the S pointer.
>    - If $r.A < s.A$, advance the R pointer.
>    - If $r.A > s.A$, advance the S pointer.
> 3. Continue until one list is exhausted.
>
> **Cost Derivation:**
>
> $$\text{Cost}_{SMJ} = 2 b_r \lceil \log_{M-1} b_r \rceil + 2 b_s \lceil \log_{M-1} b_s \rceil + (b_r + b_s)$$
>
> The $b_r + b_s$ term in the merge phase represents a single sequential pass over each sorted relation.

*(1 mark for idea, 2 marks for steps, 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for diagram/justification)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Cost Computation (7 marks)**

> **Given:** $b_r = 2000$, $b_s = 1500$, $M = 11$ buffers.
>
> **(i) Block Nested Loop Join (3.5 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Cost}_{BNLJ} &= \left\lceil \frac{b_r}{M-2} \right\rceil \cdot b_s + b_r \\
> &= \left\lceil \frac{2000}{9} \right\rceil \cdot 1500 + 2000 \\
> &= 223 \times 1500 + 2000 \\
> &= 334{,}500 + 2000 \\
> &= 336{,}500 \ \text{block I/Os}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **(ii) Sort-Merge Join (3.5 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \lceil \log_{10} 2000 \rceil &= \lceil 3.30 \rceil = 4 \\
> \lceil \log_{10} 1500 \rceil &= \lceil 3.18 \rceil = 4 \\
> \text{Cost}_{SMJ} &= 2(2000)(4) + 2(1500)(4) + 2000 + 1500 \\
> &= 16{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 3500 \\
> &= 31{,}500 \ \text{block I/Os}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Verdict:** SMJ (**31,500**) ≪ BNLJ (**336,500**).  
> The optimizer should **choose Sort-Merge Join**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the **Hash Join** algorithm. Derive its cost and list **two limitations**.  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A join is to be performed between R and S with $b_r = 5000$, $b_s = 3000$, $M = 102$ buffers, and no index on S. Compare the cost of Hash Join and Block Nested Loop Join. State which is cheaper and why.  
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Hash Join Algorithm (7 marks)**

> **Algorithm Steps:**
> 1. **Partition Phase:** Apply hash function $h_1$ to the join attribute of every tuple. Each tuple is written to one of $M-1$ disk partitions.
> 2. **Probe Phase:** For each partition $i$, read partition $i$ of R into memory, build a hash table using $h_2$. Then scan partition $i$ of S and probe the hash table for matches.
>
> **Cost Derivation:**
>
> $$\text{Cost}_{HJ} = 2 b_r + 2 b_s + b_r + b_s = 3(b_r + b_s)$$
>
> **Limitations:**
> 1. Works **only for equi-joins** (cannot handle range, theta, or non-equality predicates).
> 2. Requires **sufficient memory** to hold one partition of R in memory; otherwise, recursive partitioning is needed (degrades performance).
> 3. Sensitive to **hash skew** (bad hash distribution creates uneven partition sizes).

*(2 marks for steps, 2 marks for derivation, 3 marks for limitations)*

**Part (b) — Cost Comparison (7 marks)**

> **Given:** $b_r = 5000$, $b_s = 3000$, $M = 102$.
>
> **Hash Join Cost (3.5 marks):**
>
> $$\text{Cost}_{HJ} = 3(5000 + 3000) = 3 \times 8000 = 24{,}000 \ \text{block I/Os}$$
>
> **Block Nested Loop Join Cost (3.5 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Cost}_{BNLJ} &= \left\lceil \frac{5000}{100} \right\rceil \cdot 3000 + 5000 \\
> &= 50 \times 3000 + 5000 \\
> &= 150{,}000 + 5000 \\
> &= 155{,}000 \ \text{block I/Os}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Verdict:** Hash Join (**24,000**) is **~6.5× cheaper** than BNLJ (**155,000**). The optimizer should choose **Hash Join**. This is because BNLJ's cost grows as $O(b_r \cdot b_s)$ when $M$ is small relative to $b_r$, whereas Hash Join grows only linearly as $O(b_r + b_s)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong buffer count** — Students frequently use $M$ instead of $M-2$ in the BNLJ denominator. Always subtract 2 (one for inner, one for output) unless the question explicitly states otherwise.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the leading 2 in sort cost** — The formula $2 b_r \lceil \log_{M-1} b_r \rceil$ has a factor of 2 because external sort reads AND writes in each pass. Writing only the term $b_r \lceil \log \rceil$ loses **1 mark** minimum.
>
> 3. **Confusing $n_r$ (tuples) with $b_r$ (blocks)** — In NLJ, the inner is scanned once per *tuple* ($n_r \cdot b_s$), not once per *block* ($b_r \cdot b_s$). Mixing these up is a **fatal** error.
>
> 4. **Skipping the comparison step** — When asked to "compare" algorithms, KTU expects a **table or matrix**, not just narration. Always produce a side-by-side.
>
> 5. **Not justifying the choice** — Ending with "Hash Join is better" without showing the cost numbers invites a 0.5–1 mark penalty for lack of reasoning.
>
> 6. **Wrong ceiling/floor in log** — Always use **ceiling** for the number of sort passes. Forgetting $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ gives a non-integer answer, which is impossible in a physical I/O model.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**
>
> ✅ **Five algorithms** to memorize: NLJ, BNLJ, INLJ, SMJ, HJ.
>
> ✅ **Cost formula** for each — write them down from memory in under 2 minutes.
>
> ✅ **Buffer accounting** — $M-2$ for BNLJ, $M-1$ for Hash partition, $M-1$ for sort runs.
>
> ✅ **Sort cost** has a leading **2** (read + write).
>
> ✅ **Hash Join = $3(b_r + b_s)$** — pure linear cost, no log term.
>
> ✅ **INLJ uses index lookup cost $c$** — typically 3–4 I/Os for a B+ tree.
>
> ✅ **SMJ works for any join type** (including non-equi); **HJ works only for equi-joins**.
>
> ✅ **Pick the smaller relation as the outer** in NLJ/BNLJ/INLJ.
>
> ✅ **Pre-sorted input** skips the sort phase in SMJ (cost $\to b_r + b_s$).
>
> ✅ **Skew** is the nemesis of Hash Join; **memory** is the nemesis of BNLJ.
>
> ✅ **Hybrid Hash Join** is the production-grade upgrade when $M$ can hold one full partition.
>
> ✅ **In KTU problems, always show the substitution step** in derivations — partial marks are awarded for correct substitution even if the final number is miscalculated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Project and Set Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Algorithms for Project and Set Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Project (π)** and **Set Operations** are two fundamental categories of **Relational Algebra operations** that require specialised processing algorithms when relations are stored as disk-resident files. Since main memory cannot hold the entire relation, the DBMS must employ **file scan, sorting, and merging strategies** to evaluate these operations efficiently on external storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> - **Project Operation (π<sub>A1,A2,...,An</sub>(R))** : Extracts specified attributes from relation R while **eliminating duplicate tuples** (because relational algebra returns a *set*, not a multiset/bag).
> - **Set Operations** : Binary operations between two union-compatible relations, namely **UNION (∪), INTERSECTION (∩), SET DIFFERENCE (−), and CARTESIAN PRODUCT (×)**.

### 1.2 Why These Algorithms Matter

When a relation `R` is small enough to fit in main memory, naive algorithms work perfectly. But real-world databases have relations spanning **gigabytes to terabytes**. Hence, we measure algorithms in terms of:

- **Block Accesses** (Disk I/O) — the dominant cost
- **CPU time** — secondary
- **Memory buffer size** (`n_B` buffer blocks available)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Sorting Office

Imagine a **postal sorting office** with a limited sorting table (memory buffer):

| Real-world Object | Database Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Letters arriving in sacks | Disk blocks of a relation |
| Sorting table | Memory buffer pool |
| Final destination bags | Sorted runs / output file |
| Postal workers | CPU executing the algorithm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for PROJECT (π):** "Take only the *name* and *city* columns from the employee register, and **remove duplicate** entries." → A two-step process: (1) extract columns, (2) deduplicate.
>
> **Intuition for SET UNION (R ∪ S):** "Merge two alphabetical customer lists into one, removing customers that appear in both." → Requires **sorting both lists first**, then a single linear scan-merge pass.

### 1.4 Classification of Operations in this Module

| Operation Type | Notation | Tuple-Set Behaviour | Key Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|
| PROJECT | π<sub>A</sub>(R) | Removes duplicates | Deduplication |
| UNION | R ∪ S | Merges + removes duplicates | Requires sort or hash |
| INTERSECTION | R ∩ S | Common tuples | Sort-merge or hash join |
| SET DIFFERENCE | R − S | R tuples not in S | Asymmetric |
| CARTESIAN PRODUCT | R × S | All pairs | Combinatorial blow-up |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 External Sorting (The Backbone of Project & Set Operations)

External sorting is the **prerequisite** for almost every efficient set-based algorithm. The standard technique is **Sort-Merge**, executed in two phases.

#### Phase 1 — Sorted Run Construction (Internal Sort Phase)
1. Read `n_B` blocks of the relation into the buffer.
2. Sort these `n_B` blocks **in main memory** (e.g., quicksort).
3. Write the sorted blocks back to disk as a *sorted run*.
4. Repeat until the entire relation is processed.

$$n_{R_0} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{b_r}{n_B} \right\rceil$$

where:
- $b_r$ = number of blocks in relation R
- $n_B$ = number of buffer blocks
- $n_{R_0}$ = number of *initial sorted runs* produced in Phase 1

#### Phase 2 — Merge Phase
1. Allocate `n_B − 1` buffer blocks to read sorted runs and **1** buffer block for output.
2. Merge the runs using an `n_B`-way merge.
3. Repeat until one final sorted file remains.

$$d_{merge} \;=\; \left\lceil \log_{\,n_B-1}\, n_{R_0} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \log_{\,n_B-1}\, \left\lceil \dfrac{b_r}{n_B} \right\rceil \right\rceil$$

$$b_{transfer} \;=\; 2 \cdot b_r \cdot \left(d_{merge} + 1\right)$$

$$b_{seek} \;\approx\; 2 \cdot b_r \cdot \left(d_{merge} + 1\right) \quad \text{(approximate seek count)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'Why' does this work?** Sorting brings equal/related tuples *physically adjacent* on disk. Once adjacent, operations like duplicate elimination (PROJECT), UNION, and INTERSECTION can be done in **a single linear sweep** — turning an O(N²) problem into O(N log N).

---

### 2.2 Algorithm for PROJECT with Duplicate Elimination (π)

**Approach 1 — Sort-Based Projection**

| Step | Action | Block Cost |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scan R, write projection attributes to temp file T | $b_r + b_{out}$ |
| 2 | Sort T by the projection attributes | External sort cost |
| 3 | Scan sorted T, emit each unique tuple once | $b_{out}$ |

**Approach 2 — Hash-Based Projection**

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Partition: hash projection attributes → separate buckets on disk |
| 2 | For each bucket, fit into memory and eliminate duplicates by hash |

Hash-based projection works in **two passes** without requiring full sort — useful when the projected relation is large.

---

### 2.3 Algorithms for Set Operations (Sort-Merge Based)

**Step 0 (Pre-processing):** Sort both R and S on the **join/composite key** using external sort-merge.

**Step 1 (Merge pass):** Use a synchronized two-way (or n-way) merge scan.

#### UNION (R ∪ S) — Sort-Merge Algorithm Logic

| Condition during merge | Action |
|---|---|
| $r < s$ | Output `r`; advance R |
| $r > s$ | Output `s`; advance S |
| $r = s$ | Output once; advance both |

#### INTERSECTION (R ∩ S) — Sort-Merge Algorithm Logic

| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| $r = s$ | Output `r`; advance both |
| $r < s$ | Discard `r`; advance R |
| $r > s$ | Discard `s`; advance S |

#### SET DIFFERENCE (R − S) — Sort-Merge Algorithm Logic

| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| $r < s$ | Output `r`; advance R |
| $r > s$ | Discard `s`; advance S |
| $r = s$ | Discard both; advance both |

#### CARTESIAN PRODUCT (R × S)

- If R has $b_r$ blocks and S has $b_s$ blocks, the result has up to $b_r \cdot b_s$ blocks.
- Algorithm: nest-loop with the **smaller relation as outer** to minimise buffer pressure.
- No sorting required; cost = $b_r + b_r \cdot b_s$ block transfers (using block nested loop).

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every ESE question on this module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial sorted runs | $n_{R_0} = \lceil b_r \,/\, n_B \rceil$ | Phase 1 output count |
| 2 | Merge passes | $d = \lceil \log_{\,n_B-1}\, n_{R_0} \rceil$ | Phase 2 passes |
| 3 | Total block transfers (external sort) | $2 \cdot b_r \cdot (d + 1)$ | Read + write per pass |
| 4 | Approx. disk seeks (external sort) | $2 \cdot b_r \cdot (d + 1)$ | Per-pass seek estimate |
| 5 | Sort cost for projection (full) | $b_r \cdot (\lceil \log_{\,n_B-1} b_r \rceil + 1) + b_{out}$ | Including projection step |
| 6 | UNION cost (sort-merge) | $2 \cdot b_r \cdot (\lceil \log_{\,n_B-1} b_r \rceil + 1) + 2 \cdot b_s \cdot (\lceil \log_{\,n_B-1} b_s \rceil + 1) + b_r + b_s$ | Sort R + Sort S + Merge |
| 7 | CARTESIAN PRODUCT cost | $b_r \cdot b_s + b_r$ | Nested-loop, S as inner |
| 8 | Result size of R × S | $t_r \cdot t_s$ tuples, $b_r \cdot b_s$ blocks | Combinatorial |
| 9 | Result size of π<sub>A</sub>(R) | $\le t_r$ tuples, $\le b_r$ blocks | At most original |

> **Notation used:** $b_r, b_s$ = blocks of R, S · $t_r, t_s$ = tuples of R, S · $n_B$ = buffer blocks · $b_{out}$ = output blocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These algorithms form the **execution engine** of every commercial RDBMS (Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL) for queries involving `DISTINCT`, `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, `EXCEPT`, and `CROSS JOIN` clauses — a concept called *query compilation* and *iterator model* in database internals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — External Sort Cost

Let us derive the **total block transfer cost** of external sort-merge step-by-step.

**Given:**
- $b_r$ = number of blocks in relation R
- $n_B$ = number of available buffer blocks
- Phase 1: each pass reads and writes the entire relation.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block transfers (Phase 1)} &= b_r \;\text{(read)} + b_r \;\text{(write)} \\
&= 2 \cdot b_r
\end{aligned}
$$

After Phase 1, the number of sorted runs is:

$$
n_{R_0} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{b_r}{n_B} \right\rceil
$$

Each merge pass in Phase 2 reads the entire relation once and writes it once, contributing `2 · b_r` block transfers.

The number of merge passes required to reduce $n_{R_0}$ runs to 1 is:

$$
d \;=\; \left\lceil \log_{\,n_B-1} n_{R_0} \right\rceil
$$

*Conversion logic:* we use `n_B − 1` (not `n_B`) because **one** buffer is reserved for the merge output.

The total merge-phase block transfers are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block transfers (Phase 2)} &= d \cdot (b_r + b_r) \\
&= 2 \cdot b_r \cdot d
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing both phases:

$$
\boxed{\,T_{sort} \;=\; 2 \cdot b_r \cdot (d + 1) \;=\; 2 \cdot b_r \cdot \left( \left\lceil \log_{\,n_B-1} \left\lceil \dfrac{b_r}{n_B} \right\rceil \right\rceil + 1 \right)\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Sort Cost

**Problem:** Sort a relation R with $b_r = 1000$ blocks using $n_B = 11$ buffer blocks.

**Step 1 — Compute initial runs:**

$$
n_{R_0} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{1000}{11} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil 90.909\ldots \right\rceil \;=\; 91
$$

**Step 2 — Compute merge passes:**

$$
d \;=\; \left\lceil \log_{\,10}\, 91 \right\rceil
$$

Since $10^1 = 10$ and $10^2 = 100$:

$$
d \;=\; \left\lceil \log_{\,10}\, 91 \right\rceil \;=\; 2
$$

**Step 3 — Total block transfers:**

$$
T_{sort} \;=\; 2 \cdot 1000 \cdot (2 + 1) \;=\; 2 \cdot 1000 \cdot 3 \;=\; 6000 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of I/O operations (rough seek estimate):**

Each pass involves approximately $2 \cdot b_r$ seek operations, so:

$$
S_{sort} \;\approx\; 2 \cdot 1000 \cdot (2 + 1) \;=\; 6000 \text{ seeks}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Sorting a 1000-block relation takes **3 logical passes** (1 sort + 2 merge), with **6000 block transfers** in total. Notice the logarithmic effect — doubling the buffer size from 11 to 21 would have reduced `d` to 2, saving one pass.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Set Operation Cost Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **revision goldmine** for KTU ESE. Memorise the cost formulas and the conditions under which sorting is needed.

| Operation | Sorting Required? | Cost (Block Transfers) | Duplicate Handling |
|---|---|---|---|
| π<sub>A</sub>(R) (Sort-based) | Yes, on A | $2 \cdot b_r \cdot (\lceil \log_{\,n_B-1} b_r \rceil + 1) + b_r + b_{out}$ | Removes duplicates after sort |
| π<sub>A</sub>(R) (Hash-based) | No | $\approx 2 \cdot b_r + b_r + b_{out}$ | Removes duplicates via hashing |
| UNION (∪) | Yes, on key of R & S | $T_{sort}(R) + T_{sort}(S) + b_r + b_s + b_{out}$ | Yes, asymmetric merge |
| INTERSECTION (∩) | Yes | $T_{sort}(R) + T_{sort}(S) + b_r + b_s + b_{out}$ | Common tuples only |
| SET DIFFERENCE (−) | Yes | $T_{sort}(R) + T_{sort}(S) + b_r + b_s + b_{out}$ | Asymmetric |
| CARTESIAN PRODUCT (×) | No | $b_r \cdot b_s + b_r$ (nested loop) | All pairs kept |

---

### 3.4 Detailed Algorithm Trace — Sort-Merge UNION

**Setup:** Two relations R and S are already sorted on the join key.

**Pseudocode (standard textbook style, not Python):**

```
UNION-SORTMERGE(R, S):
    Open R and S for reading
    Open output file T for writing
    pr ← read first tuple of R
    ps ← read first tuple of S
    WHILE pr ≠ EOF AND ps ≠ EOF DO
        IF pr < ps THEN
            WRITE pr to T
            pr ← NEXT(R)
        ELSE IF pr > ps THEN
            WRITE ps to T
            ps ← NEXT(S)
        ELSE    // pr = ps
            WRITE pr to T        // write once
            pr ← NEXT(R)
            ps ← NEXT(S)
    WHILE pr ≠ EOF DO
        WRITE pr to T
        pr ← NEXT(R)
    WHILE ps ≠ EOF DO
        WRITE ps to T
        ps ← NEXT(S)
```

**Key invariant:** The output `T` remains sorted and duplicate-free, generated in **one linear pass** after sorting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** When set operations are written as SQL `UNION` (set semantics) the DBMS optimises by choosing either sort-merge or hash-based deduplication based on the estimated size of intermediate results — this decision is made by the **query optimiser** using cost-based statistics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 External Sort-Merge — Two-Phase Flow Architecture

<!-- Mermaid safeguards: alphanumeric prefixed IDs, no reserved words, no markdown inside labels. -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([Disk: Relation R - br blocks]):::disk --> B1[Read nB blocks into buffer]:::io
    B1 --> C1[Internal Sort in main memory]:::cpu
    C1 --> D1[Write Sorted Run to Disk]:::io
    D1 --> E1{More blocks in R?}:::decision
    E1 -- Yes --> B1
    E1 -- No --> F1[(Sorted Runs: nR0 files on disk)]:::disk

    F1 --> G1[Phase 2: nB-1 way Merge Pass]:::merge
    G1 --> H1{More than 1 run remaining?}:::decision
    H1 -- Yes --> G1
    H1 -- No --> I1([Final Sorted File on Disk]):::disk

```

```mermaid

    A2([Disk: Relation R]):::disk --> B2[Step 1: Scan R and Project Attributes]:::io
    B2 --> C2[(Temp File T with duplicates)]:::disk
    C2 --> D2[Step 2: External Sort on T]:::cpu
    D2 --> E2[Step 3: Linear scan and emit unique tuples]:::io

```

### 4.3 Set Operations — Sort-Merge Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3([Sorted R, Sorted S]):::disk --> B3{Compare current tuple pr vs ps}:::decision
    B3 -- pr < ps --> C3[Emit pr, advance R]:::emit
    B3 -- pr > ps --> D3[Emit ps, advance S]:::emit
    B3 -- pr equals ps --> E3[Emit once, advance both R and S]:::emit
    B3 -- UNION ONLY --> F3[Emit both pr and ps]:::emit
    B3 -- INTERSECTION ONLY --> G3[Emit pr equals ps only]:::emit
    B3 -- DIFFERENCE ONLY --> H3[Emit pr minus ps only]:::emit

    E3 --> I3
    F3 --> I3
    G3 --> I3
    H3 --> I3
```

```mermaid
        P2A[Input Run 1]:::run
        P2B[Input Run 2]:::run
        P2C[Input Run nB-1]:::run
        P2A --> P2M[Merging Comparator]
        P2B --> P2M
        P2C --> P2M
        P2M --> P2D[Output Buffer] --> P2E[(Final Sorted File)]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define external sorting. Why is it required for evaluating set operations in databases?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** External sorting is the technique of sorting a relation that is too large to fit entirely in main memory, by using a combination of internal sorting and disk-based merging. It is typically implemented using the **sort-merge** strategy in two phases: (i) creating initial sorted runs from buffer-sized chunks, and (ii) iteratively merging the runs until one final sorted file remains.

- **Why required (1.5 marks):** Set operations such as UNION, INTERSECTION, and SET DIFFERENCE require tuples of two relations to be compared based on equality. Random disk access is too expensive; sorting brings *equal tuples adjacent* on disk, so a single linear scan can resolve the operation. Without external sorting, these operations would require quadratic comparisons across pages.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**List the four set operations in relational algebra. State the conditions under which each is applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four set operations are:

1. **UNION (R ∪ S)** — Both R and S must be **union-compatible** (same number of attributes with matching domains). Returns tuples present in either relation, with duplicates removed.
2. **INTERSECTION (R ∩ S)** — Requires union-compatibility. Returns tuples present in *both* R and S.
3. **SET DIFFERENCE (R − S)** — Requires union-compatibility. Returns tuples in R that are *not* in S. It is **not commutative**.
4. **CARTESIAN PRODUCT (R × S)** — Has **no compatibility restriction**; concatenates every tuple of R with every tuple of S. Result schema is the union of R's and S's attributes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick:** UNION, INTERSECTION, DIFFERENCE need "set-theoretic" union-compatibility. CARTESIAN PRODUCT is the only one that *combines* schemas.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Question A OR Question B)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **External Sort-Merge algorithm** in detail, with the two phases. Derive the formula for the number of **block transfers** required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A relation R has $b_r = 5000$ blocks, and the buffer pool has $n_B = 26$ blocks. Compute: (i) the number of initial sorted runs, (ii) the number of merge passes, and (iii) the total block transfer cost. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Explanation of External Sort-Merge Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Phase 1 — Sorted Run Construction (3.5 marks):**

1. The DBMS reads `n_B` blocks of relation R at a time into the buffer.
2. These blocks are sorted internally (e.g., using quicksort or replacement selection) in main memory.
3. The sorted blocks are written back to disk as one *sorted run*.
4. This is repeated until all `b_r` blocks are processed.
5. The number of sorted runs created is:

$$n_{R_0} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{b_r}{n_B} \right\rceil$$

**Phase 2 — Merging (3.5 marks):**

1. Allocate `n_B − 1` buffer blocks to read input runs and reserve 1 block for the merge output.
2. Perform an `(n_B − 1)`-way merge of sorted runs by repeatedly selecting the smallest tuple among the heads of input buffers.
3. After one pass, the number of runs reduces from $n_{R_i}$ to $\lceil n_{R_i} / (n_B - 1) \rceil$.
4. Repeat until only **one** sorted run remains.

The total number of block transfers = $2 \cdot b_r$ for Phase 1 + $2 \cdot b_r \cdot d$ for Phase 2, where $d$ is the number of merge passes.

**Derivation (already shown in §3.1):**

$$
T_{sort} \;=\; 2 \cdot b_r \cdot (d + 1) \;=\; 2 \cdot b_r \cdot \left( \left\lceil \log_{\,n_B-1} \left\lceil \dfrac{b_r}{n_B} \right\rceil \right\rceil + 1 \right)
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $b_r = 5000$ blocks, $n_B = 26$ buffer blocks.

**(i) Initial sorted runs (2 marks):**

$$
n_{R_0} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{5000}{26} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil 192.307\ldots \right\rceil \;=\; 193
$$

**(ii) Number of merge passes (2 marks):**

$$
d \;=\; \left\lceil \log_{\,25}\, 193 \right\rceil
$$

Since $25^1 = 25$ and $25^2 = 625$, we have $d = 2$ passes.

**(iii) Total block transfers (3 marks):**

$$
T_{sort} \;=\; 2 \cdot 5000 \cdot (2 + 1) \;=\; 30000 \text{ block transfers}
$$

Approximately $30000$ disk seeks as well (rough estimate).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **sort-merge algorithms** for the operations **UNION, INTERSECTION, and SET DIFFERENCE**, with appropriate merge-decision logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider two relations R and S with $b_r = 1000$ and $b_s = 500$ blocks, and $n_B = 11$ buffer blocks. Calculate the **total block-transfer cost** of computing R ∪ S using the sort-merge approach. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Sort-Merge Algorithms for Set Operations (7 marks):**

**Pre-processing (2 marks):**
- Both R and S are sorted on the join key using external sort-merge. Total sort cost = $T_{sort}(R) + T_{sort}(S)$.
- This brings tuples with the same key adjacent, allowing a single linear merge pass to evaluate the set operation.

**UNION Logic (1.5 marks):**
- If `r < s` → output `r`; advance R.
- If `r > s` → output `s`; advance S.
- If `r = s` → output the tuple **once**; advance both.

**INTERSECTION Logic (1.5 marks):**
- If `r = s` → output the tuple; advance both.
- If `r < s` → discard `r`; advance R.
- If `r > s` → discard `s`; advance S.

**SET DIFFERENCE Logic (2 marks):**
- If `r < s` → output `r`; advance R.
- If `r > s` → discard `s`; advance S.
- If `r = s` → discard both; advance both.
- After one relation is exhausted, output remaining tuples of R (or discard remaining of S).

> [!NOTE]
> The same framework works because **sorting** transforms random comparisons into a **streamed, sequential** operation — the algorithmic elegance of external sorting.

---

**(b) Cost Calculation for R ∪ S (7 marks):**

Given: $b_r = 1000$, $b_s = 500$, $n_B = 11$.

**Step 1 — Sort cost of R (2 marks):**

$$
n_{R_0} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{1000}{11} \right\rceil \;=\; 91
$$

$$
d_R \;=\; \left\lceil \log_{\,10}\, 91 \right\rceil \;=\; 2
$$

$$
T_{sort}(R) \;=\; 2 \cdot 1000 \cdot (2 + 1) \;=\; 6000 \text{ block transfers}
$$

**Step 2 — Sort cost of S (2 marks):**

$$
n_{S_0} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{500}{11} \right\rceil \;=\; 46
$$

$$
d_S \;=\; \left\lceil \log_{\,10}\, 46 \right\rceil \;=\; 2
$$

$$
T_{sort}(S) \;=\; 2 \cdot 500 \cdot (2 + 1) \;=\; 3000 \text{ block transfers}
$$

**Step 3 — Merge pass cost (2 marks):**
The final merge pass reads sorted R and S once and writes the result:

$$
T_{merge} \;=\; b_r + b_s + b_{out} \;\approx\; 1000 + 500 + b_{out}
$$

(For simplicity, $b_{out}$ is small compared to sort cost.)

**Step 4 — Total (1 mark):**

$$
T_{total} \;\approx\; 6000 + 3000 + 1000 + 500 \;\approx\; 10500 \text{ block transfers}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones**
> 1. **Forgetting the '−1'** in `n_B − 1` during merge cost — losing up to 1–2 marks per sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing $b_r$ with $t_r$** in CARTESIAN PRODUCT cost — remember the result size in *blocks* is $b_r \cdot b_s$, but in *tuples* is $t_r \cdot t_s$.
> 3. **Missing duplicate-elimination step** in PROJECT and UNION — these operations *return sets*, not bags.
> 4. **Wrong direction** in SET DIFFERENCE — the operation is asymmetric: R − S ≠ S − R.
> 5. **Skipping the cost-of-Phase-1 contribution** in external sort — the formula `2·b_r·(d+1)` includes the initial run formation; writing only `2·b_r·d` is incomplete.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Recall List — Memorise Before Exam**

- **External sorting** is the foundation of efficient PROJECT and set-operation algorithms.
- **Phase 1** produces $n_{R_0} = \lceil b_r / n_B \rceil$ sorted runs.
- **Phase 2** performs $(n_B − 1)$-way merge; passes = $\lceil \log_{n_B-1} n_{R_0} \rceil$.
- **Total block transfers** for external sort = $2 \cdot b_r \cdot (d + 1)$.
- **PROJECT (π)** requires duplicate elimination → either sort-based or hash-based.
- **UNION, INTERSECTION, DIFFERENCE** all need sorting of both operands for sort-merge approach.
- **UNION:** output on equality *once*; **INTERSECTION:** output on equality only; **DIFFERENCE:** output when R-tuple has no match in S.
- **CARTESIAN PRODUCT** has the highest cost — $b_r \cdot b_s$ blocks — and does **not** require sorting.
- **Sort-merge is the classic approach**, but **hash-based** approaches are competitive when the relation is large and dedup is needed.
- **Buffer size $n_B$** dramatically affects the number of merge passes — log effect.
- **Set operations** demand *union-compatibility*; **CARTESIAN PRODUCT** does not.
- **Cost = (sort R) + (sort S) + (merge pass)** is the standard template for any binary set operation in sort-merge style.
- **Engineering reality:** Modern query optimisers choose between *sort-merge* and *hash-based* algorithms based on memory availability and relation size estimates — but the cost formulas remain the same.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Expressions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Advanced Database Systems, **Evaluation of Expressions** refers to the systematic process by which the Database Management System (DBMS) **executes a query** represented as a tree of relational algebra operations (Select, Project, Join, Union, etc.) to produce the required output relation. It encompasses the algorithms, strategies, and execution models used to translate a logical query plan into a physical plan that can be run on actual disk-resident data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> Expression evaluation is the *physical execution* phase of query processing. It takes an **expression tree** (where leaves are base relations and internal nodes are relational operators) and produces a concrete strategy for materializing the result, using algorithms like nested-loop join, hash join, sort-merge join, and evaluation mechanisms like *materialization* or *pipelining*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are preparing a **fruit salad** for a party (this is your "query result"). The recipe (the **expression tree**) tells you:
1. Take 2 mangoes and 3 apples (these are **base relations**).
2. Chop them (this is a **Selection / Project** operation).
3. Mix them in a bowl (this is a **Join / Union** operation).
4. Add sugar and stir (this is an **Aggregation**).

Now, the **Evaluation of Expressions** is the *kitchen strategy*:
- Do you **pre-chop all mangoes first, put them in a bowl, then pre-chop all apples, then mix**? — This is **Materialization** (Eager evaluation).
- Or do you **chop each mango and immediately toss it into the bowl, then chop each apple straight into the bowl**? — This is **Pipelining** (Lazy / Iterative evaluation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** Because the choice of evaluation strategy drastically changes the **I/O cost**, **CPU cost**, and **memory usage** of a query. A well-optimized evaluation plan can be 1000x faster than a naive one on large datasets.

## 1.3 Core Components of Expression Evaluation

| Component | Role in Evaluation |
|-----------|---------------------|
| **Expression Tree** | Logical plan showing operator dependencies |
| **Physical Operators** | Concrete algorithms (e.g., Sort-Merge Join) |
| **Evaluation Mechanism** | Materialization vs. Pipelining |
| **Access Methods** | How data is fetched (Index Scan, Full Scan) |
| **I/O & Buffer Management** | Disk-to-memory data movement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically expects students to differentiate between **materialized evaluation** and **pipelined evaluation**, and to understand **iterator-based evaluation** as the unifying execution model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Major Evaluation Strategies

### Strategy 1: Materialization (Eager Evaluation)

The DBMS evaluates **one operation at a time, starting from the leaves**. The intermediate result of each operation is **fully materialized (written back to disk or held in memory)** before being passed to the parent operation.

**How it works (Step-by-Step):**
1. Start at the lowest level of the expression tree.
2. Compute the result of the lowest operator by scanning its input relation(s).
3. **Store the entire intermediate relation** on disk (or in a buffer pool).
4. Pass this materialized relation up to the parent operator.
5. Repeat until the root is evaluated.

**Advantages:**
- Simple to implement and understand.
- Allows reuse of materialized results.
- Easy to swap with on-disk operators.

**Disadvantages:**
- Very high I/O cost if intermediate results are large.
- Repeated scans of the same intermediate result multiply cost.
- No parallelism between operators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Fact:** Materialization is the default strategy in many **early-generation DBMS** (System R, Ingres) and remains the basis of many **distributed query engines**.

### Strategy 2: Pipelining (Lazy / Stream-Based Evaluation)

Operators **do not wait** for their inputs to be fully computed. Instead, they pass tuples **one at a time (or in small batches)** to the parent operator as soon as they are produced. This creates a **stream (iterator)** flowing up the tree.

**Two sub-types of Pipelining:**
- **Demand-driven (Lazy / Pull-based):** The parent operator *pulls* the next tuple from the child using a `getNext()` interface.
- **Producer-driven (Eager / Push-based):** The child operator *pushes* tuples to the parent as soon as they are produced, often via buffering or message queues.

**Advantages:**
- Drastically reduces I/O — no intermediate result is written to disk.
- Enables intra-query parallelism.
- Lower memory footprint (operates on tuples, not relations).

**Disadvantages:**
- Harder to implement correctly (state management).
- Blocking operators (e.g., Sort, Hash-Aggregate) **cannot fully pipeline** — they must consume the entire input first.
- Cannot reuse partial streams.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Pipelining is the modern standard in **Volcano-style** and **vectorized** execution engines (used in PostgreSQL, Oracle Exadata, DuckDB).

### Strategy 3: Iterator-Based (Volcano) Model — The Unifying Framework

This is the **standard industry model** (introduced in the Volcano database system) that unifies both materialization and pipelining.

Every physical operator implements a **3-method interface**:
1. `open()` — Initialize the operator (allocate buffers, open child iterators, etc.).
2. `getNext()` — Produce and return the **next output tuple** (or a signal for EOF).
3. `close()` — Clean up resources.

**The execution proceeds as:**
- The root calls `open()` recursively down the tree.
- The root repeatedly calls `getNext()`, which recursively pulls tuples from children.
- Each `getNext()` call returns **one tuple** (or a vector/batch in vectorized variants).
- When the root signals completion, `close()` propagates back down.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In iterator model, **demand-driven pipelining** is natural because each `getNext()` call from the parent forces the child to compute only enough to produce one tuple.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Patterns for Expression Evaluation

### 2.2.1 Selection (σ)

```
Algorithm: Linear Scan Selection
Input: Relation R, predicate p
Output: Tuples of R satisfying p

1. for each tuple t in R do
2.     if p(t) is true then
3.         output t
4.     end if
5. end for
```

**Complexity:** O(N) where N = number of tuples in R.
**Optimization:** If an index exists on the predicate attribute, replace linear scan with **index scan** — complexity drops to O(log N + k) where k = number of matching tuples.

### 2.2.2 Projection (π)

```
Algorithm: Projection with Duplicate Elimination
Input: Relation R, attribute set A
Output: Distinct tuples projected on A

1. for each tuple t in R do
2.     t' = t restricted to attributes A
3.     if t' is not in result_set then
4.         add t' to result_set
5.     end if
6. end for
7. return result_set
```

**Duplicate elimination** is expensive — naive is O(N²). Real systems use **Hashing (O(N))** or **Sort-based (O(N log N))**.

### 2.2.3 Join Algorithms (Most KTU-Favorite Topic)

**Nested-Loop Join (NLJ):**
```
for each tuple r in R:
    for each tuple s in S:
        if r.A == s.B:
            output (r, s)
```
Cost: O(N_R × N_S) I/Os.

**Block Nested-Loop Join (BNLJ):** Loads a *block* of R into memory. Cost reduces to O((N_R / B) × N_S + N_R) where B = block size.

**Sort-Merge Join:** Sort both R and S on join key, then merge. Cost: O(N_R log N_R + N_S log N_S + N_R + N_S) I/Os.

**Hash Join:** Build a hash table on the smaller relation on the join key, then probe with the larger. Cost: O(N_R + N_S) I/Os (best case).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Use |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| $N_R$ | Number of pages of relation R | I/O cost computation |
| $T_R$ | Number of tuples in R | Result-size estimation |
| $B$ | Number of pages in buffer pool | Memory-bound calculations |
| $V(A, R)$ | Number of distinct values of attribute A in R | Selectivity estimation |
| $\sigma$ | Selection operation | $\sigma_{p}(R)$ |
| $\pi$ | Projection operation | $\pi_{A,B}(R)$ |
| $\bowtie$ | Natural join / Theta join | $R \bowtie_{A=B} S$ |
| $\rho$ | Rename operation | $\rho_{X}(R)$ |
| $C(R)$ | Cost of evaluating R | Page I/Os required |

### Key Cost Formulas

**Linear Scan:** $C = N_R$

**Selection with equality predicate on key:** $C = \log_2(N_R) + 1$ (via B+ tree index)

**Selection with range query on B+ tree:** $C = \log_2(N_R) + \frac{\sigma \cdot N_R}{V(A, R)}$ (log search + leaf scan)

**Selection factor / Selectivity ($sf$):**
$$sf = \frac{\text{Number of result tuples}}{\text{Number of input tuples}}$$

**Number of result tuples from selection:**
$$T_{\text{result}} = sf \cdot T_R$$

**Cartesian Product Size:**
$$T_{R \times S} = T_R \cdot T_S$$

**Join Result Size (rough):**
$$T_{R \bowtie S} \approx \frac{T_R \cdot T_S}{\max(V(A,R), V(B,S))}$$

**Nested-Loop Join Cost (worst case):**
$$C_{\text{NLJ}} = N_R + T_R \cdot N_S$$

**Block Nested-Loop Join Cost:**
$$C_{\text{BNLJ}} = N_R + \left\lceil \frac{N_R}{B - 2} \right\rceil \cdot N_S$$

**Sort-Merge Join Cost (assuming sorted):**
$$C_{\text{SMJ}} = N_R + N_S$$

**Hash Join Cost (assuming R fits in memory):**
$$C_{\text{HJ}} = 3 \cdot (N_R + N_S)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These cost formulas are the bedrock of the **Query Optimizer's** cost-based decision making. Every commercial DBMS (PostgreSQL's planner, Oracle's CBO, MySQL's Optimizer) uses variants of these to choose between physical operators. Mastering them is essential for careers in **Data Engineering, Database Administration, and Backend Systems**.

## 2.4 Why and How: The Underlying Logic

| Question | Answer |
|----------|--------|
| Why materialize? | To decouple operators and enable simple, predictable execution. |
| Why pipeline? | To avoid the disk I/O bottleneck on large intermediate results. |
| Why use iterators? | To unify both approaches in one clean API. |
| How to handle blocking operators? | Use **double-buffering** or **pipelined parallelism**. |
| How does the optimizer fit in? | It picks the *cheapest* combination of physical operators and evaluation strategy. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Cost of a Materialized vs. Pipelined Evaluation

Consider a simple expression tree with three operators: $\sigma$, $\bowtie$, and $\pi$.

**The Expression:**
$$E = \pi_{A, C}(\sigma_{B > 100}(R \bowtie S))$$

Suppose:
- $N_R = 1000$ pages, $N_S = 500$ pages.
- $sf(\sigma) = 0.1$ (10% of tuples pass the predicate).
- Projection removes 50% of attributes but no duplicate elimination needed.

### Step 1: Materialized Evaluation Cost (Naive)

Each intermediate result is **fully written to disk** before being read by the parent.

**Step 1.1: Cost of the Join $R \bowtie S$ (Hash Join).**
$$C_1 = 3 \cdot (N_R + N_S) = 3 \cdot (1000 + 500) = 4500 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Step 1.2: Size of the Join Result.**
$$T_{\bowtie} = \frac{T_R \cdot T_S}{\max(V(\text{join key}, R), V(\text{join key}, S))}$$

Assume 1 tuple/page for simplicity:
$$T_{\bowtie} = \frac{1000 \cdot 500}{\max(100, 200)} = \frac{500000}{200} = 2500 \text{ tuples} = 2500 \text{ pages}$$

**Step 1.3: Cost of Selection $\sigma_{B > 100}$.**
Linear scan over the join result:
$$C_2 = N_{\bowtie} = 2500 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Step 1.4: Size after Selection.**
$$T_{\sigma} = sf \cdot T_{\bowtie} = 0.1 \cdot 2500 = 250 \text{ tuples} = 250 \text{ pages}$$

**Step 1.5: Cost of Projection $\pi_{A, C}$.**
Linear scan:
$$C_3 = N_{\sigma} = 250 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Total Materialized Cost:**
$$C_{\text{materialized}} = C_1 + C_2 + C_3 = 4500 + 2500 + 250 = 7250 \text{ I/Os}$$

### Step 2: Pipelined Evaluation Cost (With Iterator Model)

The selection and projection **do not materialize** the join result. Tuples flow directly.

**Step 2.1: Cost of the Join (once, streamed).**
$$C_1 = 4500 \text{ I/Os (same as before)}$$

**Step 2.2: Selection consumes tuples on-the-fly.**
No additional disk I/O — operates in main memory buffer:
$$C_2 = 0 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Step 2.3: Projection consumes tuples on-the-fly.**
$$C_3 = 0 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Total Pipelined Cost:**
$$C_{\text{pipelined}} = 4500 \text{ I/Os}$$

### Step 3: Comparative Analysis

$$\text{Savings} = C_{\text{materialized}} - C_{\text{pipelined}} = 7250 - 4500 = 2750 \text{ I/Os}$$

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{C_{\text{materialized}}}{C_{\text{pipelined}}} = \frac{7250}{4500} \approx 1.61 \times$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Pipelining saved **38% of the I/O cost** in this case. On real workloads with multi-gigabyte intermediate results, savings of 5x–100x are common.

### Step 4: When Pipelining is Blocked

If the join is replaced with a **Sort-Merge Join**, the sort operation is **blocking** — it must consume the entire input before producing output. In this case:

- The sort step must materialize its input.
- Only *after* the sort completes can the merge produce tuples for the parent.

**Modified Cost with Sort-Merge Join:**
- Sort R: $C_{\text{sort}, R} = 2 \cdot N_R \cdot \lceil \log_B(N_R) \rceil$
- Sort S: $C_{\text{sort}, S} = 2 \cdot N_S \cdot \lceil \log_B(N_S) \rceil$
- Merge: $C_{\text{merge}} = N_R + N_S$

For B = 10, R = 1000, S = 500:
- $C_{\text{sort}, R} = 2 \cdot 1000 \cdot \lceil \log_{10}(1000) \rceil = 2 \cdot 1000 \cdot 3 = 6000$
- $C_{\text{sort}, S} = 2 \cdot 500 \cdot \lceil \log_{10}(500) \rceil = 2 \cdot 500 \cdot 3 = 3000$
- $C_{\text{merge}} = 1500$

Total = $6000 + 3000 + 1500 = 10500$ I/Os (excluding the rest, which are pipelined).

This illustrates a **key KTU insight**: blocking operators force partial materialization regardless of the overall strategy.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Best Evaluation Strategy | Reason |
|----------|--------------------------|--------|
| Small intermediate result | Materialization | Disk I/O cost is negligible; simpler to implement |
| Large intermediate result | Pipelining | Avoids expensive disk writes |
| One blocking operator present | Materialize below, pipeline above | Forced boundary |
| Repeated sub-expressions | Materialization with caching | Reuse computed result |
| Real-time / streaming query | Push-based pipelining | Lowest latency |
| Batch analytics | Vectorized pipelining | Best CPU cache utilization |
| Distributed query | Materialization + parallel pipelining | Network I/O dominates |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Expression Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SQL Query String] --> B[Parser and Query Rewriter]
    B --> C[Logical Expression Tree]
    C --> D[Query Optimizer with Cost Model]
    D --> E[Physical Plan with Iterator Tree]
    E --> F[Execution Engine]
    F --> G[Operator 1: Selection]
    F --> H[Operator 2: Join]
    F --> I[Operator 3: Projection]
    G --> J[Buffer Pool Manager]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Disk and Memory Storage]
    F --> L[Final Result Relation]
```

## 4.2 Iterator-Based Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Operator open] --> B[Child open Recursive]
    B --> C[Child open Recursive]
    C --> D[Leaf Scan First Tuple]
    D --> E[Return Tuple Up the Tree]
    E --> F[Parent getNext Pulls Next]
    F --> G{More Tuples}
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute Next Tuple]
    H --> E
    G -- No --> I[close Propagates Down]
    I --> J[Release Buffers and Resources]
```

## 4.3 Materialization vs Pipelining Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAT[Materialized Evaluation]
        M1[Leaf Relation R] --> M2[Compute Result X]
        M2 --> M3[Write X to Disk]
        M3 --> M4[Read X into Memory]
        M4 --> M5[Compute Result Y]
    end

    subgraph PIP[Pipelined Evaluation]
        P1[Leaf Relation R] --> P2[Compute Tuple 1]
        P2 --> P3[Pass Tuple 1 to Parent]
        P3 --> P4[Compute Tuple 2]
        P4 --> P3
    end
```

## 4.4 Join Algorithm Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Join Evaluation] --> B{Is the smaller relation fully memory resident?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Hash Join with Build on Smaller Relation]
    B -- No --> D{Are inputs already sorted on join key?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Sort Merge Join]
    D -- No --> F{Is the index available on inner relation join key?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Index Nested Loop Join]
    F -- No --> H[Use Block Nested Loop Join as Fallback]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between materialized evaluation and pipelined evaluation of relational algebra expressions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Materialized Evaluation | Pipelined Evaluation |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **Execution** | One operator at a time, bottom-up | Multiple operators execute concurrently |
| **Intermediate Result** | Fully written to disk before next step | Streams tuples directly to parent |
| **I/O Cost** | High — repeated disk writes and reads | Low — tuples flow in memory |
| **Memory Use** | Higher (full intermediate) | Lower (operates on tuples/batches) |
| **Latency** | Higher | Lower |
| **Parallelism** | Limited | Supports intra-query parallelism |
| **Blocking Operators** | Easy to handle | Harder — blocking ops force materialization |

> **Marking Guide (3 marks):**
> - 1 mark: Definition of each strategy.
> - 1 mark: Clear contrast (at least 3 differences).
> - 1 mark: Mention of I/O cost implication.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the iterator model of expression evaluation with its three core methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **iterator model** (also called the **Volcano model**) is a unified execution framework where every physical operator implements a common interface consisting of three methods:

1. **`open()`** — Initializes the operator. It allocates internal buffers, opens child iterators, and prepares any necessary data structures (e.g., opening an index, starting a scan).

2. **`getNext()`** — Returns the **next output tuple** of the operator. Internally, it calls `getNext()` on its child operators to fetch required input tuples. If the operator has no more tuples, it returns a special **EOF** signal. In **vectorized** execution, this returns a *batch* of tuples.

3. **`close()`** — Performs cleanup: releases buffers, closes child iterators, and deallocates resources.

**Demand-Driven Pipelining:** The execution is initiated by the **root operator** calling `getNext()` repeatedly. Each call cascades down the tree, causing children to compute only as much as needed to satisfy the parent's request. This naturally enables **pipelining** because no operator produces a tuple before it is asked for.

> **Marking Guide (3 marks):**
> - 1 mark: Naming the 3 methods with one-line each.
> - 1 mark: Explaining `getNext()` driving the pipeline.
> - 1 mark: Mention of demand-driven nature.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the process of **query evaluation** in a DBMS with a neat diagram of the **expression tree**. Describe the role of the **query optimizer** in selecting the best evaluation plan. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following relations:
- $R(A, B, C)$ with $N_R = 1000$ pages, $T_R = 10000$ tuples, $V(A, R) = 100$.
- $S(C, D, E)$ with $N_S = 500$ pages, $T_S = 5000$ tuples, $V(C, S) = 50$.

Evaluate the cost of the expression $\pi_{A, E}(\sigma_{B > 50}(R \bowtie_{R.C = S.C} S))$ using:
1. **Materialized evaluation** with Hash Join.
2. **Pipelined evaluation** with Hash Join.

Assume buffer pool has $B = 10$ pages, $sf(\sigma_{B > 50}) = 0.1$, and projection does not require duplicate elimination. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (Question A)

**Part (a) - Query Evaluation Process (7 marks):**

The DBMS processes a query through the following pipeline:

1. **Parsing and Translation:** The SQL query is parsed, syntactically validated, and translated into an **internal representation** (usually a relational algebra expression tree). Syntax errors are reported at this stage.

2. **Query Rewriting (Logical Optimization):** The expression tree is simplified using **equivalence rules** of relational algebra. For example, $\sigma_{p1}(\sigma_{p2}(R)) \equiv \sigma_{p1 \land p2}(R)$ and selections are **pushed down** the tree as much as possible to reduce intermediate result sizes.

3. **Physical Plan Generation:** The **Query Optimizer** converts the logical tree into a **physical plan** by choosing concrete algorithms for each logical operator (e.g., Join → Hash Join, NLJ, or Sort-Merge Join; Selection → Index Scan or Full Scan).

4. **Cost Estimation:** For each candidate plan, the optimizer estimates the **I/O cost, CPU cost, and memory cost** using statistics from the system catalog (relation sizes, distinct value counts, index heights, etc.).

5. **Plan Selection:** The optimizer selects the plan with the **lowest estimated cost** and passes it to the execution engine.

6. **Execution:** The **Execution Engine** runs the physical plan using the **iterator model**, applying the chosen evaluation strategy (materialization, pipelining, or hybrid).

**Expression Tree Diagram (Sample):**

```
            π(A, E)
              |
          σ(B > 50)
              |
        ⨝(R.C = S.C)
        /         \
       R           S
```

**Role of Query Optimizer:**
- Selects the best **join order** (R ⋈ S vs. S ⋈ R).
- Chooses the **join algorithm** (Hash, Sort-Merge, NLJ).
- Decides between **index-based** and **full-table** access.
- Applies **predicate pushdown** and **projection pushdown** to reduce data volume.
- Considers **parallelism** options in modern engines.

> **Marking Guide (7 marks):**
> - 2 marks: 5-step query evaluation process explained.
> - 2 marks: Expression tree diagram.
> - 2 marks: Role of query optimizer (at least 4 responsibilities).
> - 1 mark: Neat presentation and terminology.

---

**Part (b) - Cost Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N_R = 1000$, $N_S = 500$, $T_R = 10000$, $T_S = 5000$, $B = 10$, $sf = 0.1$.

**Step 1: Cost of the Join (Hash Join) — Common to Both Strategies:**
$$C_{\text{join}} = 3 \cdot (N_R + N_S) = 3 \cdot 1500 = 4500 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Step 2: Result Size of the Join:**
$$T_{\bowtie} = \frac{T_R \cdot T_S}{\max(V(C, R), V(C, S))} \approx \frac{10000 \cdot 5000}{\max(50, 50)} = \frac{50000000}{50} = 1000000 \text{ tuples}$$
$$N_{\bowtie} = 1000000 \text{ pages (1 tuple per page assumed)}$$

**Step 3: Cost of Selection (Materialized case only):**
$$C_{\text{sel}} = N_{\bowtie} = 1000000 \text{ I/Os}$$
$$T_{\sigma} = 0.1 \cdot 1000000 = 100000 \text{ tuples} = 100000 \text{ pages}$$

**Step 4: Cost of Projection (Materialized case only):**
$$C_{\text{proj}} = N_{\sigma} = 100000 \text{ I/Os}$$

### Case 1: Materialized Evaluation

$$C_{\text{materialized}} = C_{\text{join}} + C_{\text{sel}} + C_{\text{proj}}$$

$$C_{\text{materialized}} = 4500 + 1000000 + 100000 = 1104500 \text{ I/Os}$$

### Case 2: Pipelined Evaluation

The selection and projection operate on streaming tuples — no extra disk I/O:
$$C_{\text{pipelined}} = 4500 + 0 + 0 = 4500 \text{ I/Os}$$

### Comparison

$$\text{Savings} = 1104500 - 4500 = 1100000 \text{ I/Os}$$

$$\text{Speedup Factor} = \frac{1104500}{4500} \approx 245.4 \times$$

> **Marking Guide (7 marks):**
> - 2 marks: Correct join cost calculation.
> - 2 marks: Correct materialized cost (with intermediate materialization).
> - 1 mark: Correct pipelined cost.
> - 1 mark: Comparison and speedup factor.
> - 1 mark: Final boxed answer with units.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With a suitable example expression tree, discuss the **iterator-based evaluation model**. Explain how it supports **demand-driven pipelining** and discuss the limitations imposed by **blocking operators**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the following join algorithms for evaluating the join $R \bowtie S$ in a pipelined manner, given $N_R = 800$, $N_S = 400$, $B = 20$:
- (i) Block Nested-Loop Join
- (ii) Sort-Merge Join (assuming both inputs pre-sorted)
- (iii) Hash Join (assuming R fits in memory)

Compute the I/O cost for each and recommend the best algorithm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (Question B)

**Part (a) - Iterator Model (7 marks):**

**Example Expression Tree:**
```
        π(Name, Salary)
              |
        σ(Dept = 'CS')
              |
        ⨝(E.DeptID = D.DeptID)
        /         \
   EMPLOYEE E   DEPARTMENT D
```

**Iterator Interface (Volcano Model):**
- `open()` → Initialize state
- `getNext()` → Return next tuple (or vector batch)
- `close()` → Cleanup

**How Demand-Driven Pipelining Works:**

In this model, evaluation is **pull-based**. The root operator (`π`) calls `getNext()` on itself. To produce its next output tuple, it calls `getNext()` on its child (`σ`), which in turn calls `getNext()` on `⨝`, which calls `getNext()` on the base scans of `E` and `D`.

```
Root: getNext()  →  π requests tuple
σ: getNext()     →  ⨝ requests tuple
⨝: getNext()     →  E.next() and D.next()
E, D return tuples
⨝ produces joined tuple
σ filters; if pass, returns to π
π projects; returns to caller
```

This pull-based mechanism ensures that **no tuple is computed before it is needed**, achieving true pipelining.

**Blocking Operators and Their Impact:**

An operator is **blocking** if it cannot produce any output tuple until it has consumed its **entire input**. Examples:
- **Sort** (must see all tuples before producing sorted output)
- **Hash Aggregation** (must hash all tuples before producing groups)
- **Set Union / Difference** (must eliminate duplicates)
- **Sort-Merge Join's sort phase** (the sort blocks; the merge can pipeline)

**Limitations Imposed:**
- Blocking operators **force a materialization boundary** in the pipeline.
- Memory must be sufficient to hold the blocked input, or **spill to disk** (graceful degradation).
- To mitigate: use **two-pass** algorithms or **partitioned hash** when memory is insufficient.

> **Marking Guide (7 marks):**
> - 2 marks: Example tree + iterator interface.
> - 2 marks: Demand-driven pipelining explained.
> - 2 marks: Blocking operators discussed with examples.
> - 1 mark: Limitations and mitigations.

---

**Part (b) - Join Algorithm Comparison (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N_R = 800$, $N_S = 400$, $B = 20$ pages.

**(i) Block Nested-Loop Join (BNLJ):**
$$C_{\text{BNLJ}} = N_R + \left\lceil \frac{N_R}{B - 2} \right\rceil \cdot N_S$$
$$C_{\text{BNLJ}} = 800 + \left\lceil \frac{800}{18} \right\rceil \cdot 400 = 800 + 45 \cdot 400 = 800 + 18000 = 18800 \text{ I/Os}$$

**(ii) Sort-Merge Join (inputs pre-sorted):**
$$C_{\text{SMJ}} = N_R + N_S = 800 + 400 = 1200 \text{ I/Os}$$

**(iii) Hash Join (R fits in memory):**
$$C_{\text{HJ}} = 3 \cdot (N_R + N_S) = 3 \cdot 1200 = 3600 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Algorithm | I/O Cost | When Best |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| BNLJ | 18800 | Last resort; no indexes, no memory |
| Sort-Merge | 1200 | Sorted inputs; large relations |
| Hash Join | 3600 | Memory fits the build relation |

**Recommendation:** **Sort-Merge Join** is best at **1200 I/Os**, assuming both inputs are pre-sorted. If sorting is required, add the sort cost. **Hash Join** is the second best and is more general-purpose when memory is sufficient.

> **Marking Guide (7 marks):**
> - 2 marks: BNLJ calculation correct.
> - 2 marks: Sort-Merge and Hash calculations correct.
> - 2 marks: Comparison table and recommendation.
> - 1 mark: Final answer with reasoning.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing logical and physical plans** — KTU expects you to know that a logical plan has operators like "Join" while a physical plan specifies "Hash Join" or "Sort-Merge Join".
> 2. **Forgetting intermediate materialization in pipelined cost** — Many students compute pipelined cost by simply removing all disk I/O. This is wrong when **blocking operators** are present.
> 3. **Not showing the formula before plugging in numbers** — KTU examiners award partial marks for the *formula*. Always write $C_{\text{HJ}} = 3(N_R + N_S)$ before substituting.
> 4. **Wrong unit (tuples vs. pages)** — Selection cost is in **pages**, but result size after selection is in **tuples** for selectivity; convert consistently.
> 5. **Ignoring $V(A, R)$ in join size estimation** — Many students forget to divide by $\max(V(A, R), V(B, S))$.
> 6. **Confusing iterator calls** — `getNext()` is on the **operator**, not the relation.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Expression Evaluation** = physical execution of a query plan.
> - **Materialization** = full intermediate results on disk; simple but I/O-heavy.
> - **Pipelining** = stream tuples through operators; low I/O, complex.
> - **Iterator Model** = the unifying framework with `open()`, `getNext()`, `close()`.
> - **Blocking operators** (Sort, Hash-Agg) force materialization.
> - **Key formulas:**
>   * Linear scan: $N_R$
>   * BNLJ: $N_R + \lceil N_R / (B-2) \rceil \cdot N_S$
>   * Sort-Merge: $N_R + N_S$ (sorted)
>   * Hash Join: $3 \cdot (N_R + N_S)$
>   * Join size: $T_R \cdot T_S / \max(V(A, R), V(B, S))$
> - **Modern engines** (PostgreSQL, Oracle) use vectorized pipelined execution.
> - **Optimization techniques:** predicate pushdown, projection pushdown, join reordering.
> - **Trade-off:** Memory ↑ → Pipelining ↑ → Speed ↑ (subject to blocking operators).
> - **Cost unit is always pages (I/Os)**, not tuples, unless stated otherwise.
> - **Push-based vs. Pull-based** pipelining: pull (iterator) is the standard; push is used in stream engines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heuristics in Query Optimization: Optimization of Relational Algebra Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heuristics in Query Optimization: Optimization of Relational Algebra Expressions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic Query Optimization** is a rule-based strategy employed by the query optimizer of a Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) that transforms an input **Relational Algebra Expression** into an *equivalent but more efficient* expression by applying a fixed set of empirically validated transformation rules (heuristics), without exhaustively enumerating all possible execution plans and their associated costs.

A relational algebra expression is **equivalent** to another if both expressions produce **identical tuples** (same multiset of rows) over every legal database instance. The goal of heuristic optimization is to minimize the **intermediate relation size**, the **number of disk I/O operations**, and the **computational overhead** of tuple processing.

Two foundational terms used throughout this module are:
- **Cardinality**: The number of tuples in a relation.
- **Selectivity**: The fraction of tuples that satisfy a predicate (lower selectivity → fewer output tuples → better for pushing down).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Planning a Grocery Trip**
>
> Imagine you are a shopper with a long shopping list. A *naive* shopper walks into every aisle of a mega-mart, picks items off every shelf, and then sorts them in the parking lot. A *heuristically optimized* shopper first removes the items already in the fridge (**Selection push-down**), picks only the necessary aisles (**Projection push-down**), and merges trips between adjacent aisles (**Join reordering**). The result is fewer steps, less effort, and less time — exactly what the heuristic query optimizer does for SQL queries.

## 1.3 Why Heuristics are Needed in RDBMS

| Problem | Heuristic Solution |
|---|---|
| Naive evaluation scans entire tables | Push selections $(\sigma)$ close to base relations |
| Wide intermediate tuples waste I/O | Push projections $(\pi)$ close to base relations |
| Cartesian product creates huge results | Replace $(\sigma \text{ after } \times)$ with $(\bowtie)$ |
| Redundant scans of same data | Combine cascaded selections into one selection with AND |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: When a question asks *"Why do we need heuristic optimization?"*, your answer must mention at least **three benefits**: (1) reduces intermediate relation size, (2) reduces I/O cost, (3) avoids redundant computations — without needing exhaustive cost estimation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Query Tree Representation

A **query tree** is a canonical data structure that represents a relational algebra expression. The leaves of the tree are **base relations**, and the internal nodes are **relational algebra operations** $(\sigma, \pi, \times, \bowtie, \cup, -, \rho)$.

**Reading Direction:**
- **Root node** → executed last (final result).
- **Leaf nodes** → executed first (scan base relations).

> [!NOTE]
> The query tree is the *primary input* to the heuristic optimizer. The optimizer applies equivalence transformations to this tree to obtain an *equivalent but more efficient* tree.

## 2.2 Core Heuristic Transformation Rules

### Rule Group A: Selection Operation Rules

1. **Commutativity of Selection**
$$\sigma_{c_1}(\sigma_{c_2}(R)) \equiv \sigma_{c_2}(\sigma_{c_1}(R)) \equiv \sigma_{c_1 \text{ AND } c_2}(R)$$

2. **Selection with Cartesian Product → Join**
$$\sigma_{c}(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_c S$$

3. **Distributivity over Union / Intersection**
$$\sigma_c(R \cup S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) \cup \sigma_c(S)$$
$$\sigma_c(R \cap S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) \cap \sigma_c(S)$$

4. **Distributivity over Set Difference (One-Way Only!)**
$$\sigma_c(R - S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) - \sigma_c(S)$$

5. **Selection before Join (The Golden Rule)**
$$\sigma_c(R \bowtie S) \equiv (\sigma_c(R)) \bowtie S \quad \text{when } c \text{ involves only attributes of } R$$

### Rule Group B: Projection Operation Rules

1. **Cascaded Projections**
$$\pi_{L_1}(\pi_{L_2}(R)) \equiv \pi_{L_1}(R)$$

2. **Pushing Selection above Projection**
$$\pi_L(\sigma_c(R)) \equiv \pi_L(\sigma_c(\pi_{M}(R)))$$
where $M$ contains all attributes referenced in $c$ plus those in $L$.

### Rule Group C: Join and Cartesian Product Rules

1. **Commutativity**: $R \bowtie S \equiv S \bowtie R$
2. **Associativity**: $(R \bowtie S) \bowtie T \equiv R \bowtie (S \bowtie T)$
3. **Cartesian Product Commutativity**: $R \times S \equiv S \times R$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Rule (Equivalence) | Notation / Equation | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Selection Commutativity | $\sigma_{c1}(\sigma_{c2}(R)) \equiv \sigma_{c1 \wedge c2}(R)$ | Combine cascading selections |
| 2 | Select-then-Product becomes Join | $\sigma_c(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_c S$ | **Most tested rule** |
| 3 | Push Selection over Join (one side) | $\sigma_c(R \bowtie S) \equiv (\sigma_c(R)) \bowtie S$ | Reduces left operand size |
| 4 | Distributivity of $\sigma$ over $\cup$ | $\sigma_c(R \cup S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) \cup \sigma_c(S)$ | Standard distributivity |
| 5 | Distributivity of $\sigma$ over $-$ | $\sigma_c(R - S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) - S$ | **One-way only** — common trap |
| 6 | Projection Cascade | $\pi_{L1}(\pi_{L2}(R)) \equiv \pi_{L1}(R)$ | Eliminate redundant projections |
| 7 | Join Commutativity | $R \bowtie S \equiv S \bowtie R$ | Reorder joins |
| 8 | Join Associativity | $(R \bowtie S) \bowtie T \equiv R \bowtie (S \bowtie T)$ | Restructure join tree |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: These rules are embedded in every modern RDBMS optimizer (e.g., PostgreSQL's `geqo` and `rule` optimizer, MySQL's optimizer, Oracle's CBO with rule-based fallback). Understanding them explains why some SQL queries execute 1000× faster than their semantic equivalents — a critical skill for backend engineers and DBAs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Five-Step Canonical Heuristic Algorithm (KTU Standard Procedure)

According to Elmasri & Navathe's framework (the standard KTU reference), heuristic optimization of a query proceeds through the following **five canonical stages**:

### Step 1: Parse the SQL Query into a Canonical Relational Algebra Form
Decompose the query using:
- SELECT list → Projection $(\pi)$
- WHERE clause → Selection $(\sigma)$
- FROM clause → Cartesian Product $(\times)$ or Join $(\bowtie)$
- GROUP BY / HAVING → Aggregation operators

### Step 2: Convert to an Initial Query Tree
Build a tree where the root is the projection, intermediate nodes are selections and joins, and leaves are base relations.

### Step 3: Push Selections Down (Selection Push-Down)
Apply the rule:
$$\sigma_c(R \bowtie S) \equiv (\sigma_c(R)) \bowtie S \quad \text{when } c \text{ involves only } R$$
Repeat recursively until no selection can be pushed further.

### Step 4: Push Projections Down (Projection Push-Down)
Apply the rule:
$$\pi_L(\sigma_c(R)) \equiv \pi_L(\sigma_c(\pi_{M}(R)))$$
where $M$ contains attributes needed in $c$ plus $L$. This shrinks intermediate tuples.

### Step 5: Determine Final Join Ordering
Use heuristics (smallest relation first) and replace any remaining $\sigma + \times$ with $\bowtie$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

**Given Schema:**
- EMPLOYEE(<u>Fname</u>, Lname, SSN, Bdate, Salary, Dno)
- DEPARTMENT(<u>Dnumber</u>, Dname, MgrSSN)

**Query (Q):** Retrieve the first name, last name, and department name of all employees who work in the *Research* department.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**SQL Formulation:**
$$
\text{SELECT E.Fname, E.Lname, D.Dname FROM EMPLOYEE E, DEPARTMENT D WHERE E.Dno = D.Dnumber AND D.Dname = 'Research'}
$$

**Stage 1 — Convert to Initial Relational Algebra Expression:**
$$
\pi_{Fname, Lname, Dname}(\sigma_{(E.Dno = D.Dnumber) \wedge (D.Dname = \text{`Research'})}(EMPLOYEE \times DEPARTMENT))
$$

**Stage 2 — Build the Initial Query Tree (Naive Form):**

The root is $\pi$, with $\sigma$ below it, and $\times$ at the bottom between EMPLOYEE and DEPARTMENT. The naive tree forces a Cartesian product of all employees with all departments.

**Stage 3 — Apply Selection Push-Down:**

Decompose the conjunction $c = c_1 \wedge c_2$ where $c_1 = (E.Dno = D.Dnumber)$ and $c_2 = (D.Dname = \text{`Research'})$.

Apply the rule $\sigma_{c_1 \wedge c_2}(R \times S) \equiv (\sigma_{c_1}(R \times S))$ first, then split:

$$
\pi_{Fname, Lname, Dname}\big(\sigma_{E.Dno = D.Dnumber}(\sigma_{D.Dname = \text{`Research'}}(EMPLOYEE \times DEPARTMENT))\big)
$$

Push $\sigma_{D.Dname = \text{`Research'}}$ down to the DEPARTMENT relation:

$$
\pi_{Fname, Lname, Dname}\big(\sigma_{E.Dno = D.Dnumber}(EMPLOYEE \times (\sigma_{D.Dname = \text{`Research'}}(DEPARTMENT)))\big)
$$

**Stage 4 — Convert $\sigma + \times$ to Join:**

Apply the rule $\sigma_c(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_c S$:

$$
\pi_{Fname, Lname, Dname}\big(EMPLOYEE \bowtie_{E.Dno = D.Dnumber} (\sigma_{D.Dname = \text{`Research'}}(DEPARTMENT))\big)
$$

**Stage 5 — Push Projection Down:**

Since the final projection only needs Fname, Lname, Dname, we can push down a projection that retains only those plus the join attributes (Dno and Dnumber):

$$
\pi_{Fname, Lname, Dname}\big((\pi_{Fname, Lname, Dno}(EMPLOYEE)) \bowtie_{E.Dno = D.Dnumber} (\pi_{Dnumber, Dname}(\sigma_{D.Dname = \text{`Research'}}(DEPARTMENT)))\big)
$$

**Stage 6 — Final Optimized Query Tree.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost Comparison

| Metric | Naive Plan | Optimized Plan | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tuples in Cartesian Product | $\mid\text{EMPLOYEE}\mid \times \mid\text{DEPARTMENT}\mid$ | $\mid\text{EMPLOYEE}\mid \times 1$ (only Research dept) | **≈ 100×** (typical) |
| Tuples leaving intermediate join | $\mid\text{EMPLOYEE}\mid \times \mid\text{DEPARTMENT}\mid$ | $\mid\text{EMPLOYEE}\mid$ | **Huge** |
| Width of tuples flowing up | All attributes | Only 3 final attributes | **I/O bandwidth savings** |
| Disk pages touched | Very high | Significantly lower | **Direct I/O cost reduction** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip**: When a 14-mark question asks you to *"optimize a given relational algebra expression"*, you **must** show all five stages: (1) initial tree, (2) selection push-down, (3) projection push-down, (4) conversion to join, (5) final tree. Missing any stage typically costs **2–3 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Initial (Naive) Query Tree — Mermaid Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["PROJECT Fname, Lname, Dname"] --> B["SELECT E.Dno = D.Dnumber AND D.Dname = Research"]
    B --> C["PRODUCT"]
    C --> D["EMPLOYEE"]
    C --> E["DEPARTMENT"]
```

## 4.2 Optimized Query Tree — Mermaid Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A2["PROJECT Fname, Lname, Dname"] --> B2["JOIN ON E.Dno = D.Dnumber"]
    B2 --> C2["PROJECT Fname, Lname, Dno"]
    C2 --> D2["EMPLOYEE"]
    B2 --> E2["PROJECT Dnumber, Dname"]
    E2 --> F2["SELECT D.Dname = Research"]
    F2 --> G2["DEPARTMENT"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Heuristic Optimizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        Q[SQL Query String]
    end
    subgraph PARSE["PARSING STAGE"]
        P[Parser and Syntax Tree Builder]
    end
    subgraph CANONICAL["CANONICAL FORM STAGE"]
        T1[Initial Relational Algebra Expression]
        T2[Initial Query Tree]
    end
    subgraph HEURISTIC["HEURISTIC OPTIMIZATION STAGE"]
        H1["Heuristic 1: Selection Push-Down"]
        H2["Heuristic 2: Projection Push-Down"]
        H3["Heuristic 3: Cartesian to Join"]
        H4["Heuristic 4: Join Reordering"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        T3[Optimized Query Tree]
        EX[Execution Engine]
    end
    Q --> P --> T1 --> T2
    T2 --> H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> T3 --> EX
```

## 4.4 Equivalence Rule Dependency Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1["Sigma Commutativity"] --> R6["Combined Selection Push-Down"]
    R2["Sigma over Product to Join"] --> R6
    R3["Sigma over Join Push-Down"] --> R6
    R4["Projection Cascade"] --> R7["Projection Push-Down"]
    R5["Sigma over Projection"] --> R7
    R6 --> R8["Fully Optimized Tree"]
    R7 --> R8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is heuristic-based query optimization? How does it differ from cost-based optimization?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Heuristic-based query optimization uses a fixed set of pre-defined transformation rules (heuristics) to convert a relational algebra expression into an equivalent more efficient expression.
- **(1 Mark)** It does not enumerate all possible execution plans and does not rely on statistical cost estimation.
- **(1 Mark)** Cost-based optimization, in contrast, generates multiple candidate plans and uses statistics (cardinality, selectivity, I/O cost) to choose the cheapest plan. Heuristic optimization is faster but may produce sub-optimal plans compared to cost-based methods.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and briefly explain any three equivalence rules used for transforming relational algebra expressions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark) Selection Commutativity:** $\sigma_{c1}(\sigma_{c2}(R)) \equiv \sigma_{c1 \wedge c2}(R)$ — cascading selections can be combined into a single selection with a conjunctive condition.
