- **(1 Mark) Conversion of Selection over Product to Join:** $\sigma_c(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_c S$ — this avoids the explosion of intermediate tuples caused by the Cartesian product.
- **(1 Mark) Distributivity of Selection over Union:** $\sigma_c(R \cup S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) \cup \sigma_c(S)$ — the selection can be pushed into both operands, reducing intermediate size.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **DO NOT** simply draw the final tree without showing intermediate steps. Each transformation step must be **explicitly written** with the **rule name** and the **equivalence equation** applied. (-3 marks penalty).
> - **DO NOT** apply a selection push-down when the predicate references attributes from **both** sides of the join — this is invalid and will be marked wrong.
> - **Always** label your trees clearly: *"Initial Query Tree"* vs *"Optimized Query Tree"* to guide the examiner.
> - **Projection push-down**: forgetting to include the join attributes in the lower projection is a very common error (-2 marks).

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks

Consider the schema:
- **STUDENT(<u>SSN</u>, SName, Age, DeptID)**
- **COURSE(<u>CID</u>, CName, DeptID)**
- **ENROLLMENT(<u>SSN, CID</u>, Grade)**

**(a)** Translate the following SQL query into an equivalent initial relational algebra expression and draw its query tree. **(7 Marks)**

$$
\text{SELECT S.SName, C.CName, E.Grade FROM STUDENT S, COURSE C, ENROLLMENT E WHERE S.SSN = E.SSN AND C.CID = E.CID AND S.Age < 25}
$$

**(b)** Apply heuristic optimization (selection push-down, projection push-down, conversion of product to join) to obtain the optimized relational algebra expression and draw the optimized query tree. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Initial Relational Algebra Expression and Tree (7 Marks):**

The initial relational algebra expression (combining all WHERE conditions) is:

$$
\pi_{SName, CName, Grade}\big(\sigma_{(S.SSN = E.SSN) \wedge (C.CID = E.CID) \wedge (S.Age < 25)}(STUDENT \times COURSE \times ENROLLMENT)\big)
$$

**Initial Query Tree (Naive):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["PROJECT SName, CName, Grade"] --> B["SELECT S.SSN = E.SSN AND C.CID = E.CID AND S.Age less than 25"]
    B --> C["PRODUCT"]
    C --> D["PRODUCT"]
    D --> E["STUDENT"]
    D --> F["COURSE"]
    C --> G["ENROLLMENT"]
```

This naive plan performs a **3-way Cartesian product**, generating a massive intermediate relation. **(Full 7 marks awarded for correct expression + tree.)**

---

**Part (b) — Heuristic Optimization (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Decompose the conjunction:** Let $c_1 = (S.SSN = E.SSN)$, $c_2 = (C.CID = E.CID)$, $c_3 = (S.Age < 25)$.

**Step 2 — Push $c_3$ down to STUDENT (since it involves only STUDENT's attribute Age):**

$$
\pi_{SName, CName, Grade}\big(\sigma_{c_1 \wedge c_2}((\sigma_{c_3}(STUDENT)) \times COURSE \times ENROLLMENT)\big)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert remaining selections on Cartesian products into joins:**

Apply $\sigma_{c_1 \wedge c_2}(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_{c_1 \wedge c_2} S$:

$$
\pi_{SName, CName, Grade}\big((\sigma_{S.Age < 25}(STUDENT)) \bowtie_{S.SSN = E.SSN} (ENROLLMENT \bowtie_{C.CID = E.CID} COURSE)\big)
$$

**Step 4 — Push projection down to each leaf relation:**

- STUDENT projection: keep SSN, SName, Age
- ENROLLMENT projection: keep SSN, CID, Grade
- COURSE projection: keep CID, CName

**Optimized Relational Algebra Expression:**

$$
\pi_{SName, CName, Grade}\Big(\big(\pi_{SSN, SName, Age}(\sigma_{S.Age < 25}(STUDENT))\big) \bowtie_{S.SSN = E.SSN} \big(\pi_{SSN, CID, Grade}(ENROLLMENT)\big) \bowtie_{C.CID = E.CID} \big(\pi_{CID, CName}(COURSE)\big)\Big)
$$

**Optimized Query Tree:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A2["PROJECT SName, CName, Grade"] --> B2["JOIN ON S.SSN = E.SSN"]
    B2 --> C2["PROJECT SSN, SName, Age"]
    C2 --> D2["SELECT S.Age less than 25"]
    D2 --> E2["STUDENT"]
    B2 --> F2["JOIN ON C.CID = E.CID"]
    F2 --> G2["PROJECT SSN, CID, Grade"]
    G2 --> H2["ENROLLMENT"]
    F2 --> I2["PROJECT CID, CName"]
    I2 --> J2["COURSE"]
```

**(Full 7 marks awarded for showing all four optimization steps explicitly and providing the optimized tree.)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

Consider the schema:
- **EMP(<u>EmpID</u>, EmpName, Salary, DeptID)**
- **DEPT(<u>DeptID</u>, DeptName, Location)**
- **PROJECT(<u>ProjID</u>, ProjName, DeptID, Budget)**

**(a)** Write the initial relational algebra expression for the following query and draw its query tree. **(7 Marks)**

> *"Retrieve the employee name, salary, and project name of all employees who earn more than 50,000 and whose department is located in 'Kochi'."*

**(b)** Apply the heuristic optimization rules to transform the initial expression into an optimized one. Show every intermediate step and draw the optimized query tree. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Initial Expression:**

Joins are required: EMP $\bowtie$ DEPT (on DeptID) and EMP $\bowtie$ PROJECT (on DeptID). Selection: Salary > 50000 AND Location = 'Kochi'. Final projection: EmpName, Salary, ProjName.

$$
\pi_{EmpName, Salary, ProjName}\big(\sigma_{(Emp.Salary > 50000) \wedge (Dept.Location = \text{`Kochi'})}(EMP \times DEPT \times PROJECT)\big)
$$

**Initial Query Tree:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["PROJECT EmpName, Salary, ProjName"] --> B["SELECT Salary more than 50000 AND Location = Kochi"]
    B --> C["PRODUCT"]
    C --> D["EMP"]
    C --> E["DEPT"]
    C --> F["PROJECT"]
```

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Heuristic Optimization:**

**Step 1 — Selection Push-Down:**
$\sigma_{Salary > 50000}$ pushed to EMP.
$\sigma_{Location = \text{`Kochi'}}$ pushed to DEPT.

**Step 2 — Convert to Joins:**
Replace $\sigma + \times$ with $\bowtie$. The join conditions are on DeptID.

**Step 3 — Projection Push-Down:**
- EMP: keep EmpID, EmpName, Salary, DeptID
- DEPT: keep DeptID, Location (DeptName is dropped as it's not needed)
- PROJECT: keep ProjID, ProjName, DeptID (Budget dropped)

**Optimized Expression:**

$$
\pi_{EmpName, Salary, ProjName}\Big(\big(\pi_{EmpID, EmpName, Salary, DeptID}(\sigma_{Salary > 50000}(EMP))\big) \bowtie_{DeptID} \big(\pi_{DeptID, Location}(\sigma_{Location = \text{`Kochi'}}(DEPT))\big) \bowtie_{DeptID} \big(\pi_{ProjID, ProjName, DeptID}(PROJECT)\big)\Big)
$$

**Optimized Query Tree:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A2["PROJECT EmpName, Salary, ProjName"] --> B2["JOIN ON DeptID"]
    B2 --> C2["JOIN ON DeptID"]
    C2 --> D2["PROJECT EmpID, EmpName, Salary, DeptID"]
    D2 --> E2["SELECT Salary more than 50000"]
    E2 --> F2["EMP"]
    C2 --> G2["PROJECT DeptID, Location"]
    G2 --> H2["SELECT Location = Kochi"]
    H2 --> I2["DEPT"]
    B2 --> J2["PROJECT ProjID, ProjName, DeptID"]
    J2 --> K2["PROJECT"]
```

**(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Heuristic optimization = rule-based**, no cost statistics required.
- **The "Golden Rule"**: Always push selections $(\sigma)$ **as close to the base relations as possible** — this is the single most impactful transformation.
- **Projection push-down** shrinks tuple width → reduces I/O bandwidth and memory usage.
- **$\sigma_c(R \times S) \equiv R \bowtie_c S$** — always replace Cartesian products with conditional joins when a join condition exists.
- **Selection over set difference is ONE-WAY**: $\sigma_c(R - S) \equiv \sigma_c(R) - S$ is valid, but $R - \sigma_c(S)$ is **not** equivalent in general.
- **Join associativity** allows you to reorder joins for better intermediate sizes — typically place the smallest intermediate relation first.
- **The 5 canonical steps** of heuristic optimization: (1) Parse to canonical form, (2) Build initial tree, (3) Push selections down, (4) Push projections down, (5) Reorder joins and replace $\times$ with $\bowtie$.
- **Exam checklist for full marks**: Always show (i) the original expression, (ii) every intermediate transformation with the rule name, and (iii) the final optimized tree with a clearly drawn diagram.
- **Equivalence ≠ Identical Plans**: Two equivalent relational algebra expressions can have vastly different execution costs — this is precisely *why* optimization exists.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Database Design and Tuning: Introduction to Physical Database Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Database Design and Tuning: Introduction to Physical Database Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Database Design** is the process of translating the logical data model (conceptual schema) into a physical storage specification that describes the **base relations, file organizations, indexes, integrity constraints, and storage allocation** for a target Database Management System (DBMS). It defines *how* the data will be stored, accessed, and managed on secondary storage devices to satisfy the performance, availability, and scalability requirements of the enterprise.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based framework, Physical Database Design is treated as the **bridge phase** between the logical schema (independent of any DBMS) and the deployed physical database (dependent on a specific DBMS such as Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQL Server). It is the phase where abstract entities become concrete blocks on a hard disk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Database Tuning** (often called *Physical Database Tuning* or *Performance Tuning*) is the ongoing activity of adjusting the physical design parameters — such as index selection, clustering factor, partitioning strategy, buffer pool size, and query plans — to keep the database responsive as workloads evolve. It is *not* a one-time activity; it is a continuous process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **massive library system** for a city:

* **Logical Design** is deciding *what* books exist, what *categories* (tables) they belong to, and the *relationships* between them (e.g., "Author wrote Book", "Member borrows Book"). This is done without knowing the building layout.
* **Physical Design** is deciding the **actual shelving layout**: which books go on which floor, which shelves are closest to the entrance, whether to maintain a **card-catalog index** (B+ tree index) for fast lookup, whether to group all books of the same author together (**clustering**), how wide the aisles are (block size), and how the librarian will retrieve books in the fewest steps.

Tuning, then, is the librarian noticing that "Detective novels" are always in high demand and so **adding a small express-return shelf near the entrance** (creating a new index or materialized view) to speed up retrievals.

### 1.3 Why Physical Design and Tuning Matter (KTU CO Alignment)

| KTU Course Outcome | Role of Physical Design & Tuning |
|---|---|
| **CO1** – Model and design advanced database structures | Physical design defines structures such as indexes, partitions, and clusters. |
| **CO2** – Apply tuning techniques to optimize performance | Tuning directly modifies these physical structures based on observed workloads. |
| **CO3** – Evaluate trade-offs in storage vs. speed | Every physical design decision is a trade-off — indexes speed reads but slow writes. |

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industry Terms)

* **I/O Cost (Disk Access Cost)** – The number of disk blocks read or written during query execution. This is the **dominant performance metric** in disk-based DBMSs.
* **Throughput** – Number of transactions completed per unit time, measured in **TPS (Transactions Per Second)**.
* **Response Time (Latency)** – Time taken to execute a single query, typically in **milliseconds (ms)**.
* **Buffer Hit Ratio** – Fraction of requested pages found in main memory (RAM) buffer pool, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
* **Clustering Factor** – A measure (between 0 and 1) of how physically ordered the rows of a table are with respect to a given index.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, examiners reward answers that **explicitly mention I/O cost, block access, and the trade-off between query time and update overhead** when discussing physical design decisions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Phases of Database Design (Review)

Physical Database Design does not occur in isolation. It is the **final stage** of a multi-phase design methodology. The KTU 2024 syllabus places it after Conceptual and Logical design.

1. **Requirement Analysis** – User requirements, transaction throughput, response time SLAs.
2. **Conceptual Design** – ER / EER model (high-level entities, relationships).
3. **Logical Design** – Mapping ER to relational schema, normalization (up to 3NF/BCNF).
4. **Physical Design** – Storage structures, indexes, partitions, clustering.
5. **Tuning** – Continuous refinement based on real workload.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A normalized logical schema is rarely optimal physically. Physical design often involves **controlled denormalization** (e.g., pre-joining tables, storing derived columns) to trade storage for speed.

### 2.2 Inputs Required for Physical Database Design

A KTU-perfect answer must enumerate the four canonical inputs:

* **Logical Schema** – The output of logical design (relations, attributes, keys, constraints).
* **Application Program / Transaction Specifications** – Expected queries, updates, frequency, expected volume.
* **Functional Requirements** – Response time targets, security, recovery, integrity.
* **Target DBMS Features** – Available indexes (B+ tree, hash, bitmap, R-tree, GiST), partitioning types (range, list, hash, composite), clustering options, compression capabilities.

### 2.3 Decisions Made During Physical Design

These are the **high-leverage decisions** a database administrator (DBA) makes:

1. **Primary Storage Organization** – Heap (unsorted), Hash, Indexed, B+ Tree clustered.
2. **Index Selection** – Which columns to index? Single-column vs. composite? Unique vs. non-unique?
3. **Clustering Strategy** – Should related rows from multiple tables be stored physically together?
4. **Partitioning** – Should large tables be split horizontally (range/list) or vertically?
5. **Denormalization** – Should we violate normalization to speed up reads?
6. **Free Space Allocation** – Block size, fill factor, extent size, segment management.
7. **Data Placement** – On which disk, RAID level, tablespace, or even which server (distributed)?

### 2.4 Steps in the Physical Design Methodology (KTU Board Pattern)

The standard 7-step procedure tested in KTU ESE:

1. **Translate the logical data model** for the target DBMS (e.g., ER → Oracle/PostgreSQL DDL).
2. **Design the physical file organization** for each base relation.
3. **Analyze transactions** to identify hot columns and access patterns.
4. **Choose indexes** based on the WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY, and GROUP BY clauses of frequent queries.
5. **Estimate disk space** requirements for tables and indexes.
6. **Determine integrity constraints** (CHECK, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, FK) — physical considerations.
7. **Review and refine** — Tune for performance using EXPLAIN plans, wait-event analysis.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU 2024 Scheme Part B numericals and derivations.

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_s$ | Average seek time | Constant per disk model | **ms** |
| $r$ | Rotation latency (half rotation) | $r = \tfrac{1}{2N}$ where $N$ = RPM | **ms** |
| $b_{tt}$ | Block transfer time | $b_{tt} = \tfrac{B}{TR}$ where $B$ = block size, $TR$ = transfer rate | **ms** |
| $T_{avg}$ | Average block access time | $T_{avg} = T_s + r + b_{tt}$ | **ms** |
| $b_r$ | Blocks to read for a query | $b_r = \lceil \tfrac{r}{B_{rf}} \rceil$ (linear scan) | blocks |
| $C_{scan}$ | Cost of linear (full table) scan | $C_{scan} = b_r \cdot T_{avg}$ | **ms** |
| $C_{idx}$ | Cost of indexed retrieval (B+ tree) | $C_{idx} = \log_{F} b_r + 1$ where $F$ = fan-out | **block I/Os** |
| $BHR$ | Buffer Hit Ratio | $BHR = \tfrac{\text{logical reads} - \text{physical reads}}{\text{logical reads}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| $EAT$ | Effective Access Time | $EAT = BHR \cdot T_{RAM} + (1 - BHR) \cdot T_{avg}$ | **ms** |
| $CF$ | Clustering Factor | $CF \in [0, 1]$; close to 0 means rows are physically clustered by index order | dimensionless |
| $TPS$ | Transactions per second | $TPS = \tfrac{N_{tx}}{T_{total}}$ | **tx/sec** |
| $S_{table}$ | Storage for a table | $S_{table} = \tfrac{R \cdot L_R}{B \cdot U}$ where $R$ = rows, $L_R$ = row size, $U$ = utilization | **blocks** |
| $F$ | B+ Tree fan-out | $F \approx \tfrac{B}{\text{ptr\_size} + \text{key\_size}}$ | pointers/node |

> **Critical formatting note:** Absolute values and conditional expressions inside the table use `\vert` (not the bare pipe character) to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Physical database design is not academic — it directly governs the success of:

* **Banking Core Systems** – Where sub-second response on millions of transactions per day is mandatory; index selection and partitioning are business-critical.
* **E-commerce Catalogs** – Search latency directly affects revenue; bitmap and inverted indexes power faceted search.
* **IoT / Time-Series Platforms** – Range partitioning by time, descending clustered indexes, and columnar storage are all physical design choices.
* **Geospatial Systems** – R-tree and GiST indexes support GIS queries; tablespaces and data placement affect throughput.
* **Data Warehouses** – Star-schema denormalization, fact-table partitioning, and bitmap indexes are textbook physical design outcomes.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 examiners love asking *"Justify why you would choose a clustered B+ tree index over a heap organization for an Orders table accessed by OrderDate range queries."* The answer must mention **physical ordering**, **range scan efficiency**, and the **clustering factor approaching 0**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Access Time (EAT) with Buffer Pool

The Effective Access Time is the single most important performance metric a KTU examiner can ask. We derive it below **step by step** with no skips.

**Scenario:** A query requests a data block. With probability `BHR` (Buffer Hit Ratio), the block is already in RAM. With probability `(1 - BHR)`, it must be fetched from disk.

**Step 1 — Define the two states.**

* **State 1 (Cache Hit):** Block is in RAM. Access time = $T_{RAM}$.
* **State 2 (Cache Miss):** Block is on disk. Access time = $T_{avg} = T_s + r + b_{tt}$.

**Step 2 — Weighted average.**

The expected (mean) access time is the probability-weighted sum of the two states:

$$
EAT = P(\text{hit}) \cdot T_{RAM} + P(\text{miss}) \cdot T_{avg}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute probabilities.**

By definition, $P(\text{hit}) = BHR$ and $P(\text{miss}) = 1 - BHR$:

$$
EAT = BHR \cdot T_{RAM} + (1 - BHR) \cdot (T_s + r + b_{tt})
$$

**Step 4 — Expand and simplify.**

$$
EAT = BHR \cdot T_{RAM} + T_s + r + b_{tt} - BHR \cdot T_s - BHR \cdot r - BHR \cdot b_{tt}
$$

Grouping the constants:

$$
EAT = T_s + r + b_{tt} + BHR \cdot (T_{RAM} - T_s - r - b_{tt})
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

* When $BHR \to 1$, $EAT \to T_{RAM}$ (excellent, RAM-resident).
* When $BHR \to 0$, $EAT \to T_{avg}$ (poor, full disk access).
* Increasing $BHR$ by even 1% can save milliseconds — this is the **fundamental justification for buffer-pool tuning** in KTU answers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost of a Linear Scan vs. B+ Tree Index Lookup

**Given:**
* Table `Employee` has $R = 100{,}000$ rows.
* Row size $L_R = 200$ bytes.
* Block size $B = 4096$ bytes.
* B+ tree fan-out $F = 100$.
* Search key `EmpID` is unique.

**Step 1 — Compute number of blocks (blocking factor).**

Blocking factor $bfr = \lfloor B / L_R \rfloor$:

$$
bfr = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{200} \right\rfloor = 20 \text{ rows/block}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total blocks $b_r$ for the table.**

$$
b_r = \left\lceil \frac{R}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{20} \right\rceil = 5000 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost of a Linear Scan (find all qualifying rows).**

A full scan reads every block exactly once:

$$
C_{scan} = b_r = 5000 \text{ block I/Os}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost of a B+ Tree Index Lookup (find ONE row).**

Number of index levels (depth):

$$
\text{Levels} = \lceil \log_F b_r \rceil = \lceil \log_{100} 5000 \rceil
$$

$$
\log_{100} 5000 = \frac{\ln 5000}{\ln 100} = \frac{8.517}{4.605} \approx 1.85
$$

So $\text{Levels} = 2$. We add 1 I/O for the data block:

$$
C_{idx} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ block I/Os}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and conclude.**

$$
\frac{C_{scan}}{C_{idx}} = \frac{5000}{3} \approx 1667 \text{ times faster with index}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off warning:** An index that *speeds up a SELECT* by 1667x *also slows down every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE* because the index must be maintained. KTU examiners deduct marks if a student **never mentions update overhead**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: B+ Tree Fan-out and Height

**Given:** Block size $B = 4096$ bytes, key size = 8 bytes, pointer size = 6 bytes.

**Step 1 — Entry size.**

Each B+ tree index entry contains one key + one pointer (in internal nodes):

$$
E_{size} = K_{size} + P_{size} = 8 + 6 = 14 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Fan-out $F$.**

$$
F = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{E_{size}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{14} \right\rfloor = 292
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum rows indexable.**

A B+ tree of height $h$ can index up to $F^h$ leaf entries. For a 4-level B+ tree:

$$
\text{Max rows} = F^4 = 292^4 \approx 7.27 \times 10^9 \text{ rows}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why B+ trees are the de-facto index structure in virtually all RDBMSs — even with billions of rows, the tree is only 3–4 levels deep, keeping lookup cost in single-digit block I/Os.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Choose Which File Organization

| Criterion | Heap (Unsorted) | Hash | Indexed (Unclustered) | Clustered B+ Tree |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full table scan | **Best** (no overhead) | Poor | Average | Average |
| Equality search (`=`) | Poor (scan) | **Best** | Good | Good |
| Range search (`BETWEEN`) | Poor | **Worst** | Average | **Best** |
| Insert performance | **Best** | Good | Average | Average |
| Storage overhead | None | Small | Moderate | Moderate |
| Best use case | Staging tables, bulk load | Point lookups, dictionaries | OLTP on rare columns | Time-series, sorted reports |
| KTU typical score | 1 mark for stating | 1 mark for trade-off | 2 marks for analysis | 3 marks for full justification |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical (KTU ESE Pattern)

**Question:** A table has 50,000 records, each 100 bytes long, stored in blocks of 2000 bytes. The disk has an average seek time of **8 ms**, rotation latency of **4.5 ms**, and transfer rate of **5 MB/s**. Compute (a) blocking factor, (b) number of blocks, (c) average block access time, (d) cost of a full scan.

**Solution:**

**(a) Blocking factor:**

$$
bfr = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{L_R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2000}{100} \right\rfloor = 20
$$

**(b) Number of blocks:**

$$
b_r = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{20} \right\rceil = 2500 \text{ blocks}
$$

**(c) Average block access time:**

Block transfer time $b_{tt}$:

$$
b_{tt} = \frac{B}{TR} = \frac{2000 \text{ bytes}}{5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.4 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{avg} = T_s + r + b_{tt} = 8 + 4.5 + 0.4 = 12.9 \text{ ms}
$$

**(d) Full-scan cost:**

$$
C_{scan} = 2500 \times 12.9 \text{ ms} = 32{,}250 \text{ ms} \approx 32.25 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** If we add a B+ tree index with fan-out 100, the same point-query cost becomes $\lceil \log_{100} 2500 \rceil + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$ block I/Os $\approx 51.6$ ms — a **625x speedup**, but at the cost of index maintenance on every write.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Physical Database Design Workflow

This diagram captures the **end-to-end pipeline** of how logical design becomes physical storage, with feedback loops for tuning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Logical Schema Output] --> B[Translate to Target DBMS DDL]
    B --> C[Choose Primary File Organization]
    C --> D[Analyze Transaction Workload]
    D --> E[Select Indexes Single and Composite]
    E --> F[Decide Clustering and Partitioning]
    F --> G[Apply Controlled Denormalization if needed]
    G --> H[Estimate Disk Space and Sizing]
    H --> I[Deploy Database]
    I --> J[Monitor Workload]
    J --> K{Performance Acceptable?}
    K -- No --> L[Database Tuning]
    L --> E
    K -- Yes --> M[Stable Production System]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Layered Physical Storage Architecture

This shows the **hierarchy of physical storage components** from the application down to the disk platter.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[Application Query Layer] --> QP[Query Optimizer]
    QP --> BP[Buffer Pool in RAM]
    BP --> HIT{Cache Hit}
    HIT -- Yes --> RAM[Return Data from RAM]
    HIT -- No --> DISK[Disk Controller]
    DISK --> RAID[RAID Array Level 0 1 5 10]
    RAID --> TS[Tablespace and Datafile]
    TS --> SEG[Segment]
    SEG --> EXT[Extent]
    EXT --> BLK[Block Page 4 to 32 KB]
    BLK --> ROW[Row Tuple]
    BLK --> IDX1[Clustered B+ Tree Index]
    BLK --> IDX2[Secondary Unclustered Index]
    BLK --> IDX3[Bitmap or Hash Index]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — The Tuning Feedback Loop

This is the *continuous improvement cycle* that the KTU syllabus describes as Database Tuning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WK[Observed Workload] --> MON[Monitoring Tools]
    MON --> BOT[Bottleneck Identification]
    BOT --> DEC{Tuning Action}
    DEC -- Add Index --> IDX[New Index]
    DEC -- Partition --> PRT[New Partition]
    DEC -- Resize Buffer --> BPR[Buffer Pool Resize]
    DEC -- Rewrite Query --> QR[Query Rewrite]
    IDX --> DB[Physical Database]
    PRT --> DB
    BPR --> DB
    QR --> DB
    DB --> WK
```

### 4.4 Schematic — B+ Tree Index Structure on Disk

This ASCII schematic shows how a B+ tree physically lays out on disk for a KTU essay diagram.

```
                 [Root Block]              <- Disk Block 1
                /      |      \
               /       |       \
        [Block 2] [Block 3] [Block 4]      <- Internal Nodes
        /    \     /   \      /    \
       ......  Leaf-1  Leaf-2  Leaf-3  ......
       | | |  | | | |  | | |  | | | | | |
       5 10 20 25 30 35 40 45 50 55 60 65   <- Leaf Nodes (Linked List)
       ↑                               ↑
       Pointer to data rows       Pointer to next leaf block
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For a 14-mark question**, KTU examiners award full marks only when the diagram shows (1) the **linked list of leaves**, (2) **separate key ranges per internal node**, and (3) **at least one fan-out example** computed from the block size.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Logical Database Design and Physical Database Design.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

* **Logical Database Design** is DBMS-independent. It deals with the *structure* of data — tables, columns, keys, and constraints — derived from the ER/EER model. It focuses on *what* data is stored and the *relationships* between data items, with no concern for storage devices, indexes, or performance.
* **Physical Database Design** is DBMS-specific. It deals with *how* the logical schema is stored on disk. It specifies file organizations, index structures, clustering, partitioning, and access paths, all driven by the expected workload and performance requirements.
* **Key distinction:** Logical design is concerned with **correctness and integrity** (normalization, keys); Physical design is concerned with **efficiency and performance** (I/O cost, response time).

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award the 3rd mark only when the student explicitly states *which is DBMS-dependent* and *which is not*.

---

**Q2. What is a Clustering Factor? Why is it important in physical design?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

* The **Clustering Factor (CF)** is a metric, ranging from **0 to 1**, that measures how physically ordered the rows of a table are with respect to the ordering of the index keys.
* **CF ≈ 0** means rows for adjacent index keys are stored in the *same or adjacent disk blocks* (highly clustered — excellent for range scans).
* **CF ≈ 1** means rows for adjacent index keys are scattered across *different disk blocks* (unclustered — index lookups degrade to multiple single-block I/Os).
* **Importance:** A low CF dramatically reduces I/O cost for **range queries** and **ORDER BY** operations. It is the primary justification for choosing a **clustered B+ tree index** on the column most frequently used in range predicates (e.g., `OrderDate`).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **inputs required for Physical Database Design** with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **step-by-step methodology of physical database design** for a relational schema, highlighting the role of index selection. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (a):**

Physical database design is data-driven and cannot be performed in a vacuum. The four canonical inputs are:

1. **Logical Schema** — The normalized set of relations, attributes, primary keys, foreign keys, and integrity constraints produced by logical design. For example, `Orders(OrderID, CustID, OrderDate, Total)` and `Customers(CustID, Name, City, Phone)`.
2. **Application / Transaction Specifications** — A list of frequent queries (SELECT) and updates (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), with their **expected frequency** (e.g., 10,000 inserts/day, 50,000 range scans on OrderDate/day). This identifies **hot columns**.
3. **Functional & Non-Functional Requirements** — Response time targets (e.g., "all order lookups under 100 ms"), availability (99.99%), security (encryption at rest), and recovery (RTO/RPO).
4. **Target DBMS Feature Set** — Available storage engines, index types (B+ tree, hash, bitmap, partial, functional), partitioning schemes (range/list/hash), and compression options.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Logical Schema]      [Transaction Specs]      [Functional Reqs]      [DBMS Features]
        \                    |                       |                       /
         \                   |                       |                      /
          +------+-----+-----+-----+-----------+----+------+
                 |                                         |
                 v                                         v
        [Transaction Volume & Access Pattern]   [Storage Structures Available]
                 |                                         |
                 +-----------------+-----------------------+
                                   |
                                   v
                     [Physical Design Decisions]
                (File Org, Indexes, Clustering, Partitioning)
```

**Model Answer (b):**

The KTU-recommended **7-step methodology** is:

1. **Translate logical schema to target DBMS DDL** — Convert normalized relations to `CREATE TABLE` statements in the target SQL dialect (Oracle, PostgreSQL, etc.).
2. **Choose primary file organization** — Default is heap; switch to clustered B+ tree if the table is dominated by range scans on a specific column.
3. **Identify hot columns from transactions** — Run a workload profiler. Columns appearing in `WHERE`, `JOIN`, `ORDER BY`, and `GROUP BY` are candidates for indexing.
4. **Select indexes** — For each hot column:
   * **Primary key** → automatic unique B+ tree.
   * **Foreign key** → index to speed up joins and avoid full table locks.
   * **Equality predicates** → B+ tree or hash.
   * **Range predicates** → B+ tree (clustered preferred).
   * **Low-cardinality columns in WHERE** → bitmap (data warehouses).
5. **Estimate disk space** — $S_{table} = \lceil R \cdot L_R / (B \cdot U) \rceil$ blocks for the table, plus index storage $\approx 1.2$ to $1.5 \times$ the table size.
6. **Define integrity constraints at physical level** — `NOT NULL`, `CHECK`, `UNIQUE`, `FOREIGN KEY`, plus trigger-based constraints.
7. **Review, test, and refine** — Use `EXPLAIN PLAN`, `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`, or AWR reports to verify I/O cost and refine.

**Role of index selection:** Index selection is the single highest-leverage decision. A good index can speed up a query by 3–4 orders of magnitude; a bad one (e.g., indexing a low-selectivity column updated frequently) can cripple write throughput.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a **neat diagram**, explain the **B+ Tree file organization** and derive the formula for **B+ Tree search cost** in terms of block accesses. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A relation $R$ has $R = 200{,}000$ records, each of size $L_R = 250$ bytes. The block size is $B = 4096$ bytes. The disk has seek time $T_s = 10$ ms, rotational latency $r = 4$ ms, and transfer rate $TR = 10$ MB/s$. Compute:
   1. The blocking factor and number of blocks.
   2. The average block access time.
   3. The cost of a full table scan.
   4. The cost of a B+ tree lookup (assume fan-out $F = 100$). *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (a):**

A **B+ Tree** is a balanced multiway search tree in which:

* All **data records (or pointers to them) are stored in the leaf nodes**.
* Internal nodes store only **keys and child pointers** for navigation.
* All leaves are at the **same depth** and are typically **linked in a doubly-linked list** for fast sequential access.
* The tree grows and shrinks from the root, maintaining balance.

**B+ Tree Diagram (as required by KTU):**

```
                          [Root: 30 | 60]
                         /       |       \
                        /        |        \
              [Leaf L1]    [Leaf L2]    [Leaf L3]
            10 20 30     40 50 60      70 80 90
            (linked list of leaf nodes →)
```

**Search cost derivation:**

Let $b_r$ = number of leaf blocks and $F$ = fan-out of internal nodes.

The tree height $h$ is:

$$
h = \lceil \log_F b_r \rceil
$$

A search for a key requires:
* 1 I/O per level to descend: $h$ block accesses for the internal nodes.
* 1 I/O for the leaf block containing the record (or pointer): $+1$ block access.

Therefore, the total cost in block accesses is:

$$
C_{idx} = \lceil \log_F b_r \rceil + 1
$$

> The "+1" accounts for fetching the actual data block once the leaf is found. If the index is **unclustered** and the matched rows are scattered, we may need additional I/Os equal to the number of matching rows.

**Model Answer (b):**

**1. Blocking factor and number of blocks:**

$$
bfr = \left\lfloor \frac{B}{L_R} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{250} \right\rfloor = 16
$$

$$
b_r = \left\lceil \frac{R}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{200{,}000}{16} \right\rceil = 12{,}500 \text{ blocks}
$$

**2. Average block access time:**

$$
b_{tt} = \frac{B}{TR} = \frac{4096 \text{ bytes}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} = 4.096 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \approx 0.41 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{avg} = T_s + r + b_{tt} = 10 + 4 + 0.41 = 14.41 \text{ ms}
$$

**3. Cost of full table scan:**

$$
C_{scan} = b_r \times T_{avg} = 12{,}500 \times 14.41 \text{ ms} = 180{,}125 \text{ ms} \approx 180.13 \text{ seconds}
$$

**4. Cost of B+ tree lookup (one record):**

$$
C_{idx} = \lceil \log_{100} 12{,}500 \rceil + 1
$$

$$
\log_{100} 12{,}500 = \frac{\ln 12{,}500}{\ln 100} = \frac{9.433}{4.605} \approx 2.049
$$

$$
C_{idx} = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ block I/Os}
$$

In time terms:

$$
T_{idx} = 4 \times 14.41 \text{ ms} = 57.64 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** B+ tree lookup is $\tfrac{180.13}{0.0576} \approx 3125$ times faster than a full scan for a point query — but the index must be maintained at every write.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in Physical Database Design questions:**
> 1. **Confusing logical and physical design** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the answer does not explicitly state that physical design is **DBMS-specific**.
> 2. **Forgetting update overhead** — Indexes are not free. Every `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` must update all indexes. A perfect answer that says *"add an index to speed up the query"* without mentioning the write cost loses the third mark on trade-off questions.
> 3. **Skipping the block diagram in B+ tree questions** — A 7-mark B+ tree question without a labeled tree diagram typically caps at 4 marks.
> 4. **Not stating units** — "12,500" without "blocks" is technically ambiguous; always include units in numerical answers.
> 5. **Ignoring clustering factor** — When justifying a clustered index, the words *"clustering factor"* and *"physical ordering"* are mandatory keywords for full marks.
> 6. **Treating tuning as a one-time activity** — Tuning is **continuous**; stating otherwise loses 1 mark on any "What is database tuning?" question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

* **Physical Database Design** = The phase that maps a *logical schema* onto the *physical storage* of a specific DBMS, optimizing for performance.
* **Inputs** = Logical schema + Transaction specs + Functional requirements + DBMS features.
* **Key decisions** = File organization, index selection, clustering, partitioning, denormalization, free-space allocation, data placement.
* **File Organizations** = Heap, Hash, Indexed, B+ Tree (clustered/unclustered). Each has a specific strength.
* **B+ Tree** is the de-facto standard; the search cost is $\lceil \log_F b_r \rceil + 1$ block I/Os.
* **Clustering Factor (CF)** quantifies physical ordering; CF near 0 = excellent for range scans.
* **Average Block Access Time** $T_{avg} = T_s + r + b_{tt}$ — used in every I/O cost calculation.
* **Effective Access Time** $EAT = BHR \cdot T_{RAM} + (1 - BHR) \cdot T_{avg}$ — buffer hit ratio is the dominant tuning lever.
* **Trade-off mantra** — Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes. Always state this in the conclusion.
* **Tuning is continuous** — Not a one-time activity. It involves monitoring, identifying bottlenecks, applying fixes, and re-monitoring.
* **Heap vs. Clustered B+ Tree** — Heap is best for bulk insert and full scan; clustered B+ tree is best for range queries on the clustering key.
* **Hash organization** — Excellent for equality lookups; **terrible for range queries** (this is a favorite KTU MCQ).
* **Bitmap indexes** — Best for low-cardinality columns in data warehouses; not recommended in OLTP.
* **Partitions** — Range (by date), List (by region), Hash (by hash key), Composite (range + hash). They reduce the number of blocks read per query.
* **Denormalization** — Controlled violation of normalization to pre-compute joins; saves I/O at the cost of redundancy and update anomalies.
* **Buffer pool** — Larger buffer pool ⇒ higher hit ratio ⇒ lower EAT. Always compute and quote the new BHR after a tuning step.
* **KTU keywords that earn marks** — "DBMS-specific", "I/O cost", "block access", "clustering factor", "update overhead", "workload-driven", "continuous tuning".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — Factors Influencing Physical Database Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Database Design (PDD)** is the process of translating the **logical data model** (conceptual schema + external views) into a **storage-level specification** that describes the *physical storage structures*, *access paths*, *file organisations*, *indexing strategies*, *record placement*, and *data distribution mechanisms* used by the DBMS to store and retrieve data efficiently on secondary storage devices.

The **Factors Influencing Physical Database Design** are the measurable, workload-driven, and system-driven parameters that collectively guide the Database Administrator (DBA) in selecting an optimal physical storage configuration. They are evaluated during the **physical design tuning phase**, immediately after logical design and before implementation.

The primary influencing factors recognised in the KTU 2024 syllabus are categorised as:

| Category | Influencing Factors |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Workload** | Query frequency, query type, transaction mix |
| **Data** | Volume, growth rate, cardinality, distribution |
| **Performance** | Response time, throughput, availability targets |
| **System** | Disk geometry, buffer size, DBMS engine, OS |
| **Integrity** | Recovery time, backup window, security constraints |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasises that physical design is **not a one-time activity** — it must be revisited whenever workload, data volume, or hardware characteristics change significantly (this is termed **physical design reorganisation**).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Library & Warehouse" Model

Imagine you are designing the **internal storage layout of a massive Amazon-style warehouse** that stores 10 million books:

- The **logical design** (catalogue schema) tells you *which books exist* and *their bibliographic relationships* (author, ISBN, genre).
- The **physical design** decides whether the warehouse should:
  - Use **aisle labels** (clustered index on genre),
  - Use a **digital search kiosk** (secondary index on author),
  - Store books **horizontally across two buildings** (horizontal partitioning by publication year),
  - Keep **best-sellers on the ground floor** (data clustering),
  - Pre-allocate **buffer zones near the entrance** (buffer pool hints).

The **factors influencing this design** are:
- **How often** customers search by author vs. by title (query frequency),
- **How many** new books arrive daily (data growth),
- **How fast** the top customer expects delivery (response time SLA),
- **How tall** the warehouse shelves can be (disk geometry),
- **How many** forklifts operate simultaneously (concurrency).

This is exactly what physical database design optimises — translating *how data is queried and updated* into *how it is physically laid out on disk*.

### 1.3 Why Physical Design Matters in KTU Context

A poorly designed physical schema can make a logically perfect database **100x–1000x slower**, even when the SQL is identical. The factors discussed in this module directly determine:

1. **I/O cost** — the dominant performance bottleneck in any DBMS.
2. **Index effectiveness** — whether a B+-tree index actually accelerates queries.
3. **Concurrency throughput** — the number of transactions per second (TPS) the system sustains.
4. **Storage efficiency** — bytes consumed per record, including overheads.
5. **Recoverability** — the time required for media recovery after a failure.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Influencing Factors

The KTU 2024 syllabus consolidates the influencing factors into **five orthogonal dimensions**. Mastering these dimensions is essential for the ESE (End Semester Examination) and the internal module tests.

### Dimension 1 — Query & Transaction Workload Characteristics

The dominant driver of physical design. It includes:

- **Query Frequency (f):** How many times a query is executed per unit time. High-frequency queries (typically the top 5% of queries accounting for ~80% of I/O) deserve indexes, clusters, and materialised views.
- **Query Type:** *Retrieval-only* queries benefit from indexes and clustering; *update-heavy* queries suffer from index maintenance overhead.
- **Query Selectivity (S):** The fraction of rows returned, $S = \vert R_{out} \vert / \vert R_{in} \vert$. Low-selectivity queries (S < 0.01) are prime candidates for index-based access; high-selectivity queries favour full table scans.
- **Query Predicate Coverage:** Which attributes appear in WHERE, JOIN, GROUP BY, and ORDER BY clauses. These attributes are the **index candidate set**.
- **Transaction Mix:** Ratio of read-only to read-write transactions; defines whether B+-trees or bitmap indexes are appropriate.
- **Hot-Spots:** Subsets of data that are accessed far more often (e.g., the current month's orders). These dictate **partitioning keys** and **clustering keys**.

### Dimension 2 — Data Characteristics

- **Cardinality:** Number of distinct values in an attribute. Determines whether an index is *unique*, *non-unique*, or *effectively useless* (low cardinality).
- **Data Volume (V):** Total number of rows × row size. Determines file size and number of disk blocks.
- **Growth Rate (g):** Projected annual increase in volume. Affects partitioning strategy and free-space allocation.
- **Record Size Variation:** Fixed-length vs. variable-length records (VARCHAR, BLOB, CLOB). Influences blocked vs. unblocked organisation.
- **Data Skew:** Non-uniform distribution of values (e.g., 80% of orders come from 20% of customers). Affects hash partitioning and index clustering factor.

### Dimension 3 — Performance Objectives (Service Level Agreements)

- **Response Time Target ($T_{resp}$):** Maximum acceptable query latency. Often expressed in milliseconds.
- **Throughput Target (TPS):** Transactions per second the system must sustain under peak load.
- **Availability Target (e.g., 99.999%):** Drives redundancy, mirroring, and RAID configuration choices.
- **Recovery Time Objective (RTO):** Maximum tolerable downtime; influences backup frequency and log retention.

### Dimension 4 — System & Hardware Constraints

- **Disk Geometry:** Block/Page size (typically **4 KB**, **8 KB**, or **16 KB**), rotational latency, seek time, transfer rate.
- **Storage Type:** HDD (magnetic), SSD (flash), or hybrid. SSDs render some physical design decisions (e.g., clustering) less critical because random I/O is cheap.
- **Buffer Pool Size (B):** In-memory cache size. Larger buffers reduce physical I/O.
- **DBMS Engine Internals:** Query optimiser capabilities, locking granularity, page-level vs. row-level versioning.
- **Operating System File System:** Block alignment, extents, direct I/O support.

### Dimension 5 — Integrity, Security & Operational Factors

- **Backup Window:** Time available for online/offline backups; influences use of incremental vs. full backups.
- **Recovery Requirements:** Point-in-time recovery needs dictate log file sizing and archive strategy.
- **Security Constraints:** Encryption-at-rest affects page layout (e.g., encrypted pages cannot be index-compressed in certain engines).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** GDPR, HIPAA, RBI data-residency rules may force physical location choices.

## 2.2 The Physical Design Decision Pipeline

The DBA applies the influencing factors in the following structured decision flow:

1. **Workload Profiling** — Collect query logs, identify top-K queries, classify by access pattern.
2. **Volume Estimation** — Compute projected table sizes, growth rates, and block counts.
3. **Index Selection** — Choose primary, secondary, clustered, bitmap, partial, or functional indexes.
4. **Clustering & Sorting** — Decide physical row ordering within pages and pages within files.
5. **Partitioning Decision** — Horizontal (range, list, hash, composite), vertical, or hybrid.
6. **Denormalisation Trade-off** — Accept redundancy to reduce joins if read frequency dominates.
7. **File Organisation** — Heap, hashed, B+-tree clustered, or mixed.
8. **Free Space & Fill Factor** — Reserve space on each page to accommodate updates and inserts.
9. **Buffer & Cache Tuning** — Configure buffer pool, sort area, hash area.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarises the **mandatory equations** for physical design problems in the KTU 2024 examination.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Number of disk blocks in a table | $B_{table} = \lceil (N \times R_{size}) \div P_{size} \rceil$ | $N$ = rows, $R_{size}$ = row size (bytes), $P_{size}$ = page size (bytes) |
| 2 | Total table storage (bytes) | $S_{table} = B_{table} \times P_{size} \times (1 + O_{frac})$ | $O_{frac}$ = overhead fraction (typically 0.10–0.25) |
| 3 | Index selectivity | $S_{idx} = \vert V_{distinct} \vert \div N$ | $V_{distinct}$ = number of distinct key values |
| 4 | B+-tree fan-out | $F = \lfloor (P_{size} \div E_{size}) \rfloor$ | $E_{size}$ = index entry size (bytes) |
| 5 | B+-tree height (levels) | $h = \lceil \log_{F}(L) \rceil$ | $L$ = number of leaf pages |
| 6 | Index lookup cost (unique) | $C_{idx} = h + 1$ | Disk accesses to find a single row |
| 7 | Linear scan cost | $C_{scan} = B_{table}$ | All blocks must be read |
| 8 | Clustering factor (CF) | $CF = 1$ (perfect) to $N$ (unordered) | Pages reordered to match index order |
| 9 | Buffer hit ratio | $H = P_{hit} \div P_{total}$ | Probability of finding a page in buffer |
| 10 | Estimated query I/O cost | $C_{Q} = (B_{table} \times (1 - H)) + C_{idx}$ | Effective physical I/O |
| 11 | Effective cost threshold for index | $C_{scan} > C_{idx} \times Q_{freq}$ | $Q_{freq}$ = query frequency weight |
| 12 | Fill factor (FF) | $FF = P_{used} \div P_{size}$ | Fraction of page occupied by data |
| 13 | Free space per page | $F_{free} = P_{size} \times (1 - FF)$ | Bytes reserved for updates |
| 14 | Projected growth (years) | $V_{y} = V_{0} \times (1 + g)^{y}$ | $g$ = annual growth rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on absolute value notation:** Throughout the formulas, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes cardinality (count of tuples), **not** mathematical absolute value. In markdown tables this is rendered as `\vert V_{distinct} \vert` to preserve table syntax integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Physical database design decisions directly underpin:

- **OLTP systems** (banking, e-commerce): High update volume → favours narrow indexes, row-level locking, hash partitioning on transaction ID.
- **OLAP/Data Warehouses**: Read-heavy, analytical → favours star schemas, bitmap indexes, columnar storage, large scan-friendly blocks (**64 KB–1 MB**).
- **IoT/Timeseries databases**: Massive volume, append-only → favours time-range partitioning, compressed columnar storage, LSM-tree engines.
- **Geospatial systems**: Multi-dimensional queries → favours R-trees, GiST, and quad-tree indexing.
- **Hybrid Transactional/Analytical (HTAP) systems**: Mixed workload → favours in-memory columnar engines (e.g., HANA, MemSQL).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Disk Blocks Required for a Table

**Problem Statement.** Given a relation `STUDENT` with $N = 100{,}000$ rows, each row of size $R_{size} = 200$ bytes, and a page size $P_{size} = 4096$ bytes, compute the number of disk blocks required to store the table.

**Step 1 — Establish the relationship.**
Each disk page can hold at most $\lfloor P_{size} \div R_{size} \rfloor$ complete rows. Rows cannot span pages in most DBMSs (this is called the *slotted-page* restriction).

**Step 2 — Compute rows per page.**

$$
\text{Rows per page} = \left\lfloor \frac{P_{size}}{R_{size}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{200} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 20.48 \right\rfloor = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ceiling formula for total blocks.**

$$
B_{table} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{\text{Rows per page}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{20} \right\rceil = 5{,}000 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the direct formula.**

$$
B_{table} = \left\lceil \frac{N \times R_{size}}{P_{size}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000 \times 200}{4096} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 4882.81 \right\rceil = 4{,}883 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Note:** The two formulas differ because Step 2 forces row-aligned packing (no partial last row on a page). The ceiling formula in Step 4 gives the *theoretical minimum* assuming byte-level packing. The actual physical layout follows Step 2 (row-aligned), so $B_{table} = 5{,}000$ blocks.

**Step 5 — Total storage on disk.**

$$
S_{table} = B_{table} \times P_{size} = 5{,}000 \times 4096 = 20{,}480{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 19.53 \text{ MiB}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: B+-Tree Lookup Cost

**Problem Statement.** Build a B+-tree index on a column with $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ rows. Page size is $P_{size} = 4096$ bytes, index entry size is $E_{size} = 32$ bytes (8-byte key + 24-byte page pointer). Find the number of I/Os required for a unique-key equality search.

**Step 1 — Compute the order (fan-out) of the B+-tree.**

$$
F = \left\lfloor \frac{P_{size}}{E_{size}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{32} \right\rfloor = 128
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of leaf pages (L).**
Assuming each leaf page holds 128 index entries, and there are $1{,}000{,}000$ unique keys:

$$
L = \left\lceil \frac{N}{F} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{128} \right\rceil = 7{,}813 \text{ leaf pages}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the B+-tree height.**

$$
h = \left\lceil \log_{F}(L) \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_{128}(7{,}813) \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$
\log_{128}(7{,}813) = \frac{\ln(7{,}813)}{\ln(128)} = \frac{8.964}{4.852} = 1.847
$$

Therefore $h = \lceil 1.847 \rceil = 2$ levels.

**Step 5 — Total I/O cost for a unique-key lookup.**

$$
C_{idx} = h + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ disk accesses}
$$

The "+1" accounts for fetching the actual data page containing the row, after locating the leaf.

**Step 6 — Compare with linear scan.**

$$
C_{scan} = B_{table} = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \times 200}{4096} \right\rceil = 48{,}829 \text{ blocks}
$$

The index provides a speedup of:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{C_{scan}}{C_{idx}} = \frac{48{,}829}{3} \approx 16{,}276 \times
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: When to Choose an Index — Cost-Threshold Rule

**Problem Statement.** Decide whether to create an index on attribute `dept_name` of the `STUDENT` table ($N = 100{,}000$, $P_{size} = 4$ KB, $R_{size} = 200$ bytes). The query `SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE dept_name = 'CS'` runs $Q_{freq} = 1{,}000$ times per hour. The optimiser estimates that the index lookup will access **4 blocks**, whereas a full table scan accesses **4,883 blocks**.

**Step 1 — State the index-justification criterion.**

A secondary index is *warranted* if and only if:

$$
C_{scan} > C_{idx} \times Q_{freq\_weight}
$$

where $Q_{freq\_weight}$ is the frequency normalised against scan cost.

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
48{,}829 > 4 \times Q_{freq\_weight} \implies Q_{freq\_weight} < 12{,}207
$$

Since the query runs 1,000 times per hour, the cost difference vastly exceeds the maintenance penalty.

**Step 3 — Account for index maintenance cost on inserts/updates/deletes.**
Let $U_{freq}$ be the number of updates per hour. The break-even condition is:

$$
(C_{scan} - C_{idx}) \times Q_{freq} > C_{maint} \times U_{freq}
$$

where $C_{maint}$ is the per-update maintenance cost (typically 2–4 I/Os for updating leaf and possibly parent nodes).

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** If the read:write ratio exceeds **10:1**, the index is justified even with moderate write costs.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Physical Design Factor

| Workload Pattern | Recommended Physical Design Choices | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| High-frequency point queries on PK | B+-tree clustered on PK | $O(\log N)$ access, data co-located with index |
| Range queries on date column | B+-tree clustered on date + horizontal partitioning by month | Localises scans, enables partition pruning |
| Low-cardinality categorical filters | Bitmap index (DW) or B+-tree with include columns | Bitmap compresses well; B+-tree efficient with covering |
| Equality joins on foreign key | Hash index or B+-tree on join column | Enables nested-loop / hash join optimisation |
| Full-text / pattern searches | Inverted index, full-text engine | B+-tree cannot accelerate `LIKE '%pattern%'` |
| Updates dominating workload | Heap with minimal indexes | Each index adds maintenance overhead |
| Massive append-only timeseries | LSM-tree, columnar, time-range partitioning | Eliminates random writes, leverages compression |
| Geographic radius queries | R-tree, GiST | B+-tree is 1-D, R-tree is multi-D |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix above is a **KTU high-yield reference** — examiners frequently set case-study questions asking students to justify a physical design decision based on the workload pattern.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Multi-Factor Design Optimisation

**Scenario.** A library database has `BOOK(bid, title, author, genre, year, copies)` with $N = 5 \times 10^{6}$ rows, $R_{size} = 250$ bytes, $P_{size} = 8$ KB. The most frequent query is:

```sql
SELECT * FROM BOOK WHERE author = 'X' AND year BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010;
```

This query runs $Q_{freq} = 500$ times/hour. Decide the optimal indexing strategy.

**Step 1 — Compute table size in blocks.**

$$
B_{table} = \left\lceil \frac{5 \times 10^{6} \times 250}{8192} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 152{,}587.89 \right\rceil = 152{,}588 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Consider single-column index on `author`.**
Selectivity is poor (millions of distinct authors but only some have 5–10 books). Suppose 1% of books match a given author → returns $50{,}000$ rows. Index lookup cost is small, but reading the qualifying rows requires $50{,}000 \div \lfloor 8192/250 \rfloor = 50{,}000 \div 32 = 1{,}563$ blocks.

**Step 3 — Consider composite B+-tree on `(author, year)`.**
This index supports both equality and range predicates. A covering index (with `INCLUDE (title, genre)`) allows the query to be answered **without touching the table** (index-only scan).

**Step 4 — Compute composite index size.**
With key size $32 + 8 = 40$ bytes and pointer $8$ bytes, $E_{size} = 48$ bytes.

$$
F = \left\lfloor \frac{8192}{48} \right\rfloor = 170
$$

$$
L = \left\lceil \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{170} \right\rceil = 29{,}412 \text{ leaf pages}
$$

$$
h = \left\lceil \log_{170}(29{,}412) \right\rceil = \lceil 2.05 \rceil = 3
$$

**Step 5 — Total I/O for indexed query.**

$$
C_{idx} \approx h + (\text{qualifying leaf pages scanned}) = 3 + 30 = 33 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 6 — Speedup over full scan.**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{152{,}588}{33} \approx 4{,}624 \times
$$

The composite covering index is decisively the correct physical design choice.

This block diagram illustrates how each influencing factor is processed by the physical design engine and translated into a concrete storage specification.

```mermaid
graph TD

    q1 -- No --> skip[Skip indexing]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Selectivity less than 5 percent?}
    q2 -- No --> skip
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Read Write ratio greater than 10 to 1?}
    q3 -- No --> skip
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Is the column already indexed?}

    q4 -- No --> create[Create new index]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define *Physical Database Design*. List any **four factors** that influence it.

**Model Answer:**

Physical Database Design is the process of specifying the storage structures, access paths, file organisations, and indexing strategies used by a DBMS to implement a given logical schema on secondary storage.

Four influencing factors are:
1. **Query frequency** — high-frequency queries demand indexes and clustering.
2. **Data volume and growth rate** — determines partitioning and file-size estimates.
3. **Response time SLA** — dictates the choice between sequential scan and index-based access.
4. **Storage media characteristics** — HDD vs. SSD alters the cost of random I/O.

> **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between **clustered index** and **non-clustered index** with respect to physical design.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Clustered Index | Non-Clustered Index |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Physical row order | Data rows are stored in the order of the index key | Data rows are stored independently of the index |
| Number per table | Only **one** (data can be sorted in only one way) | **Many** (up to 249 in SQL Server, 64 in Oracle) |
| Lookup speed | Fastest, since leaf node IS the data page | Slower; leaf contains a pointer (RID) to the data |
| Effect on inserts | Inserts may require page splits and reordering | Inserts only update the index, not the data pages |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **workload-driven factors** that influence physical database design. Describe how a DBA performs workload profiling.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** For a table `ORDERS(oid, cid, odate, amount)` with $N = 2 \times 10^{6}$ rows, row size $R = 300$ bytes, page size $P = 8$ KB, design a physical storage layout assuming:
> - Query 1: `SELECT * FROM ORDERS WHERE oid = X;` runs 2,000 times/hour.
> - Query 2: `SELECT SUM(amount) FROM ORDERS WHERE odate BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31';` runs 50 times/hour.
>
> Compute the cost of serving Query 1 with and without a B+-tree index on `oid`.

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Workload-Driven Factors

Workload-driven factors form the **primary input** to physical design. They include:

1. **Query Frequency (f):** The number of times each query template is executed. Queries in the top 5% by frequency account for ~80% of total I/O cost.
2. **Query Type:**
   - *Point queries* (`WHERE pk = v`) — index-friendly.
   - *Range queries* (`WHERE date BETWEEN ...`) — B+-tree, partitioning.
   - *Aggregate queries* (`SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`) — favour clustering, columnar storage, materialised views.
   - *Join queries* — require indexes on join columns.
3. **Selectivity (S):** Fraction of rows returned. Indexes are profitable when $S < 0.05$ (rule of thumb).
4. **Transaction Profile:** Read-only vs. read-write ratio. Read-heavy (OLAP) and write-heavy (OLTP) systems have opposite optimal designs.
5. **Hot-Spots:** Frequently accessed data subsets drive clustering and partitioning decisions.

**Workload Profiling Process:**
1. Enable DBMS query logging (e.g., `pg_stat_statements` in PostgreSQL, `V$SQL` in Oracle).
2. Aggregate query texts by normalised template.
3. Compute execution frequency, average I/O, and CPU cost per template.
4. Rank queries by total resource consumption (Pareto analysis).
5. Identify candidate attributes for indexing from WHERE/JOIN/GROUP BY/ORDER BY clauses.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Design Solution

**Step 1 — Compute table blocks.**

$$
B_{table} = \left\lceil \frac{2 \times 10^{6} \times 300}{8192} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 73{,}242.19 \right\rceil = 73{,}243 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute B+-tree fan-out on `oid`.**
Index entry size $E = 4 + 8 = 12$ bytes (4-byte `oid` + 8-byte RID).

$$
F = \left\lfloor \frac{8192}{12} \right\rfloor = 682
$$

**Step 3 — Compute leaf pages and height.**

$$
L = \left\lceil \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{682} \right\rceil = 2{,}933 \text{ leaf pages}
$$

$$
h = \left\lceil \log_{682}(2{,}933) \right\rceil = \lceil 1.31 \rceil = 2 \text{ levels}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost of Query 1 without index (linear scan).**

$$
C_{scan} = 73{,}243 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 5 — Cost of Query 1 with B+-tree index.**

$$
C_{idx} = h + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 6 — Cost with buffer pool (assume hit ratio H = 0.7).**

$$
C_{scan}^{eff} = 73{,}243 \times (1 - 0.7) = 21{,}973 \text{ blocks}
$$

$$
C_{idx}^{eff} = 3 \times (1 - 0.7) = 1 \text{ block}
$$

The index remains overwhelmingly profitable. Speedup is $21{,}973 \times$ even with the buffer.

**Step 7 — Recommended Physical Design.**
- **Clustered B+-tree index on `oid`** (primary key) for Query 1.
- **Range partitioning by `odate`** (monthly) for Query 2; add a B+-tree on `odate` to support partition pruning.
- **Fill factor = 85%** on the clustered index to accommodate in-place updates on `amount`.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice).** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **system and hardware factors** that influence physical database design. How does the choice of **SSD vs. HDD** alter physical design decisions?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A relation `EMPLOYEE(eid, name, dept_id, salary)` has $N = 500{,}000$ rows, $R = 180$ bytes, $P = 4$ KB. The most frequent query is `SELECT * FROM EMPLOYEE WHERE dept_id = D;`, returning an average of **5,000 rows per query**, executed **200 times/hour**. Compute the cost with and without an index on `dept_id`. Recommend a physical design.

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — System and Hardware Factors

System and hardware factors include:

1. **Storage Media:**
   - **HDD** — high sequential throughput, expensive random I/O (seek + rotational latency ≈ 5–10 ms).
   - **SSD** — random and sequential I/O cost nearly equal (~0.1 ms latency).
   - **NVMe** — sub-100 µs latency, very high IOPS.
2. **Page/Block Size:** Larger pages (16–64 KB) favour full scans; smaller pages (4 KB) favour indexed access.
3. **Buffer Pool Size:** Larger buffers reduce physical I/O; affect index effectiveness.
4. **CPU and Memory Bandwidth:** Influence compression, encryption, and materialised view viability.
5. **Concurrency Control Implementation:** Lock granularity (page vs. row) impacts physical layout decisions.

**Impact of SSD vs. HDD on Physical Design:**

| Design Decision | HDD | SSD |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Indexing | Highly beneficial (random I/O is expensive) | Less critical, but still useful for sorts |
| Clustering | Essential for range queries | Less impactful |
| Partitioning | Important to reduce scan size | Still useful for manageability |
| Compression | Slower (CPU-bound) | Highly recommended |
| Fill factor | Higher (to reduce splits) | Lower (splits are cheap) |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Table blocks.**

$$
B_{table} = \left\lceil \frac{500{,}000 \times 180}{4096} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 21{,}972.66 \right\rceil = 21{,}973 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Index on `dept_id`.**
Suppose index entry size $E = 4 + 8 = 12$ bytes (4-byte `dept_id` + 8-byte RID).

$$
F = \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{12} \right\rfloor = 341
$$

Assume 50 distinct departments. Total index entries = 500,000.

$$
L = \left\lceil \frac{500{,}000}{341} \right\rceil = 1{,}467 \text{ leaf pages}
$$

$$
h = \left\lceil \log_{341}(1{,}467) \right\rceil = \lceil 1.30 \rceil = 2 \text{ levels}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost without index (linear scan).**

$$
C_{scan} = 21{,}973 \text{ blocks per query}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost with index on `dept_id`.**
Index traversal: $h = 2$ accesses. Then read qualifying rows: 5,000 rows / $\lfloor 4096/180 \rfloor$ = 5,000 / 22 = **228 blocks** (rounded up).

$$
C_{idx} = 2 + 228 = 230 \text{ blocks per query}
$$

**Step 5 — Cost per hour.**

$$
\text{No index: } 21{,}973 \times 200 = 4{,}394{,}600 \text{ blocks/hour}
$$

$$
\text{With index: } 230 \times 200 = 46{,}000 \text{ blocks/hour}
$$

**Speedup** = $4{,}394{,}600 / 46{,}000 \approx 95.5 \times$.

**Step 6 — Recommended Physical Design.**
1. **B+-tree index on `dept_id`** (non-clustered, secondary).
2. **Heap organisation** for the table (since no single column dominates range scans).
3. **Fill factor = 90%** for moderate update headroom.
4. **Consider clustering on `dept_id`** if Query 2 (by department) becomes even more frequent — clustering factor would drop from ~500,000 (unordered) to ~50 (one per department).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing logical and physical design.** Logical design is about *what* to store; physical design is about *how* to store it.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in index lookup cost.** Many students compute $h$ only and forget the data page fetch.
> 3. **Using absolute value `|x|` inside markdown tables** — this breaks table syntax in some renderers. Use `\vert x \vert` instead.
> 4. **Ignoring fill factor and page split cost** in update-heavy workloads.
> 5. **Stating that "more indexes = better"** — every index adds write overhead and storage cost; indexes must be *justified by the workload*.
> 6. **Not mentioning buffer pool effects** — the effective cost is $(1 - H) \times C_{raw}$.
> 7. **Mixing up clustered and non-clustered index limits** — a table can have *only one* clustered index.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Physical Database Design** translates logical schema into storage-level structures: files, pages, indexes, partitions, and clusters.
- **Five influencing dimensions:** Workload, Data, Performance, System, Operational.
- **Workload profiling** is the *first* step — query frequency and selectivity drive every subsequent decision.
- **Index justifiability criterion:** A secondary index is profitable if $C_{scan} \gg C_{idx}$ and the read:write ratio exceeds 10:1.
- **B+-tree lookup cost** for a unique key is $h + 1$, where $h = \lceil \log_{F}(L) \rceil$.
- **Fan-out** $F = \lfloor P_{size} / E_{size} \rfloor$.
- **Clustering factor** measures how well data rows are physically ordered to match the index; lower is better.
- **Fill factor** controls free space reserved on each page; 80–90% is typical.
- **Partitioning** (range, hash, list, composite) is driven by *data volume* and *hot-spot* distribution.
- **Buffer hit ratio** $H$ reduces effective I/O: $C_{eff} = C_{raw} \times (1 - H)$.
- **SSD vs. HDD:** SSDs reduce the penalty of random I/O, making some clustering decisions less critical.
- **One clustered index per table** — the data can be physically sorted in only one sequence.
- **Indexes cost storage and write overhead** — every index adds ~10–25% to table size and slows inserts/updates/deletes.
- **Denormalisation** may be warranted for read-heavy workloads but violates logical design principles.
- **Physical design is iterative** — use runtime monitors to feed back into the workload profiler.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always show the **cost calculation** in numerical answers; partial credit is awarded for the *method* even if the final number is off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Candidates:** Module 1 questions on "Factors Influencing Physical Database Design" typically appear as **3-mark definitions** or as **case-study-based 14-mark problems** requiring both *qualitative discussion* (factor categories) and *quantitative cost analysis* (B+-tree height, block calculations). Mastery of the formulas in §2.3 and the decision matrix in §3.4 is essential for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Database Tuning: Tuning the Conceptual Schema
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Database Tuning: Tuning the Conceptual Schema

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Database Tuning?

**Database Tuning** is the activity of making a database system (application, DBMS, hardware, OS, and network) run faster, more reliably, and with greater throughput. It is the *continuous, iterative process* of optimizing a database's performance across multiple architectural layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Database tuning refers to a set of activities designed to optimize and homogenize the performance of a database in response to specific workload requirements. It encompasses physical, logical, and conceptual adjustments to schema and queries."*

### The Three Levels of Database Tuning

| Level | Focus | Granularity |
|---|---|---|
| **Tuning the Conceptual Schema** | E-R design correctness, semantic accuracy | Highest (design level) |
| **Tuning the Logical Schema** | Normalization, integrity constraints, indexes | Middle (schema level) |
| **Tuning the Physical Schema** | Disk allocation, clustering, partitioning | Lowest (storage level) |

## 1.2 What is Tuning the Conceptual Schema?

**Tuning the Conceptual Schema** is the *highest-level* database tuning activity. It re-examines the Entity-Relationship (E-R) design to ensure that it correctly, completely, and efficiently represents the real-world enterprise. Decisions made here cascade down and influence both logical and physical schema design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** *"A well-tuned conceptual schema is the foundation upon which all subsequent tuning efforts depend. A flawed E-R design cannot be rescued by excellent physical tuning."*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint

Imagine you are building a **hospital complex**:

- **Conceptual Schema** = The **architectural blueprint** drawn by the lead architect. It decides:
  - *Which rooms exist?* (Entities)
  - *What are the walls and doors?* (Attributes)
  - *How do corridors connect wards?* (Relationships)
  - *Where is the ICU vs. General Ward?* (Specialization)
- **Logical Schema** = The **interior layout and material list** (concrete tables, columns, keys).
- **Physical Schema** = The **actual construction** (which tile, which elevator, plumbing routes).

If the architect places the **ICU on the top floor with no elevator backup**, no amount of excellent interior design or premium materials can fix the **emergency access problem**. Similarly, a poorly designed E-R diagram causes *fundamental* inefficiencies that no amount of indexing or denormalization can fully correct.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Spend **60% of design time** on the conceptual schema. It is the single most cost-effective performance investment.

## 1.4 Why Tune the Conceptual Schema?

A well-designed conceptual schema is essential because:

1. **Semantic Accuracy** — It must reflect the *real world* precisely. Misrepresenting an entity as an attribute (or vice versa) leads to data anomalies.
2. **Performance Foundation** — Wrong E-R choices cause expensive joins, redundant data, and update anomalies.
3. **Maintainability** — A clean E-R diagram simplifies future schema evolution.
4. **Reduced Redundancy** — Proper entity/attribute separation reduces duplication at the logical level.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tuning Process — A Structured Approach

Database tuning the conceptual schema follows a **top-down, iterative methodology**:

### Step 1: Re-examine the Enterprise Description
- Read the requirements specification carefully.
- Identify all **entity types**, **attributes**, and **relationship types** described in plain language.

### Step 2: Validate Entity vs. Attribute Decisions
- An **entity** is a thing that can exist independently and has its own identity.
- An **attribute** is a property describing an entity.
- **Rule of Thumb:** If the "thing" has its own properties, it is an entity; otherwise, it is an attribute.

### Step 3: Validate Entity vs. Relationship Decisions
- A **relationship** describes how two or more entities interact.
- If the interaction itself has properties (date, quantity, status), it is a relationship, not an attribute of either entity.

### Step 4: Validate Relationship Cardinality
- Decide between **1:1, 1:N, N:M** relationships.
- Avoid creating artificial intermediate entities when a direct relationship suffices.

### Step 5: Evaluate Binary vs. n-ary Relationships
- Generally, prefer **binary relationships** unless an n-ary relationship is semantically required.
- Convert n-ary relationships to binary only when it does not lose information.

### Step 6: Examine Specialization/Generalization
- Use **ISA hierarchies** to model "is-a" relationships.
- Avoid deep, fan-out hierarchies that complicate queries.

### Step 7: Verify Aggregation Choices
- Use **aggregation** when a relationship itself participates in another relationship.

### Step 8: Apply Normalization (3NF / BCNF / 4NF)
- Transform the conceptual schema into well-normalized relations.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Principle | Rule / Formula | When to Apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Entity vs. Attribute Test** | If *X* has its own descriptive properties, then *X* is an **entity** | When in doubt during E-R design |
| 2 | **Entity vs. Relationship Test** | If *X* has attributes like *date*, *quantity*, *status* | Interaction with attributes present |
| 3 | **Binary vs. n-ary Rule** | Prefer binary $(B)$; use n-ary only if $B$ loses info | Cardinality analysis |
| 4 | **N:M Decomposition** | Replace with **junction/associative entity** | Resolving many-to-many relations |
| 5 | **Normal Form Target** | Aim for $3NF \geq$ schema $\geq BCNF$ for OLTP; $3NF$ acceptable for OLAP | Logical design phase |
| 6 | **Redundancy Bound** | $\rho(R) = 0 \implies$ no lossy decomposition | Decomposition evaluation |
| 7 | **Lossless Join Condition** | $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow (R_1 \text{ or } R_2)$ | Verifying decomposition |
| 8 | **Dependency Preservation** | $(F^+ = \pi_{R_1}(F)^+ \cup \pi_{R_2}(F)^+ \cup \cdots \cup \pi_{R_n}(F)^+)$ | BCNF/3NF trade-off |
| 9 | **Specialization Constraint** | Disjoint: $D$, Overlapping: $O$; Total: $\uparrow$, Partial: $\downarrow$ | EER modeling |
| 10 | **Performance Metric** | $\text{Response Time} = T_{\text{CPU}} + T_{\text{I/O}} + T_{\text{Network}}$ | Tuning evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on vertical pipes:** In the table above, symbols like $\rightarrow$, $\cup$, $\cap$, and $\implies$ are rendered using LaTeX. The single character $\vert$ is reserved for absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Conceptual schema tuning is critical in:

- **Banking Systems** — Account vs. Customer modeling directly affects transaction throughput.
- **E-Commerce Platforms** — Order–Product–Customer ternary relationships must be carefully designed.
- **Healthcare Information Systems** — Patient–Doctor–Treatment modeling affects query performance and compliance.
- **Telecommunications** — Subscriber–Plan–Call modeling impacts billing accuracy.
- **IoT and Sensor Networks** — Device–Sensor–Reading modeling affects real-time analytics.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In *Distributed Systems*, the conceptual schema also influences **fragmentation** and **allocation** decisions. A poorly designed E-R model leads to inefficient distributed query processing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Tuning a University Database

### Initial (Unrefined) Conceptual Schema

Consider a **university** enterprise. The initial E-R design might naively be:

- **Entity:** `Student`
- **Entity:** `Course`
- **Relationship:** `Student` *enrolls in* `Course`
- **Attribute of `Student`:** `enrollment_date`, `grade`

### Step 1 — Identify the Conceptual Flaw
