The attributes `enrollment_date` and `grade` belong to the **relationship** `Enrolls_In`, not to either `Student` or `Course` individually.

**Why?** Because:
- A `Student` does not have *one* enrollment date — the date depends on which `Course`.
- A `Course` does not have a grade — the grade depends on the `Student`.

### Step 2 — Reformulate the E-R Diagram

The corrected E-R design becomes:

- **Entity:** `Student(SSN, Name, DOB)`
- **Entity:** `Course(CourseID, Title, Credits)`
- **Relationship:** `Enrolls_In(SSN, CourseID, EnrollmentDate, Grade)`

### Step 3 — Apply Normalization to the Relationship Schema

Starting relation:
$$ R(\underline{SSN}, \underline{CourseID}, EnrollmentDate, Grade) $$

The candidate key is `{SSN, CourseID}` and the primary key is the composite.

**Functional Dependencies (FDs):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FD_1 &: \{SSN, CourseID\} \rightarrow \{EnrollmentDate, Grade\} \\
FD_2 &: \{SSN, CourseID\} \rightarrow \{SSN, CourseID\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This relation is already in **BCNF** because the only non-trivial FD has a superkey on the LHS.

### Step 4 — Logical Schema Mapping

The resulting logical schema is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STUDENT}(\underline{SSN}, \text{Name}, \text{DOB}) \\
\text{COURSE}(\underline{CourseID}, \text{Title}, \text{Credits}) \\
\text{ENROLLS\_IN}(\underline{SSN}, \underline{CourseID}, \text{EnrollmentDate}, \text{Grade})
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common E-R Design Errors and Fixes

| # | Design Error | Symptom | Conceptual Fix | Performance Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Treating an entity as an attribute | Cannot represent NULLs, multi-valued properties | Promote to **entity** | Eliminates redundancy |
| 2 | Treating a relationship as an attribute | Loses cardinality information | Promote to **relationship** | Preserves semantic info |
| 3 | Using 1:N where N:M is correct | Cannot model cross-membership | Introduce **junction entity** | Prevents data loss |
| 4 | Forcing binary on a true n-ary relationship | Loses multi-party constraint | Use **n-ary relationship** | Preserves semantics |
| 5 | Over-normalization | Excessive joins, slow queries | **Controlled denormalization** | Faster reads |
| 6 | Under-normalization | Update anomalies, redundancy | Apply **3NF/BCNF** | Faster writes, integrity |
| 7 | Wrong primary key choice | Large key, slow joins | Use **surrogate key** | Faster joins |
| 8 | Overuse of specialization | Fan-out queries become complex | Limit to **2–3 levels** | Simpler queries |

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example: Normalization Walkthrough

### Initial (Bad) Schema

Suppose a course instructor naively designs:

$$ \text{COURSE\_OFFERING}(\underline{CourseID}, \text{Title}, \text{InstructorID}, \text{InstructorName}, \text{DeptID}, \text{DeptName}) $$

### Functional Dependencies

$$
\begin{aligned}
FD_1 &: \{CourseID\} \rightarrow \{Title, InstructorID, InstructorName, DeptID, DeptName\} \\
FD_2 &: \{InstructorID\} \rightarrow \{InstructorName, DeptID, DeptName\} \\
FD_3 &: \{DeptID\} \rightarrow \{DeptName\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Violation Analysis

The relation is in **1NF** but not in **2NF** (assuming candidate key is `CourseID`). It has **transitive dependency**:
$$ CourseID \rightarrow InstructorID \rightarrow DeptID \rightarrow DeptName $$

### Decomposition into 3NF

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COURSE}(\underline{CourseID}, \text{Title}, \text{InstructorID}) \\
\text{INSTRUCTOR}(\underline{InstructorID}, \text{InstructorName}, \text{DeptID}) \\
\text{DEPARTMENT}(\underline{DeptID}, \text{DeptName})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Verification: Lossless Join

The common attribute between `COURSE` and `INSTRUCTOR` is `InstructorID`. Since:
$$ \{InstructorID\} \rightarrow \{InstructorName, DeptID\} $$

The decomposition is **lossless** by the Heath's theorem.

### Verification: Dependency Preservation

All original FDs can be derived from the decomposed schemas. Therefore, the decomposition is **dependency-preserving**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 3-way decomposition reduces update anomalies and improves query performance for department-wide queries by isolating the `DEPARTMENT` relation.

## 3.4 Quantitative E-R Decision Table

| Decision | Test Question | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Is *X* an entity? | Does *X* have its own properties and identity? | Entity | Attribute |
| Is *X* a relationship? | Does *X* connect two or more entities? | Relationship | Re-examine |
| Is the relationship n-ary? | Does it require >2 participants semantically? | N-ary | Binary |
| Is aggregation needed? | Does a relationship participate in another? | Aggregation | Direct link |
| Specialization? | "Is-A" relationship exists? | ISA hierarchy | Keep flat |
| Normalize further? | Anomalies present in current NF? | Move to higher NF | Stay in current NF |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Three Levels of Database Tuning

This diagram illustrates the hierarchical relationship between the three levels of database tuning, with **Conceptual Schema Tuning** at the apex.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Tuning the Conceptual Schema] --> B[Tuning the Logical Schema]
    B --> C[Tuning the Physical Schema]
    A --> A1[E R Design Validation]
    A --> A2[Entity vs Attribute Analysis]
    A --> A3[Specialization Generalization]
    A --> A4[Aggregation Decisions]
    B --> B1[Normalization 3NF BCNF]
    B --> B2[Integrity Constraints]
    B --> B3[View Design]
    C --> C1[Index Selection]
    C --> C2[Clustering and Partitioning]
    C --> C3[Disk Allocation]
    A1 --> D[Optimized Database Performance]
    A2 --> D
    A3 --> D
    A4 --> D
    B1 --> D
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Iterative Process of Conceptual Schema Tuning

This flowchart shows the iterative methodology for tuning the conceptual schema.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Tuning Process]) --> step1[Step 1: Re examine Enterprise Description]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Identify Entity Types]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Identify Attributes]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Identify Relationship Types]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Validate Entity vs Attribute]
    step5 --> step6{Entity or Attribute?}
    step6 -->|Entity| step7[Step 6: Validate Cardinality]
    step6 -->|Attribute| step5
    step7 --> step8{N M Relationship?}
    step8 -->|Yes| step9[Create Junction Entity]
    step8 -->|No| step10[Step 7: Check Specialization]
    step9 --> step10
    step10 --> step11{Is A Relationship?}
    step11 -->|Yes| step12[Apply ISA Hierarchy]
    step11 -->|No| step13[Step 8: Apply Normalization]
    step12 --> step13
    step13 --> step14{Normal Form Achieved?}
    step14 -->|No| step15[Decompose Relation]
    step15 --> step13
    step14 -->|Yes| step16[Step 9: Validate Aggregation]
    step16 --> step17{Meets Requirements?}
    step17 -->|No| step1
    step17 -->|Yes| stop([Tuning Complete])
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: E-R Diagram Example (University Schema)

This diagram depicts a properly tuned conceptual schema for a university, showing entities, relationships, and the corrected placement of attributes.

```mermaid
erDiagram
    STUDENT {
        string SSN
        string Name
        date DOB
    }
    COURSE {
        string CourseID
        string Title
        int Credits
    }
    INSTRUCTOR {
        string InstructorID
        string Name
        string DeptID
    }
    DEPARTMENT {
        string DeptID
        string DeptName
    }
    STUDENT ||--o{ ENROLLS_IN : "registers"
    COURSE ||--o{ ENROLLS_IN : "has"
    INSTRUCTOR ||--o{ COURSE : "teaches"
    INSTRUCTOR }o--|| DEPARTMENT : "belongs to"
    ENROLLS_IN {
        date EnrollmentDate
        char Grade
    }
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Schema Tuning

This diagram shows how each tuning level feeds into the next, forming a layered architecture.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LEVEL1[LEVEL 1 CONCEPTUAL TUNING]
        L1A[Enterprise Requirements]
        L1B[E R Modeling]
        L1C[Semantic Validation]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL2[LEVEL 2 LOGICAL TUNING]
        L2A[Relational Mapping]
        L2B[Normalization]
        L2C[Constraint Definition]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL3[LEVEL 3 PHYSICAL TUNING]
        L3A[File Organization]
        L3B[Indexing Strategy]
        L3C[Query Optimization]
    end
    LEVEL1 ==> LEVEL2
    LEVEL2 ==> LEVEL3
    LEVEL3 ==> OUTPUT[Optimized DBMS Performance]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Database Tuning
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

> **Q1.** Define database tuning. List and briefly explain the three levels of database tuning.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Database tuning* is the set of activities performed to optimize the performance of a database system. The three levels are:

1. **Tuning the Conceptual Schema** (1 mark): Re-examines the E-R design to ensure semantic accuracy and proper entity-attribute-relationship classification.
2. **Tuning the Logical Schema** (1 mark): Applies normalization, defines integrity constraints, and designs views to reduce redundancy.
3. **Tuning the Physical Schema** (1 mark): Optimizes disk storage, indexing, clustering, and partitioning for faster I/O.

---

### Question 2: Conceptual Schema Tuning Principles
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

> **Q2.** What is meant by tuning the conceptual schema? Mention any four decisions that are validated during this process.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Tuning the conceptual schema* means reviewing and refining the Entity-Relationship (E-R) design so that it accurately reflects the real-world enterprise (1 mark).

Four key decisions validated are:

1. **Entity vs. Attribute decision** (0.5 marks) — ensuring independent things are modeled as entities.
2. **Entity vs. Relationship decision** (0.5 marks) — promoting interactions with properties to relationships.
3. **Binary vs. n-ary relationship decision** (0.5 marks) — using n-ary only when binary loses information.
4. **Specialization/Generalization decision** (0.5 marks) — applying ISA hierarchies appropriately.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Detailed Conceptual Schema Tuning
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

> **Q(A).** 
> **(a)** Explain in detail the process of tuning the conceptual schema with a suitable example. Discuss the guidelines for entity vs. attribute and entity vs. relationship decisions. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Differentiate between binary and n-ary relationships. With an example, show how a ternary relationship can be converted to binary when appropriate, and when it cannot. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The conceptual schema tuning process involves the following steps (1 mark each step, with example earning the 7th mark):

**Step 1: Re-examine the Enterprise Description**
Read the requirements carefully. Example: A *Library* enterprise has `Books`, `Members`, and `Loans`.

**Step 2: Identify Entity Types**
`Book`, `Member` are independent entities with their own identity.

**Step 3: Identify Attributes**
`ISBN`, `Title`, `Author` belong to `Book`; `MemberID`, `Name` belong to `Member`.

**Step 4: Identify Relationship Types**
`Borrows` connects `Member` and `Book`. Since `date_borrowed` and `return_date` are properties of the borrowing event, they belong to the `Borrows` relationship (1 mark).

**Step 5: Validate Entity vs. Attribute**
- Question: Is *Publisher* an attribute of `Book` or a separate entity?
- Answer: Since a publisher has its own attributes (name, address, contact), it should be a separate **entity** (1 mark).

**Step 6: Validate Entity vs. Relationship**
- Question: Is *Borrowing* an entity or a relationship?
- Answer: Because it has its own attributes (date, due date), it is best modeled as a **relationship** between `Member` and `Book` (1 mark).

**Step 7: Apply Normalization**
The `Borrows` relation `(MemberID, ISBN, DateBorrowed, ReturnDate)` is normalized to **BCNF** by ensuring all FDs have a superkey on the LHS (1 mark).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Binary vs. N-ary Relationships (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Binary | N-ary |
|---|---|---|
| Participants | Two entities | Three or more entities |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Convertible? | Yes (degenerate case) | Not always |

**Conversion Example (2 marks):**

A *ternary* relationship `Teaches(Instructor, Course, Student)` can sometimes be approximated by binary relationships if the ternary dependency is functional. However, if the relationship truly requires all three to be specified together, it cannot be reduced.

**When conversion is NOT possible (2 marks):**

Consider `Supplies(Supplier, Part, Project)` — a supplier supplies a specific part to a specific project. This cannot be decomposed into three independent binary relationships because the triple is the atomic unit of information.

---

### Question B: E-R Design Decisions and Normalization
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

> **Q(B).**
> **(a)** Explain the different design decisions in E-R modeling that affect the conceptual schema. Illustrate with examples how the wrong decision can lead to data anomalies. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the role of normalization in tuning the conceptual schema. Apply 3NF normalization to the following relation and verify lossless join:  
> `PROJECT(ProjectID, ProjectName, ClientID, ClientName, ClientAddress, DeptID, DeptName, ManagerID, ManagerName)` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Design Decisions in E-R Modeling (1 mark each):**

1. **Entity vs. Attribute**
2. **Entity vs. Relationship**
3. **Binary vs. N-ary**
4. **Aggregation**
5. **Specialization/Generalization**
6. **Key Selection**
7. **Cardinality Constraints**

**Examples of Anomalies (1 mark each for two examples):**

- **Wrong Example 1 — Treating Entity as Attribute:** If `Department` is an attribute of `Employee` (instead of an entity), you cannot represent a department with no employees (NULL problem) and you cannot store multiple department properties (name, location) efficiently.

- **Wrong Example 2 — Treating Relationship as Entity:** If `Marriage` between two `Person` entities is modeled as a third `Person` entity, you lose the natural pairing and introduce redundancy (1 mark).

**Consequences:** Insertion anomalies, deletion anomalies, and update anomalies arise at the logical and physical levels (1 mark).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Role of Normalization (2 marks):**

Normalization transforms a conceptual schema into a set of well-structured relations, eliminating redundancy and ensuring data integrity. It is a critical step in conceptual-to-logical schema tuning.

**Step 1: Identify FDs (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{ProjectID\} &\rightarrow \{ProjectName, ClientID, DeptID, ManagerID\} \\
\{ClientID\} &\rightarrow \{ClientName, ClientAddress\} \\
\{DeptID\} &\rightarrow \{DeptName\} \\
\{ManagerID\} &\rightarrow \{ManagerName\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Identify Transitive Dependencies (1 mark):**
- `ProjectID → ClientID → ClientName`
- `ProjectID → DeptID → DeptName`
- `ProjectID → ManagerID → ManagerName`

**Step 3: Decompose to 3NF (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PROJECT}(\underline{ProjectID}, ProjectName, ClientID, DeptID, ManagerID) \\
\text{CLIENT}(\underline{ClientID}, ClientName, ClientAddress) \\
\text{DEPT}(\underline{DeptID}, DeptName) \\
\text{MANAGER}(\underline{ManagerID}, ManagerName)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify Lossless Join (1 mark):**

For `PROJECT ⋈ CLIENT`, the common attribute is `ClientID`. Since `ClientID → ClientName, ClientAddress`, the join is lossless by Heath's theorem. Similarly, all other joins are lossless via `DeptID` and `ManagerID`.

**Conclusion:** The decomposition is in 3NF, lossless, and dependency-preserving.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing levels:** Students often mix up *conceptual*, *logical*, and *physical* schema tuning. Remember: *Conceptual* = E-R design; *Logical* = Normalization/Constraints; *Physical* = Indexing/Storage.
> 2. **Over-normalization:** Pushing every schema to BCNF/4NF/5NF without considering query performance is a common mistake. For OLTP systems, 3NF is often the practical sweet spot.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify lossless join:** Many students decompose but skip the verification step. Always check using Heath's theorem: $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_1$ or $R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_2$.
> 4. **Skipping examples:** In 14-mark questions, *examples carry 30–40% of the marks*. Always illustrate with a concrete scenario.
> 5. **Wrong cardinality notation:** Use $1:1$, $1:N$, $M:N$ notation carefully. A common error is writing $1:1$ for what should be $M:N$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Tuning the Conceptual Schema**

- **Database Tuning** is a *three-level* activity: **Conceptual → Logical → Physical**.
- **Conceptual Schema Tuning** focuses on validating the **E-R diagram** for semantic correctness.
- The **entity vs. attribute** decision is guided by: *"Does it have its own properties?"* → If yes, entity; else, attribute.
- The **entity vs. relationship** decision is guided by: *"Does it connect two or more things?"* → If yes, relationship.
- **Binary relationships** are preferred; use **n-ary** only when binary loses information.
- **N:M relationships** must be resolved by introducing a **junction (associative) entity**.
- **Specialization/Generalization** uses **ISA hierarchies** with constraints: *Disjoint (D)* / *Overlapping (O)* and *Total ($\uparrow$)* / *Partial ($\downarrow$)*.
- **Aggregation** is used when a relationship itself participates in another relationship.
- **Normalization** is a logical-level activity but is *guided* by the conceptual schema design. Aim for **3NF** in OLTP, **BCNF** when dependency preservation is non-critical.
- **Lossless Join Test:** $(R_1 \cap R_2) \rightarrow (R_1 \text{ or } R_2)$ — must hold for every decomposition step.
- **Dependency Preservation:** $(F^+ = \bigcup_i \pi_{R_i}(F)^+)$ — must hold for BCNF trade-offs.
- The **single most impactful design choice** is the **correct identification of entities**. All subsequent tuning flows from this.
- A **well-tuned conceptual schema** reduces the need for expensive denormalization at the physical level.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tuning Queries and Views - Impact of Concurrency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tuning Queries and Views - Impact of Concurrency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Query and View Tuning** is the systematic process of refining SQL queries and view definitions to minimize response time, reduce I/O cost, and improve overall throughput by exploiting indexes, statistics, query rewrite rules, and materialized views.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concurrency Impact on Tuning** refers to the performance degradation or behavioral anomalies that occur when multiple transactions execute simultaneously against a shared database, leading to contention on locks, latches, buffers, and undo/redo logs.

In the KTU 2024 framework, *Advanced Database Systems* treats tuning as a **multi-dimensional optimization problem** where the *best* query plan in a single-user environment may not remain optimal under high concurrency due to lock waits, buffer-pool churn, and contention on hot rows.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single-lane toll booth** on a highway. 
- When **one car** passes (single user), the toll collector processes it in 10 seconds — efficient, no waiting.
- When **50 cars** arrive simultaneously (high concurrency), cars queue up, the collector gets stressed, mistakes happen, and total time per car explodes.

**Database queries behave identically:**
- A perfectly tuned query on an *empty* database returns in milliseconds.
- Under heavy concurrency, the *same* query may wait for locks on rows, fight for buffer pool slots, and be blocked by other transactions — turning a 5 ms query into a 5,000 ms query.

The **toll booth** is your query execution engine. The **cars** are transactions. The **booth's waiting line** is the **lock queue**. The **booth's cash drawer** is the **buffer pool** (shared, contended memory).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for Students:** Tuning a query in isolation (single-user benchmark) is *insufficient* for production systems. You must always evaluate performance under realistic concurrent workload — this is the essence of "Impact of Concurrency."

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Concurrency Impact

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Lock Contention** — Transactions block each other waiting for row/page/table locks.
> 2. **Latch Contention** — Internal spin-locks on buffer pool hash chains, B-tree index pages.
> 3. **Logical Inconsistencies** — Phantom reads, non-repeatable reads, dirty reads that force query re-execution or result in wrong answers if isolation is misconfigured.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Tuning (KTU Board Favorites)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Query Response Time | $T_{resp}$ | milliseconds (ms) | End-to-end user-perceived latency |
| Buffer Pool Hit Ratio | $B_{hit}$ | percentage (%) | % of pages found in memory |
| Lock Wait Time | $T_{wait}$ | ms | Time blocked waiting for locks |
| Throughput | $\lambda$ | transactions/sec (TPS) | Concurrency capacity |
| Deadlock Rate | $R_{dl}$ | deadlocks/sec | Severity of lock conflicts |
| Index Selectivity | $S$ | ratio (0–1) | Fraction of rows filtered by index |
| CPU Time per Query | $T_{cpu}$ | ms | Pure processing cost |

> [!NOTE]
> Bolded in syllabus: **Buffer Pool Hit Ratio**, **Lock Wait Time**, **Throughput**, and **Index Selectivity** are the four metrics KTU examiners most frequently test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Query Tuning Workflow Under Concurrency

The KTU 2024 scheme structures tuning into **five sequential phases**. Concurrency awareness must be injected at *every* phase, not bolted on at the end.

### Phase 1 — Workload Characterization
- Identify **hot tables**, **hot rows**, and **hot indexes** using `STATSPACK`, `AWR`, or `pg_stat_statements`.
- Quantify the **read/write ratio** ($R:W$). Pure read workloads scale well with MVCC; heavy write workloads suffer lock contention.

### Phase 2 — Index Strategy
- Build **B+Tree indexes** on high-selectivity predicates.
- Use **covering indexes** to enable index-only scans, bypassing table heap access.
- Avoid **over-indexing**: each index slows down writes (DML) because every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must update the index.

### Phase 3 — Query Rewrite
- Replace correlated subqueries with **JOINs** (often 5×–10× faster).
- Push predicates down early (predicate pushdown).
- Eliminate `SELECT *` — fetch only required columns.

### Phase 4 — Materialized View Selection
- Pre-compute expensive aggregates (`SUM`, `COUNT`, `AVG`).
- Trade **storage space** and **refresh overhead** for **query speed**.
- Under concurrency, materialized views *eliminate lock contention* on base tables during read queries — a huge win.

### Phase 5 — Concurrency-Aware Execution
- Choose the correct **isolation level** (READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE, SNAPSHOT).
- Use **MVCC** (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) to give readers consistent snapshots without blocking writers.

## 2.2 Why Concurrency Degrades Query Performance — The 4 Root Causes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Cause #1: Lock Blocking**
> When transaction T1 holds a row lock and T2 requests the same lock, T2 suspends. The query response time of T2 increases by the wait duration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Cause #2: Lock Escalation**
> SQL Server (and others) may escalate many row locks into a single table lock, suddenly blocking *all* other transactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Cause #3: Buffer Pool Contention**
> Concurrent queries evict each other's pages, causing **buffer thrashing** — repeated disk I/O.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Cause #4: Log File Contention**
> The redo/undo log is a single serial resource. Heavy concurrent writes serialize at the log flush, becoming a bottleneck.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value notation in tables to avoid breaking markdown.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B_{hit} = \dfrac{H_{mem}}{H_{mem} + H_{disk}}$ | Buffer pool hit ratio (memory hits ÷ total hits) | Diagnose memory pressure |
| 2 | $T_{resp} = T_{cpu} + T_{wait} + T_{io} + T_{latch}$ | Total response time decomposition | Pinpoint bottleneck stage |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{tx}}{T_{window}}$ | Throughput = transactions ÷ time window | Measure capacity |
| 4 | $S = 1 - \dfrac{N_{distinct}}{N_{total}}$ | Index selectivity (cardinality reduction) | Choose best index |
| 5 | $C_{scan} = N_{blocks} \cdot t_{io}$ | Full table scan cost (blocks × I/O time) | Compare vs. index scan |
| 6 | $C_{index} = \log_{F} N + W \cdot t_{io}$ | B+Tree index cost (height + width pages) | Justify index choice |
| 7 | $C_{join} = C_{outer} + N_{outer} \cdot C_{inner}$ | Nested-loop join cost | Choose join algorithm |
| 8 | $C_{hash} = 3 \cdot (R + S)$ | Grace hash join cost (build + probe phases) | Two-pass hash join |
| 9 | $C_{sort} = 2N \cdot \lceil \log_{M-1} \lceil N/M \rceil \rceil$ | External sort-merge cost (passes) | ORDER BY / GROUP BY cost |
| 10 | $T_{MVCC} \approx T_{read\_snapshot}$ | MVCC read cost — independent of writers | Justify MVCC choice |
| 11 | $A_{amortized} = \dfrac{C_{refresh} + N_{reads} \cdot C_{query}}{N_{reads} + 1}$ | Amortized cost of a materialized view | Decide if MV is profitable |
| 12 | $P_{conflict} = 1 - (1-p)^{N}$ | Probability of at least one lock conflict | Estimate lock contention |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU exam, do not mix **cost** (logical estimate from formulas) with **time** (measured wall-clock). The formulas give cost in *page I/O units*, not milliseconds. Conversion requires knowing $t_{io}$, the per-page I/O time.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems** — Tune balance-query views to avoid lock waits during high-frequency transfers.
- **E-commerce Catalogs** — Use materialized views for product listings, refreshed asynchronously.
- **OLAP Data Warehouses** — Star-schema queries benefit from bitmap indexes and star-join optimization.
- **SaaS Multi-Tenant Systems** — Concurrency tuning determines how many tenants one DB instance can serve.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost Difference Between Full Scan and Index Scan Under Concurrency

**Problem Setup:**
A table `ORDERS` has $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ rows stored in $B = 10{,}000$ blocks. A query searches for orders with `status = 'SHIPPED'`, where 5% of rows match (so selectivity $S_q = 0.05$, i.e., 50,000 matching rows).

We compare three execution strategies and analyze the impact of concurrency factor $\alpha$ (number of concurrent transactions).

### Step 1 — Cost of Full Table Scan (No Index)

A full scan reads every block sequentially. Sequential I/O is fast, but it touches all 10,000 blocks.

$$
C_{scan} = B \cdot t_{io} = 10{,}000 \cdot t_{io}
$$

Under concurrency, each transaction competes for the buffer pool. The probability that a required block has been evicted by competing transactions grows with $\alpha$. Effective cost becomes:

$$
C_{scan}^{conc} = C_{scan} \cdot (1 + k \cdot (\alpha - 1))
$$

where $k$ is a contention coefficient (typically $0.05 \le k \le 0.20$).

### Step 2 — Cost of B+Tree Index Scan (Non-Unique, Status Column)

Assume the B+Tree on `status` has fan-out $F = 100$ and stores leaf pages $L$:

$$
L = \left\lceil \frac{N}{F \cdot F_{leaf}} \right\rceil \approx \left\lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{100 \cdot 50} \right\rceil = 200 \text{ leaf pages}
$$

Tree height (including root): 

$$
h = \lceil \log_{F}(N/S_q) \rceil + 1 = \lceil \log_{100}(20{,}000) \rceil + 1
$$

Since $100^{2.3} \approx 20{,}000$, we have $h = 3$.

Index traversal cost (B+Tree descent): $3$ page reads.

Matching leaf pages: 

$$
L_{match} = L \cdot S_q = 200 \cdot 0.05 = 10 \text{ pages}
$$

Each matching row requires a heap fetch (assume 1 row per block after compression, but let's say 5 rows/block average → 50,000 rows in 10,000 blocks → 5 rows/block). Matching rows: 50,000, blocks they occupy:

$$
B_{match} = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{5} \right\rceil = 10{,}000 \text{ blocks (worst case, scattered)}
$$

Realistic clustered estimate (assuming clustering factor $CF \approx 0.3$):

$$
B_{match}^{real} = B \cdot S_q \cdot (1 + CF) = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 1.3 = 650 \text{ blocks}
$$

Total index cost:

$$
C_{index} = h \cdot t_{io} + L_{match} \cdot t_{io} + B_{match}^{real} \cdot t_{io} = (3 + 10 + 650) \cdot t_{io} = 663 \cdot t_{io}
$$

### Step 3 — Cost of Covering Index (No Table Access)

If we add `status, order_date, total` all to the index, the query is satisfied *entirely* from the index — no heap access.

$$
C_{covering} = (h + L_{match}) \cdot t_{io} = (3 + 10) \cdot t_{io} = 13 \cdot t_{io}
$$

### Step 4 — Comparison Table

| Strategy | Cost (in $t_{io}$) | Speedup vs. Full Scan | Concurrency Robustness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Table Scan | 10,000 | 1× (baseline) | Low (touches all blocks → thrashes buffer) |
| B+Tree Index Scan | 663 | ~15× faster | Medium (heap fetches still cause contention) |
| Covering Index | 13 | ~770× faster | **High** (minimal buffer pollution) |

### Step 5 — Concurrency Multiplier Effect

Let $k = 0.10$, $\alpha = 10$ concurrent users.

Full scan concurrency cost:

$$
C_{scan}^{conc} = 10{,}000 \cdot (1 + 0.10 \cdot 9) = 10{,}000 \cdot 1.9 = 19{,}000 \cdot t_{io}
$$

Covering index concurrency cost:

$$
C_{covering}^{conc} = 13 \cdot (1 + 0.10 \cdot 9) = 13 \cdot 1.9 = 24.7 \cdot t_{io}
$$

The **speedup under load grows from 770× to ~770× (same ratio), but absolute degradation is much worse for the full scan.** This is the core concurrency insight: expensive queries degrade *catastrophically* under load.

## 3.2 Derivation: Amortized Cost of a Materialized View

A view $V$ is computed by aggregating a base table $T$ with $N$ rows. Without MV, every query $Q$ executes against $T$:

$$
C_{Q,noMV} = N \cdot c_{agg} \quad \text{(per query, e.g., GROUP BY cost)}
$$

With MV, the view is refreshed every $R$ queries. Total cost over $R+1$ queries:

$$
C_{total,MV} = C_{refresh} + R \cdot C_{Q,MV}
$$

where $C_{refresh} \approx 2N$ (scan + aggregate + write) and $C_{Q,MV} \approx 1$ (point lookup into MV).

Break-even condition (MV wins):

$$
(R+1) \cdot N \cdot c_{agg} > 2N + R \cdot 1
$$

Solving for $R$:

$$
R > \frac{2N - N \cdot c_{agg}}{N \cdot c_{agg} - 1} \approx \frac{2}{c_{agg}} \quad \text{(for } N \gg 1/c_{agg}\text{)}
$$

**Interpretation:** If each query would take $c_{agg} = 0.01$ cost units against the base, MV is profitable when the refresh interval is greater than ~200 queries. If the view is queried 1,000 times between refreshes, MV saves ~$1000 - 200 = 800$ cost units.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Concurrency Hazards by Query Type

| Query Type | Lock Risk | Latch Risk | Log Risk | Best Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Point Lookup (`WHERE pk = ?`) | Low | High on hot index | None | Covering index |
| Range Scan (`BETWEEN`) | Medium (range locks) | Medium | None | Partition pruning |
| Aggregate (`SUM`, `COUNT`) | High (scans many rows) | High | None | Materialized view |
| Join (2+ tables) | High (sort, hash spills) | High | None | Hash join with adequate memory |
| Update (`UPDATE ... WHERE`) | High (X-locks) | High | **High** | Batch updates, row-level locking |
| Insert (`INSERT`) | Medium | High on index | **High** | Bulk insert, disable indexes briefly |
| Delete (bulk) | Very High | High | **Very High** | Truncate, batched deletes |

> [!TIP]
> Read queries scale *almost linearly* with concurrency under MVCC. Write queries scale *sub-linearly* because of lock and log contention. This asymmetry is why read-heavy systems are easy to scale, but write-heavy systems (banking, inventory) require careful tuning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Query Execution Under Concurrency

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[Client Transaction T1] --> parser[SQL Parser]
    client2[Client Transaction T2] --> parser
    client3[Client Transaction T3] --> parser
    parser --> rewriter[Query Rewriter]
    rewriter --> optimizer[Cost-Based Optimizer]
    optimizer --> stats[(Table Statistics)]
    optimizer --> plan[Execution Plan]
    plan --> exec[Execution Engine]
    exec --> lockmgr[Lock Manager]
    exec --> bufferpool[Buffer Pool - Shared]
    exec --> mvcc[MVCC Version Store]
    lockmgr --> locktbl[(Lock Table - Shared)]
    bufferpool --> disk[(Disk - Data Files)]
    mvcc --> undolog[(Undo Log)]
    exec --> redolog[(Redo Log - Serialized)]
    exec --> resultset[Result Set]
    resultset --> client1
    resultset --> client2
    resultset --> client3
```

## 4.2 Concurrency Impact Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Query Arrives] --> check1{Is Query Read or Write?}
    check1 -->|Read| readpath[Read Path]
    check1 -->|Write| writepath[Write Path]
    readpath --> mvcc1{Isolation Level?}
    mvcc1 -->|Snapshot / MVCC| snapshot[Read from Version Store]
    mvcc1 -->|2PL / Lock-based| sharedlock[Acquire S-Lock]
    snapshot --> bufferR[Read Pages from Buffer Pool]
    sharedlock --> bufferR
    bufferR --> lockwait{Buffer Pages Latched?}
    lockwait -->|Yes| latchspin[Spin-Wait on Latch]
    lockwait -->|No| fastpath[Direct Access]
    latchspin --> bufferR
    fastpath --> resultread[Return Result]
    writepath --> xlock[Acquire X-Lock]
    xlock --> logwrite[Write to Redo Log]
    logwrite --> logserial{Log Buffer Full?}
    logserial -->|Yes| logflush[Force Flush - Serialize!]
    logserial -->|No| modify[Modify Buffer Page]
    logflush --> modify
    modify --> lockheld{Hold Lock Too Long?}
    lockheld -->|Yes| blockothers[Block Other Tx]
    lockheld -->|No| release[Release Locks]
    blockothers --> release
    modify --> resultread
```

## 4.3 View Tuning Strategy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Expensive Query] --> B{Queried Frequently?}
    B -->|No| C[Add Index or Rewrite]
    B -->|Yes| D{Result Set Small?}
    D -->|No| E[Use Partitioning]
    D -->|Yes| F[Create Materialized View]
    F --> G{Refresh Frequency?}
    G -->|High| H[Incremental Refresh]
    G -->|Low| I[Full Refresh on Schedule]
    H --> J[Query Rewriter Uses MV Automatically]
    I --> J
    C --> K[Validate with EXPLAIN PLAN]
    E --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Monitor Under Concurrency]
    L --> M{Performance Acceptable?}
    M -->|No| A
    M -->|Yes| N[Production Deploy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define materialized view and explain how it helps in query tuning under high concurrency.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **materialized view** is a database object that stores the pre-computed result of a query physically on disk, unlike a regular view which is virtual. **(1 mark)**
> Under high concurrency, materialized views help because: **(1)**
> 1. Read queries access the MV directly, avoiding expensive recomputation against base tables.
> 2. They **eliminate lock contention** on base tables since reads don't acquire shared locks on hot rows.
> 3. They reduce buffer pool pressure because MV pages are accessed repeatedly with high locality. **(1 mark for all three points)**

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three factors that cause query performance degradation under concurrent workload.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
> 1. **Lock contention** — transactions block each other on row/page locks.
> 2. **Buffer pool thrashing** — concurrent queries evict each other's pages, increasing physical I/O.
> 3. **Latch contention** — spin-locks on B+Tree index pages and buffer pool hash chains.
> (Alternative accepted: log file serialization, deadlock overhead, lock escalation.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **cost** (logical I/O estimate) with **time** (wall-clock duration). Always state units clearly. Also, do not skip the concurrency factor in your analysis — KTU 2024 explicitly tests concurrency awareness.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **query tuning techniques** for improving performance of expensive SELECT queries in a relational database. Discuss the role of indexes, query rewriting, and statistics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** How does **concurrency control** impact query performance? Explain the difference between **pessimistic** (2PL) and **optimistic** (MVCC) concurrency control in the context of query tuning. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Index-based Tuning (2 marks)**
- Create **B+Tree indexes** on columns used in `WHERE`, `JOIN`, and `ORDER BY` clauses.
- Use **covering indexes** (include all selected columns) to enable index-only scans.
- Example: `CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON ORDERS(status, order_date) INCLUDE (total);`
- Choose columns with **high selectivity** (selectivity near 1 means few duplicates, good for index).

**2. Query Rewriting (2 marks)**
- **Replace correlated subqueries with JOINs:**
  - Before: `SELECT * FROM Orders o WHERE customer_id IN (SELECT id FROM Customers WHERE country='IN');`
  - After: `SELECT o.* FROM Orders o JOIN Customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id WHERE c.country='IN';`
- **Avoid `SELECT *`** — fetch only needed columns to reduce I/O.
- **Use EXISTS instead of IN** for subqueries (often faster due to short-circuit).

**3. Statistics and Cost Estimation (2 marks)**
- Maintain up-to-date **histograms** and **cardinality estimates** on tables and indexes.
- The **cost-based optimizer** uses these to choose join order, join algorithm, and access path.
- Outdated statistics lead to **bad plans** (e.g., choosing full scan over index).

**4. Example Summary (1 mark)**
- A query `SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE status='SHIPPED' AND order_date > '2024-01-01'` benefits from a composite index `(status, order_date)`, with covering columns for index-only access.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Concurrency Impact Overview (2 marks)**
- Multiple transactions executing simultaneously create **lock contention**, **latch contention**, and **buffer pool pressure**.
- A query that runs in 5 ms single-user can take 5,000 ms under 100 concurrent users.
- **Lock waits** serialize what was supposed to be parallel work.

**2. Pessimistic Concurrency Control — 2PL (2.5 marks)**
- **Two-Phase Locking**: transactions acquire all locks before releasing any.
- **Readers block writers** and vice versa on the same row.
- High read-write contention → severe blocking.
- Example: In `READ COMMITTED` with 2PL, a long-running analytical query holding S-locks blocks all DML on those rows.

**3. Optimistic / MVCC (2.5 marks)**
- **Multi-Version Concurrency Control**: each row has multiple versions; readers see a consistent snapshot from a specific timestamp.
- **Readers never block writers** and **writers never block readers** — only writers conflict with writers.
- Excellent for read-heavy analytical workloads.
- Trade-off: **version cleanup overhead** and **storage for old versions**.

**4. Tuning Implication**
- For OLTP (write-heavy): prefer fine-grained row locking, short transactions.
- For OLAP (read-heavy): prefer MVCC, materialized views, higher isolation like SNAPSHOT.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain **materialized views** in detail. Discuss different **refresh strategies** (immediate vs. deferred, full vs. incremental) and how they interact with concurrent updates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **amortized cost** of using a materialized view versus querying the base table directly, given that the view is refreshed every $R$ queries. Show the break-even condition under which the materialized view is profitable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Materialized View Definition (1.5 marks)**
- A **materialized view** (MV) physically stores the result of a query. It is *not* recomputed on every access like a regular view.
- Useful for: aggregates, joins across large tables, denormalized snapshots.
- Example: `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_monthly_sales AS SELECT month, SUM(amount) FROM Sales GROUP BY month;`

**2. Refresh Strategies (3 marks)**
- **ON COMMIT** (immediate): view is refreshed when any base-table transaction commits. **Strong consistency**, but high write overhead.
- **ON DEMAND** / **ON SCHEDULE** (deferred): view is refreshed periodically or manually. **Lower overhead**, but reads may be **stale**.
- **FAST / INCREMENTAL refresh**: only changes since last refresh are applied using **materialized view logs**. Fast but requires logs.
- **COMPLETE / FULL refresh**: re-executes the defining query from scratch. Slow but correct.

**3. Concurrency Interaction (2.5 marks)**
- **Fast refresh** acquires row locks on base tables briefly → can block DML.
- **Full refresh** may hold long S-locks → severe contention.
- Solutions:
  - Use **partitioned MVs** so only affected partitions are refreshed.
  - Use **atomic refresh** via `DBMS_MVIEW.REFRESH` with `ATOMIC=FALSE` to truncate and reload (uses X-lock briefly).
  - Schedule refreshes during **low-traffic windows**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Base table has $N$ rows.
- Cost of a single query against base table: $C_{base} = N \cdot c_{agg}$ (where $c_{agg}$ is per-row aggregation cost).
- Cost of refreshing MV: $C_{refresh} = 2N$ (scan + aggregate + write).
- Cost of a single query against MV: $C_{MV} = 1$ (point/lookup).
- MV is refreshed every $R$ queries.

**Step 1 — Cost without MV over $R+1$ queries (including 1 refresh):** 
Wait — re-interpretation: the user runs $R$ queries between refreshes. Total system cost over $R$ queries:

$$
C_{noMV} = R \cdot N \cdot c_{agg}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost with MV over $R$ queries (1 refresh + $R$ reads):**

$$
C_{MV} = 2N + R \cdot 1 = 2N + R
$$

**Step 3 — Break-even condition ($C_{MV} < C_{noMV}$):**

$$
2N + R < R \cdot N \cdot c_{agg}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
R \cdot (N \cdot c_{agg} - 1) > 2N
$$

$$
R > \frac{2N}{N \cdot c_{agg} - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplification (for large $N$ and small $c_{agg}$):**

$$
R \approx \frac{2}{c_{agg}} \quad \text{(since } N \cdot c_{agg} \gg 1\text{)}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (2 marks):**
If $c_{agg} = 0.01$ (1% per-row cost), break-even occurs at $R > 200$ queries. If the view is queried 1,000 times between refreshes, savings = $1000 \cdot 0.01 \cdot N - (2N + 1000) \approx 8N - 1000$ cost units.

**Conclusion:** Materialized views are profitable for **frequently-queried aggregates** where refresh cost is amortized over many reads — especially valuable in concurrent systems where repeated base-table scans would cause lock contention and buffer thrashing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "view" with "materialized view"** — A regular view is virtual (re-executed every time); an MV is stored. **(−1 mark if confused)**
> 2. **Forgetting concurrency dimension** — Many students describe tuning as a single-user optimization. KTU 2024 scheme *explicitly* tests concurrency impact. **(−2 marks if missing)**
> 3. **Mixing units** — Saying "cost = 10 ms" instead of "cost = 10 page I/Os." Always state units. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps** — In Part B, you must show the *algebra*, not just the final formula. **(−3 marks for skipped steps)**
> 5. **Ignoring refresh strategies for MVs** — Simply defining MV is worth 1.5 marks, not 7. You must discuss ON COMMIT vs. ON DEMAND, FAST vs. COMPLETE. **(−3 to −4 marks)**
> 6. **Not linking to concurrency** — When discussing indexes, mention that indexes *speed up reads* but *slow down writes* (more locks, more log writes). **(−1 mark if absent)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1: Query & View Tuning + Concurrency Impact):**

- ☐ **Query tuning** = reducing I/O, CPU, and locking cost of a single query.
- ☐ **Index selectivity** $S = 1 - N_{distinct}/N_{total}$ — closer to 1 is better for indexing.
- ☐ **Covering index** = index that contains all columns needed by a query → enables index-only scan.
- ☐ **Materialized view** = physically stored query result; trades storage + refresh cost for read speed.
- ☐ **MV refresh strategies**: ON COMMIT (immediate), ON DEMAND (manual/scheduled), FAST (incremental), COMPLETE (full).
- ☐ **Concurrency hazards**: lock contention, latch contention, buffer pool thrashing, log file serialization.
- ☐ **2PL (pessimistic)**: readers block writers and vice versa → high blocking under load.
- ☐ **MVCC (optimistic)**: readers see snapshot, never block writers → ideal for read-heavy OLAP.
- ☐ **Cost formulas to memorize**: $C_{scan} = B \cdot t_{io}$, $C_{index} = h + L_{match} + B_{heap}$, $B_{hit}$ formula.
- ☐ **Amortized MV cost** break-even: $R > 2N / (N \cdot c_{agg} - 1) \approx 2/c_{agg}$.
- ☐ **Always test tuning under realistic concurrent load**, not single-user benchmarks.
- ☐ **Lock escalation** can suddenly turn row locks into a table lock — a concurrency killer.
- ☐ **Read queries scale linearly under MVCC; write queries scale sub-linearly** due to log/lock serialization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question says "explain the impact of concurrency," always structure your answer in **three layers**: (1) what causes the impact (locks, latches, logs, buffer), (2) how it manifests (slow queries, deadlocks, timeouts), and (3) how to mitigate (MVCC, materialized views, indexes, isolation level tuning). Examiners reward this structured approach with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed Database Concepts: Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Distributed Database Concepts: Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed Database (DDB):** A **distributed database** is a logically interrelated collection of data (and a description of this data) that is physically distributed over a computer network. The system is managed by a **Distributed Database Management System (DDBMS)** — software that transparently manages the distributed data while making it appear to the user as a single, unified database.

> [!NOTE]
> **DDBMS (Distributed Database Management System):** A software system that permits the management of a distributed database and makes the distribution transparent to the users. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme, a DDBMS is characterized by a set of properties known as the **"Twelve Rules of a Distributed Database"** (C.J. Date).

### 1.2 Twelve Objective Rules of a Distributed Database (C.J. Date)

A system qualifies as a fully distributed DBMS if and only if it satisfies the following **twelve rules**:

| # | Rule | Description |
|---|------|-------------|
| 1 | **Local Autonomy** | Each site is independent and can operate even when network is down |
| 2 | **No Single Point of Failure** | No single site failure halts the entire system |
| 3 | **Continuous Operation** | System runs 24/7 with minimal downtime |
| 4 | **Location Independence** | Users need not know where data resides |
| 5 | **Fragmentation Independence** | Users query without knowing data is split (horizontal/vertical) |
| 6 | **Replication Independence** | Users don't know about data copies |
| 7 | **Distributed Query Processing** | Queries are optimized across sites |
| 8 | **Distributed Transaction Management** | ACID properties maintained globally |
| 9 | **Hardware Independence** | Different hardware can coexist |
| 10 | **Operating System Independence** | Different OSs can coexist (Heterogeneity) |
| 11 | **Network Independence** | Various network topologies supported |
| 12 | **DBMS Independence** | Local DBMSs may be heterogeneous (Oracle, MySQL, etc.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Watch:** The **Twelve Rules** are a **sure-shot 7-mark Part B question** in the ESE. Memorize the rule names and their core idea.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Example)

**Imagine a large hospital chain with branches in Trivandrum, Kochi, and Calicut:**

- Each branch stores its own patient records (local data).
- Yet, a doctor in Trivandrum can instantly pull up a patient's allergy record from Kochi without knowing the server's physical location.
- If the Kochi server crashes, the Calicut server still operates (no single point of failure).
- A receptionist entering data in Trivandrum doesn't know whether the data is replicated to other cities (replication independence).

**That invisible "magic" of making distributed computers behave like ONE database is the DDBMS.**

### 1.4 Distributed Database — Formal Mathematical Definition

Let a distributed database $D$ be defined as a finite collection of fragments:

$$
D = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D_i
$$

Where:
- $D_i$ is the data fragment stored at site $i$
- $n$ is the total number of participating sites
- $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n$ are physically disjoint (or replicated) but logically unified

The system must satisfy the **Global Schema Mapping Function** $M$:

$$
M : S_{\text{global}} \rightarrow \{S_{1}, S_{2}, \ldots, S_{n}\}
$$

Where $S_{\text{global}}$ is the global conceptual schema and $S_i$ is the local schema at site $i$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Distributed Database System

A DDB has **four major components**:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Database (Storage Component):** The actual data stored at multiple sites.
> 2. **DBMS (Software Component):** The local DBMS software at each site.
> 3. **Network (Communication Component):** The physical/data-link/network layer connecting sites.
> 4. **DDBMS (Distributed Layer):** The software that orchestrates transparency across sites.

### 2.2 Distributed DBMS Reference Architecture (ANSI/SPARC 3-Level for DDB)

The classical **three-schema architecture** (External, Conceptual, Internal) is extended for DDB:

**Global Level (External + Global Conceptual):**
- Represents the unified view of the entire distributed database.
- Contains the **Global Conceptual Schema (GCS)** — the logical description of ALL data in the distributed system.
- Contains multiple **External Schemas** — user/application views.

**Local Level (Local Conceptual + Local Internal):**
- Each site has its own **Local Conceptual Schema (LCS)** — the logical organization of data at THAT site.
- Each site has its own **Local Internal Schema (LIS)** — physical storage of data at THAT site.
- A **Local Mapping Schema** translates GCS → LCS.

**Schema Hierarchy:**
$$
\text{External Schema} \rightarrow \text{GCS} \rightarrow \text{LCS} \rightarrow \text{LIS}
$$

### 2.3 Three Dimensions of Distributed Database Design (Kambayashi, 1981)

According to KTU 2024 syllabus, the architecture of a DDBMS is best understood along **three orthogonal dimensions**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimension 1 — Autonomy:** Refers to the degree of independence of individual DBMSs.
> **Dimension 2 — Distribution:** Refers to the physical placement of data across sites.
> **Dimension 3 — Heterogeneity:** Refers to the differences in hardware, OS, and DBMS software.

### 2.4 Classification by Autonomy

| Level | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| 0 | **No Autonomy** | Tightly coupled; single DDBMS controls all sites |
| 1 | **Loose Autonomy** | Federated systems — sites cooperate but retain local control |
| 2 | **Full Autonomy** | Each site operates independently; can join/leave system |

### 2.5 Classification by Distribution

| Level | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| 0 | **No Distribution** | Centralized — data at one site |
| 1 | **Client-Server** | Data at server, queries from client |
| 2 | **Peer-to-Peer** | Data and processing at every site |
| 3 | **Distributed** | Data fragmented/replicated across all sites |

### 2.6 Classification by Heterogeneity

| Level | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| 0 | **Homogeneous** | Identical DBMS at all sites |
| 1 | **Heterogeneous (Hardware/OS only)** | Different hardware/OS, same DBMS |
| 2 | **Heterogeneous (Different DBMS)** | Different DBMS software (Oracle + MySQL + PostgreSQL) |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Notation | Definition | KTU Weight |
|-------------------|----------|------------|------------|
| Fragmentation of relation $R$ | $R = \bigcup F_i$ | $R$ is divided into fragments $F_1, F_2, \ldots$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Completeness rule | $R = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} F_i$ | Each tuple of $R$ must be in some fragment | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Reconstruction rule | $R = \nabla (F_1, F_2, \ldots)$ | Fragments must reconstruct the original $R$ | ⭐⭐ |
| Disjointness rule | $F_i \cap F_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$ | No tuple appears in more than one fragment | ⭐⭐ |
| Cost of distributed query | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{CPU}} + T_{\text{I/O}} + T_{\text{comm}}$ | Total cost = CPU + I/O + Communication time | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Communication cost | $C_{\text{comm}} = C_{0} + C_{1} \cdot x$ | $C_0$ = fixed cost, $C_1$ = per-byte cost, $x$ = data size | ⭐⭐ |
| Replication factor | $r = \dfrac{N_{\text{copies}}}{N_{\text{original}}}$ | Number of copies including original | ⭐ |
| Transparency | $T_{\text{level}} \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ | 0=None, 1=Location, 2=Fragmentation, 3=Replication | ⭐⭐ |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems:** ATMs across India accessing the same customer account (replication + distribution).
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Data centers in Mumbai, Bangalore, Chennai with replicated inventory.
- **Telecom (Jio, Airtel):** Subscriber data fragmented by region for performance.
- **Cloud (AWS, Azure):** Global distributed NoSQL stores like Cassandra and DynamoDB.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Cost of a Distributed Query

In a distributed DBMS, executing a query involves three fundamental cost components. Let us derive the cost model from first principles.

**Step 1 — Identify the three cost components:**

When a query $Q$ executes across sites, the total time taken is the sum of:
- **CPU processing time** ($T_{\text{CPU}}$): Time to process tuples in memory.
- **I/O time** ($T_{\text{I/O}}$): Time to read/write from disk.
- **Communication time** ($T_{\text{comm}}$): Time to ship data between sites.

**Step 2 — Formulate the total cost equation:**

$$
T_{\text{total}}(Q) = T_{\text{CPU}}(Q) + T_{\text{I/O}}(Q) + T_{\text{comm}}(Q)
$$

**Step 3 — Communication cost model:**

The communication cost between two sites is the sum of:
- **Fixed initialization cost** ($C_0$): Time spent setting up the network call.
- **Per-byte transmission cost** ($C_1$): Time to transmit a single byte.

If a result of size $x$ bytes is transmitted:

$$
T_{\text{comm}} = C_{0} + C_{1} \cdot x
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the full distributed query cost:**

For a query that processes $n$ sites and ships a result of $x$ bytes:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{CPU}} + T_{\text{I/O}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_{0}^{(i)} + C_{1}^{(i)} \cdot x_{i} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Optimized semi-join cost reduction:**

A **semi-join** is used to reduce shipping cost. Suppose relation $R$ at site $S_1$ is joined with relation $S$ at site $S_2$:

$$
R \ltimes S = \pi_{A}(R) \bowtie S
$$

This projects $R$ to the join attribute $A$ first, ships it to $S_2$, performs the join, and ships the result back. The optimization reduces $x$ (data shipped) significantly.

**Step 6 — Fragmentation savings:**

If relation $R$ is fragmented as $R = F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \ldots \cup F_n$ and each fragment $F_i$ is at site $i$, the cost becomes:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T(F_i) + T_{\text{combine}}
$$

Where $T_{\text{combine}}$ is the cost of merging the partial results at the coordinator site.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Comparison

| Architecture Type | Coupling | Autonomy | Heterogeneity | Sites | Use Case |
|-------------------|----------|----------|---------------|-------|----------|
| **Centralized DB** | Tight | None | Homogeneous | 1 | Single office |
| **Client-Server** | Tight | Low | Homogeneous | 2-tier | Branch offices |
| **Peer-to-Peer DDB** | Tight | Low | Homogeneous | Multi | Multi-region enterprise |
| **Federated DB (Loose)** | Loose | High | Heterogeneous | Multi | Inter-bank systems |
| **Multi-Database (Tight)** | Tight | High | Heterogeneous | Multi | Inter-university research |
| **Cloud (Cassandra-style)** | Loose | Very High | Heterogeneous | Global | Web-scale apps |

### 3.3 Derivation: Completeness and Reconstruction Rules

**Given a relation $R$ fragmented into $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n$:**

**Rule 1 — Completeness:**
Every tuple in $R$ must appear in at least one fragment. The relation is the union of all fragments.

$$
R = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} F_i
$$

**Rule 2 — Reconstruction:**
The original relation $R$ can be reconstructed using a reconstruction operator $\nabla$ (union or join, depending on fragmentation type).

$$
R = \nabla(F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n)
$$

For **horizontal fragmentation** (using UNION):

$$
R = F_1 \cup F_2 \cup \ldots \cup F_n
$$

For **vertical fragmentation** (using JOIN on common key):

$$
R = F_1 \bowtie F_2 \bowtie \ldots \bowtie F_n
$$

**Rule 3 — Disjointness:**
For horizontal fragmentation:

$$
F_i \cap F_j = \emptyset \quad \text{for all } i \neq j
$$

For vertical fragmentation, the disjointness rule is relaxed to allow the primary key to be duplicated in all fragments.

### 3.4 Analytical Reasoning: Why Three Schemas?

**Reasoning Step 1:** Without schemas, a user query must specify the physical location of data — impractical for large distributed systems.

**Reasoning Step 2:** The **Global Conceptual Schema (GCS)** provides a single unified logical view of all data.

**Reasoning Step 3:** The **Local Conceptual Schema (LCS)** describes the local portion of data.

**Reasoning Step 4:** The mapping $M : \text{GCS} \rightarrow \text{LCS}$ allows transparency.

$$
\text{User Query} \rightarrow \text{External Schema} \rightarrow \text{GCS} \xrightarrow{M} \text{LCS} \rightarrow \text{LIS}
$$

This **decoupling** is what allows **location independence** and **fragmentation independence**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Schema Architecture for Distributed Database

```mermaid
graph TD
    userA["External Schema A"]
    userB["External Schema B"]
    userC["External Schema C"]
    gcs["Global Conceptual Schema GCS"]
    lcs1["Local Conceptual Schema Site 1"]
    lcs2["Local Conceptual Schema Site 2"]
    lcs3["Local Conceptual Schema Site 3"]
    lis1["Local Internal Schema Site 1"]
    lis2["Local Internal Schema Site 2"]
    lis3["Local Internal Schema Site 3"]
    db1["Site 1 Physical Storage"]
    db2["Site 2 Physical Storage"]
    db3["Site 3 Physical Storage"]

    userA --> gcs
    userB --> gcs
    userC --> gcs
    gcs --> lcs1
    gcs --> lcs2
    gcs --> lcs3
    lcs1 --> lis1
    lcs2 --> lis2
    lcs3 --> lis3
    lis1 --> db1
    lis2 --> db2
    lis3 --> db3
```

### 4.2 Distributed DBMS Reference Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph GCS_LAYER["Global Level"]
        gcsBlock["GCS Global Conceptual Schema"]
        ext1["External View 1"]
        ext2["External View 2"]
    end

    subgraph MAP_LAYER["Mapping Layer"]
        map1["Mapping Schema 1"]
        map2["Mapping Schema 2"]
        map3["Mapping Schema 3"]
    end

    subgraph LOCAL_SITE_1["Local Site 1"]
        lcs1["LCS 1"]
        lis1["LIS 1"]
    end

    subgraph LOCAL_SITE_2["Local Site 2"]
        lcs2["LCS 2"]
        lis2["LIS 2"]
    end

    subgraph LOCAL_SITE_3["Local Site 3"]
        lcs3["LCS 3"]
        lis3["LIS 3"]
    end

    gcsBlock --> map1 --> lcs1 --> lis1
    gcsBlock --> map2 --> lcs2 --> lis2
    gcsBlock --> map3 --> lcs3 --> lis3
    ext1 --> gcsBlock
    ext2 --> gcsBlock
```

### 4.3 Client-Server Architecture (Two-Tier DDB)

```mermaid
graph LR
    c1["Client Application 1"]
    c2["Client Application 2"]
    c3["Client Application 3"]
    svr["Central DDBMS Server"]
    storage["Data Storage Backend"]

    c1 --> svr
    c2 --> svr
    c3 --> svr
    svr --> storage
```

### 4.4 Peer-to-Peer Distributed Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1["Peer Site 1 with Local DBMS"]
    p2["Peer Site 2 with Local DBMS"]
    p3["Peer Site 3 with Local DBMS"]
    p4["Peer Site 4 with Local DBMS"]
    p5["Peer Site 5 with Local DBMS"]

    p1 --- p2
    p2 --- p3
    p3 --- p4
    p4 --- p5
    p5 --- p1
    p1 --- p3
    p2 --- p4
```

### 4.5 Federated Database System (Loose Coupling)

```mermaid
graph TB
    fed["Federated Global Schema Layer"]
    m1["Middleware Adapter 1 Oracle"]
    m2["Middleware Adapter 2 MySQL"]
    m3["Middleware Adapter 3 PostgreSQL"]
    db1["Local DBMS Oracle"]
    db2["Local DBMS MySQL"]
    db3["Local DBMS PostgreSQL"]

    fed --> m1 --> db1
    fed --> m2 --> db2
    fed --> m3 --> db3
```

### 4.6 Three Dimensions of DDBMS Architecture (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["DDBMS Architecture Design"]
    auto["Dimension 1 Autonomy"]
    dist["Dimension 2 Distribution"]
    hetero["Dimension 3 Heterogeneity"]

    auto --> a1["No Autonomy Tightly Coupled"]
    auto --> a2["Loose Autonomy Federated"]
    auto --> a3["Full Autonomy Independent"]

    dist --> d1["No Distribution Centralized"]
    dist --> d2["Client Server"]
    dist --> d3["Peer to Peer"]
    dist --> d4["Full Distribution"]

    hetero --> h1["Homogeneous"]
    hetero --> h2["Heterogeneous HW or OS"]
    hetero --> h3["Heterogeneous DBMS"]

    root --> auto
    root --> dist
    root --> hetero
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Distributed Database. Mention any four characteristics of a DDBMS.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> A **Distributed Database (DDB)** is a logically interrelated collection of data spread physically across multiple sites connected by a network, but managed in such a way that it appears as a single unified database to the user.
>
> **Four Characteristics:**
> 1. **Location Independence** — User need not know where data resides.
> 2. **Replication Independence** — Users are unaware of data copies.
> 3. **Fragmentation Independence** — Logical data is divided and stored.
> 4. **Distributed Query Processing** — Queries optimized across sites.
>
> **[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 × 4 for characteristics]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Distributed Databases (3 marks).**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Feature | Homogeneous DDB | Heterogeneous DDB |
> |---------|----------------|-------------------|
> | **DBMS Software** | Same at all sites | Different DBMSs (Oracle, MySQL, etc.) |
> | **Local Autonomy** | Low — controlled by single DDBMS | High — each site has its own DBMS |
> | **Heterogeneity** | Only in hardware/OS | Different hardware, OS, AND DBMS |
> | **Translator** | Not required | Middleware/gateways needed |
> | **Example** | Oracle Replicated DB across 3 servers | Bank integrating Oracle + DB2 + MySQL |
>
> **[3 Marks: 1 for table + 2 for valid points]**

---

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a) Explain the three-schema reference architecture of a Distributed DBMS with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> The three-schema architecture (also called the **ANSI/SPARC architecture**) is extended for DDB to include both global and local levels.
>
> **Three Levels:**
>
> 1. **Global Level:**
>    - **External Schemas (ES):** User-specific views of the distributed database.
>    - **Global Conceptual Schema (GCS):** The unified logical view of ALL data spread across the network. It hides the physical distribution.
>
> 2. **Local Level:**
>    - **Local Conceptual Schema (LCS):** Describes the data at a particular site logically.
>    - **Local Internal Schema (LIS):** Describes the physical storage of data at that site.
>
> 3. **Mapping Schemas:**
>    - The **GCS-to-LCS mapping** translates global logical descriptions into local logical descriptions.
>    - The **LCS-to-LIS mapping** translates logical to physical.
>
> **Advantages:**
> - Provides **data independence** (logical and physical).
> - Provides **distribution transparency**.
> - Allows different sites to use different DBMSs (heterogeneity support).
>
> ```
> External Schema → GCS → [Mapping] → LCS → LIS → Physical Storage
> ```
>
> **[7 Marks: 2 for levels + 2 for diagram + 2 for mapping + 1 for advantages]**

---

**(b) Discuss the twelve rules of a Distributed Database as defined by C.J. Date. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> C.J. Date defined **twelve rules** to characterize a *true* distributed DBMS:
>
> 1. **Local Autonomy** — Each site operates independently; can run even if network fails.
> 2. **No Single Site Dependency** — Operations don't rely on one central site.
> 3. **Continuous Operation** — System never stops; minimal planned downtime.
> 4. **Location Independence** — User queries without knowing physical site.
> 5. **Fragmentation Independence** — User queries without knowing data is fragmented.
> 6. **Replication Independence** — User queries without knowing data is copied.
> 7. **Distributed Query Processing** — System optimizes queries across sites.
> 8. **Distributed Transaction Processing** — ACID maintained across sites (2PC protocol).
> 9. **Hardware Independence** — Different hardware at different sites.
> 10. **Operating System Independence** — Heterogeneous OSs coexist.
> 11. **Network Independence** — Different network types supported.
> 12. **DBMS Independence** — Local DBMSs can be heterogeneous (Oracle + MySQL).
>
> **Significance:** Any system claiming to be a true DDBMS must satisfy ALL twelve rules.
>
> **[7 Marks: 0.5 × 12 = 6 marks for rules + 1 for explanation of significance]**

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the Client-Server and Peer-to-Peer architectures of a Distributed Database with diagrams. Compare their advantages. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Client-Server Architecture:**
> - A central **server** holds the database.
> - Multiple **clients** issue queries to the server via the network.
> - Server processes the query and returns results.
> - **Pros:** Centralized control, easy backup, simple security.
> - **Cons:** Single point of failure, network bottleneck.
>
> ```
> Client 1 → |
> Client 2 → | → DDBMS Server → Storage
> Client 3 → |
> ```
>
> **Peer-to-Peer Architecture:**
> - Every site is a **peer** — has its own DBMS and processing capability.
> - Sites cooperate to execute distributed queries.
> - No central coordinator; data is fragmented and replicated.
> - **Pros:** No single point of failure, high scalability, parallel processing.
> - **Cons:** Complex coordination, harder to maintain ACID globally.
>
> ```
> Peer A ↔ Peer B ↔ Peer C
>  ↕         ↕
> Peer D ↔ Peer E
> ```
>
> **Comparison:**
>
> | Aspect | Client-Server | Peer-to-Peer |
> |--------|---------------|--------------|
> | Control | Centralized | Distributed |
> | Failure | Server crash = total failure | Failure-tolerant |
> | Cost | Lower initial | Higher coordination cost |
> | Scalability | Limited | Highly scalable |
>
> **[7 Marks: 2 + 2 for diagrams + 3 for comparison]**

---

**(b) Describe the three dimensions of DDBMS architecture: Autonomy, Distribution, and Heterogeneity with examples. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> The architecture of a DDBMS is best characterized by three orthogonal dimensions (Kambayashi):
>
> **1. Autonomy (Degree of Independence):**
> - **No Autonomy:** A single DDBMS controls all sites — e.g., Oracle RAC cluster.
> - **Loose Autonomy:** Sites cooperate but retain local control — e.g., Federated DB.
> - **Full Autonomy:** Sites fully independent — e.g., Multi-DB on the internet.
>
> **2. Distribution (Physical Placement of Data):**
> - **No Distribution:** Centralized — e.g., a single mainframe DB.
> - **Client-Server:** Data at server, queries from clients.
> - **Peer-to-Peer:** Data and processing at every site.
> - **Full Distribution:** Data fragmented/replicated at all sites.
>
> **3. Heterogeneity (Differences in Components):**
> - **Homogeneous:** All sites have identical hardware, OS, and DBMS.
> - **Hardware/OS Heterogeneity:** Different hardware/OS, same DBMS.
> - **DBMS Heterogeneity:** Different DBMSs at different sites (Oracle + MySQL + DB2).
>
> **Example:** A multinational bank with branches in India, USA, and UK where each branch uses local DBMS, different currencies, and different time zones → **High Autonomy + Full Distribution + High Heterogeneity**.
>
> **[7 Marks: 2 + 2 + 2 for three dimensions + 1 for example]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the three-schema architecture with the three-tier architecture** (Presentation, Logic, Data). They are DIFFERENT.
> 2. **Do NOT list fewer than 12 rules** when asked about C.J. Date's rules — partial marking is severe.
> 3. **Always draw the GCS-to-LCS mapping** in diagrams; examiners specifically look for it.
> 4. **Avoid writing "DBMS" instead of "DDBMS"** — this is a costly 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **For client-server, mention "request-response" model** — partial credit depends on it.
> 6. **For P2P, mention "no central coordinator"** — key distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Distributed Database Architecture**

- **DDB** = logically unified, **physically distributed** database.
- **DDBMS** = software that manages the DDB with **distribution transparency**.
- **Twelve Rules (C.J. Date):** Local Autonomy, No Single Point of Failure, Continuous Operation, Location Independence, Fragmentation Independence, Replication Independence, Distributed Query Processing, Distributed Transaction Management, Hardware/OS/Network/DBMS Independence.
- **Three-Schema Architecture:** External → GCS → LCS → LIS.
- **GCS** = global unified view; **LCS** = local logical view; **LIS** = local physical view.
- **Three Dimensions:** Autonomy, Distribution, Heterogeneity.
- **Autonomy Levels:** None, Loose, Full.
- **Distribution Levels:** Centralized → Client-Server → Peer-to-Peer → Fully Distributed.
- **Heterogeneity Levels:** Homogeneous → HW/OS Heterogeneous → DBMS Heterogeneous.
- **Client-Server:** Central server holds data; clients query remotely.
- **Peer-to-Peer:** Every site is a peer with its own DBMS and processing.
- **Federated DB:** Loosely coupled heterogeneous DBs with a unified global view.
- **Cost Model:** $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{CPU}} + T_{\text{I/O}} + T_{\text{comm}}$.
- **Communication Cost:** $C_{\text{comm}} = C_0 + C_1 \cdot x$ (fixed + per-byte).
- **Fragmentation Rules:** Completeness, Reconstruction, Disjointness.
- **Horizontal Fragmentation:** Uses UNION for reconstruction; disjoint.
- **Vertical Fragmentation:** Uses JOIN on common key for reconstruction; primary key duplicated.
- **KTU 2024 Exam Weightage:** 14 marks (one full Part B question guaranteed).
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing three-schema with three-tier — they are NOT the same.
- **Golden Sentence for Examiner:** "The DDBMS makes distribution **transparent** to the user via the GCS."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed Data Storage: Data Fragmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Distributed Data Storage: Data Fragmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **Distributed Database Management System (DDBMS)**, the total database is the logical union of all participating sub-databases stored at geographically dispersed sites over a computer network. **Data Fragmentation** is the foundational storage design strategy wherein a global logical relation is decomposed into smaller, logical, and manageable partitions called **fragments**, which are then physically allocated to one or more sites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Ceri & Pelagatti Paradigm):**
> Data fragmentation is the process of *breaking the logical schema of a global relation $R$* into disjoint logical fragments $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$, such that the application program operates on the fragments as if they were the original relation, while the *Distribution Layer* of the DDBMS transparently reconstructs the global view.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) for Advanced Database Systems, fragmentation is studied as a critical phase of the **Distributed Database Design Methodology** that sits between conceptual schema design and physical data allocation.

### 1.2 The Three Canonical Fragmentation Types

| Fragmentation Type | What is Decomposed | Primary Key Behavior | Reconstructive Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Fragmentation (HF)** | Set of Tuples (Rows) | Preserved in every fragment | **Union** ($\cup$) |
| **Vertical Fragmentation (VF)** | Set of Attributes (Columns) | Replicated as a tuple-id in every fragment | **Natural Join** ($\bowtie$) |
| **Hybrid / Mixed Fragmentation (MF)** | Combination of rows and columns | Navigated via tuple-id and predicate | **Union + Join** |

> [!NOTE]
> A relation that is **not fragmented** but kept whole at a single site is known as the **"No Fragmentation"** or **Whole Relation** case, generally considered a degenerate baseline against which fragmentation performance is measured.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Branch Network" Intuition

Imagine the **State Central Library of Kerala (Thiruvananthapuram)** maintains a master catalog of **10 million books**. Rather than forcing every citizen to travel to the capital, the library system implements a smart **decentralization** strategy:

- **Horizontal Fragmentation Analogy** — Each district branch (Kochi, Kozhikode, Thrissur) gets a *subset of books*. The Kochi branch holds only Malayalam literature; the Trivandrum branch holds technical journals. *A book exists at exactly one branch by its full set of attributes (author, title, year, ISBN, pages).* When you ask "give me all the books," the central system fetches "all branches" and performs a **UNION** of their catalogs.
- **Vertical Fragmentation Analogy** — Instead of splitting books, the system splits the *information about each book* across branches. One branch stores only the *bibliographic metadata* (title, author), another stores *physical inventory data* (shelf number, condition), and another stores *circulation data* (borrower, due date). To get the "complete book record," the system performs a **JOIN** on a common book-id.
- **Hybrid Fragmentation** — The Kochi branch stores only the *bibliographic metadata* of *Malayalam books*. This is a horizontal cut first, then a vertical cut.

This is precisely how distributed databases treat relations, with the **Branch** acting as a **Site** and the **Book** acting as a **Tuple**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Fragment At All?**
> Fragmentation reduces **query response time** (data accessed at the local site), lowers **network bandwidth consumption**, improves **availability**, enables **parallelism**, and supports **load balancing** across sites.

---

### 1.4 Why Fragmentation? — The KTU 2024 Motivation

The decision to fragment is governed by the **"Reason for Fragmentation"** principle. KTU examiners expect students to list at least three valid justifications:

1. **Locality of Reference** — Applications typically access only subsets of a relation. Fragmenting along access patterns localizes the relevant data.
2. **Efficiency** — Data is stored close to where it is most heavily used, reducing transmission cost.
3. **Parallelism** — A single query can be executed in parallel across fragments at different sites.
4. **Security / Privacy** — Sensitive attributes (e.g., salary) can be isolated in a vertical fragment at a trusted site.
5. **Disconnected Operation** — Local fragments allow a site to function even during network partition.

---

### 1.5 The Three Correctness Rules of Fragmentation (Ceri-Pelagatti Axioms)

For any fragmentation scheme to be **semantically valid**, it must satisfy three non-negotiable rules. These are **guaranteed short questions (3 marks)** in every KTU exam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Correctness Rules:**
> 1. **Completeness** — Every data item (tuple/attribute) of the original global relation $R$ must be present in *at least one* fragment.
> 2. **Reconstruction** — It must always be possible to reconstruct the original relation $R$ from its fragments using a well-defined relational operation (Union for HF, Join for VF).
> 3. **Disjointness** — For horizontal fragmentation, no tuple appears in more than one fragment (except the primary key column in vertical fragmentation, which is replicated as a *tuple-identifier*). For vertical fragmentation, no non-key attribute appears in more than one fragment.

Let us formally state these:

Let $R$ be a global relation and $F_R = \{R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n\}$ be its fragment set.

$$\text{Completeness: } \forall \text{ data item } d \in R, \exists R_i \in F_R \mid d \in R_i$$

$$\text{Reconstruction (HF): } R = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

$$\text{Reconstruction (VF): } R = \Join_{i=1}^{n} R_i \quad \text{on } tid$$

$$\text{Disjointness (HF): } R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset \quad \forall i \neq j$$

$$\text{Disjointness (VF): } R_i \cap R_j = \text{attribute } tid \quad \forall i \neq j$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Horizontal Fragmentation (HF) — The Row-Wise Partition
