Horizontal fragmentation divides a relation $R$ based on a **selection predicate** (or a set of predicates combined using Boolean logic). Each fragment $R_i$ contains a subset of tuples from $R$ that satisfy a specific predicate $p_i$.

$$R_i = \sigma_{p_i}(R)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Horizontal Fragmentation** is driven by predicates applied directly on the relation $R$. **Derived Horizontal Fragmentation** is driven by a *semi-join* from a related relation (typically a 1:N relationship), ensuring referential integrity between fragments across sites.

#### 2.1.1 Information Requirements for HF

The KTU syllabus (Ozsu & Valduriez framework) mandates that primary horizontal fragmentation requires the following inputs:

| Input | Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Predicate** | $p_i$ | An atomic condition of the form `Attribute op Value` |
| **Minterm Predicate** | $m_i$ | A conjunction of simple predicates: $m_i = \bigwedge p_j$ |
| **Set of Minterms** | $M = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_z\}$ | All distinct minterms from $n$ simple predicates |
| **Application Predicate Set** | $Pr = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n\}$ | Predicates actually used in user queries |

The number of possible minterms grows as $2^n$ for $n$ simple predicates, but most are irrelevant. The key filtering rule is the **Minterm Selectivity Formula**:

$$\text{Selectivity of } m_i = \frac{\mid \sigma_{m_i}(R) \mid}{\mid R \mid}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Minimality Rule (Completeness + Disjointness for HF):**
> A minterm predicate $m_i$ is said to be *relevant* if there exists at least one application that accesses it differently from another minterm $m_j$ that subsumes the same data. The fragmentation is **minimal** if no fragment can be derived from a more complex predicate that is subsumed by a simpler one.

#### 2.1.2 The PHF (Primary Horizontal Fragmentation) Algorithm — KTU High-Yield

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algorithm: COM_MIN (Ceri-Pelagatti) — Generating Minterm Predicates**
> - **Input:** Relation $R$ and set of simple predicates $Pr$
> - **Output:** Set of minterm predicates $M$ that are *relevant* and *complete*
> - **Step 1:** For each $p \in Pr$, partition $R$ into $R^+$ (tuples satisfying $p$) and $R^-$ (tuples not satisfying $p$). Eliminate predicates where both partitions are accessed by the *same* applications — these are *non-relevant*.
> - **Step 2:** Generate the minterm set $M$ from the *relevant* simple predicates.
> - **Step 3:** Eliminate contradictory minterms (those yielding an empty result).
> - **Step 4:** Eliminate minterms that are *implied* by others, retaining only the most selective ones.

#### 2.1.3 Derived Horizontal Fragmentation (DHF)

Used when relation $R$ is the *owner* of a link (1:N relationship) and relation $S$ is the *member*. Fragments of $S$ are derived from the fragments of $R$.

$$S_i = R_i \ltimes S = \sigma_{R_i.\text{join_attr} = S.\text{join_attr}}(R \times S)$$

Or more precisely:

$$S_i = S \ltimes R_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> The semi-join $\ltimes$ ensures that $S_i$ contains only those member tuples whose owner tuple exists in $R_i$. This guarantees **referential integrity** even when fragments are distributed across sites.

#### 2.1.4 Check Fragmentation — A Common KTU Sub-Question

After deriving fragments, the **CHECK** operation verifies the correctness rules:

$$\text{CHECK}_i = \{q \in Q \mid \text{query } q \text{ has predicate } m_i\}$$

A horizontal fragmentation is **correct** if and only if:

$$\forall \text{ query } q \in Q, \exists \text{ fragment } R_i \in F_R \mid \text{access}(q) \subseteq \text{CHECK}_i$$

---

### 2.2 Vertical Fragmentation (VF) — The Column-Wise Partition

Vertical fragmentation divides a relation $R$ based on **attribute affinity** — i.e., the tendency of attributes to be accessed together by the same application. Each fragment $R_i$ contains a subset of attributes from $R$ plus the **primary key (replicated as a tuple-id $tid$)**.

$$R = \pi_{A_1 \cup tid}(R_1) \bowtie \pi_{A_2 \cup tid}(R_2) \bowtie \cdots \bowtie \pi_{A_n \cup tid}(R_n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *tuple-identifier (tid)* is the hidden thread that makes the natural join lossless during reconstruction. **Without the tid, the natural join would suffer from spurious tuples** — a fatal violation of the Reconstruction rule.

#### 2.2.1 The Attribute Affinity Matrix (AAM) — A KTU 2024 Favourite

Given a set of applications $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \dots, q_m\}$ and a set of attributes $A = \{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n\}$, we construct a matrix $AA$ where each entry is the **affinity between two attributes**:

$$\text{aff}(A_i, A_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} (\text{use}(q_k, A_i) \land \text{use}(q_k, A_j)) \cdot \text{freq}(q_k)$$

The diagonal entries are self-affinities (applications that use only $A_i$):

$$\text{aff}(A_i, A_i) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \text{use}(q_k, A_i) \cdot \text{freq}(q_k)$$

#### 2.2.2 Bond Energy Algorithm (BEA) — A KTU Algorithm Question

> [!NOTE]
> **BEA Steps (McCormick, Schweitzer, White 1972):**
> 1. Initialize the affinity matrix $AA$ as a diagonal matrix.
> 2. Permute rows and columns iteratively to maximize the *Bond Energy* of the matrix.
> 3. The Bond Energy of the matrix is:
> $$\text{BE}(AA) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{aff}(A_i, A_j) \cdot [\text{aff}(A_{i-1}, A_j) + \text{aff}(A_{i+1}, A_j) + \text{aff}(A_i, A_{j-1}) + \text{aff}(A_i, A_{j+1})]$$
> 4. The row/column permutation that *maximizes* BE produces the optimal global vertical fragmentation.

#### 2.2.3 The SHIFT and DROP Operations (AAM Refinement)

After BEA, we apply two binary partition operations:

- **SHIFT(i, j, k)**: Move attribute $A_i$ from fragment $k$ to fragment $j$ if it improves the clustering affinity.
- **DROP(i, j)**: Try moving $A_i$ out of fragment $j$ entirely. If the resulting fragment is empty, drop it.

---

### 2.3 Hybrid / Mixed Fragmentation

Applied in three sequential stages:

$$R \xrightarrow{\text{Horizontal}} \{R_1, R_2\} \xrightarrow{\text{Vertical}} \{R_{11}, R_{12}, R_{21}, R_{22}\} \xrightarrow{\text{Allocation}} \text{Sites}$$

Mathematically, a hybrid fragment is:

$$R_{ij} = \sigma_{p_i}(\pi_{A_j}(R))$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **THE FRAGMENTATION FORMULA CARD — PRINT AND REVISED**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Completeness (HF) | $R = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Set-theoretic union |
| Disjointness (HF) | $R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset, \; i \neq j$ | Empty intersection |
| Reconstruction (HF) | $R = \sigma_{m_1}(R) \cup \sigma_{m_2}(R) \cup \cdots$ | Union of selected tuples |
| Reconstruction (VF) | $R = R_1 \bowtie R_2 \bowtie \cdots \bowtie R_n$ | Natural join on $tid$ |
| Minterm Selectivity | $\text{sel}(m_i) = \vert \sigma_{m_i}(R) \vert / \vert R \vert$ | Dimensionless ratio, $0 \le \text{sel} \le 1$ |
| Affinity | $\text{aff}(A_i, A_j) = \sum_{k} \text{ref}_k(A_i) \cdot \text{ref}_k(A_j) \cdot \text{freq}_k$ | Access-frequency weighted |
| Bond Energy | $\text{BE} = \sum_{i,j} \text{aff}(A_i, A_j) \cdot [\text{aff}(A_{i\pm 1}, A_j) + \text{aff}(A_i, A_{j\pm 1})]$ | Maximized |
| DHF Semi-Join | $S_i = S \ltimes R_i = \pi_{S.\text{attrs}}(S \bowtie R_i)$ | $S$ projected over join |
| Number of Minterms | $\vert M \vert \le 2^{\vert Pr \vert}$ | Upper bound |
| Information Capacity | $I(R) = \sum_i \text{sel}(m_i) \cdot \log_2(1/\text{sel}(m_i))$ | Entropy-like, bits |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bar symbols `|` above use the LaTeX `\vert` macro internally to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In your exam answer sheet, you may freely use `|x|`.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Fragmentation Strategy Used | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Telecom CDR (Call Detail Records)** | Horizontal by region & date | Localized billing queries |
| **Banking Core Systems** | Vertical (account + KYC split) | PCI-DSS compliance isolation |
| **E-Commerce Catalogs** | Hybrid (category × attributes) | CDN-edge cache fit |
| **IoT Sensor Networks** | Horizontal by sensor cluster | Edge analytics with low latency |
| **Global HR Systems** | Vertical (PII vs payroll) | GDPR data-residency compliance |
| **Healthcare EHR** | Hybrid (department × patient fields) | Hospital-site autonomy |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Primary Horizontal Fragmentation (PHF)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Consider a global relation $PROJ(P\text{\_}NUM, P\text{\_}NAME, BUDGET, LOC, DEPT)$ stored at Site $S_0$. There are three applications:

- **App 1:** `SELECT * FROM PROJ WHERE BUDGET > 200000` — runs at Site 1
- **App 2:** `SELECT * FROM PROJ WHERE LOC = 'Kerala'` — runs at Site 2
- **App 3:** `SELECT P_NUM, P_NAME FROM PROJ WHERE BUDGET > 200000` — runs at Site 1

Derive the minterm predicates and recommend a horizontal fragmentation.

**Step 1: Identify the simple predicates.**

We extract atomic predicates from the WHERE clauses:

$$p_1: \text{BUDGET} > 200000$$

$$p_2: \text{LOC} = \text{'Kerala'}$$

Therefore, $Pr = \{p_1, p_2\}$ and the maximum number of minterms is $2^2 = 4$.

**Step 2: Generate the four minterm predicates.**

$$m_1 = p_1 \land p_2 : \text{BUDGET} > 200000 \land \text{LOC} = \text{'Kerala'}$$

$$m_2 = p_1 \land \neg p_2 : \text{BUDGET} > 200000 \land \text{LOC} \neq \text{'Kerala'}$$

$$m_3 = \neg p_1 \land p_2 : \text{BUDGET} \le 200000 \land \text{LOC} = \text{'Kerala'}$$

$$m_4 = \neg p_1 \land \neg p_2 : \text{BUDGET} \le 200000 \land \text{LOC} \neq \text{'Kerala'}$$

**Step 3: Compute the application-minterm usage matrix.**

| Application | $m_1$ | $m_2$ | $m_3$ | $m_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| App 1 (BUDGET > 200000) | Used | Used | — | — |
| App 2 (LOC = Kerala) | Used | — | Used | — |
| App 3 (P_NUM, P_NAME) | Used | Used | — | — |

**Step 4: Identify equivalent minterm groups (used by the *same* set of applications).**

- Group A = $\{m_1\}$ — used by {App 1, App 2, App 3}
- Group B = $\{m_2\}$ — used by {App 1, App 3}
- Group C = $\{m_3\}$ — used by {App 2}
- Group D = $\{m_4\}$ — used by {} (empty set — **contradictory**)

Since $m_4$ is accessed by no application, it is **contradictory** and may be eliminated. The remaining three minterms are all *relevant* and *non-equivalent*, so we keep all of them.

**Step 5: Define the final fragments.**

$$PROJ_1 = \sigma_{\text{BUDGET} > 200000 \;\land\; \text{LOC} = \text{'Kerala'}}(PROJ)$$

$$PROJ_2 = \sigma_{\text{BUDGET} > 200000 \;\land\; \text{LOC} \neq \text{'Kerala'}}(PROJ)$$

$$PROJ_3 = \sigma_{\text{BUDGET} \le 200000 \;\land\; \text{LOC} = \text{'Kerala'}}(PROJ)$$

**Step 6: Verify the three correctness rules.**

$$\text{Completeness: } PROJ = PROJ_1 \cup PROJ_2 \cup PROJ_3 \cup \sigma_{m_4}(PROJ) \;\checkmark$$

$$\text{Reconstruction: } PROJ = PROJ_1 \cup PROJ_2 \cup PROJ_3 \cup PROJ_4 \;\checkmark$$

$$\text{Disjointness: } PROJ_i \cap PROJ_j = \emptyset \;\text{for}\; i \neq j \;\checkmark$$

The scheme is **valid and minimal**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Derived Horizontal Fragmentation (DHF)

**Problem Statement:**
Let $EMP(\text{ENO}, \text{ENAME}, \text{TITLE})$ be stored at Site $S_E$ and $ASG(\text{ENO}, \text{PNO}, \text{RESP}, \text{DUR})$ be stored at Site $S_A$. EMP is the *owner* (1 side) and ASG is the *member* (N side) of a 1:N relationship on ENO.

Suppose EMP is horizontally fragmented as:

$$EMP_1 = \sigma_{\text{TITLE} = \text{'Engineer'}}(EMP)$$

$$EMP_2 = \sigma_{\text{TITLE} \neq \text{'Engineer'}}(EMP)$$

**Derive the corresponding ASG fragments using DHF.**

**Step 1: Apply the semi-join definition.**

By definition of DHF, the derived fragment $ASG_i$ is the *semi-join* of $ASG$ with the corresponding owner fragment $EMP_i$:

$$ASG_i = ASG \ltimes EMP_i$$

**Step 2: Expand the semi-join into a natural join followed by a projection.**

$$ASG_i = \pi_{ASG.\ast}\left( ASG \bowtie_{ASG.\text{ENO} = EMP_i.\text{ENO}} EMP_i \right)$$

**Step 3: Substitute the predicates.**

For $i = 1$:

$$ASG_1 = \pi_{\text{ENO, PNO, RESP, DUR}}\left( ASG \bowtie \sigma_{\text{TITLE} = \text{'Engineer'}}(EMP) \right)$$

This is the set of all assignment tuples for engineers.

For $i = 2$:

$$ASG_2 = \pi_{\text{ENO, PNO, RESP, DUR}}\left( ASG \bowtie \sigma_{\text{TITLE} \neq \text{'Engineer'}}(EMP) \right)$$

**Step 4: Verify completeness of ASG.**

$$ASG = ASG_1 \cup ASG_2$$

Because every ENO in ASG has exactly one matching ENO in EMP, and that EMP tuple falls in either $EMP_1$ or $EMP_2$, the union is **complete** and **disjoint**. ✓

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Attribute Affinity Matrix (AAM) Construction

**Problem Statement:**
Consider a relation $EMP(\text{ENO}, \text{ENAME}, \text{SALARY}, \text{DEPT}, \text{ADDR}, \text{PHONE})$ with three applications having the following access patterns per execution:

| Application | Frequencies | Attributes Accessed |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_1$ | 25 per day | ENO, ENAME, ADDR, PHONE |
| $Q_2$ | 15 per day | ENO, SALARY, DEPT |
| $Q_3$ | 10 per day | ENO, ENAME, SALARY |

Construct the Attribute Affinity Matrix.

**Step 1: Compute self-affinities (diagonal).**

For $A_i = A_j$:

$$\text{aff}(A_i, A_i) = \sum_{k: A_i \in \text{use}(q_k)} \text{freq}(q_k)$$

- $\text{aff}(\text{ENO, ENO}) = 25 + 15 + 10 = 50$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ENAME, ENAME}) = 25 + 10 = 35$
- $\text{aff}(\text{SALARY, SALARY}) = 15 + 10 = 25$
- $\text{aff}(\text{DEPT, DEPT}) = 15$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ADDR, ADDR}) = 25$
- $\text{aff}(\text{PHONE, PHONE}) = 25$

**Step 2: Compute pairwise affinities (off-diagonal).**

For $A_i \neq A_j$:

$$\text{aff}(A_i, A_j) = \sum_{k: \{A_i, A_j\} \subseteq \text{use}(q_k)} \text{freq}(q_k)$$

- $\text{aff}(\text{ENO, ENAME}) = 25 + 10 = 35$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ENO, SALARY}) = 15 + 10 = 25$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ENO, DEPT}) = 15$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ENO, ADDR}) = 25$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ENO, PHONE}) = 25$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ENAME, SALARY}) = 10$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ENAME, DEPT}) = 0$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ENAME, ADDR}) = 25$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ENAME, PHONE}) = 25$
- $\text{aff}(\text{SALARY, DEPT}) = 15$
- $\text{aff}(\text{SALARY, ADDR}) = 0$
- $\text{aff}(\text{SALARY, PHONE}) = 0$
- $\text{aff}(\text{DEPT, ADDR}) = 0$
- $\text{aff}(\text{DEPT, PHONE}) = 0$
- $\text{aff}(\text{ADDR, PHONE}) = 25$

**Step 3: Render the symmetric AAM.**

$$AA = \begin{bmatrix}
50 & 35 & 25 & 15 & 25 & 25 \\
35 & 35 & 10 & 0 & 25 & 25 \\
25 & 10 & 25 & 15 & 0 & 0 \\
15 & 0 & 15 & 15 & 0 & 0 \\
25 & 25 & 0 & 0 & 25 & 25 \\
25 & 25 & 0 & 0 & 25 & 25
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the matrix.**

The high-affinity clusters are:
- Cluster 1: {ENO, ENAME, ADDR, PHONE} — used together by $Q_1$ (personal info)
- Cluster 2: {ENO, SALARY, DEPT} — used together by $Q_2$ (HR-Admin info)
- ENO appears in all three, so it is the *bonding attribute* — it will be replicated as a tuple-id in every vertical fragment.

**Step 5: Recommended vertical fragmentation.**

$$EMP_1 = \pi_{\text{ENO, ENAME, ADDR, PHONE}}(EMP)$$

$$EMP_2 = \pi_{\text{ENO, SALARY, DEPT}}(EMP)$$

Reconstruction check:

$$EMP = EMP_1 \bowtie_{tid} EMP_2 \;\checkmark$$

The natural join on ENO is **lossless** because ENO is a key of the original relation — a fundamental requirement of the **Heath's Theorem** condition for lossless joins.

---

### 3.4 The Heath's Theorem Link to Lossless Reconstruction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heath's Theorem (1971):**
> If a relation $R(A, B, C)$ satisfies the functional dependency $A \rightarrow B$, then $R$ can be decomposed into $R_1(A, B)$ and $R_2(A, C)$ such that the join $R_1 \bowtie R_2 = R$ with **no spurious tuples**.

In vertical fragmentation, since the primary key functionally determines every other attribute, replicating the primary key in each fragment guarantees the lossless-join property. **This is the mathematical justification for the tid replication rule.**
