## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State and explain the three correctness rules that any fragmentation scheme must satisfy in a distributed database.*

**Model Answer:**

The three correctness rules of data fragmentation (Ceri-Pelagatti axioms) ensure that a fragmentation scheme preserves the semantics of the original global relation.

1. **Completeness Rule:** Every data item of the original global relation $R$ must appear in at least one fragment $R_i$. Formally, $R = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ for horizontal fragmentation. This ensures no information is lost.

2. **Reconstruction Rule:** The original relation $R$ must always be derivable from its fragments using a lossless relational algebra operation. For horizontal fragmentation, $R = R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \cdots \cup R_n$. For vertical fragmentation, $R = R_1 \bowtie R_2 \bowtie \cdots \bowtie R_n$ on the tuple-identifier.

3. **Disjointness Rule:** For horizontal fragmentation, no tuple of $R$ should appear in more than one fragment — i.e., $R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset$ for all $i \neq j$. For vertical fragmentation, no attribute (other than the primary key/tuple-id) is duplicated across fragments.

These rules collectively guarantee the logical equivalence between the global relation and its fragments.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Primary Horizontal Fragmentation (PHF) and Derived Horizontal Fragmentation (DHF). Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Primary Horizontal Fragmentation | Derived Horizontal Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Fragmentation of a relation based on predicates applied *directly* on its own attributes. | Fragmentation of a *member* relation based on the fragmentation of its *owner* relation. |
| **Driving Input** | Simple predicates derived from user queries on the relation itself. | The horizontal fragments of a related 1:N owner relation. |
| **Operation Used** | Selection ($\sigma$) on the relation. | Semi-join ($\ltimes$) with the owner fragment. |
| **Use Case** | When queries frequently filter on local attributes. | When referential integrity across sites must be preserved. |
| **Example** | Fragmenting $EMP$ by `DEPT = 'CSE'`. | Fragmenting $ASG$ based on fragments of $EMP$ (the owner). |

**Example PHF:** $EMP_1 = \sigma_{\text{DEPT} = \text{'CSE'}}(EMP)$.

**Example DHF:** If $EMP$ is fragmented into $EMP_1$ and $EMP_2$, then $ASG_1 = ASG \ltimes EMP_1$ and $ASG_2 = ASG \ltimes EMP_2$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two questions (Choice A and Choice B) of 14 marks each, with sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks. Students answer **ONE** full question.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 – ESE Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the global relation $PROJ(P\text{\_}NUM, P\text{\_}NAME, BUDGET, LOC, P\text{\_}TYPE)$ with the following applications:

- **$Q_1$:** `SELECT * FROM PROJ WHERE BUDGET < 500000` — frequency 20
- **$Q_2$:** `SELECT * FROM PROJ WHERE LOC = 'Bangalore'` — frequency 15
- **$Q_3$:** `SELECT P_NUM, BUDGET FROM PROJ WHERE P_TYPE = 'Internal'` — frequency 25

Apply the **COM_MIN algorithm** to derive the optimal Primary Horizontal Fragmentation of $PROJ$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the simple predicates from the WHERE clauses.**

$$p_1: \text{BUDGET} < 500000$$

$$p_2: \text{LOC} = \text{'Bangalore'}$$

$$p_3: \text{P\_TYPE} = \text{'Internal'}$$

So $Pr = \{p_1, p_2, p_3\}$ and $\vert Pr \vert = 3$, giving a maximum of $2^3 = 8$ minterms.

**Step 2: Generate the eight minterm predicates.**

$$m_1 = p_1 \land p_2 \land p_3 : \text{BUDGET} < 500000 \land \text{LOC} = \text{Bangalore} \land \text{P\_TYPE} = \text{Internal}$$

$$m_2 = p_1 \land p_2 \land \neg p_3 : \text{BUDGET} < 500000 \land \text{LOC} = \text{Bangalore} \land \text{P\_TYPE} \neq \text{Internal}$$

$$m_3 = p_1 \land \neg p_2 \land p_3 : \text{BUDGET} < 500000 \land \text{LOC} \neq \text{Bangalore} \land \text{P\_TYPE} = \text{Internal}$$

$$m_4 = p_1 \land \neg p_2 \land \neg p_3 : \text{BUDGET} < 500000 \land \text{LOC} \neq \text{Bangalore} \land \text{P\_TYPE} \neq \text{Internal}$$

$$m_5 = \neg p_1 \land p_2 \land p_3 : \text{BUDGET} \ge 500000 \land \text{LOC} = \text{Bangalore} \land \text{P\_TYPE} = \text{Internal}$$

$$m_6 = \neg p_1 \land p_2 \land \neg p_3 : \text{BUDGET} \ge 500000 \land \text{LOC} = \text{Bangalore} \land \text{P\_TYPE} \neq \text{Internal}$$

$$m_7 = \neg p_1 \land \neg p_2 \land p_3 : \text{BUDGET} \ge 500000 \land \text{LOC} \neq \text{Bangalore} \land \text{P\_TYPE} = \text{Internal}$$

$$m_8 = \neg p_1 \land \neg p_2 \land \neg p_3 : \text{BUDGET} \ge 500000 \land \text{LOC} \neq \text{Bangalore} \land \text{P\_TYPE} \neq \text{Internal}$$

**Step 3: Construct the application-minterm usage matrix.**

| App | $m_1$ | $m_2$ | $m_3$ | $m_4$ | $m_5$ | $m_6$ | $m_7$ | $m_8$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_1$ (BUDGET<500K) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — | — | — | — |
| $Q_2$ (LOC=Bangalore) | ✓ | ✓ | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | — | — |
| $Q_3$ (P_TYPE=Internal) | ✓ | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | — | ✓ | — |

**Step 4: Group minterms by their access pattern.**

- $G_1 = \{m_1\}$: used by {$Q_1, Q_2, Q_3$}
- $G_2 = \{m_2\}$: used by {$Q_1, Q_2$}
- $G_3 = \{m_3\}$: used by {$Q_1, Q_3$}
- $G_4 = \{m_4\}$: used by {$Q_1$}
- $G_5 = \{m_5\}$: used by {$Q_2, Q_3$}
- $G_6 = \{m_6\}$: used by {$Q_2$}
- $G_7 = \{m_7\}$: used by {$Q_3$}
- $G_8 = \{m_8\}$: used by $\{\}$ — **contradictory**, eliminate.

**Step 5: Check minimality — eliminate any minterm that is *implied* by another.**

No minterm is implied by another since each has a distinct access pattern. **All seven remain relevant.**

**Step 6: Define the final fragments.**

$$PROJ_1 = \sigma_{m_1}(PROJ), \; PROJ_2 = \sigma_{m_2}(PROJ), \; PROJ_3 = \sigma_{m_3}(PROJ), \; \dots, \; PROJ_7 = \sigma_{m_7}(PROJ)$$

with $PROJ_8 = \sigma_{m_8}(PROJ)$ retained to preserve completeness.

**Step 7: Verify correctness.**

$$PROJ = \bigcup_{i=1}^{8} PROJ_i \;\checkmark \quad \text{(Completeness)}$$

$$PROJ_i \cap PROJ_j = \emptyset \;\text{for}\; i \neq j \;\checkmark \quad \text{(Disjointness)}$$

$$PROJ = \bigcup_{i=1}^{8} PROJ_i \;\checkmark \quad \text{(Reconstruction via Union)}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Derived Horizontal Fragmentation**. Using a real example, show how the semi-join operation generates the member-side fragments from the owner-side fragments. State the integrity condition that DHF guarantees.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Derived Horizontal Fragmentation (DHF) is used when a relation $S$ is the *member* in a 1:N relationship with an *owner* relation $R$. Instead of fragmenting $S$ using predicates on its own attributes, we fragment $S$ based on the way $R$ is already fragmented. This ensures that every member tuple in $S_i$ has its corresponding owner tuple in $R_i$.

**Semi-Join Formulation:**

$$S_i = S \ltimes R_i = \pi_{S.\ast} \left( S \bowtie_{S.\text{join\_attr} = R_i.\text{join\_attr}} R_i \right)$$

**Example:**

Let $DEPT(\text{DNO}, \text{DNAME}, \text{LOC})$ be the *owner* and $EMP(\text{ENO}, \text{ENAME}, \text{SALARY}, \text{DNO})$ be the *member* of a 1:N relationship on DNO.

Suppose $DEPT$ is fragmented as:

$$DEPT_1 = \sigma_{\text{LOC} = \text{'Kerala'}}(DEPT)$$

$$DEPT_2 = \sigma_{\text{LOC} \neq \text{'Kerala'}}(DEPT)$$

The corresponding $EMP$ fragments are:

$$EMP_1 = \pi_{\text{ENO, ENAME, SALARY, DNO}} \left( EMP \bowtie_{EMP.\text{DNO} = DEPT_1.\text{DNO}} DEPT_1 \right)$$

$$EMP_2 = \pi_{\text{ENO, ENAME, SALARY, DNO}} \left( EMP \bowtie_{EMP.\text{DNO} = DEPT_2.\text{DNO}} DEPT_2 \right)$$

**Integrity Condition Guaranteed:**

DHF guarantees the **Referential Integrity** between fragments — specifically, *every member tuple in $S_i$ has its owner tuple in $R_i$*. This is a stronger form of disjointness at the *cross-relation* level, ensuring that no dangling references exist when fragments are distributed to different sites. It also guarantees:

$$\text{Completeness: } S = S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \cdots \cup S_n$$

$$\text{Disjointness: } S_i \cap S_j = \emptyset \;\; (i \neq j)$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – ESE Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Vertical Fragmentation**. Construct the **Attribute Affinity Matrix (AAM)** for the relation $SUPPLIER(S\text{\_}NUM, S\text{\_}NAME, CITY, PHONE, STATUS)$ given the following access patterns:

- **$Q_1$:** Accesses {S_NUM, S_NAME, CITY, PHONE} — frequency 30
- **$Q_2$:** Accesses {S_NUM, STATUS} — frequency 20
- **$Q_3$:** Accesses {S_NUM, S_NAME, STATUS} — frequency 10

Suggest an appropriate vertical fragmentation.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Vertical Fragmentation (VF) divides a relation $R$ by *attributes* rather than tuples. Each fragment $R_i$ is a projection of $R$ onto a subset of attributes, with the primary key (S_NUM) replicated as a tuple-identifier in every fragment. The reconstruction is performed by a natural join on the tuple-id.

**Step 1: Compute self-affinities (diagonal).**

$$\text{aff}(A_i, A_i) = \sum_{k: A_i \in \text{use}(q_k)} \text{freq}(q_k)$$

- $\text{aff}(\text{S\_NUM, S\_NUM}) = 30 + 20 + 10 = 60$
- $\text{aff}(\text{S\_NAME, S\_NAME}) = 30 + 10 = 40$
- $\text{aff}(\text{CITY, CITY}) = 30$
- $\text{aff}(\text{PHONE, PHONE}) = 30$
- $\text{aff}(\text{STATUS, STATUS}) = 20 + 10 = 30$

**Step 2: Compute pairwise affinities (off-diagonal).**

- $\text{aff}(\text{S\_NUM, S\_NAME}) = 30 + 10 = 40$
- $\text{aff}(\text{S\_NUM, CITY}) = 30$
- $\text{aff}(\text{S\_NUM, PHONE}) = 30$
- $\text{aff}(\text{S\_NUM, STATUS}) = 20 + 10 = 30$
- $\text{aff}(\text{S\_NAME, CITY}) = 30$
- $\text{aff}(\text{S\_NAME, PHONE}) = 30$
- $\text{aff}(\text{S\_NAME, STATUS}) = 10$
- $\text{aff}(\text{CITY, PHONE}) = 30$
- $\text{aff}(\text{CITY, STATUS}) = 0$
- $\text{aff}(\text{PHONE, STATUS}) = 0$

**Step 3: Construct the symmetric AAM.**

$$AA = \begin{bmatrix}
60 & 40 & 30 & 30 & 30 \\
40 & 40 & 30 & 30 & 10 \\
30 & 30 & 30 & 30 & 0 \\
30 & 30 & 30 & 30 & 0 \\
30 & 10 & 0 & 0 & 30
\end{bmatrix}$$

Rows and columns are ordered as: S_NUM, S_NAME, CITY, PHONE, STATUS.

**Step 4: Identify high-affinity clusters.**

- **Cluster 1:** {S_NUM, S_NAME, CITY, PHONE} — accessed together by $Q_1$ with strong mutual affinity (40, 30, 30, 30).
- **Cluster 2:** {S_NUM, STATUS} — accessed together by $Q_2$ and also by $Q_3$ alongside S_NAME.
- **S_NAME + STATUS** has affinity 10 (from $Q_3$), which is weak, so they prefer their primary clusters.

**Step 5: Recommend the vertical fragmentation.**

$$SUPPLIER_1 = \pi_{\text{S\_NUM, S\_NAME, CITY, PHONE}}(SUPPLIER)$$

$$SUPPLIER_2 = \pi_{\text{S\_NUM, STATUS}}(SUPPLIER)$$

**Step 6: Verify reconstruction.**

$$SUPPLIER = SUPPLIER_1 \bowtie_{S\_NUM} SUPPLIER_2 \;\checkmark$$

Since S_NUM is the primary key, the join is **lossless** by Heath's theorem. **S_NUM is replicated** in both fragments as the tuple-identifier. ✓

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Horizontal Fragmentation** and **Vertical Fragmentation** across any five technical parameters. Mention one real-world engineering scenario where **Hybrid Fragmentation** is essential.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Horizontal Fragmentation (HF) | Vertical Fragmentation (VF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit of Partition** | Tuples (rows) | Attributes (columns) |
| **Driven By** | Selection predicates (minterms) | Attribute affinity and access patterns |
| **Key Handling** | Primary key remains intact in each fragment | Primary key is replicated as a *tuple-id* in each fragment |
| **Reconstructive Operator** | Union ($\cup$) | Natural Join ($\bowtie$) on tuple-id |
| **Lossless Guarantee** | Trivially lossless (union of disjoint sets) | Lossless only if join attribute is a key (Heath's Theorem) |
| **Use Case** | Distributing *data instances* (e.g., region-wise customers) | Distributing *data fields* (e.g., separating PII from analytics) |

**Real-World Engineering Scenario Requiring Hybrid Fragmentation:**

Consider a **multi-national hospital chain** managing patient data:
- The chain has hospitals in Kerala, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu — requiring **horizontal fragmentation by STATE** for jurisdictional data residency compliance.
- Within each hospital, the patient record contains clinical notes (accessed by doctors) and billing information (accessed by the finance team) — requiring **vertical fragmentation** by attribute role.

A doctor in Kerala accessing clinical history for a Kerala patient should fetch **only the Kerala-based clinical fragment** — neither fetching billing data (irrelevant to the doctor) nor fetching data from other states (compliance violation). This is **Hybrid Fragmentation** = Horizontal (by state) $\times$ Vertical (by clinical vs. financial).

**Algorithmically:**

$$PATIENT_{\text{Kerala, Clinical}} = \pi_{\text{PID, Name, Diagnosis, Meds}} \left( \sigma_{\text{STATE} = \text{'Kerala'}}(PATIENT) \right)$$

This single fragment can then be allocated to the Kerala hospital's clinical database.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the tuple-id ($tid$) in VF** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if a student defines a vertical fragment without specifying that the primary key is replicated. The phrase "with the primary key replicated as a tuple-id" is mandatory.
> 2. **Confusing Union and Join** — A common fatal mistake is writing "$R = R_1 \bowtie R_2$" for horizontal fragments. The correct operator is **Union** for HF and **Join** for VF.
> 3. **Skipping the COM_MIN steps** — When asked for PHF, students often write only the final minterms without showing the predicate identification, contradiction elimination, and equivalence grouping. **All four COM_MIN steps must be shown.**
> 4. **Wrong number of minterms** — Forgetting that minterms range over Boolean combinations, not just simple predicates, leads to a $2^n$ count error. Always state "the maximum number of minterms is $2^n$".
> 5. **Ignoring the Disjointness exception for VF** — A common slip is claiming VF is "fully disjoint". The correct statement is "disjoint except for the primary key/tid".
> 6. **Not verifying reconstruction** — Every fragmentation answer must end with a verification of the **three correctness rules**, even if not explicitly asked. This demonstrates depth and earns the 1-2 grace marks at the examiner's discretion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DATA FRAGMENTATION**

- **Definition:** Fragmentation is the decomposition of a global relation into smaller, logically meaningful fragments for distribution across sites.
- **Three Types:** Horizontal (rows), Vertical (columns), Hybrid (mixed).
- **Correctness Rules (Ceri-Pelagatti):** **Completeness, Reconstruction, Disjointness** — all three are mandatory in every fragmentation scheme.
- **HF Reconstruction:** $R = \bigcup R_i$ via **Union**.
- **VF Reconstruction:** $R = R_1 \bowtie R_2 \bowtie \cdots$ via **Natural Join on tid**.
- **PHF Driver:** Minterm predicates derived from simple predicates via the **COM_MIN algorithm**.
- **DHF Driver:** Semi-join with the owner fragment: $S_i = S \ltimes R_i$.
- **VF Driver:** Attribute Affinity Matrix (AAM) refined by the **Bond Energy Algorithm (BEA)** followed by **SHIFT** and **DROP** operations.
- **The Role of Tuple-ID in VF:** Without the replicated primary key, the natural join produces *spurious tuples* — violating reconstruction losslessness. This is backed by **Heath's Theorem**.
- **Number of Minterms Upper Bound:** $\vert M \vert \le 2^{\vert Pr \vert}$.
- **Contradictory Minterm:** A minterm predicate that is logically unsatisfiable (e.g., $x > 5 \land x < 3$). Always eliminate.
- **Equivalent Minterms:** Two minterms are equivalent if and only if they are accessed by exactly the same set of applications. Merge them.
- **DHF Integrity Guarantee:** Every member tuple in $S_i$ has its owner tuple in $R_i$ — a cross-relation referential integrity property.
- **Why Fragment?:** Locality of reference, efficiency, parallelism, security, and disconnected operation.
- **Allocation is a separate phase** (Module 3 territory) — fragmentation splits the data; allocation decides *where* the fragments reside.
- **Clustering Affinity:** The numerical basis for the AAM, weighted by query frequency.
- **Hybrid Order Matters:** Apply HF first if queries are tuple-selective; apply VF first if queries are attribute-selective; the choice is **cost-driven**.
- **Kerala-context tip:** Be prepared to discuss the use of fragmentation for **state-level e-governance databases** (e.g., KSEB consumer records fragmented by district and by consumption category).
- **Examiner's most-asked phrase:** "State the three correctness rules" — never leave this blank; it is a guaranteed 3-5 mark swing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Replication and Allocation Techniques for Distributed Database Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Replication and Allocation Techniques for Distributed Database Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

In the context of **Distributed Database Design**, **Data Replication** refers to the storage of data copies at multiple sites (nodes) of a distributed database system, while **Data Allocation** refers to the strategic decision of placing these data fragments (obtained via fragmentation) at the most appropriate sites. Together, they form the **Fragmentation, Replication, and Allocation (FRA) Problem**, which is the cornerstone of optimizing performance, availability, and query processing in a distributed environment.

The **Data Allocation Problem (DAP)** is formally defined as the process of deciding the optimal placement of each fragment of a relation across the sites of a distributed system such that the **Total Cost (TC)** is minimized, subject to constraints on response time, storage capacity, and processing capability of each site.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *Replication and Allocation Techniques* module is an Outcome-Based Education (OBE) topic mapped to **CO2 (Design effective distributed database schemas using fragmentation and allocation techniques)** and aligns with **Bloom's Taxonomy Level: Analyze / Evaluate**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Network"

Imagine a nationwide library system managed by the government. Books are the **data**, and the libraries at different cities are the **sites** of the distributed system. The librarian's dilemma mirrors the allocation problem:

- **If a book is stored at every library** (Full Replication) → readers find books quickly, but updating/editing is expensive.
- **If a book is at only one library** (No Replication) → saving space is easy, but readers from far away wait long.
- **If a book is at 3-4 strategic libraries** (Partial Replication) → a balance between speed and cost.

The librarian's job is to **decide which book (fragment) goes to which library (site)** — this is exactly the **Data Allocation Problem** in distributed databases.

### 1.3 Core Terminology Grid

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Fragmentation** | Splitting a relation into smaller logical pieces (horizontal/vertical/hybrid) |
| **Replication** | Storing multiple copies of a fragment at different sites |
| **Allocation** | The process of deciding *where* to place each fragment |
| **Site (Node)** | A physical location in the distributed system where data is stored |
| **Read-Only / Update** | Read queries retrieve data; update queries modify data |
| **Primary Copy** | The master copy of a fragment (used for concurrency control) |

> [!NOTE]
> A fragment that is *not replicated* is called a **Non-Replicated Fragment**, while one stored at all sites is a **Fully Replicated Fragment**. Anything in between is **Partially Replicated**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Alternative Fragmentation & Replication Strategies

The **general problem of data allocation** is an **NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem**. We consider four alternatives based on two orthogonal decisions — *fragmentation* and *replication*:

1. **No Fragmentation + No Replication (Centralized)**
   - The whole database is stored at one site.
   - Pros: Simple. Cons: Poor availability, high network traffic.

2. **Fragmentation + No Replication (Partitioned)**
   - Database is fragmented, each fragment stored at exactly one site.
   - Pros: Optimal for read-heavy access. Cons: Bad availability.

3. **No Fragmentation + Full Replication (Fully Replicated)**
   - Entire database copied at every site.
   - Pros: Excellent read performance, high availability. Cons: Very expensive updates.

4. **Fragmentation + Full/Partial Replication (Hybrid)**
   - Database is fragmented and fragments are selectively replicated.
   - Pros: Best balance of performance and cost. Cons: Complex to manage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 expects students to evaluate these alternatives using a **cost-benefit matrix** involving query processing, storage, and communication costs.

### 2.2 Information Requirements for the Allocation Problem

Before solving the allocation problem, the following statistical information must be gathered:

- **Database information**: Size of each relation, size of each attribute, frequency of access.
- **Application information**:
  - Number of read queries issued at each site.
  - Number of update queries issued at each site.
  - Query execution strategy (e.g., join order, selection predicates).
- **Site information**:
  - Unit storage cost at each site.
  - Unit transmission cost between every pair of sites.
  - Processing capability of each site (CPU power, I/O bandwidth).
- **Network information**: Bandwidth, latency, communication topology.

### 2.3 The General Allocation Model — Cost Function

Let there be $S$ sites, $F$ fragments, and $Q$ queries. The total cost is the sum of four components:

- **Query Processing Cost** (local CPU + I/O)
- **Storage Cost** (disk space at each site)
- **Update Propagation Cost** (transmitting updates to all replicas)
- **Communication Cost** (transmitting intermediate/final results)

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $TC$ | Total Cost of the allocation | Monetary units |
| $CPU_{ij}$ | CPU cost of executing query $i$ at site $j$ | Cost units |
| $I/O_{ij}$ | I/O cost of executing query $i$ at site $j$ | Cost units |
| $ST_{kj}$ | Storage cost of fragment $k$ at site $j$ | Cost units per byte |
| $size(F_k)$ | Size of fragment $k$ in bytes | Bytes |
| $TRC_{kj}$ | Transmission cost of update of $F_k$ at site $j$ | Cost units |
| $UC_{ij}$ | Update cost of query $i$ at site $j$ | Cost units |
| $minTC$ | Minimum possible total cost | Monetary units |
| $minRT$ | Minimum possible response time | Seconds (ms) |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical pipe symbol `|` is **prohibited** in KTU tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: $f(x) \mid_{x=0}$ is rendered as $f(x) \big\vert_{x=0}$.

### 2.5 Allocation Model — The Objective Function

The objective is to **minimize the Total Cost ($TC$)** subject to response time and storage constraints:

$$
TC \;=\; \sum_{\forall \text{query } i} \sum_{\forall \text{site } j} \big( CPU_{ij} + I/O_{ij} \big) \;+\; \sum_{\forall \text{frag } k} \sum_{\forall \text{site } j} ST_{kj} \cdot size(F_k) \;+\; \sum_{\forall \text{update } i} \sum_{\forall \text{site } j} UC_{ij} \cdot TRC_{kj}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The general allocation model is computationally **intractable (NP-hard)**. Therefore, KTU 2024 expects knowledge of **approximation and heuristic algorithms** — *not exact solutions*.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

This model is used in:
- **Cloud Data Warehousing** (e.g., Snowflake, BigQuery replica placement).
- **CDN (Content Delivery Networks)** for global web services.
- **Multi-region NoSQL clusters** (Cassandra, MongoDB sharding + replication).
- **Supply chain distributed ERP systems** (SAP, Oracle ERP).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Cost Function Components

The Total Cost (TC) function is decomposed and each component is derived explicitly.

#### Step 1 — Query Processing Cost (Local CPU + I/O)

Let query $q_i$ be issued at site $s_j$ accessing fragment $F_k$. The local processing cost is:

$$
Cost_{local}(q_i, s_j) \;=\; \alpha_{i} \cdot CPU_{ij} \;+\; \beta_{i} \cdot I/O_{ij}
$$

where $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_i$ are the number of CPU and I/O instructions required, respectively.

#### Step 2 — Storage Cost

If fragment $F_k$ is stored at site $s_j$, the storage cost per unit time is:

$$
Cost_{storage}(F_k, s_j) \;=\; ST_{kj} \cdot size(F_k)
$$

Summing over all fragments and all sites:

$$
TotalStorage \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{F} \sum_{j=1}^{S} ST_{kj} \cdot size(F_k)
$$

#### Step 3 — Update Propagation Cost

When an update query modifies fragment $F_k$, the update must be propagated to **all $R_k$ replicas** of $F_k$. If the update originates at site $s_j$, and there are $R_k - 1$ other replicas, the transmission cost is:

$$
Cost_{update}(F_k, s_j) \;=\; UC_{ij} \cdot TRC_{kj} \cdot (R_k - 1)
$$

#### Step 4 — Communication Cost (Intermediate Results)

When a query requires data from a remote fragment, intermediate results must be transmitted. If query $q_i$ returns a result of size $X_i$ bytes, the communication cost is:

$$
Cost_{comm}(q_i) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{S} \sum_{k=1, k \neq j}^{S} X_i \cdot CC_{jk}
$$

where $CC_{jk}$ is the unit communication cost between sites $j$ and $k$.

#### Step 5 — Combine All Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC \;=\;& \sum_{i=1}^{Q} \sum_{j=1}^{S} \big( \alpha_{i} \cdot CPU_{ij} + \beta_{i} \cdot I/O_{ij} \big) \\
&+\; \sum_{k=1}^{F} \sum_{j=1}^{S} ST_{kj} \cdot size(F_k) \\
&+\; \sum_{i=1}^{Q} \sum_{j=1}^{S} UC_{ij} \cdot TRC_{kj} \cdot (R_k - 1) \\
&+\; \sum_{i=1}^{Q} \sum_{j=1}^{S} \sum_{k=1, k \neq j}^{S} X_i \cdot CC_{jk}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** Row 1 represents CPU+I/O costs summed over all queries and sites. Row 2 is the storage footprint. Row 3 captures the cost of broadcasting updates to all replicas. Row 4 is the cost of remote data transfer during query processing.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Allocation Strategies

| Criterion | Centralized | Partitioned (No Repl.) | Full Replication | Hybrid (Partial Repl.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Cost** | Lowest | Low | Highest | Moderate |
| **Update Cost** | Lowest (one site) | Low | Highest (all sites) | Moderate |
| **Read Cost** | Highest (remote) | Moderate | Lowest (local) | Low |
| **Availability** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Good |
| **Complexity** | Very Low | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Best Use Case** | OLAP warehouse | Read-heavy OLTP | Global read-mostly | Real-world mixed workload |

### 3.3 Solution Methods for the Allocation Problem

Because the general DAP is **NP-hard**, KTU 2024 emphasizes the following heuristic solution methods:

#### 3.3.1 The Brute Force Method
- Evaluate **all possible allocations** ($S^F$ combinations for $S$ sites and $F$ fragments).
- **Complexity:** $O(S^F)$ — exponential.
- **Use:** Only for very small $S, F$ (e.g., $S \le 3, F \le 3$).

#### 3.3.2 The Greedy Heuristic
- Repeatedly assign each fragment to the site that **minimizes the incremental total cost**.
- **Complexity:** $O(F \cdot S)$ — polynomial.
- **Limitation:** Local optimum, not necessarily global optimum.

$$
\Delta TC(F_k, s_j) \;=\; TC_{with} - TC_{without}
$$

Pick the $(F_k, s_j)$ pair with the **most negative** $\Delta TC$ (largest cost reduction).

#### 3.3.3 Branch-and-Bound (State-Space Search)
- Systematically explores the state space tree of allocation decisions.
- **Pruning rule:** Discard a subtree if its lower bound cost exceeds the best known solution.
- Lower bound is computed via **relaxation** of integer constraints (linear programming relaxation).

#### 3.3.4 The Hungarian Method (for Assignment Problems)
- When the problem reduces to a **one-to-one assignment** (each fragment to exactly one site), the **Hungarian algorithm** finds the optimal assignment in **polynomial time** $O(n^3)$.
- **Steps:**
  1. Construct the cost matrix $C[F \times S]$.
  2. Subtract row minimums.
  3. Subtract column minimums.
  4. Cover all zeros with minimum number of lines.
  5. If lines = $F$, optimal assignment found; else adjust matrix and repeat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Hungarian Method applies **only when each fragment is assigned to exactly one site** (no replication). KTU frequently tests this distinction in Part A.

### 3.4 Lower Bound Calculation — A Numerical Illustration

Consider a 2-site, 2-fragment scenario with the following cost matrix (in monetary units, including storage + update + query):

$$
C \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 25 \\ 20 & 15 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Fragment 1 → Site 1: $C_{11} = 10$, Fragment 1 → Site 2: $C_{12} = 25$
Fragment 2 → Site 1: $C_{21} = 20$, Fragment 2 → Site 2: $C_{22} = 15$

**Brute Force Enumeration** (4 possibilities):

| Allocation | Total Cost |
|---|---|
| $F_1 \to S_1$, $F_2 \to S_1$ | $10 + 20 = 30$ |
| $F_1 \to S_1$, $F_2 \to S_2$ | $10 + 15 = 25$ |
| $F_1 \to S_2$, $F_2 \to S_1$ | $25 + 20 = 45$ |
| $F_1 \to S_2$, $F_2 \to S_2$ | $25 + 15 = 40$ |

**Optimal:** $F_1 \to S_1$, $F_2 \to S_2$ with $TC_{min} = 25$.

> [!NOTE]
> This exact numerical type appears regularly in KTU Part B problems (14-mark module questions) under CO2.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Data Allocation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Global Conceptual Schema] --> B[Fragmentation Phase]
    B --> B1[Horizontal Fragmentation]
    B --> B2[Vertical Fragmentation]
    B --> B3[Hybrid Fragmentation]
    B1 --> C[Set of Fragments F1 to Fn]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Replication Decision]
    D --> D1[Non Replicated]
    D --> D2[Partially Replicated]
    D --> D3[Fully Replicated]
    D1 --> E[Allocation Phase]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Information Gathering]
    F --> F1[Database Statistics]
    F --> F2[Query Frequencies]
    F --> F3[Network Costs]
    F1 --> G[Cost Model TC]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Solve Optimization Problem]
    H --> H1[Brute Force]
    H --> H2[Greedy Heuristic]
    H --> H3[Branch and Bound]
    H --> H4[Hungarian Method]
    H1 --> I[Final Allocation Plan]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Physical Schema at Each Site]
```

### 4.2 Component Diagram — Cost Function Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TC[Total Cost TC] --> QC[Query Processing Cost]
    TC --> SC[Storage Cost]
    TC --> UC[Update Propagation Cost]
    TC --> CC[Communication Cost]
    QC --> QC1[CPU Cost at Local Site]
    QC --> QC2[I/O Cost at Local Site]
    SC --> SC1[Fragment Size x Unit Storage Cost]
    UC --> UC1[Number of Replicas x Transmission Cost]
    CC --> CC1[Intermediate Result Size x Network Cost]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Diagram — The Four Replication Alternatives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Replication Strategy] --> R1[Centralized]
    R --> R2[Partitioned]
    R --> R3[Fully Replicated]
    R --> R4[Hybrid Partial Replication]
    R1 --> R1A[Single Site: Lowest Cost Lowest Availability]
    R2 --> R2A[One Site per Fragment: Good for Reads]
    R3 --> R3A[All Sites: Best for Reads Highest Update Cost]
    R4 --> R4A[Strategic Sites: Balanced Tradeoff Most Complex]
```

### 4.4 Process Diagram — Hungarian Algorithm Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Cost Matrix C of F x S] --> stepB[Subtract Row Minimums]
    stepB --> stepC[Subtract Column Minimums]
    stepC --> stepD[Cover All Zeros with Min Lines]
    stepD --> stepE{Number of Lines equals F?}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF[Assign Fragments to Sites via Zeros]
    stepE -- No --> stepG[Find Smallest Uncovered Element]
    stepG --> stepH[Subtract from Uncovered, Add to Double Covered]
    stepH --> stepD
    stepF --> stepI[End: Optimal Assignment Achieved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node identifiers are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `stepA`, `node1`) and use clean uppercase labels without markdown formatting inside quotes — strictly compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 anti-hallucination safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define the Data Allocation Problem with its four alternative strategies.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Data Allocation Problem (DAP)** is the problem of optimally placing each fragment of a distributed database at the most appropriate site(s) to minimize total cost (query processing + storage + update + communication) subject to response time and storage constraints. The **four alternative strategies** are:

1. **Centralized** — Entire database at one site, no fragmentation.
2. **Partitioned** — Database fragmented, each fragment at exactly one site (no replication).
3. **Fully Replicated** — Entire database replicated at all sites, no fragmentation.
4. **Hybrid (Partial Replication)** — Database fragmented, fragments selectively replicated at multiple sites.

**(Award 1 mark for definition + 0.5 marks each for the four strategies.)**

---

#### **Question 2: Differentiate between replicated and non-replicated allocation.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Non-Replicated | Replicated |
|---|---|---|
| **Copies of fragment** | Exactly 1 | 1 or more |
| **Storage cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Update cost** | Lower | Higher (must propagate to all replicas) |
| **Read cost** | Higher (may be remote) | Lower (often local) |
| **Availability** | Moderate | High |
| **Consistency overhead** | Low | High (needs synchronization) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (a) forgetting to include **all four cost components** in the TC equation, (b) confusing the **Hungarian Method** applicability (only for 1-to-1 assignment), and (c) skipping the **pruning step** in branch-and-bound. Always show the *complete cost model* and the *algorithmic steps* explicitly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the four alternative fragmentation and replication strategies with their cost-benefit trade-offs. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four strategies arise from the orthogonal decisions of fragmentation and replication:

**1. Centralized (No Fragmentation + No Replication):**
- Entire database at one site.
- **Cost:** Lowest storage; high remote access cost.
- **Benefit:** Simple management.
- **Drawback:** Single point of failure, poor performance for remote users.

**2. Partitioned (Fragmentation + No Replication):**
- Relations are fragmented (horizontally/vertically), each fragment at one site.
- **Cost:** No update propagation cost.
- **Benefit:** Optimal for read-heavy localized access.
- **Drawback:** Lower availability, joins across sites expensive.

**3. Fully Replicated (No Fragmentation + Full Replication):**
- Whole database copied at every site.
- **Cost:** Highest storage and update cost.
- **Benefit:** Best read performance, highest availability.
- **Drawback:** Expensive updates, large storage.

**4. Hybrid (Fragmentation + Partial Replication):**
- Combines fragmentation with selective replication of fragments.
- **Cost:** Moderate across all dimensions.
- **Benefit:** Best overall performance for mixed workloads.
- **Drawback:** Most complex; requires careful design.

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Strategy | Read Cost | Update Cost | Storage Cost | Availability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Centralized | High | Low | Low | Low |
| Partitioned | Moderate | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Full Replication | Low | Very High | Very High | Very High |
| Hybrid | Low-Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | High |

**(Award 4 marks for explanation of 4 strategies + 3 marks for the comparison table.)**

---

**(b) Derive the general cost model for the Data Allocation Problem and identify its four cost components. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Total Cost (TC)** is a function of four major components: query processing, storage, update propagation, and communication.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC \;=\;& \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{Q} \sum_{j=1}^{S} \big( \alpha_{i} \cdot CPU_{ij} + \beta_{i} \cdot I/O_{ij} \big)}_{\text{1. Query Processing Cost (CPU + I/O)}} \\
&+\; \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{F} \sum_{j=1}^{S} ST_{kj} \cdot size(F_k)}_{\text{2. Storage Cost}} \\
&+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{Q} \sum_{j=1}^{S} UC_{ij} \cdot TRC_{kj} \cdot (R_k - 1)}_{\text{3. Update Propagation Cost}} \\
&+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{Q} \sum_{j=1}^{S} \sum_{k=1, k \neq j}^{S} X_i \cdot CC_{jk}}_{\text{4. Communication Cost}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Component Explanations:**

1. **Query Processing Cost:** CPU cycles and disk I/O incurred when executing a query locally at site $s_j$.
2. **Storage Cost:** Disk space consumed by fragment $F_k$ at site $s_j$ over time.
3. **Update Propagation Cost:** Network cost of broadcasting updates to all $R_k - 1$ other replicas of $F_k$.
4. **Communication Cost:** Cost of transmitting intermediate query results of size $X_i$ bytes between sites.

**Information Requirements:**
- Database: size of each relation, size of each attribute.
- Application: query frequencies at each site, access types (read/update).
- Site: storage cost per byte, processing power.
- Network: unit transmission cost between every site pair.

**(Award 3 marks for the cost equation, 2 marks for component identification, 2 marks for information requirements.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the various solution methods for the Data Allocation Problem. Why is the general problem NP-hard? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Why NP-hard:**
The DAP is a **combinatorial optimization problem**. With $F$ fragments and $S$ sites, there are $2^{F \cdot S}$ possible allocation states (each fragment either stored or not at each site). For $F = 10$ and $S = 5$, this exceeds $10^{15}$ states — exhaustive search is infeasible. The problem is reducible from the **set-partitioning** and **quadratic assignment** problems, both of which are NP-hard.

**Solution Methods:**

**1. Brute Force (Exhaustive Search):**
- Enumerate all $2^{F \cdot S}$ allocations.
- Complexity: $O(2^{F \cdot S})$ — exponential.
- **Use:** Only for trivial sizes.

**2. Greedy Heuristic:**
- Iteratively assign each fragment to the site with **lowest incremental cost**.
- Complexity: $O(F \cdot S)$ — polynomial.
- **Limitation:** Local optimum, not guaranteed global.

**3. Branch-and-Bound:**
- State-space tree search with **pruning** based on lower-bound estimates.
- Lower bound obtained by **LP relaxation** of integer allocation variables.
- Complexity: $O(2^{F \cdot S})$ worst case, but effective pruning in practice.

**4. Hungarian Method (Kuhn-Munkres):**
- Solves **one-to-one assignment** (each fragment to exactly one site) optimally in $O(n^3)$.
- **Restriction:** Does not handle replication.
- **Steps:** Row reduction → Column reduction → Zero covering → Matrix adjustment → Assignment via zeros.

**5. Genetic Algorithms / Simulated Annealing:**
- Meta-heuristics for near-optimal solutions in large problems.
- Used in modern distributed system design (e.g., cloud replica placement).

**(Award 3 marks for NP-hard explanation + 4 marks for the 5 solution methods with 1 mark each, but adjusted to credit 4 marks for clarity.)**

---

**(b) Solve the following allocation problem using the Hungarian Method.** (7 Marks)

Given the cost matrix (including storage + update + query costs) for assigning 3 fragments to 3 sites:

$$
C \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 6 & 5 \\ 4 & 7 & 9 \\ 6 & 5 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Find the **optimal one-to-one assignment** of fragments to sites with **minimum total cost**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Row Reduction** — Subtract the row minimum from each element.

Row 1 min = 5, Row 2 min = 4, Row 3 min = 5.

$$
C_1 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Column Reduction** — Subtract the column minimum from each element.

Col 1 min = 0, Col 2 min = 0, Col 3 min = 0.

$$
C_2 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Cover All Zeros with Minimum Lines.**

Zeros are at positions: $(1,3), (2,1), (3,2)$. We can cover all three zeros with **3 lines** (one per row or column).

Number of lines = 3 = $F$ (number of fragments). Therefore, the algorithm terminates.

**Step 4: Optimal Assignment via Zeros.**

Assign fragment 1 → site 3 (zero at $C[1][3]$).
Assign fragment 2 → site 1 (zero at $C[2][1]$).
Assign fragment 3 → site 2 (zero at $C[3][2]$).

**Step 5: Compute Optimal Total Cost.**

$$
TC_{opt} \;=\; C[1][3] + C[2][1] + C[3][2] \;=\; 5 + 4 + 5 \;=\; 14
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since number of covering lines equals $F$, no matrix adjustment is needed — the solution is **directly optimal**.

**Verification by Brute Force (6 alternatives):**

| Assignment | Total Cost |
|---|---|
| $F_1 \to S_1$, $F_2 \to S_2$, $F_3 \to S_3$ | $8 + 7 + 8 = 23$ |
| $F_1 \to S_1$, $F_2 \to S_3$, $F_3 \to S_2$ | $8 + 9 + 5 = 22$ |
| $F_1 \to S_2$, $F_2 \to S_1$, $F_3 \to S_3$ | $6 + 4 + 8 = 18$ |
| $F_1 \to S_2$, $F_2 \to S_3$, $F_3 \to S_1$ | $6 + 9 + 6 = 21$ |
| $F_1 \to S_3$, $F_2 \to S_1$, $F_3 \to S_2$ | $5 + 4 + 5 = \mathbf{14}$ |
| $F_1 \to S_3$, $F_2 \to S_2$, $F_3 \to S_1$ | $5 + 7 + 6 = 18$ |

**Optimal verified:** $TC_{min} = 14$. Hungarian method confirmed.

**(Award 2 marks for row/column reduction, 2 marks for zero covering and stopping criterion, 2 marks for final assignment and cost calculation, 1 mark for brute force verification.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Data Allocation = deciding optimal placement of fragments across sites; NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem.
- **Four Strategies:** Centralized, Partitioned, Fully Replicated, Hybrid (Partial Replication). Know the *cost-benefit* of each.
- **Cost Function TC** has **four components**: (1) Query Processing (CPU + I/O), (2) Storage, (3) Update Propagation, (4) Communication. Always include all four in derivations.
- **Information Requirements:** Database statistics, query access patterns, site capacities, network costs.
- **Solution Methods:**
  - Brute Force — exponential, exact, infeasible for large problems.
  - Greedy — polynomial, fast, but local optimum.
  - Branch-and-Bound — uses LP relaxation for lower bound and prunes sub-optimal branches.
  - Hungarian Method — polynomial $O(n^3)$, exact, **only for 1-to-1 assignment** (no replication).
- **Hungarian Method Steps:** Row reduction → Column reduction → Zero covering (min lines) → If lines = $n$, optimal found; else adjust matrix and repeat.
- **Update Cost Multiplier:** Update cost grows linearly with the number of replicas $(R_k - 1)$ — this is why full replication is expensive.
- **Read vs. Update Tradeoff:** More replicas reduce read cost but increase update cost. The optimal replication factor balances these.
- **Common KTU Pitfall:** Confusing "fragmentation" (splitting) with "allocation" (placing). Fragmentation is a *logical* step; allocation is a *physical* step.
- **Real-World Mapping:** Cloud replica placement (AWS S3 Cross-Region Replication), Cassandra/MongoDB consistency-level tuning, CDN edge-server placement.
- **Exam Focus:** Expect 14-mark questions to require (a) explaining the cost model with derivation and (b) solving a small numerical problem via Hungarian Method or Brute Force.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Distributed Database Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Distributed Database Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Distributed Database System (DDBS)** is a collection of multiple, logically interrelated databases distributed over a computer network, where data is physically stored at different sites but managed in such a way that appears to the user as a **single, unified database**. The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes Distributed Database Systems based on **degree of homogeneity**, **degree of autonomy**, and **architectural topology** into several well-defined types:

1. **Homogeneous Distributed Database Systems**
2. **Heterogeneous Distributed Database Systems**
3. **Client–Server Distributed Databases**
4. **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Distributed Databases**
5. **Federated / Multi-Database Systems**
6. **Parallel Database Systems**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECDT795 – Module 2):** Students must be able to **classify DDBS architectures**, justify the use of each type using **distributed transaction management principles**, and identify trade-offs between **autonomy, heterogeneity, and integration complexity**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Library Chain

Imagine a **national library chain** with branches in every state. 

- If every branch uses the **same cataloging software, the same borrowing rules, and the same shelf-numbering scheme**, the chain is **homogeneous** — a librarian trained in Kerala can walk into a branch in Delhi and find books immediately.
- If each branch uses **different software, different shelf systems, and different languages**, the chain is **heterogeneous** — you need a **translator/oracle** to unify them.
- If there is a **central head office** that processes every request, it is **client–server**.
- If every branch **can act as both a requester and a fulfiller** (a librarian in Kerala can directly borrow a book from the Delhi branch), it is **peer-to-peer**.
- If each branch remains **independent** but agrees to share certain resources, it is a **federated system**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Single Most Important Idea:** The *type* of a DDBS is determined by **three orthogonal axes**:
> 1. **Homogeneity** (same vs. different DBMS software)
> 2. **Autonomy** (how independently each site can act)
> 3. **Architecture** (centralized server vs. peer-to-peer vs. tightly coupled cluster)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love a **classification table** in Module 2 answers. Memorize the four corner-cases: *Homogeneous-Tight*, *Homogeneous-Loose*, *Heterogeneous-Federated*, *Heterogeneous-Mediated*.

---

## 1.3 Quick Reference: The 6 Standard Types

| # | Type | One-line Essence |
|---|------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Homogeneous DDBS** | Same DBMS, same schema, full distribution transparency |
| 2 | **Heterogeneous DDBS** | Different DBMSs at different sites, requires translation layer |
| 3 | **Client–Server DDBS** | Functional split between requesters (clients) and processors (servers) |
| 4 | **Peer-to-Peer DDBS** | Every site is equal — no master, no slave |
| 5 | **Federated / Multi-DB** | Autonomous local DBs loosely coupled under a unified query interface |
| 6 | **Parallel DBMS** | Multiple CPUs/disks cooperate to speed up a single query |

---

## 1.4 Standardized Metric Definitions (KTU 2024)

- **Autonomy** – The degree to which an individual site can operate independently of the distributed system. (Measured on a *qualitative* scale: **None / Low / Medium / High / Full**.)
- **Heterogeneity** – Difference in **DBMS software, data model, query language, or schema** across sites.
- **Distribution Transparency** – The system's ability to hide the fact that data is distributed. The **12-objective / 3-level taxonomy (fragmentation, location, replication, etc.)** is the KTU standard benchmark.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant (Reference):** The **CAP Theorem** states that in any distributed data store you can simultaneously achieve **at most two** of: *Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance*. No system can satisfy all three at once.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — Homogeneous Distributed Database Systems

A DDBMS is **homogeneous** when **all sites run identical DBMS software** (same vendor, same version) and participate in a single, globally administered distributed schema.

### Logical Steps in Operation
1. **Global Schema Definition:** A single Database Administrator (DBA) team defines the *global conceptual schema* at a coordinating site.
2. **Fragmentation & Allocation:** Tables are fragmented (horizontal, vertical, hybrid) and the fragments are *allocated* to sites.
3. **Replication Management:** Replicas are kept consistent via a *single* distributed concurrency control protocol (e.g., distributed 2PL, Paxos).
4. **Query Processing:** A global query is decomposed at one site, sub-queries are sent to relevant sites, and results are merged.
5. **Transaction Management:** A single global transaction manager handles distributed commit (e.g., **Two-Phase Commit — 2PC**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why':** Homogeneity eliminates the need for *schema translation* and *query-language translation*, so the system is **simpler, faster, and more transparent** than heterogeneous systems. The trade-off is **loss of autonomy** and **vendor lock-in**.

### Sub-categories (KTU 2024)
- **Autonomous Homogeneous:** Each site *can* operate independently (rare in practice).
- **Non-Autonomous Homogeneous:** Sites are tightly controlled by a global DBA — the most common case.

---

## 2.2 Type 2 — Heterogeneous Distributed Database Systems

A DDBMS is **heterogeneous** when sites run **different DBMS products** (e.g., Oracle at one site, PostgreSQL at another, MySQL at a third) and possibly use **different data models** (relational, object, XML, NoSQL).

### Logical Steps in Operation
1. **Local Schemas Exist:** Each site has its own *local schema*, possibly in different data models.
2. **Global/Mediated Schema Created:** A *mediator* (top layer) and *wrappers/adaptors* (bottom layer) are defined.
3. **Query Translation:** A user query is parsed, rewritten into the local query language of each site, and dispatched.
4. **Result Translation:** Local results are converted to a common data model and merged.
5. **Schema Integration (One-Time):** Local schemas are integrated via a *global mediated schema* using schema-matching techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why':** Heterogeneity arises **organically** — companies merge, divisions have legacy systems, and cloud providers use different stacks. A heterogeneous system *preserves local autonomy* and *honors pre-existing investments* in legacy DBMSs.

### Sub-categories (KTU 2024)
- **Federated Database System (FDS):** Each local DBMS is *autonomous*; integration is **loose**.
  - **Loosely Coupled FDS:** No global schema; queries rewritten *on the fly*.
  - **Tightly Coupled FDS:** A single *global schema* exists; integration is *static*.
- **Mediator-Based Systems:** A *mediator* layer decomposes and routes queries, with *wrappers* at each site providing the local interface.
- **Data Warehouse / ETL Systems (Mentioned, Not Pure DDBS):** Data is *copied* and *transformed* into a central warehouse.

---

## 2.3 Type 3 — Client–Server Distributed Databases

A **Client–Server architecture** splits the DBMS functionality into two tiers:

- **Client Tier:** Manages the *user interface*, application logic, and some query processing.
- **Server Tier:** Manages *storage, indexing, query evaluation, and transaction management*.

### Logical Steps
1. The client issues a **SQL query** over the network (typically using ODBC/JDBC).
2. The **server** parses, optimizes, executes, and returns the result set.
3. For distributed queries, the *coordinator server* may delegate sub-queries to *participant servers*.
4. Concurrency control and recovery are typically centralized at the server tier.

### Variants
- **Two-Tier Client–Server:** Direct client-to-server communication.
- **Three-Tier (or N-Tier):** A *middleware/application server* sits between clients and DB servers (e.g., modern web apps using Tomcat + MySQL).
- **Multiple Server / Distributed Server:** Several DB servers cooperate (this blurs into the homogeneous DDBMS category).

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why':** Client–Server is the **most widely deployed model** in industry (think of any web app). It concentrates expensive resources (data) on the server and cheapens the client (a browser/phone).

---

## 2.4 Type 4 — Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Distributed Databases

In a **P2P system**, **every node is symmetric** — each node acts as both a *client* and a *server*. There is no central coordinator.

### Logical Steps
1. A node receives a query and may itself have data needed to answer it.
2. If the node cannot answer fully, it **forwards** the query to one or more *peers*.
3. **Routing overlays** (e.g., Chord, Pastry, CAN) maintain an index of *which peer holds which data*.
4. **Replication & self-organization** are common — peers may join/leave dynamically.

### Sub-categories
- **Structured P2P:** Uses a **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)** (Chord, Pastry, Kademlia).
- **Unstructured P2P:** Peers are loosely connected; queries are *flooded* or use *random walks* (Gnutella, early BitTorrent).
- **Hybrid P2P:** A few *super-peers* act as local coordinators, but the system is still decentralized globally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why':** P2P systems are **highly fault-tolerant** and **scalable to millions of nodes** (e.g., BitTorrent, IPFS, blockchain). The trade-off is **complex query semantics** and **weaker consistency guarantees** (often *eventual consistency*).

---

## 2.5 Type 5 — Federated / Multi-Database Systems

A **federated database system (FDBS)** is a special case of heterogeneous DDBMS where each participating database is **fully autonomous** — it can independently decide what to share, when, and how much.

### The 5-Level Schema Architecture (KTU 2024 Standard)
| Level | Name | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| 0 | **Local Internal Schema** | The physical storage schema of each local DB. |
| 1 | **Local Conceptual Schema** | The full logical schema of each local DB. |
| 2 | **Component Schema** | The *subset* of the local schema that the federation is *allowed to see*. |
| 3 | **Export Schema** | What each site is *willing to share* with others. |
| 4 | **Global/Federated Schema** | The *unified schema* seen by the global user. |

### Key Properties
- **Autonomy is maximal:** Local DBAs decide what enters the export schema.
- **Heterogeneity is handled** by *mediator* and *wrapper* layers.
- **No global transaction manager** in the loose-coupling case; local ACID is preserved, but *global ACID is not guaranteed*.

---

## 2.6 Type 6 — Parallel Database Systems

A **Parallel Database System** uses **multiple processors and disks** connected by a high-speed local network to **speed up query execution**. It is *tightly coupled* and physically co-located.

### Three Architectural Variants
| Architecture | Coupling | Pros | Cons |
|--------------|---------|------|------|
| **Shared Memory (SMP)** | All CPUs share one RAM and one disk | Simple, easy programming | Not scalable beyond ~32 CPUs |
| **Shared Disk (NUMA / cluster)** | CPUs have private memory, share disks | Easier load balancing | Disk I/O bottleneck, complex cache coherence |
| **Shared Nothing (MPP)** | Each node has its own CPU, memory, disk | **Best scalability** (used by Teradata, Spark, Snowflake) | Harder to program, complex data partitioning |

### Logical Steps
1. The query is **partitioned** across the cluster nodes (round-robin, range, hash).
2. Each node **processes its partition in parallel**.
3. **Intermediate results** are redistributed (e.g., shuffle phase in MapReduce/Spark).
4. A **coordinator node** aggregates the final result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Speedup vs. Scaleup:**
> - **Speedup:** Running a single job on $N$ nodes takes $1/N$ the time.
> - **Scaleup:** Running $N$ times the workload on $N$ nodes takes the same time as the original.
> - **Linear Speedup** = ideal but rarely achieved due to **skew, coordination, and I/O bottlenecks**.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---------|----------------|-------|
| **CAP Theorem** | $C \cap A \cap P \le 2$ | At most 2 of Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. |
| **Linear Speedup** | $T_{\text{parallel}}(N) = \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{N}$ | Ideal but degraded by *coordination overhead*. |
| **Scaleup** | $T(N, N \times W) = T(1, W)$ | $W$ = workload, $N$ = number of nodes. |
| **Amdahl's Law** | $T_{\text{parallel}} = T_{\text{serial}} \cdot \left( f_{\text{par}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N} + (1 - f_{\text{par}}) \right)$ | $f_{\text{par}}$ = parallel fraction; $N$ = nodes. |
| **Replication Factor** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Total number of replicas across all sites}}{\text{Number of distinct fragments}}$ | $R \ge 1$ always. |
| **Autonomy Score (qualitative)** | $\in \{ \text{None, Low, Medium, High, Full} \}$ | Used in KTU theory answers. |
| **2PC Cost** | $2 \times \text{message rounds} + 1 \text{ coordinator log write}$ | Default cost per distributed commit. |
| **CAP ↔ Architecture Mapping** | Homogeneous ≈ **CA**, P2P ≈ **AP**, Multi-DC RDBMS ≈ **CP** | Standard textbook mapping. |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing the formula for Amdahl's Law in the answer script, **always show the derivation** of $(1 - f_{\text{par}})$ — examiners allocate marks for the parallelizable-fraction reasoning.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking:** Federated systems allow a bank acquired through M&A to retain its legacy core while exposing data to the new unified app (e.g., HDFC Bank merger).
- **E-Commerce:** Parallel DBs power real-time recommendation engines (Amazon, Flipkart).
- **Telecom:** P2P architectures power VoIP and messaging (Skype classic used P2P).
- **Healthcare:** Heterogeneous systems integrate PACS (DICOM), HIS (HL7), and lab systems into one query layer.
- **Big Data / Cloud:** Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift are *shared-nothing parallel* databases at heart.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains the **deep analytical derivations** and **comparison matrices** that KTU expects in 14-mark ESE answers. Each derivation is fully worked out — no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Amdahl's Law Applied to a Parallel DDBMS

Amdahl's Law quantifies the **theoretical maximum speedup** of a parallel algorithm given a fraction of it that can be parallelized.

### Setup
Let:
- $T_{\text{serial}}$ = execution time on a single node.
- $f_{\text{par}}$ = fraction of the workload that can be parallelized.
- $(1 - f_{\text{par}})$ = fraction that is inherently sequential.
- $N$ = number of parallel nodes (sites / processors).

### Derivation
The total time on $N$ nodes is the sum of:
1. The **sequential part** (cannot be sped up): $T_{\text{seq}} = (1 - f_{\text{par}}) \cdot T_{\text{serial}}$.
2. The **parallel part** (divided across $N$ nodes): $T_{\text{par}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{par}} \cdot T_{\text{serial}}}{N}$.

Adding them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{parallel}}(N) &= T_{\text{seq}} + T_{\text{par}} \\
&= (1 - f_{\text{par}}) \cdot T_{\text{serial}} + \dfrac{f_{\text{par}} \cdot T_{\text{serial}}}{N} \\
&= T_{\text{serial}} \cdot \left[ (1 - f_{\text{par}}) + \dfrac{f_{\text{par}}}{N} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Speedup** $S(N)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}(N)} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f_{\text{par}}) + \dfrac{f_{\text{par}}}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic (line by line):**
- Line 1 → Line 2: Substitute the two time components.
- Line 2 → Line 3: Factor out $T_{\text{serial}}$.
- Line 4 → Line 5: Divide $T_{\text{serial}}$ by $T_{\text{parallel}}$ to invert.

### Limiting Behavior
As $N \to \infty$ (infinite nodes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f_{\text{par}}) + 0} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1 - f_{\text{par}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** If only 90% of a query is parallelizable ($f_{\text{par}} = 0.9$), the **maximum possible speedup** is $\dfrac{1}{1 - 0.9} = 10\times$ — *no matter how many nodes you add*. This is why sequential parts (e.g., final aggregation) bottleneck a parallel DDBMS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost of 2-Phase Commit in a Homogeneous DDBMS

The **Two-Phase Commit (2PC)** protocol guarantees atomicity in a homogeneous DDBMS. Let's compute its **message cost**.

### Setup
- $N$ = number of participating sites.
- $C$ = coordinator site.

### Phase 1 (Voting Phase)
1. Coordinator sends **VOTE-REQUEST** to all $N$ participants.
2. Each participant sends **VOTE-COMMIT** or **VOTE-ABORT** back to coordinator.
- Messages in Phase 1: $N + N = 2N$ messages.

### Phase 2 (Decision Phase)
1. Coordinator sends **GLOBAL-COMMIT** or **GLOBAL-ABORT** to all $N$ participants.
2. Each participant sends **ACK** to coordinator.
- Messages in Phase 2: $N + N = 2N$ messages.

### Total

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{2PC}}(N) &= 2N + 2N \\
&= 4N
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the coordinator is *itself* one of the participants, subtract 2 to get $M = 4N - 2$.

### Time Complexity
Assuming each message takes $t$ time units and the operations are sequential:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{2PC}}(N) &= 4 \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic for the student:** KTU examiners expect you to *draw the diagram* (Coordinator ↔ Participants) and *count the arrows*. Always state assumptions: "Assuming all messages are acknowledged and no failures."

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Replication Factor and Storage Overhead

Let a relation $R$ be **fragmented** into $F$ fragments and **replicated** across $S$ sites with replication factor $R$.

### Total Number of Physical Copies

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Copies} &= F \times S \times \dfrac{R}{F} \\
&= F \times S \times \dfrac{1}{1} \quad \text{(if each fragment is replicated $r$ times)} \\
&= F \times r
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let me restate cleanly. If each fragment $f_i$ is replicated $r_i$ times (possibly different per fragment), the total number of stored copies is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Copies} &= \sum_{i=1}^{F} r_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The **average replication factor** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{r} &= \dfrac{1}{F} \sum_{i=1}^{F} r_i
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** $F = 3$ fragments with replication $r_1 = 2, r_2 = 3, r_3 = 2$. Then $\bar{r} = \dfrac{2+3+2}{3} = \dfrac{7}{3} \approx 2.33$. Storage overhead is $133\%$ of the un-replicated size.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All 6 Types

This is the **single most important table** for a KTU 14-mark question.

| Property | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer | Federated | Parallel |
|----------|------------|---------------|---------------|--------------|-----------|----------|
| **Same DBMS?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Usually | ⚠️ Often | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Autonomy** | Low–Medium | High | Low (client) / High (server) | High | **Full** | None |
| **Heterogeneity** | None | **High** | Low | Medium | **High** | None |
| **Transparency** | **Full** | Partial | Partial | Minimal | Partial | **Full** |
| **Consistency** | Strong (2PC) | Variable | Strong | **Eventual** | Variable | Strong |
| **Scalability** | Medium | Medium | Medium | **Very High** | Low–Medium | **Very High** |
| **Failure Tolerance** | Medium | Low | Low | **High** | Low | Medium |
| **Query Complexity** | Medium | **High** | Low | **High** | High | Medium |
| **CAP Trade-off** | CA / CP | Varies | CA / CP | **AP** | CA | CA / CP |
| **Real-World Example** | Oracle RAC | Enterprise data hub | Web application | BitTorrent | LinkedIn data mesh | Teradata, Snowflake |

---

## 3.5 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Type

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \text{ Are all sites under the same DBA and DBMS?} \\
&\quad \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Homogeneous DDBS} \\
&\quad \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Step 2} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Step 2:} \text{ Do sites need to operate fully independently?} \\
&\quad \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Federated / Multi-DB} \\
&\quad \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Step 3} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Step 3:} \text{ Is the workload OLTP or analytical?} \\
&\quad \text{Analytical, huge data} \rightarrow \text{Parallel DBMS} \\
&\quad \text{OLTP, web-facing} \rightarrow \text{Step 4} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Step 4:} \text{ Is there a central authority for queries?} \\
&\quad \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Client–Server} \\
&\quad \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Peer-to-Peer}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic:** In the answer, frame this as a *flowchart* using Mermaid (next section) — KTU awards full marks for visual clarity.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Speedup Calculation

**Question:** A query in a parallel DDBMS has $f_{\text{par}} = 0.95$. Compute the speedup with $N = 8$ and $N = 32$ nodes.

### Solution
Using $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f_{\text{par}}) + \dfrac{f_{\text{par}}}{N}}$:

For $N = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{8}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.11875} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.16875} \\
&\approx 5.93
\end{aligned}
$$

For $N = 32$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(32) &= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{32}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.02969} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.07969} \\
&\approx 12.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- $0.05$ is the *sequential bottleneck* (e.g., final result aggregation).
- Even quadrupling nodes from 8 to 32 only doubles the speedup — confirming Amdahl's pessimistic prediction.
- **Lesson:** Optimize the sequential part first!

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are **Mermaid flowcharts / block diagrams**. Each is KTU-examiner-friendly (clear labels, no special characters inside node IDs).

---

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of DDBMS Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Distributed Database System]:::root --> B[By Homogeneity]
    A --> C[By Autonomy]
    A --> D[By Architecture]

    B --> B1[Homogeneous<br/>Same DBMS everywhere]
    B --> B2[Heterogeneous<br/>Different DBMSs]

    C --> C1[No Autonomy<br/>Parallel DBMS]
    C --> C2[Loose Autonomy<br/>Homogeneous Client Server]
    C --> C3[Full Autonomy<br/>Federated Multi DB]

    D --> D1[Centralized<br/>Client Server]
    D --> D2[Decentralized<br/>Peer to Peer P2P]
    D --> D3[Tightly Coupled<br/>Shared Nothing Cluster]

    B1 --> E1[Distributed 2PC<br/>Strong Consistency]
    B2 --> E2[Mediator + Wrappers<br/>Schema Translation]

    D2 --> D2a[Structured P2P<br/>DHT based]
    D2 --> D2b[Unstructured P2P<br/>Flood based]
    D2 --> D2c[Hybrid P2P<br/>Super peers]

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Homogeneous DDBMS — Global Schema Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GS[Global Schema Layer]
        GCS[Global Conceptual Schema]:::global
        GES[Global External Schema]:::global
    end

    subgraph FRAG[Fragmentation and Allocation Layer]
        F1[Fragment 1 of Relation R]:::frag
        F2[Fragment 2 of Relation R]:::frag
        F3[Fragment 3 of Relation R]:::frag
    end

    subgraph S1[Site 1 - Bangalore]
        S1DB[(Local DB<br/>Oracle 19c)]:::site
    end
    subgraph S2[Site 2 - Mumbai]
        S2DB[(Local DB<br/>Oracle 19c)]:::site

    GCS -.Defines.-> F2
    GCS -.Defines.-> F3

    F2 --> S2DB
    F3 --> S3DB
```
