# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed database systems. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Homogeneous DDBS | Heterogeneous DDBS |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| **DBMS** | Identical across all sites | Different DBMSs at different sites |
| **Schema** | Single global schema | Multiple local schemas; translation needed |
| **Autonomy** | Low to medium | High |
| **Complexity** | Lower (no translation) | Higher (mediator + wrappers required) |
| **Example** | Oracle RAC across 4 data centers | Bank merger: Oracle (acquirer) + DB2 (legacy) + MySQL (web app) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Hint:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for 2 differences, 1 mark for example.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a federated database system? List its 5 levels of schema architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Federated Database System (FDBS)** is a collection of *autonomous*, possibly *heterogeneous* local databases that cooperate to provide a unified, logical view of their data without giving up local autonomy.

**5-Level Schema Architecture:**
1. **Level 0:** Local Internal Schema (physical storage)
2. **Level 1:** Local Conceptual Schema (full local logic)
3. **Level 2:** Component Schema (subset visible to federation)
4. **Level 3:** Export Schema (what the site is willing to share)
5. **Level 4:** Global/Federated Schema (unified user view)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exam:** *"Lions Climb Cliffs Effortlessly Going up"* (Local Internal, Local Conceptual, Component, Export, Global).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module 2 Internal Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines, each Module 2 Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **classification of distributed database systems** based on **homogeneity, autonomy, and architecture**. Draw a master classification diagram.

**(b) [7 marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **mediator-wrapper architecture** for a **heterogeneous distributed database system**. Discuss the **5-level schema architecture** of a federated database system.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Classification of DDBS (1 mark for introduction + 4 marks for explanation + 2 marks for diagram):**

A Distributed Database System can be classified along **three orthogonal dimensions**:

**1. By Homogeneity (2 marks)**
- **Homogeneous DDBS:** All sites use the *same DBMS product* (e.g., all Oracle 19c). Single global schema, easy distribution transparency. No schema translation overhead. Example: Oracle RAC cluster.
- **Heterogeneous DDBS:** Sites use *different DBMSs* (Oracle + MySQL + PostgreSQL). Schema translation, query rewriting required. Example: Bank merger scenario.

**2. By Autonomy (2 marks)**
- **No Autonomy:** Parallel DBMS — single coordinated system.
- **Low Autonomy:** Client–Server — clients depend on servers.
- **High Autonomy:** Homogeneous DDBS with semi-independent sites.
- **Full Autonomy:** Federated DBMS — each site is fully independent.

**3. By Architecture (2 marks)**
- **Client–Server:** Centralized functional split.
- **Peer-to-Peer:** Decentralized; all nodes symmetric.
- **Shared-Nothing Cluster (Parallel):** Tightly coupled MPP.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the master classification tree from Section 4.1.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Mediator-Wrapper Architecture (4 marks):**

In a heterogeneous DDBS, the system uses a **two-layer integration** approach:

- **Mediator (Top Layer):** A logical, virtual component that:
  - Maintains a **Mediated Schema** (a unified logical schema).
  - **Decomposes** global queries into sub-queries.
  - **Integrates** partial results from multiple sites.
  - Performs **query optimization** across sites.
  - Does *not* store data itself.

- **Wrappers (Bottom Layer):** One per data source; they:
  - **Translate** the local DBMS's query language to/from the mediated schema.
  - **Convert** data formats and result tuples.
  - **Expose** only the *capabilities* the site is willing to share.
  - Handle local query execution and return results.

**Communication Flow (2 marks):**
1. User submits query in *global* language.
2. Mediator decomposes the query.
3. Sub-queries sent to relevant wrappers.
4. Wrappers translate to local SQL (or other).
5. Local DBs execute and return data.
6. Wrappers translate results to common format.
7. Mediator integrates and returns to user.

**5-Level Schema Architecture of FDBS (1 mark):**
As listed in Q2 of Part A (Levels 0 to 4).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to mention that the **Mediator is virtual** — it does *not* physically store data. They also confuse *Wrappers* with *Replicas*. Make sure you clearly state that wrappers are *adapters*, not *copies* of data.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **client–server**, **peer-to-peer**, and **parallel** distributed database architectures. Highlight their **CAP trade-offs** and **typical use cases**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A query in a parallel DDBMS has 95% parallelizable workload. Using **Amdahl's Law**, calculate the **speedup** for $N = 4, 8, 16$ nodes. Comment on the result.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Property | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer | Parallel (Shared-Nothing) |
|----------|---------------|--------------|---------------------------|
| **Topology** | Star/hub-and-spoke | Mesh/graph | Cluster (high-speed LAN) |
| **Coordination** | Centralized (server) | Decentralized | Centralized coordinator + parallel workers |
| **Consistency** | Strong (single server) | Eventual | Strong (with 2PC) |
| **Availability** | Low (server = SPOF) | Very high | Medium |
| **CAP Trade-off** | CA / CP | **AP** | CA / CP |
| **Scalability** | Medium | Very high (millions of nodes) | Very high (hundreds of nodes) |
| **Use Case** | Web apps, OLTP | File sharing, blockchain | Data warehousing, analytics |
| **Example** | Tomcat + MySQL | BitTorrent, IPFS | Snowflake, Teradata, Spark SQL |

**Explanation (3 marks for table + 2 marks for CAP analysis + 2 marks for use cases):**
- **Client–Server** prioritizes **Consistency** and **Availability** (CA) but suffers a single point of failure.
- **P2P** sacrifices **Consistency** for **Availability** and **Partition tolerance** (AP) — accepts *eventual consistency*.
- **Parallel DBMS** uses a high-speed, low-failure-probability interconnect to maintain **CA** properties; partition tolerance is implicit because the cluster is co-located.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $f_{\text{par}} = 0.95$, so $(1 - f_{\text{par}}) = 0.05$.

**Formula:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f_{\text{par}}) + \dfrac{f_{\text{par}}}{N}}$

**For N = 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{4}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.2375} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.2875} \\
&\approx 3.48
\end{aligned}
$$

**For N = 8:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{8}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.11875} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.16875} \\
&\approx 5.93
\end{aligned}
$$

**For N = 16:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(16) &= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{16}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.059375} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.109375} \\
&\approx 9.14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (2 marks for analysis):**

1. **Diminishing returns observed:** Going from 4→8 nodes roughly **doubles** speedup (3.48→5.93, ratio 1.7), but going from 8→16 only adds **54%** (5.93→9.14, ratio 1.54).
2. **Theoretical maximum:** As $N \to \infty$, $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20\times$.
3. **Implication:** The **5% sequential part** is the bottleneck. Optimization efforts should focus on reducing $1 - f_{\text{par}}$ rather than adding more nodes.
4. **Practical recommendation:** Use $N = 16$ or fewer in production; cost of additional nodes outweighs marginal benefit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common error is writing $1 - f_{\text{par}} = 0.05$ but using the *wrong fraction* (e.g., 0.5 instead of 0.05) when substituting into the denominator. Re-read the problem carefully — *95% parallelizable* means the **5% is sequential**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Types of DDBS**

### 🔑 The 6 Types (must-memorize)
- ☐ **Homogeneous** — same DBMS, easy transparency, less autonomy.
- ☐ **Heterogeneous** — different DBMS, mediator + wrappers, complex.
- ☐ **Client–Server** — 2-tier or 3-tier, server-centric.
- ☐ **Peer-to-Peer** — fully decentralized, DHT or flood routing.
- ☐ **Federated** — 5-level schema, full autonomy, loose coupling.
- ☐ **Parallel** — shared memory / shared disk / **shared nothing** (most scalable).

### 🔑 The 3 Classification Axes
- ☐ **Homogeneity** (same vs. different DBMS)
- ☐ **Autonomy** (none / low / medium / high / full)
- ☐ **Architecture** (centralized / decentralized / cluster)

### 🔑 Key Formulas
- ☐ **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f_{\text{par}}) + \dfrac{f_{\text{par}}}{N}}$
- ☐ **2PC Message Cost:** $M = 4N$ (or $4N - 2$ if coordinator is a participant)
- ☐ **Speedup Limit:** $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f_{\text{par}}}$
- ☐ **CAP Theorem:** at most 2 of {C, A, P}

### 🔑 Must-Draw Diagrams (for full marks)
- ☐ Mediator-Wrapper block diagram
- ☐ 5-level federated schema stack
- ☐ Shared-nothing parallel cluster
- ☐ Client–Server 3-tier

### 🔑 Common KTU Pitfalls
- ☐ Confusing **wrapper** with **replica** — wrappers are *adapters*, not *copies*.
- ☐ Forgetting that **mediator is virtual** — no data is stored in the mediator.
- ☐ Saying *"P2P is more secure than client–server"* — it's not inherently; it has *different* security models.
- ☐ Mixing up **P2P** with **Parallel** — P2P is *geographically distributed*; Parallel is *co-located*.
- ☐ Claiming Federated systems give **full ACID** — they don't (no global transaction manager in loose coupling).

### 🔑 Real-World Mapping (memorize for viva)
- ☐ **HDFC Bank merger** → Federated
- ☐ **Amazon RDS Multi-AZ** → Homogeneous Client–Server
- ☐ **Snowflake** → Parallel Shared-Nothing
- ☐ **Bitcoin ledger** → Peer-to-Peer
- ☐ **ICICI's data lake integrating legacy Oracle + new MongoDB** → Heterogeneous with Mediator

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always start a 14-mark answer with a **classification table** (5 lines) and a **master diagram** (1 figure). This alone fetches 4–5 marks before you write any prose.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Types of Distributed Database Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Query Processing and Decomposition: Query Processing Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Query Processing and Decomposition: Query Processing Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Query Processing** in a Distributed Database Management System (DDBMS) is the systematic sequence of activities undertaken by the database system to translate a high-level user query (typically written in SQL) into a low-level, optimized execution plan that retrieves the requested data from one or more distributed data sources. The fundamental **objective** is to minimize the total **response time**, **resource consumption**, and **network bandwidth** while ensuring **correctness** and **completeness** of the result set.

In the context of **PECDT795 - Advanced Database Systems**, query processing objectives are categorized across three critical sub-phases:
1. **Query Decomposition** (the focus of this module) — converting the calculus-based query into an algebraic query.
2. **Query Localization (Data Localization)** — converting the distributed query into a fragmented query using reduction techniques.
3. **Global Query Optimization** — determining the most cost-effective join ordering, semi-join reductions, and execution site selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2, Section 2.1):**
> The student **must** be able to distinguish between the three layers of distributed query processing: *Decomposition*, *Localization*, and *Optimization*. Examiner tip: When asked "List the objectives of query processing," write **at least five distinct objectives** including *correctness*, *minimal cost*, *minimum response time*, and *reduced bandwidth*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pizza Delivery" Model

Imagine you are running a national pizza chain. A customer places an order: *"I want one large pepperoni pizza with extra cheese and olives."*

- **The Order (Query):** The customer's verbal or written request.
- **The Central Kitchen (Query Processor):** Receives the order.
- **The Regional Branches (Distributed Sites):** Each has different ingredients (data).
- **The Chef's Task List (Decomposition):** Break "large pepperoni with extra cheese and olives" into subtasks: *fetch dough*, *fetch pepperoni*, *fetch cheese*, *fetch olives*, *bake*, *slice*, *deliver*.
- **The Optimization:** Should the olives come from Branch A (which has fresh ones today) or Branch B (which is closer but stale)? Should we pre-bake the crust while gathering toppings? **This is exactly what distributed query optimization does** — it decides the cheapest, fastest route to assemble the answer.
- **The Delivery Boy (Execution):** Carries the finished pizza to the customer, just as the execution engine carries the result tuples back.

### 1.3 The Five Core Objectives of Distributed Query Processing

The KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum emphasizes that query processing in distributed databases must satisfy the following measurable objectives:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Golden Objectives of Query Processing**
> 1. **Correctness** — The result must be equivalent to centralized execution.
> 2. **Minimal Total Cost** — Sum of local processing cost (I/O + CPU) + communication cost.
> 3. **Minimum Response Time** — Maximize parallelism to reduce wall-clock time.
> 4. **Maximum Throughput** — Process as many queries per unit time as possible.
> 5. **Minimal Network Bandwidth Consumption** — Reduce data shipped across sites via semi-joins and fragmentation-aware processing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Query Processing

#### **Phase 1 — Query Decomposition (Module 2 Focus)**

This phase is entirely **independent of distribution**. It operates purely on the query's *semantics*, not its physical location.

**Step 1: Parsing & Validation**
- The system parses the SQL string into a **parse tree** using the grammar of the query language.
- Validates relation names, attribute names, and data types against the **global conceptual schema**.
- Constructs an **internal representation** (typically a Query Graph or Relational Algebra tree).

**Step 2: Normalization**
- Converts the relational calculus query into a **Normalized Form** suitable for further manipulation.
- Predicates are converted into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** or **Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF):**
> A predicate is in CNF if it is a conjunction ($\land$) of one or more disjunctions ($\lor$) of literals. For KTU, you must show normalization of predicates involving AND/OR/NOT into CNF.

**Step 3: Analysis (Semantic Query Optimization)**
- Detects and rejects **contradictory queries** (e.g., `age > 30 AND age < 20`).
- Simplifies using **integrity constraints** (e.g., if `age >= 0` is a constraint, drop `age >= -5`).
- Performs **type checking** and view expansion.

**Step 4: Rewriting (Algebraic Optimization)**
- The normalized query is transformed into an **equivalent relational algebra tree**.
- Applies heuristic rules (commutativity, associativity, idempotency) to obtain an initial **canonical algebra expression**.

---

#### **Phase 2 — Data Localization (Reduction)**

Transforms the global algebra query into one that references only **physical fragments**.

- **Reduction by Primary Horizontal Fragmentation:** Replace relation R by union of its fragments $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$.
- **Reduction by Vertical Fragmentation:** Replace R by **JOIN** of its vertical fragments on the common primary-key attribute.
- **Reduction by Hybrid Fragmentation:** Combination of horizontal and vertical rules.
- **Reduction by Derived Horizontal Fragmentation:** Propagate joins via semi-join programs.

---

#### **Phase 3 — Global Query Optimization**

Determines the **optimal execution strategy** for the localized query.

- **Cost-Based Optimization:** Uses statistics (cardinality, selectivity, network latency) to evaluate a cost function.
- **Join Ordering Problem:** For *n* relations, there are $(2n)! / (n+1)$ possible join orderings (Catalan's number growth).
- **Semi-Join Reduction (SDD-1 Algorithm):** Eliminates non-contributing tuples *before* transmission to minimize communication cost.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cost Model)

In distributed query processing, the **Total Cost** is computed using the following standard cost model (commonly tested in KTU):

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{IO}$ | Cost of a single disk I/O (read/write) | seconds |
| $C_{CPU}$ | Cost of CPU instruction execution | seconds |
| $C_{MSG}$ | Cost of initiating a network message (fixed overhead) | seconds |
| $C_{TR}$ | Cost of transmitting one byte (or one tuple) across the network | seconds / byte |
| $T(R)$ | Number of tuples in relation R | tuples |
| $S(R)$ | Size of one tuple of relation R | bytes |
| $|R|$ | Total size of relation R in bytes ($= T(R) \cdot S(R)$) | bytes |

---

### 2.3 The Core Cost Equations

**Equation 1 — Total Cost of a Query (KTU Standard Form):**

$$Total\_Cost = CPU\_Cost + I/O\_Cost + Communication\_Cost$$

**Equation 2 — Communication Cost for a Single Transfer:**

$$Comm\_Cost = C_{MSG} \cdot n_{msg} + C_{TR} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n_{msg}} \vert data_i \vert$$

where $n_{msg}$ is the number of messages and $\vert data_i \vert$ is the size in bytes of the $i$-th message.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Many students forget the **$C_{MSG}$ fixed cost** (message initiation overhead). In LANs, $C_{MSG}$ is small; in WANs, it dominates. Always include *both* terms.

**Equation 3 — I/O Cost for Sequential Scan:**

$$I/O\_Cost_{seq}(R) = \lceil \vert R \vert / Page\_Size \rceil$$

**Equation 4 — I/O Cost for Indexed Access (B+ Tree of order d, height h):**

$$I/O\_Cost_{idx}(R) = h + 1$$

**Equation 5 — Estimated Result Size after Selection with selectivity $\sigma$:**

$$\vert \sigma_{\theta}(R) \vert = \sigma \cdot \vert R \vert$$

**Equation 6 — Estimated Result Size after Join (worst-case cartesian bound):**

$$\vert R \bowtie S \vert \le \vert R \vert \cdot \vert S \vert$$

**Equation 7 — Semi-Join Result Size:**

$$\vert R \ltimes S \vert \le \vert R \vert$$

**Equation 8 — Site Selection Cost (Process at Site i):**

$$Cost_{site\_i} = I/O(R) + I/O(S) + C_{TR} \cdot \vert R \bowtie S \vert + C_{CPU} \cdot T(R \bowtie S)$$

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Objectives

| Engineering Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Cloud Data Warehousing** (Snowflake, BigQuery) | Cost-based optimization decides whether to push computation to data or stream data to compute. |
| **Telecom Billing Systems** | Distributed query across regional DBs for monthly billing — response time < 5 sec is critical. |
| **E-Commerce Search (Amazon, Flipkart)** | Federated query across catalog, inventory, and pricing shards — minimum bandwidth is paramount. |
| **Banking Core Systems** | Real-time fraud detection joins transaction stream against a global blacklist — correctness and throughput dominate. |
| **IoT Sensor Networks** | Edge-to-cloud query processing uses semi-joins to filter raw sensor data before transmission. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Cost Comparison of Two Join Strategies

> **Problem:** Two relations **R** (at Site 1) and **S** (at Site 2) need to be joined.
> - $T(R) = 1000$ tuples, $S(R) = 100$ bytes $\Rightarrow$ $\vert R \vert = 100{,}000$ bytes
> - $T(S) = 5000$ tuples, $S(S) = 50$ bytes $\Rightarrow$ $\vert S \vert = 250{,}000$ bytes
> - Page size = 1000 bytes $\Rightarrow$ 100 pages for R, 250 pages for S
> - $C_{MSG} = 1$ second, $C_{TR} = 0.01$ sec/1000 bytes
> - **Strategy A:** Ship R to Site 2.
> - **Strategy B:** Ship S to Site 1.

We must compute which strategy is optimal.

---

#### **Step 1 — Compute I/O Cost (same for both strategies, processing occurs at destination)**

$$I/O(R) = \lceil 100{,}000 / 1000 \rceil = 100 \text{ pages}$$

$$I/O(S) = \lceil 250{,}000 / 1000 \rceil = 250 \text{ pages}$$

$$Total\_IO = 100 + 250 = 350 \text{ pages}$$

---

#### **Step 2 — Communication Cost for Strategy A (Ship R to Site 2)**

The relation R is transmitted as a single bulk message:

$$Comm\_Cost_A = C_{MSG} + C_{TR} \cdot \vert R \vert$$

Substituting values (note: $C_{TR}$ is per 1000 bytes, so we divide $\vert R \vert$ by 1000):

$$Comm\_Cost_A = 1 + (0.01 \cdot 100) = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ seconds}$$

---

#### **Step 3 — Communication Cost for Strategy B (Ship S to Site 1)**

$$Comm\_Cost_B = C_{MSG} + C_{TR} \cdot \vert S \vert$$

$$Comm\_Cost_B = 1 + (0.01 \cdot 250) = 1 + 2.5 = 3.5 \text{ seconds}$$

---

#### **Step 4 — Total Cost Comparison**

$$Total\_Cost_A = 350 + 2 = 352 \text{ (units)}$$

$$Total\_Cost_B = 350 + 3.5 = 353.5 \text{ (units)}$$

---

#### **Step 5 — Conclusion**

**Strategy A (ship the smaller relation R to Site 2)** is cheaper by **1.5 seconds** because $\vert R \vert < \vert S \vert$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Heuristic Rule (KTU Exam Favorite):** In a two-site join without indexes, *ship the smaller relation* to the site of the larger one. For *n* sites, use a **greedy or dynamic programming** approach.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs Between Objectives

| Objective | Mathematical Driver | Algorithm Used | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimize Total Cost | $\sum (IO + CPU + Comm)$ | Dynamic Programming (DP), Greedy | Requires accurate statistics |
| Minimize Response Time | $\max(t_{site_i})$ parallel path | Parallel Hash Join, Bushy Trees | High coordination overhead |
| Minimize Bandwidth | $\vert data\_shipped \vert$ | Semi-Join (SDD-1), Bloom Join | May increase local I/O |
| Maximize Throughput | Queries / sec | Multi-query optimization | Complex to implement |
| Guarantee Correctness | Equivalence rules | Query Rewriting | May miss optimal plan |

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis: Decomposition vs. Localization vs. Optimization

| Aspect | Decomposition | Localization | Optimization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concerns** | Query correctness | Fragmentation | Execution cost |
| **Input** | SQL Query | Algebra tree from decomposition | Localized algebra tree |
| **Output** | Canonical algebra tree | Fragment-aware algebra tree | Best execution plan |
| **Key Operation** | Normalization, semantic analysis | Reduction by selection / join | Join ordering, semi-join |
| **Distribution-Aware?** | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **KTU Module Mapping** | **Module 2 (this topic)** | Module 3 | Module 4 |

---

### 3.4 Optimization Criteria — In-Depth Derivation

Let $Q$ be the set of all equivalent relational algebra expressions for a given query.

The **optimal query** $Q_{opt}$ is defined as:

$$Q_{opt} = \arg\min_{Q_i \in \mathcal{Q}} Cost(Q_i)$$

The cost function for a distributed query with operators $\{op_1, op_2, \ldots, op_k\}$ is:

$$Cost(Q) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \left[ I/O(op_j) + CPU(op_j) \right] + \sum_{m=1}^{M} \left[ C_{MSG} + C_{TR} \cdot \vert data_m \vert \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Connection:** The above equation links to **distributed join algorithms** (Nested Loop, Sort-Merge, Hash Join). For each join algorithm, the $I/O$ and $CPU$ terms are computed differently. A common 14-mark question asks: *"Compare Sort-Merge Join and Hash Join in distributed systems using the cost model above."*

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Semi-Join Cost Derivation (SDD-1 Style)

Let relations **R** at Site 1 and **S** at Site 2 be joined on attribute A.

**Step 1 — Project the join attribute at Site 2 and ship it to Site 1:**

$$Cost_{proj} = C_{MSG} + C_{TR} \cdot \vert \pi_A(S) \vert$$

**Step 2 — At Site 1, compute semi-join R ⋉ πA(S):**

$$R' = R \ltimes \pi_A(S)$$

This eliminates tuples of R whose A-values do not appear in S, reducing $T(R)$ to a smaller $T(R')$.

**Step 3 — Ship the reduced R' to Site 2:**

$$Cost_{ship} = C_{MSG} + C_{TR} \cdot \vert R' \vert$$

**Step 4 — Perform final join at Site 2:**

$$R' \bowtie S$$

> [!TIP]
> **When is semi-join beneficial?**
> Semi-join reduces bandwidth **iff** the projection is much smaller than the full relation and the join selectivity is low. Otherwise, the two extra message rounds ($C_{MSG} \cdot 2$) outweigh the savings.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Distributed Query Processing — Three-Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q1 --> S1[1. Parsing and Validation]

    S3 --> S4[4. Algebraic Rewriting]
    S4 --> AT[Canonical Relational Algebra Tree]
    
    AT --> Q2{Data Localization Phase}
    
    Q2 --> L1[Reduction by Horizontal Fragmentation]
    Q2 --> L2[Reduction by Vertical Fragmentation]
    Q2 --> L3[Reduction by Derived Fragmentation]
    L1 --> LAT[Localized Algebra Tree]
    L2 --> LAT
    L3 --> LAT
    
    LAT --> Q3{Global Query Optimization Phase}
    
    Q3 --> O1[Cost Based Optimizer]
    Q3 --> O2[Semi Join Reducer SDD-1]

    EP --> EX[Execution at Distributed Sites]
    EX --> RES[Final Result Returned to User]

```

```mermaid
    UP --> NX{More Plans to Evaluate?}

    NX -->|Yes| P
```
