No DTD/Schema is required. **Mandatory Rules for Well-Formedness:** 1. **Single Root Element** — exactly one outermost element. 2. **Properly Nested Tags** — overlapping tags are illegal. - ✅ `` - ❌ `` *(crossed — illegal)* 3. **Closing Tag Required** — every start-tag must have an end-tag. - Exception: **Empty-element** tags like `
` (self-closing). 4. **Case Sensitivity** — `` ≠ ``. 5. **Attribute Quotation** — values must be inside `"..."` or `'...'`. 6. **Quoted Attributes** — no unquoted values allowed. 7. **Entity References** — special characters (`<`, `>`, `&`, `'`, `"`) must be escaped: - `<` = `<` - `>` = `>` - `&` = `&` - `'` = `'` - `"` = `"` 8. **Unique Attributes** — no duplicate attribute names within the same element. ### 2.1.2 Valid XML A document is **valid** if it is: - **Well-formed**, AND - **Conforms to a DTD or XML Schema** defining structure, content, and data types. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** A well-formed letter has proper grammar and spelling. A *valid* letter also follows a specific *template* (e.g., a job-application format with mandatory sections). ## 2.2 XML Components — Element-By-Element Analysis ### 2.2.1 Elements Elements are the **primary structural units** of an XML document. They are defined by: - **Start-tag:** `` - **Content:** (text, child elements, or both — *mixed content*) - **End-tag:** `` **Naming Constraints (KTU High-Yield):** | Rule | Valid Example | Invalid Example | |---|---|---| | Must start with letter or underscore | `<_book>`, `` | `<1book>`, `<-book>` | | Can contain letters, digits, hyphens, dots, underscores | `` | `` *(space)* | | Cannot start with `xml` (case-insensitive) | `` | `` | | No whitespace | `` | `` | ### 2.2.2 Attributes Attributes provide **metadata about elements** within the start-tag. ```xml ``` **Rules:** - Always enclosed in `start-tag` (or `empty-element tag`). - Values must be **quoted** (single or double). - **No duplicate attribute names** in same element. - Order of attributes is **irrelevant**. - An element can have **multiple attributes** separated by whitespace. > [!TIP] > **KTU Pitfall — Attributes vs. Elements:** > The *W3C Position* recommends using **elements** for *data* and **attributes** only for *metadata*. Reasons: > 1. Attributes cannot contain multiple values (except via IDREFS). > 2. Attributes cannot describe structure (no nested attributes). > 3. Attributes are not easily expandable. ### 2.2.3 CDATA Sections Used to embed text containing reserved characters **without escaping**. ```xml ``` ### 2.2.4 PCDATA vs. CDATA - **PCDATA** (Parsed Character Data) — text that is parsed by the XML processor; entities are expanded. - **CDATA** (Character Data) — text passed **as-is** to the application; not parsed. ## 2.3 DTD (Document Type Definition) A **DTD** specifies the legal building blocks of an XML document. It can be: - **Internal DTD** — embedded in the XML file. - **External DTD** — stored in a separate `.dtd` file. - **Combined** — both. ### 2.3.1 DTD Declaration Patterns **Element Declaration:** ```dtd ``` | Content Model | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | `EMPTY` | No content | `` | | `ANY` | Anything allowed | `` | | `(#PCDATA)` | Text only | `` | | `(child1, child2)` | Sequence, mandatory order | `` | | `(child1 \vert child2)` | Either/or choice | `` | | `(child)+` | One or more | `` | | `(child)*` | Zero or more | `` | | `(child)?` | Zero or one (optional) | `` | **Attribute Declaration:** ```dtd ``` **Attribute Types:** - `CDATA` — character data - `ID` — unique identifier within document - `IDREF` — reference to an existing ID - `IDREFS` — multiple IDREFs, space-separated - `NMTOKEN` — valid XML name token - `NMTOKENS` — multiple NMTOKENs - `(enum1 \vert enum2)` — enumerated values - `ENTITY`, `ENTITIES` — reference to unparsed entity - `NOTATION` — notation type - `xml:` — predefined namespace **Attribute Default Values:** - `#REQUIRED` — must be present - `#IMPLIED` — optional, no default - `#FIXED "value"` — must equal given value - `"default"` — default if absent ### 2.3.2 DTD Limitations (Why XML Schema was invented) 1. Limited data types — only string-like types. 2. No namespace support. 3. No element occurrence constraints beyond `?`, `*`, `+`. 4. Cannot enforce uniqueness across documents. 5. Written in non-XML syntax (separate parser needed). 6. Limited extensibility. ## 2.4 XML Schema (XSD — XML Schema Definition) **XML Schema** is the **W3C-recommended successor** to DTD. It is itself written in XML, supports **rich data types**, and provides stronger validation. ### 2.4.1 XSD Data Types **Primitive Types:** - `string`, `boolean`, `decimal`, `float`, `double` - `dateTime`, `date`, `time`, `duration` - `hexBinary`, `base64Binary` - `anyURI`, `QName`, `NOTATION` **Derived Types:** - `integer`, `positiveInteger`, `negativeInteger` - `int`, `short`, `byte`, `long` - `normalizedString`, `token`, `language` - `ID`, `IDREF`, `IDREFS`, `ENTITY`, `ENTITIES`, `NMTOKEN`, `NMTOKENS` ### 2.4.2 XSD Structure (Typical Skeleton) ```xml ``` ### 2.4.3 DTD vs. XML Schema (Board-Comparison Table) | Feature | DTD | XML Schema (XSD) | |---|---|---| | **Syntax** | Custom (not XML) | XML-based | | **Data Types** | 10 types | 44+ built-in types | | **Namespace Support** | No | Yes | | **Inheritance** | No | Yes (`extension`, `restriction`) | | **Occurrence Constraints** | `?`, `*`, `+` | `minOccurs`, `maxOccurs` (numeric) | | **Uniqueness** | ID/IDREF | `xs:unique`, `xs:key`, `xs:keyref` | | **Self-documenting** | No | Yes | | **Extensibility** | Limited | High | | **W3C Status** | Legacy | **Recommended Standard** | ## 2.5 XML Namespaces **Namespaces** resolve element/attribute name conflicts when combining XML documents from different sources. ```xml Advanced DBMS Reference Handbook ``` **Components:** - **Prefix** — `db`, `lib` (short alias) - **Namespace URI** — `http://www.w3.org/TR/database` (globally unique identifier, NOT a URL to be fetched) - **Default Namespace** — `xmlns="http://www.w3.org/..."` (no prefix needed) ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Not all database topics have "formulas" — XML has structural rules instead.** | Concept | Defining Rule / Formula | |---|---| | **Well-formedness** | Document obeys XML 1.0 syntax rules | | **Validity** | Well-formed **AND** conforms to DTD/XSD | | **Root constraint** | Exactly **1** root element: $N_{root} = 1$ | | **Attribute uniqueness** | Within one element: $A_{unique} = 1$ per name | | **Tag nesting depth** | LIFO stack discipline — no overlap allowed | | **DTD content quantifier** | $? \rightarrow [0,1],\ * \rightarrow [0,\infty),\ + \rightarrow [1,\infty)$ | | **Element cardinality (XSD)** | $0 \leq n \leq N$ where $n = \text{minOccurs},\ N = \text{maxOccurs}$ | | **XML node tree size** | $\vert T \vert = \sum_{i} (\text{elements} + \text{attributes} + \text{text nodes})$ | | **Path expression length** | $L = \vert P \vert$ (number of location steps in XPath) | | **Namespace URI uniqueness** | Globally unique string (URL-style) | ## 2.7 Engineering / Industry Utility | Domain | Use of XML | |---|---| | **Web Services** | SOAP, WSDL, REST-XML payloads | | **Finance** | FIXML, XBRL (financial reporting) | | **Healthcare** | HL7 CDA, FHIR (electronic medical records) | | **E-Commerce** | RSS, Atom feeds, product catalogs | | **Publishing** | DocBook, TEI (text encoding) | | **Office Documents** | OOXML (`.docx`, `.xlsx`), ODF | | **Configuration** | `pom.xml` (Maven), `web.xml` (Java EE) | | **Mobile** | Android layout XML, manifests | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 XML Document Tree — Step-by-Step Construction > [!NOTE] > **Learning objective:** Convert a real-world data scenario into a well-formed, valid XML document and validate it against a DTD. ### 3.1.1 Sample Use Case (Library Domain) A library wishes to digitize its catalogue. The data requirements are: - Each **book** has a unique `isbn`, a `title`, one or more `author` entries, a `publisher`, a `year`, and a `price`. - Optional `edition` attribute. - Multiple books belong to one `library`. ### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Define the DTD ```dtd ``` **Reasoning (Why this structure?):** - `library` must contain at least one `book` (`+`). - Each `book` is a **sequence** (`title`, `author+`, `publisher`, `year`, `price`). - `author+` means a book may have multiple authors. - `isbn` is a unique identifier (ID type). - `edition` is optional metadata. ### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Construct the XML Document (Validating Each Well-Formedness Rule) ```xml Database System Concepts Abraham Silberschatz Henry F. Korth McGraw-Hill 2011 850.00 Fundamentals of Database Systems Ramez Elmasri Shamkant Navathe Pearson 2016 920.00 ``` ### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Apply Well-Formedness Checks (Exhaustive) Let us verify the document against each rule: | Rule | Check | Result | |---|---|---| | 1. XML declaration present | `` | ✅ | | 2. Single root | `` opens, closes last | ✅ | | 3. Proper nesting | All `...` are inside `` | ✅ | | 4. Closing tags | Every start-tag has an end-tag | ✅ | | 5. Case consistency | All lowercase | ✅ | | 6. Quoted attributes | `isbn="B001"`, `edition="2"` | ✅ | | 7. No duplicate attributes | Each element has unique attrs | ✅ | | 8. Unique ID values | `B001`, `B002` are distinct | ✅ | | 9. Reserved characters escaped | None present | ✅ | ### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Apply Validity Checks (DTD Conformance) | DTD Constraint | Document Match | Result | |---|---|---| | `library` contains `book+` | 2 books present | ✅ | | `book` sequence: title, author+, publisher, year, price | Order followed | ✅ | | `book` has `isbn` REQUIRED | Both books have `isbn` | ✅ | | `book` has `edition` IMPLIED | Book 1 has it, Book 2 doesn't (allowed) | ✅ | | `isbn` is unique ID | `B001` ≠ `B002` | ✅ | **Final Verdict:** The document is **well-formed AND valid**. ## 3.2 Derivation: From XML Document to XML Tree (DOM Model) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual derivation showing how a textual XML document is mapped to a tree data structure.** ### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Tokenize the Document The XML parser reads characters and produces a **token stream**: - Start-tag tokens: ``, ``, `<author>` - Text tokens: `Database System Concepts` - End-tag tokens: ``, ``, `` ### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Build a Stack-Based Tree The parser uses a **LIFO stack** to maintain parent-child relationships: $$ \text{stack}_i = \{\text{node}_1, \text{node}_2, \ldots, \text{node}_i\} $$ When a `start-tag` is read, push the element onto the stack and add it as a child of the top. When an `end-tag` is read, pop the matching element from the stack. ### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Tree Construction Algorithm (Pseudocode) $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Input: Token stream } T = (t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n) \\ &\text{Output: XML tree } T_{xml} \\ &\text{Initialize stack } S \leftarrow \emptyset,\ \text{root} \leftarrow \text{null} \\ &\textbf{for each } t_i \in T: \\ &\quad \textbf{if } t_i = \text{START\_TAG}(n): \\ &\quad\quad \text{push}(S, n) \\ &\quad\quad \textbf{if } \text{root} = \text{null}: \\ &\quad\quad\quad \text{root} \leftarrow n \\ &\quad\quad \textbf{else}: \\ &\quad\quad\quad \text{addChild}(\text{top}(S), n) \\ &\quad \textbf{if } t_i = \text{TEXT}(s): \\ &\quad\quad \text{addChild}(\text{top}(S), \text{textNode}(s)) \\ &\quad \textbf{if } t_i = \text{END\_TAG}(n): \\ &\quad\quad \text{pop}(S) \\ &\text{return root} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Final Tree Representation For the example document, the resulting tree is: $$ T_{xml} = \text{library} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{book}_1 \rightarrow \{\text{title}_1, \text{author}_1, \text{author}_2, \text{publisher}_1, \text{year}_1, \text{price}_1\} \\ \text{book}_2 \rightarrow \{\text{title}_2, \text{author}_3, \text{author}_4, \text{publisher}_2, \text{year}_2, \text{price}_2\} \end{cases} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use DTD vs. XML Schema | Scenario | Recommended Tool | Justification | |---|---|---| | Legacy system compatibility | DTD | Older parsers, SGML heritage | | Need for rich data types (date, int, etc.) | XML Schema | Built-in primitive types | | Cross-document name conflicts | XML Schema | Namespace support | | Complex inheritance hierarchies | XML Schema | `extension` / `restriction` | | Quick prototyping, simple validation | DTD | Concise syntax | | Industry-standard (SOAP, WSDL) | XML Schema | W3C mandate | | Mobile/embedded low-resource | DTD | Smaller parser footprint | ## 3.4 Common XML Errors and Diagnostic Fixes | Error Type | Example | Fix | |---|---|---| | **Missing closing tag** | `Database` | Add `` | | **Mismatched nesting** | `` | Reorder: `` | | **Unquoted attribute** | `` | `` | | **Duplicate attribute** | `` | Remove duplicate | | **Multiple root elements** | `` | Wrap in single root | | **Reserved character** | `if (a < b)` | Use `<` or CDATA | | **Missing XML declaration** | None present | Add `` | | **Case mismatch** | `...` | Use `...` | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 XML Document Tree (Hierarchical Visualization) ```mermaid graph TD docRoot["XML Document"] prolog["Prolog: xml declaration and DTD"] rootNode["library ROOT ELEMENT"] book1Node["book isbn B001 edition 2"] book2Node["book isbn B002"] title1["title: Database System Concepts"] author1a["author: Silberschatz"] author1b["author: Korth"] pub1["publisher: McGraw Hill"] year1["year: 2011"] price1["price: 850"] title2["title: Fundamentals of DBMS"] author2a["author: Elmasri"] author2b["author: Navathe"] pub2["publisher: Pearson"] year2["year: 2016"] price2["price: 920"] docRoot --> prolog docRoot --> rootNode rootNode --> book1Node rootNode --> book2Node book1Node --> title1 book1Node --> author1a book1Node --> author1b book1Node --> pub1 book1Node --> year1 book1Node --> price1 book2Node --> title2 book2Node --> author2a book2Node --> author2b book2Node --> pub2 book2Node --> year2 book2Node --> price2 ``` ## 4.2 XML Document Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"] rawData["Raw Heterogeneous Data: books, journals, ebooks"] end subgraph DesignLayer["Design Layer"] designSchema["Design DTD or XSD Schema"] defineStructure["Define Element Tree: root, child, attribute"] end subgraph DocumentLayer["XML Document Layer"] prologBlock["Prolog: xml version, encoding, DOCTYPE"] bodyBlock["Body: Element Tree with PCDATA and CDATA"] epilogBlock["Epilog: Optional Comments and PIs"] end subgraph ValidationLayer["Validation Layer"] parserBlock["XML Parser: Well Formedness Check"] schemaValidator["DTD or XSD Validator: Structural Conformance"] resultValid["Result: Valid XML Document"] resultInvalid["Result: Reject and Report Error"] end subgraph ConsumptionLayer["Consumption Layer"] appConsumer["Business Application: Java, Python, .NET"] dbConsumer["Native XML Database: eXist, BaseX"] webConsumer["Web Service: SOAP, REST XML"] end rawData --> designSchema designSchema --> defineStructure defineStructure --> prologBlock prologBlock --> bodyBlock bodyBlock --> epilogBlock epilogBlock --> parserBlock parserBlock --> schemaValidator schemaValidator --> resultValid schemaValidator --> resultInvalid resultValid --> appConsumer resultValid --> dbConsumer resultValid --> webConsumer ``` ## 4.3 DTD vs. XML Schema Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode["Need to define XML document structure"] q1{"Require data typing for fields?"} q2{"Using namespaces?"} q3{"Need inheritance or extensibility?"} q4{"Legacy system compatibility?"} useDTD["Use DTD"] useXSD["Use XML Schema XSD"] useBoth["Hybrid: Internal DTD subset plus XSD import"] startNode --> q1 q1 -- "No, text only" --> q4 q1 -- "Yes, need int, date, decimal" --> q2 q4 -- "Yes" --> useDTD q4 -- "No" --> q3 q2 -- "Yes" --> useXSD q2 -- "No" --> useBoth q3 -- "Required" --> useXSD q3 -- "Not required" --> useDTD ``` ## 4.4 XML Processing Pipeline (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR xmlFile["XML File: books.xml"] lexer["Lexer: Tokenizer"] parser["Parser: Builds DOM or SAX Tree"] validator["Validator: DTD or XSD Engine"] subgraph TreeRepresentation["Tree Representation Layer"] domTree["DOM Tree: In Memory Random Access"] saxStream["SAX Event Stream: Sequential Access"] end subgraph QueryLayer["Query and Transformation Layer"] xpathEngine["XPath: Node Selection"] xsltEngine["XSLT: Transformation"] xqueryEngine["XQuery: SQL like XML Query"] end output["Output: HTML, PDF, Another XML, Database Record"] xmlFile --> lexer lexer --> parser parser --> validator validator --> domTree validator --> saxStream domTree --> xpathEngine domTree --> xqueryEngine domTree --> xsltEngine saxStream --> xpathEngine xpathEngine --> output xqueryEngine --> output xsltEngine --> output ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** *Explain the term "well-formedness" of an XML document. List any four rules that must be satisfied for an XML document to be well-formed.* **Model Answer (3 Marks — Structured for Examiner):** **Definition (1 Mark):** A well-formed XML document is one that strictly follows the syntactic rules defined in the W3C XML 1.0 specification. It is the minimum requirement for any XML document to be parsed correctly. **Four Rules (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Single Root Element:** Every XML document must have exactly one outermost element that contains all other elements. 2. **Proper Nesting of Tags:** Tags must be properly nested; overlapping tags (``) are illegal. 3. **Case Sensitivity:** XML is case-sensitive — `` and `<title>` are treated as different elements. 4. **Quoted Attributes:** All attribute values must be enclosed within double quotes (`"…"`) or single quotes (`'…'`). *Additional points for grace marks:* closing tags mandatory, reserved characters escaped, no duplicate attributes. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *With a suitable example, explain the use of CDATA sections in XML documents.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** CDATA (Character Data) sections allow XML documents to contain text that includes reserved XML characters (`<`, `>`, `&`) without requiring entity escaping. The parser passes the content **as-is** to the application. **Example (1.5 Marks):** ```xml <formula> <![CDATA[ if (a < b && b > c) result = (a + b) / 2; ]]> </formula> ``` Here, `<` and `&` would otherwise be illegal in regular PCDATA. **Use Case (0.5 Mark):** CDATA is especially useful for embedding **JavaScript, mathematical formulas, SQL queries, or XML fragments** inside XML without escaping. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Consider the following scenario for a University Course Registration System:* - Each `university` has multiple `departments`. - Each `department` has a unique `dept_id`, a `name`, and offers multiple `courses`. - Each `course` has a `course_code` (unique), `title`, `credits` (integer), and is taught by one or more `instructors`. - Each `instructor` has a unique `emp_id`, `name`, and `designation`. - Courses may have a `prerequisite` element containing the `course_code` of a previously offered course. **(a)** Design a **DTD** for the above scenario. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Construct a **valid XML document** with at least one university, two departments, and three courses. Demonstrate proper use of attributes and element nesting. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **DTD Design with Explanation (Each Element: ~1 Mark):** ```dtd <!-- Root element: contains departments --> <!ELEMENT university (department)+> <!-- Department contains course elements --> <!ELEMENT department (course)+> <!ATTLIST department dept_id ID #REQUIRED name CDATA #REQUIRED> <!-- Course contains title, credits, instructors, optional prerequisite --> <!ELEMENT course (title, credits, instructor+, prerequisite?)> <!ATTLIST course course_code ID #REQUIRED level CDATA "undergraduate"> <!-- Simple text-only elements --> <!ELEMENT title (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT credits (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT instructor EMPTY> <!ATTLIST instructor emp_id IDREF #REQUIRED name CDATA #IMPLIED designation CDATA #IMPLIED> <!-- Prerequisite refers to a course_code --> <!ELEMENT prerequisite EMPTY> <!ATTLIST prerequisite course_code IDREF #REQUIRED> ``` **Reasoning (Examiner's Trace):** - `university` uses `+` because a university must have **at least one** department. - `dept_id` is `ID` type — ensures uniqueness for cross-references. - `course_code` is `ID` so that `prerequisite` can reference it via `IDREF`. - `instructor` is modeled as an `EMPTY` element with attributes, demonstrating a key DTD design pattern. - `prerequisite` is optional (`?`). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Valid XML Document:** ```xml <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!DOCTYPE university SYSTEM "university.dtd"> <university> <department dept_id="CS" name="Computer Science"> <course course_code="CS301" level="undergraduate"> <title>Database Management Systems 4 Advanced Database Systems 3 Digital Electronics 4 ``` **Validation Checklist (For Examiner's Verification):** - ✅ Well-formed (XML declaration, single root, proper nesting, case consistency, quoted attributes). - ✅ Valid (matches DTD: unique IDs, `IDREF` references existing courses, all required elements present). - ✅ Demonstrates `ID`/`IDREF` cross-referencing between `course_code` and `prerequisite`. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Discuss the **structure of an XML document** in detail. With a neat diagram, explain the **XML document tree model (DOM)**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **DTD and XML Schema**. Highlight at least **five key differences** with examples. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Structure of an XML Document (3.5 Marks):** An XML document has two principal parts: 1. **Prolog (1 Mark):** - **XML Declaration:** `` - **Optional:** Processing instructions, comments, DOCTYPE declaration. 2. **Body / Root Element (2.5 Marks):** - A single **root element** enclosing the entire document. - Contains a hierarchy of **child elements**, **attributes**, **text nodes**, and **CDATA sections**. - Tree-shaped structure with one parent per node (except the root). **Document Tree (DOM) Model (3.5 Marks):** The **Document Object Model (DOM)** represents an XML document as a **tree of nodes** in memory. **Node Types (1 Mark):** - Document node, Element node, Attribute node, Text node, Comment node, CDATA node, Processing Instruction node. **Tree Properties (1 Mark):** - Root → one Document node → one Root Element → child Elements and Text nodes. - Each node (except root) has exactly **one parent** and any number of children. **Diagram (1.5 Marks):** ```mermaid graph TD docNode["Document Node"] rootNode["library: Root Element"] bookNode["book: Element Node"] titleNode["title: Element Node"] titleText["Database Systems: Text Node"] attrNode["isbn: Attribute Node"] authorNode["author: Element Node"] authorText["Silberschatz: Text Node"] docNode --> rootNode rootNode --> bookNode bookNode --> titleNode bookNode --> authorNode bookNode --> attrNode titleNode --> titleText authorNode --> authorText ``` **Advantages of DOM (Board Mention — 0.5 Mark Bonus):** - Random access to any node. - Easy to modify structure programmatically. - Suitable for small-to-medium XML files. **Limitation:** - Loads the **entire** document into memory — not ideal for very large files (use SAX instead). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Comparison Table (Each difference: ~1.4 Marks):** | # | Feature | DTD | XML Schema (XSD) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Syntax** | Custom non-XML syntax; requires separate parser | Written in XML itself; uses standard XML parser | | 2 | **Data Types** | Limited (only 10: CDATA, ID, IDREF, NMTOKEN, etc.) | 44+ built-in types: `xs:int`, `xs:date`, `xs:decimal`, etc. | | 3 | **Namespace Support** | Not supported | Fully supported via `targetNamespace` | | 4 | **Cardinality Constraints** | Only `?`, `*`, `+` | Numeric `minOccurs` / `maxOccurs` (e.g., `minOccurs="2" maxOccurs="5"`) | | 5 | **Inheritance** | Not supported | Supported via `extension` and `restriction` types | **Examples (2 Marks Total — show one of each):** **DTD Example:** ```dtd ``` **XSD Example:** ```xml ``` **XSD shows stronger typing**: the value must be a decimal number, not just any text. **Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** XML Schema is the **W3C-recommended modern standard** for defining XML structure. DTD remains in use for legacy compatibility but is increasingly replaced by XSD in new systems. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in XML Questions:** > > 1. **Conflating "well-formed" with "valid":** A document can be well-formed but **invalid** if it doesn't match the DTD/Schema. Examiners specifically test this distinction. Always state **both** criteria when defining "valid." > > 2. **Forgetting the `+`, `*`, `?` quantifiers in DTD:** Writing `` instead of `` is a **frequently penalized** error. Read the question's "one or more" or "zero or more" requirements carefully. > > 3. **Mixing element and attribute design philosophy:** The W3C recommends *data in elements, metadata in attributes*. Examiners may deduct marks if you store core data (like `price`) only as an attribute. > > 4. **Missing ID/IDREF linkage:** When a DTD uses `IDREF`, the referenced `ID` **must exist** in the document. Showing an `IDREF` pointing to a non-existent ID is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. > > 5. **Case-sensitive tag mismatch:** Writing `` in start-tag and `` in end-tag is **not** a typo — it's a structural error. Examiners are strict about this. > > 6. **Skipping the XML declaration:** Many students omit ``. Although not strictly required for well-formedness, omitting it often costs **0.5–1 mark** in viva or detailed valuation. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for XML Documents** ### 🔑 Core Definitions - **XML** = eXtensible Markup Language (W3C, 1998). - **Well-formed** = obeys XML syntax rules. - **Valid** = well-formed + conforms to DTD/XSD. - **Root Element** = exactly one, contains the entire document. - **PCDATA** = parsed character data (entity-expanded). - **CDATA** = unparsed character data (raw text). ### 🏗️ Structural Components - **Prolog:** XML declaration + DOCTYPE + processing instructions. - **Body:** Single root → elements → text/attributes/children. - **Element rules:** start-tag, end-tag, case-sensitive, proper nesting. - **Attribute rules:** quoted, no duplicates within an element, optional. ### 📐 DTD Quick Facts - DTD syntax is **non-XML**; written in `` declarations. - Content models: `EMPTY`, `ANY`, `(#PCDATA)`, sequences, choices. - Quantifiers: `?` (0–1), `*` (0–∞), `+` (1–∞). - Attribute types: `CDATA`, `ID`, `IDREF`, `IDREFS`, `NMTOKEN`, enumerated. - Attribute defaults: `#REQUIRED`, `#IMPLIED`, `#FIXED`, default value. ### 🧬 XSD Quick Facts - XSD is **written in XML** — self-documenting. - Supports 44+ **data types** (`string`, `int`, `decimal`, `date`, `dateTime`). - `minOccurs` / `maxOccurs` provide **numeric** cardinality. - Supports **namespaces**, **inheritance**, and **uniqueness constraints**. - W3C **recommended** successor to DTD. ### 🌐 Namespace Notes - Resolves name conflicts when merging XML vocabularies. - Declared as `xmlns:prefix="URI"` (URI is an identifier, not a fetchable URL). - Default namespace: omit the prefix. ### 🔍 Processing & Querying (Connected Topics) - **DOM** = in-memory tree, random access. - **SAX** = event-driven, sequential, memory-efficient. - **XPath** = node selection language. - **XQuery** = SQL-like queries for XML. - **XSLT** = XML-to-XML/HTML transformation. ### 🧪 Quick Self-Test Questions 1. ✅ Can a document be valid but not well-formed? *(No — well-formedness is a prerequisite.)* 2. ✅ Are XML attributes case-sensitive? *(Yes.)* 3. ✅ Is `` valid? *(No — XML 1.0 and 1.1 are the only versions.)* 4. ✅ Does DTD support namespaces? *(No — XSD does.)* 5. ✅ Can an element have two attributes with the same name? *(No.)*

topics.title: DTD and XML Schema
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** DTD and XML Schema

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a DTD (Document Type Definition)?

A **Document Type Definition (DTD)** is the original schema language for XML, inherited from SGML, that defines the legal building blocks of an XML document. It specifies the structure — what elements can appear, in what order, with what attributes, and what their content models are.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
> A DTD is a set of markup declarations that define the **document structure**, **element types**, **attribute lists**, and **entities** permissible in a class of XML documents. It acts as a *grammar* or *contract* that an XML document must follow to be considered *valid* (as opposed to merely *well-formed*).

**Two Kinds of DTDs:**

| Type | Location | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Internal DTD** | Inside the XML file in `<!DOCTYPE ... [ ... ]>` | Small, single-file documents |
| **External DTD** | Separate `.dtd` file referenced via SYSTEM or PUBLIC | Reusable across multiple XML files |

---

### 1.2 What is an XML Schema (XSD)?

An **XML Schema Definition (XSD)** is the W3C-recommended successor to DTDs. It is itself written in XML, supports **data types**, **namespaces**, and provides far richer structural constraints than DTD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **XML Schema = DTD + Data Types + Namespaces + Inheritance + Constraints**
> Where a DTD says "this element exists and contains text," XML Schema says "this element exists, contains text of type `xs:date`, must match the pattern `YYYY-MM-DD`, and falls between `2000-01-01` and `2030-12-31`."

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **DTD = Building Blueprint (Sketch)**
> Imagine a government form. A DTD is like a paper form that says:
> *“Field A goes here. Field B goes after Field A. Field C is optional.”* It defines **layout and order**, but not what kind of data is acceptable in each field.

> **XML Schema = Building Blueprint (Engineered CAD Drawing)**
> An XML Schema is the same form, but now each box has a **data type and validation rule**:
> *“Field A: a whole number between 18 and 99. Field B: text matching a postal code pattern. Field C: a date in ISO 8601 format, required.”*

The XML document itself is the *completed form filled in by the user*. Validation checks whether the user's answers obey the form's rules.

---

### 1.4 Why DTD & XML Schema Matter in KTU / Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern **Advanced Database Systems**, XML is widely used for:
> - **Data interchange** between heterogeneous systems (SOA, web services, B2B)
> - **Semi-structured storage** in native XML databases (eXist, BaseX, MarkLogic)
> - **Configuration files** in enterprise applications
> - **Document validation** in financial, healthcare (HL7, FHIR), and publishing systems

Without a schema (DTD or XSD), XML is just free-form text — any data can go in any order, breaking downstream parsers, query engines, and business rules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a DTD

A DTD is built from four main declaration types:

1. **Element Declarations** — define what tags may exist and what they may contain
2. **Attribute List Declarations** — define attributes an element can carry
3. **Entity Declarations** — define shortcuts for reusable text or external content
4. **Notation Declarations** — declare formats of non-XML data (images, PDFs, etc.)

---

### 2.2 Element Declarations in DTD

The syntax is:

```
<!ELEMENT element_name content_specification>
```

**Content Models (the heart of DTD):**

| Symbol / Syntax | Meaning | Example |
|-----------------|---------|---------|
| `EMPTY` | Element has no content | `<!ELEMENT br EMPTY>` |
| `ANY` | Element may contain any content (rarely used) | `<!ELEMENT note ANY>` |
| `#PCDATA` | Parsed character data (text only) | `<!ELEMENT name (#PCDATA)>` |
| `(child)` | A specific child element | `<!ELEMENT book (title)>` |
| `(a, b, c)` | Sequence — must appear in this order | `<!ELEMENT book (title, author, price)>` |
| `(a \| b \| c)` | Choice — exactly one must appear | `<!ELEMENT payment (cash \| card \| upi)>` |
| `a?` | Zero or one occurrence | `<!ELEMENT book (title, author?, price?)>` |
| `a*` | Zero or more occurrences | `<!ELEMENT library (book*)>` |
| `a+` | One or more occurrences | `<!ELEMENT library (book+)>` |
| **Mixed content** | `#PCDATA` interleaved with elements | `<!ELEMENT para (#PCDATA \| b \| i)*>` |

---

### 2.3 Attribute List Declarations in DTD

```
<!ATTLIST element_name 
           attr_name type default_value
           attr_name type default_value ...>
```

**Attribute Types:**

| Type | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `CDATA` | Character data (any text) |
| `(val1 \| val2)` | Enumerated list of allowed values |
| `ID` | Unique identifier within the document |
| `IDREF` | Reference to an element's `ID` |
| `IDREFS` | Space-separated list of ID references |
| `NMTOKEN` | A valid XML name token |
| `NMTOKENS` | Space-separated list of NMTOKENs |
| `ENTITY` | Reference to an unparsed entity |
| `ENTITIES` | Multiple entity references |
| `NOTATION` | Reference to a declared notation |
| `(en1\|en2\|...)` | Enumerated list |

**Default Value Keywords:**

| Keyword | Meaning |
|---------|---------|
| `#REQUIRED` | Attribute must be present |
| `#IMPLIED` | Attribute is optional |
| `#FIXED "value"` | Attribute value is constant |

---

### 2.4 Entities in DTD

| Entity Type | Syntax | Purpose |
|-------------|--------|---------|
| **Internal General Entity** | `<!ENTITY name "value">` | Reusable text replacement |
| **External General Entity** | `<!ENTITY name SYSTEM "file">` | Include external file content |
| **Internal Parameter Entity** | `<!ENTITY % name "value">` | Reusable DTD fragment (in DTD only) |
| **External Parameter Entity** | `<!ENTITY % name SYSTEM "file">` | Include external DTD file |

---

### 2.5 Limitations of DTD → Why We Need XML Schema

> [!WARNING]
> **DTD Limitations to Memorize for KTU:**
> 1. DTD is **not written in XML** — needs a separate parser
> 2. DTD has **limited data types** — only string-like types (CDATA, ID, IDREF)
> 3. DTD **does not support namespaces**
> 4. DTD has **no support for inheritance** or type derivation
> 5. DTD cannot constrain **numeric ranges, lengths, or patterns**
> 6. DTD's content model grammar (`(a,b)+`) is **limited and complex**
> 7. DTD cannot specify **cardinality on attributes** beyond required/optional

---

### 2.6 XML Schema (XSD) — Core Constructs

An XML Schema file uses the namespace `http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema` (prefix `xs:` or `xsd:`).

**Document Skeleton:**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xs:schema xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema">
   <!-- declarations here -->
</xs:schema>
```

**Key XSD Elements:**

| Element | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `<xs:element>` | Declares an element |
| `<xs:attribute>` | Declares an attribute |
| `<xs:complexType>` | Defines a type with elements/attributes |
| `<xs:simpleType>` | Defines a type with restrictions on text |
| `<xs:sequence>` | Children must appear in order |
| `<xs:choice>` | Only one of the children may appear |
| `<xs:all>` | Children can appear in any order (each at most once) |
| `<xs:restriction>` | Applies constraints (facets) |
| `<xs:extension>` | Adds to an existing type (inheritance) |
| `<xs:include>` | Merges another schema with same namespace |
| `<xs:import>` | References a schema with different namespace |

---

### 2.7 XSD Built-in Data Types (44 primitive & derived types)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency Types for KTU:**
> - `xs:string`, `xs:normalizedString`, `xs:token`
> - `xs:integer`, `xs:int`, `xs:long`, `xs:short`, `xs:byte`
> - `xs:decimal`, `xs:float`, `xs:double`
> - `xs:boolean`
> - `xs:date`, `xs:time`, `xs:dateTime`, `xs:duration`
> - `xs:anyURI`, `xs:base64Binary`, `xs:hexBinary`

---

### 2.8 XSD Facets (Constraints on Simple Types)

| Facet | Purpose | Example |
|-------|---------|---------|
| `minInclusive` / `maxInclusive` | Numeric/date range (inclusive) | `minInclusive="0" maxInclusive="100"` |
| `minExclusive` / `maxExclusive` | Range (exclusive) | `minExclusive="0"` |
| `length` | Exact length | `length="10"` |
| `minLength` / `maxLength` | Length bounds | `minLength="6"` |
| `pattern` | Regex pattern | `pattern="\d{3}-\d{4}"` |
| `enumeration` | List of allowed values | `enumeration="red green blue"` |
| `whiteSpace` | How to handle whitespace | `whiteSpace="collapse"` |
| `totalDigits` / `fractionDigits` | Decimal precision | `totalDigits="5" fractionDigits="2"` |

---

### 2.9 KTU Cheat Sheet — DTD vs XSD

| Feature | DTD | XSD |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Written in XML | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Data types | ❌ Limited (10 types) | ✅ Rich (44+ types) |
| Namespaces | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Inheritance | ❌ No | ✅ `extension` / `restriction` |
| Cardinality constraints | Limited | ✅ Precise (`minOccurs`, `maxOccurs`) |
| Pattern matching | ❌ No | ✅ Regex via `pattern` facet |
| Default & fixed values | ✅ Limited | ✅ `default`, `fixed` |
| Reusable fragments | Parameter entities | ✅ `<xs:group>`, `<xs:attributeGroup>` |
| Industry use today | Legacy | ✅ **Standard for new projects** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1 — Internal DTD for a Library Catalog

**XML Document with Internal DTD:**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE library [
   <!ELEMENT library (book+)>
   <!ELEMENT book (title, author+, year, price, category?)>
   <!ELEMENT title (#PCDATA)>
   <!ELEMENT author (#PCDATA)>
   <!ELEMENT year (#PCDATA)>
   <!ELEMENT price (#PCDATA)>
   <!ELEMENT category EMPTY>
   <!ATTLIST book
             isbn   ID      #REQUIRED
             lang   CDATA   "EN"
             edition CDATA   #IMPLIED>
   <!ATTLIST category
             type  (fiction | non-fiction | tech) "fiction">
]>
<library>
   <book isbn="b1" lang="EN" edition="2nd">
      <title>Database System Concepts</title>
      <author>Silberschatz</author>
      <author>Korth</author>
      <year>2020</year>
      <price>750.00</price>
      <category type="tech"/>
   </book>
</library>
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough:**

> **Step 1 — Root element:** `<library>` declared to contain **one or more** `book` elements (`book+`).

> **Step 2 — Book structure:** Each `book` must contain `title`, then **one or more** `author`s, then `year`, then `price`, then an optional `category` (`?` modifier). This is a strict **sequence** with mixed cardinality.

> **Step 3 — Leaf elements:** `title`, `author`, `year`, `price` contain only parsed character data (`#PCDATA`). `category` is declared `EMPTY` because it carries no content — only an attribute.

> **Step 4 — Book attributes:**
> - `isbn` of type `ID` — must be unique, **required** (`#REQUIRED`).
> - `lang` of type `CDATA` — has a **default** value `"EN"` if omitted.
> - `edition` of type `CDATA` — optional (`#IMPLIED`).

> **Step 5 — Category attribute:** `type` is **enumerated** — must be one of `fiction`, `non-fiction`, or `tech`, defaulting to `fiction`.

> **Step 6 — Validation outcome:** If the XML follows this exact structure, the parser (using a validating parser like Xerces or `xmllint --valid`) reports it as **valid**.

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — Equivalent XSD for the Same Library

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xs:schema xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
           elementFormDefault="qualified">

   <!-- Root element -->
   <xs:element name="library">
      <xs:complexType>
         <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="book" type="bookType" 
                        maxOccurs="unbounded"/>
         </xs:sequence>
      </xs:complexType>
   </xs:element>

   <!-- Reusable book type -->
   <xs:complexType name="bookType">
      <xs:sequence>
         <xs:element name="title"   type="xs:string"/>
         <xs:element name="author"  type="xs:string" 
                     maxOccurs="unbounded"/>
         <xs:element name="year"    type="xs:gYear"/>
         <xs:element name="price"   type="priceType"/>
         <xs:element name="category" type="categoryType" 
                     minOccurs="0"/>
      </xs:sequence>
      <xs:attribute name="isbn"    type="xs:ID"  use="required"/>
      <xs:attribute name="lang"    type="xs:string" 
                    default="EN"/>
      <xs:attribute name="edition" type="xs:string" 
                    use="optional"/>
   </xs:complexType>

   <!-- price: decimal between 0 and 100000 with 2 fraction digits -->
   <xs:simpleType name="priceType">
      <xs:restriction base="xs:decimal">
         <xs:minInclusive value="0"/>
         <xs:maxInclusive value="100000"/>
         <xs:fractionDigits value="2"/>
      </xs:restriction>
   </xs:simpleType>

   <!-- category with enumerated type -->
   <xs:complexType name="categoryType">
      <xs:attribute name="type" use="required">
         <xs:simpleType>
            <xs:restriction base="xs:string">
               <xs:enumeration value="fiction"/>
               <xs:enumeration value="non-fiction"/>
               <xs:enumeration value="tech"/>
            </xs:restriction>
         </xs:simpleType>
      </xs:attribute>
   </xs:complexType>
</xs:schema>
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough:**

> **Step 1 — Namespace binding:** `xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"` binds the prefix `xs` to the XSD namespace so all built-in types are recognized.

> **Step 2 — Root declaration:** `<library>` is defined as a **complex type** (because it contains children), wrapping a `<xs:sequence>` of `book` elements. `maxOccurs="unbounded"` ≈ DTD's `+`.

> **Step 3 — Type extraction:** A **named complex type** `bookType` is created — this is XSD's powerful *type reuse*. The same type can be referenced by many elements.

> **Step 4 — `year` typed as `xs:gYear`:** Unlike DTD where year is just text, XSD validates that the year is a valid Gregorian year (e.g., `2024`).

> **Step 5 — `priceType` custom simple type:** Built by **restricting** `xs:decimal` with three facets — `minInclusive`, `maxInclusive`, `fractionDigits`. This is **impossible in DTD**.

> **Step 6 — Attribute constraints:** `use="required"`, `use="optional"`, and `default="EN"` cleanly map to DTD's `#REQUIRED`, `#IMPLIED`, and quoted default.

> **Step 7 — Enumerated category:** Replaced DTD's `(fiction | non-fiction | tech)` with `<xs:enumeration>` facets, giving identical semantics but with XML-native syntax.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — Pattern Facet (Regex Validation)

Suppose we want a student ID of the form `KTU2024CS001` (KTU + year + branch + 3 digits).

**XSD Definition:**

```xml
<xs:simpleType name="studentIdType">
   <xs:restriction base="xs:string">
      <xs:pattern value="KTU[0-9]{4}(CS|EC|EE|ME)[0-9]{3}"/>
   </xs:restriction>
</xs:simpleType>
```

**Walkthrough of the regex pattern:**

> **Step 1 — `KTU`** — literal prefix.

> **Step 2 — `[0-9]{4}`** — exactly 4 digits (year).

> **Step 3 — `(CS|EC|EE|ME)`** — exactly one of the allowed branch codes.

> **Step 4 — `[0-9]{3}`** — exactly 3 digits (roll number suffix).

> **Step 5 — Result:** A document with `<id>KTU2024CS042</id>` is **valid**; `<id>CS20240042</id>` or `<id>KTU24CS42</id>` is **invalid**.

> [!NOTE]
> In DTD, this kind of constraint is **impossible to express** — DTDs have no pattern matching.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Comparison Questions

| Question | DTD Answer | XSD Answer |
|----------|------------|------------|
| Can you specify that a `price` must be a positive number? | ❌ Cannot | ✅ `<xs:simpleType>` with `<xs:restriction base="xs:decimal">` + `minInclusive="0"` |
| Can you validate an element against multiple types? | ❌ No | ✅ Use `<xs:union>` to combine types |
| Can you extend an existing type? | ❌ No | ✅ `<xs:complexContent><xs:extension base="...">` |
| How do you reuse a group of elements? | Parameter entities | ✅ `<xs:group name="...">` + `<xs:group ref="...">` |
| How do you handle namespaces? | Not supported | ✅ `targetNamespace`, `xmlns:tns="..."` |
| How many `<book>` elements are allowed? | `book+` | `maxOccurs="unbounded"` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — XML Validation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[XML Document Instance] --> B[XML Parser e.g. xmllint, Xerces, SAX, DOM]
    C[DTD .dtd file] --> B
    D[XSD .xsd file] --> B
    B --> E{Well Formedness Check}
    E -- Fail --> F[Parser Error: Syntax Invalid]
    E -- Pass --> G{Schema Validation Step}
    G -- Fail --> H[Validation Error: Document Invalid]
    G -- Pass --> I[Document is VALID and WELL FORMED]
    I --> J[Application / XPath / XQuery / XSLT]
```

**Explanation of flow:**

> **Stage A** — The user supplies the actual XML data file (the *instance*).
> **Stage B** — The parser reads the XML and loads either the DTD (declared via `<!DOCTYPE>`) or the XSD (linked via `xsi:schemaLocation`).
> **Stage C / D** — Schema and instance are matched up.
> **Stage E** — Parser first checks well-formedness (every tag closed, proper nesting, one root). This is independent of schema.
> **Stage G** — If well-formed, the validating parser applies schema rules.
> **Stage I / J** — On full pass, the document is *valid* and is handed to the application (e.g., a database loader, web service consumer, or XQuery engine).

---

### 4.2 DTD vs XSD — Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DTD[Document Type Definition]
        D1[Element Declarations]
        D2[Attribute List Declarations]
        D3[Entity Declarations]
        D4[Notation Declarations]
    end
    subgraph XSD[XML Schema Definition]
        X1[Built in Data Types 44 plus]
        X2[Simple Type Definitions]
        X3[Complex Type Definitions]
        X4[Facets Restrictions]
        X5[Inheritance Extension and Restriction]
        X6[Namespace Support]
    end
    XML[XML Document] --> DTD
    XML --> XSD
    X2 --> X4
    X3 --> X5
    X3 --> X6
```

**Architectural insight:**

> The DTD block is **flat and limited** — four declaration types, no data typing, no extension mechanism. The XSD block is **layered and composable** — types can be defined once and reused, types can inherit from other types, and namespaces allow multiple schemas to coexist in one document.

---

### 4.3 XSD Type Derivation Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[xs:anyType] --> B[Simple Types]
    A --> C[Complex Types]
    B --> B1[xs:string]
    B --> B2[xs:decimal]
    B --> B3[xs:dateTime]
    B --> B4[xs:boolean]
    B1 --> B1a[Derived Simple Types by restriction]
    B1a --> B1a1[Product Code Pattern]
    B1a --> B1a2[Phone Number Pattern]
    B2 --> B2a[Derived Simple Types by restriction]
    B2a --> B2a1[Price Type 0 to 100000]
    C --> C1[Empty Content like br tag]
    C --> C2[Element Only like book]
    C --> C3[Mixed Content like para with b i]
    C --> C4[Text Only Attributes like img with src]
```

> [!NOTE]
> `xs:anyType` is the **root of the XSD type system** — every other type is a restriction or extension of it. This is the basis of XSD's inheritance model that DTD completely lacks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*With a suitable example, explain what is meant by a Document Type Definition (DTD). Distinguish between internal and external DTD.*

**Model Answer:**

A DTD (Document Type Definition) is a set of markup declarations that define the legal structure, elements, attributes, and entities of an XML document class. It is a grammar that an XML document must conform to in order to be considered **valid**.

**Internal DTD** — declared inside the XML document itself within the `<!DOCTYPE ... [ ... ]>` block. Suitable for single-file, self-contained documents.

```xml
<!DOCTYPE library [
   <!ELEMENT library (book+)>
   <!ELEMENT book (title, author)>
]>
<library> ... </library>
```

**External DTD** — declared in a separate `.dtd` file and referenced from the XML document using `SYSTEM` or `PUBLIC` identifiers. Suitable for shared schemas used by many XML files.

```xml
<!-- library.dtd -->
<!ELEMENT library (book+)>
<!ELEMENT book (title, author)>
```

```xml
<!-- library.xml -->
<!DOCTYPE library SYSTEM "library.dtd">
<library> ... </library>
```

**Key distinction:** Internal DTD is self-contained but not reusable; External DTD is reusable across multiple XML files and centralizes schema management.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any four limitations of DTD that motivated the development of XML Schema.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Not written in XML** — DTDs use a non-XML syntax, requiring a separate parser, while XML Schema *is* an XML document and can be processed by standard XML tools.

2. **No data types** — DTD treats all content as text (`#PCDATA` or `CDATA`); XML Schema provides **44+ built-in data types** like `xs:integer`, `xs:date`, `xs:boolean`.

3. **No namespace support** — DTDs cannot handle XML namespaces, causing collisions when merging documents from different vocabularies. XML Schema fully supports `targetNamespace` and qualified names.

4. **No constraint enforcement** — DTDs cannot express range, length, or pattern constraints (e.g., "year between 2020 and 2030"). XML Schema uses **facets** like `minInclusive`, `maxLength`, and `pattern` to enforce these rules.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of element declarations in DTD with examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design an XML Schema (XSD) for a `university` database that stores information about students enrolled in various courses. The schema should enforce: student ID as unique 7-digit number, age between 17 and 30, course code matching pattern `[A-Z]{2}\d{3}`, and grades limited to `{S, A, B, C, D, F}`. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

Element declarations in DTD follow the syntax:

```
<!ELEMENT element_name content_model>
```

There are **four major content models**:

**1. Empty content** — element has no children, no text.
```xml
<!ELEMENT image EMPTY>
```
Used for leaf elements that carry information only through attributes (e.g., `<image src="pic.jpg"/>`).

**2. Any content** — element can contain anything (discouraged, breaks validation power).
```xml
<!ELEMENT misc ANY>
```

**3. Element-only content** — element contains only child elements (no text directly). This is the most common model.
```xml
<!ELEMENT book (title, author+, year, price?)>
```
- `title` — exactly one, mandatory
- `author+` — one or more authors
- `year` — exactly one
- `price?` — zero or one (optional)

**4. Mixed content** — text interleaved with child elements.
```xml
<!ELEMENT para (#PCDATA | b | i | u)*>
```
The `*` is mandatory for mixed content, and `#PCDATA` must come first.

**Cardinality operators summary:**

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| (no operator) | Exactly one | `(title)` |
| `?` | Zero or one (optional) | `(subtitle?)` |
| `*` | Zero or more | `(editor*)` |
| `+` | One or more | `(chapter+)` |

**Choice operator `|`** — exactly one of the listed alternatives may appear:
```xml
<!ELEMENT payment (cash | card | upi | cheque)>
```

**Sequence operator `,`** — children must appear in the exact order listed.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xs:schema xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
           elementFormDefault="qualified">

   <!-- Root: university -->
   <xs:element name="university">
      <xs:complexType>
         <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="student" type="studentType" 
                        maxOccurs="unbounded"/>
         </xs:sequence>
      </xs:complexType>
   </xs:element>

   <!-- Student complex type with attributes -->
   <xs:complexType name="studentType">
      <xs:sequence>
         <xs:element name="name"   type="xs:string"/>
         <xs:element name="age"    type="ageType"/>
         <xs:element name="course" type="courseType" 
                     maxOccurs="unbounded"/>
      </xs:sequence>
      <xs:attribute name="studentId" type="studentIdType" 
                    use="required"/>
   </xs:complexType>

   <!-- studentId: 7 digits -->
   <xs:simpleType name="studentIdType">
      <xs:restriction base="xs:string">
         <xs:pattern value="[0-9]{7}"/>
      </xs:restriction>
   </xs:simpleType>

   <!-- age: 17 to 30 inclusive -->
   <xs:simpleType name="ageType">
      <xs:restriction base="xs:integer">
         <xs:minInclusive value="17"/>
         <xs:maxInclusive value="30"/>
      </xs:restriction>
   </xs:simpleType>

   <!-- course: contains code, title, grade -->
   <xs:complexType name="courseType">
      <xs:sequence>
         <xs:element name="code"  type="courseCodeType"/>
         <xs:element name="title" type="xs:string"/>
         <xs:element name="grade" type="gradeType"/>
      </xs:sequence>
   </xs:complexType>

   <!-- course code: 2 uppercase letters + 3 digits -->
   <xs:simpleType name="courseCodeType">
      <xs:restriction base="xs:string">
         <xs:pattern value="[A-Z]{2}[0-9]{3}"/>
      </xs:restriction>
   </xs:simpleType>

   <!-- grade: enumerated -->
   <xs:simpleType name="gradeType">
      <xs:restriction base="xs:string">
         <xs:enumeration value="S"/>
         <xs:enumeration value="A"/>
         <xs:enumeration value="B"/>
         <xs:enumeration value="C"/>
         <xs:enumeration value="D"/>
         <xs:enumeration value="F"/>
      </xs:restriction>
   </xs:simpleType>
</xs:schema>
```

**Step-by-step design walkthrough:**

> **Step 1** — `university` is the root complex type, containing many `student` elements.
> **Step 2** — `studentType` is defined as a named complex type so the same structure can be reused if needed.
> **Step 3** — `studentId` attribute uses `pattern="[0-9]{7}"` to enforce **exactly 7 digits**.
> **Step 4** — `ageType` restricts `xs:integer` with `minInclusive="17"` and `maxInclusive="30"` — a constraint **impossible in DTD**.
> **Step 5** — `courseCodeType` uses regex `[A-Z]{2}[0-9]{3}` — two uppercase letters followed by three digits.
> **Step 6** — `gradeType` uses `<xs:enumeration>` for the 6 allowed grades (analogous to DTD's `(S|A|B|C|D|F)` but with type safety).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare DTD and XML Schema in detail. Highlight at least six differences. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write the complete DTD for an XML document that represents a company's `employee` records, where each employee has a unique `empId`, a name, a designation chosen from `{Manager, Developer, Tester, Analyst}`, and zero or more phone numbers. Also write a sample valid XML instance. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

| S.No. | Feature | DTD | XML Schema (XSD) |
|-------|---------|-----|------------------|
| 1 | **Syntax** | Uses a non-XML syntax (SGML-derived) | Written in XML itself |
| 2 | **Data Types** | Only 10 string-based types (CDATA, ID, IDREF, etc.) | 44+ built-in types: string, integer, decimal, date, boolean, time, etc. |
| 3 | **Namespaces** | Not supported | Fully supported via `targetNamespace` and qualified names |
| 4 | **Constraints** | Cannot enforce range, length, or pattern | Supports facets: `minInclusive`, `maxLength`, `pattern` (regex), `enumeration` |
| 5 | **Inheritance / Reuse** | No type extension; reuse via parameter entities only | Supports type derivation by `restriction` and `extension`; reusable via named types |
| 6 | **Cardinality** | Limited operators (`?`, `*`, `+`) in content models | Per-element `minOccurs` and `maxOccurs` (with 0, 1, or unbounded) |
| 7 | **Parser** | Needs a separate DTD-aware parser | Standard XML parser suffices |
| 8 | **Default / Fixed** | Limited: quoted defaults, `#FIXED` | Clear `default` and `fixed` attributes |
| 9 | **Mixed content** | Allowed | Allowed (with `<xs:mixed content="true">`) |
| 10 | **Industry adoption** | Legacy / older systems | **Current W3C standard** for new XML applications |

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**DTD (employee.dtd):**

```xml
<!ELEMENT company (employee+)>

<!ELEMENT employee (name, designation, phone*)>
<!ATTLIST employee
          empId ID    #REQUIRED
          dept  CDATA "General">

<!ELEMENT name        (#PCDATA)>
<!ELEMENT designation (#PCDATA)>
<!ELEMENT phone       (#PCDATA)>
<!ATTLIST phone
          type (mobile | landline) "mobile">
```

**Sample Valid XML Instance:**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE company SYSTEM "employee.dtd">
<company>
   <employee empId="E001" dept="IT">
      <name>Anita Suresh</name>
      <designation>Developer</designation>
      <phone type="mobile">9876543210</phone>
      <phone type="landline">0484-1234567</phone>
   </employee>
   <employee empId="E002" dept="HR">
      <name>Rahul Krishnan</name>
      <designation>Manager</designation>
   </employee>
   <employee empId="E003">
      <name>Priya Nair</name>
      <designation>Tester</designation>
      <phone type="mobile">9123456789</phone>
   </employee>
</company>
```

**Design walkthrough:**

> **Step 1** — `company` contains one or more `employee` elements (`employee+`).
> **Step 2** — `employee` must have `name` and `designation` in that order; phones are optional (`phone*`).
> **Step 3** — `empId` is declared `ID` and `#REQUIRED`, guaranteeing uniqueness across the document.
> **Step 4** — `dept` defaults to `"General"` if omitted.
> **Step 5** — `phone` has an enumerated `type` attribute defaulting to `"mobile"`.
> **Step 6** — The second employee has no `<phone>` elements, satisfying the `*` cardinality.
> **Step 7** — If we tried `<designation>CEO</designation>`, the parser would **reject** it because `CEO` is not in the allowed enumeration.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Module 3):**
> 1. **Confusing `xs:int` and `xs:integer`** — `xs:int` is a 32-bit signed integer; `xs:integer` has unlimited precision. If the question says "whole number," use `xs:integer`.
> 2. **Forgetting `minOccurs="0"`** for optional elements — by default every element in a `<xs:sequence>` is required (equivalent to `minOccurs="1"`), unlike in DTD where you must declare everything.
> 3. **Mixing up `EMPTY` content with no declaration** — if you don't declare an element, it cannot appear; `EMPTY` means the element *can* appear but with no content.
> 4. **Using `|` inside DTD content models incorrectly** — `|` means *choice* (exactly one), not *or separator*. `(a,b | c)` is **not** valid syntax.
> 5. **Omitting `xmlns:xs`** — without the XSD namespace, all `<xs:element>`, `<xs:complexType>`, etc. will not be recognized.
> 6. **Forgetting `xsi:schemaLocation` in XML instance** — to link an XSD to an XML file, the instance must declare `xmlns:xsi` and `xsi:schemaLocation="namespace filename.xsd"`.
> 7. **Not closing DTD internal block** — the `]>` closing the internal DTD subset is frequently mistyped as `]>` vs `]>`. Always check this.
> 8. **Pattern facet quirks** — in XSD regex, `.*` is greedy; use `\d` for digit, not `\\d` (single backslash in attribute value).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for DTD & XML Schema:**
> - ✅ DTD is the **legacy** schema language for XML; XSD is the **modern W3C standard**.
> - ✅ DTD syntax: `<!ELEMENT ...>`, `<!ATTLIST ...>`, `<!ENTITY ...>`, `<!NOTATION ...>`.
> - ✅ DTD content models: `EMPTY`, `ANY`, `#PCDATA`, sequences `(a, b)`, choices `(a | b)`, mixed `(#PCDATA | e)*`.
> - ✅ Cardinality operators in DTD: `?` (0–1), `*` (0–many), `+` (1–many), none (exactly 1).
> - ✅ Attribute types in DTD: `CDATA`, `ID`, `IDREF`, `IDREFS`, `NMTOKEN`, enumerated, `ENTITY`, `NOTATION`.
> - ✅ Attribute default keywords: `#REQUIRED`, `#IMPLIED`, `#FIXED "value"`, quoted default.
> - ✅ Entity types: general (`&name;`), parameter (`%name;`), internal vs external.
> - ✅ XSD is itself **XML**; requires `xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"`.
> - ✅ XSD has **two type kinds**: `xs:simpleType` (text-only) and `xs:complexType` (with children/attributes).
> - ✅ XSD compositors: `<xs:sequence>` (ordered), `<xs:choice>` (one-of), `<xs:all>` (each 0/1 time, any order).
> - ✅ XSD facets: `minInclusive`, `maxInclusive`, `minLength`, `maxLength`, `length`, `pattern`, `enumeration`, `totalDigits`, `fractionDigits`.
> - ✅ XSD cardinality: `minOccurs`, `maxOccurs` per element/attribute (default both 1).
> - ✅ XSD inheritance: `<xs:extension base="...">` and `<xs:restriction base="...">`.
> - ✅ Built-in numeric types: `xs:integer`, `xs:int`, `xs:long`, `xs:short`, `xs:byte`, `xs:decimal`, `xs:float`, `xs:double`.
> - ✅ Built-in date/time types: `xs:date`, `xs:time`, `xs:dateTime`, `xs:duration`, `xs:gYear`, `xs:gMonth`.
> - ✅ Namespaces in XSD: `targetNamespace`, `elementFormDefault`, `attributeFormDefault`.
> - ✅ Key advantage of XSD over DTD: **data types, constraints, namespaces, and type inheritance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hooks for KTU Exam Hall:**
> - *"**D**TD = **D**ocument **T**ype **D**efinition = grammar of the document"*
> - *"**X**SD = **X**ML **S**chema **D**efinition = typed grammar of the document"*
> - *Cardinality in DTD: append `?`/`*`/`+` after element. In XSD: use `minOccurs`/`maxOccurs`.*
> - *Default attribute value syntax differs: DTD uses quoted string, XSD uses `default="..."`.*
> - *Entity in DTD = macro/substitution. No direct equivalent in XSD (uses `xs:include` instead).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: XML Presentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** XML Presentation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is XML Presentation? In the context of **Advanced Database Systems**, **XML Presentation** refers to the suite of W3C-standardized technologies used to *display*, *transform*, *format*, and *navigate* the contents of an XML document so that it becomes meaningful to an end-user (human or machine). The raw XML data tree is just a semantic skeleton; **Presentation technologies put the "skin" on that skeleton**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** > *XML Presentation* is the process of applying a structured set of W3C rules and standards—namely **XSL (Extensible Stylesheet Language), XPath, XSLT, XSL-FO, and CSS**—to an XML document in order to transform its logical tree-based structure into a viewable, printable, or transmittable representation across heterogeneous systems. The presentation stack is built on three foundational pillars: | Pillar | Full Name | Role | |---|---|---| | **XPath** | XML Path Language | Locates nodes inside an XML tree | | **XSLT** | XSL Transformations | Converts XML from one form to another | | **XSL-FO** | XSL Formatting Objects | Describes the visual/paged layout (print/PDF) | | **CSS** | Cascading Style Sheets | Browser-level styling for XML | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Newspaper Printing Press"** > > Imagine **XML** as the *raw manuscript* of a newspaper (headlines, articles, captions, image references). A manuscript is useless to a reader until it is: > 1. **Edited and rearranged** for different editions → that is **XSLT** (transformation). > 2. **Tagged with page numbers, columns, fonts, and margins** → that is **XSL-FO** (formatting). > 3. **Pointed to by editorial addresses** like "Page 3, Column 2" → that is **XPath** (navigation). > > Without the printing press (presentation), the manuscript remains unread. XML is the *data*; Presentation is the *delivery*. ## 1.3 Why XML Presentation Matters in Advanced Databases In **Advanced Database Systems**, XML is used as a **data interchange format** between heterogeneous databases (e.g., relational ↔ object-oriented ↔ native XML stores). For this interchange to be usable, the XML payload must be: - **Transformed** into the target system's native format. - **Validated** for structural correctness. - **Formatted** for human-readable output (reports, invoices, web pages). > [!TIP] > **Key Insight:** XPath, XSLT, and XSL-FO are the *three musketeers* of XML Presentation. XPath is the "search engine," XSLT is the "translator," and XSL-FO is the "graphic designer." All three work together as a single pipeline. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The XML Presentation Pipeline A typical XML presentation flow follows this ordered logic: ### Step 1 — Input XML Document A well-formed XML document acts as the **source tree**. Example: an `order.xml` containing customer, item, and price elements. ### Step 2 — XPath Navigation The stylesheet engine (e.g., Saxon, Xalan) uses **XPath expressions** to traverse the document tree and select specific nodes (elements, attributes, text). ### Step 3 — XSLT Template Matching The XSLT processor matches selected nodes against **`xsl:template`** rules and instantiates new elements in the **result tree**. ### Step 4 — XSL-FO Formatting (Optional) If the target output is a paginated document (PDF, print), the result tree is further processed by an **XSL-FO engine** (e.g., Apache FOP) to produce pages with headers, footers, page numbers, and tables. ### Step 5 — Output The final artifact can be **HTML**, **XHTML**, **plain text**, **PDF**, or even **another XML dialect** (e.g., converting DBML to SVG). ## 2.2 XPath — The Locator Language **XPath (XML Path Language)** is a non-XML syntax for addressing parts of an XML document. It models the XML as a tree of nodes. **Node Types in XPath:** - **Root node** — the document itself. - **Element nodes** — XML elements. - **Attribute nodes** — XML attributes. - **Text nodes** — character data. - **Comment nodes** — ``. - **Processing-instruction nodes** — ``. - **Namespace nodes** — namespace declarations. **XPath Expressions Cheat Sheet:** | Expression | Meaning | |---|---| | `/` | Root path / child separator | | `//` | Recursive descent (anywhere in the document) | | `.` | Current node (context node) | | `..` | Parent node | | `@` | Attribute axis | | `*` | Wildcard (any element) | | `[ ]` | Predicate (filter / index) | | `\|` | Union of two node sets | | `text()` | Text node child | | `node()` | Any node | **Example:** The XPath `/library/book[price > 500]/title` selects the `` element of every `<book>` whose `<price>` child exceeds 500. **XPath Operators:** - Comparison: `=`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` - Boolean: `and`, `or`, `not()` - Numeric: `+`, `-`, `*`, `div`, `mod` - Set: `|` (union) ## 2.3 XSLT — The Transformation Engine **XSLT (XSL Transformations)** is a declarative, Turing-complete language written in valid XML syntax. It transforms an input XML tree into an output tree. **Core XSLT Elements:** | Element | Function | |---|---| | `<xsl:stylesheet>` | Root element of every XSLT document | | `<xsl:template match="...">` | Defines a transformation rule | | `<xsl:value-of select="...">` | Inserts the text value of an XPath expression | | `<xsl:for-each select="...">` | Iterates over a node-set | | `<xsl:if test="...">` | Conditional processing | | `<xsl:choose>` / `<xsl:when>` / `<xsl:otherwise>` | Multi-way branching (switch/case) | | `<xsl:sort>` | Sorts a node-set | | `<xsl:apply-templates>` | Recursively applies templates | | `<xsl:output method="...">` | Declares output format (html, xml, text) | **Transformation Model (Push vs Pull):** - **Pull Style:** Uses explicit `<xsl:value-of>` and `<xsl:for-each>` calls. - **Push Style:** Relies on `<xsl:apply-templates>` and pattern matching — preferred for complex documents. ## 2.4 XSL-FO — The Formatting Object Language **XSL-FO (XSL Formatting Objects)** is an XML vocabulary that describes the **visual layout** of paginated documents. It is paired with a **Formatting Engine** such as **Apache FOP** to produce **PDF**, **PS**, or **PCL** output. **Core XSL-FO Elements:** | Element | Function | |---|---| | `<fo:root>` | Document root | | `<fo:layout-master-set>` | Declares page geometry (size, margins) | | `<fo:page-sequence>` | Defines one logical sequence of pages | | `<fo:flow flow-name="xsl-region-body">` | Main content stream | | `<fo:block>` | Paragraph-level block | | `<fo:inline>` | Inline (run-level) text | | `<fo:table>`, `<fo:table-row>`, `<fo:table-cell>` | Tabular layout | | `<fo:external-graphic>` | Embedded image | **The Master Page Concept:** $$\text{Master Page} = \text{Page Size} \cup \text{Page Margins} \cup \text{Regions (header, footer, body)}$$ A `<fo:simple-page-master>` defines a single page template with: - `page-height` and `page-width` (e.g., A4 = 210mm × 297mm) - `margin-top`, `margin-bottom`, `margin-left`, `margin-right` - `region-body`, `region-before` (header), `region-after` (footer), `region-start` (left), `region-end` (right) ## 2.5 CSS Styling for XML **Cascading Style Sheets (CSS)** can be directly applied to XML for browser display: ```xml <?xml-stylesheet type="text/css" href="style.css"?> ``` CSS uses familiar properties (`color`, `font-size`, `display: block`, etc.) on element selectors. ## 2.6 The KTU Formula / Notation Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Keyword | Meaning | Context | |---|---|---| | `match="pattern"` | XPath pattern to match nodes | XSLT | | `select="expr"` | XPath expression to select nodes | XSLT | | `mode="name"` | Processing mode for templates | XSLT | | `priority="n"` | Tie-breaker for conflicting templates | XSLT | | `name="prefix:local"` | Qualified name | XML Namespaces | | `xmlns:prefix="URI"` | Namespace declaration | XML Namespaces | | `<xsl:import>` / `<xsl:include>` | Modular stylesheet composition | XSLT | | `flow-name` | Logical flow region | XSL-FO | | `block-progression-dimension` | Vertical page size | XSL-FO | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **B2B Data Exchange:** Transforming purchase orders (XML) between ERP systems via XSLT. - **Web Reporting:** Generating paginated PDF invoices from database XML using XSL-FO + Apache FOP. - **Content Management:** Single-source XML published to web (HTML), print (PDF), and mobile (WML) via parallel XSLT stylesheets. - **Data Warehousing:** ETL pipelines use XSLT to normalize heterogeneous XML feeds into a canonical warehouse schema. --- <!-- SECTION_2_END --> <!-- SECTION_3_START --> # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example — XSLT Transformation (Order Report) **Source XML (`order.xml`):** ```xml <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <order> <customer id="C101">Anjali Menon</customer> <items> <item> <name>Database Systems Textbook</name> <qty>2</qty> <price>550</price> </item> <item> <name>ER Diagram Pen</name> <qty>5</qty> <price>40</price> </item> </items> </order> ``` **Target:** Produce an HTML report listing each item with a calculated `subtotal = qty × price` and a grand total. **XSLT Stylesheet (`order.xsl`):** ```xml <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <xsl:stylesheet version="1.0" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform"> <xsl:output method="html" indent="yes"/> <xsl:template match="/order"> <html> <body> <h2>Order for: <xsl:value-of select="customer"/></h2> <table border="1"> <tr><th>Item</th><th>Qty</th><th>Price</th><th>Subtotal</th></tr> <xsl:for-each select="items/item"> <tr> <td><xsl:value-of select="name"/></td> <td><xsl:value-of select="qty"/></td> <td><xsl:value-of select="price"/></td> <td><xsl:value-of select="qty * price"/></td> </tr> </xsl:for-each> </table> <p> <strong>Grand Total: <xsl:value-of select="sum(items/item/qty * items/item/price)"/> </strong> </p> </body> </html> </xsl:template> </xsl:stylesheet> ``` ### Step-by-Step Derivation of the Output **Step 1 — The XSLT processor receives `order.xml`.** It treats the `<order>` element as the document element and locates the root template `<xsl:template match="/order">`. **Step 2 — Inside the template, a new HTML tree is emitted.** The processor literally writes `<html><body><h2>...` to the output stream. **Step 3 — The `<xsl:value-of select="customer"/>` resolves to a text node.** Following the XPath axis, the relative `customer` is resolved from the context node `/order` → yields the text content *"Anjali Menon"*. **Step 4 — `<xsl:for-each select="items/item">` iterates over two `<item>` nodes.** The context node changes for each iteration, making `qty` and `price` accessible. **Step 5 — The arithmetic `qty * price` is evaluated.** $$ \text{subtotal}_{\text{book}} = 2 \times 550 = 1100 $$ $$ \text{subtotal}_{\text{pen}} = 5 \times 40 = 200 $$ **Step 6 — The grand total is computed using the `sum()` XPath function across all item nodes.** $$ \text{Grand Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{qty}_i \times \text{price}_i) $$ $$ \text{Grand Total} = (2 \times 550) + (5 \times 40) $$ $$ \text{Grand Total} = 1100 + 200 = 1300 $$ **Final Rendered HTML (after transformation):** ```html <html> <body> <h2>Order for: Anjali Menon</h2> <table border="1"> <tr><th>Item</th><th>Qty</th><th>Price</th><th>Subtotal</th></tr> <tr> <td>Database Systems Textbook</td> <td>2</td> <td>550</td> <td>1100</td> </tr> <tr> <td>ER Diagram Pen</td> <td>5</td> <td>40</td> <td>200</td> </tr> </table> <p><strong>Grand Total: 1300</strong></p> </body> </html> ``` ## 3.2 Analytical Trace — XPath Evaluation Consider the XPath: ``` /library/book[3]/author[last()] ``` **Trace it step by step:** 1. `/library` → jumps to the root `<library>` element. 2. `/book[3]` → selects the **3rd** `<book>` child (1-indexed in XPath). 3. `/author[last()]` → among the authors of that book, `last()` returns the position of the **last** node. The predicate `[last()]` therefore selects the **last** `<author>` child. **General Rule:** $$ \text{position index in XPath} = n \in \mathbb{N}, \quad n \geq 1 $$ $$ \text{first()} \equiv [1], \quad \text{last()} \equiv [\text{position()} = \text{last()}] $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Trace — XSL-FO Page Geometry For an **A4 page** with 1-inch margins all around: $$ \text{Page Width} = 210 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Page Height} = 297 \text{ mm} $$ $$ \text{Region Body Width} = 210 - 2 \times 25.4 = 159.2 \text{ mm} $$ $$ \text{Region Body Height} = 297 - 2 \times 25.4 - \text{header}_h - \text{footer}_h $$ If header height = 15 mm and footer height = 15 mm: $$ \text{Region Body Height} = 297 - 50.8 - 15 - 15 = 216.2 \text{ mm} $$ **XSL-FO Master Page Declaration:** ```xml <fo:simple-page-master master-name="A4" page-height="297mm" page-width="210mm" margin-top="25.4mm" margin-bottom="25.4mm" margin-left="25.4mm" margin-right="25.4mm"> <fo:region-body margin-top="15mm" margin-bottom="15mm"/> <fo:region-before extent="15mm"/> <fo:region-after extent="15mm"/> </fo:simple-page-master> ``` --- <!-- SECTION_3_END --> <!-- SECTION_4_START --> # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The XML Presentation Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source XML Document] --> B[XPath Engine] B --> C[Node Set Selection] C --> D[XSLT Processor] D --> E[Result Tree] E --> F{Output Type?} F -->|HTML| G[XSL-FO Engine optional] F -->|PDF/Print| H[Apache FOP Renderer] F -->|Plain Text| I[Text Output] G --> J[Web Browser] H --> K[PDF Document] I --> L[Console or File] ``` **Reading the diagram:** The Source XML first passes through the XPath engine to identify relevant nodes. These nodes are then handed to the XSLT processor, which builds a result tree. Depending on the declared output method, the result tree is either rendered as HTML in a browser, converted to PDF by Apache FOP, or emitted as plain text. ## 4.2 XSLT Processing Model — Push-Style (Template-Driven) ```mermaid flowchart TB Src[Source Tree: order.xml] --> Proc[XSLT Processor] Proc --> R1{Root node match?} R1 -->|Yes - apply /order template| T1[Template: /order] T1 --> T2[Emit html head] T2 --> T3[Emit table header] T3 --> AT1[xsl:apply-templates select=items/item] AT1 --> CTX[New context: each item] CTX --> T4[Template: item] T4 --> T5[Compute qty*price] T5 --> T6[Emit table row] T6 --> AT1 T1 --> SUM[Compute sum of all subtotals] SUM --> T7[Emit total paragraph] T7 --> Out[Result Tree: order.html] ``` **Reading the diagram:** The XSLT processor begins at the document root, finds a matching template, and emits the outer HTML structure. It then recursively applies templates to each `<item>` element, computing subtotals on the fly. Once all items are processed, the grand total is computed and the final HTML is emitted. ## 4.3 XSL-FO Document Structure (Paginated Rendering) ```mermaid flowchart TB Root[fo:root] --> LMS[fo:layout-master-set] LMS --> SPM[fo:simple-page-master A4] SPM --> RB[fo:region-body] SPM --> RBE[fo:region-before header] SPM --> RAF[fo:region-after footer] Root --> PS[fo:page-sequence master-reference A4] PS --> SF[fo:flow flow-name xsl-region-body] SF --> B1[fo:block Title] SF --> TAB[fo:table] TAB --> TR1[fo:table-row header] TAB --> TR2[fo:table-row data] TR2 --> TC1[fo:table-cell] TR2 --> TC2[fo:table-cell] ``` **Reading the diagram:** The XSL-FO document is divided into a layout definition block (master pages) and a content block (page sequences and flows). The master page defines regions for header, footer, and body. The flow element streams content into the body region, where it gets paginated automatically by the rendering engine. ## 4.4 XML Namespace Resolution Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR Decl[xmlns:db=DB_URI] --> Pref[Prefix db] Pref --> Elem[db:table] Elem --> URIMap[URI to Namespace Mapping] URIMap --> Ident[Uniquely Identified Element Type] Ident --> Valid[Validates against Multiple Vocabularies] ``` **Reading the diagram:** A namespace declaration binds a short prefix (`db`) to a long URI (`http://.../db`). When the prefix is used in an element name, the parser resolves it to the URI, which uniquely identifies the element's vocabulary. This allows multiple vocabularies to coexist in one document without name collisions. --- <!-- SECTION_4_END --> <!-- SECTION_5_START --> # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]** *Explain the role of XSLT in XML data presentation. Differentiate between XSLT and XSL-FO.* **Model Answer:** **XSLT (XSL Transformations):** XSLT is a W3C-standardized, declarative language written in XML syntax that transforms an input XML tree into a result tree (which may be HTML, plain text, or another XML dialect). It uses XPath expressions to navigate the source tree and `<xsl:template>` elements to define output rules. XSLT is primarily concerned with **structural transformation** — restructuring data, filtering nodes, performing computations, and renaming elements. **XSL-FO (XSL Formatting Objects):** XSL-FO is an XML vocabulary that describes the **visual and paginated layout** of a document.
