It defines page geometry (size, margins, regions), fonts, tables, and block-level structures. XSL-FO does not transform the *structure* of data; instead, it adds *formatting instructions* that a rendering engine (like Apache FOP) converts into PDF, PostScript, or PCL output. **Key Differences:** | Aspect | XSLT | XSL-FO | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Structural transformation | Visual formatting | | Output | XML, HTML, Text | PDF, Print-ready pages | | Engine | XSLT Processor (Saxon, Xalan) | FO Engine (Apache FOP) | | Focus | Data reshaping | Page layout | --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** *What is XPath? Explain the use of axes like `child`, `parent`, `ancestor`, and `descendant` with examples.* **Model Answer:** XPath (XML Path Language) is a non-XML syntax used to locate and address nodes within an XML document. It models the document as a tree of nodes and uses a compact path-like notation to navigate the hierarchy. **Axes in XPath:** An **axis** defines the direction of navigation from the context node. 1. **`child::`** — Selects immediate child elements. - Example: `child::book` is equivalent to `book` (the default axis). 2. **`parent::`** — Selects the immediate parent. - Example: `parent::library` selects the parent of the current node if it is a `<library>`. 3. **`ancestor::`** — Selects all ancestors up to the root. - Example: `ancestor::library` selects every `<library>` ancestor, regardless of depth. 4. **`descendant::`** — Selects all descendants at any depth. - Example: `descendant::title` selects every `<title>` element anywhere below the context node. The shorthand notation uses `/` for child axis and `//` for descendant axis. For example, `//title` is equivalent to `/descendant::title`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — XSLT Transformation and XPath Navigation **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** (a) *Consider the following XML document representing a library catalog. Write an XSLT stylesheet that transforms it into an HTML table listing all books with their title, author, and price. Books priced above ₹400 should be highlighted in a separate row color.* **(7 Marks)** ```xml <?xml version="1.0"?> <library> <book> <title>Database Concepts Silberschatz 650 ER Diagrams Made Easy Thomas 350 SQL for Beginners Bayross 500 ``` **Model Solution:** ```xml

Library Catalog

background-color:#ffdddd; background-color:#ddffdd;
Title Author Price (INR)
``` **Explanation:** - The template `match="/library"` triggers when the document root is the library element. - `` iterates through every `` child. - `` dynamically assigns a CSS background color based on the price condition. - The `` directive acts as a switch: if `price > 400`, a red tint is applied; otherwise, green. - Each table cell uses `` to extract the text content of the relevant element. (b) *For the same XML document, write the XPath expressions to:* **(7 Marks)** 1. *Select the title of the second book.* 2. *Select all authors whose book price exceeds 450.* 3. *Select the last book's price.* 4. *Count the total number of books in the library.* **Model Solution:** 1. **Title of the second book:** ``` /library/book[2]/title ``` The predicate `[2]` selects the second `` child of ``, and `title` retrieves its title child. 2. **All authors whose book price exceeds 450:** ``` /library/book[price > 450]/author ``` The predicate filters books based on the conditional check, and `author` retrieves the matching authors. 3. **Last book's price:** ``` /library/book[last()]/price ``` The `last()` function returns the position of the final book in the node-set. 4. **Count of total books:** ``` count(/library/book) ``` The `count()` aggregate function returns the number of `` nodes within the library. --- ### Question B — XSL-FO Formatting and Pipeline Architecture (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** (a) *Explain the architecture of the XML presentation pipeline with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of XPath, XSLT, and XSL-FO in this pipeline.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The XML presentation pipeline is the sequential chain of processes that converts a raw XML document into a viewable, formatted output. The architecture is depicted below: ``` [Source XML] → [XPath Navigation] → [XSLT Transformation] → [Result Tree] | v [XSL-FO Formatting Engine] | v [PDF / Print / HTML] ``` **Stage 1 — XPath Navigation:** XPath provides the addressing mechanism. Within an XSLT stylesheet, every `match` and `select` attribute is an XPath expression. XPath enables the processor to identify which nodes to include, exclude, or modify. **Stage 2 — XSLT Transformation:** The XSLT processor applies template rules to the selected nodes. It reads the source tree, matches nodes against templates, and emits a result tree. The result tree can be a completely different XML dialect or an HTML page. **Stage 3 — XSL-FO Formatting (Optional):** When paginated output is needed, the result tree uses XSL-FO vocabulary. The FO engine (Apache FOP) interprets the formatting objects and produces a final paginated artifact (PDF, PS, etc.) with headers, footers, page numbers, and table layouts. **Why this pipeline matters in Advanced Databases:** In enterprise database systems, XML is the lingua franca of data exchange. The pipeline allows the same XML payload to be re-rendered for different consumers — web dashboards (HTML), printed invoices (PDF), and machine feeds (raw XML) — without altering the source. (b) *Design an XSL-FO document that produces a paginated A4 invoice with a header, a customer section, an item table, and a grand total. Show the master page declaration.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** ```xml ACME INVOICE Customer: Anjali Menon Date: 12-Mar-2025 Item Qty Price Subtotal Textbook 2 550 1100 Pen 5 40 200 Grand Total: INR 1300/- ``` **Explanation:** - The `` defines the A4 page geometry, including margins and region definitions. - The `` binds the content to the master page named `"invoice"`. - The `` streams the main content into the body region. - Blocks are used for paragraphs, and `` structures tabular data. - The Apache FOP engine renders this into a paginated PDF with automatic page breaks. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in XML Presentation Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing XSLT with XSL-FO:** Students often write XSLT code when the question asks for XSL-FO, or vice versa. *Tip:* If the question says "transform," it is XSLT. If it says "paginated layout" or "PDF," it is XSL-FO. > 2. **Forgetting the XSLT namespace:** Every XSLT stylesheet must declare `xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform"` in the root element, or the processor will reject the file. > 3. **Wrong use of `>` in XPath predicates:** Inside an XML attribute (like `test="price > 400"`), the greater-than symbol must be escaped as `>`. Writing a raw `>` breaks the XML well-formedness. > 4. **1-indexed XPath positions:** XPath positions start at **1**, not 0. `book[1]` is the first book, not the second. > 5. **Missing `version` attribute:** XSLT 1.0 stylesheets must include `version="1.0"` in ``. XSLT 2.0 uses `version="2.0"` and offers additional features like `group-by` and user-defined functions. > 6. **Mixing content with templates:** Direct text placed inside `` outside any `` will be ignored by the processor. > 7. **Forgetting `xsl:output`:** Without ``, the result tree defaults to XML, which renders poorly in browsers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — XML Presentation** > > - **XML Presentation Stack** = XPath + XSLT + XSL-FO + (optional) CSS. > - **XPath** is the *locator* — it navigates the XML tree using axes, predicates, and functions. > - **XSLT** is the *transformer* — it restructures XML into another format (HTML, text, XML). > - **XSL-FO** is the *formatter* — it produces paginated, printable output via a FO engine. > - Every XSLT stylesheet is itself a valid XML document and must begin with ``. > - The XSLT root element is `` with the namespace `http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform`. > - `` matches a node selected by XPath pattern X. > - `` extracts the text content of node Y. > - `` iterates over a node-set Z. > - `` / `` / `` provides multi-way conditional branching. > - **XPath position functions:** `first()`, `last()`, `position()`. > - **XPath aggregate functions:** `count()`, `sum()`, `avg()` (XPath 2.0), `min()`, `max()`. > - **XSL-FO page structure:** `fo:layout-master-set` → `fo:simple-page-master` → `fo:region-*` → `fo:page-sequence` → `fo:flow` → `fo:block` / `fo:table`. > - **A4 dimensions:** 210 mm × 297 mm. **Letter dimensions:** 8.5 in × 11 in. > - **Apache FOP** is the most widely used open-source XSL-FO rendering engine. > - **Namespaces** prevent element-name collisions when multiple XML vocabularies are merged. > - XSLT is **declarative**, not procedural — you describe *what* the output should look like, not *how* to compute it step by step. > - The result of an XSLT transformation is always a **well-formed XML tree** (even if the output method is HTML, modern XSLT emits well-formed XHTML). > - **CSS can be applied to XML** directly using the `` processing instruction. > - XSLT is **Turing-complete**, meaning it can perform any computation expressible in standard programming models. ---

topics.title: XPath Queries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** XPath Queries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **XPath (XML Path Language)** is a W3C-standardized query language used to navigate and select nodes (elements, attributes, text, namespaces, processing instructions, comments, and document nodes) from an XML document tree. It models an XML document as a rooted, ordered tree and provides a non-XML syntax for addressing parts of that tree.

In the context of **PECDT795 – Advanced Database Systems**, XPath is treated as the foundational declarative query mechanism over **semi-structured (hierarchical) data**, sitting alongside XQuery, XSLT, and XML Schema. XPath expressions are used inside SQL/XML functions, inside XQuery `FLWOR` clauses, inside XSLT templates, and inside native XML database engines (e.g., BaseX, eXist-db).

The current KTU 2024 syllabus (under the NEP-2020 framework) specifically asks for:
* Location paths and node-set construction
* **Axes** (child, parent, self, descendant, ancestor, following, preceding, following-sibling, preceding-sibling, attribute, namespace, descendant-or-self, ancestor-or-self)
* **Node tests** (name tests and node-type tests)
* **Predicates** and the predicate evaluation context
* **XPath functions** (string, numeric, boolean, node-set)
* **XPath operators** (`/`, `//`, `[ ]`, `@`, `*`, `|`, `..`, `.`, `:=`)
* **Abbreviated vs. unabbreviated syntax**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition – XPath is like a *File System Path* in an XML Tree.**

Imagine your XML document is a *folder hierarchy on your computer*.

* The **root element** (`<library>`) is the root folder (`C:\Library`).
* Child elements (`<book>`) are subfolders (`C:\Library\book`).
* Attributes (`@isbn`) are like file *properties* (right-click → Properties → Metadata).
* Text inside an element is like a `.txt` file inside a folder.

Now, just as you type `C:\Library\book\title.txt` to open a file, in XPath you write `/library/book/title` to reach the `<title>` node. The **double slash** `//` means "search anywhere in the tree" — analogous to a recursive `grep -r` or Windows Search.

| File System Analogy | XPath Equivalent |
|---|---|
| `C:\` (root) | `/` (document root) |
| `\Library\Book` | `/library/book` |
| Recursive search | `//book` |
| File metadata | `@isbn` |
| Current folder | `.` |
| Parent folder | `..` |
| All files of any type | `*` |
| Filter (e.g., size > 1MB) | `[price > 500]` |

## 1.3 Key Terminology

* **Node** – A fundamental unit in the XML tree (element, attribute, text, namespace, processing instruction, comment, root).
* **Location Path** – A sequence of *location steps* that selects a node-set.
* **Location Step** – One hop in the path: `axis::node-test[predicate]`.
* **Context Node** – The node currently being processed (XPath is context-driven).
* **Node-Set** – The unordered collection of nodes returned (or used as input to a predicate).
* **Atomic Value** – A primitive (string, number, boolean) — not a node.
* **XPath 1.0** – The version most widely implemented; uses 4 data types.
* **XPath 2.0/3.x** – Adds sequences, typed values, FLWOR-like expressions; used by XQuery 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** XPath **1.0** is what the syllabus expects by default unless explicitly upgraded to XPath 2.0 in XQuery context. XPath 1.0 returns **node-sets**; XPath 2.0+ returns **sequences**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an XPath Expression

An XPath **location path** is a sequence of one or more **location steps**, separated by `/` (child axis, document order) or `//` (descendant-or-self axis, document order).

$$
\text{Location Path} \;=\; \text{RelativeOrAbsoluteStep} \;/\; \text{Step} \;/\; \dots \;/\; \text{Step}
$$

A **single location step** has the canonical form:

$$
\text{Step} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{axis}}_{\text{direction}} \;::\; \underbrace{\text{node\text{-}test}}_{\text{filter by name/type}} \;\Big[\; \underbrace{\text{predicate}}_{0 \text{ or more}} \;\Big]
$$

The shortcut (abbreviated) syntax collapses this to:

$$
\underbrace{/}_{\text{root / child}} \quad \underbrace{//}_{\text{descendant-or-self}} \quad \underbrace{..}_{\text{parent}} \quad \underbrace{@}_{\text{attribute}} \quad \underbrace{*}_{\text{any}}
$$

## 2.2 The 13 XPath Axes (Logical & Geometric Meaning)

| # | Axis Name | Abbreviation | Geometric Meaning | Returns Nodes… |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `child` | (default) | Direct children | Below the context node, one level down |
| 2 | `parent` | `..` | Direct parent | One level up |
| 3 | `self` | `.` | The context node itself | The node itself |
| 4 | `attribute` | `@` | Attribute nodes of context | Properties/metadata |
| 5 | `namespace` | — | Namespace nodes | In scope of the context |
| 6 | `descendant` | — | All descendants | Every level below |
| 7 | `ancestor` | — | All ancestors | Every level above, up to root |
| 8 | `descendant-or-self` | `//` | Self + all descendants | Used at path beginning |
| 9 | `ancestor-or-self` | — | Self + all ancestors | Used in reverse navigation |
| 10 | `following` | — | All nodes after, excluding descendants | Forward in document order |
| 11 | `preceding` | — | All nodes before, excluding ancestors | Backward in document order |
| 12 | `following-sibling` | — | Later siblings | Same parent, after |
| 13 | `preceding-sibling` | — | Earlier siblings | Same parent, before |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Axes are the "join operators" of the XML tree world. Mastering axes is the *single highest-weight* concept in XPath for KTU valuation.

## 2.3 Node Tests

A **node test** filters the candidate set produced by an axis. Two categories:

1. **Name Tests** — match a specific element/attribute name (or `*` for any).
2. **Node-Type Tests** — match by structural kind.

| Node-Type Test | Matches | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `node()` | Any node | `child::node()` |
| `text()` | Text nodes | `descendant::text()` |
| `comment()` | Comment nodes | `//comment()` |
| `processing-instruction()` | PIs | `//processing-instruction()` |

> [!TIP]
> **Common student error:** writing `text='India'` instead of `text()='India'`. Without the parentheses, XPath tries to compare a *node-set* to the string, which in XPath 1.0 produces a *boolean* (true if *any* node in the set equals the string) — a **classic valuation trap**.

## 2.4 Predicates — The Filter Engine

Predicates are **boolean expressions** enclosed in `[ ]`, applied to a node-set in **left-to-right, pairwise filter** fashion. Each predicate reduces the node-set.

$$
\text{Result}(S, [\;p\;]) \;=\; \{\, n \in S \mid \text{eval}(p, \text{context}=n) \text{ is true} \,\}
$$

Predicate forms:
* Positional: `[1]` (first), `[last()]`, `[position() mod 2 = 0]`
* Attribute comparison: `[@type='fiction']`
* Existence: `[author]` (true if any `<author>` child exists)
* Compound: `[@price > 500 and @year > 2010]`

## 2.5 XPath Functions (XPath 1.0 Subset)

| Category | Function | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Node-Set** | `last()`, `position()`, `count()`, `id()` | Position & counting |
| **String** | `string()`, `concat()`, `starts-with()`, `contains()`, `substring()`, `string-length()`, `normalize-space()`, `translate()`, `substring-before()`, `substring-after()` | Text manipulation |
| **Numeric** | `number()`, `sum()`, `floor()`, `ceiling()`, `round()` | Arithmetic |
| **Boolean** | `boolean()`, `not()`, `true()`, `false()`, `lang()` | Logical ops |

## 2.6 XPath Operators (Precedence, High → Low)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1.} \quad & \text{Path operators} \;\; / \;\; // \\
\text{2.} \quad & \text{Unary} \;\; - \;\; ( \text{negation}) \\
\text{3.} \quad & \text{Multiplicative} \;\; \ast \;\; \text{div} \;\; \text{mod} \\
\text{4.} \quad & \text{Additive} \;\; + \;\; - \\
\text{5.} \quad & \text{Comparison} \;\; \lt \;\; \gt \;\; \le \;\; \ge \;\; = \;\; != \\
\text{6.} \quad & \text{Boolean AND} \;\; \text{and} \\
\text{7.} \quad & \text{Boolean OR} \;\; \text{or} \\
\text{8.} \quad & \text{Union} \;\; \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Unabbreviated | Abbreviated | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Root node | `/` | `/` | Implicit at start |
| Document anywhere | `//` | `//` | Short for `/descendant-or-self::node()/...` |
| Any element | `child::*` | `*` | Element nodes only by default |
| Any node | `node()` | — | Includes text, comment, etc. |
| First child | `child::*[1]` | `*[1]` | XPath position is 1-based |
| Last child | `child::*[last()]` | `*[last()]` | — |
| Parent of parent | `parent::*/parent::*` | `../..` | Stacked |
| Element with attr | `*[@id]` | `*[@id]` | Existence predicate |
| Multiple predicates | `*[@a][@b]` | `*[@a][@b]` | **AND-ed**, not OR-ed |
| Union | `a \| b \| c` | `a \| b \| c` | Removes duplicates |
| Self reference | `self::node()` | `.` | — |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

XPath is not a "toy" — it powers:
* **SQL/XML** (`XMLQuery('//book[@year>2020]')` in Oracle, DB2, SQL Server).
* **Web scraping / testing** (Selenium `//*[@id='login']`).
* **XSLT** transformations (every `match=` and `select=` is an XPath pattern).
* **Native XML databases** (BaseX, eXist-db, MarkLogic).
* **SOAP / REST payloads** inspection in middleware.
* **Cybersecurity forensics** — XPath injection is a real OWASP-class vulnerability analogous to SQL injection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference XML Document (Used Throughout)

Let us define a **canonical KTU-style library XML document** that we will use for derivations.

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<library name="KTU Central" location="Kerala">
    <book category="fiction" year="2019" isbn="978-0-13-468599-1">
        <title>The Guide</title>
        <author>R. K. Narayan</author>
        <price currency="INR">499</price>
        <reviews>
            <review rating="5">Masterpiece.</review>
            <review rating="4">Wonderful read.</review>
        </reviews>
    </book>
    <book category="tech" year="2022" isbn="978-0-59-600712-6">
        <title>Database Systems</title>
        <author>Elmasri</author>
        <author>Navathe</author>
        <price currency="INR">850</price>
    </book>
    <book category="tech" year="2024" isbn="978-9-35-343010-1">
        <title>Advanced DBMS</title>
        <author>Connolly</author>
        <price currency="USD">120</price>
        <reviews/>
    </book>
</library>
```

The corresponding **rooted ordered tree** has 3 `<book>` subtrees. We will use this in every example.

## 3.2 Derivation 1 — Absolute vs. Relative Path Resolution

**Expression:** `/library/book[1]/title`

**Step-by-step logical expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:}\quad & / \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \text{document root node} \\
\text{Step 2:}\quad & \text{child::library} \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \text{the <library> element} \\
\text{Step 3:}\quad & \text{child::book[1]} \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \text{filter: keep only the 1st <book> in document order} \\
\text{Step 4:}\quad & \text{child::title} \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \text{the <title> child of that filtered <book>} \\
\text{Final Result:}\quad & \langle\texttt{<title>The Guide</title>}\rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `[1]` is the first, not `[0]`:** XPath is **1-indexed**, unlike most programming languages. This is one of the most common KTU valuation traps.

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Predicates are AND-ed, not OR-ed

**Expression:** `/library/book[@category='tech'][@year > 2020]`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:}\quad & \text{Select all <book> children of <library>} \to S_0 = \{B_1, B_2, B_3\} \\
\text{Step 2:}\quad & \text{Apply } [@category='tech'] \to S_1 = \{B_2, B_3\} \\
\text{Step 3:}\quad & \text{Apply } [@year > 2020] \to S_2 = \{B_3\} \;\;(\text{since } B_2.year=2022 \text{ and } B_3.year=2024) \\
\text{Final Result:}\quad & \text{the 3rd <book> element}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple predicates do NOT form an OR — they are sequentially AND-ed.** The rule is: $\text{Result}(S, [p_1][p_2]) = \text{Result}(\text{Result}(S, [p_1]), [p_2])$.

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Abbreviated vs. Unabbreviated Forms

The same query expressed both ways.

| Abbreviated | Unabbreviated |
|---|---|
| `//title` | `/descendant-or-self::node()/child::title` |
| `//book/@isbn` | `/descendant-or-self::node()/child::book/attribute::isbn` |
| `..` | `parent::node()` |
| `.` | `self::node()` |
| `*` | `child::*` |
| `//*` | `/descendant-or-self::node()/child::*` |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Axis Walk on a Complex Tree

**Expression:** `//book[author[2]]/title`

Logical intent: "Find the titles of books that have **at least a second author**."

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:}\quad & \text{descendant-or-self::node()/child::book} \to \{B_1, B_2, B_3\} \\
\text{Step 2:}\quad & \text{Predicate } [author[2]]: \text{ for each } b, \text{ does } b \text{ have a 2nd <author> child?} \\
& \quad B_1: \text{ 1 author } \to \text{false} \\
& \quad B_2: \text{ 2 authors } \to \text{true} \\
& \quad B_3: \text{ 1 author } \to \text{false} \\
\text{Step 3:}\quad & \text{Retained set} = \{B_2\} \\
\text{Step 4:}\quad & \text{child::title of } B_2 \to \langle\texttt{<title>Database Systems</title>}\rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Boolean Coercion Rules (XPath 1.0)

This is a **favourite KTU theory question**. When a non-boolean is used where a boolean is required (e.g., in `if`, `[ ]`, `and`, `or`, `not()`), XPath 1.0 coerces as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{String} \to \text{Boolean:}\quad & \text{non-empty} \Rightarrow \text{true};\;\; \text{empty} \Rightarrow \text{false} \\
\text{Number} \to \text{Boolean:}\quad & \text{non-zero, non-NaN} \Rightarrow \text{true};\;\; 0 \text{ or NaN} \Rightarrow \text{false} \\
\text{Node-set} \to \text{Boolean:}\quad & \text{non-empty} \Rightarrow \text{true};\;\; \text{empty} \Rightarrow \text{false} \\
\text{Object} \to \text{Boolean:}\quad & \text{always true (XPath 2.0+)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked example:** `[price]` is true if the context node has any `<price>` child element at all — regardless of its textual value.

## 3.7 Derivation 6 — Position Arithmetic and `last()`

**Expression:** `//book[position() = last()]`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:}\quad & \text{descendant-or-self::node()/child::book} \to S = \{B_1, B_2, B_3\} \\
\text{Step 2:}\quad & \text{For each } b \in S, \text{ evaluate } \text{position()} = \text{last()} \\
& \quad \text{Note: position() is index in } S;\; \text{last()} = |S| = 3 \\
& \quad B_1: \text{pos}=1,\; 1=3 \to \text{false} \\
& \quad B_2: \text{pos}=2,\; 2=3 \to \text{false} \\
& \quad B_3: \text{pos}=3,\; 3=3 \to \text{true} \\
\text{Step 3:}\quad & \text{Result} = \{B_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **`[last()-1]` does NOT mean "second to last in document order" universally** — it means "second to last in the *current* filtered set." Filter order matters!

## 3.8 Derivation 7 — XPath Set Operations

| Operation | Syntax | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Union | `\|` (pipe) | `//book[@category='tech'] \| //book[@year > 2020]` |
| Intersection | (no direct op; emulate) | `//book[@category='tech'][self::book[@year > 2020]]` |
| Difference | (no direct op) | Subtract via predicate negation |

> [!NOTE]
> XPath 1.0 supports **union** natively but **not intersection or difference** — these must be emulated with predicates. XPath 2.0+ adds `intersect` and `except` keywords.

## 3.9 KTU Analytical Matrix — Common XPath Constructs → Meanings

| Expression | Meaning in Plain English |
|---|---|
| `//*` | Every element in the document |
| `/library/*` | Every direct child element of `<library>` |
| `//@*` | Every attribute in the document |
| `//book[1]` | First `<book>` in the entire document |
| `//book[price > 500]` | Books whose first price child is > 500 |
| `//book[price][2]` | The 2nd book that has *any* price child |
| `//title/../*` | All siblings of every title (sibling-walk trick) |
| `//book/title \| //book/author` | All titles OR all authors (union) |
| `count(//book)` | Number of books in document |
| `string-length(//book[1]/title)` | Length of first book's title |
| `//book[not(@category)]` | Books that have *no* `category` attribute |
| `//book[author='Connolly']` | Books whose first author child is "Connolly" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 XML Tree Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below shows the logical XML tree of our reference document with the 13 XPath axes visualised as geometric relationships.


## 4.2 The 13 Axes — Geometric Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    CTX[CURRENT CONTEXT NODE]:::center
    CTX -->|child| CH[CHILD NODES]
    CTX -->|parent| PA[PARENT NODE]
    CTX -->|self| SF[SELF]
    CTX -->|attribute| AT[ATTR NODES]
    CTX -->|namespace| NS[NS NODES]
    CTX -->|descendant| DS[ALL DESCENDANTS]
    CTX -->|ancestor| AN[ALL ANCESTORS]
    CTX -->|descendant-or-self| DSO[SELF + DESCENDANTS]
    CTX -->|ancestor-or-self| ANO[SELF + ANCESTORS]
    CTX -->|following| FW[FORWARD NODES]
    CTX -->|preceding| BW[BACKWARD NODES]
    CTX -->|following-sibling| FS[LATER SIBLINGS]
    CTX -->|preceding-sibling| PS[EARLIER SIBLINGS]

    classDef center fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px
    classDef default fill:#f5f5f5,color:#222,stroke:#888
```

## 4.3 XPath Evaluation Pipeline (Functional Flow)

This block diagram shows the end-to-end pipeline from an XPath expression to the result node-set.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input XPath Expression]:::input --> B[Lexical Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Parser: Builds AST]
    C --> D[Step Expander: Abbreviated to Unabbreviated]
    D --> E[Context Node Setup]
    E --> F[Axis Evaluator: produces raw node set]
    F --> G[Node Test Filter]
    G --> H[Predicate Chain: AND sequential]
    H --> I{Any Nodes Left?}
    I -->|No| J[Return Empty Node Set]:::fail
    I -->|Yes| K[Document-Order Sort]
    K --> L[Duplicate Removal]
    L --> M[Output Node Set]:::success

    classDef input fill:#bbdefb,color:#0d47a1
    classDef success fill:#c8e6c9,color:#1b5e20,stroke:#1b5e20
    classDef fail fill:#ffcdd2,color:#b71c1c
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define XPath. List any four XPath axes with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **XPath (XML Path Language)** is a W3C-standardized query language used to navigate and select nodes from an XML document tree by traversing along named *axes* and applying *node tests* and *predicates*.

**Four XPath axes:**

| Axis | Description |
|---|---|
| `child` | Contains the children of the context node |
| `parent` | Contains the parent of the context node (if any) |
| `descendant` | Contains all descendants of the context node (children, grandchildren, etc.) |
| `attribute` | Contains the attributes of the context node (only meaningful for elements) |

*(4 axes × 0.5 mark + 1 mark for definition = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between the abbreviated XPath syntax `//` and `/`. When is each used?**

**Model Answer:**

| Symbol | Unabbreviated Form | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `/` | `child::` (or document root at start) | Direct child — one tree level down | `/library/book` selects direct book children of library |
| `//` | `descendant-or-self::node()/child::` | Recursive descent — search at *any* depth | `//title` selects every title element anywhere in the document |

*When to use:* Use `/` when the structure is known and fixed; use `//` when the depth is variable or unknown, or for "find anywhere" queries. *(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of an XPath location step with an example. Discuss the difference between **name tests** and **node-type tests** with suitable illustrations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the XML document given below, write XPath expressions for queries (i)–(vi) and state what each returns. **(7 marks)**

```xml
<university name="KTU">
    <department id="CSE" hod="Dr. R">
        <student roll="101" cgpa="9.2"><name>Arun</name></student>
        <student roll="102" cgpa="8.1"><name>Beena</name></student>
        <student roll="103" cgpa="7.4"><name>Chitra</name></student>
    </department>
    <department id="ECE" hod="Dr. S">
        <student roll="201" cgpa="8.8"><name>Deepa</name></student>
        <student roll="202" cgpa="9.5"><name>Eshan</name></student>
    </department>
</university>
```

**Queries:**
* (i) All students
* (ii) Students in CSE department
* (iii) Names of students with CGPA > 9.0
* (iv) The roll number attribute of the second student
* (v) HOD of the department having student "Eshan"
* (vi) Count of students in ECE

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Structure of an XPath location step (7 marks):**

A **location step** has three components:

$$
\boxed{\; \text{axis} \;::\; \text{node-test} \;[\; \text{predicate} \;] \;}
$$

* **Axis (2 marks):** Specifies the *direction* of traversal. Examples: `child`, `parent`, `descendant`, `ancestor`, `attribute`, `self`, `following`, etc.
* **Node Test (3 marks):** Filters nodes by *name* or by *type*.
  - **Name test:** `book` matches a `<book>` element; `*` matches any element; `prefix:localname` uses namespace prefix.
  - **Node-type test:** `node()` (any), `text()` (text nodes), `comment()`, `processing-instruction()`, `processing-instruction('target')`.
* **Predicate (2 marks):** A boolean expression in `[ ]` that further filters the node-set. Evaluated once per node; node retained iff predicate is true.
  - Examples: `[1]`, `[last()]`, `[@type='tech']`, `[position() mod 2 = 0]`.

**Example:** `child::book[1]/attribute::isbn`
* Step 1: `child::book[1]` → first `<book>` child
* Step 2: `attribute::isbn` → its `isbn` attribute

---

**(b) XPath expressions (7 marks — 1 mark each + 1 for correctness logic):**

| # | XPath Expression | Result |
|---|---|---|
| (i) | `//student` | All 5 `<student>` elements |
| (ii) | `/university/department[@id='CSE']/student` | 3 students: roll 101, 102, 103 |
| (iii) | `//student[@cgpa > 9.0]/name` | `<name>Arun</name>` (9.2), `<name>Eshan</name>` (9.5) |
| (iv) | `(//student)[2]/@roll` | `"102"` — note the **mandatory parentheses** to force global ordering |
| (v) | `//student[name='Eshan']/../@hod` | `"Dr. S"` — parent-axis walk via `..` |
| (vi) | `count(//department[@id='ECE']/student)` | `2` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** For query (iv), if a student writes `//student[2]/@roll`, the result will be the roll of the **second student of the first department that has at least 2 students** — *not* the globally second student. Wrap with parentheses `(//student)[2]` to enforce document-order semantics. **Common mark-loss area.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **predicates** in XPath. How are multiple predicates combined? Discuss with an example how `position()` and `last()` are used inside predicates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the XML document below, write XPath expressions for the following: **(7 marks)**

* (i) All books that have at least one `<review>` child.
* (ii) The last `<book>` in the document.
* (iii) Books published after 2020 **and** having a price > 500.
* (iv) The titles of all books in the category "tech" that have **no** reviews.
* (v) The number of books whose author's name contains the letter "a".
* (vi) Books where the *currency* of price is not "INR".

*(Use the library XML document from Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Predicates in XPath (7 marks):**

A **predicate** is a boolean expression enclosed in `[ ]` that filters a node-set.

**Rules (3 marks):**
1. Each predicate is evaluated once per node in the input set; the node is retained if the predicate evaluates to **true**.
2. Multiple predicates are **AND-ed** (conjunction), not OR-ed. They are applied **sequentially** in left-to-right order.
   $$S_{[p_1][p_2]} = \{\, n \mid n \in S,\; \text{eval}(p_1, n)=\text{true},\; \text{eval}(p_2, n)=\text{true} \,\}$$
3. Predicates can use XPath functions, comparison operators, set functions, and other location paths as sub-expressions.

**Position functions (4 marks):**
* `position()` — returns the 1-based index of the current node in the **context node-list**.
* `last()` — returns the size of the context node-list.
* `position()=1` is equivalent to `[1]`; `position()=last()` is equivalent to `[last()]`.
* `position() mod 2 = 0` selects every even-positioned node.
* `[position() > 1 and position() < last()]` selects all **except first and last**.

**Example:** `//book[position() = last()-1]` returns the second-to-last book in document order.

---

**(b) XPath Expressions (7 marks — 1 mark each + 1 for synthesis):**

| # | XPath Expression | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| (i) | `//book[reviews]` | Existence predicate — true if `<book>` has any `<reviews>` child |
| (ii) | `(//book)[last()]` | Parentheses force global ordering; returns the 3rd book |
| (iii) | `//book[@year > 2020 and price > 500]` | Boolean AND inside a single predicate |
| (iv) | `//book[@category='tech' and not(reviews/review)]` | `not()` plus a path sub-expression |
| (v) | `count(//book[contains(author, 'a')])` | `contains()` is case-sensitive in XPath 1.0 |
| (vi) | `//book[price/@currency != 'INR']` | Attribute comparison on a nested axis-walk |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** For query (iv), students often write `//book[@category='tech' and reviews='']`. This is **incorrect** because the empty-string test only works on text content of the immediate node, not on the *absence* of children. Use `not(reviews/review)` or `count(reviews/review) = 0`. **2-mark loss area.**

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density bullet summary for last-minute revision:**

* **XPath = W3C query language** for XML trees; uses path expressions with axes, node-tests, and predicates.
* **Tree model:** XML is a rooted, ordered, labeled tree with **7 node types** (root, element, attribute, text, comment, PI, namespace).
* **Location Path = sequence of Location Steps** separated by `/` or `//`.
* **Location Step structure:** `axis::node-test[predicate]` (unabbreviated) → simplified in abbreviated form.
* **13 Axes** (master these for KTU):
  - Structural: `child`, `parent`, `self`, `attribute`, `namespace`
  - Descent: `descendant`, `ancestor`, `descendant-or-self`, `ancestor-or-self`
  - Sibling: `following-sibling`, `preceding-sibling`
  - Document order: `following`, `preceding`
* **Node tests:** Name tests (`name`, `*`, `prefix:name`) and node-type tests (`node()`, `text()`, `comment()`, `processing-instruction()`).
* **Predicates** use `[ ]`; multiple predicates = **sequential AND**; **1-based** indexing; `position()` and `last()` are key.
* **Abbreviated syntax shortcuts:** `//` (descendant-or-self), `..` (parent), `.` (self), `@` (attribute), `*` (any element).
* **4 XPath 1.0 data types:** Node-set, Boolean, Number, String — with strict coercion rules.
* **Key operators (precedence high→low):** `/` `//` → unary `-` → `* div mod` → `+ -` → comparisons → `and` → `or` → `|`.
* **Boolean coercion:** Non-empty sets/strings and non-zero numbers → true.
* **Common pitfalls:** `text='X'` vs `text()='X'`; `[1]` is first not zeroth; multiple predicates = AND not OR; `[last()-1]` depends on filtered set not document.
* **Union** uses `|`; XPath 1.0 lacks intersection/difference (use predicates).
* **Engine of:** XSLT, XQuery, SQL/XML, Selenium, web-scraping, native XML DBs (BaseX, eXist-db).
* **Forced global ordering trick:** wrap in parentheses — `(//book)[2]` ≠ `//book[2]`.
* **Path-as-predicate trick:** `[author[2]]` checks *existence* of a 2nd author child.
* **XPath 2.0/3.x adds:** sequences, typed values, `intersect`, `except`, `for` expressions, anonymous functions — usually *not* required for KTU unless explicitly tied to XQuery 3.
* **Always remember:** XPath is *context-driven* — every expression is evaluated relative to a current context node, context position, and context size.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute golden rule for KTU valuation:** When asked "Write an XPath expression for X," *always* write the **abbreviated** form first, then (if the marks warrant) give the **unabbreviated** expansion in parentheses. Examiners reward axis awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: XQuery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** XQuery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**XQuery (XML Query Language)** is a **W3C-standardized, functional, declarative query language** designed to retrieve, manipulate, and transform data stored in **Extensible Markup Language (XML)** documents, native XML databases (NXDB), and XML views over relational data. It is the XML counterpart of SQL for relational databases and operates on the **XQuery/XPath Data Model (XDM)**, treating XML documents as ordered, typed trees of nodes (document nodes, element nodes, attribute nodes, text nodes, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> XQuery is officially defined by the W3C as *"a typed, functional programming language specifically designed for querying collections of XML data — the same way SQL queries relational data."* It is the foundation for native XML databases such as **BaseX**, **eXist-db**, and **MarkLogic**.

### The Three Foundational Building Blocks of XQuery

XQuery's expressive power rests on three pillars:

| Building Block | Role | SQL Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Path Expressions (XPath 2.0)** | Navigate and select nodes in an XML tree | `SELECT ... FROM` clause |
| **FLWOR Expressions** | Iterate, filter, sort, and project XML data | `SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ... ORDER BY` |
| **Built-in & User-Defined Functions** | Reusable, type-safe transformations | Stored Procedures / Functions |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Google Maps for XML"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine an XML document is a giant **multi-floor office building**. Every floor is an *element*, every room on a floor is a *child element*, and every door label is an *attribute*. XPath is like knowing the room number ("go to Floor 3, Room 305"). FLWOR is like a project manager who: **(F)or** every employee record, **(L)et** the bonus = salary × 0.1, **(W)here** department = 'CSE', **(O)rder by** bonus descending, and **(R)eturn** a neat custom report card. Together, XPath + FLWOR = XQuery.

### Why XQuery Exists — The Problem It Solves

Relational SQL **cannot natively** understand hierarchical XML structures (nested elements, mixed content, attributes). XQuery was engineered to:
1. **Query semi-structured data** without forcing a rigid relational schema.
2. **Transform** XML into HTML, XHTML, plain text, or another XML on the fly.
3. **Bridge the relational-XML divide** via **XQuery Scripting Extension (XQuery Update Facility)** and **SQL/XML** standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **W3C Recommendation Status:** XQuery 1.0 became a Recommendation in **2007**; XQuery 3.1 in **2017**.
> - **Primary Data Model:** XDM (XPath 2.0 Data Model).
> - **Atomic Types:** Based on **XML Schema (XSD)** — `xs:string`, `xs:integer`, `xs:decimal`, `xs:date`, `xs:dateTime`, `xs:boolean`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The XQuery/XPath Data Model (XDM)

XDM views an XML document as a **sequence** — an ordered collection of zero or more items. An *item* is either an **atomic value** (a string, number, date, etc.) or a **node**. There are **seven kinds of nodes**:

| # | Node Kind | Example | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Document | An entire XML file | Root container |
| 2 | Element | `<book>...</book>` | Hierarchical container |
| 3 | Attribute | `id="101"` | Metadata on an element |
| 4 | Text | "Harry Potter" | Character data between tags |
| 5 | Comment | `<!-- note -->` | Non-data annotation |
| 6 | Processing Instruction | `<?xml-stylesheet ?>` | Application directive |
| 7 | Namespace | `xmlns:xsi=...` | Identifier disambiguation |

### 2.2 FLWOR Expressions — The Heart of XQuery

**FLWOR** (pronounced "flower") is an acronym for the five clauses that form a complete XQuery query. It mirrors SQL's `SELECT-FROM-WHERE-ORDER BY` semantics but operates on XML sequences.

| Clause | Full Form | Purpose | SQL Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FOR** | **For** | Iterates over a sequence of items (binding) | `FROM` / Table scan |
| **LET** | **Let** | Assigns a variable to an entire expression (no iteration) | Inline subquery / view |
| **WHERE** | **Where** | Filters the tuples produced by FOR/LET | `WHERE` |
| **ORDER BY** | **Order by** | Sorts results (must be stable, document order preserved by default) | `ORDER BY` |
| **RETURN** | **Return** | Constructs the output (XML/HTML/text) | `SELECT` |

### 2.3 General FLWOR Syntax (Formal Grammar)

```
for $var1 in expr1,
    $var2 in expr2,
    ...
let $var3 := expr3
where condition
order by $var4 [ascending | descending]
return output_expression
```

### 2.4 Path Expressions (XPath 2.0)

XPath is the navigational substrate of XQuery. Every XQuery expression is, at its core, a path expression over the XDM tree.

| Axis | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `child::` | Direct children | `child::book` |
| `descendant::` | All descendants | `descendant::title` |
| `parent::` | Immediate parent | `parent::book` |
| `ancestor::` | All ancestors | `ancestor::library` |
| `attribute::` | Attributes | `attribute::id` |
| `self::` | Current node | `self::book` |
| `following-sibling::` | Next siblings | `following-sibling::chapter` |
| `preceding-sibling::` | Previous siblings | `preceding-sibling::chapter` |

**Abbreviated Syntax (most commonly used in KTU answers):**

| Abbreviation | Long Form |
|---|---|
| `/` | `child::` (rooted) |
| `//` | `descendant-or-self::` |
| `.` | `self::node()` |
| `..` | `parent::node()` |
| `@` | `attribute::` |

### 2.5 XQuery Type System & Operators

XQuery is a **strongly typed** language based on **XML Schema (XSD)**. The type system has:
- **Atomic types:** `xs:string`, `xs:integer`, `xs:decimal`, `xs:float`, `xs:double`, `xs:date`, `xs:dateTime`, `xs:boolean`.
- **Node types:** Element, Attribute, Document, Text, Comment, PI, Namespace.
- **Sequence type:** The *only* data structure in XDM — an ordered collection of items.

**Core Operators:**

| Operator Category | Symbols | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | `+`, `-`, `*`, `div`, `idiv`, `mod` | `idiv` = integer division |
| Comparison (Value) | `eq`, `ne`, `lt`, `le`, `gt`, `ge` | XML Schema collation |
| Comparison (General) | `=`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` | Auto-translated to value comparison |
| Node Comparison | `is`, `<<`, `>>` | Identity & document order |
| Logical | `and`, `or`, `not()` | Standard boolean |
| Sequence | `,`, `to`, `union`, `intersect`, `except` | Sequence construction & set ops |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Syntax Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Variable declaration | `$x := expr` or `$x in expr` | Bind value or sequence |
| FLWOR skeleton | `for $x in E where W order by $x return R` | Core query |
| Path step | `axis::nodetest[predicate]` | Navigation + filter |
| Element constructor | `<element>{ expr }</element>` | Build XML on the fly |
| Attribute constructor | `<e attr="{ expr }"/>` | Build attributes |
| If-then-else | `if (cond) then A else B` | Conditional |
| Function definition | `declare function f($p as type) as type { body }` | User-defined function |
| Quantified expression | `some $x in E satisfies C` | Existential check |
| Universal check | `every $x in E satisfies C` | Universal check |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

XQuery powers critical production systems:
- **Digital Libraries** (Europeana, Library of Congress) — querying millions of bibliographic XML records.
- **B2B Web Services (SOAP)** — message validation, transformation, and routing.
- **Financial Reporting** (XBRL — eXtensible Business Reporting Language).
- **Healthcare** (HL7 CDA — Clinical Document Architecture for Electronic Health Records).
- **National Security & Defence** — querying intelligence XML data streams in real time.
> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Whenever you see a *standardized XML schema* in industry (XBRL, HL7, FIXML, ACORD), XQuery is almost always the underlying query engine.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example Dataset

For all examples below, we use the following XML document, `library.xml`, representing a university library:

```xml
<library>
  <book category="fiction" year="2020">
    <title>The Hobbit</title>
    <author>J.R.R. Tolkien</author>
    <price currency="USD">15.99</price>
  </book>
  <book category="fiction" year="2018">
    <title>Harry Potter</title>
    <author>J.K. Rowling</author>
    <price currency="USD">12.50</price>
  </book>
  <book category="tech" year="2022">
    <title>Database Systems</title>
    <author>C.J. Date</author>
    <price currency="USD">65.00</price>
  </book>
  <book category="tech" year="2021">
    <title>XML Complete Reference</title>
    <author>Heather Williamson</author>
    <price currency="USD">45.00</price>
  </book>
</library>
```

### 3.2 Example 1 — Simplest Path Expression

**Problem:** Retrieve the titles of all books.

```xquery
doc("library.xml")//title
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `doc("library.xml")` → opens the XML document and returns the document node.
2. `//` → descendant-or-self axis (search entire subtree).
3. `title` → returns all `<title>` element nodes in document order.

**Output:**
```xml
<title>The Hobbit</title>
<title>Harry Potter</title>
<title>Database Systems</title>
<title>XML Complete Reference</title>
```

### 3.3 Example 2 — FLWOR with WHERE (Filtering)

**Problem:** List titles of all "tech" category books priced above **50 USD**.

```xquery
for $b in doc("library.xml")//book
where $b/@category = "tech" and $b/price > 50
return $b/title
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Iteration } \longrightarrow \forall b \in \text{seq}(//book), \text{ bind } \$b
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Filter} \longrightarrow \sigma_{(\$b/@category = "tech") \wedge (\$b/price > 50)}(\$b)
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Project} \longrightarrow \pi_{\text{title}}(\$b)
$$

**Output:**
```xml
<title>Database Systems</title>
```

### 3.4 Example 3 — LET Clause + Order By + Element Construction

**Problem:** Display each tech book with a **discounted price** (10% off), sorted by discounted price descending, as new HTML elements.

```xquery
let $books := doc("library.xml")//book[@category = "tech"]
for $b in $books
let $discount := $b/price * 0.9
order by $discount descending
return 
  <book discount="{ $discount }">
    { $b/title }
  </book>
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `let $books` — bind the *entire sequence* of tech books to a variable (no iteration yet).
2. `for $b in $books` — now iterate, one book per iteration.
3. `let $discount := $b/price * 0.9` — compute the discounted price using arithmetic.
4. `order by $discount descending` — sort iterations.
5. `return <book discount="{ $discount }"> ... </book>` — build **new XML output** using the element constructor and attribute value template `"{ ... }"`.

**Output:**
```xml
<book discount="58.5">
  <title>Database Systems</title>
</book>
<book discount="40.5">
  <title>XML Complete Reference</title>
</book>
```

### 3.5 Example 4 — User-Defined Function

**Problem:** Write a reusable function that takes a book element and returns its title + price formatted as a string.

```xquery
declare function local:summary($b as element(book)) as xs:string {
  concat($b/title/text(), " costs $", $b/price)
};

for $b in doc("library.xml")//book
return local:summary($b)
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `declare function local:summary(...)` — declare a **user-defined function** in the `local:` namespace (user functions must be in a non-default namespace).
2. `as element(book)` — type-annotated parameter (strong typing).
3. `as xs:string` — return type.
4. `concat(...)` — built-in string concatenation function.
5. `local:summary($b)` — call the function within a FLWOR.

**Output:**
```
The Hobbit costs $15.99
Harry Potter costs $12.50
Database Systems costs $65.00
XML Complete Reference costs $45.00
```

### 3.6 Example 5 — Joins in XQuery (Cross-Document Join)

**Problem:** Join `library.xml` with `authors.xml` to display author biographies.

Suppose `authors.xml` is:
```xml
<authors>
  <author name="C.J. Date">
    <bio>Pioneer of the relational model.</bio>
  </author>
  <author name="Heather Williamson">
    <bio>XML technology evangelist.</bio>
  </author>
</authors>
```

```xquery
for $b in doc("library.xml")//book[@category = "tech"],
    $a in doc("authors.xml")//author
where $b/author = $a/@name
return 
  <profile>
    { $b/title }
    { $a/bio }
  </profile>
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Cartesian product } \longrightarrow \sigma_{\text{tech}}(//book) \times //author
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Theta-join} \longrightarrow \bowtie_{(\$b/author = \$a/@name)}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Project & Construct} \longrightarrow \pi_{(\text{title}, \text{bio})} \rightarrow \langle \text{profile} \rangle
$$

**Output:**
```xml
<profile>
  <title>Database Systems</title>
  <bio>Pioneer of the relational model.</bio>
</profile>
<profile>
  <title>XML Complete Reference</title>
  <bio>XML technology evangelist.</bio>
</profile>
```

### 3.7 Example 6 — Quantified Expressions

**Problem:** Check whether *every* tech book costs more than **40 USD**.

```xquery
every $b in doc("library.xml")//book[@category = "tech"]
satisfies $b/price > 40
```

**Returns:** `true` (both 65.00 and 45.00 are > 40).

The existential variant uses `some ... satisfies ...`.

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Expression Selection Guide

| You want to... | Use... | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Just navigate the tree | Path expression | Lightweight, no construction |
| Filter rows from many | `for + where` | Iterative filtering |
| Reuse an expression in many places | `let` | Avoids re-evaluation |
| Sort output | `order by` | Stable sort |
| Build new XML/HTML | Element constructor `<...>` | Direct XML output |
| Reuse logic | `declare function` | Modular code |
| Join two XML sources | Multiple `for` clauses + `where` | Tuple-stream join |
| Existence check | `some ... satisfies` | Boolean existential |
| Universal check | `every ... satisfies` | Boolean universal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 XQuery Language Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the various XQuery constructs combine to form a complete query engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[XQuery Program] --> B[Parser and Static Type Checker]
    B --> C[XQuery Optimizer]
    C --> D[Execution Engine]

    subgraph DataSources[XML Data Sources]
        E1[XPath 2.0 Navigation]
        E2[FLWOR Expressions]
        E3[Built-in Functions]
        E4[User-Defined Functions]
        E5[Element and Attribute Constructors]
    end

    subgraph XDM[XQuery Data Model XDM]
        F1[Document Nodes]
        F2[Element Nodes]
        F3[Attribute Nodes]
        F4[Atomic Values]
        F5[Sequences]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Types]
        G1[XML]
        G2[XHTML]
        G3[HTML]
        G4[Plain Text]
    end

    D --> E1
    D --> E2
    D --> E3
    D --> E4
    D --> E5

    E1 --> XDM
    E2 --> XDM
    E3 --> XDM
    E4 --> XDM
    E5 --> XDM

    XDM --> Output

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff

    style Output fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 FLWOR Expression Pipeline (Sequential Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FOR: Iterate over sequence] --> B[LET: Bind expression]
    B --> C[WHERE: Filter tuples]
    C --> D[ORDER BY: Sort result]
    D --> E[RETURN: Construct output]

    A -.- A1[Binds variable to each item]
    B -.- B1[No iteration, full sequence]
    C -.- C1[Boolean predicate]
    D -.- D1[Ascending or Descending]
    E -.- E1[XML or atomic value]

    style A fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 XQuery vs SQL — Conceptual Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SQL[Relational SQL]
        S1[SELECT]
        S2[FROM]
        S3[WHERE]
        S4[ORDER BY]

    end

        X1[RETURN]
        X2[FOR]
        X3[WHERE]

    end

    S1 --- X1
    S2 --- X2
    S3 --- X3
    S4 --- X4
    S5 --- X5
    S6 --- X6

    style SQL fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000

```

```mermaid

    A[User Issues XQuery Expression] --> B[Lexical and Syntax Analysis]
    B --> C[Static Type Checking against XDM]
    C --> D[Query Optimization]

    E --> F[Sequence Construction]
    F --> G[Serialization to Output Format]
    G --> H[Result Stream XML / XHTML / Text]
```
