## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define XQuery. Mention any two differences between XQuery and SQL.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**XQuery** is a W3C-standardized, **functional, declarative query language** used to retrieve, filter, and transform data stored in XML documents. It operates on the XQuery Data Model (XDM), where every value is a *sequence* of items (atomic values or nodes).

**Two Differences:**

| Aspect | XQuery | SQL |
|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Hierarchical, tree-based (XML) | Relational, tuple-based |
| Primary Structure | XPath + FLWOR expressions | SELECT-FROM-WHERE |
| Result | XML/HTML/Text sequences | Result sets (tables) |
| Schema | Schema-less or XSD-typed | Rigid, pre-defined |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
*What is a FLWOR expression? List its five clauses in execution order.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **FLWOR expression** (pronounced "flower") is the core query construct in XQuery that mimics SQL's SELECT-FROM-WHERE structure but operates on XML sequences. It allows iteration, filtering, sorting, and result construction.

The five clauses in execution order:
1. **FOR** — binds a variable to each item in a sequence (iteration).
2. **LET** — binds a variable to an entire expression (no iteration).
3. **WHERE** — filters the tuples produced by FOR/LET.
4. **ORDER BY** — sorts the result sequence.
5. **RETURN** — constructs and outputs the final result.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of XQuery with a neat block diagram. Discuss its data model in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write XQuery expressions for the following on the given XML document `library.xml`:
```xml
<library>
  <book id="B1">
    <title>The Hobbit</title>
    <author>Tolkien</author>
    <price>15.99</price>
    <year>2020</year>
  </book>
  <book id="B2">
    <title>Database Systems</title>
    <author>C.J. Date</author>
    <price>65.00</price>
    <year>2022</year>
  </book>
  <book id="B3">
    <title>XML Bible</title>
    <author>Harold</author>
    <price>40.00</price>
    <year>2019</year>
  </book>
</library>
```
**(i)** Retrieve the titles and authors of books published after 2019. **(2 Marks)**
**(ii)** List the books sorted by price in descending order, showing only the title and price as a new XML element `<result>`. **(3 Marks)**
**(iii)** Write a user-defined function `getExpensive($threshold)` that returns the count of books priced above the threshold. Use it to count books priced above `30`. **(2 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Architecture of XQuery (7 Marks):**

The XQuery processor consists of the following components:

1. **Parser and Lexical Analyzer:** Tokenizes the XQuery source, builds the abstract syntax tree (AST).
2. **Static Type Checker:** Validates types against XDM using XSD atomic types. Catches errors before execution.
3. **Query Optimizer:** Rewrites the FLWOR for performance (predicate pushdown, index usage).
4. **Execution Engine:** Traverses the XML tree using XPath axes; iterates tuples from FOR clauses.
5. **Function Library:** Built-in functions (string, numeric, date, sequence) + user-defined.
6. **Constructor Module:** Builds new XML nodes via element/attribute constructors.
7. **Serializer:** Converts the in-memory sequence tree into the requested output format (XML, HTML, text).

**XQuery Data Model (XDM):**
- **Item** = Atomic value OR Node.
- **Sequence** = Ordered collection of zero or more items (the ONLY data structure).
- **Seven Node Kinds:** Document, Element, Attribute, Text, Comment, Processing Instruction, Namespace.
- **Atomic Types:** Based on XML Schema (`xs:string`, `xs:integer`, `xs:decimal`, `xs:date`, `xs:boolean`).

**(b) XQuery Expressions:**

**(i) Titles and authors of books published after 2019 (2 Marks):**
```xquery
for $b in doc("library.xml")//book
where $b/year > 2019
return <book>{ $b/title, $b/author }</book>
```

**(ii) Books sorted by price descending, only title and price in `<result>` (3 Marks):**
```xquery
for $b in doc("library.xml")//book
order by $b/price descending
return <result>{ $b/title, $b/price }</result>
```

**Output:**
```xml
<result><title>Database Systems</title><price>65.00</price></result>
<result><title>XML Bible</title><price>40.00</price></result>
<result><title>The Hobbit</title><price>15.99</price></result>
```

**(iii) User-defined function for count of expensive books (2 Marks):**
```xquery
declare function local:getExpensive($threshold as xs:decimal) as xs:integer {
  count(doc("library.xml")//book[price > $threshold])
};

local:getExpensive(30)
```
**Output:** `2` (Database Systems @ 65.00 and XML Bible @ 40.00).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the FLWOR expression in detail with a suitable example. How does it differ from SQL SELECT-FROM-WHERE? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the XML document `employees.xml`:
```xml
<company>
  <employee dept="CSE">
    <name>Anand</name>
    <salary>50000</salary>
  </employee>
  <employee dept="CSE">
    <name>Meera</name>
    <salary>60000</salary>
  </employee>
  <employee dept="ECE">
    <name>Ravi</name>
    <salary>55000</salary>
  </employee>
</company>
```
**(i)** Write an XQuery to find the names of all employees in the CSE department earning more than 55000. **(3 Marks)**
**(ii)** Write an XQuery to compute the **total salary** of each department using `let` and the `sum()` function, and return the result as `<dept name="" total="">`. **(4 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**(a) FLWOR Expression Explained (7 Marks):**

FLWOR is the SQL-equivalent query construct in XQuery. It consists of five clauses:

- **FOR:** Iterates over an input sequence, binding a variable to each item. *Example:* `for $e in doc("employees.xml")//employee`.
- **LET:** Binds a variable to a complete expression result (no iteration). Useful for caching intermediate results.
- **WHERE:** Filters the stream of tuples based on a boolean predicate.
- **ORDER BY:** Sorts the result in ascending or descending order.
- **RETURN:** Constructs the final output (XML elements or atomic values).

**Difference from SQL SELECT-FROM-WHERE:**

| Aspect | SQL | XQuery FLWOR |
|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Relational (tables, rows, columns) | XDM (sequences, nodes, atomic values) |
| Hierarchy | Flat tuples | Native nested elements/attributes |
| Return type | Result set | XML/HTML/Text sequence |
| Iteration | Implicit over rows | Explicit FOR/LET |
| Construction | Cannot construct new XML | Element/attribute constructors |

**(b) Solutions:**

**(i) CSE employees earning > 55000 (3 Marks):**
```xquery
for $e in doc("employees.xml")//employee
where $e/@dept = "CSE" and $e/salary > 55000
return $e/name
```
**Output:** `<name>Meera</name>`

**(ii) Total salary per department (4 Marks):**
```xquery
for $d in distinct-values(doc("employees.xml")//employee/@dept)
let $emps := doc("employees.xml")//employee[@dept = $d]
let $total := sum($emps/salary)
return <dept name="{$d}" total="{$total}"/>
```
**Output:**
```xml
<dept name="CSE" total="110000"/>
<dept name="ECE" total="55000"/>
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `doc()` wrapper** — In KTU answers, never write just `//book`. Always open the file: `doc("library.xml")//book`. Loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Using `WHERE` without `FOR`** — FLWOR is a tuple pipeline; without FOR, there's no stream. The standard workaround is a single tuple: `for $x in (1) where ... return ...`. Loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **String comparison vs. node comparison** — Use `eq` or `=` for value comparison; use `is` for identity. Mixing these is a common error.
> 4. **Namespace missing in user-defined functions** — All user functions MUST be in a non-default namespace (commonly `local:`). Writing `declare function summary(...)` will fail. Loss of 2 marks.
> 5. **Element constructor curly braces** — Inside `<element>...</element>`, expressions MUST be inside `{ }`. Writing `<element>$x</element>` prints the literal `$x`. Loss of 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing `,` (sequence constructor) with `return`** — In FLWOR, only the final RETURN clause emits. Multiple commas in RETURN build a sequence.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Viva / Exam:**
> - **XQuery** = W3C functional query language for XML; based on **XDM**.
> - **XDM** treats every value as an ordered **sequence** of items (atomic values or nodes).
> - **Seven node kinds:** Document, Element, Attribute, Text, Comment, PI, Namespace.
> - **FLWOR** = **F**or, **L**et, **W**here, **O**rder by, **R**eturn — the SQL-equivalent core.
> - **FOR** iterates item-by-item; **LET** binds the whole sequence at once.
> - **Path expressions** use XPath 2.0: `/`, `//`, `.`, `..`, `@`, `[predicate]`.
> - **Element constructors** `<e>{ expr }</e>` build XML on the fly; **attribute value templates** use `" { expr }"`.
> - **Strong typing:** parameters/returns annotated with `as xs:integer`, `xs:string`, `element(book)`, etc.
> - **User functions** must be in a non-default namespace (typically `local:`).
> - **Built-in functions:** `count()`, `sum()`, `avg()`, `max()`, `min()`, `concat()`, `substring()`, `string-length()`, `upper-case()`, `lower-case()`, `distinct-values()`.
> - **Joins:** implemented using multiple `for` clauses + a `where` clause (tuple-stream join).
> - **Quantifiers:** `some $x in E satisfies C` (existential), `every $x in E satisfies C` (universal).
> - **Comparison:** `=` (value), `eq`, `lt`, `gt`, etc.; `is` (identity); `<<`, `>>` (document order).
> - **Sequence ops:** `,` (concatenate), `to` (range), `union`, `intersect`, `except`.
> - **Common axes to remember:** `child::`, `descendant::`, `parent::`, `ancestor::`, `attribute::`, `following-sibling::`, `preceding-sibling::`.
> - **XQuery Update Facility (XQuery 1.0+)** and **XQuery 3.1** with **maps** and **arrays** are advanced extensions — mention if asked about evolution.
> - **Use cases:** XBRL, HL7 CDA, SOAP, digital libraries — standardized XML domains.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NoSQL Databases: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** NoSQL Databases: Introduction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)** > **NoSQL** (originally meaning "*Not Only SQL*") is a class of database management systems that depart from the traditional relational (SQL) model by employing flexible, schema-less or schema-light data models — such as key-value, document, column-family, and graph structures — designed for horizontal scalability, high availability, and the ability to handle massive volumes of unstructured, semi-structured, and polymorphic data across distributed commodity hardware. ### 1.1 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of a **traditional SQL (Relational) database** as a **strictly organized government filing cabinet**: - Every drawer has a fixed number of labeled folders (tables with rigid schemas). - Every folder must contain the *exact same set of form fields* in the *exact same order* (rows with predefined columns). - A clerk must fill in *every single field* — even if it's irrelevant (ACID compliance, normalized form). - If you want to add a new field, you must redesign *the entire cabinet* (schema migration). Now imagine a **NoSQL database** as a **giant warehouse with smart sticky notes**: - You can toss in any shape or size of sticky note (JSON document, key-value pair, graph node). - Different notes can have completely different fields (schema flexibility). - You can build more shelves anytime (horizontal scaling) without reorganizing existing ones. - The warehouse never goes "out of stock" because you can replicate notes across many aisles (distributed, eventually consistent). > [!NOTE] > **Origin of the Term "NoSQL"** > The term was first used by **Carlo Strozzi in 1998** to name his lightweight, open-source relational database that did not expose a SQL interface. It was *re-popularized* in **2009 by Johan Oskarsson** to describe the growing ecosystem of non-relational, distributed data stores that powered Web 2.0 giants like Google (Bigtable) and Amazon (Dynamo). ### 1.2 Why NoSQL Emerged — The Motivation The early-to-mid 2000s exposed critical limitations of RDBMS in the era of **Big Data, Cloud Computing, and Web-Scale Applications**: | Driver | Problem with RDBMS | NoSQL Response | |---|---|---| | **Explosive Data Volume** (Petabytes) | Vertical scaling ceiling (bigger servers) | Horizontal scaling via commodity clusters | | **Unstructured / Semi-Structured Data** (social media, IoT, logs) | Rigid pre-defined schemas | Schema-on-read, dynamic schema | | **High Availability Requirements** (24/7 global services) | Single points of failure | Built-in replication, distributed fault-tolerance | | **Agile Development** (rapid feature iteration) | Costly `ALTER TABLE` migrations | Flexible, schema-less data models | | **Read/Write Throughput** (millions of req/sec) | Performance bottlenecks at scale | Eventual consistency, partitioning, denormalization | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** NoSQL is **NOT** a replacement for SQL — it is a *complement*. The modern data stack is **polyglot persistence**: using the right database for the right job. SQL remains supreme for complex transactions and joins; NoSQL excels at scale, speed, and flexibility. ### 1.3 The Twelve Properties of NoSQL (Sadalage & Fowler, 2012) According to the seminal textbook *"NoSQL Distilled"* (Sadalage & Fowler), NoSQL databases are typically characterized by: 1. **Scale horizontally** instead of vertically 2. **Replication** of data across multiple nodes/servers 3. **Simple data model** (no complex joins) 4. **Schema flexibility** (dynamic / schema-on-read) 5. **Newer APIs** (JSON/REST over ODBC/JDBC) 6. **Eventual consistency** over strong ACID (in many cases) 7. **Open source** implementations 8. **Partition-tolerance** by design (CAP theorem) 9. **High throughput** over latency consistency trade-offs 10. **MapReduce** style distributed processing 11. **Big Data oriented** — designed for TB/PB-scale 12. **Economical** — run on commodity hardware ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The ACID vs BASE Paradigm Shift > [!IMPORTANT] > **ACID** is the *traditional* transaction model used by RDBMS. > **BASE** is the *alternative* model adopted by most NoSQL systems. #### 2.1.1 ACID — The Relational Gold Standard | Property | Full Form | Operational Meaning | |---|---|---| | **A** | **Atomicity** | A transaction is "all or nothing" — either every operation succeeds or none are applied. | | **C** | **Consistency** | The database transitions from one valid state to another; all integrity constraints are preserved. | | **I** | **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions execute as if they were serial; partial states are invisible to others. | | **D** | **Durability** | Once committed, data survives crashes, power loss, and hardware failure (written to non-volatile storage). | #### 2.1.2 BASE — The NoSQL Philosophy | Property | Full Form | Operational Meaning | |---|---|---| | **BA** | **Basically Available** | The system guarantees availability — it always responds (possibly with stale data). | | **S** | **Soft state** | The state of the system may change over time, even without input, due to eventual consistency propagation. | | **E** | **Eventual consistency** | Given enough time *and* no further updates, all replicas will converge to the same value. | > [!NOTE] > **The Engineering Trade-off:** ACID gives you *strong guarantees at the cost of speed and availability*. BASE gives you *speed and availability at the cost of temporary inconsistency*. NoSQL databases chose BASE because Web-scale workloads (likes, tweets, page-views) tolerate a few seconds of staleness but cannot tolerate downtime. ### 2.2 The CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000 — formally proven by Gilbert & Lynch, 2002)** > In any *distributed* shared-data system, you can simultaneously guarantee **at most two** of the following three properties: - **C** — **Consistency** *(every read receives the most recent write or an error)* - **A** — **Availability** *(every request receives a non-error response, without guaranteeing it contains the most recent write)* - **P** — **Partition tolerance** *(the system continues to operate despite an arbitrary number of messages being dropped or delayed between nodes due to network failures)* In a distributed system, **P (Partition tolerance) is non-negotiable** — network failures *will* happen. Therefore the real-world choice is between **CP** and **AP**: | Choice | Meaning | Real-World Example | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---| | **CA** (RDBMS) | Single-site, no partitions | Traditional Oracle/PostgreSQL | Not truly distributed | | **CP** | Consistency + Partition tolerance | MongoDB (default), HBase, Redis | Sacrifice availability during partitions | | **AP** | Availability + Partition tolerance | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB | Sacrifice strong consistency → eventual | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick Reference | Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Use Case | |---|---|---| | Eventual Consistency Bound | $T_{converge} \le \Delta t$ | Upper bound on propagation delay | | Replication Factor | $RF = N_{replicas}$ | Number of copies of each data item | | Quorum Read | $Q_{read} = \lfloor (RF/2) \rfloor + 1$ | Minimum nodes that must respond to a read | | Quorum Write | $Q_{write} = \lfloor (RF/2) \rfloor + 1$ | Minimum nodes that must acknowledge a write | | Strong Consistency Condition | $Q_{read} + Q_{write} \gt RF$ | Guarantees reading the latest write | | Horizontal Scaling Capacity | $C_{total} = N_{nodes} \times C_{single}$ | Total throughput scales linearly with nodes | | Sharding Key Cardinality | $\vert S_{key} \vert \ge N_{shards}$ | Each shard should receive roughly equal load | | CAP Trade-off Index | $\Omega = 1 - \frac{\vert C \cap A \cap P \vert}{3}$ | Quantifies the design trade-off ($\Omega \ge \frac{1}{3}$) | > [!TIP] > **Exam Trick:** Always write $\vert S_{key} \vert$ with the `\vert` LaTeX command (not the pipe `|`) when discussing cardinality inside a table — this prevents the markdown table from breaking. ### 2.4 Distributed Data System Architecture Components A typical NoSQL distributed system manages three fundamental concerns: 1. **Data Partitioning (Sharding):** Splitting the dataset across multiple nodes. - Strategies: *Hash-based* (consistent hashing), *Range-based*, *Directory-based*. 2. **Data Replication:** Copying data to multiple nodes for fault-tolerance. - Strategies: *Master-slave*, *Master-master*, *Peer-to-peer* (Dynamo-style). 3. **Consistency Management:** Coordinating reads/writes across replicas. - Strategies: *Quorum*, *Vector clocks*, *Merkle trees*, *CRDTs*. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **E-Commerce:** Product catalogs (document stores) + shopping carts (key-value) + recommendation engines (graph). - **IoT & Sensor Networks:** Time-series ingestion (column-family: Cassandra, InfluxDB). - **Social Networks:** Friend graphs, follower relations (graph: Neo4j). - **Gaming:** Leaderboards, session state (key-value: Redis, Memcached). - **Content Management:** Articles, media metadata (document: MongoDB). - **Financial Fraud Detection:** Relationship traversal (graph databases). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The CAP Theorem Trade-off #### Step 1: Formal Statement Consider a distributed system $S$ comprising $n$ nodes $\{N_1, N_2, \dots, N_n\}$ connected via an asynchronous network. Let: - $C$ = property of linearizable consistency - $A$ = property of availability (every request terminates with a response) - $P$ = property of tolerance to network partitions The CAP theorem states that it is *impossible* for a system to simultaneously guarantee all three. #### Step 2: Proof Sketch (Contradiction) Assume a system $S$ simultaneously satisfies $C \land A \land P$. Consider a network partition that isolates node $N_1$ from $N_2$. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Client } K_1 \text{ writes } W \text{ to } N_1 \text{ at time } t_1 \\ &\text{Client } K_2 \text{ reads from } N_2 \text{ at time } t_2 > t_1 \\ &\text{Due to partition: } N_1 \text{ and } N_2 \text{ cannot communicate} \\ &\text{By Availability: } K_2 \text{ must receive a response from } N_2 \\ &\text{By Consistency: the response must reflect write } W \\ &\text{Contradiction: } N_2 \text{ cannot know about } W \text{ due to the partition} \\ &\therefore C \land A \land P \text{ is impossible} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3: Practical Implication Since $P$ is mandatory in distributed systems, designers choose: $$ S_{design} = \begin{cases} C \land P & \text{(sacrifice Availability during partitions)} \\ A \land P & \text{(sacrifice strong Consistency)} \end{cases} $$ The CA case is degenerate: it applies only to *single-node* or *non-distributed* systems, which by definition do not face partitions. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Quorum Numbers for Strong Consistency Given a replication factor $RF$ and a quorum of $Q$ acknowledgements, the system can survive $F$ simultaneous failures: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Write quorum: } W \ge 1 \text{ (must write to at least one node)} \\ &\text{Read quorum: } R + W \gt RF \text{ (read and write sets must overlap)} \\ &\text{Number of failures tolerated: } F = RF - W \\ &\text{For balanced load: } R = W = \left\lfloor \frac{RF}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Example:** If $RF = 3$ and $W = 2$, then $R \ge 2$ for strong consistency. The system can tolerate $F = 3 - 2 = 1$ simultaneous node failure. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: RDBMS vs NoSQL | Dimension | RDBMS (SQL) | NoSQL | |---|---|---| | **Data Model** | Relational (tables, rows, columns) | Key-Value, Document, Column-Family, Graph | | **Schema** | Rigid, predefined (schema-on-write) | Flexible, dynamic (schema-on-read) | | **Scaling** | Primarily vertical (scale up) | Primarily horizontal (scale out) | | **Consistency** | Strong (ACID) | Eventual (BASE) or tunable | | **Transactions** | Full multi-row ACID | Limited / single-document atomicity | | **Query Language** | Standardized SQL | API-based, JSON, Gremlin, Cypher, CQL | | **Joins** | First-class operation | Avoided or limited (denormalized) | | **Hardware** | High-end, proprietary | Commodity, cloud-friendly | | **Best For** | Complex queries, OLTP, OLAP, finance | Big data, real-time web, IoT, social | | **Examples** | Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server | MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Neo4j, DynamoDB | --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: NoSQL Data Model Categories | Category | Data Structure | Strengths | Weaknesses | Real-World DBs | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Key-Value Store** | Hash table: $\{K \rightarrow V\}$ | Ultra-fast lookups, simple | No querying on value, no relations | Redis, Memcached, DynamoDB, Riak | | **Document Store** | JSON / BSON / XML documents | Flexible schema, nested data, queryable | Limited joins, larger storage | MongoDB, CouchDB, Couchbase | | **Column-Family Store** | Sparse columns grouped by row-key | Massive write throughput, time-series | Limited query flexibility | Cassandra, HBase, BigTable | | **Graph Database** | Nodes + Edges + Properties | Relationship traversal, pattern matching | Not general-purpose, partitioning hard | Neo4j, JanusGraph, ArangoDB | --- ### 3.5 Step-by-Step: When to Choose NoSQL (Decision Algorithm) A B.Tech student can apply the following decision tree in exam questions: $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Decision: SQL or NoSQL?} \\ &\text{1. Is data highly relational with many joins?} \rightarrow \text{Use SQL} \\ &\text{2. Is the workload write-heavy and latency-critical?} \rightarrow \text{Use NoSQL} \\ &\text{3. Does the data have variable / evolving schema?} \rightarrow \text{Use NoSQL} \\ &\text{4. Is the data scale in petabytes / exabytes?} \rightarrow \text{Use NoSQL} \\ &\text{5. Are strong ACID transactions mandatory?} \rightarrow \text{Use SQL} \\ &\text{6. Does the application need offline-first / mobile sync?} \rightarrow \text{Use NoSQL} \\ &\text{7. Otherwise: Use polyglot persistence (both)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 NoSQL Database Classification Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A[NoSQL Databases] --> B[Key-Value Store] A --> C[Document Store] A --> D[Column-Family Store] A --> E[Graph Database] B --> B1[Redis] B --> B2[DynamoDB] B --> B3[Memcached] B --> B4[Riak] C --> C1[MongoDB] C --> C2[CouchDB] C --> C3[Couchbase] D --> D1[Cassandra] D --> D2[HBase] D --> D3[BigTable] E --> E1[Neo4j] E --> E2[JanusGraph] E --> E3[ArangoDB] ``` ### 4.2 CAP Theorem Triangle ```mermaid graph LR C[Consistency] --- A[Availability] A --- P[Partition Tolerance] C --- P C --> CP[CP Systems: MongoDB HBase Redis] A --> AP[AP Systems: Cassandra DynamoDB CouchDB] P --> CA[CA Systems: RDBMS Single-Node] style C fill:#ffcccc style A fill:#ccffcc style P fill:#ccccff style CP fill:#ffe599 style AP fill:#ffe599 style CA fill:#ffe599 ``` ### 4.3 ACID vs BASE Architectural Flow ```mermaid graph TD subgraph ACID[RDBMS - ACID] A1[Client Request] --> A2[Transaction Begins] A2 --> A3[Lock Resources] A3 --> A4[Execute Operations] A4 --> A5{All Operations Succeed?} A5 -->|Yes| A6[Commit - Durable Write] A5 -->|No| A7[Rollback - Atomic Cancel] A6 --> A8[Response to Client] A7 --> A8 end subgraph BASE[NoSQL - BASE] B1[Client Request] --> B2[Accept Write Immediately] B2 --> B3[Return Acknowledgement] B3 --> B4[Propagate to Replicas] B4 --> B5[Eventually Consistent State] B5 --> B6[Response to Future Reads] end style ACID fill:#e1f5ff style BASE fill:#fff4e1 ``` ### 4.4 Distributed NoSQL Architecture (Sharding + Replication) ```mermaid graph TB Client[Client Application] --> LB[Load Balancer] LB --> N1[Node 1
Shard A and B] LB --> N2[Node 2
Shard B and C] LB --> N3[Node 3
Shard C and A] N1 -.Replicates.-> N2 N2 -.Replicates.-> N3 N3 -.Replicates.-> N1 N1 --> S1[Shard A Data] N1 --> S2[Shard B Data] N2 --> S3[Shard B Data] N2 --> S4[Shard C Data] N3 --> S5[Shard C Data] N3 --> S6[Shard A Data] style Client fill:#ffcccc style LB fill:#ffe599 style N1 fill:#c5e0b4 style N2 fill:#c5e0b4 style N3 fill:#c5e0b4 ``` ### 4.5 Evolution of Data Management Paradigms ```mermaid graph LR P1[1970s - Hierarchical and Network DBMS] --> P2[1980s - Relational RDBMS - SQL] P2 --> P3[1990s - Object-Oriented and Object-Relational] P3 --> P4[2000s - XML and Data Warehousing] P4 --> P5[2010s - NoSQL Movement - Big Data] P5 --> P6[2020s - NewSQL and Multi-Model Databases] style P1 fill:#fce4d6 style P2 fill:#e1f5ff style P3 fill:#fff2cc style P4 fill:#e2efda style P5 fill:#f8cbad style P6 fill:#d9d2e9 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define NoSQL databases. List any four characteristics of NoSQL databases.** **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** NoSQL (Not Only SQL) is a class of database management systems that provide a mechanism for storage and retrieval of data modeled in means other than the tabular relations used in relational databases. They are designed to handle large volumes of unstructured, semi-structured, or polymorphic data with flexible schemas. **Four Characteristics of NoSQL:** 1. **Schema Flexibility:** NoSQL databases do not require a fixed, predefined schema. Data of varying structures can coexist. 2. **Horizontal Scalability:** They scale out by adding more commodity servers rather than scaling up a single large server. 3. **High Availability & Fault Tolerance:** Data is automatically replicated across multiple nodes, ensuring the system remains operational despite failures. 4. **Eventual Consistency (BASE Properties):** In distributed settings, replicas may temporarily diverge but will eventually converge to a consistent state. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 0.5 × 4 for characteristics)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Explain the CAP theorem with a suitable diagram.** **Model Answer:** The **CAP theorem**, formulated by **Eric Brewer in 2000**, states that in any distributed shared-data system, only **two out of three** of the following properties can be simultaneously guaranteed: - **C – Consistency:** Every node sees the same data at the same time. - **A – Availability:** Every request receives a response (success/failure). - **P – Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to function despite network partitions. **Practical Interpretation:** Since network partitions (P) are inevitable in distributed systems, designers must choose between: | Combination | Trade-off | Example | |---|---|---| | **CP** | Sacrifices availability during partitions | MongoDB, HBase, Redis | | **AP** | Sacrifices strong consistency | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB | | **CA** | Only possible in single-node systems | Traditional RDBMS | *(3 marks: 1 mark statement + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark example/diagram)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice) --- ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — *Option 1* **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **ACID** and **BASE** properties in detail. Discuss why BASE is preferred in distributed NoSQL systems. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **four main categories of NoSQL databases**. Give one real-world example and typical use case for each. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — ACID vs BASE (7 marks)** | Property | ACID (RDBMS) | BASE (NoSQL) | |---|---|---| | **Atomicity** | All-or-nothing transactions; partial states forbidden | Operations may be partial; eventual success guaranteed | | **Consistency** | Strong consistency; immediate after commit | Eventual consistency; replicas converge over time | | **Isolation** | Transactions appear serial; no interference | Concurrent operations allowed; no isolation guarantees | | **Durability** | Once committed, data is permanent | Similar — writes persisted to disk/SSD | | **Philosophy** | Pessimistic, safe, slow | Optimistic, fast, resilient | | **Use Case** | Banking, ERP, OLTP | Social media, IoT, big-data analytics | **Why BASE is preferred in distributed NoSQL systems (Key Points):** 1. **Network partitions are unavoidable** in distributed environments, so availability is prioritized. 2. **Latency requirements** at Web scale demand sub-millisecond response times — strong consistency is too costly. 3. **Data volume and velocity** (Big Data) make coordination across nodes prohibitively expensive. 4. **Business tolerance:** Most Web applications (likes, page-views, recommendations) can tolerate a few seconds of staleness. 5. **Elasticity:** BASE allows seamless addition/removal of nodes without global locks. *(3 marks table + 4 marks explanation with 5 bullet points)* **Part (b) — NoSQL Categories (7 marks)** **1. Key-Value Store** — Data stored as a hash map $(K, V)$. - *Example:* **Redis** - *Use Case:* Session management, caching, leaderboards. **2. Document Store** — Data stored as semi-structured documents (JSON/BSON/XML). - *Example:* **MongoDB** - *Use Case:* Content management, product catalogs, mobile apps. **3. Column-Family Store** — Data stored in column families; rows can have variable columns. - *Example:* **Apache Cassandra** - *Use Case:* Time-series data, IoT, write-heavy workloads. **4. Graph Database** — Data stored as nodes, edges, and properties. - *Example:* **Neo4j** - *Use Case:* Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs. *(1.5 marks per category × 4 + 1 mark for diagram = 7 marks)* --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Option 2* **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Discuss the **motivations and driving factors** that led to the emergence of NoSQL databases. Explain how they differ from traditional RDBMS. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **CAP theorem** in detail. Explain with examples how MongoDB (CP) and Cassandra (AP) make different design trade-offs. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Motivations for NoSQL (7 marks)** The limitations of RDBMS in the era of **Big Data, Cloud, and Web 2.0** were: 1. **Impedance Mismatch:** Object-relational mapping was painful for complex object graphs. 2. **Vertical Scaling Ceiling:** Bigger hardware is exponentially more expensive and has physical limits. 3. **Schema Rigidity:** Modifying schema in a multi-terabyte table is slow and disruptive. 4. **Poor Performance at Scale:** JOIN-heavy queries degrade as data grows. 5. **Single Points of Failure:** Master-slave setups lacked automatic failover. 6. **Unstructured Data Explosion:** Social media posts, sensor streams, and logs don't fit tabular models. **Differences from RDBMS (Summary):** | Aspect | RDBMS | NoSQL | |---|---|---| | Schema | Fixed, predefined | Dynamic, flexible | | Scaling | Vertical | Horizontal | | Data Model | Tabular (relations) | Key-Value / Document / Column / Graph | | Transactions | ACID | BASE (eventual) | | Query Language | SQL | API / custom query | | Data Volume | GBs–TBs | TBs–PBs | | Cost | High-end servers | Commodity hardware | *(3 marks motivations + 2 marks comparison table + 2 marks examples)* **Part (b) — CAP Theorem in Detail (7 marks)** **Statement:** A distributed data store can deliver only **two of three** properties: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. **Trade-off Zones:** - **CA (Consistency + Availability):** Possible only in non-distributed setups. Example: PostgreSQL, MySQL (single-node). - **CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance):** Sacrifices availability during partitions. Example: **MongoDB**, HBase, Redis. - **AP (Availability + Partition tolerance):** Sacrifices strong consistency → eventual consistency. Example: **Cassandra**, DynamoDB. **MongoDB (CP) Trade-off:** - Uses a *primary* node for writes; all reads/writes go through it. - During a partition, secondary nodes cannot accept writes → *availability* is reduced. - Guarantees that any successful read returns the most recent committed write. **Cassandra (AP) Trade-off:** - Uses *peer-to-peer* architecture; any node can accept reads/writes. - During a partition, all nodes remain available. - Replicas may diverge temporarily; conflict resolution uses *last-write-wins* or *vector clocks* to eventually converge. *(2 marks CAP statement + 2 marks zones + 3 marks MongoDB/Cassandra comparison)* --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing "NoSQL" with "No SQL":** Many students write "NoSQL means *no SQL at all*." This is WRONG. The term officially stands for **"Not Only SQL"** — many NoSQL databases support SQL-like query layers (e.g., Cassandra CQL, Cosmos DB SQL API). Loses up to 1 mark per occurrence. > > 2. **CAP Mis-statement:** Writing "CAP says choose 2 of 3" without explaining that **P is non-negotiable in distributed systems** and the real choice is between **CP and AP**. Loses 1–2 marks. > > 3. **NoSQL ≠ Always Better:** Students often claim NoSQL is universally superior. The correct KTU-expected answer is **polyglot persistence** — use the right tool for the right job. Loses up to 2 marks. > > 4. **Missing Schema-on-Read vs Schema-on-Write distinction:** Always clarify this when comparing flexibility. Loses 1 mark if omitted. > > 5. **Diagram Skipped:** KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards marks for neat, labeled diagrams. A 14-mark answer without a CAP triangle or classification diagram typically loses 1–2 marks. > > 6. **Examples not given:** Always cite at least one real database (MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Neo4j) per category. Generic answers without examples lose marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✦ > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — NoSQL Introduction)** - ☐ **NoSQL = "Not Only SQL"** — *not* "No SQL." Coined by Strozzi (1998), re-popularized by Oskarsson (2009). - ☐ **Motivation:** Big Data, Web 2.0, schema flexibility, horizontal scaling, distributed systems. - ☐ **Twelve Properties** of NoSQL (Sadalage & Fowler): horizontal scaling, replication, simple model, flexible schema, eventual consistency, etc. - ☐ **ACID Properties:** Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — RDBMS gold standard. - ☐ **BASE Properties:** Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency — NoSQL philosophy. - ☐ **CAP Theorem (Brewer, 2000):** C, A, P — only two can coexist. P is mandatory → real choice is **CP vs AP**. - ☐ **Four NoSQL Categories:** Key-Value (Redis), Document (MongoDB), Column-Family (Cassandra), Graph (Neo4j). - ☐ **Schema-on-Write** (RDBMS) vs **Schema-on-Read** (NoSQL) — a critical distinction. - ☐ **Vertical Scaling** (scale up: bigger machine) vs **Horizontal Scaling** (scale out: more machines). - ☐ **Quorum Rule:** $R + W > RF$ guarantees strong consistency in replicated systems. - ☐ **Polyglot Persistence:** Use multiple database types in the same application — the modern best practice. - ☐ **NoSQL is a *complement* to SQL, not a *replacement*.** - ☐ **Examples to memorize:** MongoDB (Document/CP), Cassandra (Column-Family/AP), Redis (Key-Value), Neo4j (Graph), HBase (Column-Family/CP), DynamoDB (Key-Value/AP). - ☐ **Distributed System Components:** Sharding + Replication + Consistency Management. - ☐ **MapReduce** is the typical distributed processing paradigm in NoSQL ecosystems.

topics.title: Comparison of NoSQL and Relational Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Comparison of NoSQL and Relational Databases # Module 3: Comparison of NoSQL and Relational Databases ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) A **Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)** is a database system based on the **relational model** proposed by **Edgar F. Codd (1970)**, in which data is organized into **tables (relations)** consisting of **rows (tuples)** and **columns (attributes)**. Relationships between tables are expressed through **primary keys** and **foreign keys**, and the integrity of data is enforced through **ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)** properties. The schema is **rigid, predefined, and vertically scalable**, and queries are expressed using the standardized **Structured Query Language (SQL)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECDT795, Module 3):** The relational model is the foundation of traditional OLTP systems and uses **SQL** as its data definition, manipulation, and control language. Examples include Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, IBM Db2, and Microsoft SQL Server. ### 1.2 NoSQL Database A **NoSQL (Not Only SQL) database** is a class of non-relational data management systems designed for **distributed, horizontally scalable, schema-flexible** storage of large volumes of structured, semi-structured, or unstructured data. NoSQL systems relax the strict ACID guarantees in favour of the **BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency)** model and are optimised for high throughput, low-latency operations across commodity clusters. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** NoSQL databases store data in models other than the tabular relational model — typically **document**, **key-value**, **column-family**, or **graph** formats — and are engineered to handle the **"Three V's" of Big Data**: Volume, Velocity, and Variety. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine two different kinds of libraries: - **A Relational Database is like a meticulously organised municipal library.** Every book must conform to a strict catalogue (schema): title, author, ISBN, edition, subject code, shelf number. New categories of information cannot be added without redesigning the entire catalogue system. The librarian guarantees that the catalogue is always perfectly consistent (ACID). - **A NoSQL Database is like a modern digital archive (think Google Drive).** You can throw in PDFs, videos, emails, photos, and chat logs — each file can have completely different metadata. Files are spread across thousands of cheap servers worldwide. The system tolerates occasional inconsistency but guarantees that you'll *eventually* see the most up-to-date version of every document (BASE). This analogy maps directly onto the design philosophy: **RDBMS = structured, consistent, centralised planning**; **NoSQL = flexible, distributed, pragmatic scaling**. ### 1.4 The Origin of the Comparison > [!NOTE] > **Historical Context:** The term "NoSQL" was first used by **Carlo Strozzi in 1998** to describe his lightweight relational database that omitted SQL. It was later popularised by **Eric Evans (Rackspace)** in **2009** to describe the rising wave of distributed non-relational stores driven by Web 2.0 giants such as Google (Bigtable), Amazon (Dynamo), and Facebook (Cassandra/HBase). ### 1.5 Core Architectural Properties (Quick Reference) | Property | RDBMS | NoSQL | |----------|-------|-------| | Data Model | Tabular (rows/columns) | Document / KV / Column / Graph | | Schema | Rigid, predefined | Dynamic, schema-on-read | | Consistency Model | **ACID (Strong)** | **BASE (Eventual)** | | Scaling | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) | | Query Language | SQL | API / JSON / DSL | | Transaction Support | Full multi-row ACID | Limited / per-document | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Foundational Trade-offs: ACID vs BASE The deepest theoretical distinction between relational and NoSQL systems lies in how they treat **consistency, availability, and partition tolerance**. #### 2.1.1 ACID Properties (Relational) ACID is the cornerstone of the relational model: - **Atomicity:** A transaction is an *all-or-nothing* unit. Either every operation commits, or none do. - **Consistency:** Every committed transaction transitions the database from one *valid state* to another, preserving all declared constraints (keys, types, triggers). - **Isolation:** Concurrent transactions execute as if they were sequential. Phenomena such as dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantoms are prevented (SQL standard: `READ UNCOMMITTED → SERIALIZABLE`). - **Durability:** Once a transaction commits, its effects survive power failures, crashes, and disasters (typically via Write-Ahead Logging — WAL). #### 2.1.2 BASE Properties (NoSQL) BASE is the *pragmatic* alternative adopted by distributed NoSQL systems: - **Basically Available:** The system guarantees availability — every request receives a response (which may be a stale or partial one). - **Soft State:** The state of the system may change over time, even without input, due to eventual-consistency propagation. - **Eventual Consistency:** Given enough time and no further writes, all replicas will converge to the same value. ### 2.2 The CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem — 2000) The CAP theorem is **the single most important formula** for understanding distributed databases and is a guaranteed KTU high-yield topic. $$ \text{In any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee at most TWO of the following THREE:} $$ $$ C \quad \text{(Consistency)} \quad + \quad A \quad \text{(Availability)} \quad + \quad P \quad \text{(Partition Tolerance)} \quad \leq \quad 2 $$ | Combination | System Type | Example | |-------------|-------------|---------| | **CA** | Traditional RDBMS (single-node) | PostgreSQL, MySQL | | **CP** | Consistency + Partition tolerance | HBase, MongoDB (default), Redis | | **AP** | Availability + Partition tolerance | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why P is mandatory:** In any real distributed system over a network, *P (Partition Tolerance) is non-negotiable* because network failures are inevitable. Therefore, the practical choice in NoSQL is between **CP (favouring consistency)** and **AP (favouring availability)**. ### 2.3 Scaling Models $$ \text{Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up)} \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{RDBMS: } N_{\text{req}} \propto C_{\text{single-server}} $$ $$ \text{Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out)} \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{NoSQL: } N_{\text{req}} \propto k \cdot N_{\text{commodity-nodes}} $$ - **Vertical Scaling:** Increase CPU, RAM, or SSD on one server. Bounded by hardware cost and physical limits. - **Horizontal Scaling:** Add more cheap commodity nodes. Bounded by the consistency model and network topology. ### 2.4 Schema Flexibility $$ \text{RDBMS: Schema-on-Write} \;\;\;\; \text{vs.} \;\;\;\; \text{NoSQL: Schema-on-Read} $$ - **Schema-on-Write:** Structure is enforced *before* data is written (DDL at design time). Read performance is high, write-time validation cost is high. - **Schema-on-Read:** Structure is interpreted *when data is queried*. Inserts are fast and flexible; validation is deferred. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Property | RDBMS Value | NoSQL Value | |---|---------|--------------------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | CAP Theorem | $C + A + P \leq 2$ | CA (single-node) | CP or AP | | 2 | Consistency Model | ACID vs BASE | **ACID** | **BASE** | | 3 | Scaling Law | $\text{Scale-Up}$ vs $\text{Scale-Out}$ | Vertical | Horizontal | | 4 | Schema Mode | Write vs Read | Schema-on-Write | Schema-on-Read | | 5 | Indexing | B+ Tree, Hash | B+ Tree primary | B-Tree, LSM, SSTable | | 6 | Transaction Scope | Single vs Distributed | Multi-statement ACID | Single-document / per-partition | | 7 | Query Latency Target | Milliseconds (small data) | $\approx 1{-}10$ ms | $\approx 1{-}5$ ms | | 8 | Join Support | Full SQL JOINs | Yes ($\Theta$, $\bowtie$, $\sigma$) | Limited / Denormalised | | 9 | Partitioning Method | Sharding / Replication | Vertical sharding | Horizontal sharding (hash/range) | | 10 | Replication | Synchronous / Asynchronous | Synchronous (master-slave) | Asynchronous / quorum | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Preferred Choice | Reason | |--------|------------------|--------| | Banking, ERP, OLTP | **RDBMS** | ACID guarantees, complex joins, regulatory compliance | | Social Media Feeds | **NoSQL (Document/CF)** | Schema-less, high write throughput | | IoT / Sensor Streams | **NoSQL (Time-Series/Column)** | Massive volume, append-only writes | | Fraud Detection Graphs | **NoSQL (Graph — Neo4j)** | Relationship traversal at depth $\geq 3$ | | E-commerce Catalog | **Hybrid** | Product catalog in document store; orders in RDBMS | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Nine Dimensions Because this topic is fundamentally a *comparison*, the most KTU-board-friendly way to "derive" understanding is to walk through **nine formal dimensions** and show *why* each RDBMS property differs from the corresponding NoSQL property. --- #### Dimension 1: Data Model **RDBMS Data Model:** $$ D_{\text{RDBMS}} = \{(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n)\} \quad \text{where} \quad R_i = (A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_m) $$ Each relation $R_i$ is a Cartesian-product subset over its attributes. Data is normalised to eliminate redundancy via **Normal Forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF, 4NF, 5NF)**. **NoSQL Data Model:** $$ D_{\text{NoSQL}} = \{(K_1, V_1), (K_2, V_2), \ldots\} \quad \text{with} \quad V_i \in \{\text{Document}, \text{Key}, \text{Column-family}, \text{Graph-node}\} $$ Data is typically **denormalised** — duplicates are tolerated to favour read performance. **Conversion Logic:** Relational = set theory + first-order predicate logic. NoSQL = chosen per use-case (document ≈ JSON object; key-value ≈ HashMap; column-family ≈ sparse multi-dimensional map). --- #### Dimension 2: Schema Definition **Step 1** — In RDBMS, the schema $S$ is declared *before* any insert: $$ S_{\text{RDBMS}} \xrightarrow{\text{DDL}} \text{Database} $$ **Step 2** — In NoSQL, no DDL is required: $$ S_{\text{NoSQL}} = \emptyset \quad \text{at write time} \quad ; \quad S_{\text{NoSQL}} \xrightarrow{\text{Application Code}} \text{Database} $$ **Step 3** — Hence, a Document NoSQL collection can contain heterogeneous records: $$ \text{Collection}_C = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_k\} \quad \text{where} \quad d_i.\text{fields} \neq d_j.\text{fields} $$ --- #### Dimension 3: Transaction Guarantees (ACID vs BASE) **Step 1** — Define a transaction $T = \{o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_n\}$ where each $o_i$ is a read or write operation. **Step 2** — ACID demands that $\forall T \in \mathcal{T}, \text{commit}(T) \implies \text{all } o_i \text{ applied atomically}$. **Step 3** — In NoSQL (especially AP systems), the *NWR* quorum model governs writes: $$ W + R > N \quad \text{guarantees strong consistency for a write set of } N \text{ replicas} $$ where: - $N$ = total number of replicas - $W$ = number of nodes that must acknowledge a write - $R$ = number of nodes contacted for a read **Step 4** — If $W + R \leq N$, the system is eventually consistent (BASE). **Conversion Logic:** ACID is a *contract*. BASE is a *pledge*. RDBMS users pay latency for the contract; NoSQL users pay staleness for the pledge. --- #### Dimension 4: Scalability Architecture **Step 1** — Define aggregate throughput $T_{\text{agg}}$ and per-server throughput $T_{\text{node}}$. **Step 2** — Vertical scaling relation: $$ T_{\text{agg}}^{\text{RDBMS}} = T_{\text{node}} \cdot f(C_{\text{CPU}}, C_{\text{RAM}}) $$ This is **bounded** — you cannot scale a single server indefinitely. **Step 3** — Horizontal scaling relation: $$ T_{\text{agg}}^{\text{NoSQL}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{node}_i} \approx n \cdot T_{\text{node}} \quad \text{(linear, in theory)} $$ **Step 4** — In practice, the speedup factor $S(n)$ follows **Amdahl's Law**: $$ S(n) = \frac{1}{\left(1 - p\right) + \dfrac{p}{n}} $$ where $p$ is the parallelisable fraction. For distributed NoSQL, $p \approx 0.9$ to $0.95$, allowing near-linear scale-out. **Conversion Logic:** RDBMS = single high-spec server; NoSQL = many commodity servers coordinated by gossip protocols and consistent hashing. --- #### Dimension 5: Query Language **RDBMS — SQL (Declarative, Set-Oriented):** $$ \pi_{A_1, A_2}(\sigma_{\theta}(R_1 \bowtie R_2 \bowtie R_3)) $$ is expressed as: ```sql SELECT A1, A2 FROM R1 JOIN R2 ON ... JOIN R3 ON ... WHERE theta; ``` **NoSQL — Procedural / API-Driven (example: MongoDB):** ```javascript db.orders.aggregate([ { $match: { status: "paid" } }, { $group: { _id: "$customerId", total: { $sum: "$amount" } } } ]); ``` **Conversion Logic:** SQL is *declarative* — you say *what* you want. NoSQL queries are *imperative / programmatic* — you say *how* to retrieve it. This shifts optimisation responsibility from the query optimiser to the application developer. --- #### Dimension 6: Indexing Strategy **Step 1** — RDBMS typically uses **B+ Trees** for range-friendly access: $$ T_{\text{search}}^{\text{B+}} = O(\log_{m} N) \quad \text{where } m = \text{order of tree} $$ **Step 2** — NoSQL systems use either: - **B-Trees** (MongoDB WiredTiger) for general use - **Log-Structured Merge Trees (LSM)** for write-heavy workloads (Cassandra, LevelDB, RocksDB) - **Hash Indexes** for exact key lookups (Redis, DynamoDB) **Step 3** — LSM write amplification: $$ \text{WA}_{\text{LSM}} = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} \frac{W_i}{W_{\text{user}}} \approx L \cdot \text{amplification factor} $$ where $L$ = number of levels. LSM trades write amplification for read amplification (and vice-versa for B+ Trees). **Conversion Logic:** RDBMS indexes are an integrated subsystem. NoSQL indexing is application- or engine-specific and optimised for the dominant access pattern. --- #### Dimension 7: Consistency under Network Partition **Step 1** — Suppose a network partition splits $N$ nodes into $\{N_1, N_2\}$. **Step 2** — A CP system halts writes to the minority partition: $$ \text{If } \mid N_2 \mid < \text{minority threshold, then writes on } N_2 \text{ are rejected.} $$ **Step 3** — An AP system accepts writes on both partitions and reconciles via vector clocks: $$ VC_{\text{node}} = \{(\text{node}_i, \text{counter}_i)\} $$ **Step 4** — On partition healing, conflict resolution uses either: - Last-Write-Wins (LWW) by timestamp - Application-defined merge (CRDTs — Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) **Conversion Logic:** RDBMS prefers correctness over availability; AP-NoSQL prefers availability over correctness. --- #### Dimension 8: Data Distribution Strategy **Step 1** — RDBMS Sharding: $$ \text{row}_i \rightarrow \text{shard}\big( h(\text{shard-key}_i) \big) $$ Manual or middleware-based (e.g., Vitess for MySQL). **Step 2** — NoSQL Consistent Hashing: $$ \text{node}_j \rightarrow \text{position on hash ring of size } 2^{32} $$ $$ \text{key}_k \rightarrow \text{next clockwise node} $$ **Step 3** — Adding/removing a node only affects $\approx 1/n$ of keys (smooth rebalancing). **Conversion Logic:** RDBMS sharding is complex and breaks SQL semantics. NoSQL consistent hashing is native and built into the architecture. --- #### Dimension 9: Failure Recovery | Property | RDBMS | NoSQL | |----------|-------|-------| | Failure Unit | Single server (mostly) | Per-replica, multi-node | | Recovery Mechanism | WAL + ARIES protocol | Hinted handoff, read repair, anti-entropy | | Recovery Time | Minutes (cold restart) | Seconds (per-node) | | Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) | High | Low | | Availability Target | 99.9% ("three nines") | 99.99% ("four nines") | **Conversion Logic:** RDBMS sacrifices uptime for absolute correctness. NoSQL sacrifices correctness for continuous uptime. ### 3.2 Final Comparative Tabulation | Sl No. | Parameter | RDBMS | NoSQL | |--------|-----------|-------|-------| | 1 | Data Model | Tables (relations) | Document / KV / Column / Graph | | 2 | Schema | Static, predefined | Dynamic, schema-less | | 3 | Normalisation | Highly normalised (3NF/BCNF) | Denormalised | | 4 | Consistency | Strong (ACID) | Eventual (BASE) | | 5 | Transaction | Full multi-statement ACID | Single-document or limited | | 6 | Query Language | SQL | JSON / API / DSL | | 7 | Joins | Native support | No / very limited | | 8 | Scaling | Vertical (up) | Horizontal (out) | | 9 | CAP Preference | CA / CP | CP or AP | | 10 | Indexing | B+ Tree | B-Tree / LSM / Hash | | 11 | Replication | Master-Slave (sync) | Multi-master (async/quorum) | | 12 | Hardware Cost | High-end | Commodity | | 13 | Use Case | OLTP, ERP, Banking | Big Data, IoT, Social, Real-time | | 14 | Examples | Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL | MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Neo4j | | 15 | Maturity | 40+ years | 10-15 years | ## 4. Explain how it applies to NoSQL databases.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **CAP theorem**, formulated by **Eric Brewer (2000)**, states that any distributed data store can simultaneously deliver **at most two** of the following three guarantees: 1. **Consistency (C):** Every read sees the most recent committed write. 2. **Availability (A):** Every request receives a non-error response. 3. **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes. **Application to NoSQL:** Since partitions are inevitable in any real distributed system, partition tolerance is mandatory. Hence NoSQL systems must choose between **CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance)** — examples: MongoDB, HBase, Redis — and **AP (Availability + Partition tolerance)** — examples: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB. The choice is dictated by whether the business prioritises strong correctness (banking) or uninterrupted service (social media feeds). *(Examiner allocates: 1 mark definition, 1 mark explanation of the three properties, 1 mark application to NoSQL.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between ACID and BASE properties in the context of database systems.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | ACID (Used by RDBMS) | BASE (Used by NoSQL) | |----------|----------------------|-----------------------| | Atomicity | Full transaction atomicity | Single-document atomicity | | Consistency | Strong, immediate consistency | Eventual consistency | | Isolation | Serializable isolation | Weak / no isolation | | Durability | Guaranteed via WAL | Guaranteed but with replication lag | | Philosophy | Pessimistic — correctness first | Optimistic — availability first | **ACID** is a *contractual guarantee*: every transaction either completes entirely or has no effect at all, and the database always moves between valid states. **BASE** is a *pledge of eventual convergence*: the system is basically available, may have soft state, and will become consistent over time without further writes. *(Examiner allocates: 1 mark ACID explanation, 1 mark BASE explanation, 1 mark distinction.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern] **Q. (a)** Compare and contrast **Relational** and **NoSQL** databases across **at least eight** parameters, with examples for each category. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A relational database is a **schema-driven, ACID-compliant, table-oriented** system that excels at **structured data and complex joins**, whereas a NoSQL database is a **schema-flexible, BASE-oriented, distributed** system built for **scale-out, semi-structured data, and high throughput**. A detailed comparison is tabulated below: | # | Parameter | Relational (RDBMS) | NoSQL | Example (RDBMS) | Example (NoSQL) | |---|-----------|--------------------|-------|-----------------|-----------------| | 1 | Data Model | Tables (relations) | Document / KV / Column / Graph | MySQL `Customers` table | MongoDB `customers` collection | | 2 | Schema | Fixed, predefined | Dynamic, schema-less | `CREATE TABLE` DDL | Schema-on-read | | 3 | Normalisation | 3NF / BCNF | Denormalised | Joined tables | Embedded documents | | 4 | Consistency | Strong ACID | Eventual BASE | PostgreSQL ACID | Cassandra eventual | | 5 | Scalability | Vertical | Horizontal | Bigger server | More nodes | | 6 | Query Language | SQL | API / DSL | `SELECT * FROM ...` | `db.collection.find()` | | 7 | Joins | Native, optimised | None / application-level | Multi-table joins | Manual embedding | | 8 | Transactions | Multi-statement ACID | Single-document only | Bank transfers | Atomic counter increment | | 9 | Hardware | High-end single server | Commodity cluster | Oracle Exadata | Cassandra on cheap VMs | | 10 | Best Use | OLTP, ERP, Banking | Big Data, IoT, Real-time | Payroll system | Twitter timeline | **Conclusion:** NoSQL is *not* a replacement for RDBMS but a **complementary technology**. Modern applications frequently adopt **polyglot persistence**, choosing the right store for each workload. --- **Q. (b)** Discuss the **CAP theorem** in detail. Explain how **CP** and **AP** NoSQL systems differ, with one example each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** The **CAP theorem** (Brewer's conjecture, proved by Gilbert & Lynch in 2002) states that any distributed shared-data system can simultaneously provide **at most two** of the following three properties: $$ C \;(\text{Consistency}) + A \;(\text{Availability}) + P \;(\text{Partition Tolerance}) \leq 2 $$ **Detailed Explanation:** - **Consistency (C):** All clients see the same data at the same time, regardless of which node they connect to. A read must return the most recent committed write. - **Availability (A):** Every request (read or write) from a non-failing node receives a response, even if the network is partitioned. - **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate in the presence of arbitrary message loss (network failures) between nodes. **Why P is mandatory in distributed systems:** Real networks *always* experience partitions. Hence, designers must choose between sacrificing C (become AP) or sacrificing A (become CP). A single-node relational database effectively chooses **CA** since there are no partitions. **CP Systems (Consistency + Partition Tolerance):** CP systems choose to *suspend availability* on the minority side of a partition rather than serve stale data. They guarantee that once a write is acknowledged, all subsequent reads will see it. - **Example: MongoDB** — In its default configuration with replica sets, if the primary fails, writes halt until a new primary is elected, ensuring no stale reads. - **Example: HBase / Redis Cluster** — Strong consistency via ZooKeeper-coordinated leader election. **AP Systems (Availability + Partition Tolerance):** AP systems choose to *remain available* on both sides of a partition, accepting temporary inconsistency. They use mechanisms such as *vector clocks*, *last-write-wins*, *read repair*, and *anti-entropy* to converge eventually. - **Example: Cassandra** — Configurable consistency per query (`ONE`, `QUORUM`, `ALL`); defaults to `ONE` for high availability. - **Example: Amazon DynamoDB** — Designed for "always-writable" shopping carts; conflicts resolved via vector clocks or timestamps. **Practical Implication:** The CAP choice is **not permanent** — modern systems (e.g., Cosmos DB, Cassandra with quorum reads) allow *tunable consistency* so the developer can balance C, A, and latency per query. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern] **Q. (a)** Explain the **ACID** properties with examples. How do **NoSQL** systems relax these properties through **BASE**? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **ACID Properties — Detailed Explanation:** 1. **Atomicity:** A transaction is an indivisible unit. If any operation fails, the entire transaction is rolled back. *Example:* In a banking transfer, debiting Account A and crediting Account B must both succeed or both fail — no partial debit should occur. 2. **Consistency:** A transaction transforms the database from one *valid state* to another, preserving all integrity constraints (primary key, foreign key, check constraints, triggers). *Example:* A foreign key constraint on `Orders.CustomerID` ensures that no order exists without a valid customer. 3. **Isolation:** Concurrent transactions must not interfere with each other. The result of concurrent execution must equal the result of some serial execution (**serialisability**). *Example:* Two simultaneous bookings of the same airline seat must not both succeed — locks or MVCC enforce this. 4. **Durability:** Once a transaction commits, its effects are *permanent*, surviving crashes and power failures. Achieved via **Write-Ahead Logs (WAL)** and **commit records** stored on stable storage. *Example:* After a confirmed reservation, even an immediate server crash will not lose the booking. **BASE Properties — Relaxation in NoSQL:** NoSQL systems trade strict ACID guarantees for **scalability and availability**, offering BASE: - **Basically Available:** The system guarantees a response to every query, even if the data is stale. *Example:* A product page may still load during a database partition, showing slightly outdated inventory. - **Soft State:** The system's state may change over time even without new writes, due to background replication and anti-entropy processes. *Example:* Two replicas may temporarily disagree on a counter value. - **Eventual Consistency:** Given a sufficient quiescent period with no further writes, all replicas converge to the same value. *Example:* A DynamoDB read after a write may initially return the old value but will return the new value after the replication completes (typically within milliseconds). **Relaxation Mapping:** | ACID Property | BASE Counterpart | What is Sacrificed | |---------------|------------------|--------------------| | Atomicity | Best-effort per-document | Cross-document atomicity | | Consistency | Eventual | Strong read-after-write | | Isolation | Last-write-wins / CRDTs | True serialisability | | Durability | Replicated, durable but with lag | Strict synchronous durability | **Use Case Justification:** For an e-commerce cart or social-media post, occasional staleness is tolerable; for an ATM withdrawal, atomicity is non-negotiable. --- **Q. (b)** Discuss the **four major types of NoSQL databases** with **examples and use cases**. How does each type internally store data differently from relational tables? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Type 1 — Key-Value Stores:** - **Internal Storage:** A hash map of opaque binary keys to opaque binary values. The value is treated as a *black box*; the database has no knowledge of its internal structure. - **Examples:** Redis, Amazon DynamoDB, Riak, Memcached. - **Use Cases:** Caching, session management, leaderboards, shopping carts, real-time analytics. - **Storage Model:** $K \rightarrow V$ where $V$ is an uninterpreted byte string. **Type 2 — Document Stores:** - **Internal Storage:** Semi-structured documents (typically JSON, BSON, or XML) with nested fields and arrays. Each document can have a different structure. - **Examples:** MongoDB, CouchDB, Couchbase, Amazon DocumentDB. - **Use Cases:** Content management systems, product catalogues, user profiles, blog posts. - **Storage Model:** A collection holds heterogeneous JSON documents; queryable by field values via secondary indexes. **Type 3 — Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores:** - **Internal Storage:** Sparse, multi-dimensional sorted maps organised into *column families* rather than rows. Designed for analytical workloads on massive datasets. - **Examples:** Apache Cassandra, Apache HBase, Google Bigtable, ScyllaDB. - **Use Cases:** Time-series data, IoT sensor logs, recommendation engines, write-heavy OLAP. - **Storage Model:** $\text{RowKey} \rightarrow \{\text{ColumnFamily} \rightarrow \{\text{Column\_Qualifier} : \text{Value}, \text{Timestamp}\}\}$. Hundreds or thousands of columns per row are common. **Type 4 — Graph Databases:** - **Internal Storage:** Native graph structures of **nodes (vertices)**, **edges (relationships)**, and **properties** on both. Relationships are *first-class citizens* with direction and type. - **Examples:** Neo4j, OrientDB, JanusGraph, Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB. - **Use Cases:** Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, recommendation engines, network/IT operations. - **Storage Model:** $\text{Graph} = (V, E)$ where $V$ = nodes, $E$ = edges. Traversals use algorithms such as BFS/DFS and pattern matching (e.g., Cypher in Neo4j). **Comparison with Relational Tables:** | NoSQL Type | Closest Relational Analogy | Key Difference | |------------|---------------------------|----------------| | Key-Value | Two-column table `(K, V)` | No secondary indexes, no SQL joins | | Document | Single-table with `JSON` column | Each row can have different columns | | Column-Family | Sparse matrix with NULLs | Columns grouped by family, sorted by timestamp | | Graph | Two tables (Vertices, Edges) with JOINs | Edges stored as physical pointers, $O(1)$ traversal | **Conclusion:** NoSQL is *not* one technology but a **family of four distinct paradigms**, each optimised for a specific access pattern. The choice depends on the dominant query type of the application. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT write "NoSQL means No SQL."** The correct expansion is "**Not Only SQL**." Examiners *will* deduct marks for the wrong expansion. > 2. **CAP is not "pick any 2 of 3."** In a distributed system, partition tolerance is **mandatory**. You must explain that the *real* choice is between **CP and AP**. > 3. **Do not confuse BASE with "no consistency."** Eventual consistency is *still* consistency — it just has a *time bound*. > 4. **Always cite one example per NoSQL sub-type.** A generic answer without MongoDB / Cassandra / Redis / Neo4j loses at least 1 mark. > 5. **Mention Polyglot Persistence** in the conclusion of Part B answers for an easy 1-mark bonus in valuation. > 6. **Avoid one-sided criticism.** Never claim NoSQL is "better" or RDBMS is "obsolete." Examiners reward *balanced* arguments. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - [ ] **RDBMS** = Tabular, ACID, SQL, schema-on-write, vertical scaling, normalised, joins supported. - [ ] **NoSQL** = Non-tabular, BASE, API/DSL, schema-on-read, horizontal scaling, denormalised, no joins. - [ ] **CAP theorem:** $C + A + P \leq 2$; in distributed systems, choose **CP** (MongoDB, HBase, Redis) or **AP** (Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB). - [ ] **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — guarantees correctness; used by RDBMS. - [ ] **BASE** = Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency — guarantees availability; used by NoSQL. - [ ] **Four NoSQL Types:** Key-Value (Redis), Document (MongoDB), Column-Family (Cassandra), Graph (Neo4j). - [ ] **Sharding:** RDBMS uses complex middleware (Vitess); NoSQL uses **consistent hashing** natively. - [ ] **Scaling:** Vertical (scale-up, RDBMS) vs Horizontal (scale-out, NoSQL); horizontal governed by **Amdahl's Law**. - [ ] **Quorum:** $W + R > N \Rightarrow$ strong consistency; $W + R \leq N \Rightarrow$ eventual consistency. - [ ] **Polyglot Persistence:** Use multiple stores in one application — RDBMS for transactions, Document for catalogue, KV for cache, Graph for relationships. - [ ] **Brewer's Theorem proven** by Gilbert & Lynch (2002); originally conjectured in 2000. - [ ] **First use of "NoSQL"** term: Carlo Strozzi (1998); popularised by Eric Evans (2009). - [ ] **Codd's Relational Model** (1970) is the foundation of RDBMS. - [ ] **LSM Trees** used by Cassandra, LevelDB, RocksDB; **B+ Trees** used by MySQL InnoDB. - [ ] **When to use RDBMS:** Banking, ERP, OLTP, complex joins, regulatory compliance. - [ ] **When to use NoSQL:** Big Data, IoT, real-time analytics, social media, schema-flexible workloads. - [ ] **Schema-on-Write vs Schema-on-Read** is the philosophical divide between RDBMS and NoSQL. --- > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 3 — Comparison of NoSQL and Relational Databases** > *Total recommended KTU study time: 6–8 hours | Expected marks weightage: 10–14 marks per University Exam paper.*

topics.title: Database Sharding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Database Sharding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Database Sharding** is a *horizontal partitioning* technique in distributed database systems where a single logical dataset (a table or collection) is split across multiple physically independent database nodes (called **shards**), with each shard holding a unique subset of rows that share a common schema. Unlike *vertical partitioning* (which splits columns), sharding splits **rows** along a designated **shard key** (also called a *partition key*). Every shard is a fully functional, autonomous database — it can execute queries, accept writes, and serve reads without depending on the other shards for its local slice of data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation)**
> *Sharding* is the practice of splitting a large logical database into smaller physical databases (shards) so that each shard can be queried independently, thereby distributing both the storage load and the computational workload across N independent database servers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Branch System

Imagine a single, gigantic library containing **10 million books**. Finding any book would take forever, shelves would collapse, and only a few librarians could manage it. The solution? Open **8 branch libraries** across the city.

- The **catalogue number** (the first letter of the book's title) is the **shard key**.
- Books whose titles start with **A–C** go to **Branch 1**.
- Titles **D–F** go to **Branch 2**, and so on.
- A central **"Library Routing Desk"** (the *Shard Router* / *Query Router*) decides which branch a patron must visit.
- Each branch is a **complete, autonomous library** — it has its own librarian, shelves, and reading rooms.

This is *exactly* how sharding works. Each **shard** is its own database, and the **shard router** is the intelligent dispatcher that locates the correct shard for every query.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Callout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Vocabulary You MUST Memorize for KTU Exams**
>
> | Term | Meaning |
> |---|---|
> | **Shard** | An independent database instance holding a subset of rows |
> | **Shard Key** | The column(s) used to decide which shard a row belongs to |
> | **Shard Router / Query Router** | Middleware that parses the query and routes it to the correct shard |
> | **Logical Shard** | The conceptual mapping of how data SHOULD be distributed |
> | **Physical Shard** | The actual database server where a logical shard resides |
> | **Rebalancing** | Migrating data between shards when load distribution becomes uneven |
> | **Hot Spot** | A shard receiving disproportionate traffic due to poor shard-key choice |

### 1.4 Position in the Distributed-DB Toolbox

Sharding is one of **four** classical scalability strategies; the others being *replication*, *vertical partitioning*, and *denormalization*. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **distinguish** sharding from replication — keep this distinction crisp in your memory.

| Technique | Splits By | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Sharding** (Horizontal) | **Rows** | Distribute **write** load and storage |
| **Replication** | Copies whole DB | Distribute **read** load, provide fault tolerance |
| **Vertical Partitioning** | **Columns** | Separate hot vs cold columns |
| **Federation** | By domain/feature | Functional decomposition |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Sharding Architecture

Every sharded system is built from three logical components. Examiners frequently award marks just for naming these layers correctly.

1. **Shard Layer (Data Nodes):** The N physical databases that store the row subsets.
2. **Routing Layer (Middleware):** A stateless proxy service that intercepts every SQL/NoSQL query, extracts the shard key, computes the target shard ID, and forwards the query.
3. **Configuration / Metadata Store:** A small, highly-available service (often backed by **ZooKeeper**, **etcd**, or **Consul**) that tracks the mapping `shard_key_range → physical_server_address`. Without this, the router cannot locate shards.

### 2.2 Sharding Strategies (The Heart of the Module)

#### Strategy A — Range-Based Sharding
- Rows are partitioned by **contiguous ranges** of the shard key.
- Example: `UserID 1–1,000,000` → Shard 1, `1,000,001–2,000,000` → Shard 2.
- **Advantage:** Range queries (`WHERE id BETWEEN 100 AND 500`) are extremely fast — only one shard is touched.
- **Disadvantage:** Severe risk of **hot spots** if recent IDs are accessed more (e.g., e-commerce where recent orders dominate reads).

#### Strategy B — Hash-Based Sharding
- A **deterministic hash function** is applied to the shard key: `shard_id = H(key) mod N`.
- **Advantage:** Even distribution of data; eliminates hot spots.
- **Disadvantage:** Range queries now require **scatter-gather** across all N shards — slow.

#### Strategy C — Directory-Based Sharding (Lookup Table)
- A separate lookup table maps each shard-key value (or range) to a specific shard.
- **Advantage:** Maximum flexibility — any key can point to any shard; ideal for uneven distributions like multi-tenant SaaS.
- **Disadvantage:** The directory itself becomes a single point of failure and a performance bottleneck.

#### Strategy D — Geo-Sharding (Composite)
- Rows are routed to shards based on the user's **geographic region**.
- Used by global platforms (e.g., Netflix, Facebook) to satisfy data-residency laws like GDPR.

### 2.3 The Shard-Key Selection Criterion — A KTU Favourite

The choice of the shard key is the single most consequential design decision. A *bad* key destroys the system; a *good* key keeps it scalable forever. The examiner expects you to articulate these four properties:

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **High Cardinality** | The key must have many distinct values (avoid boolean flags) |
| **Even Distribution** | Values must not cluster (avoid timestamps used as PKs) |
| **Immutable** | Updating the shard key forces a cross-shard migration |
| **Frequently Queried** | Every query should ideally include the shard key in the WHERE clause |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat-Sheet Table — Memorize Before Exam**

$$
\text{shard\_id} = H(\text{shard\_key}) \bmod N
$$

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\text{sharded}} \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Throughput}_{\text{shard}_i}
$$

$$
\text{Latency}_{\text{cross-shard join}} \;\approx\; \text{Latency}_{\text{local}} \times \text{ScatterFactor}
$$

$$
\text{Capacity}_{\text{total}} \;=\; N \times \text{Capacity}_{\text{single\_node}}
$$

$$
\text{Rebalancing Cost} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{MigratedRows}_i \times \text{PerRowCost}
$$

$$
\text{HotSpotRatio} \;=\; \frac{\max(\text{RPS}_i)}{\text{mean}(\text{RPS}_i)}
$$

> A healthy sharded cluster keeps the **HotSpotRatio < 2.0**.

### 2.5 Engineering Trade-off Summary

Sharding is *not free*. The moment you shard, you lose certain RDBMS guarantees. KTU wants you to know **exactly what you lose**:

- **No native cross-shard JOINs** — must be denormalized or executed in the application layer.
- **No cross-shard transactions** — replaced by *distributed transactions* (2PC / Saga), which are far slower.
- **No global referential integrity** — foreign keys cannot be enforced across shards.
- **Operational complexity explodes** — deployments, backups, and migrations must run N times.
- **Rebalancing is painful** — moving a shard requires copying millions of rows live.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

| Industry | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **E-Commerce** | Order tables sharded by `customer_id` |
| **Social Media** | Posts sharded by `user_id` |
| **Banking** | Transactions sharded by `account_number` |
| **Gaming** | Player profiles sharded by `region_code` |
| **IoT** | Telemetry sharded by `device_serial` |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Hash-Based Shard Placement

**Problem Statement:**
A distributed system uses **hash-based sharding** with the simple hash function `H(key) = key mod 1000`. The cluster has **N = 4** shards. A user with `user_id = 4729` issues a write request. Determine the target shard.

**Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Identify inputs.}
$$

We are given the user key $k = 4729$, the modular base $M = 1000$, and the cluster size $N = 4$.

$$
\text{Step 2: Apply the hash function.}
$$

$$
H(k) \;=\; k \bmod M
$$

$$
H(4729) \;=\; 4729 \bmod 1000
$$

Because $4729 = 4 \times 1000 + 729$, the remainder is exactly $729$.

$$
H(4729) \;=\; 729
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Compute the shard index.}
$$

$$
\text{shard\_id} \;=\; H(k) \bmod N
$$

$$
\text{shard\_id} \;=\; 729 \bmod 4
$$

Performing the division: $729 = 182 \times 4 + 1$, giving remainder $1$.

$$
\text{shard\_id} \;=\; 1
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Conclude.}
$$

$$
