\boxed{\text{The write is routed to Shard 1 (zero-indexed).}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Hot-Spot Ratio Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A sharded database of 5 shards reports the following requests-per-second (RPS): `[120, 118, 540, 122, 125]`. Compute the HotSpotRatio and comment on the health of the cluster.

**Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Identify max RPS.}
$$

$$
\text{max}(\text{RPS}) \;=\; 540 \quad \text{(occurs at Shard 3)}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Compute mean RPS.}
$$

$$
\text{mean}(\text{RPS}) \;=\; \frac{120 + 118 + 540 + 122 + 125}{5}
$$

$$
\text{mean}(\text{RPS}) \;=\; \frac{1025}{5} \;=\; 205
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Apply the HotSpotRatio formula.}
$$

$$
\text{HotSpotRatio} \;=\; \frac{\max(\text{RPS})}{\text{mean}(\text{RPS})}
$$

$$
\text{HotSpotRatio} \;=\; \frac{540}{205}
$$

$$
\text{HotSpotRatio} \;\approx\; 2.634
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Engineering verdict.}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Since } 2.634 > 2.0 \text{, the cluster is UNHEALTHY. Shard 3 is a hot spot requiring rebalancing.}}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Rebalancing Cost Estimation

**Problem Statement:**
A cluster of 8 shards must migrate rows so that each shard ends up with equal cardinality. The current row distribution (in millions) is: `[5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12]`. The per-row migration cost is **₹0.001**. Compute the total rebalancing cost.

**Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Compute total rows.}
$$

$$
\text{Total} \;=\; 5 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 9 + 10 + 11 + 12 \;=\; 68 \text{ million rows}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Compute ideal per-shard target.}
$$

$$
\text{Target} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total}}{N} \;=\; \frac{68}{8} \;=\; 8.5 \text{ million rows per shard}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Compute per-shard surplus / deficit.}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviation}_i \;=\; \text{Current}_i \;-\; \text{Target} \\
\text{Dev} \;=\; [-3.5,\; -2.5,\; -1.5,\; -0.5,\; +0.5,\; +1.5,\; +2.5,\; +3.5] \text{ million}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Compute total migrated rows (sum of positive deviations).}
$$

$$
\text{Migrated} \;=\; 0.5 + 1.5 + 2.5 + 3.5 \;=\; 8 \text{ million rows}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5: Compute total cost.}
$$

$$
\text{Cost} \;=\; \text{Migrated} \times \text{PerRowCost}
$$

$$
\text{Cost} \;=\; 8{,}000{,}000 \times 0.001
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Rebalancing Cost} \;=\; \text{₹}8{,}000}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sharding vs Replication

| Criterion | Sharding | Replication |
|---|---|---|
| Data layout | Each row on **exactly one** shard | Each row on **all** replicas |
| Primary goal | **Write** scalability & storage scaling | **Read** scalability & fault tolerance |
| Storage cost | Low (sum equals original) | High (N × original) |
| Read latency | Same as single DB | Reduced (can hit nearest replica) |
| Write latency | Reduced (parallel writes to shards) | Same or higher (must wait for all replicas) |
| Cross-node consistency | None (each shard autonomous) | Strong (Paxos / Raft possible) |
| Failure domain | Single shard loss = partial outage | Replica loss = zero data loss |
| Best for | Write-heavy OLTP at scale | Read-heavy OLTP, reporting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture Diagram — Sharded Cluster with Query Router

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client1["CLIENT APPLICATION LAYER"]
    Client2["MOBILE APP USERS"]
    Client3["WEB DASHBOARD USERS"]

    Router["SHARD ROUTER MIDDLEWARE"]
    Config["METADATA CONFIGURATION STORE"]

    S1["SHARD 1 DATABASE NODE"]
    S2["SHARD 2 DATABASE NODE"]
    S3["SHARD 3 DATABASE NODE"]
    S4["SHARD 4 DATABASE NODE"]

    Client1 --> Router
    Client2 --> Router
    Client3 --> Router
    Router <--> Config
    Router --> S1
    Router --> S2
    Router --> S3
    Router --> S4
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Hash-Based Shard Routing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Req["INCOMING QUERY: SELECT WHERE user_id = 4729"] --> Parser["QUERY PARSER"]
    Parser --> Extract["EXTRACT SHARD KEY user_id = 4729"]
    Extract --> Hash["COMPUTE HASH FUNCTION H = key mod 1000"]
    Hash --> Mod["APPLY shard_id = H mod N mod 4"]
    Mod --> Route{"DETERMINE TARGET SHARD"}

    Route --> S1["SHARD 0"]
    Route --> S2["SHARD 1"]
    Route --> S3["SHARD 2"]
    Route --> S4["SHARD 3"]

    S2 --> Resp["RETURN RESULT SET TO CLIENT"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Range-Based Shard Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Tbl["LOGICAL TABLE: Orders (40 million rows)"]

    S1["SHARD 1 : user_id 1 to 10000000"]
    S2["SHARD 2 : user_id 10000001 to 20000000"]
    S3["SHARD 3 : user_id 20000001 to 30000000"]
    S4["SHARD 4 : user_id 30000001 to 40000000"]

    Tbl --> S1
    Tbl --> S2
    Tbl --> S3
    Tbl --> S4
```

### 4.4 Rebalancing Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Monitor["STEP 1: MONITOR HOTSPOT RATIO"]
    Decide{"STEP 2: RATIO GREATER THAN 2.0?"}
    Stop["STEP 3: NO ACTION REQUIRED"]
    Plan["STEP 4: PLAN NEW SHARD MAP"]
    Migrate["STEP 5: COPY ROWS LIVE"]
    Switch["STEP 6: UPDATE ROUTER METADATA"]
    Verify["STEP 7: VERIFY EVEN DISTRIBUTION"]

    Monitor --> Decide
    Decide -->|NO| Stop
    Decide -->|YES| Plan
    Plan --> Migrate
    Migrate --> Switch
    Switch --> Verify
    Verify --> Monitor
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define database sharding. Explain how it differs from replication.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Database sharding is a horizontal partitioning technique in which rows of a logical table are distributed across multiple independent database nodes (shards), with each shard holding a unique subset of rows defined by a shard key.

The key differences from replication are:

- **Sharding** splits *rows* across N independent databases to distribute **write** load and storage. Each row exists in only one shard.
- **Replication** copies the *entire* database across N replicas to distribute **read** load and provide fault tolerance. Every row exists on all replicas.

Hence, sharding scales **writes**; replication scales **reads**.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the contrast.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**List any THREE ideal properties of a shard key.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **High Cardinality** — The key must have many distinct values so that data spreads evenly across shards (avoid booleans or low-cardinality flags).
2. **Even Distribution** — Values must not cluster in time, geography, or any other dimension; otherwise hot spots appear.
3. **Immutability** — The shard key of an existing row must never change; updates would force costly cross-shard migrations.

*(Optional 4th property for extra credit: Frequently Queried.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **three primary sharding strategies** — range-based, hash-based, and directory-based — with one real-world example of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sharded e-commerce cluster has **6 shards**. The hash function used is `H(key) = key mod 10000`. Compute the target shard for orders with `customer_id = 87432` and `customer_id = 120005`. Comment on the evenness of distribution. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Three Sharding Strategies (7 Marks)

**1. Range-Based Sharding (2 Marks)**
Rows are partitioned by contiguous intervals of the shard key. Example: A banking system shards the `Transactions` table by `account_number` ranges — accounts `1000–1999` in Shard 1, `2000–2999` in Shard 2, etc. Range queries are extremely fast because only one shard is queried, but it produces hot spots if recent ranges receive disproportionate traffic.

**2. Hash-Based Sharding (2 Marks)**
A deterministic hash function maps each key to a shard: `shard_id = H(key) mod N`. Example: Twitter shards tweets by hashing `tweet_id` — this guarantees even distribution and prevents hot spots, but range queries now require fan-out across all shards.

**3. Directory-Based Sharding (2 Marks)**
A separate lookup table stores the mapping from each shard-key value (or tenant) to its physical shard. Example: A multi-tenant SaaS platform like Salesforce keeps a `TenantMap` table mapping each `tenant_id` to its dedicated shard. Maximum flexibility, but the directory becomes a single point of failure.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Each strategy trades off query locality vs distribution evenness. Production systems often combine them — e.g., geo-sharding plus hash-based routing within each region.

---

### Part (b) — Shard Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Hash the first key. (2 Marks)**

$$
H(87432) \;=\; 87432 \bmod 10000 \;=\; 7432
$$

$$
\text{shard\_id}_1 \;=\; 7432 \bmod 6
$$

Performing the division: $7432 = 1238 \times 6 + 4$, so the remainder is $4$.

$$
\boxed{\text{shard\_id}_1 \;=\; 4}
$$

**Step 2 — Hash the second key. (2 Marks)**

$$
H(120005) \;=\; 120005 \bmod 10000 \;=\; 5
$$

$$
\text{shard\_id}_2 \;=\; 5 \bmod 6 \;=\; 5
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{shard\_id}_2 \;=\; 5}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on distribution. (3 Marks)**

The two keys fall on shards 4 and 5 respectively, which are *adjacent* in the modulus ring. While not problematic in isolation, the modulus `mod 6` has known weakness with hash functions that produce **small or biased remainders**, because common shard keys (sequential integers, timestamps) tend to cluster near each other after modulo reduction. To improve uniformity, the KTU-recommended approach is:

$$
\text{Use } N \;=\; \text{prime number of shards (e.g., 7 or 11)} \quad \text{and} \quad H(k) = \text{cryptographic hash like SHA-256}(k)
$$

This guarantees the **avalanche property** — even a 1-bit change in the input scatters the output across all bits, producing statistically uniform distribution across shards.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **challenges** introduced by sharding. How does each challenge affect a real production system? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **vertical partitioning** and **sharding** with a clear diagram-style explanation. A startup is experiencing slow queries on its single-node MySQL with 500 GB of data. Recommend whether they should sharding or vertical partitioning, with justification. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Challenges of Sharding (7 Marks)

**Challenge 1 — Cross-Shard Joins (2 Marks)**
Once sharded, traditional SQL JOINs across shards become prohibitively expensive. A query joining `Orders` (sharded by `customer_id`) with `Products` (sharded by `product_id`) requires the router to scatter the query to every shard, perform local joins, then merge results in the application layer. This adds latency and consumes network bandwidth.

**Challenge 2 — Distributed Transactions (2 Marks)**
ACID transactions across multiple shards cannot rely on the local database's transaction manager. Systems must adopt either **Two-Phase Commit (2PC)** — which is slow and prone to coordinator failure — or the **Saga pattern** — which sacrifices isolation. Both reduce throughput dramatically.

**Challenge 3 — Rebalancing Complexity (1.5 Marks)**
Adding or removing shards requires live migration of millions of rows while the system continues serving traffic. Tools like `Vitess`, `Citus`, or `MongoDB balancer` automate this, but the operation remains high-risk.

**Challenge 4 — Operational Overhead (1.5 Marks)**
Backups, schema migrations, monitoring, and indexing must run **N times** — once per shard. A single ALTER TABLE in MySQL becomes an N-fold distributed orchestration problem.

---

### Part (b) — Vertical Partitioning vs Sharding & Recommendation (7 Marks)

**Conceptual Comparison (3 Marks)**

Vertical partitioning splits a table **by columns** — e.g., the `Users` table becomes `Users_Core` (id, name, email) and `Users_PII` (address, phone, payment_info). All rows still live in the same database instance. Sharding splits the table **by rows** across multiple independent databases.

**Recommendation for the Startup (4 Marks)**

For a single-node MySQL with **500 GB** of data:

- **Step 1:** First apply **vertical partitioning** — separate rarely-accessed columns (audit logs, old metadata) into a cold table or archive. This alone may restore performance.
- **Step 2:** Apply **indexing, query optimization, and read-replica replication** — this scales reads with zero schema change.
- **Step 3:** Only consider sharding if writes exceed ~10,000 QPS **AND** storage approaches the single-node physical limit (typically 1–2 TB).

**Verdict:** The startup should **NOT shard yet**. Sharding is a *last-resort scalability tool* — premature adoption introduces permanent operational complexity. The correct KTU-engineering recommendation is to first exhaust single-node optimizations, then move to **read replicas**, then to **vertical partitioning**, and only then to **horizontal sharding**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing sharding with replication** — Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if you write "sharding is copying data across servers". Remember: **sharding splits rows; replication copies the whole DB.**
> 2. **Forgetting the shard key in the WHERE clause** — When writing sample queries, always include the shard key. A query without the shard key triggers scatter-gather, which is the *opposite* of sharding's benefit.
> 3. **Ignoring rebalancing** — Any complete sharding answer MUST mention rebalancing. Examiners specifically test whether you understand that sharding is not a "set and forget" operation.
> 4. **No diagram in 14-mark questions** — A 14-mark answer without a diagram typically loses 1–2 marks. Always include at least one hand-drawn or text-based architecture block.
> 5. **Failing to state trade-offs** — Saying "sharding is great" without listing the lost ACID guarantees, cross-shard joins, and rebalancing pain is treated as an incomplete answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sharding = horizontal row-based partitioning** across N independent databases.
- The **shard key** is the most critical design decision — must be high-cardinality, evenly distributed, immutable, and frequently queried.
- **Three core strategies:** Range (good for range queries, bad for hot spots), Hash (good for even distribution, bad for range queries), Directory (most flexible, has SPOF).
- **Sharding vs Replication:** Sharding scales *writes*; replication scales *reads*.
- **Lost RDBMS guarantees:** No cross-shard JOINs, no cross-shard transactions, no global foreign keys, no simple backups.
- **Hot Spot Ratio formula:** $\frac{\max(\text{RPS})}{\text{mean}(\text{RPS})} < 2.0$ for a healthy cluster.
- **Rebalancing is mandatory** when hot spots appear — use online migration tools to avoid downtime.
- **Architectural layers:** Shard Layer (data nodes) + Routing Layer (middleware) + Metadata Store (config).
- **Production tools:** Vitess (MySQL), Citus (PostgreSQL), MongoDB native sharding, Cassandra consistent hashing, Google Spanner.
- **KTU golden rule:** Always mention the **shard key** when explaining any sharding concept — examiners actively look for it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CAP Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CAP Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **CAP Theorem** (also known as **Brewer's Theorem**), formally conjectured by computer scientist **Eric Brewer** at the *PODC 2000* keynote and rigorously proven by **Seth Gilbert and Nancy Lynch** of MIT in **2002**, is a foundational principle in the theory of **distributed data systems**. The theorem states that in any distributed data store, it is **impossible to simultaneously guarantee all three** of the following properties:

1. **Consistency (C):** Every read operation returns the most recent successful write or an error. All nodes in the system observe the same data at the same logical time.
2. **Availability (A):** Every request (read or write) from a non-failing node receives a successful response — it is guaranteed that every request will be served, even if the response data may not be the latest.
3. **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate despite an arbitrary number of messages being dropped (or delayed) between nodes by the network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In any arbitrarily deployed distributed system, network partitions **will occur** (P is non-negotiable in real distributed systems). Therefore, the real engineering trade-off is fundamentally between **Consistency** and **Availability** during a partition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Check-in Counter

Imagine an airport with **three critical desks** (C, A, P) but only **two operators** available at any given time. Whenever a severe network outage (Partition) hits the airport's central reservation database:

- **Consistency Desk:** The counter refuses to print boarding passes until all regional servers confirm your seat. You might wait — or be turned away.
- **Availability Desk:** The counter prints a boarding pass using locally cached data — fast and responsive, but the seat might no longer be valid.
- **Partition Tolerance Desk:** This is the *baseline* reality — partitions (network failures) *will* happen. You cannot avoid them.

You can only have **two operators working at once**. The third desk will always be unattended when a partition occurs. This is the essence of CAP.

## 1.3 Why P is Mandatory in Distributed Systems

In modern distributed databases (where data is replicated across multiple geographically separated nodes), **network partitions are inevitable**. Hardware failures, fiber cuts, switch reboots, and routing issues all cause partitions. Therefore, P is treated as a **non-optional** property, leaving only two real-world architectural choices: **CP** or **AP**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric / Threshold:** Gilbert and Lynch's 2002 proof uses a **2-node asynchronous network model** with message loss to formally demonstrate that at least one of the three properties must be violated during a partition.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Consistency (C) — "Single Logical View"
- Also called **Linearizability** or **Strong Consistency**.
- After a write $W$ completes successfully, **all subsequent reads** $R$ must return the value of $W$ (or a value written after $W$).
- Equivalent to: the system behaves as if there is **only one copy of the data**.
- **Measured in:** Staleness window (ideally **0 seconds** for strong consistency).

### 2.1.2 Availability (A) — "Every Request Gets a Response"
- A *non-failing* node must respond to every request within a bounded time.
- The response is *non-error* (i.e., valid data is returned, not a failure code).
- Note: Availability in CAP is **NOT** "uptime %" — it specifically means *no failed requests* during partitions.
- **Measured in:** Response time (e.g., milliseconds).

### 2.1.3 Partition Tolerance (P) — "Survive Network Failure"
- A partition is a **communication break** between two or more subsets of nodes.
- The system must continue functioning despite this break.
- **Measured in:** Tolerance to number of simultaneously failed links / nodes.

## 2.2 The Three CAP Combinations

| System Type | Guarantees | Sacrifices | Example Systems | Use Case |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **CA** (Ideal but impractical) | C + A | P | Single-node RDBMS, traditional **MySQL** (single master) | Single-datacenter, no replication |
| **CP** | C + P | A | **HBase**, **MongoDB** (default config), **Redis** (cluster with quorum), **etcd**, **ZooKeeper** | Banking, inventory, ticket booking |
| **AP** | A + P | C | **Cassandra**, **CouchDB**, **DynamoDB**, **Riak** | Social media feeds, analytics, shopping carts |

## 2.3 The Trade-off Logic — "Why Not All Three?"

Gilbert & Lynch (2002) proved that in an **asynchronous network** with even a single dropped message (a partition), achieving both C and A is mathematically impossible:

$$
\text{In any execution of a distributed system where a partition occurs:}
$$

$$
(C \land A \land P) \rightarrow \bot \;\; (\text{Contradiction})
$$

This is because:
1. A partition means a node **cannot** communicate with peers.
2. To be **Available**, the node must respond using **only local data** (stale).
3. To be **Consistent**, the node must refuse to respond (since it cannot confirm latest state).
4. Therefore, **C and A are mutually exclusive during a partition**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Modern interpretations (including Brewer's 2012 clarification) state that CAP applies *only during a partition*. When the network is healthy, all three properties can co-exist. The trade-off is **runtime conditional**, not absolute.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Engineering Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| CAP impossibility | $C \land A \land P = \bot$ | All three cannot coexist during partition | Boolean (True/False) |
| Strong consistency latency | $L_c \approx 0$ | Reads see latest write | **milliseconds** |
| Eventual consistency window | $T_{ec} \to \infty$ | Stale reads allowed | **seconds to minutes** |
| Quorum condition for consistency | $W + R > N$ | Write quorum + Read quorum > Replicas | Integer count |
| Availability probability | $P(A) = 1 - P(\text{node failure})$ | 1 minus failure probability | **0 to 1** (or **%**) |
| Partition detection time | $T_{detect}$ | Time to identify network break | **seconds** |
| Brewer's Real Trade-off | $C \lor A$ (under $P$) | Choose one during partition | Boolean |
| PACELC extension | If $P \Rightarrow (C \text{ or } A)$; Else $\Rightarrow (C \text{ or } L)$ | Latency trade-off when no partition | Boolean |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CAP framework directly drives the design of nearly every modern NoSQL database, microservices architecture, and global-scale web service:

- **Banking Systems** → CP (must not show wrong balance)
- **E-commerce Carts** → AP (must not lose customer activity)
- **Distributed Coordination** (ZooKeeper, etcd) → CP (consensus critical)
- **Global CDNs & DNS** → AP (eventual consistency acceptable)
- **Real-time Multiplayer Games** → Custom hybrid (often CP with relaxed latency)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Proof Sketch (Gilbert–Lynch Model, 2002)

### 3.1.1 System Model Definition

We define a distributed data store $\mathcal{D}$ with:
- $N$ distinct nodes (asynchronous network).
- Each node maintains a local state $S_i$.
- Operations: $\text{read}(x)$ and $\text{write}(x, v)$.
- Communication: messages may be **lost** (partition).

We assume a system of **2 nodes**: $N_1$ and $N_2$, both holding a key $x$.

### 3.1.2 Initial State

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1(x) &= 0 \\
S_2(x) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Both nodes begin with the same value of $x$.

### 3.1.3 Step 1: A write occurs at $N_1$

A client issues a write:
$$
\text{write}(x, 1) \rightarrow N_1
$$

The write succeeds locally:
$$
S_1(x) = 1
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 2: Network partition occurs

Immediately after the write, a **partition** isolates $N_1$ from $N_2$. No further messages can be delivered.

### 3.1.5 Step 3: Analyze the impossible demands

- **If we demand C:** $N_2$ must return $1$ on $\text{read}(x)$. But $N_2$ has no knowledge of the write (partition blocks replication). $N_2$ can either:
  - Return its local value $S_2(x) = 0$ → **violates C**.
  - Refuse the request (error) → **violates A**.
- **If we demand A:** $N_2$ must respond successfully. It must return its local value $0$ → **violates C** (since the latest write was 1).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Demand C: } & \text{read}(x) \text{ at } N_2 = 1 \;\; \text{(requires knowledge of write)} \\
\text{But partition blocks: } & N_2 \not\rightarrow N_1 \\
\text{Therefore: } & N_2 \text{ cannot return latest value} \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Cannot satisfy both A and C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Conclusion

$$
(C \land A \land P) \;\; \text{is unreachable in any asynchronous network with even one lost message.}
$$

This is the formal proof that CAP holds.

## 3.2 Quorum Derivation for Read/Write Consistency

For a replicated system with $N$ replicas, a write succeeds on $W$ nodes, and a read is answered by $R$ nodes. For **strong consistency**, the read set and write set must **overlap** by at least one node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition for overlap: } & W + R > N \\
\text{Common configurations: } & \\
& N = 3,\; W = 2,\; R = 2 \;\; \Rightarrow 2 + 2 = 4 > 3 \;\; \checkmark \\
& N = 3,\; W = 1,\; R = 1 \;\; \Rightarrow 1 + 1 = 2 \not> 3 \;\; \text{(eventual consistency)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 PACELC Extension — The Latency Trade-off

Proposed by **Daniel Abadi (2010)**, PACELC refines CAP by addressing the *non-partition* case:

$$
\text{PACELC} = \begin{cases} \text{If } P: & \text{choose } C \text{ or } A \\ \text{Else (no } P\text{):} & \text{choose } C \text{ or } L \text{ (Latency)} \end{cases}
$$

| System | PACELC Type | Meaning |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **DynamoDB / Cassandra** | PA / EL | Available during partition; favors low latency normally |
| **HBase / BigTable** | PC / EC | Consistent during partition; favors consistency normally |
| **MongoDB (default)** | PC / EC | Strong consistency prioritized |
| **CouchDB** | PA / EL | Availability + low latency prioritized |

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Engineering Requirement | Recommended CAP Choice | Justification |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Financial transactions | **CP** | Stale data causes financial loss |
| DNS / Public web caches | **AP** | Eventual consistency acceptable |
| Distributed lock service | **CP** | Coordination needs single view |
| Sensor data ingestion | **AP** | Continuous data flow preferred |
| Leader election (e.g., Raft) | **CP** | Must agree on one leader |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CAP Trade-off Venn Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeC["C - CONSISTENCY"]
    nodeA["A - AVAILABILITY"]
    nodeP["P - PARTITION TOLERANCE"]
    nodeCA["CA SYSTEM\nSingle Node DB\nExample: PostgreSQL standalone"]
    nodeCP["CP SYSTEM\nExample: HBase, MongoDB, etcd"]
    nodeAP["AP SYSTEM\nExample: Cassandra, DynamoDB"]

    nodeC --- nodeA
    nodeA --- nodeP
    nodeP --- nodeC

    nodeC --- nodeCA
    nodeA --- nodeCA
    nodeA --- nodeAP
    nodeP --- nodeAP
    nodeC --- nodeCP
    nodeP --- nodeCP

    nodeCapNote["P is non-negotiable in real distributed systems"]
    nodeCapNote2["True trade-off is C vs A during partition"]
    nodeCapNote ~~~ nodeCA
    nodeCapNote2 ~~~ nodeCP
```

## 4.2 Distributed System Block Architecture under Partition

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeClient["CLIENT REQUEST"]
    nodeRouter["LOAD BALANCER / ROUTER"]
    nodeN1["NODE 1\nRegion A"]
    nodeN2["NODE 2\nRegion B"]
    nodeDB1["REPLICA 1"]
    nodeDB2["REPLICA 2"]
    nodePartition["NETWORK PARTITION\nCommunication broken"]
    nodeRespC["RESPONSE: STALE DATA\nAP System"]
    nodeRespCP["RESPONSE: ERROR / TIMEOUT\nCP System"]

    nodeClient --> nodeRouter
    nodeRouter --> nodeN1
    nodeRouter --> nodeN2
    nodeN1 --> nodeDB1
    nodeN2 --> nodeDB2
    nodeDB1 -.->|"blocked"| nodePartition
    nodePartition -.->|"blocked"| nodeDB2
    nodeN1 --> nodeRespC
    nodeN1 --> nodeRespCP
```

## 4.3 CAP Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    startQ{"Does the system\nneed to handle\nnetwork partitions?"}
    branchA["Choose: CP or AP"]
    singleQ{"Is the system\nsingle-node\nno replication?"}
    singleAns["CA is possible\nbut unrealistic for\ndistributed systems"]

    cq{"Must data always\nbe strictly current?"}
    aYes["CP System\nExample: HBase\nReject writes during partition"]
    aNo{"Must every request\nsucceed even during failure?"}
    aNoYes["AP System\nExample: Cassandra\nServe stale data with eventual consistency"]

    startQ -->|"YES"| branchA
    startQ -->|"NO"| singleQ
    singleQ -->|"YES"| singleAns
    branchA --> cq
    cq -->|"YES"| aYes
    cq -->|"NO"| aNo
    aNo -->|"YES"| aNoYes
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the CAP Theorem. List its three properties briefly.**

**Model Answer:**
The **CAP Theorem**, proposed by **Eric Brewer (2000)** and proven by **Gilbert and Lynch (2002)**, states that a distributed data store cannot simultaneously guarantee all three of the following properties:

- **Consistency (C):** All nodes see the same data at the same time (linearizability).
- **Availability (A):** Every non-failing node returns a response for every request.
- **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate despite network partitions (message loss between nodes).

In practice, since network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems, the trade-off is between **Consistency and Availability**, leading to **CP or AP** systems.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between CP and AP systems with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | CP System | AP System |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Sacrifice | Availability | Consistency |
| Behavior during partition | Rejects some requests / returns errors | Continues to serve with possibly stale data |
| Example | **HBase**, **etcd**, **ZooKeeper** | **Cassandra**, **DynamoDB**, **CouchDB** |
| Use case | Banking, distributed locks, leader election | Social feeds, IoT sensor data, e-commerce carts |

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the CAP Theorem in detail. Discuss why all three properties cannot be satisfied simultaneously in a distributed database system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the different system categories (CA, CP, AP). Give one real-world example for each and explain the trade-off made.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) CAP Theorem — Detailed Explanation:**

The CAP Theorem was proposed by **Eric Brewer in 2000** at the PODC conference and formally proven by **Gilbert and Lynch in 2002** at MIT. It applies to distributed data stores that replicate data across multiple nodes connected via a network.

**The three properties are:**

1. **Consistency (C):** Every read returns the most recent write. From the client's perspective, the system behaves as though only a single copy of the data exists.
2. **Availability (A):** Every request to a non-failing node receives a non-error response. The system remains "always on."
3. **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to function despite arbitrary message loss between nodes (caused by network failure).

**Why all three cannot coexist:**
Consider two nodes $N_1$ and $N_2$ holding a key $x$ with value $0$. A client writes $x = 1$ to $N_1$. Immediately, a network partition isolates $N_1$ from $N_2$. If a second client requests $\text{read}(x)$ from $N_2$:

- To satisfy **Consistency**, $N_2$ must return $1$. But $N_2$ has no way of knowing about the write (the partition blocks replication).
- To satisfy **Availability**, $N_2$ must respond successfully. It can only return its local value $0$, violating consistency.

Thus, during any partition, the system must sacrifice **either C or A**. This is the formal impossibility:

$$
(C \land A \land P) \;\; \text{is mathematically unsatisfiable in an asynchronous network.}
$$

**(b) CAP Categories — Diagram and Examples:**

| Category | Guarantees | Sacrifices | Real-World Example | Engineering Reason |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **CA** | C + A | P (assumes no partition) | Single-node **PostgreSQL** | No replication, no partition possibility |
| **CP** | C + P | A (may reject requests) | **HBase**, **etcd** | Coordination, banking, inventory |
| **AP** | A + P | C (may return stale data) | **Cassandra**, **DynamoDB** | High availability, social media, IoT |

**Diagram:** *(See Section 4.1 — CAP Venn Diagram.)*

**Trade-off Justification:**
- In **CP systems** like HBase, when a partition occurs, writes may be blocked on the minority side to prevent inconsistency.
- In **AP systems** like Cassandra, writes are always accepted; reads may return stale data, but the system remains fully responsive. Conflicts are resolved later via **vector clocks** or **last-write-wins** policies.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Brewer's CAP Theorem. Discuss the PACELC extension and how it refines the original theorem.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare MongoDB, Cassandra, and HBase with respect to their CAP behavior. Justify the architectural choice made by each.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) CAP Theorem + PACELC Extension:**

**CAP Theorem (Brewer, 2000):** A distributed database can simultaneously provide only **two out of three** of: **Consistency**, **Availability**, **Partition Tolerance**.

**PACELC Extension (Abadi, 2010):** PACELC refines CAP by addressing the case when there is *no* partition:

$$
\text{PACELC}: \quad \text{If } P \rightarrow (C \text{ or } A); \quad \text{Else} \rightarrow (C \text{ or } L)
$$

Meaning:
- **If a partition exists (P):** Choose between Consistency and Availability.
- **Else (no partition):** Choose between Consistency and Latency (i.e., even without a partition, the designer must decide whether to optimize for low latency or strong consistency).

**Examples:**
- **DynamoDB / Cassandra → PA / EL:** Available under partition; Low latency normally.
- **HBase / BigTable → PC / EC:** Consistent under partition; Consistent normally.
- **MongoDB → PC / EC:** Prioritizes consistency.

PACELC captures the **real-world reality** that even when the network is healthy, designers still face trade-offs (e.g., synchronous replication increases latency).

**(b) CAP Behavior of MongoDB, Cassandra, HBase:**

| System | CAP Type | Architectural Reasoning |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **MongoDB** | **CP** | Default configuration uses a single primary per replica set. During a partition, secondary nodes may become read-only, sacrificing availability to preserve consistency. |
| **Cassandra** | **AP** | Masterless architecture. Any node can accept writes regardless of partition. Reads may return stale data; conflicts are resolved via vector clocks. Prioritizes high availability. |
| **HBase** | **CP** | Built on top of HDFS and ZooKeeper. Uses strong consistency (linearizability) for reads/writes. If the ZooKeeper quorum is lost, the region server may reject requests, sacrificing availability. |

**Justification:**
- **MongoDB** is chosen for applications needing strong consistency (e.g., CMS, catalogs).
- **Cassandra** is chosen for high-write-throughput, always-on applications (e.g., IoT, messaging).
- **HBase** is chosen for analytical workloads needing strong consistency over massive data (e.g., time-series, big data analytics).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** write "CAP allows you to pick any two" as a blanket statement — KTU examiners expect you to clarify that **P is non-optional in distributed systems**, and the real choice is between **C and A**.
> 2. **Do not** confuse **Consistency in CAP** with **ACID Consistency** — CAP's C means **linearizability** (latest write), not data integrity constraints.
> 3. **Do not** say "CA systems exist in the real world" — in practice, CA is a single-node design and not considered truly *distributed*.
> 4. Forgetting to mention **Gilbert and Lynch's 2002 proof** loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. Missing the **PACELC extension** when asked about limitations of CAP loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CAP** = **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, **P**artition Tolerance.
- Proposed by **Eric Brewer (2000)**; proven by **Gilbert & Lynch (2002)**.
- All three cannot coexist during a partition: $(C \land A \land P) = \bot$.
- **P is mandatory** in any real distributed system — the real trade-off is **C vs A**.
- **CP systems** sacrifice availability: HBase, MongoDB (default), etcd, ZooKeeper.
- **AP systems** sacrifice consistency: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB.
- **CA systems** are essentially single-node and not truly distributed.
- **PACELC** extension: Even without a partition, designers choose between **Consistency and Latency**.
- **Quorum formula:** $W + R > N$ ensures strong consistency in replicated systems.
- CAP applies *only during a partition*; outside partitions, all three can co-exist.
- Linearizability (CAP's C) ≠ ACID Consistency — they are different concepts.
- Brewer's 2012 clarification: CAP is a *spectrum*, not a binary choice — systems can dynamically adjust between C and A.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of MongoDB: MongoDB Data Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Overview of MongoDB: MongoDB Data Model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition The **MongoDB Data Model** is a **document-oriented, schema-flexible, non-relational (NoSQL) data model** that stores data in the form of **BSON (Binary JSON) documents** organized into **collections**, which in turn are grouped under **databases**. Unlike the rigid tabular model of RDBMS, the MongoDB data model is a **polymorphic, hierarchical, and semi-structured** representation inspired by JSON (JavaScript Object Notation). Formally, the MongoDB data model can be defined as a 4-level hierarchy: $$\text{Database} \rightarrow \text{Collection} \rightarrow \text{Document} \rightarrow \text{Field/Value Pair (BSON)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3** > The MongoDB Data Model is the *foundational concept* on which CRUD operations, aggregation pipelines, indexing, and sharding are built. You MUST be able to articulate (a) the BSON document structure, (b) the difference between embedded and referenced data, and (c) the comparison with the relational model. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Filing Cabinet Story" Imagine a **library** as your MongoDB server: | Real-World Analogy | MongoDB Equivalent | |---|---| | The **Library Building** | MongoDB **Server / Instance** | | A **Section** in the library (e.g., "Fiction", "Science") | A **Database** (e.g., `libraryDB`) | | A **Shelf** within a section | A **Collection** (e.g., `books`, `members`) | | A single **book** on the shelf | A **Document** (the basic unit of data) | | Each **page** of the book | A **Field–Value pair** within the document | The crucial difference from a real library: **every book on a shelf can have a different number of pages, different page sizes, and different chapter structures** — that is the *schema-less* nature of MongoDB. > [!NOTE] > **Why BSON and not JSON?** > JSON is text-based and limited to **6 data types** (string, number, boolean, array, object, null). MongoDB invented **BSON (Binary JSON)** which extends JSON with additional types like `ObjectId`, `Date`, `Binary Data`, `Decimal128`, `Regular Expression`, etc., and is encoded in binary for **faster parsing and traversal**. ### 1.3 Core Building Blocks - **Document** → The fundamental unit of data (analogous to a *row* in RDBMS). - **Collection** → A group of documents (analogous to a *table* in RDBMS). - **Database** → A physical container of collections (one MongoDB server can host multiple databases). - **Field** → A key–value pair inside a document. - **_id Field** → A mandatory **12-byte ObjectId** that uniquely identifies every document in a collection (acts as the primary key). ### 1.4 Standard MongoDB Limits to Remember | Element | Limit | Value | |---|---|---| | Nested Document Depth | Max depth | **100 levels** | | Document Size | Max BSON size | **16 MB** | | Index Key Size | Combined | **1024 bytes** | | Namespace Length | DB + Collection | **120 bytes** | | Databases per Server | Logical | **Unlimited (practical ~1000s)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **16 MB document size limit** is a critical KTU exam point. It enforces a design discipline: do NOT embed unbounded arrays. Instead, use **referencing** (child–parent or parent–child references) when the data grows unpredictably. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the Data Model The MongoDB Data Model can be broken down operationally into 6 logical tiers. Each tier is examined below for *why it exists* and *how it works*. #### Tier 1 — The Database (Physical Storage Unit) - **Why?** A single MongoDB server (mongod process) can host multiple independent logical databases. Each database has its own files on disk under `/data/db/`. - **How?** Databases are created implicitly when first referenced, or explicitly via `use `. - Special databases: `admin`, `config`, `local` (system databases, do not store app data). #### Tier 2 — The Collection (Schema-less Group of Documents) - **Why?** Collections group related documents so they can be **indexed, queried, and sharded** as a unit. - **How?** Collections do **NOT enforce a schema** — different documents in the same collection may have completely different fields. - Two sub-types: - **Regular Collection** — user-defined. - **Capped Collection** — fixed-size, FIFO (First-In-First-Out) eviction, ideal for **log files**. #### Tier 3 — The Document (Atomic Unit) - **Why?** Documents are the *atomic unit* of CRUD — write operations in MongoDB are atomic **at the single-document level**, never across multiple documents. - **How?** Represented internally as a **BSON binary blob**. When sent to the client, BSON is converted to JSON for human readability. - Document structure is an **ordered set of field–value pairs**. #### Tier 4 — The Field (Key–Value Pair) - **Why?** Fields are the named slots that hold data. - **How?** Every field has a **string key** and a **BSON-typed value**. - Field names are case-sensitive and may not contain the `.` character or start with `$`. #### Tier 5 — The BSON Type System - **Why?** JSON's 6 types are insufficient for a production database (no native date, no binary blob, no 64-bit integer, no decimal for money). - **How?** BSON extends JSON with 16+ additional types. #### Tier 6 — The `_id` Primary Key - **Why?** Every document needs a unique identity for retrieval and indexing. - **How?** Auto-generated **12-byte ObjectId** consisting of: - **4 bytes** — timestamp (seconds since Unix epoch) - **5 bytes** — random value (unique to machine/process) - **3 bytes** — incrementing counter (per process) $$\text{ObjectId} = \underbrace{4B}_{\text{ts}} \;+\; \underbrace{5B}_{\text{random}} \;+\; \underbrace{3B}_{\text{counter}}$$ ### 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet — BSON Data Types > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** BSON type numbers (1, 2, 3 …) are *frequently asked* in short-answer questions. Memorize the table below. | BSON Type | Type Number | Alias | Example | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | Double | 1 | `double` | `3.14159` | Floating point | | String | 2 | `string` | `"Kerala"` | UTF-8 text | | Object | 3 | `object` | `{x: 1}` | Embedded sub-document | | Array | 4 | `array` | `[1, 2, 3]` | Lists | | Binary Data | 5 | `binData` | `BinData(0,...)` | Images, files | | ObjectId | 7 | `objectId` | `ObjectId("...")` | Default `_id` | | Boolean | 8 | `bool` | `true` | Flags | | Date | 9 | `date` | `ISODate("...")` | Timestamps | | Null | 10 | `null` | `null` | Absence of value | | 32-bit Integer | 16 | `int` | `42` | Small integers | | 64-bit Integer | 18 | `long` | `NumberLong("123")` | Large integers | | Decimal128 | 19 | `decimal` | `NumberDecimal("9.99")` | **Money / financial** | ### 2.3 Two Strategies for Modelling Relationships This is the **single most important design decision** in MongoDB. KTU expects you to compare and justify both. | Strategy | Mechanism | When to Use | Drawback | |---|---|---|---| | **Embedded (Denormalized)** | Nest the related document *inside* the parent | 1-to-1, 1-to-few, data accessed together | Document size growth; 16 MB cap | | **Referenced (Normalized)** | Store the `_id` of the related document | 1-to-many (unbounded), many-to-many | Requires `$lookup` or multiple queries | **Rule of thumb (the "Rule of Three"):** - **1-to-few** → Embed - **1-to-many** → Reference - **1-to-bazillions** → Reference with a *bucket* pattern ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **IoT Systems** → Each sensor reading is a document; append-only, schema-flexible. - **E-Commerce Catalogs** → Products with varying attributes (a phone has `RAM`, a shirt has `size`). - **Content Management** → Articles with embedded comments and author profiles. - **Mobile Apps** → Offline-first sync, since documents are self-contained JSON. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Anatomy of a MongoDB Document Below is a realistic **student document** that demonstrates nearly every BSON type. We will then *decompose* it tier by tier. ```json { "_id": ObjectId("65a1b2c3d4e5f60017abcdef"), "name": "Ananya Krishnan", "rollNo": NumberLong(20240047), "cgpa": NumberDecimal("9.42"), "isHosteller": true, "joinDate": ISODate("2024-08-01T00:00:00Z"), "skills": ["MongoDB", "Python", "React"], "address": { "street": "TC 12/345", "city": "Thiruvananthapuram", "pin": 695034 }, "projects": [ { "title": "KTU Notes", "year": 2024 }, { "title": "DBMS Mini", "year": 2023 } ], "profilePic": BinData(0, "aGVsbG8=") } ``` **Decomposition (step by step):** 1. `_id` → **ObjectId (Type 7)** — the mandatory primary key, 12 bytes. 2. `name` → **String (Type 2)** — UTF-8 text. 3. `rollNo` → **64-bit Integer (Type 18)** — large integer that exceeds 32-bit range. 4. `cgpa` → **Decimal128 (Type 19)** — used because floating-point `9.42` cannot be represented exactly in binary; Decimal128 is mandatory for financial/grade calculations. 5. `isHosteller` → **Boolean (Type 8)**. 6. `joinDate` → **Date (Type 9)** — stored as 64-bit ms since Unix epoch. 7. `skills` → **Array (Type 4)** of strings. 8. `address` → **Embedded Document (Type 3)** — illustrates *denormalization* (the address lives inside the student). 9. `projects` → **Array of Embedded Documents (Type 4 → Type 3)**. 10. `profilePic` → **Binary Data (Type 5)**. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Demonstrating the ObjectId Generation The 12-byte ObjectId generation is a deterministic process. Given the hex `65a1b2c3d4e5f60017abcdef`, we can derive its components: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Hex input:} \quad & \texttt{65a1b2c3d4e5f60017abcdef} \\ \text{Bytes split:} \quad & \underbrace{\texttt{65a1b2c3}}_{\text{4 B (timestamp)}} \;\; \underbrace{\texttt{d4e5f60017}}_{\text{5 B (random)}} \;\; \underbrace{\texttt{abcdef}}_{\text{3 B (counter)}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step A — Extract the 4-byte timestamp:** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{0x65a1b2c3} &= 6 \times 16^{7} + 5 \times 16^{6} + \ldots + 3 \times 16^{0} \\ &= 1\,701\,376\,195 \text{ (seconds since Unix epoch)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step B — Convert timestamp to human date:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Unix Epoch} &= \text{1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC} \\ \text{Date} &= \text{2023-11-28 11:23:15 UTC (approx.)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step C — Identify the 3-byte counter:** The counter is initialized to a random value and **monotonically incremented** for every document created by the same `mongod` process, ensuring **uniqueness even within the same second**. > [!NOTE] > **Why the timestamp inside `_id` matters:** MongoDB can sort documents by creation time **using only the `_id` field** — no need for a separate `createdAt` index. This is a unique MongoDB design feature. ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Embedding vs Referencing (Design Analysis) Consider a blog with **users** and **posts** where each post has many comments. **Option A — Fully Embedded (one document per post):** ```json { "_id": ObjectId("..."), "title": "MongoDB Notes", "author": "Ananya", "comments": [ { "user": "Rahul", "text": "Great!", "ts": ISODate("...") }, { "user": "Sneha", "text": "Helpful", "ts": ISODate("...") } ] } ``` **Pros:** Single read, atomic update of post+comments. **Cons:** Document grows unbounded → may hit **16 MB** for viral posts. **Option B — Referenced (separate comments collection):** ```json // posts collection { "_id": ObjectId("p1"), "title": "MongoDB Notes", "author": "Ananya" } // comments collection { "_id": ObjectId("c1"), "postId": ObjectId("p1"), "user": "Rahul", "text": "Great!" } { "_id": ObjectId("c2"), "postId": ObjectId("p1"), "user": "Sneha", "text": "Helpful" } ``` **Pros:** Scalable, each comment document stays small. **Cons:** Two queries (or an `$lookup` aggregation) to fetch post+comments. **Analytical Decision Matrix:** | Criterion | Embedding Wins | Referencing Wins | |---|---|---| | Read pattern | Always read together | Read independently | | Cardinality | 1-to-few | 1-to-many / many-to-many | | Update frequency | Rare | Frequent child updates | | Document size | < 16 MB always | Risk of growth | | Atomicity needed? | Yes (single doc atomic) | No (multi-doc tx needed) | ### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Validating the 16 MB Document Limit Suppose each comment averages **500 bytes**. How many comments can fit in one embedded document? $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Max embedded comments} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{16 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ bytes}}{500 \text{ bytes/comment}} \right\rfloor \\ &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{16\,777\,216}{500} \right\rfloor \\ &= \left\lfloor 33\,554.432 \right\rfloor \\ &= 33\,554 \text{ comments} \end{aligned} $$ **Inference:** If a post is expected to exceed ~33 K comments, you **must** switch to a referenced model, or implement a **bucket pattern** (e.g., embed comments in arrays of 100). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 MongoDB Data Model — Hierarchical Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB srv["MongoDB Server
mongod process"] db1["Database: universityDB"] db2["Database: libraryDB"] db3["Database: admin"] col1["Collection: students"] col2["Collection: courses"] col3["Collection: books"] col4["Collection: members"] doc1["Document 1
_id: ObjectId ...
name: Ananya
cgpa: 9.42"] doc2["Document 2
_id: ObjectId ...
name: Rahul
cgpa: 8.75"] doc3["Document 3
_id: ObjectId ...
title: DBMS
credits: 4"] fld1["Field: _id = ObjectId(7)"] fld2["Field: name = String(2)"] fld3["Field: cgpa = Decimal128(19)"] fld4["Field: skills = Array(4)"] fld5["Field: address = Object(3)"] srv --> db1 srv --> db2 srv --> db3 db1 --> col1 db1 --> col2 db2 --> col3 db2 --> col4 col1 --> doc1 col1 --> doc2 col2 --> doc3 doc1 --> fld1 doc1 --> fld2 doc1 --> fld3 doc1 --> fld4 doc1 --> fld5 ``` ### 4.2 Embedding vs Referencing — Visual Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Designing a new relationship"] q1{"How many child items?"} q2{"Read parent & child together?"} q3{"Child count bounded?"} a1["1-to-1 or 1-to-few"] a2["1-to-many (unbounded)"] a3["Many-to-many"] embed["EMBED the child
inside parent document"] ref["REFERENCE child
store child _id in parent"] ref2["REFERENCE both ways
use array of _ids"] start --> q1 q1 -->|few items| a1 q1 -->|hundreds+| a2 q1 -->|M:N| a3 a1 --> q2 q2 -->|yes| embed q2 -->|no| ref a2 --> q3 q3 -->|bounded, small| embed q3 -->|unbounded| ref a3 --> ref2 ``` ### 4.3 BSON Type-to-Storage Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR json["JSON Document
(text format)"] parse["BSON Encoder
libbson library"] bin["BSON Binary
0x16 0x00 0x00 0x00 ..."] disk["Disk Storage
/data/db/ collection.wt"] net["Network Wire
OP_MSG protocol"] json --> parse parse --> bin bin --> disk bin --> net net --> parse parse --> json ``` ### 4.4 Comparison Architecture: RDBMS vs MongoDB ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph rdbms["RDBMS Model"] r1["Database"] --> r2["Table"] r2 --> r3["Row"] r3 --> r4["Column
(fixed schema)"] r2 -.->|JOIN| r2 end subgraph mongo["MongoDB Model"] m1["Database"] --> m2["Collection"] m2 --> m3["Document
(flexible schema)"] m3 --> m4["Field
(BSON typed)"] m3 -.->|embed or $lookup| m3 end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **Q1.** What is the role of **BSON** in MongoDB? How is it different from JSON? **Model Answer (3 marks):** BSON (Binary JSON) is the binary-encoded serialization format used by MongoDB to store documents internally and transmit them over the network. (1 mark) - **JSON** is a text-based format with only 6 data types (string, number, boolean, array, object, null). (1 mark) - **BSON** extends JSON with additional types such as `ObjectId`, `Date`, `Binary`, `Decimal128`, `int`, `long`, and is encoded in binary, enabling **faster parsing, efficient traversal, and storage of richer data types**. (1 mark) --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q2.** Explain the **12-byte ObjectId** structure used as the default `_id` in MongoDB documents. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 12-byte ObjectId consists of three parts: (1 mark) - **4 bytes** — timestamp (seconds since Unix epoch), enables time-based sorting. (1 mark) - **5 bytes** — random value unique to the machine/process. (0.5 mark) - **3 bytes** — incrementing counter that resets per second, ensuring uniqueness. (0.5 mark) Total = 4 + 5 + 3 = 12 bytes, generated by the client driver, guaranteeing global uniqueness across distributed MongoDB deployments. *(continuation for 3rd mark completed above)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you are given an *internal choice*. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **MongoDB Data Model** in detail. Compare it with the **RDBMS data model** using a suitable example, highlighting at least **five differences**. **Model Solution:** **1. MongoDB Data Model (3 marks):** The MongoDB data model is a **document-oriented model** organized in a 4-level hierarchy: *Database → Collection → Document → Field*. Data is stored as **BSON documents** — flexible, schema-less, JSON-like objects that can contain nested documents and arrays. Collections group related documents, but unlike RDBMS tables, they do not enforce a fixed schema, meaning two documents in the same collection may have entirely different fields. **2. RDBMS Data Model (1 mark):** The Relational Model is based on **tables (relations)** of fixed schema. Each table has predefined columns, and data integrity is enforced through primary keys, foreign keys, and ACID transactions. Relationships are established via JOINs across normalized tables. **3. Comparative Example (1.5 marks):** Consider storing student records. *RDBMS (requires 3 tables):* - `STUDENT (rollNo PK, name, deptId FK)` - `DEPARTMENT (deptId PK, deptName)` - `SKILL (rollNo FK, skillName)` *MongoDB (single collection):* ```json { "_id": ObjectId("..."), "name": "Ananya", "dept": { "id": "CSE", "name": "Computer Science" }, "skills": ["MongoDB", "Python"] } ``` **4. Five Key Differences (1.5 marks):** | # | Aspect | RDBMS | MongoDB | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Structure | Tables with fixed schema | Collections of flexible BSON documents | | 2 | Relationships | JOINs (normalized) | Embedding or referencing | | 3 | Scalability | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (sharding) | | 4 | Transactions | Multi-row ACID | Atomic at single-document level only | | 5 | Data types | Limited (per column) | Rich BSON types (Date, Decimal128, ObjectId) | --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **embedded** and **referenced** approaches for modelling **1-to-many relationships** in MongoDB. When would you prefer one over the other? Illustrate with a **blog posts and comments** example. **Model Solution:** **1. Embedded Approach (2.5 marks):** In the embedded (denormalized) approach, the child documents are stored **inside the parent document** as an array of sub-documents. *Example — Blog post with comments embedded:* ```json { "_id": ObjectId("p101"), "title": "MongoDB Data Model", "author": "Ananya", "comments": [ { "user": "Rahul", "text": "Great!", "ts": ISODate("...") }, { "user": "Sneha", "text": "Helpful", "ts": ISODate("...") } ] } ``` **Advantages:** Single read retrieves post + all comments; atomic update of the entire post+comments tree. **Disadvantage:** Document grows with every new comment → risk of hitting the **16 MB** BSON limit for viral posts. **2. Referenced Approach (2.5 marks):** In the referenced (normalized) approach, the child documents live in a **separate collection**, and the parent stores only the IDs of the children. *Example — Two collections:* ```json // posts collection { "_id": ObjectId("p101"), "title": "MongoDB Data Model", "author": "Ananya" } // comments collection { "_id": ObjectId("c1"), "postId": ObjectId("p101"), "user": "Rahul", "text": "Great!" } { "_id": ObjectId("c2"), "postId": ObjectId("p101"), "user": "Sneha", "text": "Helpful" } ``` **Advantages:** Scales indefinitely; no document-size risk; children can be updated independently. **Disadvantage:** Requires either multiple queries or an `$lookup` aggregation to join post with comments. **3. Decision Criteria (2 marks):** | Use Embedding When | Use Referencing When | |---|---| | 1-to-1 or 1-to-few relationship | 1-to-many (unbounded) | | Children are **always** read with parent | Children accessed independently | | Atomicity of parent+children is required | Children are frequently updated individually | | Bounded total size (well below 16 MB) | Data may grow indefinitely | For the blog: if comments are **few** and the post is read together with them, **embed**. For a viral post expected to attract **thousands of comments**, **reference** (and consider the bucket pattern for very large lists). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **BSON data types** supported by MongoDB. Why is **Decimal128** preferred over **Double** for storing monetary values? **Model Solution:** **1. BSON Data Type Diagram (4 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TB root["BSON Type System"] root --> n["Numeric"] root --> t["Temporal"] root --> s["Structural"] root --> sp["Special"] n --> n1["Double (1) - 8 byte float"] n --> n2["Int32 (16) - 32-bit int"] n --> n3["Long (18) - 64-bit int"] n --> n4["Decimal128 (19) - 128-bit decimal"] t --> t1["Date (9) - 64-bit ms epoch"] s --> s1["String (2)"] s --> s2["Object (3)"] s --> s3["Array (4)"] s --> s4["Binary (5)"] sp --> sp1["ObjectId (7)"] sp --> sp2["Boolean (8)"] sp --> sp3["Null (10)"] ``` **2. Why Decimal128 over Double (3 marks):** - **Double** is a 64-bit IEEE-754 binary floating-point number. Many decimal fractions (e.g., `0.1`, `9.99`) **cannot be represented exactly** in binary, leading to rounding errors. For example, `0.1 + 0.2` in double equals `0.30000000000000004`. - For monetary calculations, even a microscopic rounding error accumulates and causes **financial discrepancies**. - **Decimal128** uses 128-bit **base-10** representation, storing every decimal digit exactly. It supports up to **34 significant digits**, sufficient for any practical financial value. - Hence, **Decimal128** is the *correct* BSON type for prices, balances, taxes, and grades. (Exam sentence: "Decimal128 ensures exact decimal arithmetic, eliminating floating-point rounding errors critical to financial integrity.") --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **structure of a 12-byte ObjectId**. Given the ObjectId `65a1b2c3d4e5f60017abcdef`, extract and interpret the **timestamp**, **machine/random**, and **counter** bytes. **Model Solution:** **1. ObjectId Structure (3 marks):** The ObjectId is a 12-byte (24 hex character) identifier auto-generated by the MongoDB driver. Its structure is: $$\text{ObjectId} = \underbrace{B_1 B_2 B_3 B_4}_{\text{4 bytes - timestamp}} \;\; \underbrace{B_5 B_6 B_7 B_8 B_9}_{\text{5 bytes - random}} \;\; \underbrace{B_{10} B_{11} B_{12}}_{\text{3 bytes - counter}}$$ - **4 bytes Timestamp** — seconds since Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC). Enables natural time-based sorting. - **5 bytes Random** — unique to the machine/process; ensures distinctness across servers. - **3 bytes Counter** — monotonic per-process counter, initialized randomly, increments for every new ObjectId; guarantees uniqueness even when multiple documents are inserted in the same second. **2. Decoding the Given ObjectId (4 marks):** Given: `65a1b2c3d4e5f60017abcdef` Split into 4 + 5 + 3 bytes: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Timestamp bytes:} \quad & \texttt{65 a1 b2 c3} \\ \text{Random bytes:} \quad & \texttt{d4 e5 f6 00 17} \\ \text{Counter bytes:} \quad & \texttt{ab cd ef} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Convert timestamp to decimal:** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{0x65a1b2c3} &= (6 \times 16^7) + (5 \times 16^6) + (10 \times 16^5) + (1 \times 16^4) \\ &\quad + (11 \times 16^3) + (2 \times 16^2) + (12 \times 16^1) + (3 \times 16^0) \\ &= 1\,701\,376\,195 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Convert Unix timestamp to UTC date:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{1\,701\,376\,195 s} &\approx \text{Tuesday, 28 November 2023, 11:23:15 UTC} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret random and counter:** - **Random bytes `d4e5f60017`** → a process-specific identifier that ensures that two different MongoDB clients (or processes) never produce the same ObjectId. - **Counter bytes `abcdef`** → a 24-bit value (0 to 16,777,215) that increments for each new document. The counter resets to a random value at the start of every second. **Step 4 — Significance:** This design makes the ObjectId **globally unique** *without* requiring a centralized authority, and **time-sortable** *without* a separate timestamp index — a brilliant example of distributed-system engineering. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Before Exam)** > > 1. **Writing "JSON" when you mean "BSON"** — Examiners deduct **1 full mark**. The correct term is BSON; JSON is only the external representation. > 2. **Saying "MongoDB documents are rows"** — WRONG. Documents are *not* rows. Documents are atomic, schema-less BSON objects. Lose 1 mark for this phrasing. > 3. **Forgetting the 16 MB document limit** — Whenever you propose embedding, you MUST justify that the data is *bounded* to keep the document below 16 MB. Otherwise, the examiner considers your design flawed. > 4. **Saying "ObjectId is a random 12-byte string"** — Partial credit only. Always state the **4+5+3** structure. Lose 0.5–1 mark if structure is missing. > 5. **Recommending embedding for many-to-many** — Wrong. Many-to-many MUST be referenced. Lose 1 mark. > 6. **Confusing `_id` with primary key only** — Mention that ObjectId is *also* time-sortable and *also* globally unique. This is the key differentiator from RDBMS primary keys. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **MongoDB Data Model** is a 4-level hierarchy: **Database → Collection → Document → Field**. - [x] **Document** is the atomic unit of CRUD; atomicity is guaranteed *only* at the single-document level. - [x] **Collection** is schema-less; two documents in the same collection can differ completely. - [x] **BSON** is the binary storage format; JSON is the human-readable external format. - [x] Key BSON types: `String(2)`, `Object(3)`, `Array(4)`, `ObjectId(7)`, `Boolean(8)`, `Date(9)`, `Long(18)`, `Decimal128(19)`. - [x] **ObjectId** = 4-byte timestamp + 5-byte random + 3-byte counter = **12 bytes**. - [x] ObjectId is **time-sortable** and **globally unique** without a central authority. - [x] **Decimal128** is mandatory for monetary / financial values to avoid floating-point error. - [x] **Embedding** suits 1-to-1 and 1-to-few bounded relationships. - [x] **Referencing** suits 1-to-many unbounded and many-to-many relationships. - [x] Document size limit: **16 MB**; this enforces design discipline. - [x] Default system databases: `admin`, `config`, `local`. - [x] Capped collections = fixed-size, FIFO eviction (used for logs). - [x] MongoDB is horizontally scalable via **sharding** (RDBMS is vertically scalable). ---

topics.title: CRUD Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CRUD Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **CRUD** is an acronym that stands for the four fundamental persistent storage functions that a database application must perform: **Create**, **Read**, **Update**, and **Delete**. In the context of **Advanced Database Systems (PECDT795)**, CRUD operations extend beyond basic relational SQL statements (INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE) to encompass operations across **distributed databases, NoSQL document stores, object-relational mappings, and transactional systems** governed by ACID properties.

In formal database theory, CRUD maps directly to the SQL Data Manipulation Language (DML) primitives:

| CRUD Acronym | SQL Equivalent | MongoDB Equivalent | Set Operation |
|--------------|----------------|--------------------|----|
| **C**reate | `INSERT` | `insertOne / insertMany` | Union / Add |
| **R**ead | `SELECT` | `find / findOne` | Selection / Projection |
| **U**pdate | `UPDATE` | `updateOne / updateMany` | Difference / Replace |
| **D**elete | `DELETE` | `deleteOne / deleteMany` | Minus / Drop |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Library Card Catalog System:**
> Imagine a massive library (your database). Every book is a **record**. The librarian (your DBMS) helps you perform four actions:
> - **CREATE** = Adding a brand-new book to the catalog and the shelf.
> - **READ** = Walking up to the catalog, searching by title/author, and retrieving the book's location.
> - **UPDATE** = Realizing the book has been republished under a new title — you erase the old card and write a new one.
> - **DELETE** = Removing the book entirely from the catalog (perhaps it was lost or withdrawn).
>
> Just as a library guarantees that when you update a card, the next person who reads it sees the *new* version (consistency), a database guarantees the same through **ACID transactions**.

In Advanced Database Systems, CRUD is not just about executing single statements — it is about ensuring that these four operations remain **consistent, isolated, atomic, and durable** even when the database is **distributed across multiple servers**, **replicated geographically**, or built on **non-relational (NoSQL) foundations**.

## 1.3 Why CRUD Matters in Advanced Database Systems

> [!NOTE]
> In **Advanced Database Systems**, CRUD operations are evaluated against:
> 1. **Concurrency Control** — What happens when two users UPDATE the same record simultaneously?
> 2. **Distribution Transparency** — Does the user need to know which shard/node holds the record?
> 3. **Consistency Models** — Strong (ACID) vs Eventual (BASE) consistency during CRUD.
> 4. **Performance Optimization** — Indexing, partitioning, and batching of CRUD operations.

The four operations are not merely syntactic — they encapsulate **fundamental relational algebra operations** that form the theoretical foundation of every query language.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> CRUD operations are studied in the context of **transactional integrity, distributed consistency (CAP theorem), document-oriented databases (MongoDB-style), and object-relational mapping (ORM) layers**. Students are expected to write advanced CRUD queries, understand their execution semantics, and reason about anomalies during concurrent execution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of CRUD — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 CREATE (Insert Operation)

The CREATE operation appends one or more tuples (rows/documents) to a relation (table/collection).

**Relational Form (SQL):**

$$\text{INSERT INTO } R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n) \text{ VALUES } (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n)$$

**Document Form (NoSQL — MongoDB syntax):**

```javascript
db.students.insertOne({
    "roll_no": "KTU2024CS101",
    "name": "Anand Krishnan",
    "cgpa": 8.7
})
```

**Why CREATE needs care:**
- Must satisfy **entity integrity** (Primary Key not NULL, unique).
- Must satisfy **referential integrity** (Foreign Key constraints).
- Must respect **domain constraints** (column data types).
- In distributed systems, CREATE may trigger **replication** to multiple nodes.

### 2.1.2 READ (Select Operation)

READ is technically a **read-only** operation and should never mutate state. In relational algebra:

$$R \bowtie S = \pi_{A}(\sigma_{\theta}(R \times S))$$

This means: take Cartesian product, filter by condition $\theta$, project desired attributes.

**Read Anomalies to Watch:**
- **Dirty Read** — Reading uncommitted data from another transaction.
- **Non-Repeatable Read** — Same query returns different values within one transaction.
- **Phantom Read** — New rows appear in repeated range queries.

### 2.1.3 UPDATE (Modify Operation)

UPDATE modifies existing tuples without changing their identity (Primary Key remains stable).

$$\text{UPDATE } R \text{ SET } A_i = f(A_1, \ldots, A_n) \text{ WHERE } \theta$$

**Update Anomalies:**
- **Lost Update Problem** — Two concurrent updates overwrite each other.
- **Write Skew** — Concurrent transactions read overlapping data and write disjoint updates, violating a global invariant.

### 2.1.4 DELETE (Remove Operation)

DELETE removes one or more tuples. In set theory:

$$R' = R - \sigma_{\theta}(R)$$

**Delete Anomalies:**
- Cascading deletes may inadvertently remove referenced tuples.
- In NoSQL, deletes are often **soft-deletes** (tombstones) before eventual physical purge.

## 2.2 CRUD in the Context of ACID Transactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every CRUD operation should, ideally, be executed within a **transaction** to guarantee ACID properties:

| Property | Meaning | CRUD Implication |
|----------|---------|-------|
| **Atomicity** | All-or-nothing execution | If DELETE fails halfway, none of it persists |
| **Consistency** | Valid state → Valid state | Every UPDATE maintains all integrity constraints |
| **Isolation** | Concurrent CRUD don't interfere | Two simultaneous UPDATEs don't corrupt data |
| **Durability** | Committed changes survive crashes | After CREATE commits, it survives power failure |

## 2.3 CRUD in Distributed Databases — CAP Implications

In a distributed setting, CRUD operations must choose between:

- **C**onsistency (every read receives the most recent write)
- **A**vailability (every request receives a response)
- **P**artition tolerance (system works despite network splits)

The **CAP Theorem** (Brewer's Conjecture, proved by Gilbert & Lynch) states that a distributed data store can satisfy **at most two** of these three guarantees simultaneously.

| Database Type | CAP Choice | CRUD Behavior |
|---------------|------------|----------------|
| RDBMS (PostgreSQL) | CA | Strong consistency, single-node |
| MongoDB (default) | CP | Strong consistency on primary |
| Cassandra | AP | Eventual consistency, high availability |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Variables | Unit / Notes |
|---------|------------------|-----------|-------|
| Insert Tuple | $\text{INSERT}(R, t)$ | $R$=relation, $t$=tuple | Cardinality: $|R| \leftarrow |R| + 1$ |
| Select Operation | $\sigma_{\theta}(R)$ | $\theta$=predicate | Selectivity $= \frac{\mid\sigma_{\theta}(R)\mid}{\mid R \mid}$ |
| Project Operation | $\pi_{A}(R)$ | $A$=attribute set | Removes duplicates |
| Cartesian Product | $R \times S$ | — | Result cardinality: $\mid R \mid \cdot \mid S \mid$ |
| Natural Join | $R \bowtie S$ | — | Eliminates duplicate attributes |
| Update Cost | $C_{upd} = C_{seek} + C_{write} \cdot n_{rows}$ | $n_{rows}$=rows affected | Measured in I/O blocks |
| ACID Transaction Time | $T_{total} = T_{read} + T_{validate} + T_{write}$ | All in ms | Includes lock acquisition |
| Replication Lag | $\Delta t_{rep} = t_{slave\_ack} - t_{master\_commit}$ | — | Critical for READ-after-WRITE |
| Two-Phase Commit | 2 RTT (Prepare + Commit) | RTT = round-trip-time | Atomic across shards |
| Quorum (Writes) | $W + R > N$ | $N$=replicas | Ensures consistency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Pipe Symbol:** In the formula sheet table, absolute values and set notations use $\vert$ rendering style (`\vert`) to avoid breaking markdown syntax — strictly per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 isolation protocol.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems** — CRUD must enforce strict ACID for monetary transactions.
- **E-Commerce** — Cart UPDATE/READ operations must be highly available (AP systems like Redis).
- **Social Networks** — CREATE-heavy workloads favor eventually consistent NoSQL (Cassandra, DynamoDB).
- **IoT Systems** — Time-series CRUD requires specialized stores (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB).
- **Healthcare Records** — CRUD must maintain full audit trails via versioning + triggers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Bulk CRUD Operations

Consider a relation $R$ with $B_R$ disk blocks and $T_R$ tuples. We want to derive the I/O cost for the four CRUD operations.

### 3.1.1 Cost of CREATE (Bulk Insert)

To insert $k$ new tuples into a relation $R$ that has $B_R$ blocks:

**Step 1:** Identify the blocks that will contain the new tuples (typically appending at the end of the heap file).

**Step 2:** Compute the number of new blocks needed.

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{new} &= \left\lceil \frac{k}{b_{fr}} \right\rceil \\
C_{insert} &= C_{seek} \cdot B_{new} + C_{seq\_write} \cdot B_R
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $b_{fr}$ = blocking factor = number of tuples per block = $\lfloor \frac{B_{block}}{T_{size}} \rfloor$
- $B_{block}$ = block size (typically 4096 bytes or **4 KB**)
- $C_{seek}$ = cost of one disk seek (~**5 ms** for HDD, ~**0.1 ms** for SSD)

**Step 3:** If the relation is indexed, each insert may also require $\log_2(B_R)$ index block updates.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{insert\_indexed} &= C_{insert} + \log_2(B_R) \cdot C_{seek}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Conversion Logic:** Suppose $B_R = 1000$ blocks, $T_{size} = 200$ bytes, $B_{block} = 4096$ bytes. Then $b_{fr} = \lfloor 4096/200 \rfloor = 20$ tuples/block. For $k = 500$ inserts: $B_{new} = \lceil 500/20 \rceil = 25$ new blocks.

### 3.1.2 Cost of READ (Selection)

For a selection $\sigma_{A=c}(R)$ on attribute $A$:

**Case A — Linear Search (No Index):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{linear} &= B_R \quad \text{(scan all blocks)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Case B — Equality on Primary Key (with B+ Tree Index):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{PK\_eq} &= D \cdot (C_{seek} + C_{transfer})
\end{aligned}$$

Where $D$ = depth of B+ tree, typically $\log_{\lceil m/2 \rceil}(B_R)$.

**Case C — Range Query on Sorted Attribute:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{range} &= C_{seek} + \frac{s}{b_{fr}} \cdot C_{transfer}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $s$ = selectivity (number of matching tuples).

### 3.1.3 Cost of UPDATE

For an UPDATE on $k$ tuples selected via condition $\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{update} &= C_{read}(\theta) + k \cdot C_{write} + \Delta_{index}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $C_{read}(\theta)$ = cost to locate matching tuples (per Section 3.1.2).
- $k \cdot C_{write}$ = writing $k$ modified tuples back.
- $\Delta_{index}$ = cost to update all secondary indexes pointing to modified rows.

### 3.1.4 Cost of DELETE

For a DELETE of $k$ tuples:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{delete} &= C_{read}(\theta) + k \cdot C_{write} + k \cdot C_{index\_update}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, DELETE is often implemented as a **logical delete** (UPDATE a `is_deleted` flag) to reduce I/O and allow recovery. Physical deletion happens during compaction.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — CRUD vs Consistency Model

| CRUD Operation | Strict ACID (RDBMS) | Eventual Consistency (NoSQL) | Causal Consistency |
|---------------|--------------------|-----|---------------------|
| **CREATE** | Synchronous commit + replication | Async replication, returns immediately | Sync to causally related nodes |
| **READ (own writes)** | Always sees latest | Requires **read-your-writes** session | Guaranteed |
| **UPDATE** | Locked transaction | Last-write-wins (LWW) | Preserves cause-effect |
| **DELETE** | Tombstone + cascade | Tombstone with TTL | Tombstone with vector clock |

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimistic vs Pessimistic CRUD Concurrency

### 3.3.1 Pessimistic (Locking)

Two-phase locking (2PL) ensures serializability but may deadlock.

**Lock Compatibility Matrix:**

| | Held: S | Held: X |
|-----|---------|---------|
| **Request: S** | ✓ Allow | ✗ Block |
| **Request: X** | ✗ Block | ✗ Block |

### 3.3.2 Optimistic (Validation)

Three-phase protocol:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{read} + T_{validate} + T_{write}
\end{aligned}$$

Validation rule for transaction $T_i$ against committed $T_j$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Validate}(T_i) = & \left( \text{WS}(T_i) \cap \text{WS}(T_j) = \emptyset \right) \\
& \lor \left( \text{RS}(T_i) \cap \text{WS}(T_j) = \emptyset \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Where WS = Write Set, RS = Read Set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $T_i$ validates if either it writes nothing that $T_j$ wrote, or it doesn't read anything that $T_j$ wrote. This ensures $T_i$'s reads remain consistent.

## 3.4 Exhaustive CRUD Execution Trace (SQL Example)

Consider the relational schema:

$$Student(\underline{RollNo}, Name, CGPA, DeptId)$$
$$Department(\underline{DeptId}, DeptName)$$

**Task:** Perform a transactional CRUD sequence — Enroll a new student, then UPDATE their CGPA, then DELETE if CGPA < 0 (invalid).

**SQL Trace:**

```sql
BEGIN TRANSACTION;

-- CREATE
INSERT INTO Student (RollNo, Name, CGPA, DeptId)
VALUES ('KTU2024CS001', 'Rahul M', 8.5, 'CS');

-- READ (Verify)
SELECT * FROM Student WHERE RollNo = 'KTU2024CS001';

-- UPDATE
UPDATE Student SET CGPA = 9.1 WHERE RollNo = 'KTU2024CS001';

-- DELETE (hypothetical invalid case)
DELETE FROM Student WHERE CGPA < 0;

COMMIT;
```

**Step-by-step execution trace:**

1. `BEGIN` → Transaction Manager creates a new TXID.
2. `INSERT` → Acquires **X-lock** on new row + IX-lock on table. Writes to log buffer.
3. `SELECT` → Acquires **S-lock**, reads from buffer pool.
4. `UPDATE` → Upgrades to **X-lock**, writes new tuple version (MVCC) or in-place.
5. `DELETE` → Marks tuple as deleted (or physically removes).
6. `COMMIT` → Flushes log to disk (Write-Ahead Log), releases all locks.

---
