# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CRUD Operation Flow — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    appLayer[Application Layer ORM Query API]
    parser[SQL NoSQL Query Parser]
    optimizer[Query Optimizer]
    planner[Execution Plan Generator]
    txMgr[Transaction Manager ACID Controller]
    lockMgr[Lock Manager Concurrency Control]
    logMgr[Log Manager Write Ahead Log]
    bufferPool[Buffer Pool Cache Memory]
    storage[Disk Storage Tables Indexes]
    replication[Replication Manager Distributed Nodes]

    appLayer --> parser
    parser --> optimizer
    optimizer --> planner
    planner --> txMgr
    txMgr --> lockMgr
    txMgr --> logMgr
    planner --> bufferPool
    bufferPool --> storage
    txMgr --> replication

    subgraph CREATE_OP[CREATE Path]
        c1[Parse INSERT] --> c2[Validate Constraints] --> c3[Acquire X Locks] --> c4[Write to Buffer] --> c5[Log + Commit] --> c6[Replicate]
    end

    subgraph READ_OP[READ Path]
        r1[Parse SELECT] --> r2[Generate Plan] --> r3[Acquire S Locks] --> r4[Read from Buffer or Disk] --> r5[Return Result Set]
    end

    subgraph UPDATE_OP[UPDATE Path]
        u1[Parse UPDATE] --> u2[Locate Rows Index Scan] --> u3[Upgrade to X Locks] --> u4[Modify Tuples] --> u5[Update Indexes] --> u6[Log + Commit] --> u7[Replicate]
    end

    subgraph DELETE_OP[DELETE Path]
        d1[Parse DELETE] --> d2[Locate Rows] --> d3[Acquire X Locks] --> d4[Mark Deleted or Remove] --> d5[Update Indexes] --> d6[Log + Commit] --> d7[Replicate or Tombstone]
    end
```

## 4.2 CRUD vs ACID Transaction Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BeginTx
    BeginTx --> Active: BEGIN TRANSACTION
    Active --> Active: CRUD Operations INSERT SELECT UPDATE DELETE
    Active --> PartiallyCommitted: Final CRUD Done
    PartiallyCommitted --> Committed: COMMIT Log Flushed
    PartiallyCommitted --> Failed: ROLLBACK or Crash
    Active --> Failed: Deadlock or Constraint Violation
    Failed --> Aborted: Rollback Complete
    Committed --> [*]: Success Durability Achieved
    Aborted --> [*]: No State Change Persisted
```

## 4.3 Distributed CRUD — CAP Trade-off Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[New CRUD Request Arrives]
    start --> capCheck{Network Partition Detected}
    capCheck -->|Yes| apChoice[Favor Availability AP]
    capCheck -->|No| healthyChoice[Normal Operation]

    apChoice --> noSQLPath[Use Cassandra DynamoDB Style Async Replication Eventual Consistency]
    healthyChoice --> strongPath{Strong Consistency Required}

    strongPath -->|Yes Banking Inventory| sqlPath[Use PostgreSQL MySQL Distributed SQL CockroachDB Strong Consistency CP or CA]
    strongPath -->|No Social Media Feeds| noSQLPath
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CRUD in a Distributed NoSQL Cluster

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph clientTier[Client Tier]
        c1[Client Application 1]
        c2[Client Application 2]
    end

    subgraph coordTier[Coordinator Tier]
        coord1[Query Coordinator Node 1]
        coord2[Query Coordinator Node 2]
    end

    subgraph shardTier[Sharded Storage Tier]
        shardA[Shard A Primary Node]
        shardA1[Shard A Replica 1]
        shardA2[Shard A Replica 2]
        shardB[Shard B Primary Node]
        shardB1[Shard B Replica 1]
        shardB2[Shard B Replica 2]
    end

    c1 --> coord1
    c2 --> coord2

    coord1 -->|Hash Key Routing| shardA
    coord1 -->|Hash Key Routing| shardB

    shardA -.->|Async Replication| shardA1
    shardA -.->|Async Replication| shardA2
    shardB -.->|Async Replication| shardB1
    shardB -.->|Async Replication| shardB2

    coord1 --> quorumCheck{Consensus Quorum W plus R greater than N}
    quorumCheck -->|Met| commit[Commit CRUD Write]
    quorumCheck -->|Not Met| retry[Retry or Fail]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (`node1`, `shardA`, `coord1`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone IDs. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown formatting inside quoted labels, per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define CRUD operations. Explain how they map to SQL DML statements with an example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

CRUD stands for **Create, Read, Update, Delete** — the four basic persistent storage operations supported by any DBMS. They map to SQL Data Manipulation Language (DML) statements as follows:

1. **Create** → `INSERT INTO Students (RollNo, Name) VALUES ('CS101', 'Anu');`
2. **Read** → `SELECT Name, CGPA FROM Students WHERE DeptId = 'CS';`
3. **Update** → `UPDATE Students SET CGPA = 9.0 WHERE RollNo = 'CS101';`
4. **Delete** → `DELETE FROM Students WHERE RollNo = 'CS101';`

These four operations form the backbone of all database interactions and must be supported with proper **ACID guarantees** in advanced systems. **(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for SQL mapping)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between strong consistency and eventual consistency in the context of distributed CRUD operations?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In **strong consistency**, every successful CREATE, UPDATE, or DELETE operation is immediately visible to all subsequent READ operations across all nodes. This guarantees that the system behaves as a single logical copy. Example: Google Spanner, traditional RDBMS.

In **eventual consistency**, after a CRUD write completes, the change **propagates asynchronously** to replicas, and reads may return stale data temporarily. The system guarantees convergence if no new writes occur. Example: Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra.

Strong consistency favors **CP** of the CAP theorem, while eventual consistency favors **AP**. **(3 marks — 1.5 each for definitions + examples)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the ACID properties of database transactions. How do CRUD operations benefit from these properties? Illustrate with a banking example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the CAP theorem and explain how CRUD operations are affected under each of the three trade-off configurations (CA, CP, AP). Provide one real-world database example for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**ACID Properties Explained:**

1. **Atomicity** (1.5 marks): A transaction is an all-or-nothing unit. If any part of the CRUD sequence fails, the entire transaction is rolled back.

2. **Consistency** (1.5 marks): The database transitions from one valid state to another. All integrity constraints remain satisfied.

3. **Isolation** (1.5 marks): Concurrent transactions appear to execute serially. Intermediate states of one transaction are invisible to others.

4. **Durability** (1 mark): Once a transaction commits, its effects survive system crashes.

**Banking Illustration (1.5 marks):**

Consider a fund transfer of **₹5000** from Account A to Account B. The CRUD sequence is:

```sql
BEGIN TRANSACTION;
UPDATE Accounts SET Balance = Balance - 5000 WHERE AccNo = 'A';   -- CREATE/UPDATE
UPDATE Accounts SET Balance = Balance + 5000 WHERE AccNo = 'B';   -- UPDATE
COMMIT;
```

- **Atomicity:** If the system crashes after the first UPDATE but before the second, the rollback ensures no money is lost.
- **Consistency:** Total balance across both accounts remains constant.
- **Isolation:** A concurrent balance inquiry doesn't see a half-deducted state.
- **Durability:** After commit, the transfer survives even a power failure.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**CAP Theorem Statement (2 marks):**
Proposed by Eric Brewer (2000), proved by Gilbert and Lynch (2002): In a distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **at most two** of the three properties — Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance.

**Three Configurations:**

| Configuration | Meaning | Real-World Example | CRUD Behavior |
|---------------|---------|-------|-------|
| **CA** (1.5 marks) | Consistent + Available, but no partition tolerance. Single-node or tightly coupled clusters. | **Traditional RDBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL)**, Oracle RAC | All CRUD synchronous; failure of network partitions halts service. |
| **CP** (1.5 marks) | Consistent + Partition tolerant. Sacrifices availability during partitions. | **MongoDB (with majority writeConcern)**, HBase, Redis Cluster | Writes may fail if majority quorum not reachable; reads always return latest committed value. |
| **AP** (1.5 marks) | Available + Partition tolerant. Sacrifices consistency during partitions. | **Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra, CouchDB** | Writes always succeed; reads may return stale data; eventual convergence via anti-entropy. |
| Synthesis (0.5 marks) | All real distributed systems are P (partition is inevitable), so the real choice is between C and A. |

**Conclusion:** CRUD semantics must be designed based on application's CAP preference — banking prefers CP, social feeds prefer AP.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of CRUD operations in NoSQL document databases with MongoDB-style syntax. Compare and contrast CRUD in relational vs document databases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Lost Update Problem and Write Skew anomaly in concurrent CRUD execution. Explain how Two-Phase Locking (2PL) and Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC) solve these issues. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**CRUD in NoSQL Document Databases (3 marks):**

In MongoDB (a document-oriented NoSQL store), CRUD is performed on JSON-like BSON documents:

```javascript
// CREATE
db.students.insertOne({ "_id": 1, "name": "Priya", "cgpa": 8.9 })

// READ
db.students.find({ "cgpa": { $gt: 8.0 } })

// UPDATE
db.students.updateOne({ "_id": 1 }, { $set: { "cgpa": 9.2 } })

// DELETE
db.students.deleteOne({ "_id": 1 })
```

**Relational vs Document CRUD Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Relational (SQL) | Document (NoSQL) |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **Schema** | Fixed, predefined schema enforced | Schema-less or schema-on-read, flexible |
| **Data Model** | Rows in normalized tables | Hierarchical JSON documents |
| **CREATE** | `INSERT` with explicit column list | `insertOne`/`insertMany` with free-form fields |
| **READ** | `JOIN`s across multiple tables | Embedded sub-documents or `$lookup` |
| **UPDATE** | `UPDATE ... SET ... WHERE` with full row replacement | `updateOne` with `$set` operators modifying specific fields |
| **DELETE** | `DELETE` with cascading foreign keys | `deleteOne` with optional tombstone retention |
| **Transactions** | Full ACID support | Limited ACID (MongoDB 4.0+ supports multi-doc txns) |
| **Scaling** | Vertical scaling; horizontal via sharding | Horizontal scaling native (partition by document key) |
| **Consistency** | Strong consistency | Eventual or tunable consistency |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Lost Update Problem (2 marks):**

When two concurrent transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$ read the same value $x$ and update it based on the read value, the second update overwrites the first.

**Example:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{READ}(x) \rightarrow 100 \\
T_2 &: \text{READ}(x) \rightarrow 100 \\
T_1 &: \text{UPDATE } x \leftarrow x + 10 \rightarrow x = 110 \\
T_2 &: \text{UPDATE } x \leftarrow x + 20 \rightarrow x = 120 \quad \text{(overwrites } T_1\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Expected:** $x = 130$, **Actual:** $x = 120$ — loss of 10.

**Write Skew Anomaly (2 marks):**

Two transactions read overlapping data, but write to disjoint sets, violating a global invariant. Example: Two doctors each on-call; both read "two on-call required" and both simultaneously UPDATE themselves off-call. Invariant violated.

**Two-Phase Locking (2PL) Solution (1.5 marks):**

- **Growing Phase:** Acquire locks, no releases.
- **Shrinking Phase:** Release locks, no acquisitions.
- For Lost Update: $T_2$ waits for $T_1$'s X-lock → no overwrite.
- For Write Skew: requires **predicate locks** or **index locks**.

**Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC) Solution (1.5 marks):**

Three phases:
1. **Read Phase:** Track Read Set (RS) and Write Set (WS) locally.
2. **Validation Phase:** Check $\text{WS}(T_i) \cap \text{WS}(T_j) = \emptyset$.
3. **Write Phase:** Apply changes if validation passes; abort and retry otherwise.

- For Lost Update: Validation detects conflict and aborts one transaction.
- For Write Skew: Validation must check **predicate reads** against other transactions' writes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in CRUD Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DDL with DML** — CREATE in SQL (DDL) vs INSERT (DML). When the question asks for CRUD in SQL, always show `INSERT`, not `CREATE TABLE`.
> 2. **Missing ACID linkage** — A CRUD answer without mentioning transactions/integrity constraints is incomplete. Always tie CRUD to **atomicity, isolation, durability**.
> 3. **Forgetting consistency models** — When discussing distributed CRUD, students often skip the **CAP theorem** or **BASE vs ACID** distinction. Examiners specifically test this awareness in Module 3.
> 4. **No diagrams for CRUD flow** — Always include a labeled diagram of the CRUD execution pipeline (Parser → Optimizer → Executor). Diagrams earn **1–2 easy marks**.
> 5. **Ignoring concurrency anomalies** — A CRUD discussion without **Lost Update, Dirty Read, Phantom Read, Write Skew** is treated as superficial. Mention these by name.
> 6. **Incorrect CAP configuration mapping** — Remember: traditional RDBMS = CA, MongoDB = CP, Cassandra = AP. Mis-mapping is a frequent deduction.
> 7. **Skipping the SQL/NoSQL syntax** — In Advanced DBMS, you MUST show both SQL and NoSQL CRUD syntax when asked. One without the other loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CRUD Operations (KTU PECDT795 Module 3):**

- ✅ **CRUD** = Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four fundamental persistent operations.
- ✅ **SQL Mapping:** `INSERT`, `SELECT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`.
- ✅ **NoSQL Mapping:** `insertOne`, `find`, `updateOne`, `deleteOne` (MongoDB).
- ✅ **Relational Algebra Mapping:** Insertion, Selection/Projection, Modification, Minus.
- ✅ **ACID:** Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (no interference), Durability (survives crashes).
- ✅ **CAP Theorem:** Distributed systems can guarantee at most 2 of {C, A, P}.
- ✅ **Configurations:** CA = RDBMS, CP = MongoDB, AP = Cassandra.
- ✅ **Consistency Models:** Strong (immediate) vs Eventual (async convergence).
- ✅ **Concurrency Anomalies:** Lost Update, Dirty Read, Non-Repeatable Read, Phantom Read, Write Skew.
- ✅ **Solutions:** 2PL (pessimistic locking) and OCC (validation-based).
- ✅ **CRUD Cost Models:** Linear scan vs indexed access; bulk operations scale with blocking factor $b_{fr}$.
- ✅ **Document vs Relational:** Documents favor embedded data and horizontal scaling; relational favors normalization and joins.
- ✅ **Tombstones:** Logical deletes in NoSQL preserve recovery and replication guarantees.
- ✅ **Two-Phase Commit:** Atomic CRUD across distributed shards requires 2 RTT (Prepare + Commit).
- ✅ **Quorum Rule:** $W + R > N$ ensures read-write consistency in replicated systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a CRUD question appears in KTU ESE, immediately structure your answer around: **(1) Definition, (2) SQL + NoSQL syntax, (3) ACID/Tx context, (4) Distributed considerations, (5) Concurrency + anomalies, (6) Diagram**. A well-structured 14-mark answer with these six sections always scores above 12.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indexing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Indexing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Indexing (KTU 2024 Definition):** An **index** is an auxiliary access structure (separate file or data structure) built on one or more attributes of a relation (table) to provide a fast, ordered, and efficient path to the physical location of data records on secondary storage, thereby accelerating record retrieval without requiring a full sequential scan of the relation. In the relational model, an index is formally defined as an ordered (or hashed) collection of entries of the form $\langle k, ptr \rangle$, where: - $k$ is the **search key** (the indexed attribute value), and - $ptr$ is a **pointer** (block address / record id / block id) referencing the actual tuple. The indexed attribute may or may not be the primary key. If the index is built on the **primary key**, it is called a **Primary Index**; otherwise, a **Secondary Index**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Book Index > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a 1,000-page engineering textbook. If you want to find the section on "B+ Trees," would you flip page by page from cover to cover? **Absolutely not.** You go to the *back of the book* (the index), find the keyword "B+ Trees" with the corresponding page numbers, and jump directly to those pages. That is exactly what a database index does — it is the **"back-of-the-book index"** of a table that points directly to the rows containing the data you want. The same book also has: - A **Table of Contents** (small, fast scan) → resembles a **Primary Index / Clustering Index** (small, ordered). - A **Glossary** (alphabetical) → resembles a **Secondary Index**. - A **Multi-volume Encyclopedia** with a meta-index → resembles a **Multi-Level Index**. ## 1.3 Taxonomy of Indexes Indexes are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: | Axis | Classification | Meaning | |------|----------------|---------| | **Key Type** | Primary vs Secondary | Whether the search key uniquely identifies a record (candidate key) or not | | **File Organization** | Clustering vs Non-Clustering | Whether the data file is physically sorted on the search key | | **Access Method** | Ordered (Tree) vs Hash | Whether the index entries are stored in a sorted structure (B+ Tree) or in buckets via a hash function | ## 1.4 Performance Goal (Why Indexing Exists) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Cardinal Rule of Indexing:** A linear search over a relation of $N$ blocks requires $O(N)$ disk I/Os. An index reduces this to $O(\log_F N)$ for tree-based indexes or $O(1)$ (average) for hash-based indexes, where $F$ is the fan-out (branching factor) of the index. **Disk I/O is the bottleneck**, not CPU — so reducing block accesses is the *only* thing that matters. In modern DBMS engines (PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL InnoDB), the default and most commonly used index structure is the **B+ Tree** because it provides excellent worst-case guarantees for both lookups and range queries. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Indexes (Detailed) ### A. Primary Index (Clustered Index on a Sorted File) - Built on the **primary key** (an ordered, unique, non-null attribute). - The data file itself is **physically sorted** on the primary key. - The index is a **sparse index**: it stores one entry per **data block** (not per record). The key value stored is the **first key of each block**. - Lookup of a key $k$ requires only **binary search** on the index, then one more I/O for the data block. ### B. Clustering Index (on a Non-Unique Key) - Data file is **physically sorted** on a non-unique attribute (e.g., `dept_id` in an Employee table). - The index entry stores the **first key of each distinct value plus the block pointer**. - Sparse — but multiple records may share the same search key. ### C. Secondary Index (Non-Clustered / Non-Sorted File) - The data file is **not sorted** on the search key. - The index must be **dense** (one entry per record) because no block-level grouping is possible. - May have **duplicate keys**; thus secondary indexes are typically **dense + ordered by (key, record_id)**. - A secondary index file itself is usually a B+ Tree. ### D. Multi-Level Index - A single-level index may still be too large to fit in memory. - Solution: **Treat the index file as a data file and build an index on the index.** - This is exactly what a **B+ Tree** does implicitly: the non-leaf levels are a multi-level index on the leaf-level entries. ## 2.2 Ordered Indexes — Operational Steps **Search Algorithm (Primary Index on sorted file of $b_r$ data blocks, with first-level index of $b_i$ blocks):** 1. Read first-level index into memory (cost: $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil$ block accesses using binary search). 2. Identify the data block containing the target key. 3. Fetch that data block (cost: 1 I/O). 4. **Total cost:** $\lceil \log_2 b_i \rceil + 1$ block accesses. **Insertion (Primary Index, sorted file):** 1. Locate the correct position using the index. 2. Insert the record; if the block overflows, perform a **block split** with redistribution. 3. Update the first-level index if a new block is created. **Deletion (Primary Index):** 1. Locate the block using the index. 2. Delete the record; if the block falls below $\approx 50\%$ occupancy, **redistribute** with a sibling or **merge** blocks. 3. Update the first-level index if a block becomes empty and is removed. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are evaluated as the number of **disk block I/Os** (block accesses).** In KTU board exams, marks are awarded for setting up the right formula even if arithmetic is wrong — so **memorize the formulas**, not the numbers. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---------|----------------------|---------------| | 1 | Blocks needed for a relation of $r$ records | $b_r = \lceil r / bfr \rceil$ | $bfr$ = blocking factor (records per block) | | 2 | Index size (dense) | $b_i = \lceil r / bfr_i \rceil$ | $bfr_i$ = index entries per block | | 3 | Index size (sparse, primary) | $b_i = \lceil b_r / bfr_i \rceil$ | one entry per data block | | 4 | **Binary search I/Os** | $\lceil \log_2 b \rceil$ | over $b$ blocks | | 5 | **B+ Tree search cost** | $\lceil \log_{\lceil m/2 \rceil} N \rceil + 1$ | order $m$, $N$ = number of records | | 6 | **B+ Tree fan-out** | $F = \text{pointer count per non-leaf node}$ | $F \approx$ hundreds | | 7 | **Hash index — average cost** | $O(1)$ expected (1 I/O) | worst case: $O(N)$ with poor hash | | 8 | **Multi-level index search** | $\lceil \log_2 b_{i1} \rceil + \lceil \log_2 b_{i2} \rceil + \dots + 1$ | sum across levels | | 9 | **Linear scan cost** | $b_r$ | always the baseline | | 10 | B+ Tree node fill range | $\lceil m/2 \rceil \le$ keys per node $\le m$ | guarantees min **50%** space utilization | ### 2.3.1 Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in the real world:** Every query optimizer in a production DBMS (PostgreSQL's planner, MySQL's optimizer, Oracle's CBO) **estimates cost in disk block accesses** using formulas like the ones above. When a DBA asks *"Should I add an index on column X?"* the answer comes from comparing the linear-scan cost ($b_r$) with the indexed cost ($\log$-scale formula). **This is exactly the calculation KTU tests in the 14-mark questions.** ## 2.4 B+ Tree Indexing — The Workhorse A B+ Tree of **order $m$** (or degree $m$) has the following invariants: 1. Every **internal (non-leaf) node** has at most $m$ children and at least $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ children. 2. Every **internal node** (except the root) has at least $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ keys and at most $m-1$ keys. 3. The **root** has at least 2 children (if it is not a leaf). 4. A node with $k$ keys has exactly $k+1$ children (or $k$ pointers if a leaf). 5. All **leaves appear at the same level** — this is what makes B+ Trees *balanced*. 6. **Leaf nodes** are linked in a **sibling-pointer chain** to enable **efficient range queries** ($O(\log N + k)$ for $k$ matching records). ### B+ Tree vs B-Tree — The Two Key Differences | Property | B-Tree | B+ Tree | |----------|--------|---------| | Data pointers location | Internal + leaf nodes | **Leaf nodes only** | | Leaf linkage | Not linked | **Siblings linked** (range queries) | | Internal node payload | More data ⇒ lower fan-out | Pure routing ⇒ **higher fan-out** | | Search for missing key | May stop at internal node | Must reach leaf | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mantra:** *"B+ Tree = B-Tree + all data at leaves + sibling pointers."* This is the single most-tested line on indexing in the KTU syllabus. ### Fan-Out of a B+ Tree (Detailed Calculation) For a node of size $B$ bytes: - Pointer size: $p$ bytes - Search key size: $key\_size$ bytes Maximum number of pointers (order $m$) in an internal node: $$m = \left\lfloor \frac{B + p}{p + key\_size} \right\rfloor$$ This is the **fan-out** $F$. With $F$ in the hundreds (typical for a 4 KB block with 4-byte pointers and 4-byte keys: $F = 1024$), the height of a B+ Tree indexing 1 billion records is only $\log_{512}(10^9) \approx 3.3$, i.e., **3 disk accesses**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Index Levels in a B+ Tree > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Problem:** *"Given a relation with $N$ records, blocking factor $bfr$, index order $m$, derive the number of block accesses for a key search."* ### Step 1 — Determine the number of leaf-level entries. For a **dense B+ Tree leaf level**, there is one leaf entry per record. Each leaf node can hold up to $m-1$ entries (since the order $m$ means $m$ pointers). $$L = \left\lceil \frac{N}{m-1} \right\rceil \quad \text{(number of leaf nodes)}$$ ### Step 2 — Determine the number of nodes at each internal level. Each internal node can have at most $m$ children. So the number of nodes at the level just above the leaves is: $$L_1 = \left\lceil \frac{L}{m} \right\rceil$$ At the level above that: $$L_2 = \left\lceil \frac{L_1}{m} \right\rceil$$ Continue until only the root remains: $L_h = 1$. ### Step 3 — Total number of block accesses for a search. The number of levels $h$ is precisely the number of internal levels **plus one** for the leaf level. A successful search reads exactly one node per level: $$\text{Block accesses for search} = h + 1 \quad \text{(for the data block, if using a clustering index)}$$ If the index itself contains the row data (a "covering" / "index-organized" table, as in MySQL InnoDB or DuckDB), no extra data-block fetch is needed: $$\text{Block accesses for search} = h \quad \text{(for a covering B+ Tree)}$$ ### Step 3.4 — Worked Numerical Example (Most Likely KTU Question) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Textbook Example:** A relation has $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ records, blocking factor $bfr = 10$ records/block, B+ Tree order $m = 100$. #### Step A — Number of data blocks. $$b_r = \left\lceil \frac{N}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{10} \right\rceil = 100{,}000 \text{ data blocks}$$ #### Step B — Number of leaf-level index entries (dense leaf). $$L = \left\lceil \frac{N}{m-1} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{99} \right\rceil \approx 10{,}101 \text{ leaf nodes}$$ #### Step C — Number of internal nodes at level 1. $$L_1 = \left\lceil \frac{L}{m} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{10{,}101}{100} \right\rceil = 102 \text{ nodes}$$ #### Step D — Number of internal nodes at level 2. $$L_2 = \left\lceil \frac{102}{100} \right\rceil = 2 \text{ nodes}$$ #### Step E — Root. $$L_3 = 1 \text{ root node}$$ #### Step F — Total number of levels. $$h = 3 \text{ internal levels} + 1 \text{ leaf level} = 4 \text{ levels}$$ #### Step G — Block accesses. $$\text{Block accesses per search} = h = 4$$ #### Step H — Comparison with linear scan. $$\text{Linear scan} = b_r = 100{,}000 \text{ block accesses}$$ $$\text{Speedup factor} = \frac{100{,}000}{4} = 25{,}000\times$$ This is a **25,000× speedup** — and that is precisely why indexing is the *single most important* physical design decision in any database. ## 3.2 Derivation: Hash-Based Indexing — Cost Analysis ### Step 1 — Hash function properties. A hash function $h: K \rightarrow [0, M-1]$ maps a key $k$ to one of $M$ bucket addresses. **KTU expects these three properties:** 1. **Determinism:** $h(k)$ is the same every time. 2. **Uniformity:** $h$ distributes keys uniformly across buckets. 3. **Minimal collisions:** Distinct keys should map to distinct buckets with high probability. ### Step 2 — Bucket overflow handling. If two distinct keys $k_1 \neq k_2$ satisfy $h(k_1) = h(k_2)$, a **collision** occurs. Two strategies: | Strategy | Description | Trade-off | |----------|-------------|-----------| | **Open Addressing (Probing)** | Find next empty slot in the same bucket array | Fast, but degrades with load factor $\alpha > 0.7$ | | **Separate Chaining (Overflow Buckets)** | Append a linked list of overflow blocks | Robust, no clustering, slight pointer overhead | ### Step 3 — Average search cost. Let $n$ = number of records, $M$ = number of primary buckets. Define the **load factor**: $$\alpha = \frac{n}{M \cdot b_{bucket}}$$ where $b_{bucket}$ is the number of records per bucket block. For a uniform hash with chaining: $$\text{Expected search cost} = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2} \text{ block accesses}$$ For $\alpha \approx 1$, this is roughly $1.5$ block accesses on average — the legendary "$O(1)$" lookup that drives NoSQL key-value stores (Redis, DynamoDB, Riak). ### Step 4 — Why hashing cannot answer range queries. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse hash and B+ Tree indexes. **Hash indexes can answer equality lookups efficiently, but they CANNOT answer range queries** like `SELECT * FROM emp WHERE salary BETWEEN 50000 AND 60000`. The hash function destroys the ordering of keys — adjacent values in the range can be scattered across all buckets. **B+ Trees can answer range queries in $O(\log N + k)$ time** because leaves are linked and sorted. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: B+ Tree vs Hash vs ISAM | Property | ISAM | B+ Tree | Hash | |----------|------|---------|------| | **Structure** | Static multi-level index | Dynamic, self-balancing | Bucket array + chaining | | **Search cost** | $O(\log_F N)$ | $O(\log_F N)$ | $O(1)$ expected, $O(N)$ worst | | **Insert cost** | Fast but may overflow | Node splits, $O(\log_F N)$ | Bucket overflow, $O(1)$ expected | | **Delete cost** | No merge (tombstones) | Merge/redistribute, $O(\log_F N)$ | Lazy, no merge | | **Range queries** | Yes (sorted) | **Yes (best)** | **No** | | **Static vs Dynamic** | Static | Dynamic | Dynamic | | **Overflow chains** | Yes (long chains degrade) | Built-in via splits | Yes (chaining) | | **Modern usage** | Legacy, replaced by B+ Tree | **Default in all RDBMS** | Used in KV stores, join algorithms | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 B+ Tree Block-Level Architecture (Mermaid) The following diagram depicts a B+ Tree of order 4, indexing the `emp_id` attribute of an `Employee` relation. Leaf nodes are linked left-to-right for range-query support. ```mermaid graph TD A[ROOT NODE
keys 30 60 80
ptrs P1 P2 P3 P4] B[INTERNAL L1
keys 10 20
ptrs P1 P2 P3] C[INTERNAL L1
keys 40 50
ptrs P1 P2 P3] D[INTERNAL L1
keys 65 75
ptrs P1 P2 P3] E[INTERNAL L1
keys 85 90
ptrs P1 P2 P3] F[LEAF L2
keys 5 8
data ptrs + sibling ptr] G[LEAF L2
keys 10 15 20
data ptrs + sibling ptr] H[LEAF L2
keys 30 35
data ptrs + sibling ptr] I[LEAF L2
keys 40 45 50
data ptrs + sibling ptr] J[LEAF L2
keys 60 62 65
data ptrs + sibling ptr] K[LEAF L2
keys 70 75
data ptrs + sibling ptr] L[LEAF L2
keys 80 82 85
data ptrs + sibling ptr] M[LEAF L2
keys 90 95
data ptrs + sibling ptr] A -->|less than 30| B A -->|30 to 60| C A -->|60 to 80| D A -->|greater than 80| E B --> F B --> G B --> H C --> I C --> J D --> K D --> L E --> M F -.->|sibling chain| G G -.->|sibling chain| H H -.->|sibling chain| I I -.->|sibling chain| J J -.->|sibling chain| K K -.->|sibling chain| L L -.->|sibling chain| M ``` ### Diagram Walk-through (KTU-style) 1. **Root node** is always **one block** at the top of the tree. It contains separator keys. 2. **Internal (non-leaf) nodes** form a sparse, hierarchical index on the leaves. 3. **Leaf nodes** are at the **bottom level**, each containing actual `(search_key, record_pointer)` entries. 4. **Sibling pointer chain** (dashed lines in the diagram) connects leaves left-to-right. This is what enables a range query `WHERE emp_id BETWEEN 40 AND 70` to: - Locate the leaf containing 40 (via the tree, $O(\log N)$). - Walk the sibling chain through 45, 50, 60, 62, 65 (no more tree traversals). 5. **All leaves at the same level** ⇒ the tree is *height-balanced*. The number of levels $h$ depends on $N$ and the order $m$. ## 4.2 Hash Index — Bucket Array with Overflow Chains (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR H[HASH FUNCTION
h k = k mod 4] H --> B0[Bucket 0
keys 4 8 12] H --> B1[Bucket 1
keys 1 5 9] H --> B2[Bucket 2
keys 2 6 10] H --> B3[Bucket 3
keys 3 7 11] B1 -.->|overflow chain| B1O[Overflow Block
keys 13 17] B2 -.->|overflow chain| B2O[Overflow Block
keys 14] B0 --> D0[Data Block ptrs
for records 4 8 12] B1 --> D1[Data Block ptrs
for records 1 5 9] B1O --> D1O[Data Block ptrs
for records 13 17] B2 --> D2[Data Block ptrs
for records 2 6 10] B2O --> D2O[Data Block ptrs
for record 14] B3 --> D3[Data Block ptrs
for records 3 7 11] ``` ### Hash Index Walk-through 1. The hash function $h(k) = k \bmod 4$ partitions the key space into **4 primary buckets**. 2. **Bucket 1** has an **overflow block** because of collisions (e.g., $h(13) = 1$ and $h(17) = 1$). 3. To look up key 17: compute $h(17) = 1$, read bucket 1, follow the overflow chain, locate 17. Cost = 2 block accesses. 4. **No ordering** is preserved — keys 14, 15, 16, 17 are in *different* buckets, so a range query `WHERE id BETWEEN 14 AND 17` requires scanning **all four primary buckets plus overflows** (essentially a full scan). ## 4.3 Multi-Level Index — Concept Map ```mermaid graph TD Top[Top Level Index
tens of blocks, fits in memory] Mid[Middle Level Index
hundreds of blocks] Low[Lower Level Index
thousands of blocks] Data[Data File
millions of records in sorted blocks] Top -->|pointer to block in mid| Mid Mid -->|pointer to block in low| Low Low -->|pointer to data block| Data ``` **Key takeaway:** A B+ Tree *is* a multi-level index. The root is the top level, the internal nodes are the middle levels, the leaves are the lowest level, and each leaf points to the actual data. The height $h$ of a B+ Tree equals the number of index levels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight for KTU Viva:** A single-level index on a file of 100,000 blocks still requires $\lceil \log_2 100000 \rceil = 17$ block accesses. A B+ Tree with fan-out 100 reduces this to $\lceil \log_{100} 100000 \rceil = 3$ block accesses. **The fan-out is everything.** # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]` **Differentiate between primary index and secondary index. When is each used?** > **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** | Aspect | Primary Index | Secondary Index | |--------|---------------|-----------------| | **Key** | Built on the **primary key** (unique, non-null) | Built on **any non-primary attribute** | | **File order** | Data file is **physically sorted** on the key | Data file is **unsorted** (heap) on the key | | **Density** | **Sparse** (one entry per data block) | **Dense** (one entry per record) | | **Number allowed** | **Only one** per table | **Multiple** per table | | **Use case** | Range queries on the primary key, fast lookups by PK | Lookups on non-key attributes (e.g., `email`, `phone`) | **Usage:** Primary index is used when the relation is naturally sorted on the primary key and the workload is dominated by PK-based retrievals. Secondary indexes are used to support efficient lookups on attributes that are *not* the primary key (e.g., searching employees by `last_name`). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]` **Why are B+ Trees preferred over B-Trees for database indexing?** > **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** B+ Trees are preferred over B-Trees for database indexing for the following reasons: 1. **Higher fan-out:** B+ Tree internal nodes hold only routing keys (no data pointers), so each node can hold more keys. This yields a higher branching factor $F$ and therefore a shorter (shallower) tree — fewer disk accesses. 2. **Range-query efficiency:** B+ Tree leaves are linked in a sibling chain, allowing sequential range scans without re-traversing the tree. B-Tree leaves are *not* linked. 3. **Uniform node size:** All data is at the leaves, so internal nodes have a fixed, predictable size. This simplifies the disk-block allocation strategy. 4. **All records equally accessible:** Every key is found at the leaf level, so the worst-case search path length is identical for all keys. In B-Trees, a key can be found in an internal node, leading to variable path lengths. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > Common mistakes students make in indexing questions: > 1. **Confusing sparse vs dense** — sparse primary index has *one entry per data block*, NOT one per distinct key. > 2. **Forgetting the +1** — search cost is `log₂(b_i) + 1`, where the +1 is for the data block. > 3. **Off-by-one on B+ Tree height** — the height includes the leaf level. > 4. **Hash vs B+ Tree** — students often claim hash indexes "do range queries fast." They do not. This is a guaranteed zero for that part. > 5. **Units and blocking factor** — students frequently omit $bfr$ from calculations, losing 2 marks per occurrence. ### Question A (14 Marks): B+ Tree Cost Analysis `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` **Given:** A relation `STUDENT` has $r = 50{,}000$ records. The blocking factor (number of records per block) is $bfr = 10$. A B+ Tree primary index of order $m = 100$ is built on the primary key `roll_no`. **(a)** Compute the number of block accesses required to search for a record given its `roll_no`, using the B+ Tree. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the cost of this indexed search with a linear scan. What is the speedup factor? Justify whether the B+ Tree is an efficient choice. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — B+ Tree Search Cost (7 marks) **Step 1: Number of data blocks in the STUDENT relation.** $$b_r = \left\lceil \frac{r}{bfr} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{10} \right\rceil = 5000 \text{ data blocks}$$ **Step 2: Number of leaf-level entries (dense B+ Tree, so one entry per record).** $$\text{Max records per leaf node} = m - 1 = 99$$ $$L_{\text{leaves}} = \left\lceil \frac{50{,}000}{99} \right\rceil = 506 \text{ leaf nodes}$$ **Step 3: Number of nodes at internal level 1 (each internal node has up to $m = 100$ children).** $$L_1 = \left\lceil \frac{506}{100} \right\rceil = 6 \text{ internal nodes}$$ **Step 4: Number of nodes at internal level 2.** $$L_2 = \left\lceil \frac{6}{100} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ root node}$$ **Step 5: Total number of levels (root + internal + leaf).** $$h = 2 \text{ internal levels} + 1 \text{ leaf level} = 3 \text{ levels}$$ **Step 6: Block accesses for a search (covering B+ Tree, no separate data fetch needed).** $$\boxed{\text{Block accesses} = h = 3}$$ --- ##### Part (b) — Comparison with Linear Scan (7 marks) **Step 1: Cost of a linear scan over the STUDENT relation.** $$\text{Linear scan cost} = b_r = 5000 \text{ block accesses}$$ **Step 2: Speedup factor of B+ Tree search.** $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{5000}{3} \approx 1666.67 \times$$ **Step 3: Justification.** The B+ Tree search is **1666× faster** than a linear scan. This is because: - The B+ Tree reduces a linear cost $O(N)$ to a logarithmic cost $O(\log_m N)$. - The high fan-out $m = 100$ ensures the tree height is very small. - The B+ Tree also supports **range queries** with the same logarithmic access to the start of the range, plus sequential leaf traversal. - The **storage overhead** of the index is acceptable: only $506 + 6 + 1 = 513$ blocks (about 10% of the data file size). **Conclusion:** The B+ Tree is a highly efficient choice for the STUDENT relation. The trade-off (extra storage + index maintenance on insert/delete) is well worth the query-time savings for a relation of 50,000 records and a read-heavy workload. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): Hash vs B+ Tree Indexing `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 3]` **(a)** Explain the structure of a **Hash Index** with separate chaining for overflow handling. Show the hash function, bucket array, and overflow chains for a relation with 8 records having keys $\{12, 25, 37, 14, 8, 19, 31, 22\}$ using $h(k) = k \bmod 4$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss why **B+ Trees are preferred over Hash Indexes** in RDBMS for general-purpose query workloads. Use specific examples to justify your answer. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — Hash Index with Chaining (7 marks) **Step 1: Apply the hash function $h(k) = k \bmod 4$ to each key.** | Key | $h(k)$ | Bucket | |-----|--------|--------| | 12 | 0 | 0 | | 25 | 1 | 1 | | 37 | 1 | 1 | | 14 | 2 | 2 | | 8 | 0 | 0 | | 19 | 3 | 3 | | 31 | 3 | 3 | | 22 | 2 | 2 | **Step 2: Build the primary bucket array.** - **Bucket 0:** keys $\{12, 8\}$ (no overflow) - **Bucket 1:** keys $\{25, 37\}$ (no overflow) - **Bucket 2:** keys $\{14, 22\}$ (no overflow) - **Bucket 3:** keys $\{19, 31\}$ (no overflow) **Step 3: Insert in this order and identify overflows** (if the primary bucket block fills, an overflow block is appended via a separate-chaining pointer). Assume each primary bucket can hold **1 record** initially. Then the sequence of insertions and overflows is: - Insert 12 → Bucket 0 (occupies primary block). - Insert 25 → Bucket 1 (occupies primary block). - Insert 37 → Bucket 1 (overflows ⇒ **Overflow Block 1** created). - Insert 14 → Bucket 2 (occupies primary block). - Insert 8 → Bucket 0 (overflows ⇒ **Overflow Block 0** created). - Insert 19 → Bucket 3 (occupies primary block). - Insert 31 → Bucket 3 (overflows ⇒ **Overflow Block 3** created). - Insert 22 → Bucket 2 (overflows ⇒ **Overflow Block 2** created). **Step 4: Final structure.** ``` Bucket 0: [12 | •] → Overflow Block 0: [8] Bucket 1: [25 | •] → Overflow Block 1: [37] Bucket 2: [14 | •] → Overflow Block 2: [22] Bucket 3: [19 | •] → Overflow Block 3: [31] ``` **Step 5: Search cost analysis.** - For key 12: compute $h(12) = 0$, read Bucket 0 (1 I/O), find 12 in primary block. Cost = **1 block access**. - For key 37: compute $h(37) = 1$, read Bucket 1 (1 I/O), follow overflow chain, read Overflow Block 1 (1 I/O). Cost = **2 block accesses**. **Step 6: Average cost.** With 8 records and 4 primary buckets, $\alpha = 8/(4 \times 1) = 2$ records/bucket. Average chain length = 2. Hence: $$\text{Average search cost} = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2} = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ block accesses}$$ --- ##### Part (b) — B+ Tree vs Hash Index (7 marks) > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Marking Guide:** Full marks require *specific examples* (range query, equality query, sorted output) and *correct trade-off analysis* (no range support, rehashing cost, etc.). **Hash indexes are NOT preferred in general-purpose RDBMS for the following reasons:** 1. **No range query support:** - Query: `SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE roll_no BETWEEN 100 AND 200` - A B+ Tree can answer this in $O(\log_m N + k)$ block accesses, where $k$ is the number of matching records (using the sibling chain on leaves). - A hash index **cannot** answer this efficiently — it must scan all buckets because $h(100), h(150), h(200)$ are scattered randomly. The hash function destroys the key ordering. 2. **No ordered output:** - Query: `SELECT * FROM STUDENT ORDER BY roll_no LIMIT 10` - B+ Tree leaves are already sorted; a "first 10" query is $O(\log_m N + 10)$. - A hash index returns records in **arbitrary order** (the order of bucket traversal). A separate sort step is required, costing $O(N \log N)$. 3. **Rehashing cost on file growth:** - If the relation grows, the load factor $\alpha$ exceeds the threshold (typically 0.7), and the bucket array must be **rehashed** (doubled and re-bucketed). This is a **very expensive** operation. - B+ Trees grow gracefully via node splits, with no global restructuring. 4. **Worst-case behavior:** - A bad hash function (or pathological key distribution) can produce long overflow chains, degrading search from $O(1)$ to $O(N)$ in the worst case. - B+ Trees guarantee $O(\log_m N)$ in the **worst case** — a critical property for predictable query performance. 5. **Specific example of B+ Tree superiority:** - Query: `SELECT MIN(roll_no), MAX(roll_no) FROM STUDENT` - B+ Tree: $O(1)$ — go to the leftmost and rightmost leaves. - Hash: $O(N)$ — must scan all buckets. **Conclusion:** Hash indexes are excellent for **specialized workloads** (pure key-value lookups, no range queries, e.g., Redis, Memcached, DynamoDB's partition layer). But for **general-purpose relational databases** where range queries, sorting, and worst-case guarantees matter, **B+ Trees are the right choice.** This is why every major RDBMS (PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL InnoDB, SQL Server) uses B+ Trees as the default index structure. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (MUST MEMORIZE before the exam):** - ✅ An **index** is an auxiliary access structure on one or more attributes to speed up data retrieval. - ✅ A **primary index** is built on the **primary key** with the data file **physically sorted** on that key. It is **sparse** (one entry per data block). - ✅ A **clustering index** is built on a **non-unique** sort key; data is sorted but the index is sparse. - ✅ A **secondary index** is built on a **non-primary** attribute; data is **not sorted** on that key. The index is **dense** (one entry per record). - ✅ A **multi-level index** is an index on an index, used when the first-level index does not fit in memory. - ✅ A **B+ Tree of order $m$** has all data at the leaves, leaves at the same level, and **sibling pointers** between leaves. Internal nodes hold only routing keys. - ✅ The number of B+ Tree levels is $h = \lceil \log_m L \rceil + 1$, where $L$ is the number of leaf nodes. - ✅ **Block accesses for an indexed search** = $h$ (or $h + 1$ if a separate data block must be fetched). - ✅ **Block accesses for a linear scan** = $b_r = \lceil r / bfr \rceil$. - ✅ **Hash index** gives $O(1)$ expected search but **cannot** support range queries. - ✅ B+ Tree gives $O(\log_m N)$ search **and** supports **range queries** in $O(\log_m N + k)$ via the leaf sibling chain. - ✅ **ISAM is static** and replaced by B+ Tree in modern DBMS because of overflow-chain degradation. - ✅ B+ Tree node splits maintain the invariant $\lceil m/2 \rceil \le \text{keys per node} \le m$, guaranteeing **at least 50% space utilization**. - ✅ **Cost in DBMS is measured in block accesses (disk I/Os)**, not CPU time. - ✅ A **dense index** has one entry per record; a **sparse index** has one entry per data block. - ✅ **Composite (multi-attribute) index** has search keys as a tuple — column order matters! - ✅ A **covering index** contains all columns referenced by a query, so the data block fetch is avoided. - ✅ The **fan-out $F$** of a B+ Tree is the single most important factor controlling tree height. Maximize $F$ by keeping internal nodes small (no data pointers, small key size, large block size).

topics.title: Application Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Application Integration?

**Application Integration** is the process of enabling independent, heterogeneous software applications and databases to work together as a coordinated whole, sharing data and business processes to present a unified view to the end user.

In the context of **Advanced Database Systems** (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 3), Application Integration specifically deals with combining multiple, autonomous, pre-existing database systems into one logical system so that data from each can be accessed and processed in a unified manner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Friendly):**
> *Application Integration is the methodology of linking disparate database systems and enterprise applications to function as a single coherent system, enabling seamless data exchange, query processing, and transaction management across organizational boundaries.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **shopping mall with 50 independent shops**, each with its own:
- Cash register system
- Inventory list
- Pricing rule
- Customer database

Now imagine you walk to a single **Information Kiosk** in the mall lobby. You type *"Show me all red shoes available right now in any shop on the 2nd floor under ₹2000."*

The kiosk talks to all 50 shops instantly, fetches data, combines results, and gives you one clean answer.

**Application Integration = that Information Kiosk.**

Each shop is an independent database/application; the kiosk is the **integration layer**.

---

## 1.3 Motivation & Engineering Relevance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do engineers build Application Integration?**
> 1. **Legacy Modernization** – Old mainframes still hold critical data; we cannot throw them away.
> 2. **M&A (Mergers & Acquisitions)** – Two companies merge; their IT systems must talk.
> 3. **Global Operations** – A multinational has regional systems that must act as one.
> 4. **Real-Time Analytics** – Decision-making needs data from CRM + ERP + IoT sensors simultaneously.
> 5. **Cost Efficiency** – Replacing all systems costs billions; integration is cheaper.

---

## 1.4 Core Building Blocks of Application Integration

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **five canonical layers/architectures**:

| # | Layer / Approach | One-Line Definition |
|---|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Federated Database System (FDBS)** | A logically integrated collection of autonomous databases with a single global schema. |
| 2 | **Mediator / Wrapper Architecture** | A middle layer translates user queries into local queries at each source. |
| 3 | **Data Warehouse Integration** | Periodic extraction, transformation, and loading (ETL) into a central repository. |
| 4 | **Enterprise Application Integration (EAI)** | Middleware-based message routing between enterprise applications. |
| 5 | **Web Services / Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** | Standards-based (SOAP/REST) services communicating over HTTP. |

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU-Standard Glossary)

- **Component Database (CDB)** – Each autonomous database participating in the federation.
- **Schema Integration** – Combining multiple local schemas into one global schema.
- **Wrapper** – Software module that translates data from a local source into a common format.
- **Mediator** – Central component that receives queries, decomposes them, dispatches to wrappers, and combines results.
- **Heterogeneity** – Differences in DBMS, data model, schema, semantics, or query language between component databases.
- **Autonomy** – Each CDB retains full control over its own data, schema, and execution.
- **Distribution** – The CDs may reside at geographically different sites.
- **Federated Schema (Global Schema)** – The unified logical schema presented to the user.

---

## 1.6 Types of Heterogeneity (Very High-Yield for KTU Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner **always** tests whether the student can list and explain the **four types of heterogeneity**. Memorize this list.

1. **Platform / System Heterogeneity** – Different hardware, OS, network protocols.
2. **Data Model Heterogeneity** – Relational vs. Object-Oriented vs. XML vs. Document (NoSQL).
3. **Schema Heterogeneity** – Different naming, structures, and constraints for the same real-world entity.
4. **Semantic Heterogeneity** – Same data interpreted differently (e.g., "Net Salary" includes/deductions differently in HR vs. Finance).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Application Integration (Detailed)

### 2.1.1 Federated Database Systems (FDBS)

A **Federated Database System** is a collection of cooperating but autonomous component databases (CDBs), integrated to varying degrees, presenting users with a **single logical database** (the *federated schema*).

**Operational Steps (Why and How):**

- **Why FDBS?** – Organizations inherit legacy databases; a single global schema allows unified querying without rewriting legacy systems.
- **How it works:**
  1. Each CDB retains its local schema and autonomy.
  2. **Export schemas** are defined (subsets of local schemas to be shared).
  3. A **federated schema** is built by integrating the export schemas.
  4. A **global data dictionary** stores mappings between global and local schemas.
  5. User queries are written in a single language (typically SQL) against the global schema.

**FDBS Architecture Levels:**

| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Local Schema** | Native schema of a component DB (S1, S2, S3…). |
| **Component Schema** | Logical schema of a component, expressed in its native data model. |
| **Export Schema** | Subset of a component schema made available to the federation. |
| **Federated Schema** | Global, unified view integrating multiple export schemas. |
| **External Schema** | User/application-specific view derived from the federated schema. |

**FDBS Types:**

| Type | Coupling | Integration |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Loosely Coupled** | Low – no common schema | Manual, on-demand |
| **Tightly Coupled** | High – single global schema enforced | Always integrated |

---

### 2.1.2 Mediator–Wrapper Architecture

This is the most commonly tested integration pattern in KTU exams.

- **Wrapper**: A translator adapter for each component database. It converts local query language and data format into a common model.
- **Mediator**: A central server that:
  - Accepts user queries.
  - Decomposes them into sub-queries.
  - Dispatches sub-queries to the appropriate wrappers.
  - Merges, filters, and optimizes the results.
  - Returns a unified answer.

**Real-World Utility:**
- Google Search originally used a **Mediator–Wrapper** design: wrappers indexed many heterogeneous document sources, and a mediator processed queries across them.
- Modern API Gateways (e.g., Kong, Apigee) implement this pattern for microservices.

---

### 2.1.3 Data Warehouse Integration (ETL)

Unlike FDBS (real-time), the **data warehouse** approach extracts data **periodically**, transforms it, and loads it into a central store.

**ETL Pipeline Steps:**

1. **Extract** – Pull data from source systems (CRM, ERP, sensors, flat files).
2. **Transform** – Cleanse, normalize, de-duplicate, convert units, apply business rules.
3. **Load** – Insert into the warehouse using bulk-load optimizations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Be ready to differentiate between **ETL** (Extract-Transform-Load, traditional) and **ELT** (Extract-Load-Transform, modern cloud). Mention *Snowflake / BigQuery* as ELT examples.

---

### 2.1.4 Enterprise Application Integration (EAI)

EAI uses **middleware** to route messages between enterprise applications in real-time.

**Common EAI Patterns:**

| Pattern | Function |
|---------|----------|
| **Point-to-Point** | Each app talks to every other (N² complexity). |
| **Hub-and-Spoke** | One central broker (ESB) routes all messages. |
| **Message Bus** | Publish-subscribe model using a queue (Kafka, RabbitMQ). |
| **Orchestration** | Central controller sequences calls across services. |
| **Choreography** | Each service reacts to events independently. |

---

### 2.1.5 Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) & Web Services

- **Service**: A self-contained, reusable business function published with a standard interface.
- **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)**: XML-based messaging over HTTP.
- **REST (Representational State Transfer)**: Resource-based, lightweight, JSON-friendly.
- **WSDL (Web Services Description Language)**: XML descriptor for SOAP services.
- **UDDI (Universal Description, Discovery, and Integration)**: Registry for discovering services.
- **Microservices**: A modern, fine-grained evolution of SOA, where each service is independently deployable.

---

## 2.2 Schema Integration Process (High-Yield for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU's most-asked sub-question in Module 3:**
> *"Explain the steps involved in schema integration with a suitable example."*

Schema Integration is the process of producing a **federated schema** from multiple **export schemas**.

**Six canonical steps (memorize verbatim):**

1. **Pre-Integration** – Identify candidate schemas, define scope, decide integration strategy (binary vs. n-ary; top-down vs. bottom-up; ladder vs. bus).
2. **Schema Comparison** – Detect correspondences (intersection properties) and conflicts (heterogeneity).
3. **Conforming Schemas** – Resolve conflicts via renaming, type promotion, or schema restructuring.
4. **Merging / Integration** – Combine schemas into an intermediate integrated schema.
5. **Restructuring** – Adapt the integrated schema to conform to design conventions (e.g., 3NF / BCNF).
6. **Quality Assurance & Evolution** – Validate against user requirements; enable future evolution.

---

## 2.3 Types of Schema Conflicts (Mandatory Table for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> When integrating schemas, these conflicts appear. Identify and resolve each.

| Conflict Type | Example |
|---------------|---------|
| **Naming** | `Student` in DB1 vs. `Learner` in DB2. |
| **Structural** | `Grade` as attribute vs. table. |
| **Domain** | GPA stored as `FLOAT` vs. percentage as `INT`. |
| **Semantic** | "Salary" – gross vs. net. |
| **Constraint** | `Phone` is `NULL`-allowed in one DB, `NOT NULL` in another. |
| **Data Type** | `VARCHAR(50)` vs. `TEXT`. |
| **Missing Attribute** | `Email` exists in DB1 but not in DB2. |
| **Inconsistent Data** | Same student has different DOBs in DB1 and DB2. |

---

## 2.4 Query Processing in Integrated Systems

When a user issues a query $Q$ on the federated schema, the following sequence occurs:

1. **Query Decomposition** – Break $Q$ into sub-queries $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$ for each relevant CDB.
2. **Data Localization** – Translate each $q_i$ into the local query language (e.g., SQL dialect).
3. **Global Optimization** – Determine the optimal order of execution; minimize data transfer cost.
4. **Local Optimization** – Each CDB optimizes its sub-query.
5. **Execution & Result Shipping** – Send results back to the mediator.
6. **Result Merging** – Combine, de-duplicate, and format results.

**Cost Function (Total Query Cost):**

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_{local,i} + C_{trans,i} + C_{comm,i} \right)
$$

Where:
- $C_{local,i}$ = local processing cost at CDB $i$
- $C_{trans,i}$ = transformation / translation cost
- $C_{comm,i}$ = communication cost (network latency + bytes transmitted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a federated system, **communication cost dominates** because data must traverse the network. Therefore, query optimizers try to **push selections and projections down** to the wrappers (semi-join reductions).

---

## 2.5 Transaction Management in Integrated Systems

The **ACID** properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) must hold **globally** even though transactions touch multiple autonomous CDBs.

**Distributed Transaction Protocol:** **Two-Phase Commit (2PC)**.

| Phase | Action |
|-------|--------|
| **Phase 1: Prepare** | Coordinator sends `PREPARE` to all participants. Each participant writes to log and replies `YES` or `NO`. |
| **Phase 2: Commit/Abort** | If all `YES` → coordinator sends `COMMIT`. If any `NO` → sends `ROLLBACK`. |

**Concurrency Control:**

- **Distributed Locking** – Centralized or primary copy.
- **Distributed Timestamps** – Globally unique logical clocks.
- **Optimistic Concurrency Control** – Validate at commit.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula & Reference Sheet (Print-Friendly)

| Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---------|--------------------|-------|
| **Heterogeneity types** | 4 (Platform, Data Model, Schema, Semantic) | Always enumerate all four. |
| **Schema integration steps** | 6 (Pre → Compare → Conform → Merge → Restructure → QA) | Order matters. |
| **Total query cost** | $C_{total} = \sum_i (C_{local,i} + C_{trans,i} + C_{comm,i})$ | Communication cost dominates. |
| **2PC phases** | 2 (Prepare, Commit/Abort) | Atomic globally; blocking protocol. |
| **ETL** | Extract → Transform → Load | Periodic, not real-time. |
| **Wrapper role** | Translation + adaptation | One wrapper per source. |
| **Mediator role** | Decomposition + merge | One mediator per federation. |
| **CAP Theorem** | Cannot simultaneously guarantee Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance | Pick any 2 of 3. |
| **ACID** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Strong consistency for transactions. |
| **BASE** | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency | Weak consistency for scale-out. |
| **CAP Trade-off** | $C \cap A = \emptyset$ when $P$ exists in distributed systems | KTU often asks the trade-off. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Federated Query Cost Function

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A user query is decomposed into two sub-queries $q_1$ and $q_2$ to be executed at CDB1 and CDB2 respectively. Derive the total cost expression and identify the dominant factor in wide-area networks.

---

### Step 1 — Define Local Processing Cost

At each component database $CDB_i$, the cost of executing sub-query $q_i$ on relations $R_i$ is the sum of:

$$
C_{local,i} = C_{access,i} + C_{CPU,i} + C_{I/O,i}
$$

Where:
- $C_{access,i}$ = cost of reading relevant blocks from disk
- $C_{CPU,i}$ = CPU cost of selection, join, projection
- $C_{I/O,i}$ = I/O wait time

---

### Step 2 — Define Translation Cost

Each wrapper must convert the global sub-query $q_i$ into a local query and then convert the result back to the global model.

$$
C_{trans,i} = C_{W,front,i} + C_{W,back,i}
$$

Where $C_{W,front,i}$ = front-end translation, $C_{W,back,i}$ = back-end result adaptation.

---

### Step 3 — Define Communication Cost

$$
C_{comm,i} = C_{msg,i} + C_{size,i} \cdot N_i \cdot B
$$

Where:
- $C_{msg,i}$ = message setup latency (number of messages $\times$ fixed overhead)
- $C_{size,i}$ = cost per byte transferred
- $N_i$ = number of result tuples shipped
- $B$ = tuple size in bytes

---

### Step 4 — Total Cost Aggregation

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_{access,i} + C_{CPU,i} + C_{I/O,i} + C_{W,front,i} + C_{W,back,i} + C_{msg,i} + C_{size,i} \cdot N_i \cdot B \right)
$$

---

### Step 5 — Simplification (Grouping Common Terms)

$$
C_{total} = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{local,i}}_{\text{Compute}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{trans,i}}_{\text{Translate}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_{msg,i} + C_{size,i} \cdot N_i \cdot B \right)}_{\text{Communicate}}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Dominance Analysis

For wide-area networks (WAN), $C_{size,i} \cdot N_i \cdot B$ dominates because:

$$
C_{size,i} \gg C_{CPU,i} \quad \text{and} \quad C_{msg,i} \gg C_{access,i}
$$

Therefore, the optimal strategy is to **minimize $N_i$**, the number of tuples shipped. This is achieved by:

- **Push selections down** (filter locally).
- **Use semi-joins** (ship only join-projection attributes).
- **Project early** (drop unneeded columns before transmission).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Schema Integration

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample question (KTU 14-Mark style):** *Integrate the two given schemas into a single federated schema. List conflicts and apply resolutions.*

**Given Schemas:**

**DB1 (College A):**
- `Student_A(SID INT PK, Name VARCHAR(50), Marks FLOAT)`

**DB2 (College B):**
- `Learner(LearnerID VARCHAR(10) PK, FullName TEXT, Percentage INT)`

### Step 1 — Identify Correspondences

| DB1 Term | DB2 Term | Relationship |
|----------|----------|--------------|
| `Student_A` | `Learner` | Same entity: Student |
| `SID` | `LearnerID` | Same attribute: Student ID |
| `Name` | `FullName` | Same attribute: Name |
| `Marks` | `Percentage` | Same concept: Score |

### Step 2 — Identify Conflicts

| Conflict | DB1 | DB2 | Resolution |
|----------|-----|-----|------------|
| **Naming** | `Student_A` | `Learner` | Rename to `Student`. |
| **Data Type** | `INT` for ID | `VARCHAR(10)` | Promote to `VARCHAR(15)`. |
| **Data Type** | `FLOAT` Marks | `INT` Percentage | Standardize as `DECIMAL(5,2)` and store percentage. |
| **Domain Range** | Marks 0–100 | Percentage 0–100 | Same after standardization. |
| **Semantic** | Marks out of 100 | Percentage | Multiply Marks by 100 if value < 1, else keep. |

### Step 3 — Merged Federated Schema

$$
\boxed{\text{Student}(\text{StudentID VARCHAR(15) PK, Name VARCHAR(50), Marks DECIMAL(5,2))}
$$

### Step 4 — Mapping Table (Global → Local)

| Global Attribute | Local Mapping |
|------------------|---------------|
| `StudentID` | `Student_A.SID` / `Learner.LearnerID` |
| `Name` | `Student_A.Name` / `Learner.FullName` |
| `Marks` | `Student_A.Marks` / `Learner.Percentage` (divide by 100 if stored as percent) |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked)

### 3.3.1 FDBS vs. Data Warehouse

| Criterion | FDBS | Data Warehouse |
|-----------|------|----------------|
| **Data freshness** | Real-time | Periodic (delayed) |
| **Integration timing** | On query (virtual) | On load (materialized) |
| **Autonomy of sources** | Preserved | Sources can be modified freely |
| **Query response** | Slower (decomposition overhead) | Faster (pre-integrated) |
| **Historical analysis** | Weak | Strong (time-variant) |
| **Updates propagation** | Immediate | Next ETL cycle |
| **Best use case** | Real-time operational reporting | Analytical BI / OLAP |

### 3.3.2 SOAP vs. REST

| Criterion | SOAP | REST |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Data format** | XML only | XML, JSON, HTML, plain text |
| **Transport** | HTTP, SMTP, JMS, etc. | HTTP only |
| **State** | Stateful | Stateless |
| **Contract** | WSDL | Open API / Swagger |
| **Performance** | Heavier (XML parsing) | Lightweight |
| **Security** | WS-Security built-in | OAuth / TLS |
| **Use case** | Banking, enterprise contracts | Mobile apps, public APIs |

### 3.3.3 ACID vs. BASE

| Criterion | ACID | BASE |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Consistency** | Strong (immediate) | Eventual |
| **Availability** | Lower | Higher |
| **Scalability** | Vertical | Horizontal |
| **Typical systems** | RDBMS (PostgreSQL, Oracle) | NoSQL (Cassandra, DynamoDB) |
| **Use case** | Banking transactions | Social media, IoT |

### 3.3.4 Point-to-Point vs. Hub-and-Spoke vs. Message Bus

| Pattern | Connections | Scalability | Failure Domain |
|---------|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Point-to-Point** | $N(N-1)/2$ | Poor | Each link |
| **Hub-and-Spoke (ESB)** | $N$ | Moderate | Hub = single point of failure |
| **Message Bus** | $N$ + Bus | High | Bus = critical but replicated |

---

## 3.4 Decision Tree for Choosing an Integration Approach

$$
\text{Start} \rightarrow \text{Need real-time queries?} \Rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{FDBS / Mediator-Wrapper}
$$

$$
\text{Start} \rightarrow \text{Need real-time queries?} \Rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Need analytics/history?} \Rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Data Warehouse}
$$

$$
\text{Start} \rightarrow \text{Need real-time queries?} \Rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Need analytics/history?} \Rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Cross-app workflows?} \Rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{EAI / SOA}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Five-Layer Application Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U["User / Application Layer"]
    M["Mediator / Orchestration Layer"]
    W["Wrapper / Adapter Layer"]
    CDB1["CDB 1 - Relational DB"]
    CDB2["CDB 2 - Legacy Mainframe"]
    CDB3["CDB 3 - NoSQL Store"]
    CDB4["CDB 4 - SaaS API"]
    DW["Enterprise Data Warehouse"]
    ETL["ETL Pipeline"]
    MQ["Message Bus - Kafka"]

    U --> M
    M --> W
    W --> CDB1
    W --> CDB2
    W --> CDB3
    W --> CDB4
    CDB1 --> ETL
    CDB2 --> ETL
    CDB3 --> ETL
    ETL --> DW
    CDB1 -.publish.-> MQ
    CDB2 -.publish.-> MQ
    MQ -.subscribe.-> CDB3
    MQ -.subscribe.-> CDB4
```

---

## 4.2 Federated Database Architecture (Three-Level Schemas)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E["External Schema - User View 1"]
    E2["External Schema - User View 2"]
    F["Federated Global Schema"]
    EX1["Export Schema 1"]
    EX2["Export Schema 2"]
    EX3["Export Schema 3"]
    C1["Component Schema 1 - CDB1"]
    C2["Component Schema 2 - CDB2"]
    C3["Component Schema 3 - CDB3"]
    L1["Local Internal Schema 1"]
    L2["Local Internal Schema 2"]
    L3["Local Internal Schema 3"]

    E --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> EX1
    F --> EX2
    F --> EX3
    EX1 --> C1
    EX2 --> C2
    EX3 --> C3
    C1 --> L1
    C2 --> L2
    C3 --> L3
```

---

## 4.3 ETL Pipeline Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Source - CRM"]
    S2["Source - ERP"]
    S3["Source - IoT Logs"]
    EX["Extraction Engine"]
    ST["Staging Area"]
    TR["Transformation Layer - Cleanse, Dedupe, Convert"]
    LD["Loading Engine"]
    WH["Data Warehouse - Star Schema"]
    DM["Data Marts - Sales, Finance, HR"]
    BI["BI / OLAP Reports"]

    S1 --> EX
    S2 --> EX
    S3 --> EX
    EX --> ST
    ST --> TR
    TR --> LD
    LD --> WH
    WH --> DM
    DM --> BI
```

---

## 4.4 Two-Phase Commit (2PC) Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CO as Coordinator
    participant P1 as Participant 1
    participant P2 as Participant 2
    participant P3 as Participant 3

    CO->>P1: PREPARE
    CO->>P2: PREPARE
    CO->>P3: PREPARE
    P1-->>CO: YES
    P2-->>CO: YES
    P3-->>CO: NO
    CO->>P1: ROLLBACK
    CO->>P2: ROLLBACK
    Note over P3: Already aborted locally
```

---

## 4.5 Hub-and-Spoke (ESB) vs. Message Bus Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Hub and Spoke"
        A1[App A] --> H[ESB Hub]
        A2[App B] --> H
        A3[App C] --> H
        H --> A4[App D]
        H --> A5[App E]
    end

    subgraph "Message Bus"
        B1[Producer A] --> BUS[Kafka Topic]
        B2[Producer B] --> BUS
        BUS --> C1[Consumer X]
        BUS --> C2[Consumer Y]
        BUS --> C3[Consumer Z]
    end
```

---

## 4.6 Schema Integration Process (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Step 1 - Pre Integration] --> S[Step 2 - Schema Comparison]
    S --> C[Step 3 - Conforming Schemas - Resolve Conflicts]
    C --> MG[Step 4 - Merging - Intermediate Schema]
    MG --> R[Step 5 - Restructuring - Normalization]
    R --> Q[Step 6 - Quality Assurance]
    Q --> F[Final Federated Global Schema]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q:** *Define Application Integration. List and briefly explain the four types of heterogeneity.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Application Integration is the process of connecting independent, heterogeneous applications and databases so they function as a single unified system, enabling seamless data sharing and process coordination.

**Four Types of Heterogeneity:**

1. **Platform / System Heterogeneity** – Differences in hardware, operating systems, and network protocols among component systems.

2. **Data Model Heterogeneity** – Different data models used (e.g., relational, object-oriented, document, XML, key-value).

3. **Schema Heterogeneity** – Different naming, structure, types, or constraints for the same real-world entity.

4. **Semantic Heterogeneity** – Different meanings or interpretations of the same data across systems (e.g., "revenue" includes/excludes tax).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q:** *Differentiate between a Wrapper and a Mediator in application integration.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Wrapper | Mediator |
|--------|---------|----------|
| **Location** | At each component database | Central / federation level |
| **Purpose** | Translates local query language and data into a common format | Decomposes global queries, merges results |
| **Scope** | Source-specific | Global |
| **Number** | One per data source | Typically one per federation |
| **Function** | Adapter / protocol converter | Query processor / orchestrator |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:**
> For every Part B question in ESE, students must answer either **Question A OR Question B**. Both are on the same topic but with different sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q:** *With neat diagrams, explain the Mediator–Wrapper architecture for application integration. Discuss its advantages and limitations.*

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Architecture Overview [7 Marks]

The Mediator–Wrapper architecture consists of three logical tiers:

1. **Mediator (Top Layer)** – The central component that:
   - Receives user queries formulated in the global schema and language.
   - Decomposes the query into sub-queries for each relevant data source.
   - Performs global optimization to minimize communication cost.
   - Merges and processes results from wrappers.
   - Returns the unified answer to the user.

2. **Wrappers (Middle Layer)** – Source-specific adapters that:
   - Translate global sub-queries into the local query language.
   - Execute the local query.
   - Convert local results into the common data model.
   - Provide schema-mapping information to the mediator.

3. **Component Databases (Bottom Layer)** – Autonomous, heterogeneous data sources (relational, XML, NoSQL, flat files, web services).

**Block Diagram:**

```
[ User Query ] --> [ Mediator ] --> [ Wrapper 1 ] --> [ CDB 1 ]
                                --> [ Wrapper 2 ] --> [ CDB 2 ]
                                --> [ Wrapper 3 ] --> [ CDB 3 ]
              <-- Results merged and returned <--
```

#### (b) Advantages & Limitations [7 Marks]

**Advantages:**
1. **Autonomy preserved** – Component databases are not modified.
2. **Loose coupling** – Sources can be added/removed with minimal impact.
3. **Heterogeneity support** – Wrappers handle diverse data models.
4. **Scalable integration** – New sources require only a new wrapper.
5. **On-demand querying** – No data duplication, no ETL latency.

**Limitations:**
1. **Query-time overhead** – Decomposition, translation, and merging are expensive.
2. **Network bottleneck** – Real-time queries are slow over WANs.
3. **Limited query power** – Complex cross-source joins may be infeasible.
4. **Single point of failure** – Mediator downtime halts the system.
5. **Wrapper maintenance** – Every schema change in a source requires wrapper updates.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 3, 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**

> **Q:** *Explain the six-step schema integration process with a suitable example. List the types of schema conflicts that arise during integration.*

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Six-Step Schema Integration Process [7 Marks]

Consider two component schemas to be integrated:

**Schema S1 (HR Department):** `Employee(EID INT PK, EmpName VARCHAR(50), Salary FLOAT)`

**Schema S2 (Payroll Department):** `Staff(StaffNo VARCHAR(10) PK, Name TEXT, Pay DECIMAL(8,2))`

**Step 1 – Pre-Integration:**
Choose **binary, bottom-up** integration strategy. Identify S1 and S2 as candidates. Define the scope: combine `Employee` and `Staff` into a unified `Personnel` schema.

**Step 2 – Schema Comparison:**
Analyze correspondences:

| S1 Attribute | S2 Attribute | Correspondence |
|--------------|--------------|----------------|
| `Employee` | `Staff` | Same real-world entity |
| `EID` | `StaffNo` | Identifier |
| `EmpName` | `Name` | Full name |
| `Salary` | `Pay` | Compensation |

**Step 3 – Conforming Schemas (Conflict Resolution):**

| Conflict Type | Detail | Resolution |
|---------------|--------|------------|
| Naming | `Employee` vs. `Staff` | Rename to `Personnel`. |
| Naming | `EmpName` vs. `Name` | Standardize as `FullName`. |
| Data type | `INT` vs. `VARCHAR(10)` | Promote to `VARCHAR(15)`. |
| Domain | `FLOAT` vs. `DECIMAL(8,2)` | Standardize as `DECIMAL(10,2)`. |
| Semantic | `Salary` vs. `Pay` | Confirm both = gross monthly. |

**Step 4 – Merging:**
Produce the intermediate integrated schema:

$$
\text{Personnel\_Intermediate}(\text{PersonnelID VARCHAR(15), FullName VARCHAR(100), Compensation DECIMAL(10,2))
$$

**Step 5 – Restructuring:**
Apply normalization (3NF) and add referential integrity constraints. Define keys and ensure no transitive dependencies.

**Step 6 – Quality Assurance & Evolution:**
Validate with users. Verify mappings from local to global schema. Document for future schema evolution (adding new fields without breakage).

#### (b) Types of Schema Conflicts [7 Marks]

| # | Conflict Type | Example |
|---|---------------|---------|
| 1 | **Naming conflicts** | `Employee` vs. `Staff` |
| 2 | **Structural conflicts** | Department as attribute vs. separate table |
| 3 | **Domain conflicts** | `INT` vs. `VARCHAR` |
| 4 | **Semantic conflicts** | "Salary" = gross vs. net |
| 5 | **Constraint conflicts** | `NOT NULL` vs. nullable |
| 6 | **Data-type conflicts** | `FLOAT` vs. `DECIMAL` |
| 7 | **Missing attributes** | Email in S1 but not S2 |
| 8 | **Inconsistent data** | Same EID has different names |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PECDT795 Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Listing only 3 types of heterogeneity instead of 4** – Always mention **Platform, Data Model, Schema, Semantic**. Missing one = 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Confusing Wrapper with Mediator** – A wrapper is *source-specific and translates*; a mediator is *central and decomposes/merges*. Examiners deduct 2 marks for swapped definitions.
> 3. **Skipping the cost formula** – In federated query processing questions, $C_{total} = \sum(C_{local} + C_{trans} + C_{comm})$ is **mandatory**. Writing "network cost is high" without the equation loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not numbering the schema integration steps** – Examiners scan for the exact six steps in order. Out-of-order = 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing ETL without explaining each stage** – "Extract, Transform, Load" alone is 1 mark; each stage explained earns 3.
> 6. **Mentioning SOAP/REST without contrasting** – KTU loves comparisons; a table earns more than prose.
> 7. **Forgetting 2PC phases** – Any question on distributed transactions in integration MUST include the Prepare / Commit phases.
> 8. **Ignoring ACID for federated transactions** – Explain how 2PC + global serializability preserve ACID across CDBs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] Application Integration = unifying heterogeneous apps/DBs into one logical system.
