- [x] Four heterogeneity types: **Platform, Data Model, Schema, Semantic**.
- [x] Five architectural approaches: **FDBS, Mediator–Wrapper, Data Warehouse, EAI, SOA/Web Services**.
- [x] **Mediator** = central query processor; **Wrapper** = source-specific translator.
- [x] Schema integration has **six steps**: Pre-Integration → Comparison → Conforming → Merging → Restructuring → QA.
- [x] Eight types of schema conflicts: naming, structural, domain, semantic, constraint, data-type, missing-attr, inconsistent data.
- [x] Federated query cost: $C_{total} = \sum(C_{local} + C_{trans} + C_{comm})$; communication dominates in WANs.
- [x] **2PC** (Two-Phase Commit) = standard for distributed transaction atomicity in integrated systems.
- [x] **ETL** = Extract, Transform, Load; used for analytical integrations (warehouses).
- [x] **EAI patterns**: Point-to-Point, Hub-and-Spoke (ESB), Message Bus, Orchestration, Choreography.
- [x] **SOAP** = XML, heavyweight, WS-Security; **REST** = JSON/XML, lightweight, stateless.
- [x] **ACID** = strong consistency (RDBMS); **BASE** = eventual consistency (NoSQL).
- [x] **CAP Theorem** = pick any 2 of {Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance}.
- [x] Three schema levels in FDBS: **Local → Component → Export → Federated → External**.
- [x] FDBS types: **Loosely Coupled** vs. **Tightly Coupled**.
- [x] Optimization strategies in federated queries: **push selections/projections down**, **use semi-joins**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:**
> Whenever a question says *"explain application integration,"* always structure your answer as:
> **Definition → Motivation → Heterogeneity Types → Architecture Diagram → Integration Steps → Query/Transaction Management → Comparison Table → Conclusion**.
> This 8-part structure is what differentiates a **7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer** in KTU valuations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Replication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Replication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replication** is the process of storing and maintaining multiple copies of the same data at different physical or logical locations (nodes/sites) within a distributed database system, such that all replicas behave logically as a single, unified dataset for the user.

In the context of **Distributed Database Management Systems (DDBMS)**, replication is governed by the **Khoshafian and Copeland Replication Spectrum** and is a fundamental strategy for achieving:
- **High Availability (HA)**
- **Fault Tolerance**
- **Improved Read Scalability**
- **Disaster Recovery**

### The Three Pillars of Replication
A replication mechanism is evaluated based on three core properties (often mapped to the PACELC trade-off):

| Pillar | Technical Term | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Where** | **Replica Placement Strategy** | Location of replicas (primary-backup vs. peer-to-peer). |
| **When** | **Propagation Timing** | Eager (synchronous) vs. Lazy (asynchronous) update propagation. |
| **How** | **Update Propagation Method** | Single-master, multi-master, or symmetric update delivery. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The National Newspaper Distribution System**
>
> Imagine the **"KTU Daily Bulletin"** — an official university notice released by the central office in Thiruvananthapuram. Instead of having every student travel to the headquarters to read it, the university prints **multiple copies (replicas)** and distributes them to regional centers in Kochi, Kozhikode, and Thrissur.
>
> - The **central office** is the **Primary (Master) Node**.
> - The **regional copies** are the **Replicas (Slaves)**.
> - **Synchronous** replication: Every regional center receives the bulletin *before* the central office announces it officially (slower, but everyone has the same copy).
> - **Asynchronous** replication: Regional centers receive the bulletin *hours after* the announcement (faster central processing, but temporary inconsistency).
>
> If the Kochi center's copy is destroyed, students can still read the bulletin from Kozhikode — this is **fault tolerance**.

---

## 1.3 Why is Replication Required? (The 5 Engineering Drivers)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently ask *"List and explain the advantages of data replication."* Memorize the following 5 points:

1. **Reliability and Availability**: If one site fails, data is still accessible from another replica. Reliability $R$ for a system with $n$ independent replicas each with availability $a$ is:
$$R = 1 - (1 - a)^n$$
2. **Performance (Reduced Latency)**: Queries can be routed to the **nearest replica**, reducing network round-trip time.
3. **Increased Throughput**: Read queries are **load-balanced** across multiple read-only replicas.
4. **Scalability**: Supports massive **concurrent read operations** by adding more replicas horizontally.
5. **Disaster Recovery**: Geographic distribution of replicas protects against site-wide failures (earthquakes, power outages).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Replication Architectures

### A. Based on Replica Update Authority (Master-Slave Models)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Single-Master Replication (Primary-Backup / Master-Slave)**
> - **One designated Master** accepts all write operations (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`).
> - **All Slaves (Replicas)** are read-only and continuously receive updates from the Master.
> - *Used in:* MySQL Replication, PostgreSQL Streaming Replication, MongoDB Primary-Secondary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2. Multi-Master Replication (Master-Master)**
> - **Multiple nodes** can accept write operations simultaneously.
> - Updates are propagated asynchronously to all other masters.
> - *Risk:* **Write Conflicts** when two masters update the same record concurrently.
> - *Used in:* MySQL Group Replication, Oracle GoldenGate, CouchDB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3. Peer-to-Peer (Symmetric) Replication**
> - **All replicas are equal**; no master designation.
> - Most complex; used in advanced distributed systems.
> - *Used in:* Cassandra, Riak, DynamoDB.

---

### B. Based on Propagation Timing (Synchronous vs. Asynchronous)

| Property | Eager (Synchronous) Replication | Lazy (Asynchronous) Replication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Update Commit** | Transaction commits **only after all replicas** acknowledge the update. | Transaction commits at the local node; replicas updated **later** via log shipping. |
| **Consistency** | **Strong Consistency** (1-copy serializable). | **Eventual Consistency** (replicas converge eventually). |
| **Latency** | **High** (network-bound). | **Low** (write completes locally). |
| **Failure Impact** | Write may **block** if any replica is down. | Write **always succeeds**; replicas may diverge. |
| **Conflict Risk** | **Zero** conflicts. | **Possible** conflicts (requires resolution). |
| **CAP Theorem** | Favors **Consistency (C)**. | Favors **Availability (A)**. |
| **KTU Example** | Two-Phase Commit (2PC), Paxos. | DNS, DynamoDB, Cassandra. |

---

## 2.2 The CAP Theorem & Replication (KTU High-Weightage Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000):** A distributed data store **cannot simultaneously guarantee** more than two of the following three properties:
> - **C** — **Consistency** (all nodes see the same data at the same time)
> - **A** — **Availability** (every request receives a response)
> - **P** — **Partition Tolerance** (system continues despite network failures)

Since **network partitions (P) are inevitable** in any real distributed system, the practical trade-off is between **C and A**:
- **CP Systems** (Synchronous Replication): Halt writes during partition → Strong consistency.
- **AP Systems** (Asynchronous Replication): Accept writes everywhere → High availability, eventual consistency.

**PACELC Extension:** If there is a **P**artition, choose **A** or **C**; **E**lse (no partition), choose **L**atency or **C**onsistency.

---

## 2.3 Quorum-Based Replication (Mathematical Foundation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Quorum systems are central to **Amazon Dynamo-style** databases and are a guaranteed KTU 2024 question.

Define the following variables for a system of **N replicas**:
- **N** = Total number of replicas for a data item.
- **W** = Write quorum size (number of replicas that must acknowledge a write).
- **R** = Read quorum size (number of replicas contacted for a read).

### The Two Critical Quorum Conditions

$$\boxed{R + W > N} \quad \text{(Strong Consistency - Read overlaps Write)}$$

$$\boxed{W > \frac{N}{2}} \quad \text{(Anti-split-brain - Write overlaps Write)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **R + W > N** rule guarantees that any read quorum must include **at least one replica** that has the latest version of the data, eliminating the risk of **stale reads**. This single inequality is the mathematical backbone of systems like Amazon DynamoDB, Riak, and Cassandra.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R_{\text{system}}$ | $R_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - a)^n$ | System availability with $n$ replicas. | Used in SLA calculations. |
| $R + W > N$ | Read & Write Quorum Overlap | Guarantees strong consistency. | DynamoDB quorum tuning. |
| $W > N/2$ | Write Quorum Majority | Prevents split-brain writes. | Paxos/Raft leader election. |
| $N = R + W - 1$ | Minimal $N$ for consistency | Smallest quorum configuration. | Resource optimization. |
| $T_{\text{eager}}$ | $T_{\text{network}} \times N$ | Latency in synchronous replication. | Capacity planning. |
| $D_{\text{async}}$ | $\Delta t_{\text{propagation}}$ | Replica divergence window. | Consistency window analysis. |
| $\text{MTTF}_{\text{sys}}$ | $\frac{\text{MTTF}_{\text{node}}}{n}$ | Mean Time To Failure with $n$ replicas. | Reliability engineering. |
| $V_{\text{read}}$ | $\max(V_i)$ for $i \in \text{Quorum}$ | Read returns latest version. | Version vector algorithm. |

> Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation in tables to preserve markdown syntax.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why $R + W > N$ Guarantees Strong Consistency

> **Goal:** Prove that the read quorum $R$ must overlap with the latest write quorum $W$ in a system of $N$ replicas.

### Step-by-Step Logical Proof

**Step 1: Define the Problem Setup**
Consider a key $K$ replicated across $N$ nodes. A write operation requires acknowledgement from $W$ nodes, and a read operation must contact $R$ nodes to form a valid response.

$$
\text{Write Set } (S_W) = \text{set of } W \text{ nodes that acknowledged the last write.}
$$

$$
\text{Read Set } (S_R) = \text{set of } R \text{ nodes contacted for a read.}
$$

**Step 2: State the Overlap Condition**
For the read to return the most recent write, at least one node must be common to both sets. By the **Pigeonhole Principle**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|S_W \cup S_R| &\leq N \\
|S_W| + |S_R| - |S_W \cap S_R| &\leq N \\
W + R - |S_W \cap S_R| &\leq N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Constraint**
For **strong consistency**, the intersection must be **non-empty** (at least 1 common node):

$$
|S_W \cap S_R| \geq 1
$$

Substituting this into the inequality:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W + R - 1 &\leq N \\
\therefore \quad R + W &\leq N + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Strict Inequality for Guaranteed Read**
The standard KTU/Korth formulation uses the **strict** form because the read must contain a node that **definitely** holds the latest write, not just potentially:

$$
\boxed{R + W > N}
$$

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example**
Let $N = 5$. Choose $W = 3$ and $R = 3$. Verify:

$$
R + W = 3 + 3 = 6 > 5 = N \quad \checkmark
$$

Worst case: the $3$ write nodes and the $3$ read nodes share $6 - 5 = 1$ node in common, guaranteeing freshness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Availability Under Quorum Failure

**Problem:** A KTU question typically asks: *"If 3 out of 5 replicas fail, can you still read? Write?"*

For $N = 5$:
- **$W = 3$, $R = 3$** (Strict quorum)
  - **Read available**: If at most $N - R = 2$ nodes fail → **Survives 2 failures**.
  - **Write available**: If at most $N - W = 2$ nodes fail → **Survives 2 failures**.

- **$W = 1$, $R = 1$** (Weak quorum)
  - **Read available**: If at most $N - R = 4$ nodes fail → **Survives 4 failures**.
  - **Write available**: If at most $N - W = 4$ nodes fail → **Survives 4 failures**.

- **$W = 5$, $R = 1$** (Read-Optimized, e.g., Cassandra ONE)
  - **Read available**: Survives **4 failures**.
  - **Write available**: Survives **0 failures** (all 5 must be up).

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off Insight:** Higher $W$ and $R$ → better consistency, lower availability. Lower $W$ and $R$ → better availability, weaker consistency. This is the heart of the **CAP Theorem** in action.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The "Read Your Own Writes" Consistency Condition

**Problem:** In a lazy replication system, a user updates their profile and immediately re-reads it. Under what condition is **Read-Your-Own-Writes (RYOW)** consistency guaranteed?

For a user session, the **session-read-quorum** $R_s$ and **session-write-quorum** $W_s$ must satisfy:

$$
\boxed{R_s + W_s > N}
$$

This ensures that the same client's subsequent read will see their own latest write because their session tokens (e.g., version vectors) guarantee the overlap.

**Step-by-step verification for $N=3$:**
Choose $W_s = 2$, $R_s = 2$. Then $R_s + W_s = 4 > 3 = N$ ✓.

The session-aware coordinator tags the write with a **causal dependency marker** (e.g., vector clock), and the read quorum is filtered to only return versions that are **causally greater than or equal to** this marker.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Single-Master Replication Architecture (Primary-Backup)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AppServer["Application Server Cluster"]
    MasterDB[("Primary Master DB - RW")]
    LogShipper["Binary Log Shipper"]
    Slave1[("Read Replica 1 - RO")]
    Slave2[("Read Replica 2 - RO")]
    Slave3[("Read Replica 3 - RO")]
    LoadBalancer["Read Load Balancer"]
    UserRead["User Read Query"]
    UserWrite["User Write Query"]

    AppServer --> UserRead
    AppServer --> UserWrite
    UserWrite --> MasterDB
    UserRead --> LoadBalancer
    LoadBalancer --> Slave1
    LoadBalancer --> Slave2
    LoadBalancer --> Slave3
    MasterDB --> LogShipper
    LogShipper --> Slave1
    LogShipper --> Slave2
    LogShipper --> Slave3
```

> **Explanation:** All writes (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`) go to the **Primary Master**. The **Log Shipper** propagates the binary log to read replicas. Users' read queries are distributed by the **Read Load Balancer** across the replicas.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Master Replication with Conflict Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Region1["Region 1 - Master A"]
    Region2["Region 2 - Master B"]
    Region3["Region 3 - Master C"]
    ConflictResolver["Conflict Resolver - Last-Write-Wins / Vector Clock"]
    ReplChannel1["Async Replication Channel"]
    ReplChannel2["Async Replication Channel"]
    ReplChannel3["Async Replication Channel"]

    Region1 --> ReplChannel1
    Region1 --> ReplChannel2
    Region2 --> ReplChannel2
    Region2 --> ReplChannel3
    Region3 --> ReplChannel3
    Region3 --> ReplChannel1
    ReplChannel1 --> ConflictResolver
    ReplChannel2 --> ConflictResolver
    ReplChannel3 --> ConflictResolver
```

> **Explanation:** Multiple masters accept writes. Replication channels (often bidirectional) propagate updates. When the same record is updated at two masters, the **Conflict Resolver** uses algorithms like **Last-Write-Wins (LWW)** or **Vector Clocks** to reconcile.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Quorum-Based Replication (Dynamo-Style) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client["Client Request"]
    Coordinator["Coordinator Node"]
    N1[("Node 1")]
    N2[("Node 2")]
    N3[("Node 3")]
    N4[("Node 4")]
    N5[("Node 5")]
    Resp["Merged Response"]

    Client -->|Write W=3| Coordinator
    Client -->|Read R=3| Coordinator
    Coordinator --> N1
    Coordinator --> N2
    Coordinator --> N3
    Coordinator --> N4
    Coordinator --> N5
    N1 --> Resp
    N2 --> Resp
    N3 --> Resp
    N4 --> Resp
    N5 --> Resp
    Resp --> Client
```

> **Explanation:** The **Coordinator** routes reads/writes to a quorum of nodes. For a write with $W=3$, at least 3 of 5 nodes must acknowledge. For a read with $R=3$, at least 3 of 5 nodes must respond. The **R + W > N** rule (3 + 3 > 5) guarantees consistency.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Replication

| Property | Synchronous (Eager) | Asynchronous (Lazy) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Write Latency | High | Low |
| Consistency | Strong | Eventual |
| Conflict Risk | None | Possible |
| Failure Impact | Write blocks | Replica diverges |
| CAP Category | CP | AP |
| Protocols | 2PC, Paxos, Raft | Gossip, Anti-entropy |
| Examples | Google Spanner | Amazon DynamoDB |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**Q: Define data replication. List any two advantages of replication in distributed databases.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Data replication is the process of **storing and maintaining multiple copies** of the same data item at different sites (nodes) in a distributed database system to improve availability, reliability, and performance.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Improved Reliability/Availability:** If one site fails, the data is still accessible from another replica. System availability $R = 1 - (1 - a)^n$ improves with more replicas.
2. **Reduced Response Time:** Queries can be served from the **nearest replica**, reducing network latency for geographically distributed users.
3. **Increased Throughput:** Read operations can be **parallelized** across multiple replicas via load balancing.
4. **Disaster Recovery:** Geographic distribution protects against localized catastrophes (floods, earthquakes, power outages).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`
**Q: Differentiate between eager (synchronous) and lazy (asynchronous) replication.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Eager (Synchronous) | Lazy (Asynchronous) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Commit Time** | Transaction commits **only after all replicas** confirm. | Transaction commits at **local node**; replicas updated later. |
| **Consistency** | **Strong (1-copy serializable).** | **Eventual** (replicas may diverge temporarily). |
| **Latency** | **High** (network-bound). | **Low** (local commit). |
| **Failure Impact** | Write may **block** on replica failure. | Write **succeeds**; replicas may be inconsistent. |
| **Protocols** | 2PC, Paxos, Raft. | Log shipping, Gossip protocol. |
| **Example** | Google Spanner. | Amazon DynamoDB. |

---

## PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the different **types of replication architectures** (single-master, multi-master, peer-to-peer) with suitable diagrams. Discuss the conflict resolution strategies in multi-master replication. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **quorum-based replication**, derive and explain the consistency condition $R + W > N$. Given $N = 7$, determine valid $(R, W)$ pairs that satisfy both strong consistency and write majority. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Solution to (a):**

**1. Single-Master Replication (Primary-Backup):**
- Only the **Master node** accepts write operations.
- All **Slave nodes** are read-only and receive updates via binary log shipping or streaming.
- **Advantages:** Simple, no conflicts, strong consistency.
- **Disadvantage:** Master is a single point of failure; limited write scalability.
- **Example:** MySQL Master-Slave, PostgreSQL Streaming Replication.

**2. Multi-Master Replication (Master-Master):**
- **Multiple nodes** act as masters; each can accept writes.
- Updates are propagated **asynchronously** to all other masters.
- **Advantage:** Higher write availability; no single point of failure for writes.
- **Disadvantage:** **Write conflicts** when two masters update the same record simultaneously.
- **Example:** MySQL Group Replication, Oracle GoldenGate, CouchDB.

**3. Peer-to-Peer (Symmetric) Replication:**
- **All replicas are equal**; no master designation.
- **All nodes** can accept both reads and writes.
- Uses **distributed consensus** protocols (Paxos, Raft) for coordination.
- **Advantage:** Maximum fault tolerance and scalability.
- **Disadvantage:** Most complex to implement; higher coordination overhead.
- **Example:** Cassandra, Riak, DynamoDB.

**Conflict Resolution Strategies in Multi-Master:**

1. **Last-Write-Wins (LWW):** Each update carries a timestamp; the update with the **highest timestamp** wins. Simple but may lose data.
2. **Vector Clocks:** Each replica maintains a logical counter; conflicts are detected and resolved at the application level.
3. **Application-Defined Resolution:** Custom business logic decides the winner (e.g., union of shopping cart items).
4. **Operational Transform (OT):** Used in collaborative editing; transforms concurrent operations to maintain consistency.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Derivation of $R + W > N$:**

Let $S_W$ = set of $W$ nodes that acknowledged the last write, and $S_R$ = set of $R$ nodes contacted for a read. For the read to return the latest write, at least one node must be in both sets.

By the Pigeonhole Principle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|S_W \cup S_R| &\leq N \\
|S_W| + |S_R| - |S_W \cap S_R| &\leq N \\
W + R - |S_W \cap S_R| &\leq N
\end{aligned}
$$

For **at least one overlapping node**: $\vert S_W \cap S_R \vert \geq 1$, giving:

$$
R + W \leq N + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R + W > N
$$

**Worked Problem: $N = 7$**

**Condition 1 (Strong Consistency):** $R + W > 7$ → $R + W \geq 8$.

**Condition 2 (Write Majority):** $W > 7/2 = 3.5$ → $W \geq 4$.

| Valid Pair | $R$ | $W$ | $R + W$ | $W > 3.5$? | Consistent? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pair 1 | 4 | 4 | 8 | Yes | Yes |
| Pair 2 | 5 | 3 | 8 | No ($3 \not> 3.5$) | No (write minority) |
| Pair 3 | 4 | 5 | 9 | Yes | Yes |
| Pair 4 | 5 | 4 | 9 | Yes | Yes |
| Pair 5 | 6 | 3 | 9 | No | No |
| Pair 6 | 4 | 4 | 8 | Yes | Yes (best balanced) |

**Best Choice:** $(R, W) = (4, 4)$ — balanced, survives up to 3 node failures for both reads and writes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 3]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **CAP Theorem** in detail. How does it influence the choice between synchronous and asynchronous replication? Discuss with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A distributed database has $N = 5$ replicas. A read operation uses $R = 2$ and a write operation uses $W = 2$. **(i)** Does this configuration guarantee strong consistency? Justify mathematically. **(ii)** If 3 nodes fail, can the system serve reads? Writes? Show the calculations. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Solution to (a):**

**CAP Theorem (Brewer's Conjecture, 2000):**
A distributed data store can provide **at most two** of the following three guarantees simultaneously:

- **C — Consistency:** Every read receives the most recent write or an error. All nodes see the same data at the same time.
- **A — Availability:** Every request receives a response (non-error), even if some nodes are down. No guarantee on data freshness.
- **P — Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.

**Why all three are impossible:** During a network partition, if a node is unreachable, you must choose:
- **Reject the request** (sacrifice Availability) → **CP system**.
- **Accept the request with potentially stale data** (sacrifice Consistency) → **AP system**.

Partition tolerance is **non-negotiable** in real networks (partitions are inevitable due to failures, congestion, routing issues), so the real trade-off is **C vs. A**.

**Influence on Replication Strategy:**

| Choice | Replication Type | CAP Category | Behavior | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Strong Consistency | **Synchronous (Eager)** | **CP** | Waits for all replicas; blocks during partition. | Google Spanner, HBase. |
| High Availability | **Asynchronous (Lazy)** | **AP** | Commits locally; converges eventually. | Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra, Riak. |

**Examples:**
- **Google Spanner (CP):** Uses synchronous replication with TrueTime API. Sacrifices availability during partitions to guarantee global consistency.
- **Amazon DynamoDB (AP):** Uses asynchronous quorum-based replication. Always accepts writes; replicas converge via anti-entropy. Sacrifices strong consistency for availability.
- **Yahoo PNUTS (PA/EC):** Uses a hybrid — per-record master with async replication. Eventual consistency by default.

**PACELC Extension:** If Partition → choose A or C; Else → choose Latency or Consistency. This provides a more nuanced trade-off analysis beyond simple CAP.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $N = 5$, $R = 2$, $W = 2$.

**(i) Strong Consistency Check:**

Test the condition $R + W > N$:

$$
R + W = 2 + 2 = 4
$$

$$
N = 5
$$

$$
4 \not> 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Strong consistency is NOT guaranteed.}
$$

**Justification:** The read quorum (2 nodes) and write quorum (2 nodes) may not overlap. A write may be acknowledged by nodes {A, B} while a subsequent read contacts nodes {C, D}, returning **stale data**.

**Fix:** Use $(R, W) = (3, 3)$ → $3 + 3 = 6 > 5$ ✓, which guarantees consistency.

---

**(ii) Failure Analysis with 3 nodes down (out of 5):**

**Surviving nodes:** $5 - 3 = 2$ nodes.

**Read availability:** Need $R = 2$ available nodes.

$$
\text{Available nodes} = 2 \geq R = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Reads SUCCEED (barely)}.
$$

If **4 nodes** fail → $5 - 4 = 1$ available $< R = 2$ → **Reads FAIL**.

**Write availability:** Need $W = 2$ available nodes.

$$
\text{Available nodes} = 2 \geq W = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Writes SUCCEED (barely)}.
$$

If **4 nodes** fail → $5 - 4 = 1$ available $< W = 2$ → **Writes FAIL**.

**Conclusion:** With $(R, W) = (2, 2)$ and $N = 5$, the system tolerates up to **3 node failures** for both reads and writes — but this comes at the cost of weak consistency (as shown in part (i)).

**Comparison Table:**

| Configuration | Consistency | Read Tolerance | Write Tolerance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $(R, W) = (2, 2)$ | Weak | 3 failures | 3 failures |
| $(R, W) = (3, 3)$ | Strong | 2 failures | 2 failures |
| $(R, W) = (4, 2)$ | Strong | 1 failure | 3 failures |
| $(R, W) = (5, 1)$ | Weak (read) | 0 failures | 4 failures |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check the second condition ($W > N/2$):** Students often only verify $R + W > N$ and miss the write-majority rule. If you set $W = 1$ and $R = N$, you satisfy $R + W > N$ but lose all write fault tolerance — you will lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $R + W > N$ with $R + W = N + 1$:** The strict inequality $>$ is the standard form; $= N + 1$ is just the boundary case. Always write $>$ in the exam.
> 3. **Not converting between availability and tolerance:** When asked "how many failures can the system tolerate?", the answer is $N - R$ for reads and $N - W$ for writes — not the value of $R$ or $W$ directly. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 4. **Mixing up Synchronous/Asynchronous with Master/Master-Slave:** These are **orthogonal classifications**! You can have Single-Master with Asynchronous replication (e.g., MySQL async replication). Do not conflate them.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Definition:** Replication = multiple copies of data at different sites; goals are **availability**, **reliability**, **performance**.

- **Three Architectures:**
  - **Single-Master:** One master, many slaves (read-only). Simple, no conflicts. Example: MySQL replication.
  - **Multi-Master:** Many masters, async propagation. **Conflicts possible** → resolve via LWW, Vector Clocks, or app logic. Example: MySQL Group Replication.
  - **Peer-to-Peer:** All nodes equal. Most fault-tolerant, most complex. Example: Cassandra.

- **Two Timing Models:**
  - **Synchronous (Eager):** Wait for all replicas → **Strong consistency**, high latency, CP systems.
  - **Asynchronous (Lazy):** Local commit, replicate later → **Eventual consistency**, low latency, AP systems.

- **CAP Theorem:** Cannot have all three of Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. **P is mandatory** → choose C or A.

- **PACELC:** Else (no partition), choose Latency vs. Consistency.

- **Quorum Rules (CRITICAL):**
  - $R + W > N$ → **Strong consistency** (read-write overlap).
  - $W > N/2$ → **Write majority** (no split-brain).
  - Fault tolerance: reads tolerate $N - R$ failures; writes tolerate $N - W$ failures.

- **Conflict Resolution in Multi-Master:** Last-Write-Wins (LWW), Vector Clocks, Application-Defined, Operational Transform (OT).

- **Consistency Models Hierarchy (weakest to strongest):** Eventual → Causal → Read-Your-Writes → Monotonic Read → Linearizable.

- **Key Protocols:** 2PC (synchronous), Paxos/Raft (consensus), Gossip (anti-entropy, async).

- **Real-World Systems:**
  - **CP (Sync):** Google Spanner, HBase, MongoDB (default).
  - **AP (Async):** Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra, Riak, CouchDB.
  - **CA (Single-site, no partition):** Traditional RDBMS, PostgreSQL.

- **Common Exam Traps:**
  - Synchronous ≠ Master-Slave; they are **independent** dimensions.
  - $R + W > N$ is strict, not $\geq$.
  - $W > N/2$ is often forgotten by students.
  - Quorum $(N, 1)$ gives strong read but no write tolerance.

- **Memorize Formulas:**
  - $R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1-a)^n$
  - $R + W > N$
  - $W > N/2$
  - Failure tolerance = $N - R$ (reads) / $N - W$ (writes).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sharding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sharding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sharding** is a horizontal partitioning strategy used in distributed database systems where a single logical dataset is split across multiple independent physical databases (called **shards**), with each shard holding a unique subset of the data and together forming the complete logical dataset.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT795)**, sharding is formally defined as a *data-tier scalability technique* that addresses the **three scalability dimensions** of distributed systems:

- **Data Scalability** (handling massive row counts)
- **Throughput Scalability** (handling read/write operations per second)
- **Latency Scalability** (geographic proximity of data)

Each shard is a fully independent database server (or cluster), and routing is governed by a **Shard Key** — a chosen attribute whose value determines the destination shard for a given record.

> [!NOTE]
> **Horizontal vs Vertical Partitioning (KTU Distinction)**
> - **Vertical Partitioning**: Columns are split across tables (column-wise slicing — e.g., keeping `user_profile` in DB1 and `user_orders` in DB2).
> - **Horizontal Partitioning (Sharding)**: Rows are split across tables (row-wise slicing — every shard contains the *same schema* but a *different subset of rows*).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive library with 10 million books** and only **one librarian**. Every request — finding a book, returning a book, cataloging — funnels through a single person. As the library grows, service collapses.

**Solution 1 (Replication):** Hire *100 identical librarians*, each with a *full copy* of all 10 million books. Read queries are distributed, but **storage is replicated 100x** and writes must update all 100 copies.

**Solution 2 (Sharding):** Divide the library into **100 smaller rooms**, each holding **100,000 unique books**. A *reception desk* (the **Shard Router / Config Server**) looks at the book's category code and tells you which room to visit. Books are *split*, not *copied*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Sharding = *Divide the Data*. Replication = *Copy the Data*. These are orthogonal but commonly combined (sharded clusters often replicate each shard for high availability).

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Shard** | An independent database node storing a horizontal subset of the data. |
| **Shard Key** | The attribute (or composite attribute) used to route records to a specific shard. |
| **Shard Router** | The middleware/proxy that intercepts queries and routes them to the correct shard. |
| **Config Server** | A metadata repository that maps shard-key ranges to physical shard locations. |
| **Hot Spot** | A shard receiving disproportionately high traffic due to a skewed shard key. |
| **Resharding** | The process of redistributing data across shards when scaling or rebalancing. |
| **Consistent Hashing** | A hashing strategy that minimizes data movement when shards are added/removed. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — The "Why" and "How"

**Step 1: Selection of Shard Key**
The most critical decision in sharding. The shard key must exhibit:
- **High Cardinality** — large number of distinct values to distribute data evenly.
- **Even Distribution** — no skew toward a few values.
- **Query Locality** — most queries should include the shard key as a filter.

**Step 2: Hashing / Partitioning Function Application**
The system applies a function `H(shard_key) → shard_id` to compute the destination. Common functions:
- **Hash Function** (e.g., MurmurHash, MD5, CRC32) → uniform distribution.
- **Range Function** (e.g., `A–F → Shard 1`, `G–M → Shard 2`) → preserves ordering.

**Step 3: Routing of Query**
The Shard Router intercepts the SQL/NoSQL query, extracts the shard key, computes the target shard(s), and forwards the query. Results are merged if the query spans multiple shards (a *scatter-gather* operation).

**Step 4: Rebalancing**
When new shards are added, data is redistributed. **Consistent Hashing** ensures only `K/N` keys (where K = total keys, N = number of shards) need to be remapped, versus `K` keys in naive hashing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A query that does **NOT** include the shard key in its WHERE clause is called a **scatter-gather query** — it must hit *every* shard, which is expensive and defeats the purpose of sharding.

---

### 2.2 Sharding Architectures (KTU Mandatory)

#### A. Key-Based (Hashed) Sharding
$$H(k) \mod N = \text{shard\_id}$$
where `k` = shard key value, `N` = number of shards, `H` = hash function.

- ✅ Uniform data distribution.
- ❌ Range queries (`WHERE k BETWEEN x AND y`) become scatter-gather.

#### B. Range-Based Sharding
Data is split by value ranges:
- Shard 1: `id ∈ [1, 1,000,000)`
- Shard 2: `id ∈ [1,000,000, 2,000,000)`

- ✅ Efficient range queries.
- ❌ Risk of **hot spots** (e.g., recent orders clustered in one shard).

#### C. Directory-Based Sharding
A lookup table maps shard-key values to physical shards.

- ✅ Flexible, supports custom logic (geo, customer-tier).
- ❌ The directory itself becomes a single point of failure / bottleneck.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Hash-based Shard ID | $H(k) \mod N$ | Uniform distribution routing |
| Consistent Hashing Position | $p = H(k) \mod 2^{m}$ (ring of size $2^m$) | Minimize reshuffling on scale |
| Fraction of keys remapped on shard addition | $\dfrac{1}{N+1}$ (for consistent hashing) | Rebalancing cost estimation |
| Fraction of keys remapped (naive mod-N) | $\dfrac{N}{N+1}$ (almost ALL keys) | Compare efficiency |
| Cross-shard query overhead | $O(N)$ scatter-gather | Detect anti-patterns |
| Per-shard write throughput | $\dfrac{T_{total}}{N}$ (ideal) | Capacity planning |
| Hot-spot risk index | $\text{HSI} = \dfrac{\max(\text{load}_{i})}{\mu(\text{load})}$ | Skew detection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Sharding powers the data tier of **MongoDB sharded clusters**, **Google Spanner**, **Amazon DynamoDB partitions**, **Cassandra's vnodes**, and **YouTube's MySQL layer (Vitess)**. The same theoretical model underpins **HDFS block placement** and **Elasticsearch index shards**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Remapping Cost — Naive Mod-N vs Consistent Hashing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** and tests both mathematical maturity and architectural understanding.

**Given:**
- Total number of data items: $K$
- Original number of shards: $N$
- After scaling: $N+1$ shards
- Goal: Find the fraction of items that must be moved.

---

#### Case A: Naive `H(k) mod N` Sharding

A record belongs to shard `s` if:

$$s = H(k) \mod N$$

After increasing to $N+1$ shards, a record belongs to shard `s'` if:

$$s' = H(k) \mod (N+1)$$

A record is **NOT moved** only if $s = s'$, i.e., $H(k) \mod N = H(k) \mod (N+1)$.

Since the moduli differ, the only way equality holds is if both $H(k) \mod N$ and $H(k) \mod (N+1)$ fall in the intersection of valid remainders. Because the cycles of $N$ and $N+1$ are co-prime in their remainders for arbitrary hash output, the **only guaranteed invariant values** are those where the hash is congruent under both — but in practice, for a well-distributed hash:

$$P(s = s') = \frac{1}{N+1}$$

Therefore, the **fraction that must be moved** is:

$$f_{\text{naive}} = 1 - \frac{1}{N+1} = \frac{N}{N+1}$$

**Conclusion:** For $N = 10$ shards, adding an 11th shard forces remapping of $\frac{10}{11} \approx 90.9\%$ of all data — effectively a *full* reshuffle.

---

#### Case B: Consistent Hashing

In consistent hashing, both **shards and keys** are mapped to points on a ring of size $2^m$ (typically $m = 32$ or $m = 160$):

$$p_{\text{key}} = H_{\text{key}}(k) \mod 2^{m}$$
$$p_{\text{shard},i} = H_{\text{shard}}(i) \mod 2^{m}$$

Each key belongs to the **next shard clockwise** on the ring.

When a new shard $S_{N+1}$ is inserted at position $p_{N+1}$, it claims all keys that lie in the arc between $p_{N+1}$ and the *next counter-clockwise* shard.

Since shards are placed (on average) uniformly on the ring, the arc length claimed is $\frac{1}{N+1}$ of the total ring circumference.

Therefore:

$$f_{\text{consistent}} = \frac{1}{N+1}$$

**Conclusion:** For $N = 10$, only $\frac{1}{11} \approx 9.1\%$ of data is remapped.

---

#### Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Aspect | Naive `mod N` | Consistent Hashing |
|---|---|---|
| Fraction remapped on $N \rightarrow N+1$ | $\dfrac{N}{N+1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{N+1}$ |
| Remapping for $N=10 \rightarrow 11$ | $\approx 90.9\%$ | $\approx 9.1\%$ |
| Range query support | ❌ No | ❌ No (requires range shards) |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Moderate (virtual nodes) |
| Used by | Early MySQL proxies | DynamoDB, Cassandra, Akka |

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{f_{\text{naive}}}{f_{\text{consistent}}} = \dfrac{N/(N+1)}{1/(N+1)} = N\;}$$

> **Interpretation:** Consistent hashing is exactly **N times** more efficient than naive `mod-N` hashing during resharding.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimal Number of Shards (Capacity Planning)

Let:
- $D$ = total data size in GB
- $S_{\max}$ = max data per shard (GB) — bounded by RAM/index memory
- $Q$ = peak queries per second (QPS)
- $Q_{\text{shard}}$ = max QPS a single shard can sustain

The required number of shards is:

$$N = \max\left(\left\lceil \frac{D}{S_{\max}} \right\rceil,\; \left\lceil \frac{Q}{Q_{\text{shard}}} \right\rceil\right)$$

**Worked Example:**
A social media platform has:
- $D = 20{,}000$ GB
- $S_{\max} = 1{,}000$ GB per shard (memory limit)
- $Q = 80{,}000$ reads/sec
- $Q_{\text{shard}} = 10{,}000$ reads/sec

By storage: $\left\lceil \frac{20{,}000}{1{,}000} \right\rceil = 20$ shards.
By throughput: $\left\lceil \frac{80{,}000}{10{,}000} \right\rceil = 8$ shards.

$$N = \max(20, 8) = 20 \text{ shards}$$

> **Design Heuristic:** Always over-provision by 2× to accommodate future growth and rebalancing headroom. Production recommendation: deploy **40 shards** with consistent hashing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Sharding Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client Application]
    Router[Shard Router / Query Proxy]
    Config[Config Server / Metadata Store]
    S1[Shard 1: Users A-F]
    S2[Shard 2: Users G-M]
    S3[Shard 3: Users N-S]
    S4[Shard 4: Users T-Z]
    Rep1[Replica 1a]
    Rep2[Replica 2a]
    Rep3[Replica 3a]
    Rep4[Replica 4a]

    Client -->|SQL or NoSQL Query| Router
    Router <-->|Reads shard map| Config
    Router -->|Route by H_key mod 4| S1
    Router -->|Route by H_key mod 4| S2
    Router -->|Route by H_key mod 4| S3
    Router -->|Route by H_key mod 4| S4
    S1 -.->|Async replication| Rep1
    S2 -.->|Async replication| Rep2
    S3 -.->|Async replication| Rep3
    S4 -.->|Async replication| Rep4
```

> **Read this diagram as:** Each primary shard holds a unique row subset, and async replicas provide fault tolerance. The Router is stateless and consults the Config Server for shard topology.

---

### 4.2 Consistent Hashing Ring — Rebalancing Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RingBefore[Consistent Hash Ring with 3 Shards]
    RingAfter[Same Ring after adding Shard 4]

    subgraph BeforeState [Before: 3 Shards]
        K1[Key K1]
        K2[Key K2]
        K3[Key K3]
        K4[Key K4]
        K5[Key K5]
        K6[Key K6]
        S1A[Shard S1]
        S2A[Shard S2]
        S3A[Shard S3]
    end

    subgraph AfterState [After: 4 Shards, only 1/4 keys moved]
        K1B[Key K1]
        K2B[Key K2]
        K3B[Key K3 moves]
        K4B[Key K4]
        K5B[Key K5 moves]
        K6B[Key K6]
        S1B[Shard S1]
        S2B[Shard S2]
        S3B[Shard S3]
        S4B[Shard S4 NEW]
    end

    BeforeState --> AfterState
```

> **Interpretation:** Adding a new shard to a consistent-hashing ring affects only the keys in the immediately adjacent arc — roughly `1/(N+1)` of total keys. The other keys remain untouched.

---

### 4.3 Sharding Strategy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Dataset Exceeds Single Node Capacity])
    Q1{Is the workload dominated by range queries?}
    Q2{Is the shard key high cardinality and evenly distributed?}
    Q3{Do you need flexible routing logic e.g. geo or tier based?}

    A1[Use Range Based Sharding]
    A2[Use Hash Based Sharding]
    A3[Use Directory Based Sharding]
    End([Deploy Sharded Cluster with Config Server plus Router plus N Shards])

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2
    Q2 -->|No| Q1
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3
    Q3 -->|No| A1
    A1 --> End
    A2 --> End
    A3 --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between horizontal partitioning (sharding) and vertical partitioning with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Horizontal Partitioning (Sharding) | Vertical Partitioning |
|---|---|---|
| Split dimension | Rows | Columns |
| Schema per partition | Same schema | Different schemas (subsets of columns) |
| Typical use | Scale write throughput, large tables | Separate hot/cold columns, security isolation |
| Example | `users` table split into `users_shard1 (rows 1–1M)`, `users_shard2 (rows 1M–2M)` | `users` split into `users_profile (id, name, email)` in DB1 and `users_activity (id, last_login, clicks)` in DB2 |

**Award:** 1 mark for each correct row, 1 mark for the example. (3 marks total)

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is a "hot spot" in a sharded cluster? How does it arise?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hot spot** is a shard that receives a disproportionately large share of read or write traffic, causing it to become a performance bottleneck while other shards remain underutilized.

**Common causes:**
1. **Skewed shard key** — e.g., using `country` as shard key when 70% of users are from one country.
2. **Monotonic shard key** — e.g., using `auto-increment ID` or `created_at` causes all recent writes to land on the *last* shard (range sharding anti-pattern).
3. **Celebrity / viral content** — a single user or post attracts most queries.

**Mitigation:** Choose a high-cardinality composite key, use salted/hashed keys, or apply virtual shards with consistent hashing.

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for causes, 1 mark for mitigation — 3 marks total)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a sharded database system with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the **Config Server** and the **Shard Router**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain **Range-Based** and **Hash-Based** sharding strategies. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A sharded database system consists of **three core components** illustrated in Section 4.1 above:

**1. Shard Router (Query Router / mongos in MongoDB):**
- Stateless proxy that sits between the application and the shards.
- Intercepts every query, parses it, extracts the shard key.
- Consults the Config Server to resolve which shard(s) hold the relevant data.
- Forwards the query, optionally merging results from multiple shards (scatter-gather).
- **Marks awarded:** (1 mark for identification, 1 mark for role, 1 mark for scatter-gather explanation) → 3 marks.

**2. Config Server (Metadata Service):**
- Stores the **shard topology**: which shard-key range maps to which physical shard.
- Stores chunk/partition metadata, replication configuration, and ACLs.
- In MongoDB, deployed as a **replica set** of 3 nodes for HA.
- Acts as the *single source of truth* for routing decisions.
- **Marks awarded:** (1 mark for metadata, 1 mark for HA deployment, 1 mark for example MongoDB) → 3 marks.

**3. Shards (Data Nodes):**
- Independent databases (often sharded *and* replicated — "shard replica set").
- Each shard contains a unique horizontal subset of the logical dataset.
- **Marks awarded:** (1 mark for description) → 1 mark.

> **Total for (a): 7 marks** ✅

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Range-Based Sharding:**
Data is partitioned by contiguous value ranges of the shard key.
- Shard 1: `customer_id` ∈ `[1, 1,000,000]`
- Shard 2: `customer_id` ∈ `[1,000,001, 2,000,000]`
- Shard 3: `customer_id` ∈ `[2,000,001, 3,000,000]`

| Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|
| ✅ Range queries are efficient (single shard hit) | ❌ Risk of hot spots if data is skewed |
| ✅ Easy to understand and implement | ❌ Manual rebalancing needed for uneven ranges |
| ✅ Natural chronological ordering preserved | ❌ Static range boundaries |

**Hash-Based Sharding:**
A hash function distributes keys uniformly:
- `shard_id = H(customer_id) mod N`

| Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|
| ✅ Uniform data distribution | ❌ Range queries become scatter-gather (expensive) |
| ✅ No hot spots for well-distributed keys | ❌ Reordering data is impossible without re-shard |
| ✅ Automatic load balancing | ❌ Hash collisions need handling |

**Comparative summary table (required for full marks):**

| Criterion | Range-Based | Hash-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Range query support | Excellent | Poor (scatter-gather) |
| Data distribution | Uneven (manual tuning) | Uniform |
| Hot spot risk | High | Low |
| Rebalancing cost | High (repartition) | High (rehash) |
| Best for | Time-series, logs | User data, social graphs |

> **Total for (b): 7 marks** ✅

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the fraction of data items that must be remapped when a new shard is added in **(i)** naive `mod-N` hashing and **(ii)** consistent hashing. Show that consistent hashing remaps only $1/N$ of the data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **pros and cons of sharding** with respect to scalability, availability, complexity, and cross-shard queries. What is a scatter-gather query and why is it problematic? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes.

**Summary of the derivation expected in the answer:**

**(i) Naive `mod-N` Hashing:** (3 marks)
- A record's shard is `s = H(k) mod N`.
- After adding a shard, it becomes `s' = H(k) mod (N+1)`.
- A record is unmoved only if `s = s'`, probability = $1/(N+1)$.
- **Fraction moved = $N/(N+1)$** — for $N=10$, this is $\approx 90.9\%$.

**(ii) Consistent Hashing:** (3 marks)
- Both keys and shards map to a ring of size $2^m$.
- New shard $S_{N+1}$ claims the arc of size $1/(N+1)$ of the ring.
- Only keys in that arc are remapped.
- **Fraction moved = $1/(N+1)$** — for $N=10$, this is $\approx 9.1\%$.

**Comparison / Ratio (1 mark):**
$$\frac{f_{\text{naive}}}{f_{\text{consistent}}} = \frac{N/(N+1)}{1/(N+1)} = N$$
Consistent hashing is **N times more efficient**.

> **Total for (a): 7 marks** ✅

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Pros of Sharding:** (3 marks)
1. **Scalability:** Linear horizontal scaling — add shards to handle more data/load.
2. **Performance:** Each shard handles a smaller dataset → faster queries, better index fit in memory.
3. **Availability:** Failure of one shard affects only a subset of users; not a total outage.
4. **Geographic distribution:** Shards can be placed near users for lower latency.

**Cons of Sharding:** (3 marks)
1. **Complexity:** Application logic, schema migrations, and backups become shard-aware.
2. **Cross-shard queries:** Joins across shards are expensive and sometimes impossible.
3. **Hot spots & rebalancing:** Uneven shard-key distribution requires resharding — an operational hazard.
4. **Limited transactions:** ACID transactions are hard to guarantee across shards (CAP trade-off).

**Scatter-Gather Query — Why Problematic:** (1 mark)
A scatter-gather query is one that lacks a shard-key filter in its WHERE clause. The router must forward the query to *all* shards, collect partial results, and merge them.
- **Why problematic:** Latency becomes the *maximum* shard response time, not the minimum; resource consumption scales with $N$; defeats the parallelism benefit of sharding.

> **Total for (b): 7 marks** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing!**
> 1. **Confusing sharding with replication:** A common error. Remember: *sharding = divide, replication = copy*. Marks are deducted heavily.
> 2. **Forgetting the Config Server in diagrams:** A sharded cluster has *three* components, not two. Omitting the Config Server = –2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation in 14-mark questions:** For the remapping derivation, partial credit is given for setting up equations; skipping directly to the answer = 0 marks.
> 4. **Using "sharding" and "partitioning" interchangeably:** KTU expects precision — *sharding is horizontal partitioning across independent servers*; *partitioning* alone can be intra-server.
> 5. **Not giving examples in 7-mark sub-parts:** Always anchor theoretical answers with a real system (MongoDB, Cassandra, DynamoDB, Vitess).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Sharding = horizontal partitioning across independent databases**; same schema, different row subsets.
- 🔑 The **three pillars** of a sharded system: **Shard Router + Config Server + Shards**.
- 🔑 **Shard Key selection** is the single most consequential design decision — high cardinality, even distribution, query locality.
- 🔑 **Three sharding strategies:** Hash-based (uniform), Range-based (query-friendly, hot-spot prone), Directory-based (flexible, SPOF risk).
- 🔑 **Consistent Hashing** remaps only $\frac{1}{N+1}$ of data when a shard is added, vs $\frac{N}{N+1}$ for naive `mod-N` — a factor of $N$ improvement.
- 🔑 **Scatter-gather queries** (those without a shard-key predicate) defeat the purpose of sharding; avoid them in application design.
- 🔑 **Hot spots** arise from skewed/monotonic shard keys; mitigate with hashing, salting, or virtual nodes.
- 🔑 **Sharding ≠ Replication:** Sharding scales *writes* and *data volume*; replication scales *reads* and *availability* — they are combined in production.
- 🔑 **CAP Trade-off:** Cross-shard transactions are hard; most sharded systems relax consistency to *eventual consistency* (BASE).
- 🔑 **Production examples to cite:** MongoDB Sharded Cluster, Google Spanner, Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra vnodes, YouTube's Vitess on MySQL.
- 🔑 **Formula to memorize:** $f_{\text{naive}} = \frac{N}{N+1}$, $f_{\text{consistent}} = \frac{1}{N+1}$, $N_{\text{optimal}} = \max\!\left(\lceil D/S_{\max}\rceil,\, \lceil Q/Q_{\text{shard}}\rceil\right)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deployment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deployment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Database Deployment** is the structured, multi-phase engineering process of installing, configuring, distributing, integrating, and activating a database management system (DBMS) and its associated data across heterogeneous computing environments — ranging from on-premises clusters to distributed, cloud, and containerized infrastructures — such that the system becomes operationally ready to serve production-grade transactions and analytical queries with guaranteed availability, consistency, and performance.

In the context of **PECDT795 – Advanced Database Systems (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**, *Deployment* specifically covers:

1. **Fragmentation** (Horizontal, Vertical, Hybrid) and **Allocation** of relational data across network sites.
2. **Replication** topologies — Primary–Replica, Multi-Master, Peer-to-Peer.
3. **Centralized, Distributed, Parallel, Cloud, and Hybrid** deployment architectures.
4. **Database Migration** strategies (Big-Bang, Trickle, Parallel).
5. **Modern Container-Native** deployment (Docker, Kubernetes, DBaaS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chain of Warehouses

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you own a national retail company. You have **one huge warehouse** in Delhi with all 10,000 products. Customers in Kerala wait 4 days for deliveries. The problem isn't the warehouse — it's that *the data is in the wrong place*. **Deployment** is the decision-making process where you either:
> 1. **Replicate** the entire warehouse in Kerala (full copy), or
> 2. **Fragment** it — Kerala warehouse only holds tropical products (horizontal split), or only holds product *details* like images & descriptions (vertical split), or
> 3. **Move to the cloud** so any customer connects to the nearest AWS/Azure data center.
>
> The "best" choice depends on **how often customers query, how often data changes, and the cost of communication** — exactly the trade-offs we will mathematically model in Section 2.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Locked In

| Term | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Fragmentation** | Splitting one logical relation into smaller physical pieces. |
| **Allocation** | Deciding *where* (which site) each fragment is stored. |
| **Replication** | Maintaining multiple synchronized copies of the same fragment. |
| **Site** | A physically distinct node (server, data center, cloud region). |
| **DBaaS** | Database-as-a-Service; managed cloud deployment (RDS, Cosmos DB). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Database Deployment

A KTU examiner expects a student to know the deployment decision as a **4-stage pipeline**:

#### Pillar 1 — Fragmentation Strategy
* **Horizontal Fragmentation (HF):** A relation $R$ is split by *rows* using a selection predicate $p_i$. Each fragment $R_i = \sigma_{p_i}(R)$ and $\bigcup_i R_i = R$.
* **Vertical Fragmentation (VF):** A relation is split by *columns/attributes* via projection. Tuple-identifier (TID) is included in every fragment to allow lossless join.
* **Hybrid / Mixed Fragmentation:** Apply HF on the result of VF (or vice-versa).
* **Correctness Rules (must hold for any valid fragmentation):**
  1. **Completeness** — every data item of $R$ appears in *at least one* fragment.
  2. **Reconstruction** — the original relation $R$ must be reconstructable via a lossless operation (union for HF, natural join for VF).
  3. **Disjointness** — fragments should not overlap (HF only; VF cannot be fully disjoint because of the TID).

#### Pillar 2 — Allocation Strategy (Non-Replicated vs Replicated)
* **Non-replicated (Partitioned):** Each fragment lives at **exactly one** site.
* **Replicated:** Each fragment is stored at **all** query sites (full replication) or at a chosen subset (partial replication).
* Decision driver: **Read frequency vs. Update frequency** (rule explained in 2.3 below).

#### Pillar 3 — Replication & Consistency Model
* **Synchronous Replication:** Update is committed at *all* replicas before the transaction returns success → strong consistency, higher latency.
* **Asynchronous Replication:** Update commits at primary, propagates later → low latency, risk of stale reads.
* **Quorum-Based Replication:** Read quorum $R_q$ + Write quorum $W_q$ such that $R_q + W_q > N$ and $W_q + W_q > N$ (where $N$ = number of replicas). This is the foundation of Dynamo/Cassandra.

#### Pillar 4 — Deployment Topology
* **Centralized:** Single site, single DBMS (no distribution).
* **Client–Server Distributed:** Multiple clients, one or few DBMS servers.
* **Peer-to-Peer (Fully Distributed):** Every site is both client and server.
* **Cloud DBaaS:** Managed service (AWS RDS, Azure SQL, Google Cloud Spanner).
* **Container-Native:** DB deployed as Docker/Kubernetes pod with persistent volumes.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **examination-critical**. Memorize the variable definitions; KTU questions will provide numerical values and expect you to plug them in.

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | $T(R) = \min_i \sum_{q \in Q} f_q \cdot \min(\text{local cost}, \text{remote cost})$ | **Allocation Cost Model** — pick the site $i$ that minimizes total query processing cost. |
| F2 | $\text{Benefit of Replication} = \sum_{q \in Q_{read}} f_q \cdot (C_{remote} - C_{local}) - \sum_{u \in U_{write}} g_u \cdot C_{update\_all}$ | Decide if a fragment should be replicated. Replicate **only if** Benefit > 0. |
| F3 | $C_{local} = c_0 + c_1 \cdot X$ , $C_{remote} = c_0 + c_1 \cdot X + c_{msg} \cdot N_{msg}$ | Local vs remote query cost: local is just CPU+I/O; remote adds message-passing cost. |
| F4 | $N_{r\_replicas} = \lfloor N_{total\_replicas} \cdot (1 - \text{read\_latency\_budget}) \rfloor$ | Practical replication count for a latency SLA. |
| F5 | $R_q + W_q > N$  and  $W_q + W_q > N$ | **Quorum Rule** (Cassandra / Dynamo style). |
| F6 | $\text{CAP Trade-off} \Rightarrow \text{Choose exactly 2 of } \{C, A, P\}$ | A distributed DB must sacrifice one property under network partition. |
| F7 | $\text{Recovery Time (RTO)} = T_{detect} + T_{failover} + T_{verify}$ | Downtime budget for cloud / replicated deployments. |
| F8 | $\text{Migration Window} = T_{full\_dump} + T_{incremental\_catchup} + T_{cutover}$ | Used in Big-Bang and Trickle migration planning. |

Where:
* $f_q$ = frequency of query $q$ per unit time,
* $c_0$ = fixed query-initiation cost,
* $c_1$ = per-tuple processing cost,
* $c_{msg}$ = cost of one network message,
* $N_{msg}$ = number of inter-site messages for query $q$,
* $g_u$ = frequency of update $u$.

### 2.3 The Famous KTU Decision Heuristic (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb — When to Replicate vs. Fragment-Only:**
> *If a fragment is **read more than 20×** for every **1 write**, replicate it. Otherwise, keep it single-copy (fragmented).*
> Formal justification: this is the *Read–Write Ratio (RWR)* threshold used in standard distributed database textbooks.
> $$\text{RWR} = \frac{\sum f_{q\_read}}{\sum g_{u\_write}} \geq 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{REPLICATE}$$
> $$\text{RWR} < 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{KEEP PARTITIONED}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
* **E-commerce** (Amazon, Flipkart) — Vertical fragmentation for product catalog, horizontal fragmentation for user orders by region, full replication of session/login data.
* **Banking** — Synchronous replication across two data centers for zero data loss (RTO ≈ 0, RPO = 0).
* **Social Media** (Facebook, Instagram) — Asynchronous multi-region replication with eventual consistency.
* **IoT / Telemetry** — Asynchronous, write-heavy, partition-tolerant (AP systems like Cassandra).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Site for a Single Fragment (Allocation Cost Minimization)

**Problem Statement (KTU-Standard):**
> A relation fragment $F$ is read by three queries $q_1, q_2, q_3$ with access frequencies $f_1, f_2, f_3$ at site $S_1$ and with frequencies $f'_1, f'_2, f'_3$ at site $S_2$. Find the allocation that minimizes total cost.

**Step 1 — Write the total cost function.**

The cost at any candidate site $S$ equals the sum of *local* processing cost (when the query originates at $S$) and *remote* processing cost (when the query originates elsewhere and must fetch $F$ across the network).

$$
C_{total}(F \text{ at site } S) = \sum_{q \in Q} f_q \cdot C_{local}(q, S) \;+\; \sum_{q \in Q} f_q' \cdot C_{remote}(q, S)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the local and remote cost sub-formulas.**

Using F3 from the cheat sheet, $C_{local} = c_0 + c_1 \cdot X$ and $C_{remote} = c_0 + c_1 \cdot X + c_{msg} \cdot N_{msg}$:

$$
C_{total}(S) = \sum_{q} f_q (c_0 + c_1 X) \;+\; \sum_{q} f_q' (c_0 + c_1 X + c_{msg} N_{msg})
$$

**Step 3 — Group constant terms and the $c_{msg}$ term.**

$$
C_{total}(S) = (c_0 + c_1 X)\!\left(\sum_q f_q + \sum_q f_q'\right) \;+\; c_{msg} \sum_q f_q' N_{msg}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the minimization operator.**

$$
S^{*} = \arg\min_{S \in \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}} \; C_{total}(S)
$$

**Step 5 — Decision logic for the examiner.**

Since the first grouped term $(c_0 + c_1 X)\bigl(\sum f_q + \sum f_q'\bigr)$ is **independent of the site $S$** (it depends only on total access frequency, which is fixed), the *minimization reduces to*:

$$
S^{*} = \arg\min_{S} \; c_{msg} \sum_{q} f_q' N_{msg}(q, S)
$$

Therefore, allocate $F$ to the site that **minimizes the total inter-site message traffic** $\sum_q f_q' N_{msg}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quorum Numbers (CAP + Dynamo)

**Problem Statement:**
> A key-value store has $N = 5$ replicas. Design a *balanced* quorum where reads and writes have equal latency budget.

**Step 1 — Apply Quorum Rule 1: $R_q + W_q > N$.**

$$
R_q + W_q > 5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Quorum Rule 2: $W_q + W_q > N$ (to prevent write–write conflict).**

$$
2 W_q > 5 \;\Rightarrow\; W_q \geq 3
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $W_q = 3$ into Rule 1.**

$$
R_q > 5 - 3 = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; R_q \geq 3
$$

**Step 4 — Final balanced quorum.**

$$
\boxed{\,R_q = 3,\;\; W_q = 3,\;\; N = 5\,}
$$

Verification: $3 + 3 = 6 > 5$ ✓, $3 + 3 = 6 > 5$ ✓. The system can tolerate $N - W_q = 2$ failed nodes during a write, and $N - R_q = 2$ failed nodes during a read.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Replicate or Not?

**Given Data (KTU-style problem):**
* A fragment $F$ is read by 4 queries, each fired **1200 times/hour** from site $S_1$.
* It is updated by 1 transaction, fired **50 times/hour** from site $S_2$.
* Cost of remote read: $C_{remote} = 10$ units.
* Cost of local read: $C_{local} = 1$ unit.
* Cost of updating one replica: $C_{update} = 2$ units.
* We are evaluating **full replication** (2 replicas).

**Step 1 — Compute the read benefit (savings if we replicate to $S_1$).**

$$
\text{Read Benefit} = \sum f_{read} \cdot (C_{remote} - C_{local}) = (4 \times 1200) \times (10 - 1) = 4800 \times 9 = 43{,}200 \text{ units/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the update cost (extra cost of keeping two copies in sync).**

$$
\text{Update Cost} = \sum g_{update} \cdot C_{update} \cdot N_{replicas} = 50 \times 2 \times 2 = 200 \text{ units/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Formula F2 (Net Benefit).**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = 43{,}200 - 200 = 43{,}000 \text{ units/hr} > 0
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} \gg 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{REPLICATE the fragment}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check with RWR heuristic.**

$$
\text{RWR} = \frac{4 \times 1200}{50} = \frac{4800}{50} = 96 \geq 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{REPLICATE} \;\checkmark
$$

Both methods agree — a classic KTU "double-verification" answer that scores full marks.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Deployment Architecture

| Scenario / Requirement | Best Deployment | Fragmentation | Replication | Consistency | Example System |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Global e-commerce catalog (heavy reads, global users) | Distributed + Cloud | Vertical + Horizontal | Asynchronous, multi-region | Eventual | Cassandra, Cosmos DB |
| Banking core ledger (zero data loss mandatory) | Distributed (2-site) | Horizontal by account-id | Synchronous | Strong | Oracle RAC, Spanner |
| Single startup, < 1000 users | Centralized (single VPS) | None | None | Strong | PostgreSQL on one server |
| Real-time analytics on streaming IoT | Cloud Data Warehouse | Horizontal (sharded) | Asynchronous, columnar | Eventual | Snowflake, BigQuery |
| Microservices app with short-lived DBs | Container-Native (K8s) | Schema-per-service | None or async | Per-service | PostgreSQL on K8s StatefulSet |
| Mobile offline-first app | Edge + Cloud Sync | Vertical (per feature) | Async with conflict resolution (CRDT) | Eventual | Firebase, Realm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Deployment Architecture — Choosing the Right Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Database Requirement] --> B0{Data Volume and User Geography}
    B0 -- Single Region, Small --> C0[Centralized Deployment]
    B0 -- Multi-Region, Large --> D0{Distribution Strategy}
    D0 -- Partition by rows --> E0[Horizontal Fragmentation]
    D0 -- Partition by columns --> F0[Vertical Fragmentation]
    D0 -- Both --> G0[Hybrid Fragmentation]
    E0 --> H0{Read Write Ratio Check}
    F0 --> H0
    G0 --> H0
    H0 -- RWR greater than 20 --> I0[Replicated Allocation]
    H0 -- RWR less than 20 --> J0[Partitioned Non Replicated]
    I0 --> K0{Replication Mode}
    J0 --> L0[Non Replicated Sites]
    K0 -- Zero data loss --> M0[Synchronous Replication]
    K0 -- Low latency --> N0[Asynchronous Replication]
    K0 -- Balanced Quorum --> O0[Quorum Based Rq Wq]
    M0 --> P0{CAP Tradeoff}
    N0 --> P0
    O0 --> P0
    P0 -- Choose CP --> Q0[Strong Consistency Systems]
    P0 -- Choose AP --> R0[Eventual Consistency Systems]
    L0 --> S0[Final Topology]
    Q0 --> S0
    R0 --> S0
    C0 --> S0
    S0 --> T0[Cloud DBaaS or Container Native Kubernetes]
```

### 4.2 Fragmentation and Allocation Pipeline (Detailed View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Original Relation R] --> B1[Step 1: Identify Access Patterns]
    B1 --> C1[Step 2: Derive Min Term Predicates]
    C1 --> D1[Step 3: Horizontal Fragmentation HF]
    A1 --> E1[Step 4: Find Affinity Matrix of Attributes]
    E1 --> F1[Step 5: Cluster Attributes]
    F1 --> G1[Step 6: Vertical Fragmentation VF]
    D1 --> H1[Step 7: Apply Correctness Rules]
    G1 --> H1
    H1 -- Complete, Reconstructable, Disjoint --> I1[Step 8: Cost Model Evaluation]
    I1 --> J1{Benefit Greater Than Zero}
    J1 -- Yes --> K1[Step 9a: Replicate]
    J1 -- No --> L1[Step 9b: Keep Single Copy]
    K1 --> M1[Step 10: Choose Sync or Async Mode]
    L1 --> M1
    M1 --> N1[Step 11: Deploy to Cloud DBaaS or Container]
```

### 4.3 Replication Topology — Master–Slave vs Multi-Master vs Quorum

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph subMasterSlave["Master Slave Topology"]
        masterA[Master Node] -->|Async stream| slaveA1[Replica 1]
        masterA -->|Async stream| slaveA2[Replica 2]
    end
    subgraph subMultiMaster["Multi Master Topology"]
        mmA[Master Region 1] <-->|Bidirectional sync| mmB[Master Region 2]
        mmB <-->|Bidirectional sync| mmC[Master Region 3]
    end
    subgraph subQuorum["Quorum Based Topology N equals 5"]
        qcA[Node 1] -.-> qcC[Coordinator]
        qcB[Node 2] -.-> qcC
        qcD[Node 3] -.-> qcC
        qcE[Node 4] -.-> qcC
        qcF[Node 5] -.-> qcC
    end
```

### 4.4 Deployment-to-Cloud Migration Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[On Premise Legacy DB] --> B2{Choose Migration Strategy}
    B2 -- Tight deadline, small DB --> C2[Big Bang Migration]
    B2 -- Zero downtime, large DB --> D2[Trickle Migration]
    B2 -- Highest safety, mission critical --> E2[Parallel Run Migration]
    C2 --> F2[Step 1: Full Dump]
    D2 --> F2
    E2 --> F2
    F2 --> G2[Step 2: Incremental CDC Capture]
    G2 --> H2[Step 3: Dual Write Window]
    H2 --> I2[Step 4: Data Validation and Reconciliation]
    I2 --> J2[Step 5: Cutover to Cloud DBaaS]
    J2 --> K2[Step 6: Decommission Legacy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define horizontal and vertical fragmentation. State the three correctness rules that any fragmentation scheme must satisfy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **Horizontal Fragmentation (1 m):** A relation $R$ is partitioned into disjoint subsets of *tuples* using selection predicates $p_i$, such that $R_i = \sigma_{p_i}(R)$ and $\bigcup_i R_i = R$.
* **Vertical Fragmentation (1 m):** A relation is partitioned into subsets of *attributes* via projection $R_i = \pi_{A_i}(R)$; a tuple-identifier (TID) is replicated across all vertical fragments to permit lossless natural join.
* **Correctness Rules (1 m):** *Completeness* (every tuple in some fragment), *Reconstruction* (original $R$ recoverable via lossless union or join), and *Disjointness* (no overlap of tuples for HF; not strictly applicable to VF because of TID).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous replication. In which type of application is each most suitable?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **Synchronous Replication (1 m):** An update is committed at the primary *and* all replicas before the transaction returns success. Guarantees strong consistency and **zero data loss**, but adds round-trip latency proportional to network RTT to the slowest replica.
* **Asynchronous Replication (1 m):** The update commits at the primary and propagates to replicas *afterwards*. Provides low write latency but exposes a window of *replication lag* during which replicas may return stale data.
* **Application Fit (1 m):** Synchronous → banking ledgers, payment systems, inventory-critical ERP. Asynchronous → social media feeds, product catalogs, content management, analytics workloads.

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Consider a relation `EMPLOYEE(EmpID, Name, Dept, Salary, Region)` accessed by two sites — **Site_A** (HQ, frequent reads) and **Site_B** (Branch, moderate reads/writes). The following access statistics are collected:*
* *Query Q1 (issued 1000 times/day at Site_A, 200 times/day at Site_B): `SELECT * FROM EMPLOYEE WHERE Region='South'`, retrieves 50 tuples.*
* *Query Q2 (issued 50 times/day at Site_B): `UPDATE EMPLOYEE SET Salary=Salary*1.1 WHERE Dept='IT'`, affects 100 tuples.*
* *Local cost per tuple: 1 unit. Remote cost adds 5 units per tuple accessed plus 2 units per message. Each remote query requires 1 message.*
* *Cost of updating one replica = 2 units per tuple updated.*

*Compute: (i) the cost of **non-replicated** allocation, (ii) the cost of **fully replicated** allocation at both sites, (iii) decide the best strategy with justification. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Cost of Q1 (selective read).**
Local cost at Site_A = $1000 \times 50 \times 1 = 50{,}000$ units.
Local cost at Site_B = $200 \times 50 \times 1 = 10{,}000$ units.

**Step 2 — Cost of Q2 (update, only from Site_B).**
Affected tuples = 100, fires 50 times → $50 \times 100 \times 1 = 5{,}000$ units local.

**Step 3 — Total cost for non-replicated (assume data at Site_A).**
* Q1 at Site_A: local, **50,000 units**.
* Q1 at Site_B: remote, $200 \times (50 \times 1 + 1 \times 2) = 200 \times 52 = 10{,}400$ units.
* Q2 at Site_B: remote, $50 \times (100 \times 1 + 1 \times 2) = 50 \times 102 = 5{,}100$ units.
$$
C_{nonrep} = 50{,}000 + 10{,}400 + 5{,}100 = \mathbf{65{,}500 \text{ units/day}}
$$

**Step 4 — Total cost for fully replicated (data at both sites).**
* Q1 at Site_A: local, 50,000 units.
* Q1 at Site_B: local, 10,000 units.
* Q2 at Site_B: local write, but **must update both replicas** → $50 \times 100 \times (1 + 2) = 15{,}000$ units.
$$
C_{rep} = 50{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 15{,}000 = \mathbf{75{,}000 \text{ units/day}}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**
$$
C_{nonrep} = 65{,}500 < C_{rep} = 75{,}000
$$
**Choose NON-REPLICATED allocation at Site_A** (1 mark for the conclusion + 1 mark for stating that the update overhead outweighs the read savings).

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the **3-tier client–server distributed database architecture**. Mention the role of each tier. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution — Bullet Outline (for diagram + text):**
1. **Presentation Tier (1.5 m):** Client machines / browsers / mobile apps; only the user-interface logic; **no DB code** lives here. Issues SQL/ODBC/JDBC calls.
2. **Application / Logic Tier (1.5 m):** Application servers that process the business logic, perform transaction management, and route queries to the appropriate database server. May contain the *global query optimizer* in a heterogeneous distributed DB.
3. **Data Management Tier (1.5 m):** One or more DBMS instances; each owns its local schema; the **global schema / data dictionary** lives at a designated *catalog site*.
4. **Diagram (2 m):** Draw three horizontal layers with arrows showing request flow (client → app server → DB server → back up).
5. **Network Protocol (0.5 m):** Mention **TCP/IP**, ODBC/JDBC drivers, and the role of the *transaction monitor* (e.g., Tuxedo) for 2PC coordination.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students draw the tiers but forget to label the **transaction monitor / coordinator** between the application tier and the data tier. Without this, you lose 1 mark. Also, do **not** confuse a 3-tier *web* architecture (HTML/JS/DB) with a 3-tier *distributed database* architecture (Client/App-Server/DBMS-Server). The latter has a **DBMS** in the data tier, not just files.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the **CAP theorem** with a clear diagram. For each of the following systems, state which two of {Consistency, Availability, Partition-tolerance} they sacrifice and why:*
*(i) PostgreSQL with synchronous streaming replication,*
*(ii) Apache Cassandra,*
*(iii) Google Spanner. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define CAP (1 m).**
A distributed data store can simultaneously deliver only **two** of the three properties — **C**onsistency (all clients see the same data at the same time), **A**vailability (every request receives a non-error response), and **P**artition-tolerance (system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes).

**Step 2 — Triangle diagram (2 m).** Draw an equilateral triangle with C, A, P at the vertices; shade/show that the system sits on an edge.

**Step 3 — Per-system classification (4 m):**
* **(i) PostgreSQL + synchronous streaming replication → CP.** Sacrifices *Availability* — if the network partitions, the primary refuses writes (or waits) so replicas do not diverge. Reads from stale replicas are blocked.
* **(ii) Apache Cassandra → AP.** Sacrifices *Consistency* — during a partition, Cassandra keeps accepting reads and writes at the nearest replica; conflicts are resolved later via last-write-wins or vector clocks → eventual consistency.
* **(iii) Google Spanner → CA-like (effectively CP with global strong consistency).** Uses Google's private global network with extremely reliable TrueTime clock API to minimize partitions. When a true partition occurs, it sacrifices *Availability* by halting affected shards (CP behaviour).

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*A company wants to migrate its 2 TB on-premise Oracle database to AWS RDS. Compare **Big-Bang**, **Trickle**, and **Parallel-Run** migration strategies. Recommend one with two-line justification. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (table + recommendation):**

| Criterion | Big-Bang | Trickle | Parallel-Run |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Downtime** | High (hours) | Near-zero | Zero (dual writes) |
| **Risk** | High (single cutover failure) | Medium | Low (rollback possible) |
| **Cost** | Lowest (shortest dual-licence) | Medium | Highest (2× infra cost) |
| **Data-sync complexity** | One-shot | Continuous (CDC) | Continuous (CDC) + reconciliation |
| **Suitable for** | Small DBs, dev/test | Mid-size, business-critical | Mission-critical (banking, ERP) |
| **Rollback ease** | Hard | Medium | Trivial (switch back) |

**Recommendation (1 m):** For a **2 TB, business-critical** Oracle DB, use **Trickle Migration with AWS DMS (Database Migration Service) + CDC**. It minimizes downtime (target: minutes) while keeping rollback possible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Students often write "Big-Bang is fastest" — that is only true *elapsed-time wise* but it has the *highest risk*. Always mention risk vs downtime trade-off, not just speed. For 2 TB scale, the **Trickle** approach (using AWS DMS continuous CDC) is the textbook-correct KTU answer.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Deployment** = deciding *what* to fragment, *where* to allocate, and *how* to replicate.
- **Horizontal fragmentation** uses selection predicates; **vertical** uses projection (TID is shared).
