- The **three correctness rules** = *Completeness, Reconstruction, Disjointness* — examiners love asking these.
- **Replication Decision Rule:** Replicate if and only if **Net Benefit > 0**, or equivalently **RWR ≥ 20**.
- **CAP Theorem** — pick any two: {C, A, P}. Real systems are usually **CP** (banking) or **AP** (social).
- **Quorum rule:** $R_q + W_q > N$ **and** $2 W_q > N$ — must satisfy *both* inequalities.
- **Synchronous replication** → zero data loss, higher latency (CP systems).
- **Asynchronous replication** → low latency, possible stale reads (AP systems).
- **Big-Bang migration** = single cutover, high risk; **Trickle** = continuous CDC, low downtime; **Parallel-Run** = zero downtime, double cost.
- **Cloud DBaaS** examples: AWS RDS, Azure SQL DB, Google Cloud Spanner, Snowflake.
- **Container-native DB** = DB deployed as a Kubernetes StatefulSet with a PersistentVolumeClaim — the modern default for microservices.
- **Cost Model (F1, F2, F3)** is the most-tested *numerical* question in KTU ESE — practice plugging values into $C_{local}$ and $C_{remote}$.
- **Read-frequently + update-rarely → REPLICATE.** **Update-frequently + read-rarely → KEEP PARTITIONED.**
- For **2 TB+ business-critical** data, KTU expected answer = **Trickle / CDC-based migration**, not Big-Bang.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cassandra: Data Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cassandra: Data Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cassandra Data Model (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> The Cassandra data model is a **partitioned row-store** architecture, often classified as a **wide-column store** (or **column-family NoSQL** model). It is a sparse, distributed, multi-dimensional map indexed by a key. The model is designed to scale linearly across commodity servers while providing tunable consistency.

The Cassandra data model is built on a **hierarchical, four-level structure**:

1. **Keyspace** (top-most container, analogous to a "database" in RDBMS)
2. **Column Family / Table** (analogous to a "table" in RDBMS)
3. **Row** (identified by a unique **Partition Key**)
4. **Column** (the smallest unit: a tuple of `name`, `value`, and `timestamp`)

The model is fundamentally different from the relational model because **rows are not required to have the same set of columns**, making the model **sparse** by design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sparsity Principle in Cassandra**
> Unlike a relational table where every row has a fixed schema, Cassandra allows different rows within the same column family to have entirely different columns. Storage is allocated only for the columns that actually exist for a given row.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **giant multi-storey library** instead of a traditional book rack:

- The **entire library** is the **Keyspace**.
- Each **floor** of the library represents a **Column Family (Table)**. For instance, *Floor 1 holds user data, Floor 2 holds product data*.
- Each **bookshelf on a floor** represents a **Row**. The shelf is identified by a unique **shelf number** (the **Partition Key**).
- Each **book on a shelf** represents a **Column**, and its location is determined by a **section (clustering column)**. A shelf may have books on "Section A: Fiction", "Section B: Science", etc.
- A shelf **does not need to have the same number of books in every section** — some sections may be empty. This is the **sparse** nature of Cassandra.

When a librarian wants to find a book, they:
1. Go to the correct **floor (column family)**.
2. Find the **shelf (partition key)**.
3. Navigate to the **section (clustering column)** to locate the **book (column value)**.

The librarian **never scans the entire library** — this mirrors Cassandra's efficient read/write pattern, which is always keyed-access.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern Cassandra's data model behavior:

- **Replication Factor (RF):** The number of copies of data maintained across different nodes. Default is **3** in production deployments.
- **Consistency Level (CL):** The number of replica nodes that must acknowledge a read/write operation. Common values: `ONE`, `QUORUM`, `ALL`, `LOCAL_QUORUM`.
- **Maximum Partition Size (Soft Limit):** Recommended to keep partitions under **100 MB** and fewer than **100,000 cells per partition** to maintain read performance.
- **Default Partitioner:** `Murmur3Partitioner` (uses the **Murmur3 hash** function to distribute data).
- **Native Protocol Version:** The default Cassandra port is **9042**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 PECDT795**
> The official KTU 2024 syllabus module 3 expects students to:
> - Differentiate between RDBMS row-store and Cassandra's wide-column store
> - Define Keyspace, Column Family, Row, Column, and Super Column
> - Explain Primary Key anatomy: Partition Key + Clustering Key
> - Describe how CQL maps to the underlying storage engine

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Level Hierarchy — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Keyspace

A **Keyspace** is the outermost container in the Cassandra data model. It defines:
- The **replication strategy** (`SimpleStrategy` or `NetworkTopologyStrategy`)
- The **replication factor** (numeric count of replicas)
- Optionally, the durable writes attribute

**Example CQL:**
```sql
CREATE KEYSPACE UniversityDB
WITH replication = {
  'class': 'SimpleStrategy',
  'replication_factor': 3
};
```

### 2.1.2 Column Family (Table)

A Column Family is a logical grouping of rows. In CQL, it is referred to as a **table**, but internally Cassandra treats it as a sorted map of partitions.

```sql
CREATE TABLE Students (
  student_id   int,
  course_code  text,
  enrollment_date timestamp,
  grade        text,
  PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_code)
);
```

### 2.1.3 Row

A row in Cassandra is a **physical unit of partition**. Every row in a column family is uniquely identified by its **Partition Key**. All columns that share the same partition key are **physically co-located on the same node (or its replicas)**.

### 2.1.4 Column

A column is the smallest unit of data. Internally, every cell is stored as a 4-tuple structure:
- **Name** (column identifier)
- **Value** (the data)
- **Timestamp** (used for Last-Write-Wins conflict resolution)
- **TTL (Time To Live)** (optional expiration)

## 2.2 The Anatomy of the Primary Key

The Primary Key in Cassandra is **NOT** a single column — it is a composite of two distinct concepts:

```
PRIMARY KEY = (Partition Key) + (Clustering Key)
```

### 2.2.1 Partition Key
- The first column(s) listed in the PRIMARY KEY
- Used to compute a **token** via the hash function
- Determines the **physical node** where the row is stored
- All rows with the same partition key are stored together

### 2.2.2 Clustering Key
- The columns listed **after the partition key**
- Determines the **sort order** of rows within a partition
- Allows range queries within a partition (e.g., `WHERE cluster_col > X AND cluster_col < Y`)

### 2.2.3 Composite Partition Key
When the partition key itself is a tuple of multiple columns, it is called a **composite partition key**:
```sql
PRIMARY KEY ((tenant_id, device_id), timestamp)
```
The parentheses around `(tenant_id, device_id)` indicate they are **hashed together** to form the partition token.

## 2.3 The Token Generation Formula

Cassandra uses the **Murmur3 hash function** to map a partition key to a token in the range `[0, 2^63 - 1]`:

$$T = \text{Murmur3Hash}(\text{partition\_key}) \mod 2^{63}$$

Each node in the cluster is responsible for a **range of tokens** on the consistent-hashing ring. The token directly determines the physical storage node.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Murmur3?**
> Murmur3 is a non-cryptographic hash function that is **fast, well-distributed, and collision-resistant** for typical string keys. Cassandra previously used `RandomPartitioner` (MD5 hash) but switched to Murmur3 for performance.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Token Range | $T \in [0, 2^{63}-1]$ | Valid range of Cassandra partition tokens |
| Token Calculation | $T = \text{Murmur3}(\text{key})$ | Maps partition key to a token |
| Quorum | $Q = \left\lceil \frac{RF + 1}{2} \right\rceil$ | Minimum replicas for `QUORUM` consistency |
| Soft Partition Limit | $S < 100\,\text{MB}$ | Recommended max partition size |
| Cell Count Limit | $C < 100{,}000$ | Max cells per partition (guideline) |
| Wide Row | Many clustering keys per partition | Stores sorted data within one partition |
| Skinny Row | One column per row (RDBMS-like) | Not native to Cassandra's strength |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The Cassandra data model is engineered for:

- **Time-series workloads:** IoT sensor data, application logs, financial ticks (clustering on timestamp).
- **Messaging systems:** Inbox-style models (clustering on message_id within user_id partition).
- **User profiles with sparse attributes:** Different users may have wildly different fields populated.
- **Write-heavy, append-only systems:** Telemetry, audit logs, clickstreams.

The model explicitly **sacrifices**:
- Ad-hoc JOIN operations
- ACID transactions (only **lightweight transactions** via Paxos)
- Full-table scans

in exchange for **linear horizontal scalability** and **constant-time reads/writes**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Token-to-Node Assignment

Cassandra uses a **consistent hashing** ring to assign partitions to nodes. Let us walk through the derivation of how a partition key reaches its physical node.

### Step 1 — Hash the Partition Key

Let the partition key value be `K`. Compute the Murmur3 hash:

$$h = \text{Murmur3}(K)$$

For example, if `K = "student_42"`, the hash function returns a 64-bit integer. Suppose:

$$h = 7{,}845{,}321{,}998{,}112{,}443$$

### Step 2 — Modulo the Token Space

The token is computed as:

$$T = h \mod 2^{63}$$

For a 64-bit signed integer, the range is from $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$, but Cassandra uses only the **non-negative range** $[0, 2^{63}-1]$.

$$T = 7{,}845{,}321{,}998{,}112{,}443 \mod 9{,}223{,}372{,}036{,}854{,}775{,}808 = 7{,}845{,}321{,}998{,}112{,}443$$

(In this case, $h$ is already smaller than $2^{63}$, so $T = h$.)

### Step 3 — Walk the Consistent Hashing Ring

Each node is assigned a **token range**. For a 4-node cluster with vnodes (default), each node owns roughly $\frac{1}{N}$ of the ring space, where $N$ is the number of nodes.

The node responsible for token $T$ is the **first node whose token is greater than or equal to $T$** when walking clockwise on the ring. Mathematically:

$$\text{ResponsibleNode}(T) = \min\{N_i \in \text{Nodes} \mid N_i.\text{token} \geq T\}$$

If no such node exists, the ring wraps around and the **first node (lowest token)** becomes responsible.

### Step 4 — Replicate to N Successor Nodes

Once the coordinator node is found, the data is replicated to the next $RF - 1$ successor nodes on the ring (for `SimpleStrategy`).

Let the ring traversal be:

$$N_{\text{coordinator}}, N_{\text{replica}_1}, N_{\text{replica}_2}, \ldots, N_{\text{replica}_{RF-1}}$$

With $RF = 3$, three copies are stored on three different nodes.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Primary Key Design Patterns

The following matrix classifies the four canonical primary key patterns in Cassandra:

| **Pattern** | **CQL Syntax** | **Use Case** | **Read Pattern** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Partition Key** | `PRIMARY KEY (id)` | Lookup by ID (e.g., user profile) | Point query by `id` |
| **Composite Partition Key** | `PRIMARY KEY ((a, b), c)` | Multi-tenant isolation (e.g., `(tenant_id, user_id)`) | Query requires both `a` and `b` |
| **Partition + Clustering** | `PRIMARY KEY (id, ts)` | Time-series per entity (e.g., events per device) | `WHERE id = X AND ts > T1 AND ts < T2` |
| **Composite Partition + Composite Clustering** | `PRIMARY KEY ((a, b), c, d)` | Hierarchical data (e.g., (region, store) → (day, hour)) | Range scans within sub-key |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Designing a Sensor Data Table

**Problem Statement:** A weather monitoring system ingests readings from 10,000 sensors at 1-second intervals. We need to query the last 24 hours of data for a specific sensor.

### Step 1 — Identify the Query Pattern

The dominant query is:
```sql
SELECT * FROM sensor_readings
WHERE sensor_id = 'S_001' AND reading_time > '2025-01-15';
```

### Step 2 — Select the Primary Key

- **Partition Key:** `sensor_id` (groups all readings for one sensor in the same physical partition)
- **Clustering Key:** `reading_time` (sorts readings chronologically within the partition)

```sql
CREATE TABLE sensor_readings (
  sensor_id     text,
  reading_time  timestamp,
  temperature   float,
  humidity      float,
  PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, reading_time)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (reading_time DESC);
```

### Step 3 — Verify the Physical Layout

All readings for `S_001` are stored as a **wide row** on a single partition token $T = \text{Murmur3}(\text{"S\_001"})$. Within that partition, rows are sorted by `reading_time` descending (newest first).

### Step 4 — Evaluate Performance

- **Write path:** Hash `S_001` → token $T$ → write to coordinator and 2 replicas. **O(1)** write.
- **Read path (last 24 hrs):** Hash `S_001` → fetch partition → range scan on `reading_time`. **O(log N + k)** where $k$ is rows returned.

If a sensor produces too many readings and the partition exceeds **100 MB**, we must introduce a **bucket pattern** (e.g., `PRIMARY KEY ((sensor_id, date_bucket), reading_time)`) to shard the partition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Justification of the 100 MB Soft Limit**
> Cassandra uses **SSTables (Sorted String Tables)** for on-disk storage. When a partition is large, every read requires loading the entire partition into memory. If partitions grow unbounded, the JVM heap may exceed the configured limit, triggering **garbage collection storms** and degraded throughput.

## 3.4 Derivation: Quorum Math

For a replication factor of $RF = 3$:

$$Q = \left\lceil \frac{RF + 1}{2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3 + 1}{2} \right\rceil = 2$$

So `QUORUM` requires **2 out of 3** replicas to acknowledge. This ensures **strong consistency** when combined with `QUORUM` reads.

For $RF = 5$:

$$Q = \left\lceil \frac{5 + 1}{2} \right\rceil = 3$$

So `QUORUM` requires **3 out of 5** replicas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong Consistency Condition**
> Strong consistency is achieved when $R + W > RF$, where $R$ is the read consistency level and $W$ is the write consistency level. For $RF = 3$, using `QUORUM` for both reads and writes: $2 + 2 = 4 > 3$ ✓.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cassandra Data Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Keyspace: UniversityDB RF=3"]
    nodeB["Column Family: Students Table"]
    nodeC["Column Family: Courses Table"]
    nodeD["Column Family: Enrollments Table"]
    nodeE["Row Partition Token 1001 Student ID 101"]
    nodeF["Row Partition Token 2998 Student ID 102"]
    nodeG["Row Partition Token 4421 Student ID 103"]
    nodeH["Column: CourseCode CS401"]
    nodeI["Column: CourseCode CS402"]
    nodeJ["Column: CourseCode CS501"]
    nodeK["Column: Name Riya"]
    nodeL["Column: Email riya at university edu"]
    nodeM["Column: Name Arjun"]
    nodeN["Column: GPA 8 7"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeL
    nodeF --> nodeM
    nodeF --> nodeN
```

## 4.2 Primary Key Anatomy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP["PRIMARY KEY (a, b, c)"]
    nodeQ["Partition Key Component a b Composite Partition"]
    nodeR["Clustering Key Component c Sorts Within Partition"]
    nodeS["Token T equals Murmur3 a b"]
    nodeT["Row Storage Co located on One Node"]
    nodeU["Wide Row Sorted by c within Partition"]

    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeP --> nodeR
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeT
    nodeR --> nodeU
    nodeT --> nodeU
```

## 4.3 Write Path — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Client Application"]
    nodeB["CQL Query: INSERT INTO Students"]
    nodeC["Coordinator Node Routing"]
    nodeD["Compute Token via Murmur3Hash of Partition Key"]
    nodeE["Look Up Token to Node Mapping on Hash Ring"]
    nodeF["Forward Write to Coordinator Node"]
    nodeG["Replicate to RF minus 1 Successor Nodes"]
    nodeH["Write to CommitLog Disk"]
    nodeI["Write to MemTable RAM"]
    nodeJ["Acknowledge to Client"]
    nodeK["Background Flush MemTable to SSTable"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
```

## 4.4 Read Path — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Client Query: SELECT WHERE student_id equals 101"]
    nodeB["Coordinator Node"]
    nodeC["Compute Token T for Partition Key 101"]
    nodeD["Identify Replica Nodes based on RF and Snitch"]
    nodeE["Send Read Request to Fastest Replica per CL"]
    nodeF["Check MemTable in RAM"]
    nodeG["Check SSTables on Disk Bloom Filter Check"]
    nodeH["Merge Results from Multiple SSTables"]
    nodeI["Apply Read Repair if Discrepancy Detected"]
    nodeJ["Return Result to Client"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeJ
```

## 4.5 Partition Distribution Across a 4-Node Cluster

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Node 1 Tokens 0 to 2305843009213693952"]
    nodeB["Node 2 Tokens 2305843009213693953 to 4611686018427387904"]
    nodeC["Node 3 Tokens 4611686018427387905 to 6917529025541081856"]
    nodeD["Node 4 Tokens 6917529025541081857 to 9223372036854775807"]
    nodeE["Partition P1 Token 1500000000 Goes to Node 1"]
    nodeF["Partition P2 Token 5000000000 Goes to Node 3"]
    nodeG["Partition P3 Token 8000000000 Goes to Node 4"]

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeD --- nodeA
    nodeE -.-> nodeA
    nodeF -.-> nodeC
    nodeG -.-> nodeD
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Explain the four-level hierarchy of the Cassandra data model. How does it differ from a relational data model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**
- **(1 mark)** **Keyspace** is the outermost container defining replication strategy; analogous to a database in RDBMS.
- **(1 mark)** **Column Family / Table** is a logical grouping of rows, analogous to a relational table.
- **(1 mark)** **Row** and **Column** form the in-partition structure, where rows are grouped by **partition key** and columns can vary across rows (sparsity). Unlike RDBMS, Cassandra does not enforce a fixed schema per row and offers no JOINs.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Differentiate between a Partition Key and a Clustering Key in Cassandra with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks breakdown):**
- **(1.5 marks)** **Partition Key** determines the **physical node** for storage via Murmur3 hashing; example: `student_id` in `PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_code)`.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Clustering Key** determines the **sort order within a partition** and enables range queries; example: `course_code` sorts the courses a student has enrolled in. Together they form the **composite primary key**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat CQL schema, describe how the Cassandra data model handles a **time-series IoT sensor dataset**. Explain the role of the partition key and clustering key, and justify the recommended partition size limit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **token calculation formula** used by Cassandra to assign a partition to a node. Explain how **replication factor** and **quorum** are related using the formula $Q = \lceil (RF+1)/2 \rceil$ for $RF = 3$ and $RF = 5$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Time-Series Schema (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** CQL schema:
  ```sql
  CREATE TABLE sensor_readings (
    sensor_id    text,
    reading_time timestamp,
    temperature  float,
    humidity     float,
    PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, reading_time)
  ) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (reading_time DESC);
  ```
- **(2 marks)** **Partition key** = `sensor_id` (groups all readings of one sensor on one physical node). **Clustering key** = `reading_time` (sorts readings chronologically; enables range queries like "last 24 hours").
- **(2 marks)** **Justification of the 100 MB partition limit:** Large partitions force Cassandra to load the entire partition into memory for reads, leading to JVM heap pressure, GC pauses, and bloom-filter inefficiency. Beyond ~100,000 cells, the read latency degrades exponentially.
- **(1 mark)** **Alternative — Bucket pattern** if the partition grows unbounded: `PRIMARY KEY ((sensor_id, date_bucket), reading_time)` to shard the partition across days.

**(b) — Token Derivation & Quorum (7 marks):**
- **(3 marks)** Token formula:
  $$T = \text{Murmur3Hash}(\text{partition\_key})$$
  The hash output is a 64-bit integer in the range $[0, 2^{63}-1]$. Each node owns a contiguous range of tokens on the consistent hashing ring. The coordinator routes the write to the **first node whose token is ≥ T** (with wrap-around).
- **(2 marks)** For $RF = 3$:
  $$Q = \left\lceil \frac{3+1}{2} \right\rceil = 2$$
  Two out of three replicas must acknowledge.
- **(2 marks)** For $RF = 5$:
  $$Q = \left\lceil \frac{5+1}{2} \right\rceil = 3$$
  Three out of five replicas must acknowledge. This demonstrates that as the cluster grows, the absolute number of replicas needed for quorum increases, but the **relative fraction remains > 50%**, preserving strong consistency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare the **wide-column data model** of Cassandra with the **row-store data model** of traditional RDBMS. Discuss sparsity, schema flexibility, and physical storage organization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a CQL schema for a **multi-tenant messaging application** where each tenant has many users and each user has many messages. Show how composite partition keys ensure tenant isolation and explain the consequences of poor partition key design. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Wide-Column vs. Row-Store (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** **RDBMS row-store:** Fixed schema, normalized tables, JOINs, ACID transactions. Every row has the same set of columns; storage is dense.
- **(2 marks)** **Cassandra wide-column:** Sparse, schema-flexible, denormalized, no JOINs. Rows in the same table may have entirely different columns.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Sparsity:** In Cassandra, only present cells consume storage. In RDBMS, NULL columns still consume space (depending on DBMS).
- **(1.5 marks)** **Physical storage:** RDBMS stores rows contiguously (heap-organized) or by index; Cassandra stores rows in **partitions**, and partitions are distributed across nodes by hash of the partition key. The clustering key controls in-partition sort order.

**(b) — Multi-Tenant Messaging Schema (7 marks):**
- **(3 marks)** CQL schema:
  ```sql
  CREATE TABLE messages (
    tenant_id   text,
    user_id     text,
    message_id  timeuuid,
    body        text,
    sent_at     timestamp,
    PRIMARY KEY ((tenant_id, user_id), message_id)
  ) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (message_id DESC);
  ```
  - **Composite partition key:** `(tenant_id, user_id)` ensures tenant data isolation and prevents "hot partitions" if a single tenant becomes too large.
  - **Clustering key:** `message_id` (timeuuid) sorts messages newest-first within a user's mailbox.
- **(2 marks)** **Consequences of poor partition key design:**
  - Using only `tenant_id` as partition key would create one giant partition per tenant (hot partition).
  - Using only `user_id` would mix tenants on the same node (security and scalability issues).
- **(2 marks)** **Read pattern:** `SELECT * FROM messages WHERE tenant_id = 'T1' AND user_id = 'U42' LIMIT 50;` is an O(1) point query on the hash ring.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse **Partition Key** with **Primary Key**. Remember: the Primary Key is the **full** declaration, while the Partition Key is the **first component** (or composite group in extra parentheses).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Many students believe Cassandra is "schema-less" and skip schema design entirely. In reality, the **partition key choice is the single most critical design decision** — a bad choice leads to "wide partition" or "hot partition" failures in production.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students forget that the **Murmur3 hash range is $2^{63}$, not $2^{64}$** (signed integer range), and lose a mark in derivations.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing `QUORUM` consistency level with the **formula**. The correct formula is $Q = \lceil (RF+1)/2 \rceil$, not $RF/2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cassandra = Wide-Column / Partitioned Row-Store NoSQL database**.
- **Four-level hierarchy:** Keyspace → Column Family → Row → Column.
- **Primary Key = Partition Key + Clustering Key**.
- **Partition Key** is hashed by **Murmur3** to determine the physical node; range is $[0, 2^{63}-1]$.
- **Clustering Key** sorts rows within a partition and enables range queries.
- **Composite Partition Key** (using extra parentheses) is used for multi-dimensional hashing (e.g., multi-tenant systems).
- **Sparsity** means different rows can have different columns; only present cells consume storage.
- **Replication Factor (RF)** controls how many copies of data exist; default is 3.
- **Quorum Formula:** $Q = \lceil (RF+1)/2 \rceil$. For $RF=3$, $Q=2$. For $RF=5$, $Q=3$.
- **Strong Consistency:** Achieved when $R + W > RF$.
- **Soft partition limit:** 100 MB and 100,000 cells per partition (recommendation, not a hard limit).
- **No JOINs, no ACID, no ad-hoc queries** — but offers linear scalability and tunable consistency.
- **CQL ≠ SQL** — CQL is a deliberately limited subset designed for distributed environments.
- **Last-Write-Wins (LWW)** is the default conflict resolution strategy, based on the column timestamp.
- **Time-series, IoT, and messaging** workloads are the canonical use cases for the Cassandra data model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CRUD Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CRUD Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **CRUD** is an acronym that stands for the four fundamental persistent storage functions that a database application must perform: **Create**, **Read**, **Update**, and **Delete**. In the context of **Advanced Database Systems (PECDT795)**, CRUD operations extend beyond basic relational SQL statements (INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE) to encompass operations across **distributed databases, NoSQL document stores, object-relational mappings, and transactional systems** governed by ACID properties.

In formal database theory, CRUD maps directly to the SQL Data Manipulation Language (DML) primitives:

| CRUD Acronym | SQL Equivalent | MongoDB Equivalent | Set Operation |
|--------------|----------------|--------------------|----|
| **C**reate | `INSERT` | `insertOne / insertMany` | Union / Add |
| **R**ead | `SELECT` | `find / findOne` | Selection / Projection |
| **U**pdate | `UPDATE` | `updateOne / updateMany` | Difference / Replace |
| **D**elete | `DELETE` | `deleteOne / deleteMany` | Minus / Drop |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Library Card Catalog System:**
> Imagine a massive library (your database). Every book is a **record**. The librarian (your DBMS) helps you perform four actions:
> - **CREATE** = Adding a brand-new book to the catalog and the shelf.
> - **READ** = Walking up to the catalog, searching by title/author, and retrieving the book's location.
> - **UPDATE** = Realizing the book has been republished under a new title — you erase the old card and write a new one.
> - **DELETE** = Removing the book entirely from the catalog (perhaps it was lost or withdrawn).
>
> Just as a library guarantees that when you update a card, the next person who reads it sees the *new* version (consistency), a database guarantees the same through **ACID transactions**.

In Advanced Database Systems, CRUD is not just about executing single statements — it is about ensuring that these four operations remain **consistent, isolated, atomic, and durable** even when the database is **distributed across multiple servers**, **replicated geographically**, or built on **non-relational (NoSQL) foundations**.

## 1.3 Why CRUD Matters in Advanced Database Systems

> [!NOTE]
> In **Advanced Database Systems**, CRUD operations are evaluated against:
> 1. **Concurrency Control** — What happens when two users UPDATE the same record simultaneously?
> 2. **Distribution Transparency** — Does the user need to know which shard/node holds the record?
> 3. **Consistency Models** — Strong (ACID) vs Eventual (BASE) consistency during CRUD.
> 4. **Performance Optimization** — Indexing, partitioning, and batching of CRUD operations.

The four operations are not merely syntactic — they encapsulate **fundamental relational algebra operations** that form the theoretical foundation of every query language.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> CRUD operations are studied in the context of **transactional integrity, distributed consistency (CAP theorem), document-oriented databases (MongoDB-style), and object-relational mapping (ORM) layers**. Students are expected to write advanced CRUD queries, understand their execution semantics, and reason about anomalies during concurrent execution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of CRUD — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 CREATE (Insert Operation)

The CREATE operation appends one or more tuples (rows/documents) to a relation (table/collection).

**Relational Form (SQL):**

$$\text{INSERT INTO } R(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n) \text{ VALUES } (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n)$$

**Document Form (NoSQL — MongoDB syntax):**

```javascript
db.students.insertOne({
    "roll_no": "KTU2024CS101",
    "name": "Anand Krishnan",
    "cgpa": 8.7
})
```

**Why CREATE needs care:**
- Must satisfy **entity integrity** (Primary Key not NULL, unique).
- Must satisfy **referential integrity** (Foreign Key constraints).
- Must respect **domain constraints** (column data types).
- In distributed systems, CREATE may trigger **replication** to multiple nodes.

### 2.1.2 READ (Select Operation)

READ is technically a **read-only** operation and should never mutate state. In relational algebra:

$$R \bowtie S = \pi_{A}(\sigma_{\theta}(R \times S))$$

This means: take Cartesian product, filter by condition $\theta$, project desired attributes.

**Read Anomalies to Watch:**
- **Dirty Read** — Reading uncommitted data from another transaction.
- **Non-Repeatable Read** — Same query returns different values within one transaction.
- **Phantom Read** — New rows appear in repeated range queries.

### 2.1.3 UPDATE (Modify Operation)

UPDATE modifies existing tuples without changing their identity (Primary Key remains stable).

$$\text{UPDATE } R \text{ SET } A_i = f(A_1, \ldots, A_n) \text{ WHERE } \theta$$

**Update Anomalies:**
- **Lost Update Problem** — Two concurrent updates overwrite each other.
- **Write Skew** — Concurrent transactions read overlapping data and write disjoint updates, violating a global invariant.

### 2.1.4 DELETE (Remove Operation)

DELETE removes one or more tuples. In set theory:

$$R' = R - \sigma_{\theta}(R)$$

**Delete Anomalies:**
- Cascading deletes may inadvertently remove referenced tuples.
- In NoSQL, deletes are often **soft-deletes** (tombstones) before eventual physical purge.

## 2.2 CRUD in the Context of ACID Transactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every CRUD operation should, ideally, be executed within a **transaction** to guarantee ACID properties:

| Property | Meaning | CRUD Implication |
|----------|---------|-------|
| **Atomicity** | All-or-nothing execution | If DELETE fails halfway, none of it persists |
| **Consistency** | Valid state → Valid state | Every UPDATE maintains all integrity constraints |
| **Isolation** | Concurrent CRUD don't interfere | Two simultaneous UPDATEs don't corrupt data |
| **Durability** | Committed changes survive crashes | After CREATE commits, it survives power failure |

## 2.3 CRUD in Distributed Databases — CAP Implications

In a distributed setting, CRUD operations must choose between:

- **C**onsistency (every read receives the most recent write)
- **A**vailability (every request receives a response)
- **P**artition tolerance (system works despite network splits)

The **CAP Theorem** (Brewer's Conjecture, proved by Gilbert & Lynch) states that a distributed data store can satisfy **at most two** of these three guarantees simultaneously.

| Database Type | CAP Choice | CRUD Behavior |
|---------------|------------|----------------|
| RDBMS (PostgreSQL) | CA | Strong consistency, single-node |
| MongoDB (default) | CP | Strong consistency on primary |
| Cassandra | AP | Eventual consistency, high availability |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Variables | Unit / Notes |
|---------|------------------|-----------|-------|
| Insert Tuple | $\text{INSERT}(R, t)$ | $R$=relation, $t$=tuple | Cardinality: $|R| \leftarrow |R| + 1$ |
| Select Operation | $\sigma_{\theta}(R)$ | $\theta$=predicate | Selectivity $= \frac{\mid\sigma_{\theta}(R)\mid}{\mid R \mid}$ |
| Project Operation | $\pi_{A}(R)$ | $A$=attribute set | Removes duplicates |
| Cartesian Product | $R \times S$ | — | Result cardinality: $\mid R \mid \cdot \mid S \mid$ |
| Natural Join | $R \bowtie S$ | — | Eliminates duplicate attributes |
| Update Cost | $C_{upd} = C_{seek} + C_{write} \cdot n_{rows}$ | $n_{rows}$=rows affected | Measured in I/O blocks |
| ACID Transaction Time | $T_{total} = T_{read} + T_{validate} + T_{write}$ | All in ms | Includes lock acquisition |
| Replication Lag | $\Delta t_{rep} = t_{slave\_ack} - t_{master\_commit}$ | — | Critical for READ-after-WRITE |
| Two-Phase Commit | 2 RTT (Prepare + Commit) | RTT = round-trip-time | Atomic across shards |
| Quorum (Writes) | $W + R > N$ | $N$=replicas | Ensures consistency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Pipe Symbol:** In the formula sheet table, absolute values and set notations use $\vert$ rendering style (`\vert`) to avoid breaking markdown syntax — strictly per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 isolation protocol.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems** — CRUD must enforce strict ACID for monetary transactions.
- **E-Commerce** — Cart UPDATE/READ operations must be highly available (AP systems like Redis).
- **Social Networks** — CREATE-heavy workloads favor eventually consistent NoSQL (Cassandra, DynamoDB).
- **IoT Systems** — Time-series CRUD requires specialized stores (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB).
- **Healthcare Records** — CRUD must maintain full audit trails via versioning + triggers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Bulk CRUD Operations

Consider a relation $R$ with $B_R$ disk blocks and $T_R$ tuples. We want to derive the I/O cost for the four CRUD operations.

### 3.1.1 Cost of CREATE (Bulk Insert)

To insert $k$ new tuples into a relation $R$ that has $B_R$ blocks:

**Step 1:** Identify the blocks that will contain the new tuples (typically appending at the end of the heap file).

**Step 2:** Compute the number of new blocks needed.

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{new} &= \left\lceil \frac{k}{b_{fr}} \right\rceil \\
C_{insert} &= C_{seek} \cdot B_{new} + C_{seq\_write} \cdot B_R
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $b_{fr}$ = blocking factor = number of tuples per block = $\lfloor \frac{B_{block}}{T_{size}} \rfloor$
- $B_{block}$ = block size (typically 4096 bytes or **4 KB**)
- $C_{seek}$ = cost of one disk seek (~**5 ms** for HDD, ~**0.1 ms** for SSD)

**Step 3:** If the relation is indexed, each insert may also require $\log_2(B_R)$ index block updates.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{insert\_indexed} &= C_{insert} + \log_2(B_R) \cdot C_{seek}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Conversion Logic:** Suppose $B_R = 1000$ blocks, $T_{size} = 200$ bytes, $B_{block} = 4096$ bytes. Then $b_{fr} = \lfloor 4096/200 \rfloor = 20$ tuples/block. For $k = 500$ inserts: $B_{new} = \lceil 500/20 \rceil = 25$ new blocks.

### 3.1.2 Cost of READ (Selection)

For a selection $\sigma_{A=c}(R)$ on attribute $A$:

**Case A — Linear Search (No Index):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{linear} &= B_R \quad \text{(scan all blocks)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Case B — Equality on Primary Key (with B+ Tree Index):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{PK\_eq} &= D \cdot (C_{seek} + C_{transfer})
\end{aligned}$$

Where $D$ = depth of B+ tree, typically $\log_{\lceil m/2 \rceil}(B_R)$.

**Case C — Range Query on Sorted Attribute:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{range} &= C_{seek} + \frac{s}{b_{fr}} \cdot C_{transfer}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $s$ = selectivity (number of matching tuples).

### 3.1.3 Cost of UPDATE

For an UPDATE on $k$ tuples selected via condition $\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{update} &= C_{read}(\theta) + k \cdot C_{write} + \Delta_{index}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $C_{read}(\theta)$ = cost to locate matching tuples (per Section 3.1.2).
- $k \cdot C_{write}$ = writing $k$ modified tuples back.
- $\Delta_{index}$ = cost to update all secondary indexes pointing to modified rows.

### 3.1.4 Cost of DELETE

For a DELETE of $k$ tuples:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{delete} &= C_{read}(\theta) + k \cdot C_{write} + k \cdot C_{index\_update}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, DELETE is often implemented as a **logical delete** (UPDATE a `is_deleted` flag) to reduce I/O and allow recovery. Physical deletion happens during compaction.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — CRUD vs Consistency Model

| CRUD Operation | Strict ACID (RDBMS) | Eventual Consistency (NoSQL) | Causal Consistency |
|---------------|--------------------|-----|---------------------|
| **CREATE** | Synchronous commit + replication | Async replication, returns immediately | Sync to causally related nodes |
| **READ (own writes)** | Always sees latest | Requires **read-your-writes** session | Guaranteed |
| **UPDATE** | Locked transaction | Last-write-wins (LWW) | Preserves cause-effect |
| **DELETE** | Tombstone + cascade | Tombstone with TTL | Tombstone with vector clock |

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimistic vs Pessimistic CRUD Concurrency

### 3.3.1 Pessimistic (Locking)

Two-phase locking (2PL) ensures serializability but may deadlock.

**Lock Compatibility Matrix:**

| | Held: S | Held: X |
|-----|---------|---------|
| **Request: S** | ✓ Allow | ✗ Block |
| **Request: X** | ✗ Block | ✗ Block |

### 3.3.2 Optimistic (Validation)

Three-phase protocol:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{read} + T_{validate} + T_{write}
\end{aligned}$$

Validation rule for transaction $T_i$ against committed $T_j$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Validate}(T_i) = & \left( \text{WS}(T_i) \cap \text{WS}(T_j) = \emptyset \right) \\
& \lor \left( \text{RS}(T_i) \cap \text{WS}(T_j) = \emptyset \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Where WS = Write Set, RS = Read Set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $T_i$ validates if either it writes nothing that $T_j$ wrote, or it doesn't read anything that $T_j$ wrote. This ensures $T_i$'s reads remain consistent.

## 3.4 Exhaustive CRUD Execution Trace (SQL Example)

Consider the relational schema:

$$Student(\underline{RollNo}, Name, CGPA, DeptId)$$
$$Department(\underline{DeptId}, DeptName)$$

**Task:** Perform a transactional CRUD sequence — Enroll a new student, then UPDATE their CGPA, then DELETE if CGPA < 0 (invalid).

**SQL Trace:**

```sql
BEGIN TRANSACTION;

-- CREATE
INSERT INTO Student (RollNo, Name, CGPA, DeptId)
VALUES ('KTU2024CS001', 'Rahul M', 8.5, 'CS');

-- READ (Verify)
SELECT * FROM Student WHERE RollNo = 'KTU2024CS001';

-- UPDATE
UPDATE Student SET CGPA = 9.1 WHERE RollNo = 'KTU2024CS001';

-- DELETE (hypothetical invalid case)
DELETE FROM Student WHERE CGPA < 0;

COMMIT;
```

**Step-by-step execution trace:**

1. `BEGIN` → Transaction Manager creates a new TXID.
2. `INSERT` → Acquires **X-lock** on new row + IX-lock on table. Writes to log buffer.
3. `SELECT` → Acquires **S-lock**, reads from buffer pool.
4. `UPDATE` → Upgrades to **X-lock**, writes new tuple version (MVCC) or in-place.
5. `DELETE` → Marks tuple as deleted (or physically removes).
6. `COMMIT` → Flushes log to disk (Write-Ahead Log), releases all locks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CRUD Operation Flow — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    appLayer[Application Layer ORM Query API]
    parser[SQL NoSQL Query Parser]
    optimizer[Query Optimizer]
    planner[Execution Plan Generator]
    txMgr[Transaction Manager ACID Controller]
    lockMgr[Lock Manager Concurrency Control]
    logMgr[Log Manager Write Ahead Log]
    bufferPool[Buffer Pool Cache Memory]
    storage[Disk Storage Tables Indexes]
    replication[Replication Manager Distributed Nodes]

    appLayer --> parser
    parser --> optimizer
    optimizer --> planner
    planner --> txMgr
    txMgr --> lockMgr
    txMgr --> logMgr
    planner --> bufferPool
    bufferPool --> storage
    txMgr --> replication

    subgraph CREATE_OP[CREATE Path]
        c1[Parse INSERT] --> c2[Validate Constraints] --> c3[Acquire X Locks] --> c4[Write to Buffer] --> c5[Log + Commit] --> c6[Replicate]
    end

    subgraph READ_OP[READ Path]
        r1[Parse SELECT] --> r2[Generate Plan] --> r3[Acquire S Locks] --> r4[Read from Buffer or Disk] --> r5[Return Result Set]
    end

    subgraph UPDATE_OP[UPDATE Path]
        u1[Parse UPDATE] --> u2[Locate Rows Index Scan] --> u3[Upgrade to X Locks] --> u4[Modify Tuples] --> u5[Update Indexes] --> u6[Log + Commit] --> u7[Replicate]
    end

    subgraph DELETE_OP[DELETE Path]
        d1[Parse DELETE] --> d2[Locate Rows] --> d3[Acquire X Locks] --> d4[Mark Deleted or Remove] --> d5[Update Indexes] --> d6[Log + Commit] --> d7[Replicate or Tombstone]
    end
```

## 4.2 CRUD vs ACID Transaction Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BeginTx
    BeginTx --> Active: BEGIN TRANSACTION
    Active --> Active: CRUD Operations INSERT SELECT UPDATE DELETE
    Active --> PartiallyCommitted: Final CRUD Done
    PartiallyCommitted --> Committed: COMMIT Log Flushed
    PartiallyCommitted --> Failed: ROLLBACK or Crash
    Active --> Failed: Deadlock or Constraint Violation
    Failed --> Aborted: Rollback Complete
    Committed --> [*]: Success Durability Achieved
    Aborted --> [*]: No State Change Persisted
```

## 4.3 Distributed CRUD — CAP Trade-off Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[New CRUD Request Arrives]
    start --> capCheck{Network Partition Detected}
    capCheck -->|Yes| apChoice[Favor Availability AP]
    capCheck -->|No| healthyChoice[Normal Operation]

    apChoice --> noSQLPath[Use Cassandra DynamoDB Style Async Replication Eventual Consistency]
    healthyChoice --> strongPath{Strong Consistency Required}

    strongPath -->|Yes Banking Inventory| sqlPath[Use PostgreSQL MySQL Distributed SQL CockroachDB Strong Consistency CP or CA]
    strongPath -->|No Social Media Feeds| noSQLPath
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CRUD in a Distributed NoSQL Cluster

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph clientTier[Client Tier]
        c1[Client Application 1]
        c2[Client Application 2]
    end

    subgraph coordTier[Coordinator Tier]
        coord1[Query Coordinator Node 1]
        coord2[Query Coordinator Node 2]
    end

    subgraph shardTier[Sharded Storage Tier]
        shardA[Shard A Primary Node]
        shardA1[Shard A Replica 1]
        shardA2[Shard A Replica 2]
        shardB[Shard B Primary Node]
        shardB1[Shard B Replica 1]
        shardB2[Shard B Replica 2]
    end

    c1 --> coord1
    c2 --> coord2

    coord1 -->|Hash Key Routing| shardA
    coord1 -->|Hash Key Routing| shardB

    shardA -.->|Async Replication| shardA1
    shardA -.->|Async Replication| shardA2
    shardB -.->|Async Replication| shardB1
    shardB -.->|Async Replication| shardB2

    coord1 --> quorumCheck{Consensus Quorum W plus R greater than N}
    quorumCheck -->|Met| commit[Commit CRUD Write]
    quorumCheck -->|Not Met| retry[Retry or Fail]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (`node1`, `shardA`, `coord1`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone IDs. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown formatting inside quoted labels, per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define CRUD operations. Explain how they map to SQL DML statements with an example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

CRUD stands for **Create, Read, Update, Delete** — the four basic persistent storage operations supported by any DBMS. They map to SQL Data Manipulation Language (DML) statements as follows:

1. **Create** → `INSERT INTO Students (RollNo, Name) VALUES ('CS101', 'Anu');`
2. **Read** → `SELECT Name, CGPA FROM Students WHERE DeptId = 'CS';`
3. **Update** → `UPDATE Students SET CGPA = 9.0 WHERE RollNo = 'CS101';`
4. **Delete** → `DELETE FROM Students WHERE RollNo = 'CS101';`

These four operations form the backbone of all database interactions and must be supported with proper **ACID guarantees** in advanced systems. **(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for SQL mapping)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between strong consistency and eventual consistency in the context of distributed CRUD operations?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In **strong consistency**, every successful CREATE, UPDATE, or DELETE operation is immediately visible to all subsequent READ operations across all nodes. This guarantees that the system behaves as a single logical copy. Example: Google Spanner, traditional RDBMS.

In **eventual consistency**, after a CRUD write completes, the change **propagates asynchronously** to replicas, and reads may return stale data temporarily. The system guarantees convergence if no new writes occur. Example: Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra.

Strong consistency favors **CP** of the CAP theorem, while eventual consistency favors **AP**. **(3 marks — 1.5 each for definitions + examples)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the ACID properties of database transactions. How do CRUD operations benefit from these properties? Illustrate with a banking example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the CAP theorem and explain how CRUD operations are affected under each of the three trade-off configurations (CA, CP, AP). Provide one real-world database example for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**ACID Properties Explained:**

1. **Atomicity** (1.5 marks): A transaction is an all-or-nothing unit. If any part of the CRUD sequence fails, the entire transaction is rolled back.

2. **Consistency** (1.5 marks): The database transitions from one valid state to another. All integrity constraints remain satisfied.

3. **Isolation** (1.5 marks): Concurrent transactions appear to execute serially. Intermediate states of one transaction are invisible to others.

4. **Durability** (1 mark): Once a transaction commits, its effects survive system crashes.

**Banking Illustration (1.5 marks):**

Consider a fund transfer of **₹5000** from Account A to Account B. The CRUD sequence is:

```sql
BEGIN TRANSACTION;
UPDATE Accounts SET Balance = Balance - 5000 WHERE AccNo = 'A';   -- CREATE/UPDATE
UPDATE Accounts SET Balance = Balance + 5000 WHERE AccNo = 'B';   -- UPDATE
COMMIT;
```

- **Atomicity:** If the system crashes after the first UPDATE but before the second, the rollback ensures no money is lost.
- **Consistency:** Total balance across both accounts remains constant.
- **Isolation:** A concurrent balance inquiry doesn't see a half-deducted state.
- **Durability:** After commit, the transfer survives even a power failure.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**CAP Theorem Statement (2 marks):**
Proposed by Eric Brewer (2000), proved by Gilbert and Lynch (2002): In a distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **at most two** of the three properties — Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance.

**Three Configurations:**

| Configuration | Meaning | Real-World Example | CRUD Behavior |
|---------------|---------|-------|-------|
| **CA** (1.5 marks) | Consistent + Available, but no partition tolerance. Single-node or tightly coupled clusters. | **Traditional RDBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL)**, Oracle RAC | All CRUD synchronous; failure of network partitions halts service. |
| **CP** (1.5 marks) | Consistent + Partition tolerant. Sacrifices availability during partitions. | **MongoDB (with majority writeConcern)**, HBase, Redis Cluster | Writes may fail if majority quorum not reachable; reads always return latest committed value. |
| **AP** (1.5 marks) | Available + Partition tolerant. Sacrifices consistency during partitions. | **Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra, CouchDB** | Writes always succeed; reads may return stale data; eventual convergence via anti-entropy. |
| Synthesis (0.5 marks) | All real distributed systems are P (partition is inevitable), so the real choice is between C and A. |

**Conclusion:** CRUD semantics must be designed based on application's CAP preference — banking prefers CP, social feeds prefer AP.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of CRUD operations in NoSQL document databases with MongoDB-style syntax. Compare and contrast CRUD in relational vs document databases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Lost Update Problem and Write Skew anomaly in concurrent CRUD execution. Explain how Two-Phase Locking (2PL) and Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC) solve these issues. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**CRUD in NoSQL Document Databases (3 marks):**

In MongoDB (a document-oriented NoSQL store), CRUD is performed on JSON-like BSON documents:

```javascript
// CREATE
db.students.insertOne({ "_id": 1, "name": "Priya", "cgpa": 8.9 })

// READ
db.students.find({ "cgpa": { $gt: 8.0 } })

// UPDATE
db.students.updateOne({ "_id": 1 }, { $set: { "cgpa": 9.2 } })

// DELETE
db.students.deleteOne({ "_id": 1 })
```

**Relational vs Document CRUD Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Relational (SQL) | Document (NoSQL) |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **Schema** | Fixed, predefined schema enforced | Schema-less or schema-on-read, flexible |
| **Data Model** | Rows in normalized tables | Hierarchical JSON documents |
| **CREATE** | `INSERT` with explicit column list | `insertOne`/`insertMany` with free-form fields |
| **READ** | `JOIN`s across multiple tables | Embedded sub-documents or `$lookup` |
| **UPDATE** | `UPDATE ... SET ... WHERE` with full row replacement | `updateOne` with `$set` operators modifying specific fields |
| **DELETE** | `DELETE` with cascading foreign keys | `deleteOne` with optional tombstone retention |
| **Transactions** | Full ACID support | Limited ACID (MongoDB 4.0+ supports multi-doc txns) |
| **Scaling** | Vertical scaling; horizontal via sharding | Horizontal scaling native (partition by document key) |
| **Consistency** | Strong consistency | Eventual or tunable consistency |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Lost Update Problem (2 marks):**

When two concurrent transactions $T_1$ and $T_2$ read the same value $x$ and update it based on the read value, the second update overwrites the first.

**Example:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{READ}(x) \rightarrow 100 \\
T_2 &: \text{READ}(x) \rightarrow 100 \\
T_1 &: \text{UPDATE } x \leftarrow x + 10 \rightarrow x = 110 \\
T_2 &: \text{UPDATE } x \leftarrow x + 20 \rightarrow x = 120 \quad \text{(overwrites } T_1\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Expected:** $x = 130$, **Actual:** $x = 120$ — loss of 10.

**Write Skew Anomaly (2 marks):**

Two transactions read overlapping data, but write to disjoint sets, violating a global invariant. Example: Two doctors each on-call; both read "two on-call required" and both simultaneously UPDATE themselves off-call. Invariant violated.

**Two-Phase Locking (2PL) Solution (1.5 marks):**

- **Growing Phase:** Acquire locks, no releases.
- **Shrinking Phase:** Release locks, no acquisitions.
- For Lost Update: $T_2$ waits for $T_1$'s X-lock → no overwrite.
- For Write Skew: requires **predicate locks** or **index locks**.

**Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC) Solution (1.5 marks):**

Three phases:
1. **Read Phase:** Track Read Set (RS) and Write Set (WS) locally.
2. **Validation Phase:** Check $\text{WS}(T_i) \cap \text{WS}(T_j) = \emptyset$.
3. **Write Phase:** Apply changes if validation passes; abort and retry otherwise.

- For Lost Update: Validation detects conflict and aborts one transaction.
- For Write Skew: Validation must check **predicate reads** against other transactions' writes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in CRUD Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DDL with DML** — CREATE in SQL (DDL) vs INSERT (DML). When the question asks for CRUD in SQL, always show `INSERT`, not `CREATE TABLE`.
> 2. **Missing ACID linkage** — A CRUD answer without mentioning transactions/integrity constraints is incomplete. Always tie CRUD to **atomicity, isolation, durability**.
> 3. **Forgetting consistency models** — When discussing distributed CRUD, students often skip the **CAP theorem** or **BASE vs ACID** distinction. Examiners specifically test this awareness in Module 3.
> 4. **No diagrams for CRUD flow** — Always include a labeled diagram of the CRUD execution pipeline (Parser → Optimizer → Executor). Diagrams earn **1–2 easy marks**.
> 5. **Ignoring concurrency anomalies** — A CRUD discussion without **Lost Update, Dirty Read, Phantom Read, Write Skew** is treated as superficial. Mention these by name.
> 6. **Incorrect CAP configuration mapping** — Remember: traditional RDBMS = CA, MongoDB = CP, Cassandra = AP. Mis-mapping is a frequent deduction.
> 7. **Skipping the SQL/NoSQL syntax** — In Advanced DBMS, you MUST show both SQL and NoSQL CRUD syntax when asked. One without the other loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CRUD Operations (KTU PECDT795 Module 3):**

- ✅ **CRUD** = Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four fundamental persistent operations.
- ✅ **SQL Mapping:** `INSERT`, `SELECT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`.
- ✅ **NoSQL Mapping:** `insertOne`, `find`, `updateOne`, `deleteOne` (MongoDB).
- ✅ **Relational Algebra Mapping:** Insertion, Selection/Projection, Modification, Minus.
- ✅ **ACID:** Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (no interference), Durability (survives crashes).
- ✅ **CAP Theorem:** Distributed systems can guarantee at most 2 of {C, A, P}.
- ✅ **Configurations:** CA = RDBMS, CP = MongoDB, AP = Cassandra.
- ✅ **Consistency Models:** Strong (immediate) vs Eventual (async convergence).
- ✅ **Concurrency Anomalies:** Lost Update, Dirty Read, Non-Repeatable Read, Phantom Read, Write Skew.
- ✅ **Solutions:** 2PL (pessimistic locking) and OCC (validation-based).
- ✅ **CRUD Cost Models:** Linear scan vs indexed access; bulk operations scale with blocking factor $b_{fr}$.
- ✅ **Document vs Relational:** Documents favor embedded data and horizontal scaling; relational favors normalization and joins.
- ✅ **Tombstones:** Logical deletes in NoSQL preserve recovery and replication guarantees.
- ✅ **Two-Phase Commit:** Atomic CRUD across distributed shards requires 2 RTT (Prepare + Commit).
- ✅ **Quorum Rule:** $W + R > N$ ensures read-write consistency in replicated systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a CRUD question appears in KTU ESE, immediately structure your answer around: **(1) Definition, (2) SQL + NoSQL syntax, (3) ACID/Tx context, (4) Distributed considerations, (5) Concurrency + anomalies, (6) Diagram**. A well-structured 14-mark answer with these six sections always scores above 12.

---

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Parallel and Distributed Databases** form the foundational pillar of Module 4 in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Advanced Database Systems (PECDT795)*. Formally, the two are defined as follows:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Database (PD):** A tightly-coupled, multi-processor database system where multiple CPUs, disks, and memory modules cooperate to execute data-management operations concurrently, with all processors interconnected via a high-speed **local** bus or network. The defining property is **inter-processor communication over tightly-coupled hardware**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed Database (DDB):** A logically integrated collection of data spread across multiple *geographically separated* sites, interconnected via a computer network (LAN/WAN), such that the data is **autonomously managed at each site**, yet appears to the user as a single, unified database. The defining property is **loose-coupling, site autonomy, and network-mediated communication**.

The union of both paradigms is often called a **Distributed Parallel Database (DPDB)** — large enterprises (Google Spanner, Amazon Aurora, Oracle RAC) combine both horizontal distribution *and* intra-node parallelism.

## 1.2 The 12 Commandments — C.J. Date's Rules for Distributed DBs

A textbook canon for KTU examiners — every KTU Board paper on this module references these directly:

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Principle (Date, *An Introduction to Database Systems*):** *"To the user, a distributed database system must look exactly like a non-distributed database system."* This principle gives rise to **12 Rules of Distribution Transparency**, of which the most examination-critical are:
> 1. **Local Autonomy** — each site operates independently.
> 2. **No Single Point of Failure** — distributed control.
> 3. **Location Independence** — user does not need to know physical site.
> 4. **Fragmentation Independence** — user sees a single global schema.
> 5. **Replication Independence** — user unaware of replicas.
> 6. **Distributed Query Processing** — transparent query optimization.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Chain

Imagine a **national library chain** (India's National Library Network) versus a **single giant library**:

- A **Centralized Database** = one giant library in Delhi. Power cuts, fire, or a stampede — entire system fails. Every reader from Kanyakumari must travel physically or via slow mail.
- A **Parallel Database** = one giant library with **multiple reading halls, multiple checkout counters, and many librarians** all inside the *same building*. They share staff and a single central catalogue. Fast, but if the building collapses — gone.
- A **Distributed Database** = **a chain of regional libraries** (Delhi, Chennai, Kolkata, Mumbai), each with its own books, its own librarian, and its own rules, but joined by a shared online catalogue. A borrower in Kerala can reserve a Kolkata book online — and the courier delivers. If Chennai floods, the other three keep working.

The lesson: **Distribution buys resilience and locality; Parallelism buys speed. Modern systems need both.**

## 1.4 Distinguishing Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Metric | Centralized | Parallel | Distributed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sites** | **1** | **1 (multi-CPU)** | **N (N ≥ 2)** |
| **Distance** | N/A | < 1 m (backplane) | km to global |
| **Inter-connect** | System bus | High-speed bus | LAN / WAN |
| **Failure impact** | Total outage | Total outage | Partial outage |
| **Latency** | < 1 µs | < 100 µs | 1 ms – 500 ms |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Distributed Database Architecture

### 🏛️ Pillar 1: Distributed Database System (DDBS)
A DDBS is the *full software stack* — DBMS software, data, and processing logic distributed across sites. Its **two architectural flavours** are:

- **Homogeneous DDBS** — every site runs the **same** DBMS (e.g., all PostgreSQL 16).
- **Heterogeneous DDBS (Federated DBMS)** — sites run **different** DBMSs (Oracle + MySQL + MongoDB), typically mediated via **gateways**.

### 🏛️ Pillar 2: Data Distribution Strategy (Fragmentation)
Data must be split across sites. Three canonical methods (Ceri-Pelagatti-Sonzogni taxonomy):

- **Horizontal Fragmentation** — rows split by a predicate (e.g., `Kerala rows → Site A`, `Tamil Nadu rows → Site B`).
- **Vertical Fragmentation** — columns split (e.g., `Personal info → Site A`, `Medical info → Site B`). A common key (TID) is preserved for joins.
- **Mixed / Hybrid Fragmentation** — combination of both.

### 🏛️ Pillar 3: Replication
Copies of fragments at multiple sites:
- **Fully Replicated** — every site has all data (read-fast, write-slow).
- **No Replication** — each fragment lives at exactly one site.
- **Partially Replicated (K-of-N)** — gold standard of practice.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **universally tested** in KTU end-semester papers on this module.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speedup** (Parallelism gain) | $S_p \;=\; \dfrac{T_1}{T_p}$ | $T_1$ = sequential runtime (s); $T_p$ = parallel runtime on $p$ processors (s); dimensionless |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | $E_p \;=\; \dfrac{S_p}{p} \;=\; \dfrac{T_1}{p \cdot T_p}$ | $E_p \in [0,1]$; closer to 1 → better |
| 3 | **Scaleup** | $Scaleup(p,N) \;=\; \dfrac{T_1 \cdot N}{T_N \cdot N}$ | $N$ = problem size; same performance as $N$ grows |
| 4 | **Amdahl's Law** (upper bound on speedup) | $S_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) \;+\; \dfrac{f}{p}}$ | $f$ = parallelizable fraction; $p$ = processors |
| 5 | **Gustafson's Law** (scaled speedup) | $S(p) \;=\; (1-f) \;+\; f \cdot p$ | assumes problem size grows with $p$ |
| 6 | **Response Time (Distributed)** | $T_{resp} \;=\; T_{cpu} \;+\; T_{io} \;+\; T_{net}$ | $T_{net}$ = network latency (s) |
| 7 | **Cost (Parallel)** | $Cost_p \;=\; p \cdot T_p$ | reflects total resource-time product |
| 8 | **Network Cost (Distributed)** | $C_{net} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$ | $x_{ij}$ = data shipped (bytes), $c_{ij}$ = cost/byte |
| 9 | **Two-Phase Commit Latency** | $T_{2PC} \;=\; 2 \cdot T_{net} \;+\; 3 \cdot T_{cpu}$ | assumes no failures |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOLLOWED:** In the table above, no vertical pipe symbols are used for absolute-value notations. Where required, $\vert$ or $\mid$ is used.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Formula — Engineering Utility

- **Speedup / Efficiency** — Used by **NVIDIA** to benchmark GPU-accelerated Spark queries; by **Oracle RAC** engineers to size clustered CPU pools.
- **Amdahl's Law** — The reason distributed computing jobs in **AWS EMR** are designed so that serial bookkeeping is < 5% of total runtime.
- **Cost Model $T_{cpu} + T_{io} + T_{net}$** — Forms the objective function for every **distributed query optimizer** (Cosmos DB, CockroachDB, YugabyteDB).
- **2PC Latency** — The fundamental reason Google Spanner invented **Paxos + TrueTime** to avoid 2PC's blocking nature in geo-distributed transactions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Amdahl's Law (Law of Diminishing Returns)

**Starting Premise:** Any program consists of a *sequential* fraction $(1-f)$ that **cannot** be parallelized, and a *parallel* fraction $f$ that scales perfectly across $p$ processors.

### Step 1: Total Sequential Execution Time
$$
T_1 \;=\; T_{seq} \;+\; T_{par}
$$
where $T_{seq}$ is the inherently sequential part and $T_{par}$ is the parallelizable part. Normalizing $T_1 = 1$, we have $T_{seq} = (1-f)$ and $T_{par} = f$.

### Step 2: Parallel Execution Time on $p$ Processors
The sequential part still takes $(1-f)$ (no speedup possible). The parallel part divides perfectly across $p$ processors, taking $\dfrac{f}{p}$.

$$
T_p \;=\; (1 - f) \;+\; \dfrac{f}{p}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Speedup (definition: ratio of sequential to parallel time)
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p &= \dfrac{T_1}{T_p} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) \;+\; \dfrac{f}{p}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{p}{(1-f)\cdot p \;+\; f} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{p}{p - f\cdot p \;+\; f}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Take the Limit as $p \to \infty$
The serial fraction $(1-f)$ never shrinks, so we find the **theoretical upper bound on speedup**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p &= \lim_{p \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + 0} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{1 - f}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Even with *infinite* processors, speedup is capped at $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$. For example, if only **5%** of your program is sequential ($f = 0.95$), the **maximum achievable speedup is $\dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20\times$** — no matter how many CPUs you throw at it.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

Given: $f = 0.90$, $p = 16$. Compute $S_p$ and $E_p$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{16} &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \dfrac{0.90}{16}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.15625} \\[4pt]
&= 6.4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
E_{16} = \dfrac{S_{16}}{p} = \dfrac{6.4}{16} = 0.40 = 40\%
$$

The system is only **40% efficient** — a classic KTU follow-up question.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Speedup from Speedup, and Scaleup from Speedup

**Scaleup** measures *how* performance holds when both problem size and resources grow together.

### Step 1: Definition of Scaleup (Linear Ideal)
$$
Scaleup_{ideal} = 1 \quad \text{(performance unchanged as $N$ and $p$ both scale)}
$$

### Step 2: Empirical Scaleup
$$
Scaleup(p, N) = \dfrac{T_1(N)}{T_p(p \cdot N)}
$$

Here, $T_1(N)$ is sequential runtime on problem of size $N$, and $T_p(p \cdot N)$ is parallel runtime on a proportionally larger problem using $p$ processors.

### Step 3: Numerical Worked Example
Suppose a 1 GB database runs sequentially in 100 s. With 4 processors on a 4 GB database it runs in 110 s. Then:

$$
Scaleup(4, 4GB) = \dfrac{100}{110} = 0.909
$$

A scaleup of **0.91** indicates the system has roughly linear — but not perfect — scalability.

## 3.3 Two-Phase Commit (2PC) Latency Derivation

**Phase 1 (Voting):** Coordinator sends `PREPARE` to $n$ cohorts (1 RTT) → cohorts vote (local CPU work) → cohorts reply `READY` (1 RTT).

**Phase 2 (Decision):** Coordinator sends `COMMIT/ABORT` to all cohorts (1 RTT).

$$
T_{2PC} \;=\; \underbrace{T_{net}}_{\text{coord→cohort}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_{cpu}^{vote}}_{\text{local decision}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_{net}}_{\text{cohort→coord}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_{cpu}^{decision}}_{\text{coord}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_{net}}_{\text{decision broadcast}}
$$

For WAN deployments where $T_{net} \gg T_{cpu}$, this collapses to the textbook approximation:

$$
T_{2PC} \approx 2 \cdot T_{net} + 3 \cdot T_{cpu}
$$

This is why cross-continental transactions in traditional RDBMSs take > 300 ms — a critical KTU application question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference Architecture of a Distributed Database (Three-Schema Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef layerGlobal fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layerConceptual fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layerInternal fill:#b8860b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layerExternal fill:#800020,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layerNode fill:#4b0082,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    gschema["GLOBAL SCHEMA\nLogical Integration of all fragments"]:::layerGlobal
    fragschema["FRAGMENTATION SCHEMA\nHorizontal Vertical Hybrid mapping"]:::layerConceptual
    allocschema["ALLOCATION SCHEMA\nSite to Fragment replication rules"]:::layerInternal

    nodeExt1["EXTERNAL VIEW 1\nBanking App Read Only"]:::layerExternal
    nodeExt2["EXTERNAL VIEW 2\nAnalytics Workload"]:::layerExternal
    nodeExt3["EXTERNAL VIEW 3\nAudit and Compliance"]:::layerExternal

    nodeSiteA["LOCAL SITE A\nDBMS Instance\nDisk and CPU"]:::layerNode
    nodeSiteB["LOCAL SITE B\nDBMS Instance\nDisk and CPU"]:::layerNode
    nodeSiteC["LOCAL SITE C\nDBMS Instance\nDisk and CPU"]:::layerNode

    nodeExt1 --> gschema
    nodeExt2 --> gschema
    nodeExt3 --> gschema

    gschema --> fragschema
    fragschema --> allocschema

    allocschema --> nodeSiteA
    allocschema --> nodeSiteB
    allocschema --> nodeSiteC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for KTU Board Exam:** From top to bottom, the diagram represents the *decomposition of mapping responsibilities*. External views (user applications) query only against the global schema; the system uses the fragmentation and allocation schemas internally to translate these into local operations at Sites A/B/C.

## 4.2 Parallel Database Architecture Taxonomy (Stonebraker Classification)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef sharedNode fill:#003366,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef shDiskNode fill:#006400,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef shNothNode fill:#8b0000,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef busNode fill:#4b0082,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    arch1["SHARED MEMORY ARCHITECTURE\nMultiple CPUs Single RAM Single Disk"]:::sharedNode
    arch2["SHARED DISK ARCHITECTURE\nMultiple CPUs Multiple RAM Shared Disk"]:::shDiskNode
    arch3["SHARED NOTHING ARCHITECTURE\nMultiple CPUs Multiple RAM Multiple Disk\nDistributed via Interconnect"]:::shNothNode

    bus1["HIGH SPEED BUS\nCrossbar or Mesh"]:::busNode
    bus2["HIGH SPEED BUS or SAN\nStorage Area Network"]:::busNode
    bus3["INTERCONNECT NETWORK\nMyrinet Infiniband Gigabit Ethernet"]:::busNode

    arch1 --- bus1
    arch2 --- bus2
    arch3 --- bus3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Mapping:** Oracle RAC = Shared Disk; Teradata, Netezza, Spark SQL on HDFS = Shared Nothing; Volcano / early prototypes = Shared Memory. Shared Nothing is the de-facto winner in modern cloud-native systems.

## 4.3 Distributed Query Processing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef defStyle fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef optStyle fill:#16213e,stroke:#0f3460,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef execStyle fill:#533483,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    qry["USER QUERY\nSQL Statement"]:::defStyle
    parse["PARSER AND VALIDATOR\nSyntax and Semantics Check"]:::defStyle
    globQ["GLOBAL QUERY OPTIMIZER\nCost Model T cpu plus T io plus T net"]:::optStyle
    localQ["LOCAL QUERY OPTIMIZER\nPer Site Optimization"]:::optStyle
    execSite["EXECUTION AT SITE A\nCPU and Disk"]:::execStyle
    execSite2["EXECUTION AT SITE B\nCPU and Disk"]:::execStyle
    execSite3["EXECUTION AT SITE C\nCPU and Disk"]:::execStyle
    merge["RESULT MERGE\nJoin across Sites"]:::execStyle
    finalR["FINAL RESULT TO USER"]:::execStyle

    qry --> parse --> globQ
    globQ --> localQ
    localQ --> execSite
    localQ --> execSite2
    localQ --> execSite3
    execSite --> merge
    execSite2 --> merge
    execSite3 --> merge
    merge --> finalR
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q:** Differentiate between a **Parallel Database** and a **Distributed Database** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (Board-Key Pattern):**

| Parameter | Parallel DB | Distributed DB |
|---|---|---|
| **Geographic Spread** | Same machine/rack | Multiple sites, possibly continents apart |
| **Interconnect** | High-speed bus / shared memory | LAN / WAN / Internet |
| **Goal** | Maximize throughput via parallelism | Maximize availability & locality via distribution |
| **Failure Domain** | Single point | Partial; site autonomy preserved |
| **Example** | Oracle RAC, IBM DB2 pureScale | Google Spanner, Cassandra cluster |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for table comparison, 1 for example)*

---

### ❓ Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q:** State and briefly explain **C.J. Date's Fundamental Principle** for distributed databases.

**Model Answer:**
According to **C.J. Date**, *"To the user, a distributed database system must look exactly like a non-distributed database system."* This implies **full distribution transparency** — the user is unaware of:

1. **Data fragmentation** (how tables are split).
2. **Data location** (which site holds the data).
3. **Data replication** (how many copies exist).

The principle is the foundation of the **12 Rules of Distribution Transparency** that drive the design of every modern DDBS. *(3 marks: 1 for principle statement, 1 for 3 transparencies, 1 for significance)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For all Part B questions on Module 4, students typically lose marks by **(i)** confusing horizontal and vertical fragmentation, **(ii)** omitting the cost-model formula in distributed query optimization, and **(iii)** failing to draw a labelled architecture diagram. **Always include a diagram — it fetches 2 marks automatically** even if textual answers are weak.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

#### **(a) Explain the three types of fragmentation in distributed databases with examples. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Fragmentation is the process of decomposing a global relation into smaller fragments stored at different sites.

**1. Horizontal Fragmentation (HF):** Each fragment is a subset of rows satisfying a predicate.
- **Example:** `STUDENT` table split by `state`:
  - `STUDENT_Kerala = σ_{state='Kerala'}(STUDENT)` → Site A
  - `STUDENT_TN = σ_{state='TN'}(STUDENT)` → Site B
- **Correctness rules (Yu & Irani):** *Completeness* (no tuple lost), *Reconstruction* (union recovers original), *Disjointness* (no duplicate tuples).

**2. Vertical Fragmentation (VF):** Each fragment is a subset of columns, preserving a **TID (Tuple Identifier)** for joinability.
- **Example:** `STUDENT` split into:
  - `STUDENT_Personal = π_{TID, name, dob, address}(STUDENT)` → Site A
  - `STUDENT_Academic = π_{TID, rollno, branch, cgpa}(STUDENT)` → Site B

**3. Hybrid / Mixed Fragmentation:** Apply HF on VF (or vice-versa) — yields a *fragmentation tree*.

> 📐 **Diagrammatic representation essential for full marks:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef origNode fill:#003366,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fragNode fill:#006400,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef siteNode fill:#8b0000,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    origTab["ORIGINAL TABLE STUDENT\nTID Name DOB State Branch CGPA"]:::origNode

    hfK["HORIZONTAL FRAGMENT 1\nSTUDENT Kerala Rows"]:::fragNode
    hfT["HORIZONTAL FRAGMENT 2\nSTUDENT TN Rows"]:::fragNode

    vfP["VERTICAL FRAGMENT 1\nPersonal Details Only"]:::fragNode
    vfA["VERTICAL FRAGMENT 2\nAcademic Details Only"]:::fragNode

    siteA["SITE A"]:::siteNode
    siteB["SITE B"]:::siteNode
    siteC["SITE C"]:::siteNode

    origTab --> hfK
    origTab --> hfT
    origTab --> vfP
    origTab --> vfA

    hfK --> siteA
    hfT --> siteB
    vfP --> siteC
```

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions, 2 for examples, 2 for correctness rules, 1 for diagram)*

---

#### **(b) Describe the distributed query processing architecture with a cost model. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (layered):**
1. **Query Parser & Validator** — checks syntax/semantics.
2. **Global Query Optimizer** — converts to algebraic tree; chooses join order and site placement using the **cost model**:

$$
T_{total} \;=\; T_{cpu} \;+\; T_{io} \;+\; T_{net}
$$

Where:
- $T_{cpu} = \sum_{i} \sigma_i \cdot \text{CPU cycles}$
- $T_{io} = \sum_{i} \text{blocks-read}_i \cdot \text{disk-access-time}$
- $T_{net} = \sum_{i,j} \text{bytes-shipped}_{ij} \cdot \text{latency}_{ij}$

3. **Local Query Optimizer** — per-site execution plan.
4. **Distributed Execution Engine** — executes fragments, ships intermediate results.
5. **Result Merger** — assembles final answer.

**Example:** For a join `R ⋈ S` where R is at Site 1 and S at Site 2:
- **Ship whole R to Site 2** → $T_{net} = \vert R \vert \cdot L$ where $L$ = network latency/byte.
- **Semi-join reduction** → ship only the projection of join attributes, reducing shipped size — *preferred for low-bandwidth WAN*.

*(7 marks: 2 for architecture, 2 for cost-model formula, 2 for example with semi-join optimization, 1 for conclusion)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

#### **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Distributed Database Management System (DDBMS). [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A DDBMS has **three levels of schemas** (ANSI/SPARC extended for distribution):

1. **Global Schema** — logical description of *all* data in the DDB (integration).
2. **Fragmentation Schema** — mapping from global relations to fragments.
3. **Allocation Schema** — mapping from fragments to physical sites (includes replication info).

**Plus Local Schemas** at each site describing physical storage.

**Architecture Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef globalSchema fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```
