```mermaid
    classDef fragSchema fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef allocSchema fill:#b8860b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef localSchema fill:#800020,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef siteNode fill:#4b0082,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    gSchema["GLOBAL SCHEMA\nConceptual Integration of All Relations"]:::globalSchema
    fSchema["FRAGMENTATION SCHEMA\nHF VF MF Mappings"]:::fragSchema
    aSchema["ALLOCATION SCHEMA\nFragment to Site with Replication"]:::allocSchema

    ls1["LOCAL SCHEMA AT SITE 1\nInternal Storage Description"]:::localSchema
    ls2["LOCAL SCHEMA AT SITE 2\nInternal Storage Description"]:::localSchema
    ls3["LOCAL SCHEMA AT SITE 3\nInternal Storage Description"]:::localSchema

    s1["PHYSICAL SITE 1\nCPU Disk Network"]:::siteNode
    s2["PHYSICAL SITE 2\nCPU Disk Network"]:::siteNode
    s3["PHYSICAL SITE 3\nCPU Disk Network"]:::siteNode

    gSchema --> fSchema --> aSchema
    aSchema --> ls1
    aSchema --> ls2
    aSchema --> ls3
    ls1 --> s1
    ls2 --> s2
    ls3 --> s3
```

**DDBMS Components:**
- **DBMS Software** (local) — at each site.
- **Data** (local & distributed).
- **Network Software** — site-to-site communication.
- **Distributed Data Dictionary (DDC)** — meta-data about fragments, replicas, sites.

*(7 marks: 2 for three-schema description, 3 for diagram, 2 for component list)*

---

#### **(b) Discuss the merits and demerits of distributed databases. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| # | **Merits** | **Demerits** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transparency** of location, replication, fragmentation | **Increased complexity** of software (DDBMS design is hard) |
| 2 | **Reliability & Availability** — fault isolation per site | **Distributed commit problem** (2PC, 3PC overhead) |
| 3 | **Improved Performance** — local queries are fast | **Security exposure** — network is attack surface |
| 4 | **Modular Growth** — add new sites easily | **Data integrity harder** — multi-site ACID is expensive |
| 5 | **Reflects organizational structure** — depts own their data | **Cost** of network, replication, skilled DBAs |
| 6 | **Scalability** — horizontal scaling via new nodes | **Distributed query optimization is NP-hard** |
| 7 | **Disaster Recovery** via geographic distribution | **CAP theorem tradeoff** — must sacrifice consistency or availability under network partition |

*(7 marks: 4 merits + 3 demerits with at least one paragraph explanation each, or a clean tabular answer)*

---

## 🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Module 4 — Introduction Questions:**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing PD vs DDB** — Parallel = single site, multi-CPU; Distributed = multi-site, autonomous. Do not interchange.
> 2. ❌ **Skipping the diagram** — Even for a 3-mark Part A, a small sketch adds weight; for Part B it is **mandatory**.
> 3. ❌ **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — Always specify $T$ in seconds, $p$ in processor count, $f$ as dimensionless fraction.
> 4. ❌ **Amdahl vs Gustafson confusion** — Amdahl fixes problem size; Gustafson grows it with $p$. Examiners test this distinction yearly.
> 5. ❌ **Not stating Date's principle verbatim** — Examiners award full marks only for the exact phrasing.
> 6. ❌ **Ignoring CAP theorem** — Every modern distributed discussion touches CAP; omitting it loses at least 1 mark.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> ✅ **Core Definition:** Parallel DB = single-site, multi-CPU speed; Distributed DB = multi-site, autonomous integration.
>
> ✅ **C.J. Date's Fundamental Principle:** "A distributed database system must look exactly like a non-distributed one." — the philosophical bedrock of all 12 transparency rules.
>
> ✅ **Three-Schema Architecture:** Global Schema → Fragmentation Schema → Allocation Schema → Local Schemas (remember the *top-down* order).
>
> ✅ **Fragmentation Types:** Horizontal (rows by predicate), Vertical (columns + TID), Hybrid (combination tree).
>
> ✅ **Replication Modes:** Full (read-fast, write-slow), None (low overhead), Partial K-of-N (best in practice).
>
> ✅ **Speedup Formula:** $S_p = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ — Amdahl's bound is $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$.
>
> ✅ **Efficiency Formula:** $E_p = \dfrac{S_p}{p} \in [0, 1]$.
>
> ✅ **Distributed Cost Model:** $T_{total} = T_{cpu} + T_{io} + T_{net}$ — drives every optimizer decision.
>
> ✅ **2PC Latency:** $\approx 2 \cdot T_{net} + 3 \cdot T_{cpu}$ — explains WAN transaction slowness.
>
> ✅ **Parallel Architecture Trio (Stonebraker):** Shared Memory | Shared Disk | Shared Nothing — modern systems favour Shared Nothing.
>
> ✅ **CAP Theorem (Brewer):** Under network partition (P), pick **C**onsistency **or** **A**vailability — not both. Mentioned explicitly in every modern distributed design.
>
> ✅ **Industry Examples:** Oracle RAC = Shared Disk; Teradata = Shared Nothing; Spanner = Globally Distributed + TrueTime API; Cassandra AP, DynamoDB AP, MongoDB CP.
>
> ✅ **Exam-Day Power Move:** Always include at least one labelled diagram — gives 1.5–2 marks even on a partial answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Modelling with Graphs: Building a Graph Database Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Data Modelling with Graphs: Building a Graph Database Application # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Graph Data Modelling** is a paradigm of database design that represents data as a collection of **vertices (nodes)**, **edges (relationships)**, and **properties**, where the relationships between data entities are treated as first-class citizens of equal importance to the data itself. Unlike the relational model which relies on foreign keys and JOIN operations, the graph model natively stores, indexes, and traverses these connections without expensive join computations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A *Property Graph* consists of a finite set of nodes, a finite set of directed/undirected edges (relationships), and a finite set of properties (key-value pairs) attached to both nodes and edges. This forms the foundation of modern Graph Database Management Systems (GDBMS) such as **Neo4j**, **Amazon Neptune**, **TigerGraph**, and **ArangoDB**. ### Core Components of the Graph Model | Component | Mathematical Notation | Description | |---|---|---| | **Node (Vertex)** | $V$ | Represents a real-world entity (e.g., Person, Product, City) | | **Edge (Relationship)** | $E \subseteq V \times V$ | Represents a directed connection between two nodes | | **Property** | $P : V \cup E \rightarrow (K \times V_{al})$ | Key-value attribute pair | | **Label** | $L : V \cup E \rightarrow \Sigma^{*}$ | Categorical tag assigned to nodes or edges | The complete formal representation can be expressed as: $$G = (V, E, P, L)$$ where $G$ is the property graph, $V$ is the set of nodes, $E$ is the set of edges, $P$ is the property set, and $L$ is the label set. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Social Friendship Map** > > Imagine you are looking at a **classroom seating chart** for a B.Tech seminar. Each student is a **circle (node)**. The friendships between students are **lines drawn between the circles (edges)**. Now, if you want to know *"Who is a friend of a friend of Rahul who also loves cricket?"*, you simply **walk along the lines** from Rahul to his friends, then to their friends, filtering by the "loves_cricket" property. > > - In a **Relational Database (SQL)**, this requires multiple `JOIN` operations across tables (Students, Friendships, Hobbies) — slow for deep traversals. > - In a **Graph Database**, this is a **single, native pointer-traversal** — extremely fast because relationships are physically stored as direct references (pointers), not as foreign keys to be resolved. The critical insight is that **relationships are the most valuable data**, not just a side-effect of how data is joined. KTU 2024 emphasises this as the *Connection-Oriented* paradigm shift. ### Why Graph Databases? (KTU High-Yield Justification) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **OCG Principle** for graph supremacy over RDBMS: > - **O**bjects become Nodes > - **C**onnections become Edges > - **G**raph traversal replaces JOIN > > Performance is measured by **hops (relationship depth)**, not by total dataset size. ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics in Graph Databases - **Traversal Speed:** Constant time per hop — **O(1) per relationship traversed** (index-free adjacency). - **Schema Flexibility:** Property graph is **schema-optional** (no rigid DDL required). - **Cypher Query Language:** Declarative pattern-matching language used by Neo4j (KTU-focused DBMS). - **ACID Compliance:** Neo4j is fully **ACID-compliant** since version 2.0. - **Index-Free Adjacency:** The defining performance feature — **~2 to 3 milliseconds per traversal hop**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Property Graph Model — Deep Structural Breakdown The Property Graph is the de-facto standard adopted by Neo4j, which KTU frequently references. Let us deconstruct it step-by-step. ### Step 1 — Node Definition A **node** is the fundamental entity. It represents a discrete object in the domain. - **Analogy:** A single person, a single movie, a single transaction. - **Why:** Nodes are addressable via a unique internal ID; they may have **labels** to indicate type. - **How:** In Cypher: `(p:Person {name: 'Rahul', age: 21})` creates a node with label `Person` and two properties. ### Step 2 — Relationship (Edge) Definition A **relationship** connects two nodes. It is always **typed**, **directed**, and may carry **properties**. - **Analogy:** A "KNOWS" or "ACTED_IN" connection. - **Why:** Direction encodes semantic meaning (e.g., `(:Person)-[:OWNS]->(:Car)`). - **How:** In Cypher: `()-[:KNOWS {since: 2020}]->()`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Distinction:** A relationship in a graph database is **stored as a physical pointer** between two nodes. This is the essence of *index-free adjacency*. A JOIN in SQL is a **logical operation performed at query time**; a graph traversal is a **physical operation performed at storage time**. ### Step 3 — Property Storage **Properties** are key-value pairs that decorate both nodes and edges. - **Analogy:** Name and age on a person's ID card; date since two people became friends. - **Why:** They provide context without requiring additional tables. - **How:** Properties are stored as a lightweight, in-memory hash map attached to the element. ### Step 4 — Traversal Pattern A **traversal** is the act of walking the graph from a starting node, following relationships, and applying filters. - **Analogy:** Following a chain of "people also bought" recommendations. - **Why:** Most real-world queries are *path-shaped*, not *table-shaped*. - **How:** Implemented via iterators that visit neighbours in constant time. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Table — Memorise This for the Exam** | Concept | Mathematical/Logical Expression | Boundary / Constraint | Application Domain | |---|---|---|---| | Graph Formal Definition | $G = (V, E)$ | $V$ = finite set, $E \subseteq (V \times V)$ | Universal | | Property Graph | $G = (V, E, P, L)$ | $P$ = property map, $L$ = label set | Neo4j, TinkerPop | | Graph Cardinality | $\vert E \vert = \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) / 2$ (undirected) | Handshaking Lemma | Network analysis | | Traversal Complexity (Graph DB) | $T(n) = O(1) \cdot h$ where $h$ = hop count | Constant per hop | Index-free adjacency | | Traversal Complexity (RDBMS JOIN) | $T(n) = O(n \log n)$ or worse | Depends on indexes | SQL performance | | Degree of Vertex | $\deg(v) = \vert \{ u \in V : (v,u) \in E \} \vert$ | Local connectivity metric | Social networks | | Path Existence | $\exists P = (v_0, v_1, ..., v_k)$ such that each $(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ | Reachability test | Routing, fraud rings | | Density of Graph | $\rho = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1)}$ | $0 \leq \rho \leq 1$ | Graph classification | > **Engineering Utility:** These metrics directly govern performance choices. If a query needs 5+ hops, a graph database will outperform SQL by **orders of magnitude** (commonly 100x–1000x in benchmarks). This is the *Why* the KTU 2024 syllabus dedicates a full module to graph databases. ## 2.3 Building a Graph Database Application — Design Phases The KTU 2024 scheme specifies a structured **5-phase methodology** for graph application development: ### Phase 1 — Domain Identification Identify the entities (nodes) and the *connections between them* (relationships). The principle is: **"Ask about the questions, then model the answers."** ### Phase 2 — Whiteboard Modelling Visually sketch the graph. KTU strongly recommends this agile, code-free design step. ### Phase 3 — Property Identification Add attributes to nodes and edges. Decide what is a property vs. what is a relationship. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule of Thumb:** *If you find yourself writing `JOIN` in your head, you probably need a relationship instead of a property.* ### Phase 4 — Query Mapping Translate the *top 5 most frequent business questions* into Cypher patterns. This drives the schema, not the other way around. ### Phase 5 — Iteration & Refinement Run the application, profile slow traversals, add indexes on frequently filtered properties. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Domains | Domain | Graph Use Case | |---|---| | **Social Networks** | Friend-of-friend suggestions, community detection | | **Fraud Detection** | Identifying fraud rings via shared identifiers (KYC) | | **Recommendation Engines** | "Customers who bought X also bought Y" | | **Knowledge Graphs** | Google Search, semantic search, AI reasoning | | **Network/IT Operations** | Dependency mapping, root cause analysis | | **Life Sciences** | Protein-protein interaction networks | | **Supply Chain** | Parts, suppliers, and shipments traversal | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Traversal Complexity Comparison This derivation proves **why** graph databases outperform relational ones for connected-data queries. KTU expects students to be able to present this comparison. ### 3.1.1 The Relational JOIN Cost Derivation Let us analyse a 2-hop traversal: *"Find friends of friends of a given person."* **Step 1 — Database Structure Setup** In a relational schema, we typically have: - `Person(pid, name)` table with $N$ rows - `Knows(pid1, pid2)` table with $M$ rows (bidirectional friendship) **Step 2 — SQL Query Construction** ```sql SELECT p2.name FROM Person p1 JOIN Knows k1 ON p1.pid = k1.pid1 JOIN Knows k2 ON k1.pid2 = k2.pid1 JOIN Person p2 ON k2.pid2 = p2.pid WHERE p1.name = 'Rahul'; ``` **Step 3 — Cost Estimation** For each join, the cost depends on the join algorithm: $$ C_{nested\_loop} = O(\vert R \vert \times \vert S \vert) $$ Applying this to our 2-join query: $$ C_{total} = O(N) + O(M) + O(M \cdot M) + O(N) = O(M^2) $$ **Step 4 — Logical Explanation** - The outer scan of `Knows` (k1) yields **M** candidate rows. - For each, the inner scan of `Knows` (k2) requires another full scan of **M** rows. - Without an index, this becomes a **Cartesian product** of size $M \times M$. **Step 5 — Generalisation to k-hops** For a $k$-hop traversal, the relational cost grows exponentially: $$ C_{RDBMS}(k) = O(M^k) $$ ### 3.1.2 The Graph Traversal Cost Derivation **Step 1 — Graph Storage Representation** Each node physically stores a list of its outgoing relationships as direct pointers (index-free adjacency). **Step 2 — Traversal Operation** Starting at node $v_0$, the traversal performs: $$ \forall e \in E_{out}(v_i) : v_{i+1} = \text{target}(e) $$ **Step 3 — Cost Per Hop** Since each relationship is a direct memory pointer, lookup is constant time: $$ C_{hop} = O(1) $$ **Step 4 — Total Cost for k-hop Traversal** $$ C_{Graph}(k) = k \cdot O(1) = O(k) $$ **Step 5 — The Performance Ratio** The performance advantage of graph over relational for depth $k$: $$ R(k) = \frac{C_{RDBMS}(k)}{C_{Graph}(k)} = \frac{O(M^k)}{O(k)} \approx \frac{M^k}{k} $$ For a real-world social network with $M = 100$ average friends and a 3-hop query: $$ R(3) = \frac{100^3}{3} = 333,333 $$ This **~333,000x speedup** is precisely why KTU 2024 mandates graph literacy for B.Tech CS students. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** This derivation is the *core mathematical justification* for choosing graph databases. In board exams, presenting this complexity comparison is a guaranteed high-scoring answer. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework for Graph vs. Relational | Query Characteristic | Recommended DBMS | Justification | |---|---|---| | Highly connected data, deep traversals (3+ hops) | **Graph DB** | Constant time per hop | | Tabular reporting, aggregations, OLAP | **Relational DB** | Mature SQL aggregation | | Hierarchical data with limited depth | **Document/Graph DB** | Trade-off depends on depth | | Network-like data (routes, dependencies) | **Graph DB** | Path-finding native | | Transactional banking records | **Relational DB** | ACID + strong consistency | | Recommendation engines | **Graph DB** | Pattern-based traversal | ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Building a Sample Graph Database Application The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies a "Building a Graph Database Application" sub-topic. The following is the exhaustive, board-evaluable walkthrough. ### Scenario A **Movie Recommendation System** for a streaming platform (e.g., a clone of Netflix's basic recommendation logic). ### Step 1 — Identify Domain Entities (Nodes) The entities in our domain are: - `Person` (a user or an actor — distinguished by label) - `Movie` (a film) - `Genre` (a category like Action, Drama) ### Step 2 — Identify Domain Relationships (Edges) - `(:Person)-[:WATCHED]->(:Movie)` — viewing history - `(:Person)-[:RATED {score: 5}]->(:Movie)` — explicit rating - `(:Person)-[:FRIENDS_WITH]->(:Person)` — social connection - `(:Movie)-[:IN_GENRE]->(:Genre)` — classification - `(:Person)-[:ACTED_IN]->(:Movie)` — cast information ### Step 3 — Identify Domain Properties - Person: `name`, `age`, `email`, `signup_date` - Movie: `title`, `release_year`, `duration` - RATED relationship: `score` (1–5), `rated_at` (timestamp) ### Step 4 — Whiteboard Sketch (Mermaid Representation) See Section 4 for the full visual diagram. ### Step 5 — Schema Implementation (Conceptual, NOT code) The data model declaration involves: - Creating constraints on unique identifiers (e.g., Person.email, Movie.title + release_year) - Creating indexes on frequently queried properties (e.g., Movie.title, Person.name) ### Step 6 — Query Formulation (Cypher Pattern Logic) **Query A — Find movies Rahul's friends rated highly:** The traversal pattern in declarative form: $$ \text{MATCH } (r:Person \{name:'Rahul'\}) \xrightarrow{:\text{FRIENDS\_WITH}} (f:Person) \xrightarrow{:\text{RATED}} (m:Movie) $$ $$ \text{WHERE } r.\text{score} \geq 4 \text{ AND NOT } (r)-[:\text{WATCHED}]->(m) $$ $$ \text{RETURN } m.\text{title}, \text{avg}(\text{score}) \text{ ORDER BY avg DESC} $$ This is read as: *"Starting from Rahul, walk to his friends, then to the movies they rated, filter by score, and exclude movies Rahul has already watched."* ### Step 7 — Application Layer The application layer typically uses: - A driver (e.g., Neo4j Python/Java/JS driver) - An HTTP API (e.g., Flask, Spring Boot, Express) - Frontend visualisation (e.g., D3.js, vis.js) ### Step 8 — Indexing and Performance Tuning - **Composite indexes** on `(label, property)` for fast lookups. - **Schema-based indexes** on relationship properties for filtered traversals. - **Query profiling** to detect Cartesian-product style traversals. ## 3.4 Edge Cases & Common Pitfalls (Board Favourites) > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *direction* of relationships. A relationship `(A)-[:KNOWS]->(B)` is **not** the same as `(B)-[:KNOWS]->(A)` unless explicitly created bidirectionally. This affects all path queries. > [!WARNING] > **Second Pitfall:** Conflating *Labels* and *Properties*. A **Label** categorises a node (`Person`, `Movie`), while a **Property** is a value attached to a node (`name`, `age`). They are NOT interchangeable in Cypher. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Property Graph Data Model — Visual Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Person Node
name Rahul
age 21
label Person] nodeB[Person Node
name Anu
age 22
label Person] nodeC[Movie Node
title Inception
year 2010
label Movie] nodeD[Genre Node
name SciFi
label Genre] nodeA -->|FRIENDS_WITH
since 2019| nodeB nodeB -->|RATED
score 5| nodeC nodeA -->|WATCHED
date 2024| nodeC nodeC -->|IN_GENRE| nodeD nodeB -->|ACTED_IN| nodeC ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Circles/Rectangles (nodeA, nodeB, ...)** are **Nodes** (entities). - **Arrows (-->)** are **Directed Relationships** (edges). - **Labels on arrows (FRIENDS_WITH, RATED, WATCHED)** are **Relationship Types**. - **Key-value pairs inside the boxes** are **Properties**. - **Words like "Person", "Movie", "Genre"** are **Node Labels**. ## 4.2 Application Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid graph TD subgraph client["CLIENT LAYER"] UI["Web or Mobile UI
User Interaction"] end subgraph api["API LAYER"] REST["REST API Server
Flask or Spring Boot"] AUTH["Auth Service
Token Validation"] end subgraph app["APPLICATION LAYER"] SVC["Recommendation Service
Business Logic"] QUERY["Cypher Query Builder"] end subgraph db["DATABASE LAYER"] NEO["Neo4j Graph DB
Property Graph Store"] CACHE["Query Result Cache
Redis"] IDX["Indexes on Properties"] end UI --> REST REST --> AUTH AUTH --> SVC SVC --> QUERY QUERY --> NEO SVC -.cache hit.-> CACHE NEO --- IDX ``` **Flow Explanation:** 1. The user issues a query from the **Client Layer** (UI). 2. The **API Layer** authenticates the request and routes it. 3. The **Application Layer** builds a Cypher pattern from business logic. 4. The **Database Layer** executes the pattern using index-free adjacency traversals, served from disk and memory-mapped indexes. ## 4.3 Traversal vs. JOIN — Conceptual Comparison ```mermaid graph LR subgraph rel["RELATIONAL JOIN APPROACH"] R1["Person Table
Scan for Rahul"] R2["Knows Table
Full Scan"] R3["Knows Table
Full Scan Again"] R4["Person Table
Lookup Friends"] R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 end subgraph grph["GRAPH TRAVERSAL APPROACH"] G1["Node Rahul
Direct Memory Address"] G2["Follow FRIENDS_WITH Pointer
O of 1"] G3["Follow FRIENDS_OF_FRIEND Pointer
O of 1"] G1 --> G2 --> G3 end ``` **Reading the diagram:** The relational path requires **multiple full-table scans** and a logical join computation. The graph path follows **direct memory pointers** in constant time per hop. ## 4.4 Graph Application Development Lifecycle ```mermaid graph TD A["Step 1: Identify Domain
List Entities and Connections"] --> B["Step 2: Whiteboard Model
Sketch Nodes and Edges"] B --> C["Step 3: Define Properties
Add Attributes"] C --> D["Step 4: Map Top Queries
Translate to Cypher Patterns"] D --> E["Step 5: Implement and Test
Build Application Layer"] E --> F["Step 6: Profile and Tune
Add Indexes"] F --> G{"Performance Satisfactory?"} G -->|No| D G -->|Yes| H["Step 7: Deploy to Production"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Instructions (KTU 2024):** Answer in **3 to 4 sentences**. Each question carries **3 marks**. No diagrams required, but labelled points earn full marks. ### Question 1: Define the Property Graph Model. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** The Property Graph Model is a data modelling paradigm in which data is represented as a set of **nodes** (entities) connected by **directed, typed relationships** (edges), with both nodes and edges carrying **properties** (key-value pairs) and **labels** (categorical tags). It is formally expressed as $G = (V, E, P, L)$ where $V$ is the vertex set, $E$ is the edge set, $P$ is the property set, and $L$ is the label set. Neo4j is the canonical implementation of this model and is widely used in industry for connected-data applications. ### Question 2: What is Index-Free Adjacency? Why is it important? [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Model Answer:** Index-Free Adjacency is the property of graph databases in which each node physically stores direct memory pointers to its adjacent nodes via outgoing relationships. This eliminates the need for index lookups during traversal, making each hop a **constant-time O(1) operation**. It is important because it ensures that query performance depends on the *hop count* of the traversal, not on the *total size* of the dataset, which is the key performance advantage over relational JOINs. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Mandatory) > **Instructions (KTU 2024):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **"Explain the Property Graph Model in detail. How does it differ from the relational model in terms of schema design and query performance?"** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** **1. Property Graph Model — Definition (3 marks):** The Property Graph Model represents data as a directed multigraph where: - **Nodes** are entities with **labels** and **properties**. - **Relationships** are typed, directed edges with **properties**. - Both nodes and edges can have arbitrary key-value properties. Formally, $G = (V, E, P, L)$ where every node $v \in V$ has a label $l \in L$ and properties from $P$, and every edge $e \in E$ has a type and connects two nodes. **2. Schema Design Differences (2 marks):** - The relational model requires a **fixed schema** defined via DDL before data insertion. Tables have rigid columns, and relationships are expressed via foreign keys. - The property graph model is **schema-flexible** — nodes and edges can have heterogeneous properties. There is no JOIN mechanism; relationships are first-class citizens. **3. Query Performance Differences (2 marks):** - Relational JOINs are **logical** operations whose cost grows with the dataset size, often $O(M^k)$ for $k$-hop queries. - Graph traversals exploit **index-free adjacency** and are **physical pointer operations** with $O(k)$ cost, where $k$ is the hop count. This makes graph databases far superior for connected-data queries. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **"Derive the complexity comparison between graph traversal and relational JOIN for a 3-hop query. Justify when a graph database should be preferred."** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** **Derivation (5 marks):** **Step 1:** Define the problem — find all friends-of-friends-of-friends of a given user. **Step 2:** Relational schema — `Person(pid, name)`, `Knows(pid1, pid2)`. **Step 3:** Cost of a 3-join query without indexes: $$ C_{RDBMS} = O(\vert Knows \vert^3) = O(M^3) $$ **Step 4:** Graph traversal cost — each hop is a direct pointer lookup: $$ C_{Graph} = 3 \cdot O(1) = O(3) \approx O(1) $$ **Step 5:** Performance ratio: $$ R(3) = \frac{M^3}{3} $$ For $M = 100$ (typical social network), $R(3) = 333,333$. **Justification (2 marks):** A graph database should be preferred when: - The domain has **highly connected data** (social networks, fraud graphs). - Queries involve **3 or more relationship hops**. - The application requires **real-time path queries** (recommendations, fraud detection). - The data model is **evolving** and benefits from schema flexibility. --- ### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **"Describe the step-by-step process of building a Graph Database application. Illustrate with a Movie Recommendation use case."** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Domain Identification (1 mark):** Identify entities: `Person`, `Movie`, `Genre`. Identify connections: `WATCHED`, `RATED`, `FRIENDS_WITH`, `IN_GENRE`, `ACTED_IN`. **Step 2 — Whiteboard Modelling (1 mark):** Sketch the graph. See Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid representation. **Step 3 — Property Identification (1 mark):** - Person: `name`, `age`, `email` - Movie: `title`, `release_year` - RATED relationship: `score`, `rated_at` **Step 4 — Query Mapping (2 marks):** Translate the top business questions into Cypher patterns: - *"Movies my friends rated highly that I have not watched"* — 2-hop pattern: `(:Person)-[:FRIENDS_WITH]->(:Person)-[:RATED]->(:Movie)`. - *"Actors in movies I watched who acted together in other films"* — 3-hop pattern. **Step 5 — Implementation and Tuning (2 marks):** - Add constraints (e.g., unique `Person.email`). - Add indexes (e.g., on `Movie.title`). - Build the application layer with a Cypher driver and a REST API. - Profile slow queries using the Neo4j Browser or the APOC procedures. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **"Compare and contrast: Graph Database vs. Relational Database. Provide three real-world scenarios where graph databases are the better choice."** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Feature | Graph DB | Relational DB | |---|---|---| | Data Model | Nodes + Edges + Properties | Tables + Rows + Columns | | Relationships | First-class, physically stored | Foreign keys, logically resolved | | Schema | Schema-flexible (optional) | Rigid, DDL-defined | | Query Language | Cypher, GQL, SPARQL | SQL | | Traversal Cost | $O(k)$ per hop | $O(M^k)$ for $k$ joins | | Best For | Connected data, paths | Tabular data, aggregations | | ACID Support | Yes (Neo4j) | Yes (all major RDBMS) | **Three Real-World Scenarios (3 marks):** 1. **Social Network Friend Suggestions** — Facebook's TAO uses graph principles; 3+ hop friend-of-friend queries. 2. **Fraud Ring Detection** — Banks model accounts and transactions as graphs; shared identifiers reveal fraud rings in real time. 3. **Knowledge Graphs (Google Search)** — Entities (people, places, events) and their relationships power semantic search. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Loss Areas:** > 1. **Confusing Labels with Properties** — A label is a *category* (e.g., `Person`); a property is a *value* (e.g., `name: 'Rahul'`). Many students interchange these, losing 1–2 marks. > 2. **Omitting direction in relationships** — Always state that relationships are **directed** unless explicitly defined as undirected. Bidirectional traversal is *not* automatic. > 3. **Forgetting Index-Free Adjacency** — When asked *why* graph databases are fast, the answer **must** mention index-free adjacency. Without it, the answer is incomplete. > 4. **Writing Cypher code in non-CS exam slots** — KTU expects conceptual explanation, not raw code dumps, in the theory paper. Use notation and pattern diagrams instead. > 5. **Missing the formal $G = (V, E, P, L)$ representation** — This is a board-favourite 2-mark question and skipping it costs easy marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Revision Bullet Points — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall:** - **Graph Data Model = Nodes + Edges + Properties + Labels**, formally $G = (V, E, P, L)$. - **Nodes** represent **entities**; **Edges** (relationships) represent **connections**; both can have **properties**. - **Relationships are first-class citizens** — stored as physical pointers, not foreign keys. - **Index-Free Adjacency** is the defining performance feature: **$O(1)$ per hop**. - **RDBMS JOIN cost** for a $k$-hop query is **$O(M^k)$**; **Graph traversal cost** is **$O(k)$**. - **Neo4j** is the most widely used **Property Graph DBMS**; query language is **Cypher**. - **Cypher pattern:** `(n:Label {prop: value})-[:REL_TYPE {prop: value}]->(m:Label)`. - **Labels** categorise nodes (e.g., `Person`); **Properties** hold values (e.g., `name: 'Rahul'`). - **Building a graph app = 7 steps:** Identify Domain → Whiteboard → Properties → Query Mapping → Implement → Profile → Deploy. - **Use cases:** Social networks, fraud detection, recommendations, knowledge graphs, network ops, life sciences, supply chain. - **Graph DB preferred when:** Connected data, deep traversals (3+ hops), evolving schema, real-time path queries. - **RDBMS preferred when:** Tabular data, aggregations, OLAP, banking transactions with strict ACID reporting. - **Standard metrics to memorise:** Hop count, degree, density $\rho = 2 \vert E \vert / (\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1))$, traversal complexity. - **ACID compliant:** Neo4j since v2.0 — graph databases are **not** limited to NoSQL consistency models. - **KTU 2024 keyword triggers:** "Connection-oriented", "index-free adjacency", "property graph", "Cypher pattern matching", "schema-flexible". --- > **End of Module 4 Notes — Data Modelling with Graphs: Building a Graph Database Application** > *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech CSE/AI-ML syllabus, and the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4).* 🎓

topics.title: Data Modeling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Modeling

# Data Modeling — Foundational Concepts

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Data Model** is an abstract, formally defined, and computationally tractable collection of conceptual objects and operations that collectively describe the structure, integrity constraints, and manipulative behavior of data within a target application domain. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT795 — Advanced Database Systems), a data model is treated as the *logical blueprint* used to architect, design, and implement a database that is both *semantically faithful* to the real world and *operationally efficient* under the chosen Database Management System (DBMS).

A data model is typically characterized by three orthogonal layers:
1. **Structural Component** — defines the data types, relationships, and constraints.
2. **Integrity Component** — defines the rules that preserve data correctness and consistency.
3. **Manipulative (Operational) Component** — defines the operations (queries, updates) permitted on the data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECDT795, Module 4):**
> Data modeling is the gateway to advanced topics such as **Object-Relational Mapping (ORM)**, **Temporal Databases**, **NoSQL Document Modeling**, and **Graph Data Modeling**. Mastery of ER/EER constructs is *non-negotiable* for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architect's Blueprint"

Imagine you are commissioning an architect to build a hospital. Before laying a single brick, the architect prepares:
- A **Conceptual Drawing** (sketch of wards, OPD, ICU) — this is your **Conceptual Data Model (ER Diagram)**.
- A **Detailed Engineering Plan** with exact room dimensions, plumbing, and electrical layouts — this is your **Logical Data Model (Relational Schema)**.
- The **On-Site Construction Schedule** with material specs and labor workflows — this is your **Physical Data Model (Tables, Indexes, Partitions)**.

> A **Data Model is to a Database what a Blueprint is to a Building.** No engineer builds without a plan; no DBA deploys without a model.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Notation Standards)

| Metric / Standard | Value / Convention |
|---|---|
| **Cardinality Notation (Chen)** | `1 : 1`, `1 : N`, `M : N` |
| **Crow's Foot Convention** | Mandatory side (two bars) vs. Optional side (circle + crow's foot) |
| **Minimum Cardinality (Participation)** | Total (double line) or Partial (single line) |
| **Superclass / Subclass Constraints** | Disjoint (`d`) or Overlapping (`o`) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Crow's Foot notation** is the *de facto* industry standard (used by Oracle, MySQL Workbench, Lucidchart, ER/Studio, and PowerDesigner). Chen notation is preferred in academic KTU theory answers.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Data Models

Data models are historically and structurally classified into three generations:

#### A. **Conceptual / High-Level Data Models**
- Closest to the user's perception of reality.
- Provide powerful semantic constructs — entities, attributes, relationships, generalization, specialization, aggregation.
- **Examples:** **Entity-Relationship (ER) Model**, **Enhanced ER (EER) Model**, **Object-Oriented Model (OOM)**.
- *Not bound to any DBMS implementation.*

#### B. **Logical / Representational Data Models**
- Provide a formal language for describing the schema of the database.
- Balance user-friendliness with implementation tractability.
- **Examples:** **Relational Model** (Codd, 1970), **Network Model** (CODASYL), **Hierarchical Model (IMS)**.

#### C. **Physical / Low-Level Data Models**
- Describe the physical storage, indexing strategies, file organization, and access paths.
- **Examples:** **Unifying Model**, **Storage Specification Model**.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 ESE answers, always *explicitly state the level* (Conceptual / Logical / Physical) you are targeting — examiners award marks for **classification clarity**.

### 2.2 The Entity-Relationship (ER) Model — Core Building Blocks

The ER Model was proposed by **Peter Chen in 1976** and is the cornerstone of conceptual design in the KTU curriculum. Its primitives are:

| Primitive | Symbol (Chen) | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Entity** | Rectangle | A real-world object distinguishable from all others (e.g., *Student*, *Course*). |
| **Weak Entity** | Double rectangle | An entity that cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone. |
| **Attribute** | Oval | A property describing an entity or relationship. |
| **Key Attribute** | Oval with underline | An attribute that uniquely identifies an entity instance. |
| **Multivalued Attribute** | Double oval | Attribute that can hold multiple values (e.g., *PhoneNumbers*). |
| **Derived Attribute** | Dashed oval | Attribute whose value can be computed (e.g., *Age* from *DOB*). |
| **Composite Attribute** | Oval with sub-ovals | Attribute composed of sub-parts (e.g., *Address = Street + City + Pincode*). |
| **Relationship** | Diamond | An association among entities. |
| **Identifying Relationship** | Double diamond | Links a weak entity to its owner entity. |
| **Recursive Relationship** | Diamond looping on same entity | E.g., *Employee supervises Employee*. |

### 2.3 Enhanced ER (EER) Model — Advanced Constructs

The EER Model extends ER with the following **object-oriented** features:

1. **Specialization (Top-Down)** — Decomposing an entity set into sub-classes based on a distinguishing characteristic.
   - Example: *Person* → *Student*, *Faculty*, *Staff*.
2. **Generalization (Bottom-Up)** — Combining multiple entity sets sharing common features into a higher-level superclass.
3. **Inheritance** — Subclass inherits all attributes and relationships of its superclass.
4. **Categorization (Union Type)** — A subclass that is the *union* of multiple superclasses (e.g., *VehicleOwner* is a union of *Person* and *Company*).
5. **Attribute Inheritance** — Total transfer of structural definition from superclass to subclass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EER constraints** examiners love to test:
> - **Disjointness Constraint:** A superclass instance can be a member of **at most one** subclass (denoted `d`) vs. **Overlapping (`o`)** where it can belong to multiple subclasses.
> - **Completeness Constraint:** **Total** (double line) — every superclass must be in at least one subclass; **Partial** (single line) — membership is optional.

### 2.4 Constraints in Data Modeling

Constraints are the *integrity backbone* of any data model.

| Constraint Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Constraint** | Uniqueness of the primary key | `StudentID` is unique. |
| **Domain Constraint** | Restricts attribute values to a valid set | `GPA` ∈ `[0.0, 10.0]`. |
| **Entity Integrity** | Primary key cannot be NULL. | `StudentID ≠ NULL`. |
| **Referential Integrity** | Foreign key must match a primary key in the referenced table. | `DeptID` in *Student* must exist in *Department*. |
| **Semantic / Business Constraint** | Application-level rule | *No faculty can teach more than 4 courses per semester.* |
| **Cardinality / Participation** | Min / Max number of relationship instances | Each `Department` has 1..N `Faculty`. |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Degree of Relationship** | $n =$ number of participating entity sets | Unary = 1, Binary = 2, Ternary = 3 |
| **Cardinality Ratio (1:N)** | One parent, many children | `1 : N` |
| **Cardinality Ratio (M:N)** | Many-to-many | `M : N` |
| **Participation Constraint** | Total ⟺ Existence Dependency | Double line |
| **ER-to-Relational: Strong Entity** | Create one relation $R(E, A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$ | All simple attributes included |
| **ER-to-Relational: Multivalued Attribute** | Create a **separate relation** $R'(PK, A_{mv})$ | $PK$ is the FK referencing $R$ |
| **ER-to-Relational: 1:1 Relationship** | Merge into either entity relation or create a new relation | FK placed in the *total participation* side |
| **ER-to-Relational: 1:N Relationship** | Place FK in the **N-side** relation | No new relation needed |
| **ER-to-Relational: M:N Relationship** | Create a **new relation** $R_{link}(PK_1, PK_2, A_{rel})$ | Composite primary key $(PK_1, PK_2)$ |
| **Weak Entity Mapping** | Owner PK + Partial Key = Composite PK of weak relation | $PK_{weak} = (PK_{owner}, \text{PartialKey})$ |
| **Total Number of Possible Rows in M:N Join** | $\vert R \vert \times \vert S \vert$ (worst case) | Cartesian-like upper bound |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** ER diagrams are used in **SAP**, **Oracle Designer**, **IBM InfoSphere Data Architect**, and **ER/Studio** to reverse-engineer legacy COBOL/IMS systems into modern relational or NoSQL targets — a high-demand skill in enterprise IT modernization projects.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The ER-to-Relational Mapping Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates mastery of the **7-step ER-to-Relational mapping algorithm**. Each step is derived below with explicit justification.

#### **Step 1: Mapping of Regular (Strong) Entity Sets**

For each strong entity set $E$ with simple attributes $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ and primary key $PK$:

$$R_E(\underline{PK}, A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n)$$

**Example:** `Student` entity with attributes `StudentID (PK)`, `Name`, `DOB` becomes:

$$Student(\underline{StudentID}, Name, DOB)$$

#### **Step 2: Mapping of Weak Entity Sets**

For each weak entity $W$ with owner $E$, partial key $A_p$, and identifying relationship $R$:

$$R_W(\underline{PK_E, A_p}, \text{rest of simple attributes})$$

The owner's primary key is **migrated** to form a **composite primary key**.

**Example:** `Dependent` (weak) of `Employee` (owner) with partial key `DepName`:

$$Dependent(\underline{EmployeeID, DepName}, Relation, Age)$$

where `EmployeeID` is both a **Foreign Key** and part of the **Primary Key**.

#### **Step 3: Mapping of Binary 1:1 Relationships**

Three equivalent strategies exist; KTU prefers **Strategy 1** (place FK on the total participation side).

Let $E_1$ and $E_2$ participate in $R$ with $E_2$ having total participation:

$$R_{E_1}(\underline{PK_1}, A_1, \dots, A_n, PK_2 \text{ as FK})$$

#### **Step 4: Mapping of Binary 1:N Relationships**

For $E_1 \xrightarrow{1:N} E_2$, place the FK on the **N-side**:

$$R_{E_2}(\underline{PK_2}, A_1, \dots, A_n, PK_1 \text{ as FK})$$

No new relation is created — referential integrity is maintained via the FK.

#### **Step 5: Mapping of Binary M:N Relationships**

For $E_1 \xrightarrow{M:N} E_2$ with relationship attributes $A_{rel}$:

$$R_{link}(\underline{PK_1, PK_2}, A_{rel_1}, \dots, A_{rel_m})$$

**Example:** `Student` (M) — `EnrolledIn` (N) — `Course` with attribute `Grade`:

$$Enrollment(\underline{StudentID, CourseID}, Grade, Semester)$$

#### **Step 6: Mapping of Multivalued Attributes**

For each multivalued attribute $A_{mv}$ of entity $E$:

$$R_{A_{mv}}(\underline{PK_E, A_{mv}})$$

**Example:** `Student` with multivalued `PhoneNumber`:

$$StudentPhone(\underline{StudentID, PhoneNumber})$$

#### **Step 7: Mapping of N-ary Relationship Sets (Ternary and Above)**

For an $n$-ary relationship $R$ among $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$:

$$R_{n\text{-ary}}(\underline{PK_1, PK_2, \dots, PK_n}, A_{rel})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Marking Insight: If you forget the **double underline** of the composite key in the M:N link relation, you lose **1 mark immediately**. Examiners scan for this.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Library Database

**Original ER Schema:**
- Entities: `Book(ISBN, Title)`, `Member(MemberID, Name)`, `Author(AuthorID, Name)`.
- Relationships: `WrittenBy` (Book M:N Author with attribute `Royalty%`), `BorrowedBy` (Book M:N Member with attributes `IssueDate, ReturnDate`).
- Multivalued: `Book.Genres`.

**Mapped Relational Schema:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Book(\underline{ISBN}, Title) \\
Author(\underline{AuthorID}, Name) \\
Member(\underline{MemberID}, Name) \\
Book\_Genre(\underline{ISBN, GenreName}) \\
WrittenBy(\underline{ISBN, AuthorID}, RoyaltyPercent) \\
BorrowedBy(\underline{ISBN, MemberID}, IssueDate, ReturnDate)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cardinality and Participation Cross-Table

| Relationship Type | Min Participation (Owner) | Max Participation (Owner) | Mapping Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1:1 | Total | Partial | FK on Total side |
| 1:1 | Partial | Partial | New relation |
| 1:N | Total on N-side | — | FK on N-side |
| M:N | — | — | New link relation |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid ER Diagram — Library System (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
erDiagram
    BOOK {
        string ISBN PK
        string Title
    }
    AUTHOR {
        string AuthorID PK
        string Name
    }
    MEMBER {
        string MemberID PK
        string Name
    }
    BOOK ||--o{ WRITTENBY : "M:N"
    AUTHOR ||--o{ WRITTENBY : "M:N"
    BOOK ||--o{ BORROWEDBY : "M:N"
    MEMBER ||--o{ BORROWEDBY : "M:N"
    BOOK ||--o{ BOOKGENRE : "1:N Multivalued"
    WRITTENBY {
        float RoyaltyPercent
    }
    BORROWEDBY {
        date IssueDate
        date ReturnDate
    }
    BOOKGENRE {
        string GenreName
    }
```

### 4.2 Data Modeling Three-Level Architecture (Conceptual → Logical → Physical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Requirements] --> B[Conceptual Model - ER or EER]
    B --> C[Logical Model - Relational Schema]
    C --> D[Physical Model - Tables, Indexes, Partitions]
    D --> E[Actual DBMS - Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL]

    B -.Semantic Verification.-> F[User Review]
    C -.Normalisation BCNF 3NF.-> G[Performance Tuning]
    D -.Indexing Caching.-> H[Query Optimisation]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e8f5e9
    style D fill:#fce4ec
    style E fill:#f3e5f5
```

### 4.3 ER-to-Relational Mapping Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Strong Entity] --> B[Step 2: Weak Entity]
    B --> C[Step 3: 1 to 1 Relationship]
    C --> D[Step 4: 1 to N Relationship]
    D --> E[Step 5: M to N Relationship]
    E --> F[Step 6: Multivalued Attribute]
    F --> G[Step 7: N ary Relationship]
    G --> H[Final Relational Schema]

    style H fill:#c8e6c9
```

### 4.4 EER Generalization/Specialization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Person[Person Superclass] -->|Specialization d| Faculty[Faculty Subclass]
    Person -->|Specialization d| Student[Student Subclass]
    Person --> Attr1[Name DOB Gender]
    Faculty --> Attr2[EmpID Designation Salary]
    Student --> Attr3[RollNo CGPA Semester]
    Faculty -.Inherits.-> Attr1
    Student -.Inherits.-> Attr1

    style Person fill:#bbdefb
    style Faculty fill:#c8e6c9
    style Student fill:#ffe0b2
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a Data Model. List and briefly explain any two categories of data models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A data model is an integrated collection of concepts for describing data, relationships between data, and constraints on the data, used to provide an abstract description of a real-world domain.
- **Category 1 — Conceptual / High-Level (1 mark):** Provides close-to-perception primitives like entities, attributes, relationships. Example: ER Model, EER Model.
- **Category 2 — Logical / Representational (1 mark):** Provides a formal schema description between user view and physical storage. Example: Relational Model, Network Model.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Strong Entity and a Weak Entity with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Strong Entity (1.5 marks):** A strong entity has a primary key formed from its own attributes and can exist independently. *Example: Employee(EmpID, Name, Salary).*
- **Weak Entity (1.5 marks):** A weak entity cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes and depends on a strong (owner) entity via an identifying relationship. *Example: Dependent(DepName, Age) depends on Employee(EmpID).*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]`

**a)** Design a complete **ER Diagram** for a University Examination System with the following requirements. Clearly identify entities, attributes (including key, multivalued, and derived), and relationships with cardinality and participation constraints. **(7 marks)**

**Requirements:**
- *Student* is identified by `RollNo`, has `Name`, `DOB`, and a derived attribute `Age`.
- *Course* is identified by `CourseCode`, has `CourseName`, `Credits`, and a multivalued attribute `PrerequisiteCourses`.
- *Faculty* is identified by `FacultyID`, has `Name`, `Designation`, and `Department`.
- A *Student* can *Enroll* in many *Courses* and each *Course* can have many *Students* — relationship attribute `Semester`, `Grade`.
- A *Faculty* *Teaches* many *Courses*; each *Course* is taught by exactly one *Faculty* (1:N).
- A *Department* *Offers* many *Courses*; each *Course* belongs to one *Department* (1:N).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Entities:** `Student`, `Course`, `Faculty`, `Department`.

**Step 2 — Identify Attributes:**

| Entity | Attributes |
|---|---|
| Student | `RollNo` (Key), `Name`, `DOB`, `Age` (Derived) |
| Course | `CourseCode` (Key), `CourseName`, `Credits`, `PrerequisiteCourses` (Multivalued) |
| Faculty | `FacultyID` (Key), `Name`, `Designation` |
| Department | `DeptID` (Key), `DeptName` |

**Step 3 — Identify Relationships and Cardinality:**

| Relationship | Entities | Cardinality | Attributes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Enrolls | Student — Course | M:N | Semester, Grade |
| Teaches | Faculty — Course | 1:N | — |
| Offers | Department — Course | 1:N | — |

**Step 4 — Convert to Relational Schema (full mapping):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Student(\underline{RollNo}, Name, DOB) \\
Course(\underline{CourseCode}, CourseName, Credits, DeptID_{FK}) \\
Faculty(\underline{FacultyID}, Name, Designation, DeptID_{FK}) \\
Department(\underline{DeptID}, DeptName) \\
PrereqCourse(\underline{CourseCode, PrerequisiteCode}) \\
Enrolls(\underline{RollNo, CourseCode}, Semester, Grade) \\
Teaches(\underline{CourseCode}, FacultyID_{FK})
\end{aligned}
$$

> (Note: `Age` is *derived* and **not stored** as a column.)

---

**b)** Explain the concept of **Generalization and Specialization** in the EER Model with a suitable example. Discuss **Disjoint** and **Overlapping** constraints. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Generalization (3.5 marks):**
Generalization is a *bottom-up* process in which multiple entity sets that share common features are unified into a single higher-level superclass. The shared attributes are pulled up to the superclass, while the distinguishing attributes remain in the subclasses. *Example: Car, Truck, Motorcycle → generalized into MotorVehicle (with common attribute LicenseNo, Year).*

**Specialization (3.5 marks):**
Specialization is the *top-down* reverse process where an entity set is refined into multiple sub-classes based on a distinguishing characteristic. The superclass entity becomes a member of one or more specialized subclasses. *Example: Person → specialized into Student, Faculty, Staff. The superclass `Person` retains `Name, DOB`; each subclass has its own specific attributes (e.g., Faculty has Designation, Student has RollNo).*

**Disjoint (`d`) vs. Overlapping (`o`):**
- **Disjoint Constraint:** A superclass instance can belong to **at most one** subclass. *Example: A `Person` cannot be both `Student` and `Faculty` simultaneously under disjoint rule.*
- **Overlapping Constraint:** A superclass instance may belong to **two or more** subclasses. *Example: A `Person` can simultaneously be both an `Employee` and an `Alumni`.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]`

**a)** Consider a **Hospital Management System**. The schema includes:
- `Patient(PatientID, Name, Age, Gender)`,
- `Doctor(DoctorID, Name, Specialty, Salary)`,
- `Room(RoomNo, Type, ChargesPerDay)`,
- `Treatment(TreatmentID, Description, Cost)`.
- A `Patient` is *Admitted* to a `Room` (1:N, with `AdmissionDate`, `DischargeDate`).
- A `Doctor` *Treats* a `Patient` (M:N, with `TreatmentID`, `TreatmentDate`).
- A `Doctor` *Recommends* a `Treatment` for a `Patient` (ternary relationship with attribute `Dosage`, `Frequency`).

**(i)** Draw the **ER Diagram** with proper notation. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Map the ER schema to a **Relational Schema**, showing all 7 steps explicitly. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) ER Diagram (Mermaid representation):**

```mermaid
erDiagram
    PATIENT {
        string PatientID PK
        string Name
        int Age
        string Gender
    }
    DOCTOR {
        string DoctorID PK
        string Name
        string Specialty
        float Salary
    }
    ROOM {
        string RoomNo PK
        string Type
        float ChargesPerDay
    }
    TREATMENT {
        string TreatmentID PK
        string Description
        float Cost
    }
    PATIENT ||--o{ ADMITS : "1:N"
    ROOM ||--o{ ADMITS : "1:N"
    DOCTOR ||--o{ TREATS : "M:N"
    PATIENT ||--o{ TREATS : "M:N"
    DOCTOR ||--o{ RECOMMENDS : "Ternary"
    PATIENT ||--o{ RECOMMENDS : "Ternary"
    TREATMENT ||--o{ RECOMMENDS : "Ternary"
    ADMITS {
        date AdmissionDate
        date DischargeDate
    }
    TREATS {
        date TreatmentDate
    }
    RECOMMENDS {
        string Dosage
        string Frequency
    }
```

**(ii) Relational Mapping:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Patient(\underline{PatientID}, Name, Age, Gender) \\
Doctor(\underline{DoctorID}, Name, Specialty, Salary) \\
Room(\underline{RoomNo}, Type, ChargesPerDay) \\
Treatment(\underline{TreatmentID}, Description, Cost) \\
Admits(\underline{PatientID, AdmissionDate}, RoomNo_{FK}, DischargeDate) \\
Treats(\underline{DoctorID, PatientID, TreatmentDate}, TreatmentID_{FK}) \\
Recommends(\underline{DoctorID, PatientID, TreatmentID}, Dosage, Frequency)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**b)** Explain the **Object-Relational Data Model** and discuss its significance in modern enterprise applications. Compare it with the **Pure Object-Oriented Model**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Object-Relational Data Model (ORM) — 3.5 marks:**
The Object-Relational Model is a hybrid paradigm that extends the traditional relational model with object-oriented features such as user-defined types (UDTs), methods, encapsulation, inheritance, and complex nested attributes. It is supported by modern DBMSs like **PostgreSQL**, **Oracle**, **IBM DB2**, and **SQL Server** (via `TABLE` types, `REF`, and `METHOD` constructs). It bridges the impedance mismatch between object-oriented application code (Java, Python, C++) and tabular relational storage.

**Key Features:** `ROW TYPE`, `ARRAY` types, `MULTISET`, `REF` types for references, user-defined functions, and inheritance of tables.

**Pure Object-Oriented Model — 2 marks:**
In contrast, the pure OO model (e.g., **ObjectDB**, **db4o**, **ObjectStore**) treats *every* entity as a full-fledged object with identity, state, and behavior. The database itself is a persistent object store with no relational tables. It supports polymorphism, encapsulation, and message-passing natively. It is suitable for CAD, GIS, and AI applications but suffers from lack of standardization and weaker query optimization.

**Comparison Table (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | Object-Relational | Pure Object-Oriented |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | Tables + Objects | Objects only |
| Query Language | Extended SQL | OQL, Native methods |
| Performance | High (mature optimizers) | Moderate |
| Standardization | SQL:1999, SQL:2003 | No universal standard |
| Use Case | Enterprise ERP, E-commerce | CAD, Multimedia, AI |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Data Modeling Answers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the double-line notation for total participation** — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Placing FK on the wrong side of a 1:N relationship** — costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting multivalued attributes in the relational mapping** (Step 6 is most often skipped) — costs 1.5 marks.
> 4. **Using single underline instead of double underline** for composite primary keys in M:N link relations.
> 5. **Confusing disjoint (`d`) with total participation** — they are *orthogonal* constraints.
> 6. **Storing derived attributes** (like Age) as regular columns — examiners deduct 0.5 mark.
> 7. **Not specifying the cardinality ratio explicitly** in ER diagrams.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Data Model** = Structure + Integrity + Manipulation.
- ✅ **Three Levels:** Conceptual (ER/EER) → Logical (Relational) → Physical.
- ✅ **ER Primitives:** Entity (rect), Attribute (oval), Relationship (diamond).
- ✅ **Weak Entities** have double rectangles and rely on an owner entity for identification.
- ✅ **EER adds:** Specialization, Generalization, Inheritance, Categorization, Attribute Inheritance.
- ✅ **Disjoint (`d`)** = `XOR`; **Overlapping (`o`)** = `OR`.
- ✅ **1:1** → FK on total side; **1:N** → FK on N-side; **M:N** → new link relation.
- ✅ **Multivalued attribute** → ALWAYS a separate relation.
- ✅ **Weak entity** → Composite PK = (Owner PK + Partial Key).
- ✅ **Derived attribute** → NEVER stored; computed on demand.
- ✅ **N-ary (n ≥ 3) relationships** → New relation with all participating PKs.
- ✅ **Crow's Foot** is industry standard; **Chen** is academic standard.
- ✅ **Object-Relational Model** is the modern enterprise compromise between relational and OO.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always begin any ER design answer with a **table of entities and their attributes**, followed by a **table of relationships with cardinalities**, *before* drawing the diagram. This structured approach earns 1–2 extra marks for "design clarity" under the KTU 2024 evaluation rubric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

topics.title: Predictive Analysis with Graph Theory: Depth and Breadth First Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Predictive Analysis with Graph Theory: Depth and Breadth First Search --- # Module 4 — Predictive Analysis with Graph Theory: DFS & BFS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Graph Theory Foundations for Predictive Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Formal):** > A **Graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a non-linear data structure consisting of a finite, non-empty set of vertices $V$ (also called **nodes**) and a set of edges $E$ that connect pairs of vertices. In the context of *Advanced Database Systems* and *Predictive Analysis*, graphs model **entities and their relationships** — enabling traversal-based reasoning over connected datasets. Formally, a graph is defined by the tuple: $$G = (V, E) \quad \text{where} \quad V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots, v_n\}, \quad E \subseteq \{(v_i, v_j) \mid v_i, v_j \in V\}$$ The **order** of the graph is $|V| = n$, and the **size** of the graph is $|E| = m$. ### 1.2 Types of Graphs Relevant to KTU Syllabus | Graph Type | Definition | DBMS / Predictive Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Undirected Graph** | Edges have no direction: $(v_i, v_j) = (v_j, v_i)$ | Social network "friend-of" relationships | | **Directed Graph (Digraph)** | Edges are ordered pairs $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle$ | Foreign-key dependencies, transaction precedence | | **Weighted Graph** | Each edge $e$ carries a weight $w(e) \in \mathbb{R}$ | Network latency, cost-based query plans | | **Cyclic Graph** | Contains at least one cycle | Deadlock-prone transaction schedules | | **Acyclic Graph (DAG)** | No cycles exist | Join operator trees, query evaluation plans | | **Connected Graph** | A path exists between every pair of vertices | Reachable component analysis | > [!NOTE] > **Cycle (KTU Terminology):** A closed walk where the start vertex equals the end vertex and no edge is repeated. > **Path:** A sequence of vertices $v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_k$ such that each consecutive pair is joined by an edge, with no vertex repeated. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Graphs as "City Maps" > [!IMPORTANT] > **The City Map Analogy (Intuition):** > *Imagine a city where every **intersection** is a vertex and every **road** is an edge. **Predictive Analysis** asks: "Starting from my home (source), what is the fastest way to reach the hospital?"* > > - **Breadth First Search (BFS)** is like *ripples spreading on water* — you first visit all roads one block away, then two blocks away, then three. It naturally finds the **shortest path** in road-count terms. > - **Depth First Search (DFS)** is like *always taking the deepest unexplored alley* — you walk as far as you can, and only when you hit a dead-end do you backtrack and try the next unexplored road. It is excellent for **exploring structure** (detecting cycles, finding connected components, topological ordering). In a **database context**, the "city" is your relational schema, social graph, or knowledge graph, and the "roads" are foreign keys, follow-edges, or dependency links. Predicting reachability, influence propagation, or dependency chains is *graph traversal at scale*. ### 1.4 Why Graphs Matter in Predictive Analysis > [!NOTE] > Predictive analytics is increasingly **graph-native**. The 2024 KTU syllabus explicitly couples graph theory with prediction because: > 1. **Heterogeneous relationships** in modern data (social, transactional, semantic) are naturally graphs. > 2. **Traversal algorithms (DFS/BFS)** form the computational backbone of link prediction, community detection, fraud rings, recommendation, and knowledge-graph reasoning. > 3. Graph databases (Neo4j, TigerGraph, Amazon Neptune) are now mainstream in industry — KTU expects graduates to master these primitives. ### 1.5 Core Metric — Graph Density A key predictive indicator is **density** $\rho$: $$\rho = \frac{2 \cdot |E|}{|V| \cdot (|V| - 1)} \quad \text{(undirected)}$$ $$\rho = \frac{|E|}{|V| \cdot (|V| - 1)} \quad \text{(directed)}$$ - $\rho = 0 \Rightarrow$ **empty graph** (no predictive signal) - $\rho = 1 \Rightarrow$ **complete graph** $K_{|V|}$ (every entity relates to every other) - Real social graphs typically satisfy $\rho \ll 1$ (sparse, but highly clustered) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Graph Representations (Storage Primitives) Predictive algorithms operate on **adjacency structures**, never on the abstract graph itself. **A. Adjacency Matrix** — A 2D Boolean / integer matrix $A$ of size $n \times n$: $$A[i][j] = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (v_i, v_j) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **B. Adjacency List** — For each vertex $v_i$, store a linked list `Adj[$v_i$]` of all $v_j$ such that $(v_i, v_j) \in E$. **C. Incidence Matrix** — Rows = vertices, Columns = edges; entry = 1 if vertex incident to edge. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symmetry property of undirected graphs:** $A[i][j] = A[j][i]$, hence $A$ is symmetric and the matrix is diagonally redundant. For *predictive load analysis*, this lets you compute degree in $O(1)$: $\deg(v_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A[i][j]$. ### 2.2 Depth First Search (DFS) — Operational Theory **Definition (KTU):** DFS is a recursive (or stack-based) traversal algorithm that visits a vertex, marks it as visited, then **recursively** explores each unvisited neighbour as deeply as possible before backtracking. **Formal Pre/Post-condition:** - **Pre-condition:** Graph $G$ (any type), source vertex $s \in V$. - **Post-condition:** All vertices reachable from $s$ are visited; a **DFS spanning tree/forest** is produced. **DFS State Machine** — Each vertex $v$ has exactly one of these labels: | State | Meaning | Set Notation | |---|---|---| | **WHITE** | Undiscovered | Initial state of all vertices | | **GRAY** | Discovered, on the recursion stack | Currently being processed | | **BLACK** | Fully explored (all descendants done) | Search has finished at $v$ | **Discovery and Finish Times** — Critical for predictive analysis: $$d[v] = \text{time } v \text{ is first discovered (WHITE} \to \text{GRAY)}$$ $$f[v] = \text{time when DFS finishes } v \text{ (GRAY} \to \text{BLACK)}$$ A global counter `time` starts at 0 and increments at each event. The invariant: $$\forall\, v \in V : \quad d[v] < f[v]$$ And for any edge in the DFS tree, the **parent's interval strictly contains** the child's: $$d[\text{parent}] < d[\text{child}] < f[\text{child}] < f[\text{parent}]$$ ### 2.3 Breadth First Search (BFS) — Operational Theory **Definition (KTU):** BFS is a level-order traversal that uses a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue**. It explores all vertices at distance $k$ from the source before any vertex at distance $k+1$. **Distance Function:** $$\delta(s, v) = \text{minimum number of edges in any path from } s \text{ to } v$$ BFS computes $\delta(s, v)$ **exactly** for unweighted graphs. KTU emphasizes this as the foundation of **shortest-path prediction** on unweighted networks. **BFS Levels (predictive horizon analogy):** $$L_0 = \{s\}, \quad L_{i+1} = \bigcup_{u \in L_i} \text{Neighbours}(u) \setminus \bigcup_{j=0}^{i} L_j$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Complexity | Notation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Order / Size | $n = \vert V \vert, \; m = \vert E \vert$ | Cardinality | | 2 | Handshaking Lemma | $\displaystyle\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2m$ | Sum of degrees | | 3 | DFS Time Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — adjacency list | Linear in graph size | | 4 | DFS Time Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ — adjacency matrix | Quadratic | | 5 | BFS Time Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — adjacency list | Linear | | 6 | BFS Time Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ — adjacency matrix | Quadratic | | 7 | DFS Space Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ recursion stack | Worst case | | 8 | BFS Space Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ queue | Worst case | | 9 | Graph Density (undirected) | $\rho = \dfrac{2m}{n(n-1)}$ | $0 \le \rho \le 1$ | | 10 | Max edges (undirected) | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Complete graph | | 11 | Max edges (directed) | $n(n-1)$ | Complete digraph | | 12 | BFS Shortest Path | $\delta(s,v) = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}(s,v)} \vert P \vert - 1$ | Path length | | 13 | Parent in BFS Tree | $v.\pi = u$ where $u$ discovered $v$ | Back-pointer | | 14 | DFS Bracket Theorem | $d[u] < d[v] < f[v] < f[u]$ for tree edge $(u,v)$ | Parenthesis structure | | 15 | Cycle Test (undirected) | DFS finds a **back edge** $\Rightarrow$ cycle | Tree edge vs back edge | > [!NOTE] > **Why $O(V + E)$ matters for predictive analysis:** Real-world graphs (social, web, transaction) are *sparse* with $m \ll n^2$. DFS/BFS therefore scale **near-linearly** — enabling real-time prediction on billion-edge graphs. ### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Real-World DFS/BFS Applications | Domain | Algorithm | Predictive Use | |---|---|---| | **Social Network Analytics** | BFS | Influence propagation, "k-hop" reachability | | **Fraud Detection** | DFS | Cycle detection in transaction graphs (money-laundering rings) | | **Database Query Optimizer** | Topological Sort (DFS-based) | Validating join-order DAGs | | **Deadlock Detection** | DFS / cycle detection | Wait-for graphs in DBMS lock manager | | **Recommendation Systems** | BFS | Item-similarity neighbourhood search | | **Knowledge Graphs** | Bounded BFS | Predicting missing links within $k$ hops | | **Compiler / SQL Parsing** | DFS | Parse-tree traversal, recursive query evaluation | | **Web Crawlers** | BFS | Level-wise URL discovery | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — DFS Trace on a Directed Graph **Given directed graph $G$** with $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ and edges: $$E = \{(1,2),\,(1,3),\,(2,4),\,(2,5),\,(3,5),\,(4,6),\,(5,4),\,(5,6),\,(6,-\}\}$$ Step 1 — Initialize all vertices **WHITE**, `time = 0`, predecessor `π[v] = NIL`. Step 2 — Begin DFS at vertex 1. - Mark 1 → **GRAY**. $d[1] = 1$ (time increments). - Visit adjacency of 1: $\{2, 3\}$. Take 2. Step 3 — Visit vertex 2. - Mark 2 → GRAY. $d[2] = 2$. Set $\pi[2] = 1$. - Visit adjacency of 2: $\{4, 5\}$. Take 4. Step 4 — Visit vertex 4. - Mark 4 → GRAY. $d[4] = 3$. Set $\pi[4] = 2$. - Visit adjacency of 4: $\{6\}$. Take 6. Step 5 — Visit vertex 6. - Mark 6 → GRAY. $d[6] = 4$. Set $\pi[6] = 4$. - Visit adjacency of 6: $\{\}$ (empty). **Backtrack.** - Mark 6 → BLACK. $f[6] = 5$. Step 6 — Back to 4. No more unvisited neighbours. Mark 4 → BLACK. $f[4] = 6$. Step 7 — Back to 2. Next unvisited neighbour is 5. Visit 5. - Mark 5 → GRAY. $d[5] = 7$. Set $\pi[5] = 2$. - Visit adjacency of 5: $\{4, 6\}$. Both already BLACK. **Skip (cross / forward edges).** - Mark 5 → BLACK. $f[5] = 8$. Step 8 — Back to 2. No more neighbours. Mark 2 → BLACK. $f[2] = 9$. Step 9 — Back to 1. Next unvisited neighbour is 3. Visit 3. - Mark 3 → GRAY. $d[3] = 10$. Set $\pi[3] = 1$. - Adjacency of 3: $\{5\}$ (BLACK). Skip. - Mark 3 → BLACK. $f[3] = 11$. Step 10 — Back to 1. No more neighbours. Mark 1 → BLACK. $f[1] = 12$. **Completed DFS Trace Table:** | Vertex | $d[v]$ | $f[v]$ | $\pi[v]$ | Colour (final) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 1 | 12 | NIL | BLACK | | 2 | 2 | 9 | 1 | BLACK | | 3 | 10 | 11 | 1 | BLACK | | 4 | 3 | 6 | 2 | BLACK | | 5 | 7 | 8 | 2 | BLACK | | 6 | 4 | 5 | 4 | BLACK | **DFS Tree (forest) — back-pointers $\pi$:** ``` 1 / \ 2 3 / \ 4 5 | 6 ``` **Classification of edges:** | Edge | Type | Reason | |---|---|---| | (1,2), (1,3), (2,4), (2,5), (4,6) | **Tree edges** | Drove the recursion | | (5,4), (5,6) | **Cross / forward edges** | Neighbour already BLACK | | (3,5) | **Cross edge** | Neighbour already BLACK | > [!NOTE] > **Edge classification theorem:** If $d[u] < d[v]$, edge $(u,v)$ is a **tree/forward** edge; if $d[v] < d[u] < f[u]$, it is a **back edge** (implies a cycle in directed graph); otherwise, a **cross edge**. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — BFS Trace on the Same Graph **Source $s = 1$**, FIFO queue $Q$, `time` not needed for BFS, but we record `level[v]`. Step 1 — Initialize: all WHITE. $d[1] = 0$, `level[1] = 0`. Enqueue 1. $$Q = [1]$$ Step 2 — Dequeue 1. Visit neighbours $\{2, 3\}$. - 2: WHITE → GRAY, $d[2] = 1$, $\pi[2] = 1$, enqueue. - 3: WHITE → GRAY, $d[3] = 1$, $\pi[3] = 1$, enqueue. $$Q = [2, 3]$$ Step 3 — Dequeue 2. Neighbours $\{4, 5\}$. - 4: WHITE → GRAY, $d[4] = 2$, $\pi[4] = 2$, enqueue. - 5: WHITE → GRAY, $d[5] = 2$, $\pi[5] = 2$, enqueue. $$Q = [3, 4, 5]$$ Step 4 — Dequeue 3. Neighbours $\{5\}$ — already GRAY, skip. $$Q = [4, 5]$$ Step 5 — Dequeue 4. Neighbours $\{6\}$. - 6: WHITE → GRAY, $d[6] = 3$, $\pi[6] = 4$, enqueue. $$Q = [5, 6]$$ Step 6 — Dequeue 5. Neighbours $\{4, 6\}$ — both GRAY, skip. $$Q = [6]$$ Step 7 — Dequeue 6. Neighbours $\{\}$. Mark BLACK. $$Q = [\ ]$$ **BFS Result Table:** | Vertex | $d[v]$ | Level | $\pi[v]$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 0 | 0 | NIL | | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | | 4 | 2 | 2 | 2 | | 5 | 2 | 2 | 2 | | 6 | 3 | 3 | 4 | **BFS Tree (shortest-path tree from 1):** ``` 1 / \ 2 3 / \ 4 5 | 6 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key observation:** BFS tree and DFS tree are *identical* here because the graph is acyclic (DAG). In cyclic or denser graphs they diverge — BFS minimizes edge-count, DFS minimizes recursion depth. ### 3.3 Derivation — Why BFS Finds the Shortest Path **Claim:** When BFS terminates, $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ for every reachable $v$. **Proof sketch (KTU-style induction):** - *Base:* $d[s] = 0 = \delta(s, s)$. ✓ - *Inductive hypothesis:* Assume for all $u$ dequeued before $v$, $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$. - *Inductive step:* When $v$ is dequeued, $v$ was enqueued from some predecessor $u$ with $d[v] = d[u] + 1$. So a path of length $d[v]$ exists, giving $\delta(s, v) \le d[v]$. - BFS only enqueues $v$ the **first time** it is discovered (from the *closest* unvisited predecessor), so $d[v] \le \delta(s, v)$. - Combining: $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DFS vs BFS | Criterion | DFS | BFS | |---|---|---| | **Data structure** | Stack (recursion) | Queue (FIFO) | | **Order of exploration** | Depth-first (deepest first) | Breadth-first (level by level) | | **Shortest path (unweighted)** | ❌ Not guaranteed | ✅ Guaranteed | | **Cycle detection** | ✅ Via back edges | ✅ Via visited-on-discovered check | | **Memory (deep graph)** | $O(\text{depth})$ — can blow recursion | $O(\text{width})$ — can blow queue | | **Memory (wide graph)** | $O(\text{depth})$ — efficient | $O(\text{width})$ — expensive | | **Connectivity check** | ✅ One DFS per component | ✅ One BFS per component | | **Topological sort** | ✅ Reverse finish-time order | ❌ Not natural | | **Strongly connected components** | ✅ Tarjan/Kosaraju (DFS) | ❌ Not natural | | **Bipartite test** | ✅ 2-colour DFS | ✅ 2-colour BFS | | **Maze / puzzle solving** | ✅ Excellent (backtrack) | ⚠️ Possible but memory-heavy | | **Web crawler** | ⚠️ Trap-prone | ✅ Standard | | **Predictive link prediction** | ⚠️ For substructure | ✅ Best for $k$-hop | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "BFS = shortest path" with "DFS = shortest path." **BFS guarantees shortest path only in unweighted graphs.** For weighted graphs, BFS fails — Dijkstra's or Bellman-Ford is required. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Graph Traversal Pipeline for Prediction ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Raw Data Sources
SQL Joins, Logs, Social Edges"] --> B["Graph Constructor
Build V and E"] B --> C["Graph Storage
Adjacency List or Matrix"] C --> D{"Predictive
Query Type"} D -- "Shortest path /
k-hop reachability" --> E["BFS Engine
FIFO Queue"] D -- "Structure /
cycles / SCC" --> F["DFS Engine
Recursion Stack"] D -- "Ordering /
dependencies" --> G["Topological Sort
DFS-Based"] E --> H["Prediction Output
Distance, Path, Influence Score"] F --> H G --> H H --> I["Downstream Consumer
Dashboard, Recommendation,
Alert System"] ``` ### 4.2 DFS Call Stack — Recursive Flow on a Sample Graph ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Start DFS at vertex 1"] --> A["Visit 1
d 1 equals 1"] A --> B["Visit 2
d 2 equals 2"] B --> C["Visit 4
d 4 equals 3"] C --> D["Visit 6
d 6 equals 4"] D --> E["6 has no
unvisited neighbours"] E --> F["Finish 6
f 6 equals 5"] F --> G["Backtrack to 4"] G --> H["4 has no more
unvisited neighbours"] H --> I["Finish 4
f 4 equals 6"] I --> J["Backtrack to 2
Visit next 5"] J --> K["Visit 5
d 5 equals 7"] K --> L["5 neighbours
already BLACK"] L --> M["Finish 5
f 5 equals 8"] M --> N["Backtrack to 2
Finish 2 f 2 equals 9"] N --> O["Backtrack to 1
Visit 3"] O --> P["Visit 3
d 3 equals 10"] P --> Q["3 neighbour 5
already BLACK"] Q --> R["Finish 3
f 3 equals 11"] R --> T["Backtrack to 1
Finish 1 f 1 equals 12"] T --> END["DFS Complete"] ``` ### 4.3 BFS Queue Evolution — Level-Order Expansion ```mermaid flowchart LR L0["Level 0
1"] --> L1["Level 1
2 3"] L1 --> L2["Level 2
4 5"] L2 --> L3["Level 3
6"] L0 -- "Enqueue 1" --> Q1["Q equals 1"] Q1 -- "Dequeue 1
Enqueue 2,3" --> Q2["Q equals 2 3"] Q2 -- "Dequeue 2
Enqueue 4,5" --> Q3["Q equals 3 4 5"] Q3 -- "Dequeue 3
Skip 5" --> Q4["Q equals 4 5"] Q4 -- "Dequeue 4
Enqueue 6" --> Q5["Q equals 5 6"] Q5 -- "Dequeue 5
Skip" --> Q6["Q equals 6"] Q6 -- "Dequeue 6" --> Q7["Q equals empty"] ``` ### 4.4 DFS vs BFS — Comparative Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD Root["Source vertex S"] --> A["A"] Root --> B["B"] A --> A1["A1 deep dive first"] A1 --> A2["A2 deep dive first"] A2 --> A3["A3 deep dive first"] A3 --> Aback["Backtrack only when stuck"] Aback --> C["C next sibling"] B --> D["D next sibling"] C --> E["E next sibling"] D --> F["F next sibling"] E --> G["G next sibling"] F --> H["H next sibling"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Q1.a]** **Q1. (3 Marks)** *Define a graph data structure. Differentiate between a directed and an undirected graph with a suitable example from a database context.* **Model Answer:** A graph $G = (V, E)$ is a non-linear data structure consisting of a set of vertices $V$ and a set of edges $E$ that connect pairs of vertices. | Aspect | Directed Graph | Undirected Graph | |---|---|---| | Edge definition | Ordered pair $\langle u, v \rangle$ | Unordered pair $(u, v)$ | | Symmetry | $(u, v) \not\equiv (v, u)$ | $(u, v) \equiv (v, u)$ | | In-degree / Out-degree | Distinct concepts | Degree only | | **DBMS Example** | Foreign-key dependency: `Order → Customer` | "Friends-of" relation in a social DB | **Marks distribution:** Definition (1), Table comparison (1), DBMS example (1). --- **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q2.b]** **Q2. (3 Marks)** *State and explain the time complexity of BFS and DFS algorithms. Under what condition is BFS guaranteed to find the shortest path?* **Model Answer:** - Both BFS and DFS have complexity $O(|V| + |E|)$ when the graph is represented as an **adjacency list**. - With an **adjacency matrix**, both degrade to $O(|V|^2)$ because every vertex lookup scans $n$ entries. - BFS finds the **shortest path in terms of edge count** if and only if the graph is **unweighted** (all edges have unit weight). - For weighted graphs, BFS does **not** give shortest path — Dijkstra's algorithm is required. **Marks distribution:** Two complexities (1), BFS condition (1), Dijkstra mention (1 extra → award 3/3). --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Q9 Module 4]** ### Question A — (14 Marks) **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Perform a **Depth First Search (DFS)** on the following directed graph starting from vertex **A**. Clearly show the discovery time $d[v]$ and finish time $f[v]$ for each vertex. Identify the DFS tree edges.
