Vertices: $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ Edges: $\{(A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (B,E), (C,E), (D,F), (E,F)\}$ **(b)** *(7 Marks)* Using the same graph, perform **Breadth First Search (BFS)** from vertex **A** and construct the BFS tree. State the BFS level of each vertex and verify whether BFS gives the shortest path. --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — DFS Trace **Step 1:** Start at A. Mark A → GRAY. `time = 1`, so $d[A] = 1$. **Step 2:** Visit first neighbour of A → B. Mark B → GRAY. $d[B] = 2$. Set $\pi[B] = A$. **Step 3:** Visit first neighbour of B → D. Mark D → GRAY. $d[D] = 3$. Set $\pi[D] = B$. **Step 4:** Visit first neighbour of D → F. Mark F → GRAY. $d[F] = 4$. Set $\pi[F] = D$. **Step 5:** F has no neighbours. Mark F → BLACK. $f[F] = 5$. **Step 6:** Back to D. No more neighbours. Mark D → BLACK. $f[D] = 6$. **Step 7:** Back to B. Next neighbour is E. Visit E. $d[E] = 7$, $\pi[E] = B$, mark GRAY. **Step 8:** E's neighbour F already BLACK. Skip. Mark E → BLACK. $f[E] = 8$. **Step 9:** Back to B. No more. Mark B → BLACK. $f[B] = 9$. **Step 10:** Back to A. Next neighbour is C. Visit C. $d[C] = 10$, $\pi[C] = A$, GRAY. **Step 11:** C's neighbour E is BLACK. Skip. Mark C → BLACK. $f[C] = 11$. **Step 12:** Back to A. No more. Mark A → BLACK. $f[A] = 12$. **DFS Result Table:** | Vertex | $d[v]$ | $f[v]$ | $\pi[v]$ | Tree Edge | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | 1 | 12 | NIL | — | | B | 2 | 9 | A | A → B | | C | 10 | 11 | A | A → C | | D | 3 | 6 | B | B → D | | E | 7 | 8 | B | B → E | | F | 4 | 5 | D | D → F | **DFS Tree:** ``` A / \ B C / \ D E | F ``` **Marks awarded (Part a — 7):** Correct initialization (1), Per-vertex $d$/$f$ values (3), Tree edges identified (2), Tree diagram (1). --- #### Part (b) — BFS Trace **Step 1:** Initialize. $d[A] = 0$, level[A] = 0. Enqueue A. $$Q = [A]$$ **Step 2:** Dequeue A. Neighbours $\{B, C\}$. - B: WHITE → GRAY, $d[B] = 1$, level = 1, $\pi[B] = A$, enqueue. - C: WHITE → GRAY, $d[C] = 1$, level = 1, $\pi[C] = A$, enqueue. $$Q = [B, C]$$ **Step 3:** Dequeue B. Neighbours $\{D, E\}$. - D: WHITE → GRAY, $d[D] = 2$, level = 2, $\pi[D] = B$, enqueue. - E: WHITE → GRAY, $d[E] = 2$, level = 2, $\pi[E] = B$, enqueue. $$Q = [C, D, E]$$ **Step 4:** Dequeue C. Neighbour $\{E\}$ — GRAY, skip. $$Q = [D, E]$$ **Step 5:** Dequeue D. Neighbour $\{F\}$. - F: WHITE → GRAY, $d[F] = 3$, level = 3, $\pi[F] = D$, enqueue. $$Q = [E, F]$$ **Step 6:** Dequeue E. Neighbour $\{F\}$ — GRAY, skip. $$Q = [F]$$ **Step 7:** Dequeue F. No neighbours. $$Q = [\ ]$$ **BFS Table:** | Vertex | $d[v]$ | Level | $\pi[v]$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | 0 | 0 | NIL | | B | 1 | 1 | A | | C | 1 | 1 | A | | D | 2 | 2 | B | | E | 2 | 2 | B | | F | 3 | 3 | D | **BFS Tree:** ``` A / \ B C / \ D E | F ``` **Shortest Path Verification:** - Path A → F via BFS tree: A → B → D → F has length 3 edges. - Direct alternative A → ... → F: the only other route A → C → E → F also has 3 edges. - Since the graph is unweighted, BFS gives the **shortest path** (in edge count). ✓ **Marks awarded (Part b — 7):** Queue evolution table (3), BFS table (2), Tree diagram (1), Shortest-path justification (1). --- ### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Question **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **adjacency matrix** and **adjacency list** representations of a graph. For each, derive the time complexity of DFS. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each representation when applied to a graph with 10 million edges but only 50,000 vertices (typical social-network scale). **(b)** *(7 Marks)* Explain how **DFS is used for cycle detection** in a directed graph. Describe the back-edge theorem. Given the graph with edges $\{(1,2), (2,3), (3,1), (3,4), (4,5), (5,3)\}$, perform DFS from vertex 1, classify each edge, and determine whether the graph contains a cycle. --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Representations and Complexity **Adjacency Matrix:** - 2D array $A[1..n][1..n]$ where $A[i][j] = 1$ iff $(v_i, v_j) \in E$. - Space: $O(n^2)$. - DFS complexity: each call to check all neighbours of $v_i$ scans $n$ entries → $O(n)$ per vertex → total $O(n^2)$. **Adjacency List:** - Array of $n$ lists; `Adj[$v_i$]` contains every neighbour of $v_i$. - Space: $O(n + m)$. - DFS complexity: total neighbour iterations $\sum_{v} \deg(v) = 2m$ → total $O(n + m)$. **For a sparse social graph ($n = 50{,}000$, $m = 10{,}000{,}000$):** | Aspect | Adjacency Matrix | Adjacency List | |---|---|---| | Memory | $n^2 = 2.5 \times 10^9$ entries — **~10 GB** ❌ | $n + m \approx 10.05 \times 10^6$ — **~40 MB** ✅ | | Edge-existence check | $O(1)$ ✅ | $O(\deg(v))$ ⚠️ | | DFS scalability | $O(n^2) = 2.5 \times 10^9$ — infeasible ❌ | $O(n + m) \approx 10^6$ — fast ✅ | | **Advantage** | Constant-time edge lookup | Memory efficient on sparse graphs | | **Disadvantage** | Wastes memory on missing edges | Slow edge-existence lookup | **Conclusion:** Adjacency list is the **industry standard** for sparse predictive graphs. **Marks awarded (Part a — 7):** Definitions (2), Complexity derivations (2), Sparse-graph comparison (2), Conclusion (1). --- #### Part (b) — DFS Cycle Detection via Back Edges **Back-edge Theorem (KTU Statement):** > A directed graph $G$ contains a cycle **if and only if** DFS on $G$ encounters at least one **back edge** — i.e., an edge $(u, v)$ where $v$ is an ancestor of $u$ in the DFS tree (equivalently, $v$ is GRAY when $(u, v)$ is explored). **Algorithm:** Maintain WHITE / GRAY / BLACK colours. If during DFS we encounter a GRAY neighbour, classify $(u, v)$ as a back edge → cycle exists. **Trace on the given graph** with edges $\{(1,2), (2,3), (3,1), (3,4), (4,5), (5,3)\}$: | Step | Action | $d$ | $f$ | Colour | Edge Classified | |:---:|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|---| | 1 | Visit 1 → GRAY | 1 | — | GRAY | — | | 2 | Visit 2 → GRAY | 2 | — | GRAY | (1,2) Tree | | 3 | Visit 3 → GRAY | 3 | — | GRAY | (2,3) Tree | | 4 | Explore (3,1): 1 is GRAY | — | — | — | **(3,1) BACK EDGE — cycle detected** | | 5 | Backtrack to 3; visit 4 → GRAY | 4 | — | GRAY | (3,4) Tree | | 6 | Visit 5 → GRAY | 5 | — | GRAY | (4,5) Tree | | 7 | Explore (5,3): 3 is GRAY | — | — | — | **(5,3) BACK EDGE — second cycle** | | 8 | Finish 5 → BLACK | — | 6 | BLACK | — | | 9 | Finish 4 → BLACK | — | 7 | BLACK | — | | 10 | Finish 3 → BLACK | — | 8 | BLACK | — | | 11 | Finish 2 → BLACK | — | 9 | BLACK | — | | 12 | Finish 1 → BLACK | — | 10 | BLACK | — | **Cycle Detection Result:** - Two back edges found: $(3,1)$ and $(5,3)$. - **Cycles present:** $\{1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1\}$ and $\{3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 3\}$. - Graph contains a cycle ⇒ **NOT a DAG**. **Marks awarded (Part b — 7):** Theorem statement (2), Colour-based logic (1), Trace table (3), Cycle conclusion (1). --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DFS/BFS Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to increment `time` correctly** — students often write $d[v]$ and $f[v]$ in the wrong order, violating $d[v] < f[v]$. > 2. **Confusing back edge with cross edge** — in DFS, an edge to a **GRAY** vertex is a back edge (cycle). An edge to a **BLACK** vertex is a cross/forward edge (no cycle in digraph from this edge alone). > 3. **BFS shortest path claim on weighted graphs** — never write "BFS finds shortest path" without specifying "in unweighted graphs." Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this oversight. > 4. **Not labelling BFS levels** — KTU expects `level[v]` or `d[v]` distinct from $d[v]$ of DFS. Mixing the two notations loses **1 mark**. > 5. **Recursion stack overflow in DFS** — KTU may ask why DFS fails on graphs with $10^6$ depth; answer: stack overflow. Use **iterative DFS** with explicit stack in production. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Predictive Analysis with Graphs (DFS & BFS):** > - **Graph $G = (V, E)$** — vertices + edges; order $n = |V|$, size $m = |E|$. > - **Handshaking Lemma:** $\sum_v \deg(v) = 2m$ (undirected). > - **Adjacency matrix:** $O(n^2)$ space; fast edge lookup; bad for sparse graphs. > - **Adjacency list:** $O(n + m)$ space; ideal for sparse predictive graphs (social, web, transactions). > - **DFS:** Stack / recursion; $O(V + E)$; produces discovery/finish times; yields DFS tree/forest; detects cycles via back edges. > - **BFS:** FIFO queue; $O(V + E)$; produces shortest path (edge-count) in **unweighted** graphs; yields BFS shortest-path tree. > - **WHITE / GRAY / BLACK** states are essential for DFS edge classification. > - **Tree / Forward / Back / Cross edges** — only back edges imply cycles in directed graphs. > - **DFS** is preferred for: cycle detection, SCC, topological sort, strongly connected analysis. > - **BFS** is preferred for: shortest path, $k$-hop reachability, bipartite test, level-order predictive horizons. > - **Density $\rho$** quantifies connectedness: $\rho \approx 0$ sparse (predictive challenge), $\rho \approx 1$ complete (trivial prediction). > - **Engineering rule:** real-world predictive graphs are *sparse* + *clustered* → adjacency list + $O(V+E)$ algorithms are the only scalable choice. > - **Pitfall to avoid:** BFS shortest-path claim must always be qualified as "unweighted."

topics.title: Path-Finding with Dijkstra's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Path-Finding with Dijkstra's Algorithm # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Advanced Database Systems**, **Path-Finding with Dijkstra's Algorithm** refers to the application of Edsger W. Dijkstra's classic single-source shortest-path algorithm to compute optimal routes within graph-structured data stored in databases such as **Graph Databases (Neo4j, JanusGraph)** and **Spatial Databases (PostGIS, Oracle Spatial)**. The algorithm, conceived in 1956, systematically determines the minimum cumulative cost (distance, time, or weight) from a designated **source node** to every other reachable node in a weighted graph having **non-negative edge weights**. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the algorithm is classified as a **greedy, label-setting shortest-path procedure** that operates on the mathematical structure $G = (V, E, w)$ where $V$ is the set of vertices (entities), $E$ is the set of edges (relationships), and $w: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ is the weight function. It maintains a priority queue of tentative distances and permanently finalizes ("settles") the node with the smallest tentative distance at each iteration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** Dijkstra's algorithm forms the computational backbone of modern routing engines, social-network "degrees-of-separation" queries, supply-chain optimization, and SQL/PGQ recursive traversals in graph databases. It is a mandatory topic under "Advanced Query Processing in Graph and Spatial Databases." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **Kerala State Road Transport (KSRTC) traveler** standing at *Thiruvananthapuram Central* (the source) and wanting to find the **cheapest (minimum toll + fuel) route** to reach every other major city in Kerala. You have a map where every road (edge) has a cost written on it. Your strategy: 1. From your current city, look at all directly-connected cities. 2. Pick the city with the **lowest cumulative cost so far** — settle it permanently. 3. From this newly settled city, explore its neighbors and **update** (relax) the tentative cost of any city that can now be reached more cheaply via this new path. 4. Repeat until every city is settled. This "always expand the cheapest unsettled city first" rule is the **greedy heart of Dijkstra's algorithm**. The algorithm never revisits a settled node because the non-negative weight guarantee ensures the first time a node is popped from the priority queue, it has the true shortest distance. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** Dijkstra = "Always walk down the cheapest unexplored road, and never second-guess a road you've already committed to." ## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Source Vertex (s)** | The starting node from which all shortest paths are computed. | | **Tentative Distance `dist[v]`** | The current best-known shortest distance from `s` to `v`, initially $\infty$. | | **Finalized / Settled Node** | A node whose `dist[v]` is provably optimal and no longer updated. | | **Edge Relaxation** | The act of checking whether reaching `v` through `u` is cheaper than the current `dist[v]`. | | **Priority Queue (Min-Heap)** | The data structure that always extracts the unvisited node with the minimum `dist[]`. | | **Predecessor Array `prev[v]`** | Stores the parent of `v` on the shortest path, used for path reconstruction. | | **Time Complexity** | $O((V + E) \log V)$ using a binary heap; $O(V^2)$ using an array. | | **Space Complexity** | $O(V + E)$ for the graph, priority queue, and auxiliary arrays. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Precondition:** All edge weights **MUST be non-negative** ($w(u,v) \geq 0$). If negative weights exist, the Bellman–Ford algorithm must be used instead. This is a frequent board-exam trap. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic — A Structured Walk-Through Dijkstra's algorithm follows a precise sequence of operations. Below is the **bulleted logic spine** that the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce: ### Phase 1 — Initialization 1. Create a distance array `dist[1..n]` and set `dist[s] = 0` and `dist[v] = ∞` for all $v \neq s$. 2. Create a predecessor array `prev[1..n]` and set `prev[v] = NULL`. 3. Insert all vertices into a **min-priority queue** keyed by their current `dist[]` value. 4. Maintain a `visited[]` (or `settled[]`) boolean array, initially all `FALSE`. ### Phase 2 — Iterative Greedy Selection 5. **While** the priority queue is not empty: - Extract the vertex `u` with the **minimum tentative distance** (Extract-Min operation). - If `u` is already visited, skip it (lazy deletion). - Mark `u` as **visited / settled** — its `dist[u]` is now final. - **For every neighbor `v` of `u`**: - Compute the **alternative path cost**: `alt = dist[u] + w(u, v)`. - If `alt < dist[v]` (the **relaxation condition**), then: - Update `dist[v] = alt`. - Update `prev[v] = u`. - **Decrease-Key** the priority queue entry for `v` to `alt`. ### Phase 3 — Termination & Reconstruction 6. The loop terminates when all reachable vertices are settled (or the priority queue empties). 7. To reconstruct the actual shortest path from `s` to a target `t`, backtrack through `prev[]`: `t → prev[t] → prev[prev[t]] → ... → s`, then reverse the sequence. ### Phase 4 — Why the Greedy Choice is Correct (Proof Sketch) - The algorithm maintains the **invariant**: for every settled vertex `u`, `dist[u]` equals the true shortest-path distance from `s`. - The invariant is preserved because Dijkstra always selects the *unsettled* vertex `u` with the **globally minimum** `dist[u]`. Any alternative path to `u` must pass through an unsettled vertex `w` with `dist[w] ≥ dist[u]`. Since all edge weights are $\geq 0$, no path through `w` can be shorter than `dist[u]`. Therefore, `u`'s distance is final and cannot be improved. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" — Explained for Beginners:** The non-negative weight rule is what makes the greedy choice safe. If a road had *negative* toll (a refund, say), the algorithm could be fooled into thinking a longer path is cheaper just because a later refund made it look attractive — but the algorithm has already committed. That's exactly why Dijkstra forbids negative edges. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table — it appears in 80% of KTU Dijkstra problems.** | # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use | |---|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Initial distance | $\text{dist}[s] = 0,\ \text{dist}[v \neq s] = \infty$ | Graph DB initialization in Neo4j `gds.shortestPath` | | 2 | Relaxation condition | $\text{alt} = \text{dist}[u] + w(u,v) < \text{dist}[v]$ | Edge update during traversal | | 3 | Distance update | $\text{dist}[v] = \text{alt},\ \text{prev}[v] = u$ | Finalizing cheaper route | | 4 | Path reconstruction | $P(s,t) = \langle s, \dots, \text{prev}[t], t \rangle$ | Returning route to user in GPS apps | | 5 | Time complexity (binary heap) | $T(n) = O((V + E) \log V)$ | Standard implementation | | 6 | Time complexity (array) | $T(n) = O(V^2)$ | Dense graphs, no heap | | 7 | Time complexity (Fibonacci heap) | $T(n) = O(E + V \log V)$ | Theoretical optimum | | 8 | Space complexity | $S(n) = O(V + E)$ | Memory footprint for spatial DBs | | 9 | Non-negativity constraint | $w(u,v) \geq 0\ \forall\ (u,v) \in E$ | Validates input graph | | 10 | Settled node guarantee | Once popped, $\text{dist}[u]$ is optimal | Used in A* search termination | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |--------|-------------| | **Graph Databases (Neo4j)** | Cypher queries like `MATCH p = shortestPath((a)-[*]-(b))` invoke Dijkstra under the hood via the **Graph Data Science (GDS) library**. | | **Spatial Databases (PostGIS, Oracle Spatial)** | `pgRouting` extension uses Dijkstra for road-network routing and isochrone generation. | | **Network Routing (OSPF, IS-IS)** | Link-state routers use a Dijkstra-like SPF (Shortest Path First) algorithm to compute routing tables. | | **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Amazon, Flipkart, and Zomato use it for last-mile delivery route optimization. | | **Social Networks** | Computing "degrees of separation" between users in LinkedIn / Facebook. | | **Telecommunications** | Call routing in SS7 signaling networks; minimum-hop path in MPLS. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** In a production KTU project, you would typically pair Dijkstra with a **heuristic function** (turning it into **A\***) to prune the search space, since pure Dijkstra on a road network with millions of intersections is computationally expensive. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete Worked Example — The "Kerala City Network" Consider the following weighted undirected graph representing distances (in km) between six Kerala cities. We want to find the **shortest path from `A` (Kochi) to all other cities**. **Graph Edges and Weights:** - $w(A,B) = 4$, $w(A,C) = 2$ - $w(B,C) = 1$, $w(B,D) = 5$ - $w(C,D) = 8$, $w(C,E) = 10$ - $w(D,E) = 2$, $w(D,F) = 6$ - $w(E,F) = 3$ ### Step 0 — Initialization | Vertex | `dist[]` | `prev[]` | `visited` | |:------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---------:| | A | **0** | NULL | FALSE | | B | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | | C | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | | D | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | | E | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | | F | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | **Priority Queue (PQ):** $\{A:0,\ B:\infty,\ C:\infty,\ D:\infty,\ E:\infty,\ F:\infty\}$ --- ### Step 1 — Extract Minimum: `A` (dist = 0) `A` is settled. Relax all edges from `A`: - **Edge $(A, B, w=4)$:** $\text{alt} = 0 + 4 = 4 < \infty$. Update $\text{dist}[B] = 4$, $\text{prev}[B] = A$. - **Edge $(A, C, w=2)$:** $\text{alt} = 0 + 2 = 2 < \infty$. Update $\text{dist}[C] = 2$, $\text{prev}[C] = A$. | Vertex | `dist[]` | `prev[]` | `visited` | |:------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---------:| | A | 0 | NULL | **TRUE** | | B | 4 | A | FALSE | | C | 2 | A | FALSE | | D | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | | E | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | | F | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | **PQ:** $\{C:2,\ B:4,\ D:\infty,\ E:\infty,\ F:\infty\}$ --- ### Step 2 — Extract Minimum: `C` (dist = 2) `C` is settled. Relax all edges from `C`: - **Edge $(C, B, w=1)$:** $\text{alt} = 2 + 1 = 3 < 4$. Update $\text{dist}[B] = 3$, $\text{prev}[B] = C$. ✅ **Improved!** - **Edge $(C, D, w=8)$:** $\text{alt} = 2 + 8 = 10 < \infty$. Update $\text{dist}[D] = 10$, $\text{prev}[D] = C$. - **Edge $(C, E, w=10)$:** $\text{alt} = 2 + 10 = 12 < \infty$. Update $\text{dist}[E] = 12$, $\text{prev}[E] = C$. | Vertex | `dist[]` | `prev[]` | `visited` | |:------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---------:| | A | 0 | NULL | TRUE | | B | **3** | **C** | FALSE | | C | 2 | A | **TRUE** | | D | 10 | C | FALSE | | E | 12 | C | FALSE | | F | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | **PQ:** $\{B:3,\ D:10,\ E:12,\ F:\infty\}$ --- ### Step 3 — Extract Minimum: `B` (dist = 3) `B` is settled. Relax all edges from `B`: - **Edge $(B, A, w=4)$:** `A` is already visited — skip (no point updating a settled node). - **Edge $(B, C, w=1)$:** `C` is already visited — skip. - **Edge $(B, D, w=5)$:** $\text{alt} = 3 + 5 = 8 < 10$. Update $\text{dist}[D] = 8$, $\text{prev}[D] = B$. ✅ **Improved!** | Vertex | `dist[]` | `prev[]` | `visited` | |:------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---------:| | A | 0 | NULL | TRUE | | B | 3 | C | **TRUE** | | C | 2 | A | TRUE | | D | **8** | **B** | FALSE | | E | 12 | C | FALSE | | F | $\infty$ | NULL | FALSE | **PQ:** $\{D:8,\ E:12,\ F:\infty\}$ --- ### Step 4 — Extract Minimum: `D` (dist = 8) `D` is settled. Relax all edges from `D`: - **Edge $(D, B, w=5)$:** `B` is visited — skip. - **Edge $(D, C, w=8)$:** `C` is visited — skip. - **Edge $(D, E, w=2)$:** $\text{alt} = 8 + 2 = 10 < 12$. Update $\text{dist}[E] = 10$, $\text{prev}[E] = D$. ✅ **Improved!** - **Edge $(D, F, w=6)$:** $\text{alt} = 8 + 6 = 14 < \infty$. Update $\text{dist}[F] = 14$, $\text{prev}[F] = D$. | Vertex | `dist[]` | `prev[]` | `visited` | |:------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---------:| | A | 0 | NULL | TRUE | | B | 3 | C | TRUE | | C | 2 | A | TRUE | | D | 8 | B | **TRUE** | | E | **10** | **D** | FALSE | | F | 14 | D | FALSE | **PQ:** $\{E:10,\ F:14\}$ --- ### Step 5 — Extract Minimum: `E` (dist = 10) `E` is settled. Relax all edges from `E`: - **Edge $(E, C, w=10)$:** `C` is visited — skip. - **Edge $(E, D, w=2)$:** `D` is visited — skip. - **Edge $(E, F, w=3)$:** $\text{alt} = 10 + 3 = 13 < 14$. Update $\text{dist}[F] = 13$, $\text{prev}[F] = E$. ✅ **Improved!** | Vertex | `dist[]` | `prev[]` | `visited` | |:------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---------:| | A | 0 | NULL | TRUE | | B | 3 | C | TRUE | | C | 2 | A | TRUE | | D | 8 | B | TRUE | | E | 10 | D | **TRUE** | | F | **13** | **E** | FALSE | **PQ:** $\{F:13\}$ --- ### Step 6 — Extract Minimum: `F` (dist = 13) `F` is settled. All neighbors (`D`, `E`) are already visited — no relaxations. | Vertex | `dist[]` | `prev[]` | `visited` | |:------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---------:| | A | 0 | NULL | TRUE | | B | 3 | C | TRUE | | C | 2 | A | TRUE | | D | 8 | B | TRUE | | E | 10 | D | TRUE | | F | 13 | E | **TRUE** | **PQ:** $\emptyset$ — **Algorithm Terminates**. --- ## 3.2 Final Shortest-Path Table (The Analytical Matrix) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the exact "final answer matrix" the KTU examiner expects on a 14-mark question.** | Destination | Shortest Distance from `A` | Predecessor Chain | Reconstructed Path | |:-----------:|:--------------------------:|:-----------------:|:------------------:| | B | **3** | $B \leftarrow C \leftarrow A$ | $A \to C \to B$ | | C | **2** | $C \leftarrow A$ | $A \to C$ | | D | **8** | $D \leftarrow B \leftarrow C \leftarrow A$ | $A \to C \to B \to D$ | | E | **10** | $E \leftarrow D \leftarrow B \leftarrow C \leftarrow A$ | $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$ | | F | **13** | $F \leftarrow E \leftarrow D \leftarrow B \leftarrow C \leftarrow A$ | $A \to C \to B \to D \to E \to F$ | ### 3.3 Mathematical Verification (Path $A \to F$) We claim the shortest path from `A` to `F` has total weight 13. Let us verify by **enumeration of plausible candidate paths**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Path 1:} \quad & A \to C \to B \to D \to E \to F = 2 + 1 + 5 + 2 + 3 = 13 \\ \text{Path 2:} \quad & A \to B \to D \to E \to F = 4 + 5 + 2 + 3 = 14 \\ \text{Path 3:} \quad & A \to C \to D \to E \to F = 2 + 8 + 2 + 3 = 15 \\ \text{Path 4:} \quad & A \to C \to E \to F = 2 + 10 + 3 = 15 \\ \text{Path 5:} \quad & A \to B \to D \to F = 4 + 5 + 6 = 15 \\ \end{aligned} $$ The minimum across all enumerated candidates is **13**, achieved uniquely by Path 1. This confirms the correctness of the algorithm's output: $$\text{dist}[A \to F] = \min\{\text{all valid A-to-F paths}\} = 13$$ # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Define Dijkstra's single-source shortest-path algorithm. State its time complexity when implemented with a binary heap and the precondition on edge weights.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Dijkstra's algorithm is a **greedy, label-setting procedure** that computes the minimum cumulative weight from a designated **source vertex `s`** to every other vertex in a weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with non-negative edge weights. **Working Principle:** It maintains a min-priority queue keyed on the tentative distance `dist[v]`. At each step, it extracts the unsettled vertex with the smallest `dist[]`, marks it final, and **relaxes** the weights of all its outgoing edges. A relaxation of edge $(u, v)$ updates `dist[v] = min(dist[v], dist[u] + w(u, v))`. **Time Complexity:** Using a **binary min-heap**, the complexity is: $$T(V, E) = O\bigl((V + E) \log V\bigr)$$ **Mandatory Precondition:** All edge weights must satisfy $w(u, v) \geq 0$. If negative weights exist, the algorithm produces incorrect results and **Bellman–Ford** must be used instead. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip (1-mark bonus):** Mentioning that the algorithm is **greedy** and **label-setting** (in contrast to Bellman–Ford which is *label-correcting*) fetches full marks. --- ### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: What is edge relaxation in the context of Dijkstra's algorithm? Explain with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Edge relaxation** is the core update operation performed when a vertex `u` is settled. For every neighbor `v` of `u`, the algorithm computes an *alternative path cost* via `u`: $$\text{alt} = \text{dist}[u] + w(u, v)$$ If `alt` is **strictly less than** the current `dist[v]`, the algorithm performs the update: $$\text{dist}[v] = \text{alt}, \quad \text{prev}[v] = u$$ **Example:** Suppose `dist[A] = 4`, `dist[C] = 2`, and edge $(A, C)$ has weight 2. The alternative path through `C` to `A` would have cost $2 + 2 = 4$, which is **not less** than the current `dist[A] = 4`, so **no relaxation occurs**. If however `dist[A] = 5` and the alternative cost was 4, then `dist[A]` would be relaxed to 4 with `prev[A] = C`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two full alternative questions are provided below, and in the actual exam you must answer **either OR**. --- ### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **Q: (a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the working of Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm. Mention its time and space complexity. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the following graph with source vertex `S`. Construct the shortest-path tree and list the shortest distance and path from `S` to every other vertex. **(7 Marks)** | Edge | Weight | |------|:------:| | S – A | 1 | | S – B | 4 | | A – B | 2 | | A – C | 5 | | B – C | 1 | | C – D | 3 | | B – D | 7 | --- #### Model Solution to B-A(a) — 7 Marks Dijkstra's algorithm operates in three phases: **Phase 1 — Initialization:** Set `dist[S] = 0`, `dist[v] = ∞` for all other vertices, `prev[v] = NULL`, and insert all vertices into a min-priority queue. **Phase 2 — Greedy Iteration:** While the PQ is non-empty: 1. Extract vertex `u` with minimum `dist[u]` (Extract-Min). 2. Mark `u` as visited; its `dist[u]` is now final. 3. For each neighbor `v` of `u`, perform **edge relaxation**: - Compute `alt = dist[u] + w(u, v)`. - If `alt < dist[v]`, then `dist[v] = alt`, `prev[v] = u`, and perform **Decrease-Key** on `v` in the PQ. **Phase 3 — Path Reconstruction:** Trace `prev[]` backwards from the target to the source, then reverse. **Complexity Analysis:** | Implementation | Time Complexity | |----------------|:---------------:| | Array-based (no heap) | $O(V^2)$ | | Binary min-heap | $O((V + E) \log V)$ | | Fibonacci heap | $O(E + V \log V)$ | **Space Complexity:** $O(V + E)$ — required for the adjacency list, `dist[]`, `prev[]`, and the priority queue. The **correctness** of the algorithm rests on the invariant: *when a vertex is settled, its `dist` value is optimal*. This holds because any alternative path to it must pass through an unsettled vertex whose tentative distance is at least as large, and edge weights are non-negative. *(Refer to the flowchart in Section 4.1 for the visual representation.)* --- #### Model Solution to B-A(b) — 7 Marks **Step-by-step execution:** **Initial State:** | Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | |:------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | `dist[]` | 0 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | `prev[]` | NULL | NULL | NULL | NULL | NULL | **Iteration 1: Settle `S` (dist = 0)** - Edge $S \to A$: $\text{alt} = 0 + 1 = 1 < \infty$ → `dist[A] = 1`, `prev[A] = S` - Edge $S \to B$: $\text{alt} = 0 + 4 = 4 < \infty$ → `dist[B] = 4`, `prev[B] = S` | Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | |:------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | `dist[]` | 0 | **1** | 4 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | | `prev[]` | NULL | S | S | NULL | NULL | **Iteration 2: Settle `A` (dist = 1)** - Edge $A \to B$: $\text{alt} = 1 + 2 = 3 < 4$ → `dist[B] = 3`, `prev[B] = A` ✅ - Edge $A \to C$: $\text{alt} = 1 + 5 = 6 < \infty$ → `dist[C] = 6`, `prev[C] = A` | Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | |:------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | `dist[]` | 0 | 1 | **3** | 6 | $\infty$ | | `prev[]` | NULL | S | A | A | NULL | **Iteration 3: Settle `B` (dist = 3)** - Edge $B \to C$: $\text{alt} = 3 + 1 = 4 < 6$ → `dist[C] = 4`, `prev[C] = B` ✅ - Edge $B \to D$: $\text{alt} = 3 + 7 = 10 < \infty$ → `dist[D] = 10`, `prev[D] = B` | Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | |:------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | `dist[]` | 0 | 1 | 3 | **4** | 10 | | `prev[]` | NULL | S | A | B | B | **Iteration 4: Settle `C` (dist = 4)** - Edge $C \to D$: $\text{alt} = 4 + 3 = 7 < 10$ → `dist[D] = 7`, `prev[D] = C` ✅ | Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | |:------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | `dist[]` | 0 | 1 | 3 | 4 | **7** | | `prev[]` | NULL | S | A | B | C | **Iteration 5: Settle `D` (dist = 7)** — all neighbors visited, no relaxations. **Final Shortest-Path Tree & Distances:** | Vertex | Shortest Distance from `S` | Path | |:------:|:--------------------------:|:-----| | A | 1 | $S \to A$ | | B | 3 | $S \to A \to B$ | | C | 4 | $S \to A \to B \to C$ | | D | 7 | $S \to A \to B \to C \to D$ | **Verification by direct enumeration:** $$ \begin{aligned} S \to D \text{ via } S \to A \to B \to C \to D &= 1 + 2 + 1 + 3 = 7 \\ S \to D \text{ via } S \to B \to C \to D &= 4 + 1 + 3 = 8 \\ S \to D \text{ via } S \to A \to B \to D &= 1 + 2 + 7 = 10 \end{aligned} $$ The minimum is 7, confirming the algorithm's result. ✅ --- ### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]` **Q: (a)** Explain how Dijkstra's algorithm is applied in **graph databases** (such as Neo4j) and **spatial databases** (such as PostGIS with pgRouting). Give two real-world use cases. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare Dijkstra's algorithm with the **Bellman–Ford algorithm** and the **A\* search algorithm** with respect to: edge-weight constraint, time complexity, optimality, and use case. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B-B(a) — 7 Marks **Application 1 — Graph Databases (Neo4j):** In Neo4j, the Graph Data Science (GDS) library provides a procedure `gds.shortestPath.dijkstra` that internally runs Dijkstra's algorithm. The Cypher equivalent query is: ```cypher MATCH (source:City {name: 'Kochi'}), (target:City {name: 'Kollam'}) CALL gds.shortestPath.dijkstra.stream('roadNetwork', { sourceNode: source, targetNode: target, relationshipWeightProperty: 'distance' }) YIELD path, totalCost RETURN path, totalCost; ``` The algorithm traverses the labeled-property graph, with vertices representing entities (cities, users, products) and edges representing weighted relationships (distances, friendship strengths, transaction costs). **Application 2 — Spatial Databases (PostGIS / pgRouting):** The `pgRouting` extension provides a SQL function: ```sql SELECT * FROM pgr_dijkstra( 'SELECT id, source, target, cost, reverse_cost FROM road_ways', source_id, target_id, directed := false ); ``` This returns the **ordered sequence of road segments** forming the shortest path, which is then visualized on a map using QGIS or Leaflet. **Real-World Use Cases:** 1. **Swiggy / Zomato Delivery Routing:** Each restaurant and delivery location is a vertex; the road network is the edge set weighted by travel time. Dijkstra computes the minimum-time route for a delivery executive in real time. 2. **LinkedIn "Degrees of Separation":** Computing the shortest connection path between two professionals (e.g., "You are connected to Person X in 4 hops") is a graph shortest-path problem. 3. **BSNL / Airtel Network Routing:** Link-state protocols (OSPF) inside telecom backbones run Dijkstra to compute the SPF (Shortest Path First) tree every few seconds. 4. **Google Maps / MapMyIndia:** Road-network routing at continental scale uses contraction hierarchies (an optimized variant of Dijkstra) to achieve sub-second query times. --- #### Model Solution to B-B(b) — 7 Marks **Comparison Table:** | Property | Dijkstra | Bellman–Ford | A* Search | |----------|:--------:|:------------:|:---------:| | **Edge-weight constraint** | Non-negative only ($w \geq 0$) | Handles negative weights (no negative cycles) | Non-negative only ($w \geq 0$) | | **Time complexity** | $O((V+E) \log V)$ with binary heap | $O(V \cdot E)$ | $O((V+E) \log V)$ worst case; often much faster in practice | | **Optimality** | Optimal (greedy) | Optimal for graphs without negative cycles | Optimal if heuristic is *admissible* (never overestimates) | | **Heuristic used?** | No | No | Yes — $f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$ where $h$ is the heuristic | | **Data structure** | Min-priority queue | None (simple array relaxation) | Min-priority queue ordered by $f(v)$ | | **Typical use case** | Single-source shortest path in road networks, graph DBs | Detecting negative-weight cycles; distributed routing (RIP) | Pathfinding in games, robotics, GPS with geographic heuristic | | **Output** | All shortest paths from `s` | All shortest paths from `s` | Single shortest path from `s` to a specified goal | | **Memory** | $O(V+E)$ | $O(V)$ additional | $O(V+E)$ | **Key Insights:** - **Dijkstra vs Bellman–Ford:** Dijkstra is faster ($O(E \log V)$ vs $O(VE)$) but **cannot handle negative weights**. Bellman–Ford is more general but slower, and is preferred in protocols like **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)** where robustness outweighs speed. - **Dijkstra vs A\*:** A\* augments Dijkstra's `g(v) = dist[v]` with a heuristic $h(v)$ that estimates the remaining cost to the goal. With an **admissible** heuristic (e.g., straight-line distance in a road network), A\* prunes the search space dramatically and can be exponentially faster, while still guaranteeing optimality. A\* degrades to plain Dijkstra when $h(v) = 0$. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standard:** Modern GPS engines (Google Maps, HERE, TomTom) use **A\*** with carefully crafted admissible heuristics (e.g., contracted road-network lower bounds), not pure Dijkstra. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Dijkstra's Algorithm Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `prev[]` array (–2 marks):** Examiners explicitly want the predecessor chain and the reconstructed path, not just the distance. Always maintain and report `prev[]`. > 2. **Not showing the priority-queue state at each step (–2 marks):** Board examiners award marks for demonstrating the *mechanics* of the algorithm. List the PQ contents after every iteration. > 3. **Settling a node but failing to show the relaxation step (–1 to –2 marks):** Every time a node is settled, you must explicitly show `alt = dist[u] + w(u,v)` for **each** neighbor, even when no update occurs. > 4. **Confusing Dijkstra with BFS (–3 marks):** BFS gives shortest *hop count*, not shortest *weighted* path. They are NOT the same. If weights are involved, BFS is wrong. > 5. **Applying Dijkstra to a graph with negative edges (–full credit):** The algorithm silently produces wrong results on negative-weight graphs. Always state the non-negative-weight precondition. > 6. **Missing the `visited[]` check on re-extraction (–1 mark):** A "lazy deletion" implementation may extract an already-visited vertex; you must mention that this is a valid optimization. > 7. **Wrong complexity stated (–1 mark):** $O((V+E) \log V)$ is the heap-based complexity. $O(V^2)$ is for the naive array version. Do not write $O(V+E)$ — that would be BFS. > 8. **Not mentioning the data structure used (–1 mark):** Always state "implemented using a min-priority queue (binary min-heap)." --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your study wall!)** - **Core Identity:** Dijkstra's algorithm = **greedy + label-setting + single-source + non-negative weights**. - **Three Arrays, One Set:** Maintain `dist[]`, `prev[]`, `visited[]`, plus a min-PQ. - **Initialization Rule:** `dist[source] = 0`, all others `= ∞`. - **Greedy Selection:** Always pop the *unvisited* vertex with the *minimum* `dist[]`. - **Relaxation Formula:** $\text{alt} = \text{dist}[u] + w(u, v)$. If $\text{alt} < \text{dist}[v]$, update. - **Predecessor Update:** $\text{prev}[v] = u$ (only when relaxation succeeds). - **Path Reconstruction:** Backtrack `prev[]` from target to source, then reverse. - **Time Complexity (Binary Heap):** $O((V + E) \log V)$. - **Time Complexity (Array):** $O(V^2)$ — preferred for dense graphs. - **Space Complexity:** $O(V + E)$. - **Hard Precondition:** $w(u, v) \geq 0$ for **all** edges. Violation → use **Bellman–Ford**. - **Correctness Invariant:** Once settled, `dist[u]` is *final and optimal*. - **Database Context:** Neo4j `gds.shortestPath.dijkstra`, PostGIS `pgr_dijkstra`. - **Difference from BFS:** BFS minimizes *hop count* (unweighted); Dijkstra minimizes *sum of weights* (weighted). - **Difference from A\*:** A\* = Dijkstra + admissible heuristic; faster on large spatial networks. - **Difference from Bellman–Ford:** Bellman–Ford handles negative weights but is $O(VE)$; Dijkstra is faster but forbids negatives. - **Negative Cycle Detection:** Dijkstra *cannot* detect negative cycles — that is Bellman–Ford's job. - **Lazy Deletion:** A vertex may be extracted multiple times from the PQ; skip if already visited. - **Decrease-Key:** When `dist[v]` improves, update the PQ entry — the standard heap supports this in $O(\log V)$. - **Real-World Variants:** Contraction Hierarchies, ALT (A* + Landmarks + Triangle inequality), Hub Labels — all are Dijkstra optimizations used in production route planners. - **Examiner's Three Magic Words:** *"Greedy, label-setting, non-negative weights."* Open or close every Dijkstra answer with this phrase.

topics.title: Graph Theory and Predictive Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graph Theory and Predictive Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Graph Theory – Formal Definition

**Graph Theory** is the branch of discrete mathematics that studies the structural and combinatorial properties of **graphs** — abstract representations of a set of objects (called **vertices** or **nodes**) in which some pairs of objects are related through **edges** (or **links**). Formally, a graph $G$ is defined as an ordered pair:

$$G = (V, E)$$

where $V$ is a non-empty finite set of **vertices** and $E \subseteq V \times V$ is a set of **edges** connecting pairs of vertices.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of *Advanced Database Systems*, graph theory is the mathematical foundation of **graph databases** such as **Neo4j**, **Amazon Neptune**, and **TigerGraph**, which store data as nodes, relationships, and properties rather than in rigid relational tables.

## 1.2 Predictive Modeling – Formal Definition

**Predictive Modeling** is the process of using statistical algorithms, machine learning techniques, and historical data to forecast future outcomes, behaviors, or trends. It is a core component of **data mining**, **knowledge discovery in databases (KDD)**, and modern **AI-driven database systems**. The output is a trained **model** $f: X \to Y$ that maps input features $X$ to a target variable $Y$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Expected Answer):**
> Predictive modeling is a statistical technique using historical and current data to predict future outcomes by analyzing patterns and relationships among variables to generate a probabilistic score for unknown events.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The City Map and the Crystal Ball"

Imagine you are a **city planner** standing in front of a large road map:

- **Vertices (Nodes)** = Cities or intersections on the map.
- **Edges** = Roads connecting them (one-way = *directed*, two-way = *undirected*).
- **Edge Weights** = Distance, travel time, or toll cost.
- **Graph Algorithms** = Your GPS finding the *shortest* path, *fastest* route, or detecting *traffic clusters*.

Now imagine the same map also contains **historical accident data** on every road. By overlaying past accident patterns on the graph structure, you can **predict** which intersection will likely be the site of the next accident — this fusion of **graph structure + historical data** is exactly *Graph Theory meets Predictive Modeling*.

- **Vertices** with many connections = **influencers** (high degree centrality).
- **Shortest path** = routing queries in graph databases.
- **Predictive model** = the brain that uses the graph to forecast outcomes.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Advanced Database Systems

| Aspect | Relational DB | Graph + Predictive System |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Fixed tables, JOINs | Flexible nodes/relationships |
| **Query for "friends of friends"** | Many expensive JOINs | Single traversal (Cypher) |
| **Prediction** | SQL aggregates only | Built-in ML pipelines + graphs |
| **Use case** | Transactional data | Social, fraud, recommendation |

> [!TIP]
> Modern graph-augmented predictive systems power **Facebook friend suggestions**, **Google Maps ETA prediction**, **credit-card fraud detection**, and **Netflix content recommendation** — all topics frequently asked in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of Graph Theory

### 2.1.1 Types of Graphs (with Examples)

A graph $G = (V, E)$ can be classified along several orthogonal dimensions. The following list enumerates the categories most frequently tested in KTU examinations:

- **Undirected Graph** — Edges have no orientation. $(u, v) = (v, u)$. Example: Facebook friendship.
- **Directed Graph (Digraph)** — Edges have a direction $(u \to v)$. Example: Twitter followers.
- **Weighted Graph** — Each edge carries a numerical weight $w(u, v)$. Example: road distances.
- **Unweighted Graph** — All edges are treated as equivalent (weight = 1).
- **Simple Graph** — No self-loops, no multiple edges between the same pair.
- **Multigraph** — Multiple edges allowed between the same two vertices.
- **Complete Graph $K_n$** — Every pair of distinct vertices is connected. Number of edges = $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- **Bipartite Graph** — Vertices can be split into two disjoint sets $V_1, V_2$ such that every edge connects a vertex in $V_1$ to a vertex in $V_2$.
- **Cyclic Graph** — Contains at least one cycle.
- **Acyclic Graph (DAG)** — Directed graph with no directed cycles (used in topological sorting, dependency resolution).
- **Connected Graph** — There is a path between every pair of vertices.
- **Subgraph** — A graph formed from a subset of vertices and edges of $G$.

### 2.1.2 Essential Vertex and Edge Properties

- **Degree of a vertex** $\deg(v)$ = number of edges incident to $v$.
- **In-degree** $\deg^-(v)$ = number of incoming edges in a digraph.
- **Out-degree** $\deg^+(v)$ = number of outgoing edges in a digraph.
- **Handshaking Lemma** (always tested in KTU):
  $$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$$
- **Adjacency matrix** $A$ — an $n \times n$ matrix where $A_{ij} = 1$ if there is an edge from $i$ to $j$, else $0$.
- **Adjacency list** — a list of vertices, where each vertex stores a list of its neighbors (sparse-graph friendly).

### 2.1.3 Why These Concepts Matter

- The **handshaking lemma** is a guaranteed 3-mark question in KTU.
- The **adjacency matrix vs. adjacency list** comparison is essential for understanding **graph database storage engines**.
- **DAGs** form the basis of **dependency graphs** in query optimizers.

## 2.2 Predictive Modeling Building Blocks

### 2.2.1 The Predictive Modeling Pipeline

1. **Data Collection** — From RDBMS, NoSQL, or graph databases.
2. **Data Preprocessing** — Cleaning, normalization, feature engineering.
3. **Feature Engineering from Graphs** — Degree centrality, betweenness, PageRank, clustering coefficient.
4. **Model Selection** — Linear regression, logistic regression, decision tree, random forest, neural network, GNN.
5. **Training & Validation** — Cross-validation, train/test split.
6. **Evaluation** — Accuracy, precision, recall, F1, RMSE, AUC-ROC.
7. **Deployment** — Embedded into the database as a stored procedure or ML pipeline (e.g., Neo4j ML, BigQuery ML).

### 2.2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **canonical formulas** you must memorize for KTU exams. Remember the **Anti-`|` Rule** in tables: vertical bars are replaced with `\vert` or `\mid`.

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Handshaking Lemma | $\sum \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$ | Sum of degrees = twice the number of edges |
| Complete graph edges | $\vert E \vert = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $K_n$ has $n$ vertices |
| BFS/DFS time | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Traversal cost |
| Dijkstra complexity | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ | Min-heap version |
| PageRank vector | $\mathbf{r} = d \cdot M \mathbf{r} + \dfrac{1-d}{n}\mathbf{1}$ | $d$ = damping factor (≈ 0.85) |
| Linear regression | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_n x_n$ | $\beta$ = learned weights |
| Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Lower is better |
| Root MSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same unit as $y$ |
| Sigmoid (Logistic) | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Squashes to $(0,1)$ |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| Precision | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive predictive value |
| Recall | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Sensitivity / TPR |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean |
| Euclidean distance | $d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Used in k-NN |
| Cosine similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}}{\vert\mathbf{A}\vert \cdot \vert\mathbf{B}\vert}$ | Recommendation systems |
| Clustering coefficient | $C_i = \dfrac{2 e_i}{k_i (k_i - 1)}$ | $k_i$ = degree, $e_i$ = edges among neighbors |
| Betweenness centrality | $C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | $\sigma_{st}$ = shortest paths from $s$ to $t$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications** — Use graph theory to optimize network routing and detect failures.
- **Social Network Analysis** — Identify influencers (PageRank) and communities (Louvain).
- **Fraud Detection** — Build a graph of transactions; predictive models flag anomalous sub-graphs.
- **Recommendation Engines** — Use graph embeddings + cosine similarity for "users who liked X also liked Y".
- **Bioinformatics** — Protein–protein interaction graphs help predict disease pathways.
- **Transportation** — Shortest path algorithms power Google Maps and Uber routing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Handshaking Lemma

**Statement:** For any undirected graph $G = (V, E)$,
$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$$

### Step-by-Step Proof

1. Consider an arbitrary undirected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges.
2. Choose any edge $e = \{u, v\} \in E$.
3. By definition, $e$ contributes **exactly 1** to $\deg(u)$ and **exactly 1** to $\deg(v)$.
4. Therefore, edge $e$ contributes **exactly 2** to the running total $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v)$.
5. Summing this contribution over all $m$ edges, the total contribution is $2m = 2 \vert E \vert$.
6. Hence the lemma is proved.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) &= \sum_{e = \{u, v\} \in E} \big[ \mathbf{1}_{\{u \in e\}} + \mathbf{1}_{\{v \in e\}} \big] \\
&= \sum_{e \in E} 2 \\
&= 2 \vert E \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

> Each edge is counted exactly twice — once at each endpoint. The indicator functions make this precise.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PageRank Vector

**PageRank** is the original Google algorithm that uses the *graph of hyperlinks* to rank web pages. It is a **predictive model** of "how important" a page is.

### Power-Iteration Form

$$
\mathbf{r}^{(k+1)} = d \cdot M \cdot \mathbf{r}^{(k)} + \frac{1 - d}{n} \mathbf{1}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

1. **Random Surfer Model** — Imagine a web user clicking links at random. They are on some page $i$.
2. With probability $d$ (the **damping factor**, typically $0.85$) they follow a random outgoing link.
3. With probability $1 - d$ they "teleport" to any of the $n$ pages uniformly at random.
4. Let $\mathbf{r}$ be the stationary probability distribution of the surfer.
5. The Markov chain transition matrix $M$ has entries $M_{ji} = 1 / \deg^+(i)$ for every link $i \to j$.
6. Combining the two behaviors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_j &= d \sum_{i \to j} \frac{r_i}{\deg^+(i)} + \frac{1 - d}{n} \\
\mathbf{r} &= d \cdot M \mathbf{r} + \frac{1 - d}{n} \mathbf{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The damping factor prevents rank sinks — closed loops that would absorb all probability mass.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Regression (Ordinary Least Squares)

We are given $n$ training points $(x_i, y_i)$. We want to find the line $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ that minimises the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**.

$$
\text{SSE}(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \big(y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i)\big)^2
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

1. Differentiate with respect to $\beta_0$ and set to zero:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \text{SSE}}{\partial \beta_0} &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \big(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\big) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \beta_0 &= \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

2. Differentiate with respect to $\beta_1$ and set to zero:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \text{SSE}}{\partial \beta_1} &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \big(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\big) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \beta_1 &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. Combining the two normal equations yields the **closed-form OLS solution**.

> This is the bedrock of every predictive modeling course; in KTU exams, at least one 7-mark sub-question expects the OLS derivation.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Logistic Regression and the Sigmoid

When the target $y$ is binary ($0$ or $1$), we use the **logistic** function to model the probability:

$$
P(y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x
$$

The model is trained by **Maximum Likelihood Estimation**. The log-likelihood is:

$$
\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \big[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log (1 - \hat{y}_i) \big]
$$

We maximise $\ell$ using **gradient ascent** (or equivalently minimise $-\ell$). The gradient is:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \big( y_i - \hat{y}_i \big) x_{ij}
$$

> The logistic function converts any real-valued score into a probability, making it the workhorse of binary predictive classification.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm

Given a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights, Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from source $s$ to every other vertex.

### Step-by-Step Pseudocode Walk-Through

1. **Initialise** $d(s) = 0$ and $d(v) = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$. Mark all nodes as unvisited.
2. Set current node $u = s$ (the unvisited node with smallest tentative distance).
3. For each neighbour $v$ of $u$ not yet visited, compute the **relaxation step**:
$$
d(v) \leftarrow \min \big( d(v),\ d(u) + w(u, v) \big)
$$
4. Mark $u$ as visited. It will not be processed again.
5. Repeat steps 2 – 4 until all reachable nodes are visited.
6. **Output** — the array $d(\cdot)$ contains the shortest distance from $s$ to every vertex.

### Example Trace (4-Node Graph)

Let $V = \{A, B, C, D\}$ and edges $A-B = 4, A-C = 2, C-B = 1, C-D = 5, B-D = 3$.

| Step | Visited | $d(A)$ | $d(B)$ | $d(C)$ | $d(D)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\emptyset$ | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 1 | $\{A\}$ | 0 | $0+4=4$ | $0+2=\mathbf{2}$ | $\infty$ |
| 2 | $\{A, C\}$ | 0 | $\min(4, 2+1)=\mathbf{3}$ | 2 | $2+5=7$ |
| 3 | $\{A, C, B\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | $\min(7, 3+3)=\mathbf{6}$ |
| 4 | $\{A, C, B, D\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 |

Final shortest distances from $A$: $A=0, B=3, C=2, D=6$.

> Dijkstra is the canonical graph algorithm used in KTU databases — every modern graph database implements some variant of it (Neo4j uses **Bidirectional Dijkstra** internally).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Predictive Model Suitability

| Model | Type | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression | Supervised | Continuous target, linear trend | Fails on non-linear data |
| Logistic Regression | Supervised | Binary classification | Linear decision boundary |
| Decision Tree | Supervised | Mixed data, interpretability | Overfits easily |
| Random Forest | Ensemble | High accuracy, robust | Black-box, slow |
| k-Nearest Neighbors | Instance-based | Small datasets, pattern matching | Curse of dimensionality |
| Neural Network | Deep learning | Images, NLP, complex patterns | Needs lots of data |
| **Graph Neural Network (GNN)** | **Graph-based** | **Social/recommender/network data** | Computationally expensive |

## 3.7 Worked Example — Link Prediction in a Social Graph

**Problem.** Given a social network where 4 users (A, B, C, D) are connected as $A - B$, $B - C$, $A - C$, $C - D$, predict whether a new edge $B - D$ is likely to form.

**Step 1 — Compute Jaccard Coefficient of common neighbours.**

- Neighbours of $B$: $\{A, C\}$.
- Neighbours of $D$: $\{C\}$.
- Common Neighbours: $\{C\}$.
- Union of Neighbours: $\{A, C\}$.
- Jaccard Coefficient: $J(B, D) = \dfrac{\vert N(B) \cap N(D)\vert}{\vert N(B) \cup N(D)\vert} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$.

**Step 2 — Compute Adamic-Adar Index (more accurate).**

$$
\text{AA}(B, D) = \sum_{z \in N(B) \cap N(D)} \frac{1}{\log \deg(z)}
$$

- The only common neighbour is $C$ with $\deg(C) = 3$ (connected to $A, B, D$).
- Therefore, $\text{AA}(B, D) = \dfrac{1}{\log 3} \approx 0.91$.

**Step 3 — Decision.** Both metrics are non-zero, so the model predicts a *likely* future edge $B - D$ (high probability of friendship formation).

> This is exactly the **graph-based predictive modeling** pipeline used by Facebook's "People You May Know" feature.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

The following diagrams use **only alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters** and **clean uppercase labels** with no markdown formatting inside quotes, complying with the Mermaid safeguards.

## 4.2 Diagram 1 — Graph Database Architecture with Predictive Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Data Sources RDBMS CSV IoT Streams] --> B[Graph Database Engine Neo4j Neptune TigerGraph]
    B --> C[Storage Layer Nodes Edges Properties]
    B --> D[Query Engine Cypher GQL SPARQL]
    D --> E[Graph Algorithms BFS DFS Dijkstra PageRank]
    E --> F[Feature Engineering Node Degree Centrality Community]
    F --> G[Predictive Modeling Layer Regression GNN Random Forest]
    G --> H[Predictions Output Recommendation Fraud Score]
    H --> I[Application Layer Dashboard API Web Mobile]
    I --> J[Feedback Loop Model Retraining]
    J --> G
```

> This block-level architecture shows the **end-to-end pipeline** of an advanced database system that fuses graph theory with predictive modeling. Every KTU 14-mark question on this topic expects you to draw or describe such a pipeline.

## 4.3 Diagram 2 — Sample Social Network Graph

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1((ALICE)) --- B1((BOB))
    B1 --- C1((CAROL))
    A1 --- C1
    C1 --- D1((DAVID))
    B1 --- D1
    A1 --- E1((EVE))
    E1 --- C1
    D1 --- F1((FRANK))
    F1 --- B1
```

> The graph above has **6 vertices and 8 edges**. KTU students are often asked to verify the handshaking lemma: $\sum \deg = 3 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 2 = 18 = 2 \times 8 = 2 \vert E \vert$ ✓.

## 4.4 Diagram 3 — Predictive Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Data] --> P2[Cleaning and Preprocessing]
    P2 --> P3[Feature Engineering Graph Features]
    P3 --> P4[Train Test Split]
    P4 --> P5[Model Training]
    P5 --> P6[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    P6 --> P7[Model Evaluation RMSE F1 AUC]
    P7 --> P8{Performance OK}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Deployment to Database]
    P8 -- No --> P5
    P9 --> P10[Real Time Predictions]
    P10 --> P11[Monitoring and Retraining]
    P11 --> P5
```

## 4.5 Diagram 4 — BFS vs. DFS Traversal Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BFS["BFS Level by Level"]
        direction TB
        BR[(ROOT)] --> BL1[(LEVEL 1 NODE 1)]
        BR --> BL2[(LEVEL 1 NODE 2)]
        BR --> BL3[(LEVEL 1 NODE 3)]
        BL1 --> BL11[(LEVEL 2 NODE 1A)]
        BL1 --> BL12[(LEVEL 2 NODE 1B)]
        BL2 --> BL21[(LEVEL 2 NODE 2A)]
    end
    subgraph DFS["DFS Depth First"]
        direction TB
        DR[(ROOT)] --> DL1[(CHILD 1)]
        DL1 --> DL2[(GRANDCHILD 1A)]
        DL2 --> DL3[(GREAT GRANDCHILD)]
        DL3 --> DL4[(DEEP NODE)]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 2 Mark Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define a graph in the context of database systems. Differentiate between a directed and an undirected graph with one real-world example each.**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Model Answer:**

A graph $G$ is an ordered pair $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is a finite non-empty set of vertices and $E \subseteq V \times V$ is the set of edges.

- **Directed Graph (Digraph):** Edges have an orientation. Example: Twitter followers — if $A$ follows $B$, an arrow $A \to B$ exists, but the reverse need not.
- **Undirected Graph:** Edges have no orientation. Example: Facebook friendships — if $A$ and $B$ are friends, the connection is symmetric.

**Difference (in tabular form expected in KTU valuation):**

| Feature | Directed | Undirected |
|---|---|---|
| Edge notation | $(u, v)$ | $\{u, v\}$ |
| Symmetry | Not symmetric | Symmetric |
| Degree type | In-degree, out-degree | Single degree |
| Example | Web hyperlinks | Road network (two-way) |

---

### Question 2
**Q: List any four graph-based features that can be used as inputs to a predictive model.**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Degree Centrality** — number of edges incident to a node.
2. **Betweenness Centrality** — fraction of shortest paths passing through a node.
3. **PageRank** — recursive importance based on incoming links.
4. **Clustering Coefficient** — density of triangles around a node.
5. **Closeness Centrality** — reciprocal of the sum of shortest path distances to all other nodes.

These features are then used as columns in a feature matrix fed to regression/classification models.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern Note]** In the 2024 scheme, Module-end 14-mark questions have **internal choice**. Below, both alternatives are provided with full mark breakdown.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the Handshaking Lemma and apply it to verify the degree sum of the following graph: $V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ with edges $\{AB, BC, CD, DE, EA, AC, CE\}$. (7 Marks)**

**(b) With suitable diagrams, explain the architecture of a graph database integrated with a predictive modeling pipeline. List any three real-world applications. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — State the Handshaking Lemma.**

For any undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges:

$$
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert
$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual degrees.**

| Vertex | Neighbours | Degree |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $B, E, C$ | 3 |
| $B$ | $A, C$ | 2 |
| $C$ | $B, D, A, E$ | 4 |
| $D$ | $C, E$ | 2 |
| $E$ | $D, A, C$ | 3 |

**Step 3 — Apply the lemma.**

$$
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 3 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 3 = 14
$$

$$
\vert E \vert = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 \vert E \vert = 14
$$

Since $14 = 14$, the lemma is verified ✓.

**Step 4 — Conclude.** The handshaking lemma holds, confirming the graph is a valid simple undirected graph.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Architecture of a Graph Database Integrated with Predictive Modeling**

A complete modern system has the following layers:

1. **Data Ingestion Layer** — Accepts structured/unstructured data from relational DBs, logs, APIs.
2. **Graph Storage Layer** — Persists nodes, edges, and properties (e.g., Neo4j's native graph storage).
3. **Query Processing Layer** — Executes declarative graph queries using **Cypher** or **GQL**.
4. **Graph Algorithm Engine** — Runs in-database algorithms (PageRank, shortest path, community detection).
5. **Feature Engineering Layer** — Extracts graph metrics (centrality, embeddings) and combines with non-graph features.
6. **Predictive Modeling Layer** — Trains ML models (regression, GNN, random forest) using extracted features.
7. **Inference / Serving Layer** — Returns predictions via API (REST/GraphQL) to consuming applications.
8. **Feedback / Retraining Loop** — Continuously updates the model as new data arrives.

**Three Real-World Applications:**

1. **Fraud Detection (PayPal, Mastercard)** — Build a transaction graph, run community detection, predict fraudulent rings.
2. **Recommendation Systems (Netflix, Amazon)** — Build a user–item bipartite graph, predict likely next interactions using collaborative filtering + GNNs.
3. **Social Network Analysis (LinkedIn)** — Predict "People You May Know" using common-neighbour and Adamic-Adar features.
4. *(Bonus)* — Drug discovery (protein–protein interaction prediction) and traffic forecasting.

> The KTU valuation key requires the **layered diagram** (refer to Section 4.2) for full marks. A textual list alone scores 5/7.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**(a) With a worked example, explain Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain linear and logistic regression with their cost functions. Show how both are used in predictive modeling for graph-based data. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — State the Problem.**

Given a weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with non-negative weights, find the shortest distance from a source $s$ to every other vertex.

**Step 2 — Describe the algorithm steps** (refer to Section 3.5 for the full derivation).

**Step 3 — Worked Example** (5-vertex graph).

Let $V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and weighted edges:

$$w(A,B) = 4,\ w(A,C) = 2,\ w(C,B) = 1,\ w(C,D) = 5,\ w(B,D) = 3,\ w(B,E) = 7,\ w(D,E) = 1$$

Initialise $d = \{A:0, B:\infty, C:\infty, D:\infty, E:\infty\}$.

| Iter | Visited | $d(A)$ | $d(B)$ | $d(C)$ | $d(D)$ | $d(E)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\emptyset$ | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 1 | $\{A\}$ | 0 | 4 | **2** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 2 | $\{A, C\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | **7** | $\infty$ |
| 3 | $\{A, C, B\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | **10** |
| 4 | $\{A, C, B, D\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | **7** |
| 5 | All | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 7 |

Final shortest distances from $A$: $A=0, B=3, C=2, D=6, E=7$.

**Step 4 — Complexity.** Using a binary min-heap, $T = O\big((\vert V\vert + \vert E\vert)\log \vert V\vert\big)$.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Linear Regression** assumes a linear relationship between input features $X$ and a continuous target $y$:

$$
\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_j
$$

The cost function is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**:

$$
J(\beta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\big(y_i - \hat{y}_i\big)^2
$$

Minimisation yields the OLS closed form (refer to Section 3.3 for derivation).

**Logistic Regression** is used when the target is binary. It applies the **sigmoid** function:

$$
P(y=1 \mid X) = \sigma(\beta_0 + \beta^T X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta^T X)}}
$$

The cost function is the **binary cross-entropy (log loss)**:

$$
J(\beta) = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \big[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i)\log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \big]
$$

**Application to Graph-Based Data:**

- **Linear Regression** can predict continuous graph metrics (e.g., the number of new edges a node will acquire next month) using centrality features as inputs.
- **Logistic Regression** can predict **link existence** (link prediction) — given two unconnected nodes $u$ and $v$, compute features like common neighbours, Jaccard coefficient, Adamic-Adar, and feed them to logistic regression to classify whether a future edge is likely.
- In **Graph Neural Networks (GNNs)**, the input features are node embeddings learned from the graph, and the output layer is essentially a logistic/softmax regression.

> The KTU key requires **both the cost function and a concrete example** to award full 7 marks for this part.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the handshaking lemma — a single missed coefficient costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up the adjacency matrix dimensions** — always state that $A$ is $\vert V\vert \times \vert V\vert$.
> 3. **Wrong sigmoid notation** — many students write $1/(1+e^x)$ (positive exponent) instead of $1/(1+e^{-x})$; this is marked wrong.
> 4. **Omitting the cost function** in regression derivations — the OLS closed form without $\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ scored 0 in past KTU keys.
> 5. **Confusing PageRank with HITS** — PageRank uses a single score vector; HITS uses *authority* and *hub* scores. Do not mix them.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** for linear regression (linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, normality of residuals). Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for missing assumptions.
> 7. **Drawing the graph with self-loops** when the question specifies a **simple graph** — a full mark cut in graph-theory questions.
> 8. **Using `|` in LaTeX inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table rendering; the KTU digital answer sheet may mark the formula as missing. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **graph** $G = (V, E)$ consists of vertices $V$ and edges $E$. The **handshaking lemma** is $\sum \deg(v) = 2 \vert E\vert$.
- **Complete graph** $K_n$ has $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ edges. **Bipartite** graphs have a 2-colourable vertex set. **DAGs** have no directed cycles.
- **Graph databases** (Neo4j, Neptune) use **property graph models**: nodes, relationships, properties. They outperform relational DBs for deep relational queries.
- **Graph algorithms** — BFS/DFS run in $O(\vert V\vert + \vert E\vert)$; Dijkstra in $O((\vert V\vert + \vert E\vert)\log \vert V\vert)$.
- **Centrality metrics** — Degree, Betweenness, Closeness, Eigenvector, PageRank.
- **Predictive modeling** pipeline — Data → Preprocessing → Features → Model → Train → Evaluate → Deploy.
- **Linear regression** cost: MSE. **Logistic regression** cost: binary cross-entropy + sigmoid output.
- **Evaluation metrics** — Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, RMSE, AUC-ROC.
- **Graph-enhanced predictive modeling** — Common-neighbour, Jaccard, Adamic-Adar, graph embeddings, GNNs.
- **PageRank** vector: $\mathbf{r} = d M \mathbf{r} + \frac{1-d}{n}\mathbf{1}$, damping $d \approx 0.85$.
- **KTU high-yield points** — the handshaking lemma proof, OLS derivation, sigmoid + cost function, Dijkstra trace, layered architecture diagram.
- Always include **assumptions, complexity analysis, and a real-world use case** for full marks in KTU 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geographic Information Systems (GIS): Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT795 - ADVANCED DATABASE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Geographic Information Systems (GIS): Components # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Geographic Information System (GIS)** is a computer-based information system that captures, stores, manipulates, analyzes, manages, and presents spatial or geographic data. In the context of the KTU 2024 *Advanced Database Systems* syllabus (Module 4), GIS is treated as a specialized extension of database technology — one that handles **geo-referenced data** (data tied to real-world coordinates on the Earth's surface). The **five classical components** of a GIS, as recognized by the GIS community and standardized in academic literature, are: 1. **Hardware** — the physical computing infrastructure. 2. **Software** — the programs, algorithms, and DBMS extensions used to process spatial data. 3. **Data** — the geo-referenced (spatial) and attribute (descriptive) information. 4. **People** — the trained professionals who design, operate, and interpret GIS output. 5. **Procedures (Methods)** — the standardized workflows, protocols, and business rules that govern GIS operation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of *Advanced Database Systems (PECDT795)* introduces GIS strictly as an **application domain of advanced / spatial databases**. You are expected to know how spatial databases (e.g., PostgreSQL/PostGIS, Oracle Spatial) connect with the five GIS components. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are running a **smart city control room**. You have a giant digital map of the city on a wall-sized screen. When a water pipe bursts, you don't just want to know *that* it broke — you want to know **exactly where** on the map, how many houses are within a **500-metre radius**, and which valves to shut off. A GIS is exactly this "smart map" but as a software system. It links **two kinds of information**: - **WHERE** something is (the *spatial* part — coordinates, boundaries, topology). - **WHAT** that thing is like (the *attribute* part — name, population, material, year built). A traditional DBMS (like MySQL or Oracle) stores the *WHAT* beautifully in tables. GIS bolts on a powerful *WHERE* engine on top — using coordinates, projections, and spatial indexing (R-trees, Quad-trees). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of GIS as a **layered cake**. Each layer holds one type of geographic feature (roads, rivers, buildings, population density). You can peel, combine, or query any combination of layers instantly — something a paper map can never do. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in GIS The following standard metrics and constants are referenced throughout GIS component discussions and appear commonly in KTU-level questions: - **Standard Coordinate Reference System:** WGS 84 (World Geodetic System 1984) — used by GPS. - **Earth's Mean Radius:** approximately **6,371 km** (commonly rounded to 6,371,000 m for distance calculations). - **Angular Unit:** Degree (°), with 360° = full circle, 1° ≈ 111.32 km at the equator. - **Standard Spatial Data Formats:** Shapefile (.shp), GeoJSON, KML, GDB. - **Resolution Scale:** Small scale (large area, low detail) vs. large scale (small area, high detail). --- ## 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping | CO ID | Course Outcome | Alignment with GIS Topic | |-------|----------------|--------------------------| | **CO4** | Understand spatial and temporal databases | GIS is a direct application of spatial DBMS | | **CO5** | Apply advanced DBMS concepts to real-world domains | GIS components represent real-world DB architecture | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Components — Structured Logic Breakdown ### Component 1: Hardware **Why it matters:** Spatial data is *massive* (satellite imagery alone can run into terabytes). GIS hardware must be capable of high-throughput I/O and parallel processing. **Core elements:** - **Workstations / Servers** with multi-core CPUs, ≥ **16 GB RAM** minimum (≥ **64 GB** recommended for production GIS). - **High-capacity Storage** (RAID arrays, SSD, NAS/SAN) — minimum **1 TB** for small deployments. - **GPS Receivers / Total Stations** for field data capture (sub-meter accuracy). - **Digitizers and Plotters** for legacy paper-map conversion. - **High-resolution Displays** for cartographic editing. **How it integrates:** Hardware is the substrate — without specialized capture devices (GPS) and rendering displays, the rest of the system is useless. --- ### Component 2: Software **Why it matters:** Software is the *brain* that translates geographic coordinates into meaningful queries. **Core elements:** - **GIS Application Suites:** ArcGIS (Esri), QGIS (open-source), MapInfo, GRASS GIS. - **Spatial DBMS Engines:** PostgreSQL/PostGIS, Oracle Spatial, SQL Server Spatial, MongoDB (GeoJSON support). - **Spatial Extensions / Libraries:** GDAL/OGR, GEOS, PROJ (coordinate reprojection). - **Web GIS Servers:** GeoServer, MapServer, ArcGIS Server. **How it integrates:** Software handles four functions — *input, management, analysis, output*. In KTU context, focus on the **spatial DBMS layer**, which extends SQL with spatial types (`GEOMETRY`, `GEOGRAPHY`), spatial functions (`ST_Distance`, `ST_Intersects`, `ST_Buffer`), and spatial indexes (R-tree, GiST). --- ### Component 3: Data — The Heart of GIS **Why it matters:** GIS is only as good as the data it holds. Data in GIS has **two inseparable dimensions**. **Two fundamental data types:** $$ \text{GIS Data} = \langle \text{Spatial Component} \rangle \oplus \langle \text{Attribute Component} \rangle $$ | Dimension | Description | Example | |-----------|-------------|---------| | **Spatial (Geometry)** | Shape, location, topology | Point (37.7749° N, 122.4194° W), Polygon (Kerala boundary) | | **Attribute (Non-spatial)** | Descriptive properties | City name = "Kochi", Population = 677,381, Year = 2024 | **Two primary data models:** | Model | Storage | Strength | Example Format | |-------|---------|----------|----------------| | **Vector** | Points, lines, polygons with coordinates | Precise boundaries, network analysis | Shapefile, GeoJSON | | **Raster** | Grid of cells (pixels) with values | Continuous phenomena (elevation, temperature) | GeoTIFF, JPEG2000 | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Vector data is stored in relational tables (one row per feature); raster data is stored as multi-dimensional arrays. The spatial DBMS bridges both via the `GEOMETRY` type and raster extensions. --- ### Component 4: People **Why it matters:** GIS is not a "plug and play" tool — it requires specialized human expertise. **Roles:** - **GIS Analyst / Scientist** — performs spatial analysis and modeling. - **Database Administrator (DBA)** — manages the spatial DBMS. - **Cartographer** — designs map products. - **Field Surveyor** — collects ground-truth data with GPS. - **End User / Decision Maker** — interprets outputs (e.g., urban planner). --- ### Component 5: Procedures (Methods) **Why it matters:** Without standardized workflows, GIS outputs become non-reproducible and legally questionable. **Core elements:** - **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** for data capture, QA/QC. - **Metadata Standards:** ISO 19115, FGDC, INSPIRE. - **Data Governance Policies:** access control, privacy, update frequency. - **Analytical Workflows:** how a query moves from user → DBMS → spatial engine → result. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following formulas are essential for solving numerical/analytical questions on GIS components and spatial databases in KTU 2024 examinations. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes | |---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------| | 1 | **Great-circle (Haversine) distance** | $\Delta \phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1$, $\Delta \lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1$
$a = \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)$
$d = 2R \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{a}\right)$ | $R \approx 6{,}371$ km; $\phi$ = latitude (rad); $\lambda$ = longitude (rad) | Computes shortest distance between two lat/long points | | 2 | **Buffer area (circle)** | $A = \pi r^2$ | $r$ = buffer radius (m); $A$ in m² | Used in spatial SQL `ST_Buffer(geom, r)` | | 3 | **Map scale** | $\text{Scale} = \dfrac{\text{Map Distance}}{\text{Ground Distance}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (e.g., 1:50,000) | Determines spatial detail vs. coverage | | 4 | **Raster resolution** | $\text{Ground Resolution} = \text{Pixel Size} \times \text{Scale}$ | meters/pixel | Higher resolution → larger file size | | 5 | **UTM zone number** | $\text{Zone} = \lfloor \tfrac{\lambda + 180}{6} \rfloor + 1$ | $\lambda$ = longitude in degrees | Each UTM zone spans 6° of longitude | | 6 | **Polygon area (planar)** | $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$ | Coordinates in meters; $A$ in m² | **Use `\vert` for absolute value** to avoid table syntax break | | 7 | **Database storage size estimate** | $S = N \times (R_{sp} + R_{at}) \times C$ | $N$ = features; $R_{sp}$ = spatial bytes; $R_{at}$ = attribute bytes; $C$ = compression factor | Critical for hardware sizing | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** When writing GIS formulas inside markdown tables, **never** use the bare vertical bar $\vert$ for absolute value. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table column breakage. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | How GIS Components Are Used | |--------------------|----------------------------| | **Urban Planning (Kerala Smart Cities)** | Hardware: GPS + servers; Software: QGIS; Data: building footprints; People: planners; Procedures: KMBR norms | | **Disaster Management (Kerala Floods 2018)** | Raster DEMs + vector inundation layers → rescue routing | | **Transportation Engineering** | Network analysis using vector road data + Dijkstra's shortest path | | **Environmental Monitoring** | Raster NDVI from satellite imagery → land-cover classification | | **E-Governance (Kerala K-Smart Project)** | Web GIS portals backed by PostgreSQL/PostGIS | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Haversine Distance (KTU High-Yield) The Haversine formula is the most commonly asked numerical in KTU for spatial database topics.
